2.7: YHWH amar elai beni atta ani hayom yelidticha (Yhwh ʾāmar ʾēlay, bĕnî ʾattâ ʾănî hayyôm yĕlídtîkā): 'The LORD said to me: You are my Son; today I have begotten you.' The royal adoption formula of Ps 2:7 was applied to the Davidic king at his enthronement — the king became YHWH's 'son' in a representative capacity (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 89:26-27). The NT applies it to Jesus at three Christological moments: the baptism (Matt 3:17), the transfiguration (2 Pet 1:17), and the resurrection (Acts 13:33; Heb 1:5; 5:5). The resurrection is the decisive 'today' in NT usage: the day of Christ's enthronement at the Father's right hand is the day of divine sonship's full eschatological declaration.
2.1:
לָמָּה רָגְשׁוּ גוֹיִם (lāmmāh rāgĕshû gôyim) — "why do the nations rage?" The verb rāgash (to be in tumult, to throng/conspire) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible outside of this form; the LXX translates ἐφρύαξαν (to be insolent/neigh like horses). The word suggests uncontrolled, agitated motion — the nations in restless, futile commotion. Acts 4:25 cites this verse at the Jerusalem church's prayer after the arrest of Peter and John, identifying the "nations" with Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and the people of Israel.

רִיק (rîq, "in vain") — the peoples plot rîq: emptiness, futility. The futility is built into the conspiracy from the beginning; not that it fails but that it is empty of substance.
2.2: מְשִׁיחוֹ (mĕshîḥô, "his Anointed One") — from māshaḥ, to anoint with oil. The word appears here in the context of the Davidic king on whom the LORD's oil was poured. "His Anointed" is the one who belongs to the LORD by virtue of anointing — the divine claim on the person. The conspiracy is therefore not against a human king but against the one who has been set apart by God.
2.3: מוֹסְרוֹתֵימוֹ (môsĕrôtêmô, "their bonds") and עֲבֹתֵימוֹ (ʿăbōtêmô, "their cords") — the rebels' self-understanding: they experience divine sovereignty as constraint. The cords (ʿăbōt) can also mean "ropes" used to bind animals — they picture themselves as livestock under a yoke. The irony is that what they call bonds are the ordering structures that make flourishing possible.
2.4: יִשְׂחַק (yisḥāq, "laughs") — the divine response to the conspiracy is not panic but laughter. The same root as the name Isaac (yitsḥāq), from ṣāḥaq — to laugh, to play. The LORD who sits enthroned in the heavens finds the nations' plots incongruous with reality. יִלְעַג (yilʿag, "scoffs") — to mock, to stammer in imitation; the LORD's scoffing is not contemptuous anger but the amusement of absolute security.
2.5: בְּאַפּוֹ (bĕʾappô, "in his wrath") and בַחֲרוֹן אַפּוֹ (baḥărôn ʾappô, "in his burning anger") — ʾap literally means "nostril/nose"; anger in Hebrew is conceived as snorting/fuming breath. Ḥărôn (burning, from ḥārāh, to burn) intensifies it: not merely irritation but heated wrath. The divine speech that follows (v6) is the decree that settles the matter.
2.6: נָסַכְתִּי (nāsaktî, "I have installed") — the verb nāsak typically means to pour out (as a libation) or to cast metal; in this context it means "to set in place" as one sets a foundation stone. The perfect tense (nāsaktî) declares a completed act: the installation of the King is already done. Zion as הַר-קָדְשִׁי (har qodshî, "my holy hill") designates the place as consecrated territory belonging to the LORD.
2.8: שְׁאַל מִמֶּנִּי (shĕʾal mimmennî, "ask of me") — the imperative of shāʾal: the invitation to request. The Anointed One is invited to ask for his inheritance — the nations and the ends of the earth. The inheritance motif connects to the Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:6) and the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). The gift is cosmic in scope: אַפְסֵי-אָרֶץ (ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ) — the "ends/limits of the earth," the outermost extremities.
2.9: תְּרֹעֵם בְּשֵׁבֶט בַּרְזֶל (tĕrōʿēm bĕshêbet barzel, "you shall rule them with an iron rod") — shêbet is both "rod/staff" (the shepherd's implement) and the scepter of authority. Iron makes it a weapon of force rather than a pastoral tool. תְּנַפְּצֵם (tĕnappĕṣêm, "shatter them") — piel of nāpaṣ: to smash into pieces, used of breaking pottery (Jer 19:11). This verse is cited three times in Revelation (2:27; 12:5; 19:15) as the authority given to the risen Christ and shared with those who overcome.
2.10: הַשְׂכִּילוּ (haśkîlû, "be wise") — hiphil imperative of śākal: not merely "be intelligent" but "act with insight/prudence" — wisdom that produces right action. The address turns from narrative to direct exhortation. הִוָּסְרוּ (hiwwāsĕrû, "take warning") — niphal of yāsar: receive instruction/discipline; the word is often used for parental or divine discipline.
2.11: עִבְדוּ אֶת-יְהוָה בְּיִרְאָה (ʿibdû ʾet-YHWH bĕyirʾāh, "serve the LORD with reverent fear") — ʿābad (serve/work) can refer to labor, worship, and service simultaneously; the kings are called to re-orient their service from self-appointed dominion to submission to the LORD. גִּילוּ בִּרְעָדָה (gîlû birʿādāh, "celebrate with trembling") — gîl is exultation, a word for intense, even physical, joy; rĕʿādāh is shaking, trembling. The pairing is paradoxical: joyful trembling, not joyless fear. Paul echoes this: "work out your salvation with fear and trembling" (Phil 2:12).
2.12: בַּר (bar, "Son") — an Aramaic word (not the Hebrew ben) appearing in this Hebrew poem; its rarity has made it a textual puzzle. The MT is supported by many manuscripts as bar ("son"), giving "pay homage to the Son." The LXX takes a different path (δράξασθε παιδείας, "embrace discipline"), suggesting the Greek translators read differently or interpreted the difficult term. The MKT follows MT. The psalm ends on the same note as it began: אַשְׁרֵי (ʾashrê) — "Blessed" — forming an inclusio with Psalm 1:1: the blessed life belongs to those who take refuge in the Son.

8.4: mah enosh ki tizkerenu uben adam ki tipqedenu (mâ-ʾĕnôš kî-tizqĕrennû ûben-ʾādām kî tipqĕdennû): 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?' Ben adam (son of man) in Ps 8 is the representative human made lower than the heavenly beings and crowned with glory — a meditation on Gen 1:26-28's dominion mandate. The NT (Heb 2:5-9) applies Ps 8 to Christ as the true human who fulfills the Adamic mandate: 'We do not yet see everything in subjection to him, but we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death.' Christ recapitulates and fulfills humanity's Ps 8 vocation.
8.1:
יְהוָה אֲדֹנֵינוּ (YHWH ʾădōnênû, "O LORD our Lord") — a compressed theologoumenon: the personal covenant name YHWH combined with the sovereign title ʾādôn (master/lord) with the first-person plural suffix: our sovereign. The doubled address — YHWH as personal name, ʾAdônênû as title — appears identically at the psalm's close (v9), forming an inclusio that brackets the entire meditation.

אֲשֶׁר-תְּנָה הוֹדְךָ עַל-הַשָּׁמָיִם (ʾăsher-tĕnāh hôdĕkā ʿal-hashāmāyim, "You have placed your glory above the heavens") — hôd (splendor/majesty/glory) exceeding even the heavens in scope. The same word used of royal majesty (1 Chr 29:25) and the garment of divine splendor (Ps 104:1). The glory that cannot be contained in the heavens (1 Kgs 8:27) is the overflow of the Creator's nature.
8.2: מִפִּי עוֹלְלִים וְיֹנְקִים (mippî ʿôlĕlîm wĕyōnĕqîm, "From the mouths of children and nursing infants") — ʿôlĕlîm (children, those who glean or play) and yōnĕqîm (nursing infants, from yānaq, to suck): the most dependent and inarticulate of human beings. יִסַּדְתָּ עֹז (yissadtā ʿōz, "you have ordained praise") — the key translation decision: ʿōz means "strength/fortress/might" but the LXX rendered it αἶνον (praise), and that is how Jesus cites it in Matt 21:16 ("from the mouth of children you have prepared praise"). The MKT follows the LXX interpretation; the Hebrew is genuinely ambiguous — "you have established strength/praise" through the weakest voices.
8.3: מַעֲשֵׂה אֶצְבְּעֹתֶיךָ (maʿăśê ʾeṣbĕʿōtêkā, "the work of your fingers") — ʾeṣbaʿ (finger): the delicate, precise digit rather than the hand. Ps 19:1 uses "hands" for the heavens; here Psalm 8:3 uses "fingers" for the moon and stars — the fine-detail work of precise craftsmanship, as if the stars were set in place individually with fingertips. The cosmos as divine handiwork of unimaginable precision and care.
8.5:
וַתְּחַסְּרֵהוּ מְּעַט מֵאֱלֹהִים (watĕḥassĕrêhû mĕʿaṭ mêʾĕlōhîm, "Yet you have made him just a little lower than the heavenly beings") — the central interpretive crux: is ʾĕlōhîm God himself, or divine/heavenly beings (angels)? All three options have ancient support: the LXX reads ἀγγέλους (angels), which Heb 2:7 follows and applies to Christ. If the MT means God, the gap between humanity and the creator is rendered almost infinitesimal — a remarkable statement of human dignity. The MKT "heavenly beings" preserves the ambiguity while leaning toward the LXX/Hebrews reading.

וְכָבוֹד וְהָדָר תְּעַטְּרֵהוּ (wĕkābôd wĕhādār tĕʿaṭṭĕrêhû, "crowned him with glory and honor") — the same royal vocabulary used of the Davidic king (Ps 21:5) applied to humanity as a whole. The crown (ʿāṭar) is the royal coronation — humanity as the crown-bearing representative of the Creator in the created order.
8.6: תַּמְשִׁילֵהוּ בְּמַעֲשֵׂי יָדֶיךָ (tamshîlêhû bĕmaʿăśê yādêkā, "You have given him dominion over the works of your hands") — māshal (hiphil: to cause to rule/have dominion); the same verb root that underlies "ruling" in Gen 1:26-28. Humanity is placed in the position of ruling over what God's hands made — a profound delegation of creative authority. כֹּל שַׁתָּה תַחַת-רַגְלָיו (kōl shattāh taḥat-raglāyw, "you have placed everything under his feet") — "all" (kōl) without qualification; "under his feet" (taḥat raglāyw) is the posture of subjects before a king, enemies before a conqueror. Heb 2:8 acknowledges that "we do not yet see everything subjected to him" — the psalm describes the eschatological reality that Christ will fully enact.
8.7: צֹנֶה וַאֲלָפִים כֻּלָּם (ṣōneh waʾălāpîm kullām, "all sheep and cattle") — the specification of "all sheep and cattle" begins the inventory of the creation under human dominion, descending from domesticated animals to wild, then to the sky, then the sea. The comprehensiveness matches Gen 1:28's categories. The verse has no syntactic complexity — it is a list, and the simplicity amplifies the sweep of the grant.
8.8: צִפּוֹר שָׁמַיִם וּדְגֵי הַיָּם (ṣippôr shāmayim ûdĕgê hayyām, "the birds of the sky and the fish of the sea") — the inventory now covers the three realms: land (v7), sky, and sea. עֹבֵר אָרְחוֹת יַמִּים (ʿōbêr ʾorĕḥôt yammîm, "whatever moves along the ocean's currents") — the participle ʿōbêr (moving along) and the plural ʾorĕḥôt (paths/currents) of yammîm (seas): whatever passes through the lanes of the sea. The underwater paths suggest the orderly structure even of ocean currents — the cosmos is not chaotic but has paths. All creatures in all domains are named as subject to the human delegate.
8.9: The verbal repetition of v1 — יְהוָה אֲדֹנֵינוּ מָה-אַדִּיר שִׁמְךָ בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ — closes the inclusio. The meditation moves from praise of the Name (v1), through the marvel of human dignity within the creation (vv2-8), back to praise of the Name (v9). The theological movement is: the majestic Creator has made frail humans (ʾĕnôsh, v4) as his ruling representatives — a paradox that resolves only in the one who is fully both: the Son of Man who is also Lord of all.

22.1: eli eli lamah azavtani (ʾēlî ʾēlî lāmāh ʿăzabtānî): 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The opening of the Passion Psalm in its Hebrew form. The Aramaic at the cross (Mark 15:34: Eloi eloi lema sabachthani) uses the Aramaic form of the Psalm's Hebrew opening, suggesting Jesus was quoting from memory in the vernacular. The Psalm moves from dereliction (vv. 1-21) to proclamation and praise (vv. 22-31) — ending not in abandonment but in universal witness ('all the ends of the earth shall turn to the LORD', v. 27). The early church understood Jesus's citation of the Psalm's opening as invoking the whole Psalm, including its vindication ending.
22.2: ʾĕlōhay ʾezʿaq yômām wĕlōʾ taʿăneh (ʾĕlōhay ʾezʿaq yômām wĕlōʾ taʿăneh): 'O my God, I cry by day, but you do not answer.' The sustained cry yômām ... wĕlāylâ ('by day ... and by night') conveys relentlessness — prayer without intermission yet without response. The phrase wĕlōʾ dûmîyâ lî, 'and I have no silence/rest,' is better read 'there is no silence for me' — the psalmist cannot stop crying out because the distress is too great. The silence of God is the opposite of the heard cry in Ps 9:12; it is the extremity that makes this psalm the most anguished in the Psalter.
22.3: wĕʾattâ qādôš yôšēb tĕhillôt yiśrāʾēl (wĕʾattâ qāḏôš yôšēḇ tĕhillôṯ yiśrāʾēl): 'Yet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.' The waw-adversative wĕʾattâ ('but you') introduces the theological anchor: God's holiness and his enthronement on praise are not negated by the psalmist's experience of abandonment. The noun tĕhillôt ('praises') used as the seat of divine enthronement means that Israel's worship constitutes the medium in which God is present and sovereign — a remarkable theology of liturgy. The verb yōšēb ('sit/dwell') connects to the divine throne imagery of Ps 9:7 and 11:4.
22.4: bĕkā bāṭĕḥû ʾăbōtênû bāṭĕḥû wattĕpallĕṭēm (bĕḵā ḇāṭĕḥû ʾăḇōṯênû bāṭĕḥû wattĕp̄allĕṭēm): 'In you our fathers trusted; they trusted, and you delivered them.' The double bāṭĕḥû ('they trusted ... they trusted') is emphatic — the ancestral trust was complete and unequivocal. The result: wattĕpallĕṭēm ('and you delivered them') — a perfect of completed action. The pattern establishes precedent: trust in YHWH has historically issued in rescue. The psalmist invokes this history as the basis for his own prayer — not to claim guaranteed deliverance but to remind God of his own track record.
22.5: ʾēlêkā zāʿăqû wĕnimmalāṭû (ʾēlêḵā zāʿăqû wĕnimmalāṭû): 'To you they cried and were saved.' The verb zāʿaq ('cry aloud, cry for help') is repeated from v.2 in a contrasting pattern: the fathers cried (v.5) and were saved; the psalmist cries (v.2) and is not answered. The phrase bĕkā bāṭĕḥû wĕlōʾ bôšû, 'in you they trusted and were not put to shame,' uses bôš ('be ashamed, disappointed') — the ultimate fear of the one whose prayer goes unanswered. The fathers' non-shame is the psalmist's hope and the ground of his continued crying.
22.6: wĕʾānōkî tôlaʿat wĕlōʾ-ʾîš (wĕʾānōḵî ṯôlaʿaṯ wĕlōʾ-ʾîš): 'But I am a worm and not a man.' The contrast wĕʾānōkî ('but I') is emphatic: the honored ancestors (vv.4-5) versus the degraded present. The noun tôlaʿat ('worm') is used for a crimson-producing grub (tôlāʿ); to be a worm is to be beneath the dignity of human personhood. The phrase ḥerpat ʾādām ûbĕzûy ʿām, 'a reproach of mankind and despised by the people,' uses ḥerpâ ('reproach, taunt') and bāzâ ('despised') — social contempt that reduces a person to sub-human status. The NT applies this to Christ's passion (cf. Isa 53:3).
22.7: kol-rōʾay yilʿăgû-lî (ḵol-rōʾay yilʿăḡû-lî): 'All who see me mock me.' The verb lāʿag ('to mock, deride') describes the social humiliation of the lamenter. The specific gestures — yaptîrû bĕśāpâ yānîʿû rōʾš, 'they make mouths at me, they wag their heads' — are the recognized gestures of contempt and derision in the ancient world (cf. Job 16:4; Lam 2:15). Matthew explicitly cites this verse in the passion narrative: 'those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads' (Matt 27:39), understanding the mockers at the cross as fulfilling the posture described here.
22.8: gōl ʾel-YHWH yĕpallĕṭēhû (ḡōl ʾel-YHWH yĕp̄allĕṭēhû): 'He rolled his burden upon the LORD; let him deliver him.' The qal imperative gōl ('roll!') draws on the idiom of rolling a burden onto another (cf. Prov 16:3). The mockers' quotation — yitpallĕṭēhû kî ḥāpeṣ bô, 'let him rescue him, for he delights in him' — is savage irony: if YHWH truly favors this person, let him prove it by rescue. Matthew 27:43 cites this verse directly: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him; for he said, I am the Son of God.' The verbal correspondence between the Psalm's mockers and the crucifixion mockers is exact.
22.9: kî-ʾattâ gōḥî mibbāṭen (kî-ʾattâ ḡōḥî mibbāṭen): 'Yet you are he who took me from the womb.' The particle kî ('yet, for') introduces a counter-argument to the mockery: the psalmist appeals to the intimacy of his origins. The verb gōḥî (hiphil of gāḥ, 'to burst forth, draw out') describes the violent creative act of birth — YHWH was the one who drew him out. The phrase maḇṭîḥî ʿal šĕdê ʾimmî, 'you made me trust at my mother's breasts,' means that even the infant's trust in the mother's provision was mediated by YHWH — divine care precedes rational faith.
22.10: ʿālêkā hošlaktî mērāḥem (ʿālêḵā hošlaktî mērāḥem): 'Upon you was I cast from birth.' The hophal hošlaktî ('I was cast/thrown') — a striking passive that places the infant's placement in God's hands — inverts the language of abandonment: the psalmist was not abandoned but cast upon YHWH from the womb (mērāḥem, 'from the womb'). The phrase mibbeten ʾimmî ʾēlî ʾāttâ, 'from my mother's womb you have been my God,' makes the covenant relationship pre-rational and pre-voluntary — YHWH is 'my God' from before the psalmist could choose it. This grounds the prayer: if you have been my God since birth, you cannot abandon me now.
22.11: ʾal-tirhaq mimmennî kî ṣārâ qĕrôbâ (ʾal-tirhaq mimmennî kî ṣārâ qĕrôbâ): 'Be not far from me, for trouble is near.' The negative imperative ʾal-tirhaq ('do not be far') is the direct petition — the counterpart to the lament of v.1 (God is far). The crisis: ṣārâ qĕrôbâ ('trouble is near') contrasts with YHWH who seems far — nearness and farness are inverted. The phrase kî ʾên ʿôzēr, 'for there is no helper,' identifies the absence of human aid: YHWH is the only recourse, which makes his apparent absence catastrophic.
22.12: sĕbābûnî pārîm rabbîm ʾabbîrê bāšān kiṯṭĕrûnî (sĕḇāḇûnî p̄ārîm rabbîm ʾabbîrê ḇāšān kiṯṭĕrûnî): 'Many bulls encompass me; strong bulls of Bashan surround me.' The imagery of bulls — pārîm rabbîm ('many bulls') and ʾabbîrê bāšān ('strong bulls of Bashan') — draws on the famous breeding-stock of Bashan's well-fed pastures (cf. Amos 4:1, where Samaria's oppressive wealthy are called 'cows of Bashan'). The encircling movement — sĕbābûnî ... kiṭṭĕrûnî — suggests being surrounded, cut off, with no exit. The enemies are powerful, well-fed, and coordinated.
22.13: pāṣû ʿālay pîhem ʾaryēh ṭārēp wĕšōʾēg (pāṣû ʿālay pîhem ʾaryēh ṭārēp̄ wĕšōʾēḡ): 'they open wide their mouths at me, like a lion ravening and roaring.' The verb pāṣâ ('to open wide') combined with the lion imagery — ʾaryēh ṭārēp ('a lion tearing prey') wĕšōʾēg ('and roaring') — creates the soundscape of predation. The open mouth of the lion is simultaneously an attack (devouring) and a threat display (roaring). The enemies' open mouths echo the mockers of v.7 — hostility expressed through the mouth, whether by derision or by consuming violence.
22.14: kammayim nišpaktî wĕhitpardĕdû kol-ʿaṣmôtāy (kammayim nišpaktî wĕhiṯp̄ardĕdû ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāy): 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint.' The two images — poured water and disjointed bones — describe the utter dissolution of physical integrity. The pual nišpaktî ('I am poured out') is passive: the psalmist is not emptying himself but being drained. The verse continues: hāyâ libbî kaddônāg nāmēs bĕtôk mēʿāy, 'my heart has become like wax, melted in the midst of my inward parts.' Wax melting in heat loses its form — the heart's structural capacity for emotion and decision collapses. Early Christian reflection saw in these physical descriptions a prophetic anticipation of crucifixion's physiological reality (hypovolemic shock, joint dislocation).
22.15: yābēš kāḥerpĕśî kōḥî ûlĕšônî mĕdubbāq malqôḥāy (yāḇēš ḵāḥerpĕśî ḵōḥî ûlĕšônî mĕḏubbāq malqôḥāy): 'my strength is dried up like a potsherd; and my tongue cleaves to my jaws.' The simile kāḥerpĕśî ('like a potsherd') — a broken fragment of fired clay, completely dehydrated — captures the state of desperate thirst and physical exhaustion. The phrase ûlĕšônî mĕdubbāq malqôḥāy, 'my tongue sticks to my jaws,' is vivid dehydration — the tongue literally adhering to the mouth from lack of moisture. The closing statement wĕlāʿāpar māwet tišpĕtēnî, 'and you lay me in the dust of death,' makes YHWH the agent of this extremity — a radical theology of divine sovereignty over suffering.
22.16: kî sĕbābûnî kĕlābîm ʿădat mĕrēʿîm hiqqîpûnî (kî sĕḇāḇûnî ḵĕlāḇîm ʿăḏaṯ mĕrēʿîm hiqqîp̄ûnî): 'for dogs encompass me; a company of evildoers encircles me.' The enemies shift from bulls (v.12) to dogs — the unclean scavengers of the ancient world who consumed corpses. The phrase kārû yādāy wĕraglāy, traditionally rendered 'they have pierced my hands and my feet,' is textually disputed: the MT has kāʾărî ('like a lion'), while some Hebrew manuscripts, the LXX, and Syriac read kārû ('they dug/pierced'). The piercing reading became the primary NT exegetical reading (John 19:37; Luke 24:39; Rev 1:7) for the crucifixion. The Masoretic 'like a lion' makes grammatical sense as an extension of the predator imagery.
22.17: ʾăsappēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāy hēmmâ yabbîṭû yirʾû-bî (ʾăsappēr ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāy hēmmâ yabbîṭû yirʾû-ḇî): 'I can count all my bones — they stare and gloat over me.' The ability to count bones (ʾăsappēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāy) describes the emaciated body: skin pulled tight over protruding bones, every rib visible. The enemies observe this display with yabbîṭû ('they stare') and yirʾû ('they look') — their gaze is that of spectators at an execution, unmoved and satisfied. The spectacle of the dying righteous man as public entertainment is the social context of the passion: 'And sitting down they watched him there' (Matt 27:36).
22.18: yĕḥallĕqû bĕgādāy lāhem wĕʿal-lĕbûšî yappîlû gôrāl (yĕḥallĕqû ḇĕḡāḏāy lāhem wĕʿal-lĕḇûšî yappîlû ḡôrāl): 'they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' The verb ḥālaq (piel, 'to divide, apportion') and the lot-casting (yappîlû gôrāl) for the outer garment are among the most literally-fulfilled prophetic details in the Psalter. John 19:23-24 cites this verse explicitly: 'that the Scripture might be fulfilled, they said, Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it.' John's citation names two garments — the divided outer garments and the un-divided tunic — corresponding to the psalm's two-part description. The precision of the fulfillment citation is unusual even by NT standards.
22.19: wĕʾattâ YHWH ʾal-tirḥāq ʾeyālûtî lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ (wĕʾattâ YHWH ʾal-tirḥāq ʾeyālûṯî lĕʿezrāṯî ḥûšâ): 'But you, O LORD, be not far off; O my strength, come quickly to my aid!' The waw-adversative wĕʾattâ ('but you') — the same structure as v.3 and v.9 — marks a repeated pivot from suffering to address to God. The word ʾeyālûtî ('my strength/might' — from ʾayil, 'ram, strong one') is an unusual divine title, used only here in the Psalter. The imperative ḥûšâ ('hurry!') — from ḥûš ('to move quickly') — adds urgency: not merely 'come' but 'come now.'
22.20: haṣṣîlâ mēḥereb napšî miyyad-keleb yĕḥîdātî (haṣṣîlâ mēḥereḇ napšî miyyaḏ-ḵeleḇ yĕḥîḏāṯî): 'Deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of the dog.' The hiphil haṣṣîlâ ('deliver, snatch away') is an urgent rescue verb. The three paired enemies — sword (ḥereb), dog (keleb), lion (v.21) — represent different modes of violent death. The word yĕḥîdātî ('my only one, my precious/unique life') from yāḥîd ('only, solitary') intensifies the stakes: the psalmist has only one life; its loss cannot be recovered. The LXX reads 'my unique soul' — the soul's uniqueness making its loss irreversible.
22.21: hôšîʿēnî mippî ʾaryēh ûmiqarnê rēmîm ʿănîtānî (hôšîʿēnî mippî ʾaryēh ûmiqarnê rēmîm ʿănîtānî): 'Save me from the mouth of the lion; from the horns of the wild oxen you have answered me!' The verse is theologically pivotal: the lament section ends and the praise section begins, but the transition is grammatically abrupt. The qal perfect ʿănîtānî ('you have answered me') is unexpected — suddenly the rescue is narrated as accomplished. Whether this represents a sudden assurance experienced in prayer, or a shift to prophetic confidence, the effect is that death's worst threat (v.15-18) yields to divine answer. The psalm turns on this verb.
22.22: ʾăsappĕrâ šimkā lĕʾaḥay bĕtôk qāhāl ʾăhallĕlekā (ʾăsappĕrâ šimkā lĕʾaḥay bĕṯôḵ qāhāl ʾăhallĕleḵā): 'I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.' The cohortative ʾăsappĕrâ ('I will recount') signals the vow of praise that typically concludes the lament, but here it launches an extended praise section (vv.22-31). Hebrews 2:12 cites this verse as the voice of Jesus: 'I will tell of your name to my brothers, in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise' — understanding the risen Christ as the one who declares the Father's name to the church. The congregation (qāhāl) is the community of the redeemed.
22.23: yirʾê YHWH hallĕlûhû kol-zeraʿ yaʿăqōb kabbĕdûhû (yirʾê YHWH hallĕlûhû ḵol-zeraʿ yaʿăqōḇ kaббĕḏûhû): 'You who fear the LORD, praise him! All you offspring of Jacob, glorify him!' The imperatives — hallĕlûhû ('praise him!'), kabbĕdûhû ('glorify him!'), wĕgûrû mimmennû ('stand in awe of him!') — summon three concentric groups: those who fear YHWH, Jacob's offspring, and Israel. The universal address begins here and expands in vv.27-31. The ground: kî lōʾ-bāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănût ʿānî wĕlōʾ-histîr pānāyw mimmennû, 'for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him' (v.24). The praise pivots on the unexpected answer of v.21.
22.24: kî lōʾ-bāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănût ʿānî (kî lōʾ-ḇāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănaṯ ʿānî): 'for he has not despised or abhorred the affliction of the afflicted.' The double negation — lōʾ-bāzâ ('has not despised') and lōʾ šiqqaṣ ('has not treated as detestable/abhorred') — are the strongest possible negatives applied to the failure mode the lament feared. The noun ʿănût ʿānî ('the affliction of the afflicted') encompasses the entire suffering of vv.6-21. The theological revolution: what appeared as divine abandonment (v.1) was actually sustained divine attention — ûbĕšawwĿʿô ʾēlāyw šāmaʿ, 'and when he cried to him, he heard.' The hearing was always happening; the abandonment was perceived, not real.
22.25: mēʾittĕkā tĕhillātî bĕqāhāl rāb (mēʾittĕḵā tĕhillātî bĕqāhāl rāḇ): 'From you comes my praise in the great assembly.' The phrase mēʾittĕkā ('from you') is theologically significant: even the psalmist's praise is a divine gift, originating in YHWH rather than in the psalmist's autonomous virtue. The plural nĕdārāy ʾăšallēm, 'I will pay my vows' — the vows made in distress (v.22) are now fulfilled in the public worship of the qāhāl rāb ('great congregation'). The assembly of worshippers becomes the theater in which the drama of lament-answered-by-rescue is publicly proclaimed.
22.26: yōʾkĕlû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśbāʿû (yōʾḵĕlû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśbāʿû): 'The afflicted shall eat and be satisfied.' The shared meal — yōʾkĕlû ... wĕyiśbāʿû ('they eat and are satisfied') — is the communal dimension of the rescue: the ʿănāwîm ('afflicted, poor, humble') who share the psalmist's condition share also in the celebration. The phrase yĕhallĕlû YHWH dōrĕšāyw yĕḥî lĕbabkem lāʿad, 'those who seek him shall praise the LORD; may your hearts live forever!' introduces yĕḥî lĕbabkem lāʿad ('may your hearts live forever') — a blessing of perpetual life tied to the community of YHWH-seekers. The meal's eschatological overtones anticipate the messianic banquet (Rev 19:9).
22.27: yizkĕrû wĕyāšûbû ʾel-YHWH kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ (yizkĕrû wĕyāšûḇû ʾel-YHWH ḵol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ): 'All the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the LORD.' The scope expands from the congregation (v.25-26) to the universe: kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ ('all the ends of the earth') and kol-mišpĕḥôt haggôyim ('all the families of the nations'). The verbs — yizkĕrû ('they shall remember'), yāšûbû ('they shall turn/return'), wĕyištaḥăwû ('they shall bow down') — describe the conversion of the nations as remembering + turning + worship. This sequence mirrors the missionary vision of Ps 67:2-3 and the NT universal proclamation (Acts 1:8; Rev 7:9).
22.28: kî laYHWH hammelûkâ ûmōšēl baggôyim (kî laYHWH hammelûkâ ûmōšēl baggôyim): 'for kingship belongs to the LORD, and he rules over the nations.' The divine kingship declaration laYHWH hammelûkâ is absolute: the definite article on hammelûkâ ('the kingship') asserts that there is only one legitimate sovereign, and it belongs to YHWH. The participle mōšēl ('the one who rules') expresses ongoing governance. This verse is the theological center of vv.27-31: the universal conversion of nations is grounded in the universality of YHWH's sovereignty — all must turn to him because he already rules them.
22.29: ʾāklû wĕhištaḥăwû kol-dišnê-ʾereṣ (ʾāḵlû wĕhišṯaḥăwû ḵol-dišnê-ʾereṣ): 'All the prosperous of the earth eat and bow down.' The phrase dišnê-ʾereṣ ('the fat ones of the earth') — the wealthy and powerful — also bow. Worship is not limited to the poor (v.26) but extends to the prosperous. The counterpart: kol-yôrĕdê ʿāpār wĕnapšô lōʾ ḥiyyâ, 'before him shall bow all who go down to the dust, even the one who cannot keep his soul alive.' Even the dying, those reduced to dust (returning to Gen 3:19's ʿāpār), worship. Death itself is not an exception to divine sovereignty.
22.30: zeraʿ yaʿăbĕdennû yĕsuppār laʾdōnāy laddôr (zeraʿ yaʿăḇoḏennû yĕsuppār laʾḏōnāy laḏḏôr): 'A seed shall serve him; it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation.' The noun zeraʿ ('seed, offspring') looks forward: the rescue of the psalmist generates a continuing community of servants. The phrase yĕsuppār laʾdōnāy laddôr ('it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation') describes inter-generational witness — each generation hears and tells. The passive yĕsuppār ('it shall be told') implies that the story propagates on its own power: the report of YHWH's rescue is self-perpetuating good news.
22.31: yābōʾû wĕyaggîdû ṣidqātô lĕʿam nôlād kî ʿāśâ (yāḇōʾû wĕyaḡḡîdû ṣiḏqāṯô lĕʿam nôlāḏ kî ʿāśâ): 'They shall come and proclaim his righteousness to a people yet to be born, that he has done it.' The final phrase kî ʿāśâ, 'that he has done it' — two Hebrew words, among the most theologically dense in the Psalter — is the summary of the psalm's entire movement: God acted. This bare declaration is the kerygmatic core: from dereliction (v.1) to proclamation (v.31), the entire psalm is the testimony of one who cried out, was answered, and now tells. The psalm ends not with the psalmist's experience but with the report going forward to ʿam nôlād ('a people yet to be born') — the endless echo of rescue through history.

110.1: neum YHWH laadoni shev limini ad ashit oyvecha hadom leraglecha (nĕʾum Yhwh laʾdōnî, šēb liymînî ʿad-ʾāšît ʾōyĕbêkā hădōm lĕraglêkā): 'The oracle of YHWH to my lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool.' Neum (oracle of) is the formula for divine speech — the highest possible speech-authority marker in Hebrew prophecy. David writes laadoni (to my lord), creating the theological puzzle Jesus exploits in Matt 22:41-46: how can David's descendant also be David's lord? The Davidic Messiah must be more than a Davidic son; only divine sonship accounts for the double identity. Shev limini (sit at my right hand): enthronement at the supreme position of divine authority — the NT applies this to the resurrection/ascension as Christ's enthronement (Acts 2:34-35; Eph 1:20-21; Heb 1:3).
110.4: nishba YHWH velo yinachem atta kohen leolam al divrati malkitsedek (nišbaʿ Yhwh wĕlōʾ yinnāḥēm ʾattā-kōhēn lĕʿôlām ʿal-diḇrātî malkîṣedeq): 'YHWH has sworn and will not change his mind: You are a priest forever after the manner of Melchizedek.' The divine oath (nishba ... velo yinachem) makes this the most certain OT promise after the Abrahamic oath. Malkitsedek (Melchizedek, king of righteousness): the mysterious priest-king of Gen 14:18-20 who blessed Abraham and received a tithe — without genealogy, without recorded birth or death. Hebrews 7 uses the silence of Scripture about Melchizedek's origins as a typological argument: a priesthood with no recorded beginning or end points to an eternal priesthood.
110.2: מַטֵּה-עֻזְּךָ יִשְׁלַח יְהוָה מִצִּיּוֹן רְדֵה בְּקֶרֶב אֹיְבֶיךָ (maṭṭêh-ʿuzzĕkā yishlah YHWH miṣṣiyyôn rĕdêh bĕqereb ʾōyĕbêkā, "The LORD extends your powerful scepter from Zion: rule in the midst of your enemies!") — maṭṭêh-ʿuzzĕkā: the rod/scepter of your strength (the symbol of royal authority — Gen 49:10: "the scepter shall not depart from Judah"; Num 24:17: "a scepter shall rise from Israel"). The scepter is sent/stretched out from Zion (the place of the divine throne — Ps 2:6) into the midst of the enemies — not to a safe territory but to the enemy's own domain. Rĕdêh bĕqereb ʾōyĕbêkā: "rule in the midst of your enemies!" — the present-tense command to exercise dominion before the enemies are defeated. Sovereignty is exercised in the midst of opposition, not after its resolution (Rom 8:37: "in all these things we are more than conquerors").
110.3: עַמְּךָ נְדָבֹת בְּיוֹם חֵילֶךָ בְּהַדְרֵי-קֹדֶשׁ מֵרֶחֶם מִשְׁחָר לְךָ טַל יַלְדֻתֶיךָ (ʿammĕkā nĕdābōt bĕyôm ḥêlekā bĕhadĕrê-qōdesh mêreḥem mishḥar lĕkā ṭal yaldutêkā, "On the day you march to war, your people are freewill-offerings, in holy splendor; like dew from the womb of the dawn, your young warriors come to you!") — nĕdābōt: freewill-offerings/volunteers (from nādab: to volunteer, to give freely — Judg 5:2: "when the leaders led and the people volunteered, bless the LORD!"; Exod 35:21,29: the free-will giving for the tabernacle). The army is not conscripted but volunteer — those who come because they want to, as freewill offerings. Bĕhadĕrê-qōdesh: in holy splendors (the sacred adornment — Ps 29:2; 96:9; 1 Chr 16:29). Mêreḥem mishḥar lĕkā ṭal yaldutêkā: "from the womb of the dawn, like dew, is your youth/young warriors" — the dawn's womb generating dew as rapidly and abundantly as the Messiah's army materializes: innumerable, fresh, and forming before anyone expected them (the dew's miraculous appearance).
110.5: אֲדֹנָי עַל-יְמִינְךָ מָחַץ בְּיוֹם-אַפּוֹ מְלָכִים (ʾădōnāy ʿal-yĕmînĕkā māḥaṣ bĕyôm-ʾappô mĕlākhîm, "The Lord stands at your right hand; he will crush kings on the day of his wrath!") — a remarkable inversion: in v1, the Messiah sits at YHWH's right hand; here, ʾădōnāy (the Lord) is at the Messiah's right hand. The two verses together describe a dynamic royal relationship: the Messiah is exalted to YHWH's right hand (v1), and YHWH supports the Messiah at his right hand (v5) when he goes into battle. Māḥaṣ bĕyôm-ʾappô mĕlākhîm: he shatters/crushes kings on the day of his wrath (the divine anger making the enthroned Messiah's enemies the target — Ps 2:12: "kiss the Son, lest he be angry"). The day of wrath is the day of royal judgment.
110.6: יָדִין בַּגּוֹיִם מָלֵא גְוִיּוֹת מָחַץ רֹאשׁ עַל-אֶרֶץ רַבָּה (yādîn baggôyim māllêʾ gĕwiyyôt māḥaṣ rōʾsh ʿal-ʾereṣ rabbāh, "He will bring judgment on the nations, filling the land with the dead; he will crush the ruler across the wide earth!") — yādîn baggôyim: he executes judgment among the nations (the final worldwide judgment — the scope of the Messiah's authority established in Ps 2:8: "I will give you the nations as your inheritance"). Māllêʾ gĕwiyyôt: filling with corpses (gĕwiyyāh: body/corpse — the bodies of the defeated). Māḥaṣ rōʾsh: he shatters the head (the decisive blow to the singular ruler — rōʾsh as the leadership representative — Hab 3:13: "you crushed the head of the house of evil"). The crushing of the head recalls Gen 3:15: "he will crush your head."
110.7: מִנַּחַל בַּדֶּרֶךְ יִשְׁתֶּה עַל-כֵּן יָרִים רֹאשׁ (minnaḥal badderek yishteh ʿal-kên yārîm rōʾsh, "He drinks from the stream along the road, and because of this he will hold his head high!") — the enigmatic final verse of Psalm 110. Minnaḥal badderek yishteh: "from the wadi/stream in the road he drinks" — mid-pursuit, the warrior-king refreshes himself from a stream rather than stopping to make camp. This is the image of relentless pursuit that does not pause for full provision. The parallel is Gideon's 300 who lapped water like dogs and kept moving (Judg 7:5-7 — the select few who did not kneel to drink). ʿAl-kên yārîm rōʾsh: "therefore he will lift up his head" (rûm rōʾsh: to raise the head — the gesture of triumph and vindication — Ps 3:3: "you lift up my head"; Gen 40:13,20 — the butler's head lifted in restoration). The king who drinks from the stream in the way is refreshed for the final victory.

1.1:
אַשְׁרֵי (ʾashrê) — "Blessed" opens the Psalter but the Hebrew is not bārûk (the word for formal blessing, as in "blessed be God"). ʾAshrê is a plural construct meaning "the blessednesses of" or "O the felicity of!" — an exclamation about a person's flourishing state, not a pronouncement of blessing over them. The Beatitudes of Matthew 5 use this form (μακάριος in the LXX).

The three-part movement — not walking in the counsel of the wicked, not standing in the way of sinners, not sitting in the seat of scoffers — traces a progressive entrenchment: the wicked begin as advisers, become companions on the same road, and finally become the community one settles in. The syntax moves from walking to standing to sitting, each stage more settled.
1.2:
תּוֹרָה (tôrāh, "instruction") — the MKT uses "instruction" rather than "law" to capture the Hebrew root yārāh (to shoot/point/direct). Torah is not primarily a legal code but a body of teaching that orients the life. The Psalter opens by depicting the righteous person as one who finds their delight (ḥāpēṣ) in this directing word.

יֶהְגֶּה (yehgeh, "meditates") is from hāgāh, the same word used for the growling of a lion (Isa 31:4) and the cooing of a dove (Isa 38:14). It is not silent, abstract reflection but oral, murmuring recitation — the physical act of speaking the words under the breath "day and night." Ancient reading was always oral.
1.3:
שָׁתוּל (shātûl, "planted") is not random growth but deliberate transplanting — the word implies an intentional hand placing the tree beside the water source. פַּלְגֵי מַיִם (palgê māyim, "streams of water") are not a natural river but irrigation channels — human-made conduits bringing water to where it is placed. The image is of a life deliberately situated near a sustained supply, not accidental proximity.

"Its leaf does not wither" (לֹא-יִבּוֹל עָלֵהוּ) — the contrast is not only with the wicked (v4) but with a tree away from water, where even in summer the leaves hold. The fruitful in its season phrase (פִּרְיוֹ יִתֵּן בְּעִתּוֹ) implies that faithfulness is not always immediately visible but yields at the appointed time.
1.4: כַּמֹּץ (kammōts, "like chaff") — the contrast between the rooted tree (v3) and chaff is total: the tree has roots, location, moisture, and fruit; chaff (môts) is the husk of grain winnowed out, weightless, rootless, and of no nutritional value. The wind does not destroy it so much as reveal what it always was. The judgment of the wicked (v5) is not punishment added from outside but exposure of what is already the case.
1.5: בַּקָּהָל (baqqāhāl, "in the assembly") — qāhāl is the formal covenant assembly, not just any gathering. The wicked will not "stand" (יָקֻמוּ) in it — the verb is the same as "stand" in the court, suggesting judicial standing. They will not have standing in the assembly of the righteous because they have no ground to stand on — chaff has no weight.
1.6: יוֹדֵעַ (yôdēaʿ, "watches over") — the MKT renders "the LORD watches over the way of the righteous," but the Hebrew is literally "the LORD knows the way of the righteous." Knowledge here is relational, not merely informational — the same yāḏaʿ used for intimate knowing (Gen 4:1). The LORD has a knowing relationship with the path the righteous walk. The way of the wicked "will perish" (תֹּאבֵד) — imperfect: it is in the process of perishing, heading toward destruction, even now.

3.1:
מִזְמוֹר לְדָוִד (mizmôr lĕdāwid, "A Psalm of David") — mizmôr is from zāmar (to sing with string accompaniment, to pluck/play), not a generic word for poem. It specifically indicates a song meant for instruments. The superscription situates the psalm in the aftermath of Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 15-18), giving the corporate lament an autobiographical anchor.

רַבּוּ צָרָי (rabbû ṣārāy, "how many are my foes!") — the opening exclamation without a particle: the proliferation of enemies is not calmly observed but blurted out. Ṣārāy (my adversaries) from ṣārar: to press tight, bind, besiege — not casual opponents but those who press hard against him.
3.2: אֵין יְשׁוּעָתָה לּוֹ בֵאלֹהִים (ʾên yĕshûʿātāh llô bēlōhîm, "there is no salvation for him in God") — the enemies' taunt is theological: they claim that even God is not able or willing to rescue. The final particle selāh (סֶלָה) appears here for the first time in the Psalter; its precise meaning is uncertain (a musical interlude? a pause for reflection? from sālal, to lift up), but it marks a break after which the Psalmist turns to address God directly.
3.3: מָגֵן בַּעֲדִי (māgên baʿădî, "a shield around me") — māgên is the small handheld shield (vs. ṣinnāh, the full-body shield of Ps 5:12). God as personal protection surrounding the Psalmist on all sides (baʿădî = "about me/on my behalf"). מֵרִים רֹאשִׁי (mêrîm rōʾshî, "the one who lifts my head") — the participle indicates ongoing action: God is continuously the one lifting the head, a gesture of honor, dignity, and courage in contrast to the enemies' contempt.
3.4: קוֹלִי אֶל-יְהוָה אֶקְרָא (qôlî ʾel-YHWH ʾeqrāʾ, "I cried out to the LORD") — the imperfect (ʾeqrāʾ) here functions as a future-historical narration: "I will call/I called." מֵהַר קָדְשׁוֹ (mêhar qodshô, "from his holy hill") — the answer comes from Zion, the place of the divine dwelling, reinforcing that the LORD who installs the King (Ps 2:6) also hears from that same place.
3.5: שָׁכַבְתִּי וָאִישָׁנָה (shākabetî wāʾîshānāh, "I lay down and slept") — both verbs are in the perfect (completed action): David actually slept. The waw-consecutive pattern (wāʾîshānāh) presents the actions as sequential narrative. In the middle of violent crisis — enemies surrounding him — he slept. הֱקִיצוֹתִי (hĕqîṣôtî, "I awoke") — the act of waking is attributed to divine sustaining: "the LORD sustained me." Sleep and waking alike are acts of trust, not statements of invulnerability.
3.6: לֹא-אִירָא (lōʾ-ʾîrāʾ, "I will not fear") — imperfect negated: ongoing, habitual non-fear. The tens of thousands (רִבְבוֹת, rĕbābôt) surrounding on every side (שָׁתוּ עָלַי סָבִיב) represent the most extreme situation imaginable — total encirclement by vast numbers. The confidence of v5-6 flows from the fact of v4: God heard and answered.
3.7: קוּמָה יְהוָה (qûmāh YHWH, "Rise up, O LORD!") — the imperative qûmāh is an urgent call; the cohortative form adds emotional intensity. The archaic battle cry echoes Num 10:35 (the ark-procession cry: "Rise up, O LORD!"). שִׁבַּרְתָּ (shibbartā, "you shatter") — the perfect of confidence: not a future hope but a declaration of what God does, treating the anticipated rescue as accomplished. The jaw/teeth imagery (לֶחִי, cheek; שִׁנֵּי, teeth) disables the enemy's capacity to wound.
3.8: לַיהוָה הַיְשׁוּעָה (laYHWH hayyĕshûʿāh, "Salvation belongs to the LORD") — the noun with the definite article: the salvation, not a salvation. It is a possession of the LORD, not a human achievement or political strategy. This declaration closes the psalm as a theological assertion: whatever the enemies say (v2), they are wrong. Selāh closes the psalm as it marked the crisis of v2.

4.1:
אֱלֹהֵי צִדְקִי (ʾĕlōhê ṣidqî, "God of my righteousness") — not "my righteous God" (which would be ʾĕlōhay haṣṣaddîq) but "the God who is the source/vindicator of my righteousness." Ṣedeq/ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) here has a forensic sense: vindication, the declaration that one's cause is just. God is appealed to as the one who will adjudicate David's case.

בַּצָּר הִרְחַבְתָּ לִּי (baṣṣār hirḥabtā llî, "you freed me when I was in distress") — literally "in the tight place you enlarged me." Ṣār (distress) and rāḥab (wide/broad) are antonyms: God takes the person from constriction to wide-open space. The spatial metaphor of salvation as expansion is common in the Psalms.
4.2: בְּנֵי אִישׁ (bĕnê ʾîsh, "O men") — literally "sons of man" using ʾîsh (distinguished man, man of rank), not ʾādām (common humanity). The address is to the distinguished and influential, those who should know better. כָּזָב (kāzāb, "lies") — falsehood, deception; the accusers are those who "love what is worthless (rîq) and pursue lies." Rîq appears again from Ps 2:1 — the futile, empty plotting.
4.3: הִפְלָה יְהוָה חָסִיד לוֹ (hiphlāh YHWH ḥāsîd lô, "the LORD has set apart the faithful for himself") — hiphlāh (hiphil of pālāh): to make distinct, to set apart as remarkable or special. The ḥāsîd (faithful/godly one) is the one characterized by ḥesed (covenant loyalty) — not merely religious but relationally faithful to the covenant. The LORD has specially distinguished this person as his own.
4.4:
רִגְזוּ וְאַל-תֶּחֱטָאוּ (rigzû wĕʾal-teḥĕṭāʾû, "Tremble with awe and do not sin") — Paul cites this in Eph 4:26 ("be angry but do not sin"), and the LXX translates it ὀργίζεσθε καὶ μὴ ἁμαρτάνετε. The Hebrew rāgaz primarily means to tremble/shake (used of earthquakes and of the earth's response to the divine presence); its translation as "anger" may import a secondary meaning. The MKT "tremble with awe" recovers the primary sense: physical trembling in response to God's reality.

אִמְרוּ בִלְבַבְכֶם (ʾimrû bilbabkem, "search your hearts") — literally "say in your hearts." The heart (lēbāb) is the organ of thought, will, and intention in Hebrew anthropology — not primarily emotion but cognition and decision. On your beds (מִשְׁכְּבוֹתֵיכֶם), in silence (וְדֹמּוּ, from dāmam: to be still/silent).
4.5: זִבְחוּ זִבְחֵי-צֶדֶק (zibḥû zibḥê-ṣedeq, "Offer right sacrifices") — literally "sacrifices of righteousness," using ṣedeq as a genitive of quality: sacrifices characterized by rightness. Not merely technically correct ritual but offering from a right internal orientation. The instruction parallels Ps 51:17-19 where David learns that God does not desire sacrifice per se but a broken and contrite heart.
4.6: נְסָה-עָלֵינוּ אוֹר פָּנֶיךָ (nĕsāh-ʿālênû ʾôr pānêkā, "Let the light of your face shine on us") — the verb nāsāh (to lift up, to display) and the phrase ʾôr pānêkā (light of your face) both come from the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25: "The LORD make his face shine on you"). The communal prayer invokes the priestly blessing pattern: the turned and shining face of God is the answer to the question "who will show us any good?"
4.7: נָתַתָּה שִׂמְחָה בְלִבִּי (nātattāh śimḥāh bĕlibbî, "You have placed more joy in my heart") — the joy is given (nātan, perfect: completed gift). It is not self-generated. The comparison is explicit: more than "when their grain and wine overflow" (מֵעֵת דְּגָנָם וְתִירוֹשָׁם רָבּוּ). The joy of the harvest (the maximum moment of agrarian celebration) is less than the joy God places internally. The external versus internal contrast is sharp.
4.8: בְּשָׁלוֹם יַחְדָּו אֶשְׁכְּבָה וְאִישָׁן (bĕshālôm yaḥdāw ʾeshkĕbāh wĕʾîshān, "In peace I will lie down and sleep") — shālôm (completeness/peace) as the condition of sleep: rest that is whole, not anxious. Yaḥdāw (together/at once) intensifies: simultaneously, without delay. לְבַדָד (lĕbaddō, "you alone") — not divine plurality but exclusive reliance: "you, you alone, O LORD, cause me to dwell in safety." The security is not social, political, or personal — only divine.

5.1: אֲמָרַי (ʾamāray, "my words") and הֲגִיגִי (hăgîgî, "my groaning") — two distinct levels of speech. ʾImrāh (words, utterances) refers to articulated speech; hăgîg (from hāgāh) is the inarticulate murmuring of Ps 1:2, the inward meditation that may be pre-verbal. Both are addressed to the LORD: the fully-formed words and the groan that underlies them. The superscription "for the flutes" (הַנְּחִילוֹת, hanĕḥîlôt) is uncertain — possibly meaning "for wind instruments" or referring to a tune.
5.2: מַלְכִּי וֵאלֹהַי (malkî wēʾlōhay, "my King and my God") — the double vocative places God in the role of sovereign ruler whom the Psalmist approaches as a subject approaching the throne. The prayer is a royal petition, not merely a private devotion. ʾElōhay (my God) with the first-person suffix: personal relationship, not generic deity.
5.3: בֹּקֶר תִּשְׁמַע קוֹלִי (bōqer tishmaʿ qôlî, "in the morning you hear my voice") — the morning is the time of the daily sacrifice and the moment when petitions were heard. אֲעָרְכָה-לְּךָ (ʾăʿārkāh-llĕkā, "I lay my prayer before you") — ʿārak: to arrange in order, to set in array (as a battle formation or an offering laid out). Prayer as deliberate, ordered presentation — not random petition but careful placement. אֲצַפֶּה (ʾăṣappeh, "and wait") — ṣāpāh: to watch/look out (as a watchman on a wall). Prayer followed by watchful waiting for the answer.
5.4: לֹא אֵל-חָפֵץ רֶשַׁע אָתָּה (lōʾ ʾêl-ḥāpēṣ reshaʿ ʾāttāh, "you are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness") — the logic of prayer: the God addressed is one whose character makes wickedness alien. Ḥāpēṣ (to delight in/desire) — not mere neutral toleration but active delight; God has no such delight in reshaʿ (wickedness — the condition of being fundamentally wrong/twisted). יָגֻר (yāgur, "cannot dwell") from gûr: to sojourn, to lodge as a guest. Evil cannot even temporarily reside with God.
5.5: לֹא-יִתְיַצְּבוּ הוֹלְלִים (lōʾ-yityaṣṣĕbû hôlĕlîm, "the arrogant cannot stand in your sight") — hôlĕlîm (from hālal, root of Hallelujah): here in a negative sense, those who are boastful, bragging fools. They "cannot stand" (yityaṣṣĕbû, hithpael of nāṣab: to take one's stand, to present oneself) — judicial language: they have no standing to appear before this God. שָׂנֵאתָ (śānêʾtā, "you hate") — the perfect of established, settled disposition: not a momentary emotion but a stable orientation of God against evil-workers.
5.6: תְּאַבֵּד דֹּבְרֵי כָזָב (tĕʾabbêd dōbĕrê kāzāb, "you destroy those who speak falsehood") — ʾābad (piel): to cause to perish, to destroy utterly. The specific evil targeted is speech: false testimony, lies. תְּעֵב יְהוָה (tĕʿêb YHWH, "the LORD abhors") — tāʿab: to abhor, to find detestable (used of the ritual abomination of idols and the moral abomination of injustice). The ʾîsh-dāmîm (man of bloodshed) and the mirmāh (deceitful) man are equally objects of divine abhorrence.
5.7: בְּרֹב חַסְדְּךָ (bĕrōb ḥasdĕkā, "through the abundance of your steadfast love") — ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love) is the ground of access. Not merit but ḥesed opens the way in. אֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה (ʾeshtaḥăweh, "I will bow down") — hithpael of shāḥāh: the full prostration of reverence. The Psalmist who enters by grace bows in reverence — access through ḥesed does not reduce the awe of presence.
5.8: הוֹרֵנִי יְהוָה דַּרְכֶּךָ (hôrênî YHWH darkkekā, "lead me, O LORD, in your righteousness") — hiphil imperative of yārāh, the same root as tôrāh: "teach me/point me/direct me." The prayer for divine direction uses the word from which Torah gets its meaning. לְמַעַן שׁוֹרְרָי (lĕmaʿan shôrĕrāy, "because of those who lie in wait for me") — shōrĕr is from the root meaning to watch/observe with hostile intent. Those who spy on the Psalmist create the urgency for divine guiding — without it, he will walk into their traps.
5.9: קִבְרָם גְּרוֹנָם (qibrām gerônām, "their throat is an open grave") — one of the most striking images in the Psalter: the speech coming out of the enemy's throat has the quality of an open tomb — the stench of death and corruption. Qeber (grave) and gārôn (throat/neck) are in a construct chain: their throat is a-grave-of-them, a grave that belongs to and characterizes them. Paul cites this in Romans 3:13 as part of his catena of OT verses demonstrating universal human sinfulness. חֲלַק (ḥālāq, "flatter") — to make smooth; their tongues are smooth/slippery — the polished, dangerous speech of flattery that conceals hostile intent.
5.10: הַאֲשִׁימֵם אֱלֹהִים (haʾăshîmêm ʾĕlōhîm, "Hold them guilty, O God") — hiphil of ʾāsham: to declare guilty, to cause to bear their own guilt. This is an imprecatory element — the Psalmist calls on God to bring justice on the wicked. The grounds are explicit: "for their many rebellions (פִּשְׁעֵיהֶם, pishʿêhem)" and "for they have defied you (מָרוּ בָךְ)." The judgment called for is not personal revenge but divine justice against covenant-breaking rebellion.
5.11: יִשְׂמְחוּ כָל-חוֹסֵי בָךְ (yismĕḥû kol-ḥôsê vāk, "let all who take refuge in you rejoice") — ḥāsāh (to take refuge) is one of the key Psalmic words for trust — running to God for shelter as one runs to a fortress. The call to joy (śāmaḥ) and shout (rānan) is grounded in the security of refuge, not the absence of enemies. תָּסֵךְ עָלֵימוֹ (tāsêk ʿālêmô, "spread your protection") — sākak: to cover over, to provide shelter; the canopy of divine protection.
5.12: כַּצִּנָּה רָצוֹן תַּעְטְרֶנּוּ (kaṣṣinnāh rāṣôn taʿṭĕrennû, "you surround him with favor as with a shield") — ṣinnāh is the large body-shield (contrast māgên of Ps 3:3) — the shield that covers the whole person. Rāṣôn (favor, goodwill, delight) surrounds the righteous as a shield surrounds a soldier: divine favor is the outer layer protecting the one who trusts. The psalm ends by answering its opening: the one who prays in the morning (v3) is the one whom God shields.

6.1: שְׁמִינִית (shĕmînît, "Sheminith") — literally "eighth"; a musical term appearing also in Ps 12:1. Its precise meaning is uncertain — possibly an eight-stringed instrument, a particular musical mode, or a pitch register (Sheminith may indicate a low-pitched performance, contrasted with ʿalāmôt in Ps 46:1). אַל-בְּאַפְּךָ תוֹכִיחֵנִי (ʾal-bĕʾappĕkā tôkîḥênî, "do not rebuke me in your anger") — yākhaḥ (hiphil tôkîaḥ) is a legal term: to argue a case, to convict, to bring a charge. The Psalmist is not asking God to be kind instead of strict, but to not prosecute him in anger. The same plea appears in Ps 38:1. The fear is not of discipline per se but of the full force of divine judicial wrath.
6.2: חָנֵּנִי יְהוָה כִּי אֻמְלַל אָנִי (ḥonnênî YHWH kî ʾumlal ʾānî, "Be gracious to me, O LORD, for I am weak") — ḥānan: to be gracious, to show mercy, to give something not deserved. ʾUmlal (pual of ʾāmal): weak, languishing, withered — a passive form suggesting a state being done to him, not chosen. עֲצָמַי נִבְהָלוּ (ʿăṣāmay nibhālû, "my bones are in agony") — nābhal (niphal): to be dismayed, to be terrified — bones as the deepest physical structure of the person are shaken to the core. Ancient medicine located the deepest vitality in the bones.
6.3: נַפְשִׁי נִבְהֲלָה מְאֹד (napshî nibhălāh mĕʾōd, "my whole being is deeply troubled") — nefesh (soul/life/whole self — often the entire person) is deeply dismayed (mĕʾōd: greatly, intensely). וְאַתָּה יְהוָה עַד-מָתָי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ʿad-mātay, "But you, O LORD — how long?") — the question breaks off: the sentence is not finished. "How long?" without a verb — the most compressed form of lament, the half-spoken cry. The Hebrew allows the incompleteness to stand as the most natural expression of extremity.
6.4: שׁוּבָה יְהוָה חַלְּצָה נַפְשִׁי (shûbāh YHWH ḥallĕṣāh napshî, "Turn back, O LORD, rescue my life") — shûb (return/turn) is used of God turning his face or attention toward the suppliant. Ḥālaṣ (piel): to strip off, to deliver — the image is of being stripped free from a tight place (related to the root in v5:1 "tight/distress"). לְמַעַן חַסְדֶּךָ (lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā, "for the sake of your steadfast love") — the appeal to ḥesed as the motive for rescue: God's covenant loyalty demands he act on behalf of his covenant partner.
6.5: כִּי אֵין בַּמָּוֶת זִכְרֶךָ (kî ʾên bammāwet zikrekkā, "for among the dead no one remembers you") — a theologically significant statement of pre-resurrection anthropology: in Sheol (בִּשְׁאוֹל), the dead cannot give thanks or praise. The Psalmist uses the logic of God's interest in ongoing worship to argue for rescue: if he dies, God loses a worshiper. This is not a bargaining chip but a recognition that praise belongs to life, not death — a reality the resurrection overturns (see the connections in 1 Cor 15:20).
6.6: יָגַעְתִּי בְאַנְחָתִי (yāgaʿtî bĕʾanḥātî, "I am exhausted with my moaning") — yāgaʿ: weary, fatigued; the moaning itself is exhausting. אַשְׂחֶה בְכָל-לַיְלָה מִטָּתִי (ʾaśḥeh bĕkol-layĕlāh miṭṭātî, "every night I drench my bed") — śāḥāh: to float, to swim; the bed is "flooded" with tears. The hyperbole communicates total saturation — no part of sleep unmarked by weeping.
6.7: עָשְׁשָׁה מִכַּעַס עֵינִי (ʿāshĕshāh mikkacʿas ʿênî, "My eye has grown dim from grief") — ʿāshash (poel): to decay, to waste away — the eyes literally deteriorating from prolonged weeping and grief. Kaʿas (grief, vexation, anger): the word can cover both sorrow and the anger of frustration. The physical deterioration (eyes wasting) is a sign of the total impact of the crisis on the body.
6.8: סוּרוּ מִמֶּנִּי כָּל-פֹּעֲלֵי אָוֶן (sûrû mimmennî kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen, "Depart from me, all you who do evil") — the sudden turn from weeping (v1-7) to command (v8-10). The Psalmist now dismisses the wicked as those whose presence is already rendered impossible by the divine response. The confidence of v8 is grounded in v8b: שָׁמַע יְהוָה קוֹל בִּכְיִי (shāmaʿ YHWH qôl bikeyî) — "the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping." The weeping was not private; it was heard.
6.9: שָׁמַע יְהוָה תְּחִנָּתִי (shāmaʿ YHWH tĕḥinnātî, "The LORD has heard my supplication") — tĕḥinnāh: a plea for grace/mercy, the verbal noun from ḥānan (be gracious). The LORD has heard and now receives (יִקַּח, yiqqaḥ, imperfect of certainty): the prayer is accepted. The shift from petition (v1-7) to announcement (v8-10) is abrupt — the text does not explain what changed, only that it did. The Psalmist perceives the answer and declares it.
6.10: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיִבָּהֲלוּ מְאֹד (yêbōshû wĕyibbāhălû mĕʾōd, "shall be ashamed and greatly disturbed") — bûsh (to be ashamed, to be disappointed in one's expectations) and bāhal (to be terrified, dismayed — the same word used of the Psalmist's bones in v2). The enemies will experience what the Psalmist experienced — reversal. יָשֻׁבוּ יֵבֹשׁוּ רָגַע (yāshûbû yêbōshû rāgaʿ, "they shall turn back and be disgraced in an instant") — rāgaʿ: suddenly, in a moment — the swift judgment of the confident Psalmist who has heard God's answer.

7.1:
שִׁגָּיוֹן (shiggāyôn) — a transliterated genre term appearing only here (the plural shigionoth in Hab 3:1). The derivation is uncertain: possibly from shāgāh (to wander, err, reel) suggesting an irregular, impassioned lyric; or from a cognate Akkadian root for lamentation. The LXX renders it "Psalm." Whatever the genre, it signals a distinctive, passionate form of expression.

The superscription references "Cush the Benjaminite" — an otherwise unknown figure who apparently slandered David; this personal historical hook gives the corporate lament a specific occasion.
7.2: פֶּן-יִטְרֹף כְּאַרְיֵה נַפְשִׁי (pen-yiṭrōp kĕʾaryēh napshî, "lest they tear my soul apart like a lion") — ṭārap: to tear, to rend (as a lion tears its prey); the verb is used of predatory animals. Nefesh (soul/life/person) as the target — the total person at risk of being torn apart. וְאֵין מַצִּיל (wĕʾên maṣṣîl, "with no one to rescue") — the hiphil participle of nāṣal: one who snatches away to safety. The situation is complete vulnerability with no human helper.
7.3: יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי אִם-עָשִׂיתִי זֹאת (YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾim-ʿāśîtî zōʾt, "O LORD my God, if I have done this") — the conditional oath structure (v3-5) is a form of self-imprecation: "if I am guilty, then let the curse fall on me." The Psalmist invites divine judgment as proof of innocence — a legal procedure akin to a trial by ordeal, but directed to God rather than a human tribunal.
7.4: אִם-גָּמַלְתִּי שׁוֹלְמִי רָע (ʾim-gāmaltî shôlmî rāʿ, "if I have repaid evil to one who was at peace with me") — gāmal: to deal out, to repay; the word also means to wean (as weaning is completing a stage of nurturing). Shôlmî: the one who is at peace/shalom with me, one with whom I have a covenant of friendship. The specific denial is of betrayal of a peaceful relationship — directly relevant if the accusation involves treachery.
7.5: יִרְדֹּף אוֹיֵב נַפְשִׁי (yirdōp ʾôyēb napshî, "let the enemy pursue and overtake me") — the self-imprecation is fully stated: if guilty, let the worst happen. יִרְמֹס לָאָרֶץ חַיָּתִי (yirmōs lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî, "trample my life into the ground") — rāmas: to trample down; ḥayyātî (my life/living self). כְּבוֹדִי לֶעָפָר יַשְׁכֵּן (kĕbôdî leʿāpār yashkên, "lay my honor in the dust") — kābôd (glory/honor/weight) reduced to ʿāpār (dust/dirt): the reversal of honor to shame. Selāh marks the weight of the oath.
7.6: קוּמָה יְהוָה בְּאַפֶּךָ (qûmāh YHWH bĕʾappekā, "Rise up, O LORD, in your anger") — the same call as Ps 3:7 but now invoked in the context of established innocence (vv3-5): because David is innocent, the enemy's rage is rebellion against the LORD's order. עוּרָה אֵלַי (ʿûrāh ʾêlay, "rouse yourself") — ʿûr: to awake from sleep, to bestir oneself (used of the divine "sleeping" in Ps 44:23). מִשְׁפָּט צִוִּיתָ (mishpāṭ ṣiwwîtāh, "you have decreed judgment") — the judgment is already established by divine command; God is called to execute what he has already decreed.
7.7: וַעֲדַת לְאֻמִּים (waʿădat lĕʾummîm, "the assembly of the peoples") — the image expands from David's personal enemies to a cosmic assize: all peoples gathered around the enthroned judge. עֲלֶיהָ לַמָּרוֹם שׁוּבָה (ʿălêhā lammārôm shûbāh, "take your seat on high above them") — God is called to ascend to the high seat of judgment above the assembly, not a retreat from the scene but an elevation to the judge's bench. The imagery is of a divine court session.
7.8: יְהוָה יָדִין עַמִּים (YHWH yādîn ʿammîm, "The LORD judges the peoples") — the imperfect here is a declaration of God's characteristic activity: this is what the LORD does, continually. שָׁפְטֵנִי יְהוָה כְּצִדְקִי (shāpĕṭênî YHWH kĕṣidqî, "vindicate me, O LORD, according to my righteousness") — the appeal to ṣedeq (righteousness) in the forensic sense: judge my case on the merits of my actual conduct, not the accusations of enemies. Ûtĕmimmî (my integrity/wholeness) — tōm: completeness, uprightness without a crack or hidden defect.
7.9: בֹּחֵן לִבּוֹת וּכְלָיוֹת (bōḥên libbôt ûkĕlāyôt, "the righteous God searches hearts and minds") — bāḥan: to test, assay (as metal is tested); the participle expresses characteristic ongoing action. Libbôt (hearts — plural of lēb) = the organ of thought, will, intention; kĕlāyôt (kidneys) = the deepest seat of emotion and secret purpose. Together they indicate the total interior life — nothing hidden from this divine search. Rev 2:23 applies this directly to Christ: "I am he who searches minds and hearts."
7.10: מָגִנִּי עַל-אֱלֹהִים (māginnî ʿal-ʾĕlōhîm, "My shield is God, the Most High") — māgên (small handheld shield, as in Ps 3:3) with the proposition ʿal (upon/above/through): the shield that is upon me comes from God. מוֹשִׁיעַ יִשְׁרֵי-לֵב (môshîaʿ yishĕrê-lēb, "who saves those who are upright in heart") — yāshar: straight, right; those whose heart is without crookedness. The divine savior (môshîaʿ) is the one who rescues the innocent, consistent with the appeal of vv3-8.
7.11: אֵל שׁוֹפֵט צַדִּיק (ʾêl shôpêṭ ṣaddîq, "God is a righteous judge") — three predicates: ʾêl (God/El), shôpêṭ (participle: continuously judging), ṣaddîq (righteous). The participle is important: not a future judgment only but a present, ongoing activity. וְאֵל זֹעֵם בְּכָל-יוֹם (wĕʾêl zōʿêm bĕkol-yôm, "a God who expresses indignation every day") — zāʿam: to be indignant, to be enraged; the participle again indicates habitual, ongoing activity. Divine anger is not periodic but continuous in response to ongoing wickedness — not stored up for an eventual reckoning but always present.
7.12: אִם-לֹא יָשׁוּב חַרְבּוֹ יִלְטוֹשׁ (ʾim-lōʾ yāshûb ḥarbô yilṭôsh, "If a man does not repent, God will sharpen his sword") — the "if" is conditional on continued unrepentance: shûb (to turn/repent) is the condition. Lāṭash: to sharpen, to whet (as a sword is sharpened on a stone). God is depicted as an armed judge preparing weapons — the imagery of divine warrior preparing for battle against the unrepentant. קַשְׁתּוֹ דָּרַךְ וַיְכוֹנְנֶהָ — "bent his bow and strung it" — two completed actions (perfect): the bow is already bent and made ready.
7.13: לוֹ הֵכִין כְּלֵי-מָוֶת (lô hêkîn kĕlê-māwet, "He has prepared deadly weapons against the persecutors") — hêkîn (hiphil of kûn): to establish, to make firm/ready. The "weapons of death" are prepared specifically for the persecutors. חִצָּיו לַדֹּלְקִים יִפְעַל (ḥiṣṣāyw laddōlĕqîm yipʿal, "his arrows will be fiery shafts") — dālaq: to pursue hotly, to inflame; those who hotly pursue the Psalmist become the targets of flaming arrows. The imagery picks up the ancient Canaanite war-god iconography while applying it to the LORD's justice.
7.14: הִנֵּה יְחַבֶּל-אָוֶן (hinnēh yĕḥabbel-ʾāwen, "Look: the wicked person conceives evil") — three birth-metaphors for the production of evil: conceiving (yĕḥabbel, piel of ḥābal: to be in birth-pangs, to conceive), becoming pregnant (הָרָה, hārāh: pregnant), giving birth (וְיָלַד, wĕyālad: to bear/give birth). Evil is not accidental but the product of a deliberate generative process — it is what the wicked person produces when they are most fully themselves.
7.15: בּוֹר כָּרָה וַיַּחְפְּרֵהוּ (bôr kārāh wayyaḥpĕrêhû, "He dug a pit, made it deep") — two verbs for the excavation work: kārāh (to dig) and ḥāpar (to dig/search): he dug and then deepened it. The pit is the trap set for another — but וַיִּפֹּל בְּשַׁחַת יִפְעָל (wayyippōl bĕshaḥat yipʿal): "and fell into the very pit he made." The self-entrapment is the theme of vv15-16 — the retributive principle built into the moral order: evil returns on its maker.
7.16: יָשׁוּב עֲמָלוֹ בְרֹאשׁוֹ (yāshûb ʿămālô bĕrōʾshô, "His mischief comes back on his own head") — ʿāmāl (trouble, mischief, toil — the laborious effort put into evil); it "returns" (yāshûb) to the head — the top of the one who worked it. עַל-קָדְקֳדוֹ חֲמָסוֹ יֵרֵד (ʿal-qodqodô ḥāmāsô yêrêd, "his violence falls down on his own skull") — ḥāmās (violence) descending on the qodqōd (skull/crown of the head). The image is of the violence thrown upward returning on its thrower.
7.17: אוֹדֶה יְהוָה כְּצִדְקוֹ (ʾôdeh YHWH kĕṣidqô, "I will give thanks to the LORD for his righteousness") — the psalm closes where it began: God's righteousness. The same ṣedeq appealed to in v8 (vindicate me according to my righteousness) is now the content of praise: God's righteousness is itself the reason for thanksgiving. אֲזַמְּרָה שֵׁם-יְהוָה עֶלְיוֹן (ʾăzammĕrāh shêm-YHWH ʿelyôn) — "sing praise to the name of the LORD Most High" — ʿelyôn (Most High, the Canaanite divine title applied to the LORD) completes the psalm with the highest divine title, appropriate for the cosmic judge of vv7-8.

9.1: ʾôdeh YHWH bĕkol-libbî (ʾōḏeh YHWH bĕḵol-libbî): 'I will praise the LORD with my whole heart.' The qal imperfect ʾôdeh (from yādâ, 'to praise/confess') expresses votive resolve. The superscription assigns the psalm to David and connects it to ʿal-mût labbēn — a phrase of uncertain meaning (possibly 'death of a son' or a melody title). The pairing of Psalms 9–10 as a single incomplete acrostic (the two share contiguous Hebrew letters ʾālep–kāp) suggests original unity; scribal or liturgical tradition divided them.
9.2: ʾeśmĕḥâ wĕʾeʿlōs bĕkā (ʾeśmĕḥâ wĕʾeʿlōs bĕḵā): 'I will be glad and exult in you.' Two cohortative forms — śāmaḥ (gladness) and ʿālas (exulting/leaping for joy) — express the emotional fullness of praise. The preposition bĕ- ('in you') grounds the joy in YHWH's person, not merely his acts. The phrase ʾăzammĕrâ šimkā 'elyôn, 'I will sing praises to your name, O Most High,' introduces the divine title ʿelyôn (Most High), associated with Melchizedek's blessing in Gen 14:18-20 and used especially in Psalms for YHWH's universal sovereignty.
9.3: bĕšûb ʾôyĕbay ʾāḥôr (bĕšûḇ ʾōyĕḇay ʾāḥôr): 'when my enemies turn back.' The infinitive construct bĕšûb introduces a temporal clause: the psalmist's praise is grounded in past experience of enemies' flight. The root kāšal ('stumble/fall') describes their collapse; yōbĕdû ('they perished') is a niphal perfect — passive voice capturing finality. The presence of YHWH is the cause: mippānêkā ('from before your face'), a recurring Psalter motif where the divine countenance both repels enemies and sustains the righteous.
9.4: kî ʿāśîtā mišpāṭî wĕdînî (kî ʿāśîṯā mišpāṭî wĕḏînî): 'for you have maintained my judgment and my cause.' The paired nouns mišpāṭ and dîn both designate legal vindication; together they constitute a hendiadys — 'my just cause.' The verb ʿāśâ is a perfect of completed action: YHWH has already acted as judge. The image of yāšabtā lĕkissēʾ, 'you sat down to the throne,' pictures YHWH enthroned as cosmic judge — language that anticipates the heavenly court scenes of Dan 7 and Rev 4–5.
9.5: gāʿartā gôyim ʾibbadtā rāšāʿ (gāʿartā gôyim ʾibbadtā rāšāʿ): 'you have rebuked the nations, destroyed the wicked.' The verb gāʿar ('rebuke with authority') is the same verb used in Ps 18:15 and Ps 106:9 for YHWH's rebuke of sea and nations — a term of theophanic power. Paired with ʾibbadtā (piel perfect of ʾābad, 'to cause to perish'), the two verbs are a tight synonymous parallel: rebuke leads to destruction. The blotting of the wicked man's name — šĕmām māḥîtā — echoes the covenant curse of obliterating remembrance (cf. Deut 9:14).
9.6: ḥorbôt tammû lānĕṣaḥ (ḥorbôṯ tammû lānĕṣaḥ): 'their ruins have come to a perpetual end.' The noun ḥorbôt (ruins, desolations) is plural and emphatic: entire cities reduced to waste. The phrase tammû lānĕṣaḥ — 'ended forever' — uses lānĕṣaḥ, a word meaning 'to perpetuity/to victory,' which in Psalms often signals eschatological finality. The enemy is addressed in second person in the Hebrew: 'O enemy — finished, ruins forever!' This apostrophe sharpens the rhetorical force of the vindication.
9.7: YHWH lĕʿôlām yēšēb (YHWH lĕʿôlām yēšēḇ): 'the LORD sits enthroned forever.' Against the transience of the wicked (v.6), YHWH's eternal session on the throne provides the structural antithesis. The verb yāšab ('sit/be enthroned') is used for royal stability; konnēn lammišpāṭ kissēʾô, 'he has established his throne for justice,' adds that the throne's purpose is mišpāṭ — not raw power but righteous governance. The phrase anticipates Ps 93–99, the enthronement psalms.
9.8: wĕhûʾ yišpōṭ-tēbēl bĕṣedeq (wĕhûʾ yišpōṭ-tēḇēl bĕṣeḏeq): 'and he will judge the world in righteousness.' The noun tēbēl ('world, inhabited earth') expands the scope beyond Israel to the entire populated world. The prepositional pair bĕṣedeq and bĕmêšārîm ('in righteousness / in uprightness') function as ethical instruments: YHWH's judgment is characterized by covenant fidelity and impartial equity. This verse is a seed of the NT vision of Christ judging the world 'in righteousness' (Acts 17:31).
9.9: wîhî YHWH miśgāb laddāk (waYHWH miśgāḇ laddāḵ): 'and the LORD is a high refuge for the oppressed.' The noun miśgāb ('high fortress, stronghold') comes from śāgab ('to be high/inaccessible'); YHWH himself is the elevated place to which the poor flee. The rare noun dāk ('crushed one, oppressed') points to those who have been ground down by the wicked — the same social group the preceding verses described the wicked exploiting. The verb yihyeh (imperfect) indicates ongoing, present-tense provision: not a one-time rescue but a permanent refuge.
9.10: wĕyibṭĕḥû bĕkā yôdĕʿê šĕmekā (wĕyiḇṭĕḥû ḇĕḵā yôḏĕʿê šĕmeḵā): 'and those who know your name trust in you.' The verb bāṭaḥ ('trust, rely upon') in the qal expresses confident, settled reliance — not merely intellectual belief. The qualifier yôdĕʿê šĕmekā, 'those who know your name,' is covenant language: knowing the name in the OT means experiencing YHWH's revealed character (Exod 3:14-15; 6:3). The result: kî lōʾ-ʿāzabtā dōrĕšêkā YHWH, 'for you have not forsaken those who seek you' — a theological anchor for unanswered prayer: seeking is never in vain.
9.11: zammĕrû laYHWH yōšēb ṣiyyôn (zammĕrû laYHWH yōšēḇ ṣiyyôn): 'sing praises to the LORD who dwells in Zion.' The piel imperative zammĕrû ('make music, sing') summons communal praise. The designation yōšēb ṣiyyôn, 'the one who dwells in Zion,' grounds YHWH's cosmic throne (v.7) in a specific earthly locus — Zion/Jerusalem as the place where heaven and earth meet. The second half, haggîdû bāʿammîm ʿălîlôtāyw, 'declare his deeds among the peoples,' extends the witness of praise to the nations — a missionary impulse within the Psalter.
9.12: kî dōrēš dāmîm ʾôtām zākar (kî dōrēš dāmîm ʾôṯām zāḵar): 'for he who avenges blood remembers them.' The participle dōrēš dāmîm ('one who investigates/requires blood') draws on the legal language of blood-vengeance: YHWH as the one who holds killers accountable. The phrase lōʾ šākaḥ ṣaʿaqat ʿănāwîm, 'he does not forget the cry of the afflicted,' creates an inclusio with v.9: the oppressed who flee to YHWH are never outside his attention. The noun ʿănāwîm ('afflicted, poor, humble') is a key Psalter term for those who trust wholly in YHWH (cf. Ps 22:26; 37:11; Matt 5:5).
9.13: ḥonnēnî YHWH rĕʾēh ʿonî mēśonĕʾay (ḥonnēnî YHWH rĕʾēh ʿonî mēśônĕʾāy): 'Be gracious to me, O LORD; see my affliction by those who hate me.' The qal imperative ḥonnēnî invokes the ḥānan root (grace, favor), used of the unmerited favor of a superior to an inferior. The verb rĕʾēh ('see/observe') is a plea for divine attention — the counterpart to lōʾ šākaḥ in v.12. The phrase mǎrômmî miššaʿărê māwet, 'you who lift me from the gates of death,' introduces the gates of death (Sheol's threshold) as the extremity from which YHWH rescues.
9.14: lĕmaʿan ʾăsappĕrâ kol-tĕhillātekā (lĕmaʿan ʾăsappĕrâ ḵol-tĕhillāṯeḵā): 'that I may declare all your praise.' The purpose clause with the cohortative ʾăsappĕrâ ('I will tell/recount') shows that deliverance is not merely personal relief but has a doxological telos — it creates testimony. The setting is bĕšaʿărê bat-ṣiyyôn, 'in the gates of the daughter of Zion,' i.e., at the city gate where public life and worship converged. The pledge to praise publicly in the assembly is a votive form common in Psalter laments (cf. Ps 22:22, 25).
9.15: ṭāb'û gôyim bĕšaḥat ʿāśû (ṭāḇĕʿû gôyim bĕšaḥaṯ ʿāśû): 'the nations have sunk in the pit they made.' The verb ṭābaʿ ('sink, be submerged') is used of sinking in mud or deep waters; applied to nations, it vivifies their sudden collapse. The theological principle is lex talionis retributive justice: bāreŝet zû ṭāmānû nilkĕdâ raglām, 'in the net they hid, their own foot has been caught.' This principle — the wicked falling into their own traps — appears throughout the Psalter (cf. Ps 7:15-16; 35:8) and grounds confidence in ultimate justice.
9.16: nôdaʿ YHWH mišpāṭ ʿāśâ (nôḏaʿ YHWH mišpāṭ ʿāśâ): 'the LORD has made himself known; he has executed judgment.' The niphal nôdaʿ ('he was made known') means that YHWH's nature is revealed through his acts of judgment — theodicy is also theology. The phrase bĕpōʿal yādāyw nôqēš rāšāʿ, 'the wicked is snared in the work of his own hands,' echoes v.15: the trap-maker becomes the trapped. The enigmatic annotation Higgaion Selah may indicate a meditative musical interlude, inviting reflection on this reversal.
9.17: yāšûbû rĕšāʿîm lišĕʾôl (yāšûḇû rĕšāʿîm lišĕʾôl): 'the wicked shall return to Sheol.' The verb šûb ('return, go back') implies that Sheol is the wicked's natural origin or destination — dust to dust, darkness to darkness. The phrase kol-gôyim šĕkēḥê ʾĕlōhîm, 'all the nations that forget God,' extends the verdict beyond Israel to gentile nations who suppress the knowledge of God (cf. Rom 1:21). This verse forms the negative counterpart to v.10 — those who know the name trust; those who forget God perish.
9.18: kî lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššākēaḥ ʾebyôn (kî lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššāḵēaḥ ʾeḇyôn): 'for the needy shall not always be forgotten.' The word ʾebyôn ('poor, destitute') denotes material poverty and, in the Psalter's theology, those who depend entirely on YHWH for survival. The phrase lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššākēaḥ is emphatically negated: the forgetting is not permanent. The second half, tiqwat ʿănāwîm tōʾbad lāʿad, 'the hope of the afflicted shall not perish forever,' uses ʾeḇed ('perish') — the same verb used for the wicked's destruction in v.3 — now negated for the righteous. Hope is imperishable.
9.19: qûmâ YHWH ʾal-yĕʿōz ʾĕnôš (qûmâ YHWH ʾal-yĕʿōz ʾĕnôš): 'Arise, O LORD; let not man prevail.' The imperative qûmâ ('arise!') is a liturgical summons to divine action, common in Psalter prayers (cf. Ps 7:6; 44:26; 74:22). The noun ʾĕnôš ('mortal man' — from the root implying frailty/sickness) emphasizes human finitude: it is absurd for frail humans to triumph over the eternal God. The phrase yiššāpĕṭû gôyim ʿal-pānêkā, 'let the nations be judged before you,' repeats the court language of v.4-8 — the psalm ends where it began, in the courtroom of heaven.
9.20: šîtâ YHWH môrâ lāhem (šîṯâ YHWH môrâ lāhem): 'Put them in fear, O LORD.' The noun môrâ (from yārēʾ, 'to fear') connotes the dread that falls on enemies when God acts. The phrase yēdĕʿû gôyim ʾĕnôš hēmmâ, 'let the nations know that they are but men,' is the psalm's culminating reversal: those who thought themselves lords of history (v.4-6) are exposed as ʾĕnôš — mortal, contingent, creature. The Selah punctuates the closing appeal for all humanity to recognize creatureliness before the creator.

10.1: lāmmâ YHWH taʿămōd bĕrāḥôq (lāmmâ YHWH taʿămōḏ bĕrāḥôq): 'Why, O LORD, do you stand far off?' The acrostic continues from Psalm 9 (together covering ʾālep through kāp), though Psalm 10 lacks an explicit superscription in Hebrew — further evidence of original unity. The question lāmmâ ('why?') opens a lament of divine hiddenness: God's standing bĕrāḥôq ('at a distance') and hiding bĕʿittôt bĕṣārâ ('in times of trouble') contradicts the refuge he is declared to be in 9:9. The tension is deliberate: the psalmist prays out of experienced abandonment toward known theology.
10.2: bĕgaʾăwat rāšāʿ yiddalaqu ʿānî (bĕḡaʾăwaṯ rāšāʿ yiddalaqu ʿānî): 'in arrogance the wicked pursues the poor.' The verb dālaq ('pursue hotly, burn after') describes predatory intensity. The noun gaʾăwâ ('pride, arrogance') is the root evil: the wicked man's self-elevation drives his exploitation. The passive/reflexive prayer yitkĕṭĕšû bĕmaḥšĕbôt zû ḥāšābû, 'let them be caught in the schemes they have devised,' echoes the lex talionis principle established in ch.9 — the trap-set becomes the trap-caught.
10.3: kî hillēl rāšāʿ ʿal-taʾăwat napšô (kî hillēl rāšāʿ ʿal-taʾăwaṯ napšô): 'for the wicked praises himself for the desire of his soul.' The piel hillēl ('to praise, boast') — root of hallĕlûyāh — is here inverted: the wicked praises himself rather than YHWH, treating his own desires as ultimate.
