The phrase ûbōṣēaʿ bērēk niʾēṣ YHWH, 'and the one who grasps for gain blesses himself but despises the LORD,' displays the theological corruption of covetousness: greed simultaneously demands self-blessing and despises God. The noun bōṣēaʿ ('one who cuts off/gains by violence') implies criminal acquisition.
10.4: rāšāʿ kĕgōbah ʾappô bal-yidrōš (rāšāʿ kĕḡōḇah ʾappô ḇal-yiḏrōš): 'the wicked, in his haughtiness, does not seek.' The phrase ʾên ʾĕlōhîm kol-mĕzimmôtāyw, 'there is no God in all his schemes,' is not theoretical atheism but practical atheism: the wicked operates as though God neither sees nor judges. The noun mĕzimmôt ('plans, schemes') is morally neutral in itself but here describes a mind from which God has been systematically excluded. Paul draws on vv.7 and 14 (LXX: Pss 9–10 were one psalm in LXX) in his catena of universal sinfulness in Rom 3:10-18.
10.5: yāḥîlû dĕrākāyw bĕkol-ʿēt (yāḥîlû dĕrāḵāyw bĕḵol-ʿēṯ): 'his ways prosper at all times.' The hiphil yāḥîlû (from ḥûl, 'twist/writhe/succeed') may mean 'his ways are firm/stable.' The apparent prosperity of the wicked is the classic theodicy problem (cf. Ps 73). The phrase mārôm mišpāṭêkā miggĕdô, 'your judgments are on high, out of his sight,' means the wicked cannot perceive divine governance — his immediate success blinds him. He puffs contempt at his enemies: yāpîaḥ bāhem, 'he blows at them,' an image of arrogant dismissal.
10.6: ʾāmar bĕlibbô bal-ʾemmôṭ (ʾāmar bĕlibbô ḇal-ʾemôṭ): 'he said in his heart, 'I shall not be moved.'' The inner speech ʾāmar bĕlibbô ('he said in his heart') introduces the wicked man's self-deception. The boast bal-ʾemmôṭ lĕdōr wādōr ʾăšer lōʾ bĕrāʿ, 'I shall not be moved; throughout all generations I shall not meet adversity,' is a counterfeit of YHWH's own eternal stability (9:7). The wicked imitates divine permanence — precisely the Edenic temptation (Gen 3:5: 'you will be like God').
10.7: ʾālâ pîhû māʾēn ûmirmâ (ʾālâh pîhû māʾēn ûmirmâ): 'his mouth is full of cursing and deceit and oppression.' The noun ʾālâ ('oath, curse') refers to invoking divine curses against others; mirmâ is 'treachery/deceit'; tōk is 'oppression/fraud.' Three words capture the weaponization of speech. Paul cites a version of this verse in Rom 3:14 ('their mouth is full of cursing and bitterness') as evidence of universal sinfulness — a NT appropriation of what the psalmist observed in a specific oppressor to diagnose a universal human condition.
10.8: yēšēb bĕmaʾărab ḥăṣērîm (yēšēḇ bĕmaʾĂraḇ ḥăṣērîm): 'he sits in ambush in the villages.' The noun maʾărab ('ambush') describes premeditated predation; ḥăṣēr ('village, courtyard') is where the vulnerable live. The imagery intensifies: the wicked hides bĕmistārîm ('in hiding places') to murder nāqî, 'the innocent.' His eyes secretly watch (bĕmistārîm yeṣappĕnû ʿênāyw) for the helpless — surveillance used for predation rather than protection.
10.9: yeʾĕrōb bammistār kĕʾaryēh bĕsukkô (yeʾĕrōḇ ḇammistār kĕʾaryēh bĕsukkô): 'he lies in wait in secret like a lion in its den.' The lion simile (kĕʾaryēh) is the Psalter's stock image for a predator who stalks rather than fights openly. The victim is ʿānî ('the poor/afflicted'), the same social group the psalm defends. The verb yimšōk ('he drags/draws away') — used of dragging a net catch — captures the violent, irreversible nature of the oppression. The poor man is prey.
10.10: yiddakkeh yāšōaḥ wĕnāpal (yiddakkeh yāšōaḥ wĕnāpal): 'he crouches, he stoops, and the poor man falls.' The sequence of three verbs — yiddakkeh (crushed down), yāšōaḥ (stoops), nāpal (falls) — creates a cinematic slow-motion of the predator's approach and the victim's collapse. The phrase bĕḥēlĕkāyw ḥêlekā ʿăniyyîm, 'by his mighty ones the poor are crushed,' suggests the wicked man's network of power — he does not act alone but commands enforcers.
10.11: ʾāmar bĕlibbô šākaḥ ʾēl (ʾāmar bĕlibbô šākaḥ ʾēl): 'he says in his heart, 'God has forgotten.'' The second inner monologue (cf. v.6) deepens the portrait of practical atheism: hissātîr pānāyw lōʾ-rāʾâ lānĕṣaḥ, 'he has hidden his face, he will never see.' The triple claim — God forgets, God hides, God never sees — mirrors an inversion of the divine assurances in Ps 9:12-13 (God remembers, God sees). The psalmist is not endorsing this view; he quotes the wicked to expose their delusion before calling on God to disprove it.
10.12: qûmâ YHWH ʾēl nĕśēʾ yādekā (qûmâ YHWH ʾēl nĕśēʾ yāḏeḵā): 'Arise, O LORD! O God, lift up your hand!' The liturgical imperative qûmâ returns from 9:19 — the lament section ends and the petition begins. The phrase ʾal-tiškaḥ ʿănāwîm, 'do not forget the afflicted,' directly refutes the wicked man's claim in v.11 (God has forgotten). The psalmist's prayer strategically uses the enemy's own language against him: if the wicked says 'God forgets,' the psalmist replies by praying 'do not forget.'
10.13: ʿal-mah niʾēṣ rāšāʿ ʾĕlōhîm (ʿal-mah niʾēṣ rāšāʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'Why does the wicked despise God?' The niph'al niʾēṣ ('despise, treat with contempt') addresses divine honor at stake: YHWH's failure to act is the wicked man's theodicy in reverse — his impunity 'proves' God doesn't care. The wicked claims lōʾ tidrōš, 'you will not call to account,' treating divine patience as permanent indifference. The psalmist's complaint is partly apologetic: YHWH's honor requires that he vindicate the poor and disprove the wicked's theology.
10.14: rāʾîtâ kî-ʾattâ ʿāmāl wākaʿas tabbîṭ (rāʾîṯā ḵî-ʾattâ ʿāmāl wāḵaʿas tabbîṭ): 'You do see, for you observe trouble and vexation.' Against the wicked man's claim that God neither sees nor acts (v.11), the psalmist asserts rāʾîtâ — 'you have seen.' The infinitive lātet bĕyādekā, 'to give into your hand,' frames justice as a divine responsibility: the oppressed 'commits himself' (yaʿăzōb) to YHWH, and the fatherless (yātôm) finds his helper. The polarity of blindness (v.11) and sight (v.14) structures the entire psalm's theodicy.
10.15: šĕbōr zĕrôaʿ rāšāʿ wārāʿ (šĕḇôr zĕrôaʿ rāšāʿ wārāʿ): 'Break the arm of the wicked and evildoer.' The qal imperative šĕbōr ('break!') calls for decisive intervention: the arm (zĕrôaʿ) represents power — to break it is to disarm the oppressor. The phrase tidrōš rišʿô bĕl-timṣāʾ, 'seek out his wickedness until you find none,' envisions complete justice — not merely punishment but the full unraveling of wickedness until nothing remains. This is eschatological hope within an historical prayer.
10.16: YHWH melek ʿôlām wāʿed (YHWH melek ʿôlām wāʿeḏ): 'The LORD is king forever and ever.' The declaration of divine kingship (melek) forms the climax after the appeals: YHWH answers not as a distant judge but as a king whose rule is ʿôlām wāʿed ('age upon age'). The noun gôyim ('nations') appears again — āḇĕdû gôyim mēʾarṣô, 'the nations have perished from his land' — but now as past-tense historical fulfillment, not just eschatological hope. YHWH's land-claim has expelled the wicked.
10.17: tawat ʿănāwîm šāmaʿtâ YHWH (taʾăwaṯ ʿănāwîm šāmaʿtâ YHWH): 'The desire of the afflicted you have heard, O LORD.' The perfect šāmaʿtâ is a perfect of confidence: the psalmist speaks of future hearing as certain fact. The verb tākîn libbām ('you will establish their heart') suggests that divine attention strengthens the inner resolve of the afflicted — hearing transforms not just circumstances but persons. The outcome taqšîb ʾoznekā, 'you will incline your ear,' emphasizes receptive divine posture.
10.18: lišpōṭ yātôm wādāk (lišpōṭ yātôm wādāk): 'to do justice to the fatherless and the oppressed.' The infinitive lišpōṭ summarizes YHWH's purpose: to judge/vindicate. The pair yātôm wādāk ('fatherless and crushed') represent the most vulnerable — those with no human advocate. The final phrase bal-yôsip ʿôd laʿărōṣ ʾĕnôš min-hāʾāreṣ, 'that man of earth may strike terror no more,' returns to the ʾĕnôš ('mortal') of 9:20: the psalm's closing vision is the end of human tyranny before divine sovereignty. The earthly oppressor — whatever his momentary power — is finally, irreversibly exposed as merely mortal.

11.1: baYHWH ḥāsîtî (ḇaYHWH ḥāsîṯî): 'In the LORD I take refuge.' The opening confession — baYHWH ḥāsîtî — is the psalm's thesis. The root ḥāsâ ('to take refuge, flee for protection') is used extensively in the Psalter for covenant trust; it implies running to a physically stronger protector. The advice of unnamed advisors (ʾîmrû lĕnapšî nûdî harkĕm ṣippōrĕkem, 'flee like a bird to your mountain!') is rejected before it is quoted. The psalm's genre has been called a 'confidence psalm' — the trust expressed in v.1 needs no argument; it is the starting point from which everything else follows.
11.2: kî hinnēh rĕšāʿîm yidrekûn qešet (kî hinnēh rĕšāʿîm yiḏreḵûn qāšeṯ): 'for behold, the wicked bend the bow.' The verb dārak ('to tread/bend') applied to the bow describes arming for attack. The imagery of arrow shot bĕmô-ʾōpel, 'in the dark,' emphasizes treachery — the attack is hidden, impossible to defend against. The target is lĕyišrê-lēb, 'at the upright in heart.' The contrast — wicked with weapons / righteous with no defence — creates the crisis that makes the question of v.3 so pressing.
11.3: kî hāšĕtôt yehāressûn mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq (kî hāšŝtôṯ yehārēsûn mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq): 'when the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?' The noun šātôt ('foundations, pillars') — from šît, 'to set, establish' — refers to the moral and social structures that make ordered life possible. The rhetorical question mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq? ('what can the righteous do?') is the voice of despair: if the foundations collapse, no human action suffices. The psalm's answer is implicit: what the righteous do is precisely what v.1 declares — take refuge in YHWH, the one on whose throne the foundations ultimately rest.
11.4: YHWH bĕhêkal qodšô YHWH baššāmayim kissĕʾô (YHWH bĕhêḵal qodšô YHWH baššāmayim kissĕʾô): 'The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD — his throne is in heaven.' The two-fold affirmation — earthly temple and heavenly throne — is programmatic: YHWH simultaneously inhabits the Jerusalem sanctuary (locus of covenant presence) and the heavenly court (locus of sovereign judgment). The participle bōḥēn ('he tests/examines') combined with ʿênāyw yĕḥĕzû ('his eyes see') directly answers the wicked man's claim in Ps 10:11 that God neither sees nor acts. Divine omniscience is the foundation that the wicked man's practical atheism cannot survive.
11.5: YHWH ṣaddîq yibḥān wĕrāšāʿ wĕʾōhēb ḥāmās śānĕʾâh napšô (YHWH ṣaddîq yiḇḥān wĕrāšāʿ wĕʾōhēḇ ḥāmās śānĕʾāh napšô): 'The LORD tests the righteous, but the wicked and the one who loves violence his soul hates.' The verb bāḥan ('test, examine, refine') — used of metallic refinement — indicates that YHWH's scrutiny of the righteous is purifying, not merely punitive. The phrase napšô śānĕʾâh ('his soul hates') applied to the wicked is strong: divine hate is not emotional pique but settled moral revulsion at evil. The 'one who loves violence' (ʾōhēb ḥāmās) identifies the wicked by affection rather than merely behavior.
11.6: yamṭēr ʿal rĕšāʿîm paḥîm (yamṭēr ʿal-rĕšāʿîm paḥîm): 'he rains snares on the wicked.' The divine judgment is pictured as a storm of paḥîm ('snares, traps'), ʾēš ('fire'), wĕgāprit ('brimstone'), and rûaḥ zîlĕʿôt ('a scorching wind'). The sulfur-and-fire imagery recalls Sodom (Gen 19:24) — the paradigmatic judgment on cities that exchanged hospitality and justice for violence. The phrase zōt mĕnat kôsām, 'this is the portion of their cup,' uses kôs ('cup') as a metonym for fate or divine allotment — the wicked receive what their choices earned.
11.7: kî ṣaddîq YHWH ṣĕdāqôt ʾāhēb (kî ṣaddîq YHWH ṣĕdāqôt ʾāhēḇ): 'for the LORD is righteous; he loves righteous deeds.' The final verse resolves the crisis of v.3: because YHWH himself is ṣaddîq ('righteous'), he necessarily loves ṣĕdāqôt ('acts of righteousness'). The wicked foundations (v.3) cannot finally stand because righteousness is ontologically grounded in YHWH's character. The concluding phrase yāšār yĕḥĕzû pānêmô, 'the upright shall behold his face,' is the psalm's beatific vision — the reward of those who flee to YHWH (v.1) is intimacy with him, seeing the face that the wicked cannot bear (cf. Rev 22:4; Matt 5:8).

12.1: hôšîʿâ YHWH kî gāmar ḥāsîd (hôšîʿâ YHWH kî gāmar ḥāsîd): 'Save, O LORD, for the godly man ceases.' The qal perfect gāmar ('to come to an end, cease') describes the ḥāsîd ('covenant-loyal one, godly person') as nearly extinct. The word ḥāsîd (from ḥesed, 'steadfast love') denotes one who embodies covenant faithfulness. Its near-disappearance signals covenantal crisis. The companion phrase kî passû ʾĕmûnîm mibbĕnê hāʾādām, 'for the faithful have vanished from among the children of man,' uses ʾĕmûnîm ('faithful ones') — those whose lives are characterized by ʾĕmûnâ (faithfulness/reliability).
12.2: šāwĕʾ yĕdabbĕrû ʾîš ʾet rēʿēhû (šāwĕʾ yĕdabbĕrû ʾîš ʾeṯ-rēʿēhû): 'Each speaks falsehood to his neighbor.' The noun šāwĕʾ ('vanity, falsehood, emptiness') describes speech that has no correspondence to reality. The phrase śĕpat ḥălāqôt bĕlēb wālēb yĕdabbĕrû, 'with flattering lips and a double heart they speak,' introduces bĕlēb wālēb ('with a double heart' — literally 'with heart and heart'). The doubled lēb signals divided loyalty: the speaker's external words do not match the internal reality. This linguistic duplicity is the social crisis the psalm laments.
12.3: yakrēt YHWH kol-śiptê ḥălāqôt (yaḵrēt YHWH kol-śipĕtê ḥălāqôṯ): 'May the LORD cut off all flattering lips.' The hiphil jussive yakrēt ('may he cut off') moves from description to petition. The hendiadys śiptê ḥălāqôt wĕlāšôn mĕdabberet gĕdōlôt, 'flattering lips and the tongue that speaks great things,' pairs the instrument (lips/tongue) with the content (great boasts/grand arrogance). The contrast between the godly who have ceased (v.1) and these boasters who dominate drives the prayer for reversal.
12.4: ʾăšer ʾāmĕrû lĕlāšōnēnû nagbîr (ʾăšer ʾāmĕrû lĕlāšōnēnû naḡḇîr): 'who have said, 'With our tongue we will prevail.'' The hiphil nagbîr ('we will be strong, prevail') — from gābar ('to be mighty') — claims that rhetorical power is sovereign. The phrase śĕpātênû ʾittānû, 'our lips are our own/with us,' asserts linguistic autonomy: speech is self-owned, accountable to no one. The closing question mî ʾādôn lānû, 'who is lord over us?', is the boast's logical conclusion and the psalm's central theological problem: the rejection of any authority beyond the self.
12.5: mišōd ʿăniyyîm mēʾanqat ʾebyônîm (mišōḏ ʿăniyyîm mēʾanqaṯ ʾeḇyônîm): 'Because of the devastation of the poor, because of the groaning of the needy.' The noun šōd ('devastation, destruction') describes violent oppression; ʾanqâ ('groaning') is the sound of extremity. YHWH's response is direct speech: ʿattâ ʾāqûm yōʾmar YHWH, 'now I will arise, says the LORD.' The divine yōʾmar ('says the LORD') — an oracle formula — gives the next words prophetic authority: ʾāšît bĕyēšaʿ yāpîaḥ lô, 'I will place in safety the one who pants/longs for it.' The verb yāpîaḥ ('to breathe heavily, pant') captures the desperate longing of the oppressed.
12.6: ʾimrôt YHWH ʾimrôt tĕhōrôt (ʾimrôt YHWH ʾimrôt ṭĕhōrôṯ): 'The words of the LORD are pure words.' The repetition ʾimrôt ... ʾimrôt ('words ... words') is emphatic: this is definitional. The adjective tāhôr ('pure, clean') is the technical term for cultic purity — what is unadulterated, without contamination. The simile kesep ṣārûp bĕʿalîl lāʾāreṣ mĕzuqqāq šibʿātayim, 'silver refined in a furnace on the ground, purified seven times,' specifies the purity: smelted and resmelted until all dross is gone. In context this is a pointed contrast to the hollow words of v.2-4 — human speech is śāwĕʾ ('vain'); divine speech is tāhôr.
12.7: ʾattâ YHWH tišmĕrēm (ʾattâ YHWH tišmĕrēm): 'You, O LORD, will keep them.' The subject ʾattâ ('you') is emphatic — against all human protectors who have failed, YHWH is the one who guards. The referent of tišmĕrēm is debated: the 'them' may refer to 'the poor' of v.5 or 'the words' of v.6. Both yield coherent sense. The phrase tiṣṣĕrennû min-haddôr zû lĕʿôlām, 'you will guard us from this generation forever,' shifts to first person — the psalmist includes himself and the community among those preserved. The phrase min-haddôr zû ('from this generation') indicates the corrupt contemporaries of vv.1-4.
12.8: sābîb rĕšāʿîm yithallākûn (sāḇîḇ rĕšāʿîm yiṯhallāḵûn): 'The wicked walk about on every side.' The hitpael yithallākûn ('walk back and forth') suggests restless movement of the wicked who dominate the landscape. The phrase kĕrum zullût libnê ʾādām, 'when what is vile is exalted among the children of man,' names the inversion: zullût ('vileness, baseness') rises to prominence. The psalm ends not with resolution but with honest observation — the present moment is bleak. Faith in YHWH's word (v.6) and his guardianship (v.7) does not eliminate the experience of living in a corrupt generation.

13.1: ʿad-ʾānâ YHWH tišĕkāḥēnî nĕṣaḥ (ʿaḏ-ʾānâ YHWH tišĕḵāḥēnî nĕṣaḥ): 'How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?' The double question ʿad-ʾānâ / ʿad-ʾānâ ('how long / how long') opens Psalm 13 in anaphora — the same opening repeats in vv.1-2 a total of four times, creating a rhetorical escalation of anguish. The verb šākaḥ ('forget') is the same verb the wicked apply to God in Ps 10:11; here the psalmist fears it may be true of himself. The adverb nĕṣaḥ ('forever, to perpetuity') intensifies: will the abandonment never end? This is honest lament theology — the question is addressed to God, not denied.
13.2: ʿad-ʾānâ ʾāśît ʿēṣôt bĕnapšî (ʿaḏ-ʾānâ ʾāśîṯ ʿēṣôṯ ḇĕnapšî): 'How long must I take counsel in my soul?' The four 'how long' questions span two verses: (1) divine forgetting, (2) divine hiding, (3) internal anguish ('counsel in my soul'), (4) enemy triumph. The noun ʿēṣôt ('counsel, plans') combined with yāgôn bĕlibî, 'sorrow in my heart,' shows the psalmist at an inner limit — exhausted by trying to think his way through the crisis. The rise of the enemy — yārûm ʾôyĕbî ʿālay ('my enemy is exalted over me') — is the external dimension of the same abandonment.
13.3: habbîṭâ ʿănēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhāy (habbîṭâ ʿănēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhāy): 'Consider and answer me, O LORD my God.' The three imperatives — habbîṭâ ('look!'), ʿănēnî ('answer me!'), hāʾîrâ ʿênay ('light up my eyes!') — are the psalm's petition block. The phrase pen-ʾîšan hammāwet, 'lest I sleep the death sleep,' uses sleep as a euphemism for death; the dimming of eyes in death is contrasted with the eyes 'lit up' by divine attention. The possessive ʾĕlōhāy ('my God') is intimate — even in the darkest lament, the covenant relationship is assumed.
13.4: pen-yōʾmar ʾôyĕbî yĕkoltîw (pen-yōʾmar ʾôyĕbî yĕḵoltîw): 'lest my enemy say, 'I have prevailed over him.'' The psalmist's concern is not merely personal survival but the theological stakes: if he falls, the enemy's gloating — yāgîlû ṣārāyw kî ʾemmôṭ, 'his adversaries rejoice when I am moved' — will be a public refutation of trust in YHWH. The logic is the same as Moses' appeal to YHWH's reputation in Exod 32:12 and Num 14:13-16: the outcome of the psalmist's situation reflects on the credibility of YHWH's protection.
13.5: waʾănî bĕḥasdĕkā bāṭaḥtî (waʾănî ḇĕḥasdĕḵā ḇāṭaḥtî): 'But I have trusted in your steadfast love.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but as for me') marks the psalm's dramatic turn: from lament (vv.1-2) and petition (vv.3-4) to trust and praise (vv.5-6). The perfect bāṭaḥtî ('I have trusted') indicates an established disposition — not a new resolution but a prior settled stance. The noun ḥesed ('steadfast love, covenant loyalty') is YHWH's defining character; to trust in it is to rest on the most reliable foundation available. The result: yāgēl libbî bîšûʿātekā, 'my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.'
13.6: ʾāšîrâ laYHWH kî gāmal ʿālay (ʾāšîrâ laYHWH kî ḡāmal ʿālay): 'I will sing to the LORD, because he has dealt bountifully with me.' The cohortative ʾāšîrâ ('I will sing!') resolves the psalm with a vow of praise. The verb gāmal ('to deal with, requite, complete') — in the qal, 'to wean, to fully develop' — here means 'to deal bountifully, to complete one's care for.' The phrase kî gāmal ʿālay projects completed deliverance from the perspective of trust: the psalmist sings before the rescue arrives, confident it will come. This gap between petition and answered prayer is bridged by ḥesed-trust, making Psalm 13 a template for faith under unanswered prayer.

14.1: ʾāmar nābāl bĕlibbô ʾên ʾĕlōhîm (ʾāmar nāḇāl bĕlibbô ʾên ʾĕlōhîm): 'The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.'' The noun nābāl ('fool') in Hebrew does not primarily denote intellectual deficiency but moral and relational failure — the nābāl is one who is morally obtuse, living without reference to God or covenant. The phrase bĕlibbô ('in his heart') again signals practical atheism: the claim is not philosophical but operational. The result: hišḥîtû hiṭʿîbû ʿălîlâ, 'they are corrupt, they do abominable works' — the inner denial produces outward moral collapse. Paul quotes vv.1-3 (with additions from Pss 5 and 140) in Rom 3:10-12 to establish universal sinfulness.
14.2: YHWH miššāmayim hišqîp ʿal-bĕnê ʾādām (YHWH miššāmayim hišqîp ʿal-bĕnê ʾāḏām): 'The LORD looks down from heaven on the children of man.' The hiphil hišqîp ('to look down, peer over') places YHWH at elevated vantage — an anthropomorphism for omniscience. The purpose: lirʾôt hăyēš maśkîl dōrēš ʾet-ʾĕlōhîm, 'to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God.' The participle maśkîl (from śākal, 'to have insight/wisdom') describes someone who grasps reality rightly — the opposite of the nābāl. The divine investigation sets up the devastating verdict of v.3.
14.3: hakkōl sār yaḥdāw neʾĕlāḥû (hakkōl sār yaḥḏāw neʾĕlāḥû): 'They have all turned aside; together they have become corrupt.' The all-inclusive hakkōl ('all') and yaḥdāw ('together') close every exit: there is no exception. The niphal neʾĕlāḥû ('become sour/corrupt') uses a term related to fermented milk that has turned — the image is of thoroughgoing contamination. Paul's citation in Rom 3:12-18 extends this to add quotations from Psalms 5, 140, and Isaiah 59, building a catena of human sinfulness — all humanity, Jew and Gentile, stands condemned before the bar of the law.
14.4: hălōʾ yādĕʿû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen (hălōʾ yāḏĕʿû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen): 'Have they no knowledge, all the evildoers?' The rhetorical question expresses shocked incomprehension. The phrase ʾōkĕlê ʿammî ʾāklû leḥem, 'they eat up my people as they eat bread,' uses the image of consuming bread — casual, daily, without moral awareness — to describe the exploitation of the ʿam ('people') by the pōʿălê ʾāwen ('workers of iniquity'). The additional indictment wĕʾet-YHWH lōʾ qārāʾû, 'and they do not call upon the LORD,' is the cultic symptom of the practical atheism declared in v.1.
14.5: šām pāḥădû pāḥad (šām pāḥăḏû pāḥaḏ): 'There they were in great terror.' The cognate accusative pāḥădû pāḥad ('they feared fear') is intensive — overwhelming dread. The phrase kî ʾĕlōhîm bĕdôr ṣaddîq, 'for God is with the generation of the righteous,' reveals the source of the wicked's terror: they discover that the God they denied (v.1) is actually present, defending the righteous (ṣaddîq) they were exploiting. The reversal echoes the Psalter's pervasive theme: the practical atheist who lives as though God does not see is surprised by the reality of divine presence.
14.6: ʿăṣat ʿānî tābîšû (ʿăṣaṯ ʿānî ṯāḇîšû): 'You would shame the plans of the poor.' The verb bôš ('shame, humiliate') in the hiphil tābîšû describes the wicked's attempt to undermine the counsel of the ʿānî ('poor, afflicted'). The response: kî YHWH maḥsēhû, 'because the LORD is his refuge.' The word maḥseh ('refuge, shelter') from ḥāsâ ('take refuge') — the same root as Ps 11:1 — positions YHWH as the answer to the wicked's attack on the poor. What the wicked do not reckon with is that their victim has a protector they cannot see.
14.7: mî yittēn miṣṣiyyôn yĕšûʿat yiśrāʾēl (mî yittēn miṣṣiyyôn yĕšûʿaṯ yiśrāʾēl): 'Oh that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion!' The exclamatory mî yittēn ('who will give? / oh that!') shifts from individual crisis to national hope. The phrase bĕšûb YHWH šĕbût ʿammô, 'when the LORD restores the fortunes of his people,' uses šûb šĕbût — an idiom for comprehensive restoration (used also in Jer 30:18; Job 42:10). This verse transforms the psalm from individual lament about fools to communal hope: the deliverance from practical atheism at every level — personal and national — issues from Zion, the place of divine kingship.

15.1: YHWH mî-yāgûr bĕʾohŏlekā (YHWH mî-yāḡûr bĕʾohŏleḵā): 'O LORD, who shall sojourn in your tent?' Psalm 15 is a torah-liturgy or entrance psalm — an answer to the question posed at the sanctuary gate, identifying who may enter to worship. The noun ʾōhel ('tent') recalls the wilderness tabernacle and its successor at Jerusalem; har qodšekā ('your holy hill') is Zion/Mount Moriah. The double question — who may sojourn (gûr, temporary residence), who may dwell (šākan, settled habitation) — parallels Ps 24:3 ('who shall ascend the hill of the LORD?'). The answer is not ethnic or ritual but ethical.
15.2: hôlēk tāmîm ûpōʿēl ṣedeq (hôlēḵ tāmîm ûp̄ōʿēl ṣeḏeq): 'He who walks blamelessly and does what is right.' The present participles — hôlēk ('one who walks'), pōʿēl ('one who does'), dōbēr ('one who speaks') — describe character as persistent disposition rather than isolated acts. The noun tāmîm ('blameless, complete, without defect') is used of sacrificial animals without blemish (Lev 1:3) and of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Abraham (Gen 17:1) — moral completeness. The phrase wĕdōbēr ʾĕmet bĕlibābô, 'who speaks truth in his heart,' combines speech and interiority: the truthful person does not merely say true things but thinks truly.
15.3: lōʾ-rāgal ʿal-lĕšōnô (lōʾ-rāḡal ʿal-lĕšōnô): 'who does not slander with his tongue.' The verb rāgal ('to spy, go as a foot-soldier') in the piel means 'to slander, go about as a tale-bearer' — the image of someone who covertly undermines others. Three negatives characterize the qualified worshipper: no slander, no evil done to a neighbor, no reproach taken up against a friend. The phrase lōʾ nāśāʾ ḥerpâ ʿal-qĕrôbô ('does not take up a reproach against his neighbor') suggests not repeating or amplifying rumors about those close to him.
15.4: nibzeh bĕʿênāyw nimʾās (niḇzeh bĕʿênāyw nimʾās): 'in whose eyes a vile person is despised.' The niphal nibzeh ('despised, held in contempt') and nimʾās ('rejected, disdained') — the righteous person's moral perception correctly evaluates character rather than status. The contrast: wĕʾet-yirʾê YHWH yĕkabbēd, 'but he honors those who fear the LORD.' Moral evaluation follows theological reality, not social prestige. The crucial phrase: nišbaʿ lĕhāraʿ wĕlōʾ yāmîr, 'who swears to his own hurt and does not change.' The righteous person keeps an oath even when it costs him — the ultimate test of verbal integrity (cf. Matt 5:37).
15.5: kaspô lōʾ nātan bĕnešek (ḵaspô lōʾ nāṯan bĕnešeḵ): 'who does not put out his money at interest.' The noun nešek ('interest, biting') from nāšak ('to bite') describes usury — taking a bite from the poor through compound interest. The combination of nešek and tarbît ('increase') in Lev 25:36-37 was explicitly prohibited in lending to the poor within Israel. The final clause: wĕšōḥad ʿal-nāqî lōʾ lāqaḥ, 'who does not take a bribe against the innocent.' The word šōḥad ('bribe') is the corruption of the justice system that should protect the nāqî ('innocent'). The closing promise — ʿōśēh ʾēlleh lōʾ yimmôṭ lĕʿôlām, 'the one who does these things shall never be moved' — echoes lōʾ yimmôṭ of Ps 13:4 (the enemy's boast), now granted to the covenant-faithful as divine assurance.

16.1: šomrēnî ʾēl kî ḥāsîtî bāk (šomrēnî ʾēl kî ḥāsîṯî ḇāḵ): 'Preserve me, O God, for I take refuge in you.' The miktam superscription (miktam lĕdāwid) is of uncertain meaning — possibly 'inscription' or related to ketam ('gold'), suggesting a precious composition. The qal imperative šomrēnî ('guard, keep, preserve') from šāmar invokes the divine watchfulness that is YHWH's primary covenant function (Num 6:24; Ps 121:7-8). The ground kî ḥāsîtî bāk ('because I have taken refuge in you') — the ḥāsâ root — places the psalmist entirely on YHWH's protective provision rather than any alternative.
16.2: ʾāmart laYHWH ʾădōnāy ʾātâ (ʾāmartî laYHWH ʾăḏōnāy ʾāttâ): 'I say to the LORD, 'You are my Lord.'' The confession ʾădōnāy ʾātâ ('you are my Lord/Master') is the foundational sovereignty declaration. The phrase ṭôbātî bal-ʿālêkā, 'my good is not beyond you / my good has no reference point above you,' affirms that all genuine blessing is located in and derived from YHWH. LXX renders this with some difficulty; the MT is also textually challenging. But the theological point is clear: YHWH is the psalmist's supreme good, not a means to other goods.
16.3: liqdōšîm ʾăšer bāʾāreṣ hēmmâ (liqḏôšîm ʾăšer-bāʾāreṣ hēmmâ): 'As for the holy ones in the land, they are the excellent ones.' The referent of qĕdôšîm ('holy ones') is debated: foreign gods/their devotees, or faithful Israelites. The following phrase wĕʾaddîrê kol-ḥepṣî bām ('and the noble/majestic ones, all my delight is in them') and v.4's contrast with those who 'run after another' suggests the qĕdôšîm here are the faithful covenant community. The psalmist's delight is with this community — a social theology of belonging to the people of YHWH.
16.4: yirbû ʿaṣṣĕbôtām ʾaḥēr māhārû (yirbû ʿaṣṣĕḇôṯām ʾaḥēr māhārû): 'The sorrows of those who run after another god shall multiply.' The noun ʿaṣṣābôt ('sorrows, idols') — related to ʿeṣeb ('pain/idol') — captures the double meaning: idol worship produces pain. The phrase ʾaḥēr māhārû, 'they run after another,' uses ʾaḥēr ('another') for foreign deities — a pejorative, refusing them divine titles. The psalmist's triple refusal — no drink offerings of blood, no taking their names on his lips, no offering — constitutes comprehensive rejection of the rival cult.
16.5: YHWH mĕnat-ḥelqî wĕkôsî (YHWH mĕnaṯ-ḥelqî wĕḵôsî): 'The LORD is my chosen portion and my cup.' The nouns ḥēleq ('portion, allotment') and kôs ('cup') together describe the totality of inheritance. In the land distribution (Josh 13-21), each tribe received a ḥēleq; Levi's portion was YHWH himself (Deut 10:9; 18:2). By claiming YHWH as his ḥēleq, the psalmist spiritualizes the levitical ideal: anyone may choose YHWH as the substance of their inheritance. The phrase ʾattâ tômîk gôrālî, 'you hold my lot,' pictures YHWH himself holding the lot-cast that determines the psalmist's future.
16.6: ḥăbālîm nāplû-lî binnĕʿīmîm (ḥăḇālîm nāplû-lî ḇannĕʿîmîm): 'The lines have fallen for me in pleasant places.' The noun ḥebel ('measuring line') refers to land surveying — the lines marking one's lot. The passive nāplû ('have fallen') is the language of lot-casting: the result of throwing the lot is that his portion lies in pleasant territory. The phrase ʾap-naḥălat šāpĕrâ ʿālay, 'indeed, I have a beautiful inheritance,' uses the rare verb šāpar ('to be beautiful/fair'). When YHWH is one's portion (v.5), the inheritance is inherently beautiful — the psalm's meditation on contentment grounded in divine choice.
16.7: ʾăbārēk ʾet-YHWH ʾăšer yĕʿāṣānî (ʾăḇārēḵ ʾeṯ-YHWH ʾăšer yĕʿāṣānî): 'I bless the LORD who gives me counsel.' The verb yāʿaṣ ('to counsel, advise') describes YHWH as the psalmist's wisdom-source. The phrase ʾap-lêlôt yissĕrûnî kilĕyôtāy, 'even in the night my kidneys (reins) instruct me,' attributes the body's night-time processing to divine instruction: the kidneys (kĕlāyôt), understood in Hebrew thought as the seat of deep feeling and moral intuition, function as an inner voice that continues YHWH's counsel in sleep. This nocturnal instruction anticipates the 'meditation day and night' of Ps 1:2.
16.8: šiwwîtî YHWH lĕnegdî tāmîd (šiwwîṯî YHWH lĕneḡḏî ṯāmîd): 'I have set the LORD always before me.' The verb šāwâ ('to set, place') in the piel šiwwîtî means 'I have placed/levelled before me as a standard.' The phrase kî mimînî bal-ʾemmôṭ, 'because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved,' positions YHWH as the constant companion who prevents collapse. Peter cites vv.8-11 in Acts 2:25-28, arguing that David — who wrote this — did not die permanently (Acts 2:29), therefore the psalm speaks of the Messiah: Jesus who was raised from the dead is the true referent of the one who would not be moved.
16.9: lākēn śāmaḥ libbî wayyāgel kĕbôdî (lāḵēn śāmaḥ libbî wayyāḡel kĕḇôḏî): 'Therefore my heart is glad, and my glory rejoices.' The causal lākēn ('therefore') links the settled trust of v.8 to the threefold joy: heart (lēb), glory/honor (kābôd), and flesh (bāśār). The phrase ʾap-bĕśārî yiškon lābeṭaḥ, 'my flesh also rests in security,' is programmatic for Acts 2:26: Peter reads the resting of flesh in security as pointing beyond David's own confidence to the resurrection body of the Messiah — flesh that genuinely rested in the security of incorruption.
16.10: kî lōʾ-taʿăzōb napšî lišĕʾôl (kî lōʾ-taʿăzōḇ napšî lišĕʾôl): 'For you will not abandon my soul to Sheol.' The key word napšî ('my soul/life') — the whole self — will not be abandoned (ʿāzab) to Sheol (the realm of the dead). The parallelism: lōʾ-tittēn ḥăsîdĕkā lirʾôt šāḥat, 'nor will you give your Holy One to see the Pit.' The noun šāḥat ('pit, destruction') is parallel to Sheol. Peter's argument in Acts 2:27 is precise: since David died and his tomb is known (Acts 2:29), David could not have spoken of himself. The one who did not see corruption is Jesus, raised before his body could decay. Paul makes the same argument in Acts 13:35-37.
16.11: tôdîʿēnî ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm (tôḏîʿēnî ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm): 'You make known to me the path of life.' The hiphil tôdîʿēnî ('you cause me to know') from yādaʿ signals experiential, relational knowledge — not merely information about life but being shown its path. The phrase śōbaʿ śĕmāḥôt ʾet-pānêkā, 'fullness of joys with your face,' places the highest joy in the divine presence (pānêkā, 'your face'). The final phrase nĕʿīmôt bîmînĕkā nĕṣaḥ, 'pleasures at your right hand forevermore,' uses nĕṣaḥ ('forever') — the same word used of divine permanence in Ps 9:7. The path of life leads to perpetual divine presence: this verse grounds Christian eschatology in the Psalter's vision of beatific communion with YHWH.

17.1: šimʿâ YHWH ṣedeq haqšîbâ rinnātî (šimʿâ YHWH ṣedeq haqšîbâ rinnātî): 'Hear a just cause, O LORD; attend to my cry.' Psalm 17 is a ṭĕpillâ ('prayer') — one of only five psalms so labeled (cf. Pss 86, 90, 102, 142). The opening appeal to ṣedeq ('righteousness/justice') frames the prayer as a legal appeal: the psalmist presents his case before the divine judge. The phrase bĕlōʾ śiptê mirmâ, 'from lips free of deceit,' establishes the speaker's credibility — this is not manipulation but genuine appeal. The term rinnâ ('cry, ringing cry') captures the emotional intensity of the plea.
17.2: millĕpānêkā mišpāṭî yēṣēʾ (millĕp̄āneḵā mišpāṭî yēṣēʾ): 'Let my vindication come from your presence.' The noun mišpāṭ ('judgment, vindication, sentence') is what the psalmist awaits from the divine court. The phrase ʿênêkā tiḥzenâ mêšārîm, 'let your eyes see what is right,' invites YHWH's direct judicial inspection. The use of millĕpānêkā ('from before your face') — divine presence as the source of the verdict — anticipates the beatific conclusion of v.15 where the psalmist will see that same face in righteousness.
17.3: bāḥantā libbî pāqadtā lāylâ (ḇāḥantā libbî pāqaḏtā lāylâ): 'You have tested my heart; you have visited me by night.' The two verbs — bāḥan ('test, examine,' the metallurgical term from Ps 11:4-5) and pāqad ('visit, muster, examine') — describe thorough divine investigation. The test occurs lāylâ ('by night') — when the psalmist is most vulnerable, most unguarded, most truly himself. The result: ṣĕraptanî bal-timṣāʾ, 'you have refined me; you found nothing.' The psalmist is not claiming sinlessness but innocence in the specific matter of the prayer — his hands are not contaminated by the enemy's violence.
17.4: lĕpĕʿullôt ʾādām bidbar śĕpātêkā (lipĕʿullôṯ ʾāḏām biḏḇar śĕp̄āṯeḵā): 'Regarding the works of man, by the word of your lips.' The phrase biḏbar śĕp̄āṯeḵā ('by the word of your lips') identifies YHWH's revealed word as the standard of behavior. The clause ʾănî šāmartî ʾōrĕḥôt pārîṣ, 'I have guarded myself from the paths of the violent,' uses pārîṣ ('violent one, destroyer') — the same social category as the enemy psalms describe. The claim is not moral perfectionism but covenant integrity: the psalmist has not followed the way of violence.
17.5: tāmōk ʾăšûray bĕmaʿglôtêkā (ṯāmōk ʾăšûray bĕmaʿgĕlôṯeḵā): 'My steps have held fast to your paths.' The phrase bal-nāmôṭû pĕʿāmāy, 'my feet have not slipped,' uses the same root môṭ ('to waver, slip') that appears in Ps 15:5 (the righteous 'shall not be moved') and Ps 13:4 (the enemy's boast). The psalmist's stability is attributed to divine holding (tāmōk, 'you hold firm') — not self-generated virtue but YHWH's grip on him as he walks in covenant paths.
17.6: ʾănî qĕrāʾtîkā kî-taʿănēnî ʾēl (ʾănî qĕrāʾṯîḵā ḵî-ṯaʿănēnî ʾēl): 'I call upon you, for you will answer me, O God.' The perfect qĕrāʾtî ('I have called') paired with the imperfect taʿănēnî ('you will answer') creates a confidence structure: past invocation grounds future expectation. The phrase haṭ-ʾoznĕkā lî šmaʿ ʾimrātî, 'incline your ear to me; hear my speech,' uses the anthropomorphism of a king bending close to hear a petition — personal, attentive, relational hearing.
17.7: haphlēʾ ḥăsādêkā môšîaʿ ḥôsîm (haphlēʾ ḥăsāḏeḵā môšîaʿ ḥôsîm): 'Show your marvelous steadfast love, O Savior of those who take refuge.' The hiphil haphlēʾ ('make wonderful/extraordinary') from pālāʾ ('to be marvelous') calls for ḥesed that exceeds ordinary expectation. The divine title môšîaʿ ḥôsîm, 'savior of those who take refuge,' combines the verb yāšaʿ ('save') with ḥāsâ ('take refuge') — matching the psalm's opening self-description as one who relies on YHWH. The phrase mimmitqômĕmîm bîmînekā, 'from those who rise up against your right hand,' places the enemies in opposition to divine power.
17.8: šomrēnî kĕʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin (šomrēnî kĕʾîšôn baṯ-ʿāyin): 'Keep me as the apple of the eye.' The phrase ʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin ('apple of the daughter of the eye') — the pupil — is the most tender image of intimate protection: the tiny, precious, reflexively guarded part of the body. The second image, bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā tastîrēnî, 'hide me in the shadow of your wings,' draws on the protective imagery of a mother bird shielding her chicks (Deut 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Matt 23:37). Together, the two images — the guarded eye and the sheltering wing — express total, personal, enveloping protection.
17.9: mippĕnê rĕšāʿîm zû šaddûnî (mippĕnê rĕšāʿîm zû šaddûnî): 'from the wicked who do me violence.' The relative clause zû šaddûnî ('who destroy/deal violently with me') uses the root šādad ('to ruin, deal violently') — a characteristic activity of the wicked in the Psalter. The phrase ʾōyĕbay bĕnepeš yaqqîpû ʿālay, 'my deadly enemies surround me' — literally 'enemies against my soul/life who encircle me' — uses the napšî construction to indicate mortal enmity, not merely personal hostility.
17.10: ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû pîmô dibbĕrû bĕgēʾût (ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû pîmô dibbĕrû ḇĕḡēʾûṯ): 'They have closed their hearts; with their mouths they speak arrogantly.' The phrase ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû — literally 'their fat they have closed' — uses ḥeleb ('fat') as a metaphor for calloused insensitivity (cf. Ps 119:70: 'their heart is fat as grease'). The noun gēʾût ('pride, arrogance') describes the content of their speech. The psalmist characterizes the enemy by two failures: a closed heart that cannot receive correction and an arrogant mouth that overreaches all limits.
17.11: ʾašûrênû ʿattâ sĕbābûnû (ʾašûrênû ʿattâ sĕḇāḇûnû): 'In our steps they now surround us.' The plural surreptitiously shifts from 'me' (the individual psalmist) to 'us' — the community of the righteous who share this threat. The phrase ʿênêhem yāšîtû liṭṭôt bāʾāreṣ, 'they set their eyes to cast down to the ground,' describes the enemy's fixed gaze directed at bringing the righteous to earth — the same spatial language of exaltation and humiliation that runs through Psalms 9-10.
17.12: dimmyōnô kĕʾaryēh yiksopu litrup (dimmyōnô kĕʾaryēh yiḵsôp liṭrôp): 'He is like a lion eager to tear.' The simile kĕʾaryēh ('like a lion') returns from Ps 10:9 — the predator imagery for the wicked reaches its intensity here: not merely lurking but eager (yiksopu, 'yearns, longs') for prey. The parallel wĕkĕkĕpîr yōšēb bĕmistārîm, 'like a young lion crouching in ambush,' adds the youth-lion (kĕpîr) — strong and fierce. The two images — mature lion's desire and young lion's ambush — combine appetite with strategy.
17.13: qûmâ YHWH qaddĕmâh pānāyw (qûmâ YHWH qaḏḏĕmâ p̄ānāyw): 'Arise, O LORD! Confront him, cause him to bow.' The imperative qûmâ ('arise!') is the liturgical summons to divine action (cf. Ps 9:19; 10:12). The hiphil hakriʿēhû, 'cause him to bow/subdue him,' requests decisive defeat. The phrase pallĕṭâ napšî mērāšāʿ ḥarbĕkā, 'deliver my soul from the wicked by your sword,' invokes divine weaponry — YHWH himself fighting as a warrior on behalf of the vulnerable. The phrase ḥarbĕkā ('your sword') is an instrument of covenant judgment.
17.14: mĕmētîm yādĕkā YHWH mĕmētîm miḥeled (mĕmētîm yāḏĕḵā YHWH mĕmētîm miḥeled): 'by your hand, O LORD, from men of the world.' The phrase mĕmētîm ('from the dead / from men who are dying') combined with miḥeled ('from this transient world') categorizes the enemies by their mortality — they belong to the perishable world order. The description of their prosperity — ḥelqām bĕḥayyîm ûṣĕpûnĕkā tĕmallēʾ biṭnām, 'whose portion is in this life and whose belly you fill with your treasure' — does not condemn wealth per se but identifies those whose satisfaction is entirely worldly: they have their reward here (cf. Luke 6:24). The phrase yiśbĕʿû bānîm wĕhinnîḥû yitrām lĕʿôlĕlêhem, 'they are satisfied with children, and they leave their abundance to their infants,' is ironic: the legacy the wicked leave is material, transient, destined to be inherited by those equally vulnerable to mortality.
17.15: ʾănî bĕṣedeq ʾeḥĕzeh pānêkā (ʾănî bĕṣeḏeq ʾeḥĕzeh p̄āneḵā): 'As for me, I shall behold your face in righteousness.' The emphatic ʾănî ('as for me') makes a final contrast to the worldly satisfied of v.14. The verb ḥāzâ ('to behold, see in a vision') carries visionary weight — not a casual glance but a face-to-face encounter. The phrase bĕhāqîṣ ʾeśbaʿ tĕmûnātekā, 'when I awake, I shall be satisfied with your likeness,' introduces the moment of waking — ʾāqîṣ (from qîṣ, 'to awake from sleep'). The word tĕmûnâ ('likeness, form, similitude') — the same word used in Num 12:8 for Moses seeing the form of YHWH — is the ultimate beatific vision. Whether the psalmist speaks of morning prayer, resurrection, or eschatological encounter, the hunger of Ps 17 reaches its resolution here: satisfaction comes not from the world's abundance but from the face of God.

18.1: The superscription identifies this as David's victory song, paralleled in 2 Sam 22. אֶרְחָמְךָ יְהוָה חִזְקִי (ʾerḥāmĕkā YHWH ḥizqî, "I love you, LORD, my strength") — the verb rāḥam is from the root reḥem (womb): the tender, womb-like compassion a mother has for her child. Nowhere else in the Psalter is this verb used for the Psalmist's love toward God — it is normally used of God's compassion for humans (Ps 103:13). The opening is uniquely intimate: David declares a tender, interior attachment rather than the more conventional honor-vocabulary.
18.2: Six metaphors accumulate without pause: סַלְעִי (salʿî, "my rock" — a cliff-face of limestone, the kind a fugitive hides in), מְצוּדָתִי (mĕṣûdātî, "my fortress/stronghold"), מְפַלְטִי (mĕphalṭî, "my rescuer/deliverer" — the one who causes to escape), מָגִנִּי (māginnî, "my shield" — the small personal shield), קֶרֶן-יִשְׁעִי (qeren yishʿî, "the horn of my salvation" — the raised animal horn as the symbol of lifted, victorious strength), and מִשְׂגַּבִּי (miśgabbî, "my high tower/stronghold" — a fortified high place). The accumulated images do not build logically; they accumulate intensively, each adding another dimension of divine protective capacity.
18.3: מְהֻלָּל אֶקְרָא יְהוָה (mĕhullāl ʾeqrāʾ YHWH, "I called to the LORD, who is worthy of all praise") — the participle mĕhullāl (pual of hālal: praised, worthy of praise) precedes the verb "I called." The worthiness of the LORD to be praised is stated as the presupposition of prayer: one calls on the one who deserves to be called on. The perfect of confidence follows: "and I am saved (wĕʾiwwāsheaʿ) from my enemies."
18.4: חֶבְלֵי-מָוֶת (ḥeblê-māwet, "cords of death") — ḥebel can mean either "cord/rope" or "birth-pang/anguish" (the same consonants, different vocalization). The context (wrapping around, entangling) points to the "cord" sense, though the birth-pang connotation adds a layer: the death-convulsions that seized him. נַחֲלֵי בְלִיַּעַל (naḥălê bĕliyaʿal, "floods of chaos/destruction") — bĕliyaʿal literally means "without profit/worth"; as a cosmic term it represents destructive, anti-order forces (2 Sam 22:5 parallel: "floods of ungodliness"). The imagery merges physical danger with cosmic chaos.
18.5: חֶבְלֵי שְׁאוֹל (ḥeblê shĕʾôl, "ropes of Sheol") — the realm of the dead personified as an active snare-setter. מוֹקְשֵׁי מָוֶת (môqĕshê māwet, "snares of death") — môqēsh: a trap or snare for birds; death as a hunter laying traps. The four images of vv4-5 are parallel pairs: cords/ropes and floods/snares, building the picture of inescapable entrapment in mortal danger.
18.6: בַּצַּר-לִי אֶקְרָא יְהוָה (baṣṣar-lî ʾeqrāʾ YHWH, "In my distress I called to the LORD") — the same ṣar (tight place/distress) as Ps 4:1; prayer from within the constriction. מֵהֵיכָלוֹ יִשְׁמַע קוֹלִי (mêhêkālô yishmaʿ qôlî, "From his temple he heard my voice") — the cry that rose from the tight earthly space is heard in the heavenly temple (hêkāl = palace/temple). שַׁוְעָתִי לְפָנָיו תָּבוֹא בְאָזְנָיו (shawyʿātî lĕpānāyw tābôʾ bĕʾāznāyw) — "my cry came before him into his ears": the two-stage movement (before his face, then into his ears) suggests deliberate hearing, not accidental reception.
18.7: The theophany begins. וַתִּגְעַשׁ וַתִּרְעַשׁ הָאָרֶץ (wattīgʿash wattirʿash hāʾāreṣ, "The earth shook and trembled") — two verbs for earth-movement: gāʿash (to quake, storm) and rāʿash (to shake, tremble). Both are used in theophanic contexts (Isa 24:19-20). כִּי-חָרָה לוֹ (kî-ḥārāh lô, "for he was angry") — literally "for it burned to him" — ḥārāh uses the burning metaphor for divine anger (cf. Ps 2:5). The earth's convulsion is the response to divine wrath, not an independent seismic event.
18.8: עָלָה עָשָׁן בְּאַפּוֹ (ʿālāh ʿāshān bĕʾappô, "Smoke poured from his nostrils") — the anthropomorphism is striking: smoke rises from the divine nose/anger (ʾap = nostril/anger). The imagery draws on volcano imagery and on the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18: "Mount Sinai was covered with smoke, for the LORD descended on it in fire"). וְאֵשׁ מִפִּיו תֹּאכֵל (wĕʾêsh mippîw tōʾkel, "consuming fire from his mouth") — divine speech as fire. Gāḥālîm (coals/embers) blazing before him: the attendant of divine presence.
18.9: וַיֵּט שָׁמַיִם וַיֵּרַד (wayyêṭ shāmayim wayyêrad, "He bowed the heavens and came down") — the heavens as a fabric that can be bent down (nāṭāh: to stretch out, to incline). The divine descent through bent-down heaven is the theophanic idiom for God entering the human plane. וַעֲרָפֶל תַּחַת רַגְלָיו (waʿărāpel taḥat raglāyw, "dark cloud under his feet") — ʿărāpel is the thick, impenetrable darkness associated with divine presence at Sinai (Deut 4:11; 1 Kgs 8:12). Cloud as the floor the divine warrior walks on.
18.10: וַיִּרְכַּב עַל-כְּרוּב וַיָּעֹף (wayyirkab ʿal-kĕrûb wayyāʿōp, "He mounted a cherub and flew") — the divine chariot imagery: God riding the cherub. The cherubim are the ark-attendants (Exod 25:18-20) and the throne-bearers of the divine chariot (Ezek 1:5-21). יִדֶּה עַל-כַּנְפֵי-רוּחַ (yiddeh ʿal-kanpê-rûaḥ, "he soared on the wings of the wind") — the wind as the vehicle of divine movement, consistent with Ps 104:3-4.
18.11: יָשֶׁת חֹשֶׁךְ סִתְרוֹ (yāshet ḥōshek sitrô, "He made darkness his shelter") — paradox: the God of light (Ps 27:1; 1 John 1:5) conceals himself in darkness (ḥōshek). The divine warrior approaches unseen, in the cloud-darkness that protects his unapproachable holiness. סְכָּתוֹ סְבִיבוֹתָיו (sĕkātô sĕbîbôtāyw, "his canopy about him") — sukkāh as divine shelter: the same word for the booths of the Festival of Tabernacles.
18.12: מִנֹּגַהּ נֶגְדּוֹ (minnegah negdô, "From the brightness of his presence") — nōgah: brightness, radiance; even from within the dark cloud, the divine presence radiates outward. The clouds burst forth with בָּרָד וְגַחֲלֵי-אֵשׁ (bārād wĕgaḥălê-ʾêsh, "hailstones and coals of fire") — the storm weapons of the divine warrior, inverting the expected: the hidden dark cloud does not merely conceal but actively discharges judgment.
18.13: וְעֶלְיוֹן יִתֵּן קֹלוֹ (wĕʿelyôn yittên qōlô, "the Most High raised his voice") — thunder as divine speech. Qôl YHWH (voice of the LORD) is the thunder motif developed fully in Ps 29. The title ʿElyôn (Most High) is the Canaanite divine title absorbed into Israelite theology — used here alongside YHWH, declaring that the Most High God and the covenantal LORD are one.
18.14: וַיִּשְׁלַח חִצָּיו וַיְפִיצֵם (wayyishlah ḥiṣṣāyw wayyĕpîṣêm, "He shot his arrows and scattered them") — lightning as divine arrows. The imagery maps onto the Canaanite storm-god Baal who wielded lightning-weapons, but here repurposed entirely for YHWH's combat on behalf of his servant. The enemies scattered by divine lightning are not the cosmic enemies but David's historical opponents, showing that cosmic theophanic power is deployed for concrete human rescue.
18.15: וַיִּגָּלוּ אֲפִיקֵי מַיִם (wayyiggālû ʾăpîqê māyim, "The channels of the sea were exposed") — at the divine rebuke, the sea retreats and its underwater channels are laid bare. This echoes the Sea of Reeds: the OT consistently uses the Exodus sea-crossing as the type of divine warrior rescue. מִגַּעֲרָתְךָ יְהוָה (migaʿărātĕkā YHWH, "at your rebuke, O LORD") — gĕʿārāh (rebuke, scolding): the sea retreats not from force but from the divine word of rebuke — the same word used in Ps 104:7 for the waters fleeing at creation.
18.16: יִשְׁלַח מִמָּרוֹם יִקָּחֵנִי (yishlah mimmārôm yiqqāḥênî, "He reached down from on high and took hold of me") — the rescue is initiated entirely from above. Mārom (height, the high place) is God's position; from there he sends and takes (lāqaḥ: to take, seize, receive). מִמַּיִם רַבִּים (mimmaym rabbîm, "from deep waters") — the many/great waters as the chaos-force from which rescue is made; the same waters exposed in v15 are the peril from which the Psalmist is drawn. The image mirrors the infant Moses drawn from the Nile (māshāh, Exod 2:10 — "because I drew him out of the water").
18.17: מֵאֹיְבִי עָז (mêʾōyĕbî ʿāz, "from my powerful enemy") — ʿāz (strong, mighty): the enemy is not merely hostile but powerful, genuinely stronger than the Psalmist (v17b: "for they were stronger than I was"). The admission of inferiority makes the rescue more remarkable — not a story of triumph by superior David, but of weak David rescued by the omnipotent LORD.
18.18: יְקַדְּמוּנִי בְיוֹם-אֵידִי (yĕqaddĕmûnî bĕyôm-ʾêdî, "They confronted me in the day of my disaster") — qādham (piel): to meet/confront (with hostile intent). ʾÊd (calamity, disaster): the word for appointed doom/misfortune — the day when everything was against him. וַיְהִי יְהוָה לְמִשְׁעָן לִי (wayhî YHWH lĕmishʿān lî, "but the LORD was my support") — mishʿān: a staff to lean on, a prop. God as the physical support when the Psalmist could not stand on his own.
18.19: וַיֹּצִיאֵנִי לַמֶּרְחָב (wayyôṣîʾênî lammerhāb, "He brought me out into an open place") — the same spatial metaphor as Ps 4:1 (hirchabātā, "you enlarged me"): salvation as movement from the tight place (ṣar) to wide-open space (merehāb). כִּי חָפֵץ בִּי (kî ḥāpēṣ bî, "because he was pleased with me") — the reason for rescue: divine delight/desire (ḥāpēṣ). Not David's merit but the divine pleasure as the ground of rescue — the same word used in Ps 5:4 (God does not delight in wickedness) deployed in its positive form.
18.20: יִגְמְלֵנִי יְהוָה כְּצִדְקִי (yigmĕlênî YHWH kĕṣidqî, "The LORD rewarded me according to my righteousness") — the theme of righteous vindication: gāmal (to repay, to deal out according to). Ṣedeq (righteousness) and bōr yādāyw (cleanness of hands) are the covenantal integrity that David claims — not sinless perfection but covenant faithfulness. The specific righteousness claimed in vv20-24 is not absolute but relational: faithfulness to the covenant relationship.
18.21: שָׁמַרְתִּי דַּרְכֵי יְהוָה (shāmartî darkê YHWH, "I have kept to the LORD's ways") — shāmar: to keep, guard, observe (the same word used for the cherubim guarding the garden, Gen 3:24, and for keeping the commandments). The claim is of faithfulness to the covenantal path, not of personal superiority. לֹא-רָשַׁעְתִּי מֵאֱלֹהָי (lōʾ-rāshaʿtî mêʾĕlōhāy, "I have not turned away from my God in rebellion") — rāshaʿ: to act wickedly, to be in the wrong. The negative formulation: no deliberate departure.
18.22: כִּי כָּל-מִשְׁפָּטָיו לְנֶגְדִּי (kî kol-mishpāṭāyw lĕnegdî, "All his rules were set before me") — mishpāṭ (judgment/rule/ordinance) as the standard placed before the eyes. Lĕnegdî: before me, in my sight — the image of the covenantal instruction as a constant visual reference. חֻקֹּתָיו לֹא-אָסִיר מֶנִּי (ḥuqqōtāyw lōʾ-ʾāsîr mennî, "I did not set aside his statutes") — ḥōq (statute/ordinance) in contrast to mishpāṭ: the fixed decrees vs the case-by-case judgments.
18.23: וָאֶהְיֶה תָמִים עִמּוֹ (wāʾehyeh tāmîm ʿimmô, "I was blameless before him") — tāmîm: unblemished, whole, without defect — the same word used for the sacrificial animal that must be without blemish (Exod 12:5; Lev 1:3). The Psalmist claims to have been before God with the quality of a valid offering. וָאֶשְׁתַּמֵּר מֵעֲוֹנִי (wāʾeshtammêr mêʿăwōnî, "I kept myself from wrongdoing") — hithpael reflexive of shāmar: actively guarding oneself from one's own potential iniquity (ʿāwôn).
18.24: The repetition of v20's language: כְּצִדְקִי (kĕṣidqî, "according to my righteousness") and כְּבֹר יָדַי (kĕbōr yādāy, "according to the cleanness of my hands") in God's sight (לְנֶגֶד עֵינָיו). The claim of vv20-24 forms a unit bounded by these matching formulas, with the specific evidences of faithfulness in vv21-23 as the middle section.
18.25:
עִם-חָסִיד תִּתְחַסָּד (ʿim-ḥāsîd titḥassād, "To the faithful you show yourself faithful") — the divine mirror principle: each verb is a niphal or hithpael reflexive showing God "reflecting back" the character of the one approaching him. The pattern is: ḥāsîd (faithful/loyal one) → titḥassād (you act as the faithful/loyal one toward them). Three pairs follow:

v25: faithful/loyal (ḥāsîd → titḥassād) — v25b: blameless (tāmîm → tittammām) — v26a: pure (bārûr → tittabbār) — v26b: devious/crooked (ʿiqqêsh → tittappe.l, "you show yourself cunning/twisting"). The final pair is the reversal: with the crooked, God acts according to their crookedness — not by becoming unjust, but because justice to the unjust has the appearance of cunning from their perspective.
18.26: וְעִם-עִקֵּשׁ תִּתְפַּתָּל (wĕʿim-ʿiqqêsh tittapattāl, "but to the devious you show yourself cunning") — ʿiqqêsh: perverse, twisted (from ʿāqash, to twist). Tittapattāl (hithpael of pātal): to twist, wrestle, be twisted. The reciprocity is not that God becomes devious but that his consistency in pursuing justice "looks like" cunning or resistance to those who are crooked. The divine way is single; its appearance differs depending on the orientation of the one encountering it.
18.27: כִּי-אַתָּה עַם-עָנִי תוֹשִׁיעַ (kî-ʾattāh ʿam-ʿānî tôshîaʿ, "For you save an afflicted people") — ʿānî: poor, afflicted, humble — the recipient of divine salvation is specifically the one without resources or pretension. וְעֵינַיִם רָמוֹת תַּשְׁפִּיל (wĕʿênayim rāmôt tashpîl, "but proud eyes you humble") — rāmôt: raised, lifted up; shāpal (hiphil): to bring low. The reversal is consistent: the humble are lifted (v19: to the open place) and the proud are brought down.
18.28: כִּי-אַתָּה תָּאִיר נֵרִי (kî-ʾattāh tāʾîr nêrî, "you light my lamp") — nêr: lamp, light; ʾûr (hiphil: to cause to shine, to light). The lamp as the symbol of continuing life and divine favor (1 Kgs 11:36: "I will give him one tribe, so that my servant David may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem"). יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי יַגִּיהַּ חָשְׁכִּי (YHWH ʾĕlōhay yagîah ḥāshkî, "the LORD my God brightens my darkness") — nāgah (hiphil: to cause to shine). The darkness (danger, defeat, despair) becomes light through divine illumination — not the removal of the dark situation but the illumination within it.
18.29: כִּי בְךָ אָרֻץ גְּדוּד (kî bĕkā ʾāruṣ gĕdûd, "For by your help I can charge against a raiding party") — gĕdûd: a raiding band, a marauding troop. Rûṣ (to run, to charge). וּבֵאלֹהַי אֲדַלֶּג-שׁוּר (ûbêʾĕlōhay ʾădallēg-shûr, "by my God I can scale a wall") — dālag: to leap/spring; shûr: a wall or enemy stronghold. Both images contrast the Psalmist's own limitation with what becomes possible "in God" (bĕkā, bêʾĕlōhay) — the "in Christ" language of Paul has its OT template here.
18.30: הָאֵל תָּמִים דַּרְכּוֹ (hāʾêl tāmîm darkô, "This God — his way is perfect") — tāmîm (perfect/unblemished) applied now to God's way rather than David's conduct (v23). אִמְרַת יְהוָה צְרוּפָה (ʾimrat YHWH ṣĕrûpāh, "the LORD's word is refined and true") — ṣārap: to smelt, refine (as gold or silver is refined in fire to remove impurities). The divine word has undergone the refinement process — it is pure without alloy. Ps 12:6 uses the same image for the divine promises.
18.31: כִּי מִי אֱלוֹהַ מִבַּלְעֲדֵי יְהוָה (kî mî ʾĕlôah mibbaleʿădê YHWH, "For who is God besides the LORD?") — the rhetorical question presupposes monolatry at minimum: no other claimant to divinity has the qualities just described. מִי צוּר זוּלָתִי אֱלֹהֵינוּ (mî ṣûr zûlātî ʾĕlōhênû, "who is a rock except our God?") — ṣûr (rock as refuge and fortress): the test of a divine claim is whether the one claiming divinity can actually provide the stability and protection the rock-metaphor requires. The exclusive answer: only YHWH.
18.32: הָאֵל הַמְאַזְּרֵנִי חָיִל (hāʾêl hamĕʾazzĕrênî ḥāyil, "It is God who arms me with strength") — ʾāzar (piel: to gird, to clothe with a belt/girdle): the image of equipping for battle by fastening the belt/sash that holds the weapons. Ḥāyil: strength, valor, military might. God as the one who does the arming — the passive recipient of divine equipment. וַיִּתֵּן תָּמִים דַּרְכִּי (wayyittên tāmîm darkî, "and makes my way perfect") — tāmîm applied to the Psalmist's path: God completes or perfects what was incomplete.
18.33: מְשַׁוֶּה רַגְלַי כָּאַיָּלוֹת (mĕshawweh raglāy kāʾayyālôt, "he makes my feet like the feet of a deer") — shāwāh (piel: to make equal, to level, to set properly); ʾayyālāh (doe, hind — the nimble mountain deer). The feet of the deer that navigate rocky terrain without slipping are the image for divinely-given sure-footedness in difficult circumstances. עַל בָּמֹתַי יַעֲמִידֵנִי (ʿal bāmōtay yaʿămîdênî, "sets me securely on the heights") — bāmāh: high place; to stand on the heights is military advantage — commanding ground.
18.34: מְלַמֵּד יָדַי לַמִּלְחָמָה (mĕlammêd yādāy lammilḥāmāh, "he trains my hands for battle") — lāmad (piel: to teach, train — the same root as talmîd, disciple): God as the military trainer who teaches the hands their craft. וְנִחֲתָה קֶשֶׁת-נְחוּשָׁה זְרֹעֹתָי (wĕniḥătāh qeshet-nĕḥûshāh zĕrōʿōtāy, "my arms can bend a bow of bronze") — the bronze bow would normally exceed human strength; divine-given capacity enables what is naturally impossible.
18.35: וַתִּתֶּן-לִי מָגֵן יִשְׁעֶךָ (wattitten-lî māgên yishʿekā, "You have given me the shield of your salvation") — the shield is specifically "of your salvation" (yeshûʿâ): protective, yes, but the protection is the product of divine saving activity. וַעֲנָוַתְךָ תַרְבֵּנִי (waʿănāwātĕkā tarbênî, "your gentleness made me great") — the key term ʿănāwāh: condescension, humility, gentleness. It is the LORD's condescension (ʿānāw: humble/meek) toward the Psalmist that makes him great. This is the paradox at the heart of the psalm: greatness comes not from David's power but from divine condescension that stoops to lift.
18.36: תַּרְחִיב צַעֲדִי תַחְתָּי (tarḥîb ṣaʿădî taḥtāy, "You gave wide room for my steps beneath me") — rāḥab (hiphil: to widen/enlarge): the spatial metaphor of salvation as room to move, returning from Ps 4:1 and 18:19. Ṣaʿad (steps, stride) — a wide stride, confident movement. וְלֹא מָעֲדוּ קַרְסֻלָּי (wĕlōʾ māʿădû qarsullāy, "my feet did not slip") — māʿad: to slip, to totter; qarsôl: ankle. The ankle as the vulnerable joint of confident movement: divine enlargement of the path prevented the ankle-turning that a tight path would cause.
18.37: אֶרְדֹּף אוֹיְבַי וְאַשִּׂיגֵם (ʾerdōp ʾôyĕbay wĕʾassîgêm, "I pursued my enemies and overtook them") — the pursuit language reverses vv4-5: David was the hunted one, now he pursues. Rādap (to pursue, chase) and nāśag (to overtake, reach): the decisive engagement. וְלֹא-אָשׁוּב עַד-כַּלּוֹתָם (wĕlōʾ-āshûv ʿad-kallôtām, "and did not turn back until they were finished") — kālāh (to complete/finish/destroy): no premature withdrawal; the pursuit continues to completion.
18.38: אֶמְחָצֵם וְלֹא-יֻכְלוּ קוּם (ʾemḥāṣêm wĕlōʾ-yûklû qûm, "I struck them down so that they could not rise") — māḥaṣ: to shatter, to smite decisively. The inability to rise after being struck is the language of permanent defeat. יִפְּלוּ תַּחַת רַגְלָי (yippĕlû taḥat raglāy, "they fell under my feet") — the posture of complete submission: under the feet, the position of the vanquished before the victor (cf. Ps 110:1; Josh 10:24).
18.39: וַתְּאַזְּרֵנִי חַיִל לַמִּלְחָמָה (watĕʾazzĕrênî ḥayil lammilḥāmāh, "For you armed me with strength for the battle") — resuming the divine-equipping motif of v32; the battle's outcome is not David's achievement but the product of divine arming. תַּכְרִיעַ קָמַי תַּחְתָּי (takrîaʿ qāmay taḥtāy, "you brought down under me those who rose against me") — kāraʿ (hiphil: to cause to kneel/bow down): God as the one who brings the adversaries to their knees beneath David.
18.40: וְאֹיְבַי נָתַתָּה לִּי עֹרֶף (wĕʾōyĕbay nātattāh llî ʿōrep, "You made my enemies turn their backs in retreat") — ʿōrep (back of the neck): to show the back of the neck is to flee in retreat; the enemies are turned to expose their most vulnerable position. וּמְשַׂנְאַי אַצְמִיתֵם (ûmĕsanʾay ʾaṣmîtêm, "I destroyed those who hated me") — ṣāmat: to silence, destroy, cut off (rare word; the hiphil indicates causing to be silenced).
18.41: יְשַׁוְּעוּ וְאֵין-מוֹשִׁיעַ (yĕshawwĕʿû wĕʾên-môshîaʿ, "They cried for help, but there was no one to save them") — the enemies' cry reverses David's: he cried (v6) and was heard; they cry and find no môshîaʿ (savior/deliverer). עַל-יְהוָה וְלֹא עָנָם (ʿal-YHWH wĕlōʾ ʿānām, "they even cried to the LORD, but he did not answer them") — the contrast is sharp: the same LORD who heard David's cry (v6) did not answer their cry. Not arbitrary: they were the enemies of his anointed and had no relational claim on his response.
18.42: וְאֶשְׁחָקֵם כְּעָפָר עַל-פְּנֵי-רוּחַ (wĕʾeshḥāqêm kĕʿāpār ʿal-pĕnê-rûaḥ, "I ground them fine as windblown dust") — shāḥaq: to grind, to pulverize (the same root as fine dust). Kĕʿāpār ʿal-pĕnê-rûaḥ: "like dust on the face of the wind." The contrast with v3 (chaff blown by the wind in Ps 1) is instructive: the enemies become the chaff, scattered by the wind of divine judgment.
18.43: תְּפַלְּטֵנִי מֵרִיבֵי עָם (tĕpalĕṭênî mêrîbê ʿām, "You saved me from conflicts with my own people") — rîb: legal dispute, quarrel; the enemies within the people, not only foreign nations. תְּשִׂימֵנִי לְרֹאשׁ גּוֹיִם (tĕsîmênî lĕrōʾsh gôyim, "you made me head of the nations") — the scope of David's kingship extends to the nations; the language anticipates the royal universalism of Ps 2:8. Paul cites v49 in this context as the ground for Gentile worship.
18.44: לִשְׁמוֹעַ אֹזֶן יִשָּׁמְעוּ-לִי (lishmoaʿ ʾōzen yishshāmĕʿû-lî, "As soon as they heard of me, they obeyed") — literally "at the hearing of the ear they obeyed me." Shāmaʿ appears three times in this phrase, emphasizing the immediacy and totality of the submission. בְּנֵי-נֵכָר יְכַחֲשׁוּ-לִי (bĕnê-nêkār yĕkaḥăshû-lî, "foreigners submitted to me") — kāḥash: to fail, deny, cringe/submit reluctantly; the foreigners' submission is not voluntary homage but the submission of the defeated.
18.45: בְּנֵי-נֵכָר יִבֹּלוּ (bĕnê-nêkār yibbōlû, "Foreigners lost their courage") — nābēl: to wither, to fade (as a leaf withers); the courage of the foreign peoples wilted. וְיַחְגְּרוּ מִמִּסְגְּרוֹתָם (wĕyaḥgĕrû mimmisĕgrôtām, "came out of their fortresses trembling") — ḥāgar: to gird oneself (for movement or battle); misegĕrôt: strongholds, locked places. The irony: they gird themselves to emerge from their fortresses — not in bold battle-readiness but in trembling, forced submission.
18.46: חַי-יְהוָה וּבָרוּךְ צוּרִי (ḥay-YHWH ûbārûk ṣûrî, "The LORD lives! Blessed be my Rock!") — ḥay YHWH: "As the LORD lives!" — an oath formula certifying that what follows is true. But here it also functions as a declaration of divine life in contrast to the dead idols who cannot hear or respond (contrast v41). Bārûk ṣûrî: formal blessing directed toward God, now using bārûk (the formal blessing-word) rather than ʾashrê. וְיָרוּם אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׁעִי (wĕyārûm ʾĕlōhê yishʿî, "Exalted be the God of my salvation") — a wish or declaration: may the God of my salvation be lifted high.
18.47: הָאֵל הַנֹּתֵן נְקָמוֹת לִי (hāʾêl hannōtên nĕqāmôt lî, "the God who gives me justice") — nĕqāmāh: vengeance, vindication; the MKT renders it "justice" to capture the sense of setting right what was wrong. וַיַּדְבֵּר עַמִּים תַּחְתָּי (wayyaddêr ʿammîm taḥtāy, "subdues peoples under me") — dābar (hiphil?): actually this should be wĕyaddêr from dābar meaning to subdue/bring low; the peoples placed under him.
18.48: מְפַלְּטִי מֵאֹיְבָי (mĕpallĕṭî mêʾōyĕbāy, "who rescued me from my enemies") — the same root pālaṭ from v2 (mĕphalṭî): rescuer. The psalm circles back to the opening cluster of rescue metaphors. אַף מִן-קָמַי תְּרוֹמְמֵנִי (ʾap min-qāmay tĕrômĕmênî, "you lifted me above those who rose against me") — rûm (hiphil: to lift up, exalt): the divine lifting is above those who tried to bring down.
18.49: עַל-כֵּן אוֹדְךָ בַגּוֹיִם יְהוָה (ʿal-kên ʾôdĕkā baggôyim YHWH, "Therefore I will give you thanks, O LORD, among the nations") — the climactic thanksgiving. בַגּוֹיִם (baggôyim): "among the nations/Gentiles" — the Davidic king's testimony extends beyond Israel to the watching Gentile nations. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 15:9 as a proof text that the Scriptures anticipated Gentile worship: "I will praise you among the Gentiles." The Psalm's historical statement (David's international testimony) becomes the NT's foundation for Gentile inclusion in the praise of God.
18.50: מַגְדִּיל יְשׁוּעוֹת מַלְכּוֹ (magdîl yĕshûʿôt malkô, "He gives great salvation to his king") — gādal (hiphil participle: causing to be great, magnifying): ongoing activity of magnifying salvations. The plural yĕshûʿôt (salvations) suggests multiple repeated rescues. וְעֹשֶׂה-חֶסֶד לִמְשִׁיחוֹ לְדָוִד וּלְזַרְעוֹ עַד-עוֹלָם (wĕʿōseh-ḥesed limshîḥô lĕdāwid ûlĕzarʿô ʿad-ʿôlām, "shows steadfast love to his anointed — to David and his descendants forever") — the ḥesed (covenant loyalty) promised to the māshîaḥ (anointed) extends forever (ʿad-ʿôlām). The psalm closes on the covenant: the Davidic line as the recipient of perpetual divine ḥesed, a promise that points beyond historical David to the eternal Anointed One.

19.1: הַשָּׁמַיִם מְסַפְּרִים כְּבוֹד-אֵל (hashāmayim mĕsappĕrîm kĕbôd-ʾêl, "The heavens declare the glory of God") — the participle mĕsappĕrîm (from sāpar: to count, recount, narrate): an ongoing, unceasing declaration. The heavens are not said to have declared or to eventually declare — they are perpetually in the act of narrating. וּמַעֲשֵׂה יָדָיו מַגִּיד הָרָקִיעַ (ûmaʿăśê yādāyw maggîd hārāqîaʿ, "the expanse proclaims his handiwork") — rāqîaʿ: the firmament/expanse (from rāqaʿ, to hammer/beat out thin). The beaten-out sky continually announces (participle) the work of God's hands.
19.2: יוֹם לְיוֹם יַבִּיעַ אֹמֶר (yôm lĕyôm yabbiʿa ʾōmer, "Day after day pours out its speech") — nābaʿ (hiphil: to pour out, to bubble up): the image of water welling up from a spring — speech as inexhaustible, effervescent outpouring. ʾŌmer: speech, word (a poetic word). וְלַיְלָה לְּלַיְלָה יְחַוֶּה-דָּעַת (wĕlaylāh llĕlaylāh yĕḥawweh-dāʿat, "and night after night reveals knowledge") — ḥāwāh (piel: to show, to tell, to reveal): night declaring what day cannot. The entire calendar — every day, every night — participates in the continuous revelation.
19.3: אֵין-אֹמֶר וְאֵין דְּבָרִים (ʾên-ʾōmer wĕʾên dĕbārîm, "There is no speech, there are no words") — the paradox that defines the whole passage: the declaration of vv1-2 is made in no human language. Bĕlî nishmaʿ qôlām: "without their voice being heard." The communication is real but without the normal medium of communication. This is general revelation: information without human words, encounter without verbal address.
19.4: בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ יָצָא קַוָּם (bĕkol-hāʾāreṣ yāṣāʾ qawwām, "Yet their proclamation goes out through all the earth") — qaw: a measuring line, a cord; by extension, a line of text or a voice-line. The LXX translates it φθόγγος (sound/voice), which Paul uses in Rom 10:18: "their voice has gone out into all the earth." The silent proclamation nevertheless "goes out" (yāṣāʾ: to go forth, exit) to all the earth. הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ שָׂם אֹהֶל בָּהֶם (hashemesh śām ʾōhel bāhem, "In the heavens he has pitched a tent for the sun") — at v4b the Psalm turns to the sun as the supreme example of the heavens' declaration.
19.5: וְהוּא כְּחָתָן יֹצֵא מֵחֻפָּתוֹ (wĕhûʾ kĕḥātān yōṣêʾ mêḥuppātô, "which rises like a bridegroom leaving his chamber") — ḥātān: bridegroom; ḥuppāh: the bridal chamber. The sun rising is like a bridegroom emerging from the wedding chamber — full of energy, beauty, and anticipation. יָשִׂישׂ כְּגִבּוֹר לָרוּץ אֹרַח (yāśîś kĕgibbôr lārûṣ ʾōraḥ, "rejoices like a champion eager to run his course") — gibbôr: hero, mighty man; rûṣ: to run. The sun as an eager athlete who cannot wait to begin his course — joy and strength combined.
19.6: מִקְצֵה הַשָּׁמַיִם מוֹצָאוֹ (miqṣeh hashāmayim môṣāʾô, "Its rising is from one end of the heavens") — the full arc of the sun's path described in spatial terms. וְאֵין נִסְתָּר מֵחַמָּתוֹ (wĕʾên nistār mêḥammātô, "nothing is hidden from its heat") — nistār (niphal participle: hidden, concealed); ḥammāh: heat, warmth. The universal reach of the sun's heat prepares the transition to vv7-9: just as the sun reaches everywhere, so the Torah of the LORD illuminates everything.
19.7: תּוֹרַת יְהוָה תְּמִימָה מְשִׁיבַת נָפֶשׁ (tôrat YHWH tĕmîmāh mĕshîbat nāpesh, "The instruction of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul") — the first of six attribute + effect pairs describing the divine word. Tôrāh (instruction, as in Ps 1:2); tĕmîmāh (perfect, unblemished — the sacrificial adjective now applied to Torah: it is the word without flaw). Mĕshîbat nāpesh: "restoring/reviving the soul" — shûb (to return/restore): the instruction brings back what has wandered or declined. The image of the word as nourishment that restores strength.
19.8: פִּקּוּדֵי יְהוָה יְשָׁרִים (piqqûdê YHWH yĕshārîm, "The precepts of the LORD are right") — piqqûd: what is entrusted for oversight, a commanded charge (plural piqqûdîm). Yāshar (right, straight): the precepts go in a straight line, without deviation. Mĕsamĕḥê lêb: "making the heart rejoice" — the straight path produces the joy of knowing one's feet are rightly placed. מִצְוַת יְהוָה בָּרָה (miṣwat YHWH bārāh, "the commandment of the LORD is radiant") — bārāh: pure, bright, clear; mĕʾîrat ʿênāyim: "enlightening the eyes." The singular miṣwāh (commandment) as the totality of divine command — clear and eye-opening.
19.9: יִרְאַת יְהוָה טְהוֹרָה (yirʾat YHWH ṭĕhôrāh, "The fear of the LORD is pure") — the sixth term in the series is not a document (tôrāh, ʿêdût, piqqûdîm, miṣwat, mishpĕṭê) but an attitude: the fear of the LORD itself. Ṭāhôr: ritually and morally clean, pure (the priestly purity vocabulary). ʿOmedet lāʿad: "enduring forever" — the fear of the LORD is not a passing emotion but the stable orientation that persists. מִשְׁפְּטֵי-יְהוָה אֱמֶת (mishpĕṭê-YHWH ʾĕmet, "the judgments of the LORD are true") — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness, reliability): the judgments correspond to reality.
19.10: הַנֶּחֱמָדִים מִזָּהָב (hanneḥĕmādîm mizzāhāb, "They are more desirable than gold") — nāḥmad (niphal participle: desired, coveted — from the same root as the tenth commandment's "do not covet"). The divine words are desirable in the same way that gold and honey are desirable. וּמֶתֶק מִדְּבַשׁ וְנֹפֶת צוּפִים (ûmeteq mĕkdbash wĕnōpet ṣûpîm, "sweeter than honey, than honey dripping from the comb") — nōpet: the dripping of a honeycomb, the finest honey; ṣûpîm: flowing, dripping honeycombs. The sweetness is the most refined and abundant form available.
19.11: גַּם-עַבְדְּךָ נִזְהָר בָּהֶם (gam-ʿabdĕkā nizhar bāhem, "your servant is warned by them") — zāhar (niphal: to be warned, to be enlightened by a warning light — from a root meaning to shine): warned as one is warned by a light that reveals a danger ahead. בְּשָׁמְרָם עֵקֶב רָב (bĕshomrām ʿêqeb rāb, "in keeping them there is great reward") — ʿêqeb: consequence, result, reward (literally "at the heel" — what follows behind). Keeping the word has a heel-trail of great reward.
19.12: שְׁגִיאוֹת מִי-יָבִין (shĕgîʾôt mî-yābîn, "Who can understand his own errors?") — shĕgîʾāh: an error, a mistake, a wandering from the path (from shāgāh: to wander/err; the same root as shiggāyôn in Ps 7:1). The rhetorical question implies the answer: no one fully understands their own errors. מִנִּסְתָּרוֹת נַקֵּנִי (minnestārôt naqqênî, "Cleanse me from sins I cannot see") — nistarôt: hidden things, secret faults — not morally hidden sins (deliberate concealment) but sins that are invisible to the sinner because of their own moral blind spots. Nāqāh (piel: to cleanse, acquit).
19.13: גַּם מִזֵּדִים חֲשֹׂךְ עַבְדֶּךָ (gam mizzêdîm ḥăśōk ʿabdekā, "Keep your servant also from willful sins") — zêdîm: the arrogant, presumptuous ones; from zûd (to boil up, to be insolent). The contrast with nistarôt is crucial: v12 covers unconscious errors; v13 covers deliberate, willful transgressions. This exactly maps onto the Num 15 categories of bishgāgāh (by mistake) vs bĕyad rāmāh (with a high/defiant hand) sin — the former has a sacrificial remedy; the latter does not. אָז אֵיתָם (ʾāz ʾêtam, "Then I will be blameless") — "then" (ʾāz) indicates the consequence: kept from willful sin, the Psalmist would attain the blamelessness of Ps 18:23.
19.14: יִהְיוּ לְרָצוֹן אִמְרֵי-פִי (yihyû lĕrāṣôn ʾimrê-pî, "May the words of my mouth be pleasing in your sight") — rāṣôn: pleasure, delight, acceptance (the same root as rāṣāh used in Ps 18:19 — "he was pleased with me"). The prayer is that speech and meditation (hĕgyôn libbî — the inward hāgāh murmuring of Ps 1:2) be acceptable before God. צוּרִי וְגֹאֲלִי (ṣûrî wĕgōʾălî, "my rock and my redeemer") — the two closing titles: ṣûr (rock, as in Ps 18) and gōʾêl (kinsman-redeemer — the one who has the legal right and relational obligation to buy back what has been lost). The psalm closes with a prayer to the one who is both immovable foundation and closest redeeming kinsman.

20.1: יַעַנְךָ יְהוָה בְּיוֹם צָרָה (yaʿankā YHWH bĕyôm ṣārāh, "May the LORD answer you in your day of trouble!") — the jussive yaʿankā (may he answer you) opens a series of blessings for the king before battle. The congregation speaks in the third person about the king and the LORD. Yôm ṣārāh: the day of tight distress (same root as ṣar, Ps 4:1; 18:6). יְשַׂגֶּבְךָ שֵׁם אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (yĕśaggĕbkā shêm ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb, "May the name of the God of Jacob set you on high!") — śāgab (piel: to set on high, to make inaccessible/safe): the name of the God of Jacob as the one who lifts up to safety. The title "God of Jacob" invokes the patriarchal covenant.
20.2: יִשְׁלַח-עֶזְרְךָ מִקֹּדֶשׁ (yishlah-ʿezrĕkā miqqōdesh, "May he send you help from his sanctuary") — the divine help is dispatched (shālaḥ: to send, to release) from the sanctuary (qōdesh): the heavenly temple, the source of all aid. וּמִצִּיּוֹן יִסְעָדֶךָּ (ûmîṣṣiyyôn yisʿādekā, "support you from Zion!") — sāʿad: to support, sustain (as one supports another who is about to fall); Zion as the earthly location of divine presence.
20.3: יִזְכֹּר כָּל-מִנְחֹתֶיךָ (yizkōr kol-minḥōtêkā, "May he remember all your offerings") — zākar: to remember with active concern, to call to mind and act on. The minḥāh (grain offering) and ʿôlāh (burnt offering — what goes up in smoke entirely) as the worshiping acts that precede battle. The prayer is that God accepts and responds to these acts of worship. Selāh marks the pause after the first unit of blessing.
20.4: יִתֶּן-לְךָ כִלְבָבֶךָ (yittĕn-lĕkā kilbābĕkā, "May he grant you your heart's desire") — the prayer for divine alignment with the king's desires — not a blank check but the prayer that the king's desires are already aligned with God's will (implied by the preceding religious context). וְכָל-עֲצָתְךָ יְמַלֵּא (wĕkol-ʿăṣātĕkā yĕmallêʾ, "and fulfill all your plans!") — ʿēṣāh: counsel, plan, strategy. Mālêʾ (piel: to fill, to fulfill, to bring to completion).
20.5: נְרַנְּנָה בִּישׁוּעָתֶךָ (nĕrannĕnāh bîshûʿātekā, "May we shout for joy at your victory") — rānan: to shout, to ring out (a ringing cry of joy or lamentation — context determines which); here the shout of victory. The congregation anticipates celebrating the king's salvation as their own. וּבְשֵׁם-אֱלֹהֵינוּ נִדְגֹּל (ûbĕshêm-ʾĕlōhênû nidgōl, "in the name of our God raise our banners!") — dāgal: to raise a banner/standard; the military banner as the visible rallying point. Raising banners in God's name rather than in the king's name or the nation's.
20.6: עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי הוֹשִׁיעַ יְהוָה מְשִׁיחוֹ (ʿattāh yādaʿtî kî hôshîaʿ YHWH mĕshîḥô, "Now I know that the LORD saves his anointed one") — the singular first person breaks in: "now I know." Whether a priest, prophet, or the king himself speaking, this is a declaration of certainty. Yādaʿ (to know): experiential, relational knowing. יַעֲנֵהוּ מִשְּׁמֵי קָדְשׁוֹ (yaʿănêhû mishshĕmê qodshô, "he answers him from his holy heaven") — the answer comes from the holy heavens (shĕmê qodshô: heavens of his holiness), not from earthly resources. Bighĕbûrôt yêshaʿ yĕmînô: "by the saving power (gĕbûrôt, mighty acts/power) of his right hand."
