20.7: אֵלֶּה בָרֶכֶב וְאֵלֶּה בַסּוּסִים (ʾêlleh bārekeb wĕʾêlleh bassûsîm, "Some boast in chariots and some in horses") — the contrast is absolute: the military establishment of Israel's neighbors depends on chariot-forces and cavalry (the dominant military technology of the ancient Near East). וַאֲנַחְנוּ בְּשֵׁם-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ נַזְכִּיר (waʾănaḥnû bĕshêm-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû nazkîr, "but we will boast in the name of the LORD our God") — zākar (hiphil: to make remembered, to invoke, to call to mind): not merely "mention" but actively invoke the name as the basis of confidence.
20.8: הֵמָּה כָּרְעוּ וְנָפָלוּ (hêmmāh kāreʿû wĕnāpālû, "They collapse and fall") — the confident perfect tense of certainty: it is as if already accomplished. Those who trust chariots (kāraʿ: bow, bend the knee — in defeat; nāpal: fall) will fail. וַאֲנַחְנוּ קַּמְנוּ וַנִּתְעוֹדָד (waʾănaḥnû qamnû wannitʿôdād, "but we rise and stand firm") — qûm (to rise) and ʿûd (hithpael: to strengthen oneself, to rally): those who trust in the divine name rise and are strengthened.
20.9: יְהוָה הוֹשִׁיעָה (YHWH hôshîʿāh, "LORD, give victory to the king!") — the hiphil imperative of yāshaʿ: save! The abrupt imperative after vv6-8's confident declarations is the cry that gives voice to the underlying dependence. The psalm ends as a prayer rather than a triumphant statement: confidence is founded on divine saving, so the final word is a request for exactly that. הַמֶּלֶךְ יַעֲנֵנוּ בְיוֹם-קָרְאֵנוּ (hammelekh yaʿănênû bĕyôm-qārĕʾênû, "Answer us when we call") — the MT is difficult: "the King will answer us in the day we call" — may be a statement of confidence rather than a petition, reading yaʿănênû as a declarative rather than a jussive.

21.1: יִשְׂמַח-מֶלֶךְ בְּעֻזְּךָ יְהוָה (yismah-melekh bĕʿuzzĕkā YHWH, "The king rejoices in your strength, LORD") — the royal psalm of thanksgiving following the victory anticipated in Ps 20. The king's rejoicing is grounded in ʿōz (strength): the same word as Ps 18:1 (ḥizqî, "my strength") but now the divine strength specifically. וּבִישׁוּעָתְךָ מַה-יָּגִיל מְאֹד (ûbîshûʿātĕkā māh-yāgîl mĕʾōd, "in your salvation how greatly he exults!") — gîl: intense, even physical rejoicing (Ps 19:5 used it of the sun's running); mĕʾōd: greatly, exceedingly.
21.2: תַּאֲוַת לִבּוֹ נָתַתָּה לּוֹ (taʾăwat libbô nātattāh llô, "You have given him his heart's desire") — the same concept prayed for in Ps 20:4 is now reported as granted. Taʾăwāh: desire, longing — the deep wanting of the heart. Nātan (perfect: given, completed act). וַאֲרֶשֶׁת שְׂפָתָיו בַּל-מָנַעְתָּ (waʾăreshet śĕpātāyw bal-mānaʿtā, "have not withheld what his lips requested") — ʾăreshet: request, what the lips petition. Mānaʿ (to withhold, deny): not withheld. The paired statement: given what he desired, not withheld what he requested. Selāh.
21.3: כִּי-תְקַדְּמֶנּוּ בִּרְכוֹת טוֹב (kî-tĕqaddĕmennû birĕkôt ṭôb, "For you meet him with blessings of goodness") — qādam (piel: to come before, to meet in advance): God goes ahead of the king and meets him with blessings. Birĕkôt ṭôb: blessings of goodness/prosperity — not a single blessing but multiple. תָּשִׁית לְרֹאשׁוֹ עֲטֶרֶת פָּז (tāshît lĕrōʾshô ʿăṭeret paz, "you set a crown of finest gold upon his head") — ʿăṭāret: a crown, a wreath of honor; paz: refined gold, the purest gold. The divine coronation: God himself places the finest crown on the king's head, not the human coronation ceremony alone.
21.4: חַיִּים שָׁאַל מִמְּךָ נָתַתָּה לּוֹ (ḥayyîm shāʾal mimmĕkā nātattāh llô, "He asked you for life, and you gave it to him") — the king's petition for life (ḥayyîm, plural of fullness) was answered. אֹרֶךְ יָמִים עוֹלָם וָעֶד (ʾōrek yāmîm ʿôlām wāʿed, "length of days forever and ever") — the phrase ʿôlām wāʿed: forever and continuing — the most emphatic temporal expression. For a historical king, this is the prayer for dynasty; for the Messianic king, it is literal eternal life. Heb 1:8 cites the parallel Ps 45:6 ("your throne, O God, will last forever and ever") for Christ; Ps 21:4's language functions in the same register for NT readers.
21.5: גָּדוֹל כְּבוֹדוֹ בִּישׁוּעָתֶךָ (gādôl kĕbôdô bîshûʿātekā, "Through your salvation his glory is great") — the king's glory (kābôd: weight, honor, splendor) is great specifically "in your salvation" — the salvation is the source of the glory, not the king's own achievement. הוֹד-וְהָדָר תְּשַׁוֶּה עָלָיו (hôd-wĕhādār tĕshawweh ʿālāyw, "you have bestowed splendor and majesty upon him") — hôd (splendor, the divine majesty vocabulary of Ps 8:1) and hādār (majesty, beauty) — the two terms of divine glory now conferred on the human king. The transfer of divine glory-vocabulary to the royal figure is one of the foundations of Messianic expectation.
21.6: כִּי-תְשִׁיתֵהוּ בְרָכוֹת לָעַד (kî-tĕshîtêhû bĕrākôt lāʿad, "For you make him most blessed forever") — shît (to place, set, make): God makes the king into a source of blessing (bĕrākôt: blessings, the formal benedictions). Lāʿad: for eternity, perpetually. תְּחַדֵּהוּ בְשִׂמְחָה אֶת-פָּנֶיךָ (tĕḥaddêhû bĕśimḥāh ʾet-pānêkā, "you fill him with gladness in your presence") — ḥādāh (piel: to make glad, to fill with joy); pānêkā: your face/presence. Joy in the divine presence as the supreme royal blessing.
21.7: כִּי-הַמֶּלֶךְ בֹּטֵחַ בַּיהוָה (kî-hammelekh bōṭêaḥ baYHWH, "For the king trusts in the LORD") — bāṭaḥ: to trust, to feel secure (the physical sense of leaning against something solid). The participle: ongoing, habitual trust. וּבְחֶסֶד עֶלְיוֹן בַּל-יִמּוֹט (ûbĕḥesed ʿelyôn bal-yimmôṭ, "through the steadfast love of the Most High he will not be shaken") — ḥesed ʿElyôn: the covenant loyalty of the Most High (the highest divine title). Māṭ (niphal: to totter, to be shaken): the king who rests in divine ḥesed cannot be toppled. Ps 125:1 used the same image for the immovable mountain of Zion.
21.8: תִּמְצָא יָדְךָ לְכָל-אֹיְבֶיךָ (timṣāʾ yādĕkā lĕkol-ʾōyĕbêkā, "Your hand will reach all your enemies") — the address shifts to the LORD (second person): "your hand." The hand as the instrument of divine power that extends to find and reach every enemy. יְמִינְךָ תִּמְצָא שֹׂנְאֶיךָ (yĕmînĕkā timṣāʾ śōnĕʾêkā, "your right hand will seize those who hate you") — the right hand (yāmîn): the hand of power and authority (Ps 18:35: the right hand held the Psalmist up). Here it is the right hand that finds the enemy — the divine arm extended in judgment.
21.9: תְּשִׁיתֵמוֹ כְּתַנּוּר אֵשׁ (tĕshîtêmô kĕtannûr ʾêsh, "You will make them like a burning oven") — tannûr: an oven/furnace (used for baking bread; also for smelting). The enemies as fuel for an oven at the time of the LORD's appearance (lĕʿêt pānêkā: "in the time of your face/presence"). יְהוָה בְּאַפּוֹ יְבַלְּעֵם (YHWH bĕʾappô yĕballĕʿêm, "the LORD in his wrath will swallow them up") — bālaʿ (piel: to swallow, to engulf): total destruction by being consumed. ʾAp (nostril/anger): the burning anger of Ps 18:8.
21.10: פִּרְיָמוֹ מֵאֶרֶץ תְּאַבֵּד (piryāmô mêʾereṣ tĕʾabbêd, "You will destroy their offspring from the earth") — pĕrî: fruit, offspring; ʾābad (piel: to destroy, to cause to perish). The destruction is total and extends to the next generation. וְזַרְעָם מִבְּנֵי אָדָם (wĕzarʿām mibbĕnê ʾādām, "their descendants from among mankind") — zeraʿ: seed, offspring, descendants. Both terms (offspring and seed) cover the complete lineage.
21.11: כִּי-נָטוּ עָלֶיךָ רָעָה (kî-nāṭû ʿālêkā rāʿāh, "For they intended evil against you") — the reason for judgment: their intent (nāṭāh: to incline, to aim) was evil directed at the LORD himself, not merely at the king. The king's enemies are by that fact enemies of the LORD — the Davidic covenant means that attacking the king is attacking God's anointed. חָשְׁבוּ מְזִמָּה בַּל-יוּכָלוּ (ḥāshĕbû mĕzimmāh bal-yûkālû, "devised a plot they cannot carry out") — zāmam: to devise, plan (often with evil intent); mĕzimmāh: scheme/device. Their scheming cannot succeed.
21.12: כִּי-תְשִׁיתֵמוֹ שֶׁכֶם (kî-tĕshîtêmô shekem, "You will put them to flight") — shekem: shoulder/back (here used idiomatically for putting someone to retreat — "turning the shoulder/back"). בְּמֵיתָרֶיךָ תְּכוֹנֵן עַל-פְּנֵיהֶם (bĕmêtārêkā tĕkônên ʿal-pĕnêhem, "as you aim your bow at their faces") — mêtār: a bow-string; the image of aiming the bow directly at their faces.
21.13: רוּמָה יְהוָה בְּעֻזֶּךָ (rûmāh YHWH bĕʿuzzekā, "Rise in your strength, O LORD!") — the psalm ends with a call and a response. Rûm (hiphil imperative: to lift yourself up, to be exalted) echoing Ps 3:7 (qûmāh YHWH) and Ps 7:6 (qûmāh YHWH). נָשִׁירָה וּנְזַמְּרָה גְּבוּרָתֶךָ (nāshîrāh ûnĕzammĕrāh gĕbûrātekā, "We will sing and praise your mighty deeds") — shîr (to sing) and zāmar (to sing with instrumental accompaniment — the word from which mizmôr comes): the full liturgical response of the congregation to the divine strength. The psalm closes the pre-battle liturgy cycle (Ps 20-21) with a vow to sing after the victory God provides.

23.1: YHWH rōʿî lōʾ ʾeḥsār (YHWH rōʿî lōʾ ʾeḥsār): 'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.' The opening declaration YHWH rōʿî ('the LORD is my shepherd') is the psalm's entire theology in three words: divine identity (YHWH), relational role (rōʿeh/'shepherd'), and personal claim (rōʿî/'my shepherd'). The shepherd metaphor (rāʿâ) in the ANE was applied to kings — the king as shepherd of his people — but here inverted: the divine King functions as the personal shepherd of the individual. The result: lōʾ ʾeḥsār ('I shall not want/lack') — a permanent negation of deprivation. Jesus applies this psalm to himself in John 10:11, 14: 'I am the good shepherd.'
23.2: binʾôt dešeʾ yarbîṣēnî (ḇinʾôṯ dešeʾ yarḇîṣēnî): 'He makes me lie down in green pastures.' The hiphil yarbîṣēnî ('he makes me lie down') is causative: the lying down is not self-initiated but given by the shepherd. The phrase binʾôt dešeʾ ('in green/tender grass pastures') — the nāʾweh ('beautiful pasture') lush with vegetation — captures provision. The second image, ʿal-mê mĕnûḥôt yĕnahălēnî, 'he leads me beside still waters,' uses the hiphil nāhal ('to lead to a watering place') — a term specifically for leading animals to water. The mê mĕnûḥôt ('waters of stillness/rest') are not turbulent; they are the calm pools from which sheep can drink without fear.
23.3: napšî yĕšôbēb (napšî yĕšôḇēḇ): 'He restores my soul.' The polel yĕšôbēb (from šûb, 'to turn/return') means 'he causes to return, restores.' The soul (nepeš) exhausted or diverted is brought back to its proper orientation by the shepherd. The phrase yanhēnî bĕmaʿgĕlê ṣedeq, 'he leads me in paths of righteousness,' uses maʿgāl ('track, well-worn path') — the grooves worn by repeated passage through correct routes. The decisive qualifier: lĕmaʿan šĕmô ('for his name's sake') — the shepherd's guidance serves not primarily the sheep's comfort but the divine reputation. YHWH leads rightly because his name is at stake.
23.4: gam kî ʾēlēk bĕgê ṣalmāwet lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ (ḡam kî ʾēlēk bĕḡê ṣalmāwet lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ): 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil.' The phrase gê ṣalmāwet ('valley of deep darkness/shadow of death') — from ṣalmāwet ('deep darkness, death-shadow') — describes the most terrifying terrain. The ḡam kî ('even though, even if') is concessive: the peril is real, not imaginary. The consequent: lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ ('I will not fear evil') — complete fearlessness grounded in divine presence: kî ʾattâ ʿimmādî, 'for you are with me.' The instruments šibṭĕkā ûmišʿantekā, 'your rod and your staff,' are the shepherd's tools — the šēbeṭ for protection against predators, the mišʿenet for guiding sheep.
23.5: taʿărōk lĕpānay šûlḥān neged ṣōrĕrāy (taʿărōḵ lĕp̄ānay šûlḥān negeḏ ṣōrĕrāy): 'You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies.' The shift in imagery from shepherd/sheep to host/guest is striking — v.5 enters the realm of banquet hospitality. The table (šulḥān) set before (lĕpānay) the psalmist neged ṣōrĕrāy ('in the sight of my adversaries') is a public act: the enemies witness the psalmist being honored. The verb diššantā bĕšemen rōʾšî, 'you anoint my head with oil,' describes the honored guest receiving perfumed oil at a feast. The concluding kôsî rĕwāyâ, 'my cup overflows,' uses the niphal of rāwâ ('to be saturated, overflow') — abundance beyond capacity.
23.6: ʾak ṭôb wāḥesed yirdĕpûnî kol-yĕmê ḥayyāy (ʾaḵ ṭôḇ wāḥeseḏ yirḏĕp̄ûnî ḵol-yĕmê ḥayyāy): 'Surely goodness and steadfast love shall follow me all the days of my life.' The particle ʾak ('surely, only') is emphatic certainty. The verb rādap ('pursue, follow hotly') — normally used of enemies pursuing — is here applied to ṭôb wāḥesed ('goodness and steadfast love'): divine blessings as relentless pursuers who will not allow the psalmist to escape them. The final phrase wĕšabttî bĕbêt YHWH lĕʾōrek yāmîm, 'and I shall dwell in the house of the LORD forever,' uses šûb ('return, dwell') — the psalmist's permanent orientation is toward the divine dwelling. The psalm ends where theology must end: in the house of the shepherd-king.

24.1: laYHWH hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh tēbēl wĕyōšĕbê bāh (laYHWH hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh tēḇēl wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh): 'The earth is the LORD's and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell in it.' The psalm opens with a creation-sovereignty declaration that Paul cites in 1 Cor 10:26 regarding the lawfulness of eating food offered to idols — if the earth belongs to YHWH, no corner of it is excluded from his lordship. The double statement — hāʾāreṣ ûmĕlōʾāh ('the earth and its fullness') and tēbēl wĕyōšĕbê bāh ('the world and those who dwell in it') — encompasses both the land and its inhabitants. The definite laYHWH ('to/belonging to the LORD') is exclusive ownership.
24.2: kî hûʾ ʿal-yammîm yĕsādāh (kî hûʾ ʿal-yammîm yĕsāḏāh): 'for he has founded it upon the seas.' The verb yāsad (piel, 'to found, establish') describes YHWH as creator and sustainer of the cosmic order. The phrase ʿal-yammîm yĕsādāh wĕʿal-nĕhārôt yĕkônĕnehā, 'he has established it upon the rivers,' draws on the ancient cosmology of the earth founded above primordial waters (Gen 1:9-10; Ps 136:6). The founding of the earth on the seas is the ground of YHWH's ownership: the creator owns the creation. This cosmological foundation makes the entrance-liturgy of vv.3-10 coherent — only the creator's anointed can stand on this holy mountain.
24.3: mî yāʿăleh bĕhar YHWH ûmî yāqûm bimqôm qodšô (mî yāʿăleh ḇĕhar YHWH ûmî yāqûm bimqôm qodšô): 'Who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? And who shall stand in his holy place?' The double rhetorical question mirrors Ps 15:1. The verb ʿālâ ('to go up, ascend') is the pilgrimage term (cf. ʿaliyyâ to Jerusalem); qûm bimqôm qodšô ('stand in his holy place') is the liturgical term for entering the sanctuary precinct and remaining. The answer given in v.4 is moral, not ritual — not ceremonial purity but ethical integrity qualifies one to ascend.
24.4: nĕqî kappayim ûbar lēbāb (nĕqî ḵappayim ûḇar lēḇāḇ): 'He who has clean hands and a pure heart.' The binary of hands and heart — outward act and inward disposition — defines integrity comprehensively. nĕqî kappayim ('clean of hands') denotes innocent of bloodshed and unethical dealings; bar lēbāb ('pure/clear of heart') denotes undivided loyalty. Matt 5:8 ('blessed are the pure in heart') echoes the lēb bārûr. The next clause: ʾăšer lōʾ nāśāʾ laššāwĕʾ napšô wĕlōʾ nišbaʿ lĕmirmâ, 'who does not lift up his soul to what is false, and does not swear deceitfully' — adds truthfulness in oath-taking (cf. Ps 15:4) as a second criterion.
24.5: yiśśāʾ bĕrākâ mēʾēt YHWH ûṣĕdāqâ mēʾĕlōhê yišʿô (yiśśāʾ ḇĕrāḵâ mēʾēṯ YHWH ûṣĕḏāqâ mēʾĕlōhê yišʿô): 'He will receive blessing from the LORD and righteousness from the God of his salvation.' The one who qualifies (v.4) receives bĕrākâ ('blessing') and ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness') — both from YHWH. The ṣĕdāqâ here is not mere ethical standing but the verdict of righteousness from the divine judge: to be declared righteous by the God who saves. The phrase ʾĕlōhê yišʿô ('the God of his salvation') personalizes it — this is 'my God who saves.'
24.6: zeh dôr dōrĕšāyw mĕbaqqĕšê pānêkā yaʿăqōb (zeh dôr dōrĕšāyw mĕbaqqĕšê p̄āneḵā yaʿăqōḇ): 'Such is the generation of those who seek him, who seek your face, O Jacob.' The dôr ('generation') of seekers is the community defined not by birth but by orientation — those who seek (dāraš) YHWH and seek his face (bāqaš pānêkā). The address 'O Jacob' (Selah) is unusual — it may be a gloss or liturgical call. The generation of seekers constitutes the people of God in each era: the covenant community is defined by the pattern of life described in v.4, not ethnic origin.
24.7: śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem wĕhinnāśĕʾû pitḥê ʿôlām wĕyābôʾ melek hakkābôd (śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem wĕhinnāśĕʾû piṯḥê ʿôlām wĕyāḇôʾ meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ): 'Lift up your heads, O gates! And be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in.' The imperatives — śĕʾû ('lift up!') and hinnāśĕʾû ('be lifted up!') — personify the gates as figures bowing before a greater king. The phrase melek hakkābôd ('the King of glory') — YHWH as the kābôd-bearing sovereign — is the psalm's climactic title. The setting was likely the procession of the ark into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6) or an annual liturgical re-enactment. The NT applies the entry imagery to the ascension of Christ through the heavenly gates (Heb 1:6; Ps 47:5).
24.8: mî zeh melek hakkābôd YHWH ʿizzûz wĕgibbôr (mî zeh meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ YHWH ʿizzûz wĕḡibbôr): 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD, strong and mighty.' The dialogue format — question (v.8a) / answer (v.8b) — may reflect a liturgical antiphony between two groups: those outside the gate asking, those inside answering. The divine title YHWH ʿizzûz wĕgibbôr YHWH gibbôr milḥāmâ, 'the LORD, strong and mighty, the LORD, mighty in battle,' invokes the divine warrior tradition (Exod 15:3; Isa 42:13). YHWH's glory is specifically the glory of a warrior who wins battles — a counter-claim against any military power that oppresses Israel.
24.9: śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem ûśĕʾû pitḥê ʿôlām (śĕʾû šĕʿārîm rāšêkem ûśĕʾû piṯḥê ʿôlām): 'Lift up your heads, O gates! Lift them up, O ancient doors.' The repetition of the summons (cf. v.7) with slight variation — the second half reads ûśĕʾû rather than wĕhinnāśĕʾû — creates the intensification of liturgical repetition. The repetition is not redundancy but escalation: the call goes out a second time, more urgent. The doors (pitḥê ʿôlām, 'eternal doors') are designated by their antiquity — they have witnessed ages of divine activity; now they open for the greatest entrance.
24.10: mî hûʾ zeh melek hakkābôd YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt hûʾ melek hakkābôd (mî hûʾ zeh meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôṯ hûʾ meleḵ haḵḵāḇôḏ): 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD of hosts, he is the King of glory!' The final identification YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ('LORD of hosts/armies') is the most comprehensive divine title in the Psalter — YHWH as the commander of the heavenly armies (the stars, angels, and cosmic forces). The declaration hûʾ melek hakkābôd ('he is the King of glory') is the psalm's answer to the question it has posed throughout: the one who owns the earth (v.1), who founded it (v.2), whose holy mountain is approached only by the ethically qualified (vv.3-6), who enters in military triumph (vv.7-9) — this one is the LORD of hosts. Selah closes the psalm with a meditative pause on the name.

25.1: ʾēlêkā YHWH napšî ʾeśśāʾ (ʾēlêḵā YHWH napšî ʾeśśāʾ): 'To you, O LORD, I lift up my soul.' Psalm 25 is an acrostic psalm — each verse begins with the successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The opening gesture — lifting the soul (nepeš) to YHWH — is an act of total self-offering: the self (nepeš = life-breath, will, appetite) directed entirely toward God. The preposition ʾēlêkā ('toward you') and the perfect tense of surrender set the psalm's posture: not merely asking but orienting the entire self toward the divine.
25.2: ʾĕlōhay bĕkā bāṭaḥtî ʾal-ʾēbôšâ (ʾĕlōhay bĕḵā ḇāṭaḥtî ʾal-ʾēḇôšâ): 'O my God, in you I trust; let me not be put to shame.' The pairing bāṭaḥtî ('I trust') with ʾal-ʾēbôšâ ('let me not be put to shame') shows that shame is the ultimate risk of trust: if YHWH does not respond, the psalmist's public reliance is exposed as futile. The phrase ʾal-yaʿalĕṣû ʾōyĕbay lî, 'let not my enemies exult over me,' is the social dimension — the enemy's victory would be the psalmist's humiliation. Trust in God is a public stake that requires God to act in order to vindicate both the psalmist and himself.
25.3: gam kol-qōwêkā lōʾ yēbōšû (ḡam ḵol-qōwêḵā lōʾ yēḇōšû): 'Indeed, none who wait for you shall be put to shame.' The qāwâ ('to wait, hope with taut expectation') group — all those who wait on YHWH — are collectively protected from shame. The contrast: yēbōšû habbôgĕdîm rêqām, 'those who are wantonly treacherous shall be put to shame.' The word rêqām ('empty-handed, in vain') characterizes the treacherous: they pursue betrayal but achieve nothing lasting. Shame is redirected from the trusting to the treacherous.
25.4: dĕrāḵêkā YHWH hôdîʿēnî ʾōrĕḥôtêkā lammĕdēnî (dĕrāḵêḵā YHWH hôḏîʿēnî ʾōrĕḥôṯêḵā lammĕḏēnî): 'Make me to know your ways, O LORD; teach me your paths.' The two pedagogical imperatives — hôdîʿēnî ('make me know') and lammĕdēnî ('teach me') — are requests for covenantal guidance. The pair dĕrāḵêkā ('your ways') and ʾōrĕḥôtêkā ('your paths') — both plural — suggest multiple dimensions of the divine will, not a single rigid rule. The request is not for information but for formation: to be shaped into a person who walks in YHWH's ways habitually.
25.5: haddirēkēnî bĕʾamittĕkā wĕlammĕdēnî (haḏḏirēḵēnî ḇĕʾămiṯṯĕḵā wĕlammĕḏēnî): 'Lead me in your truth and teach me.' The hiphil haddirēkēnî ('lead, guide') from dārak ('to tread a path') asks for active divine direction, not passive instruction. The prepositional phrase bĕʾamittĕkā ('in your truth/faithfulness') uses ʾĕmet ('truth, reliability') as the medium of guidance: YHWH's truth is not merely propositional but the way on which one walks. The confession: kî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhê yišʿî, 'for you are the God of my salvation' — grounds the request in covenant identity. The phrase ʾôtĕkā qiwwîtî kol-hayyôm ('you I wait for all the day') sustains the ʿal-qōwêkā of v.3.
25.6: zĕkōr-raḥămêkā YHWH waḥăsādeykā kî mēʿôlām hēmmâ (zĕḵor-raḥămêḵā YHWH waḥăsāḏeḵā kî mēʿôlām hēmmâ): 'Remember your mercy, O LORD, and your steadfast love, for they have been from of old.' The imperative zĕkōr ('remember!') is the covenantal appeal: YHWH's raḥămîm ('mercies, compassion' — from reḥem/'womb,' the deep visceral tenderness of a mother) and ḥăsādîm ('acts of steadfast love') have a history — mēʿôlām hēmmâ, 'they have been from of old.' The prayer is not asking for a novel attribute but for the continuation of YHWH's established pattern of compassion. Memory becomes the engine of covenant faithfulness.
25.7: ḥaṭṭōʾt nĕʿûray ûpĕšāʿay ʾal-tizkor (ḥaṭṭōʾṯ nĕʿûray ûp̄ĕšāʿay ʾal-tizkor): 'Remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions.' The prayer inverts v.6: the psalmist asks God to remember (ḥăsādîm) and to forget (ḥaṭṭōʾt). The pairing ḥaṭṭōʾt ('sins, failures to hit the mark') and pĕšāʿîm ('transgressions, deliberate rebellions') covers the full range of moral failure. The phrase nĕʿûray ('of my youth') acknowledges that the young have always been vulnerable to folly — the past is real but need not define the future. The appeal: kĕḥasdĕkā zĕkār-lî ʾattâ lĕmaʿan ṭûbĕkā YHWH, 'according to your steadfast love remember me, for your goodness' sake, O LORD' — the ground of forgiveness is divine character (ḥesed, ṭôb), not human merit.
25.8: ṭôb wĕyāšār YHWH ʿal-kēn yôreh ḥaṭṭāʾîm baddārek (ṭôḇ wĕyāšār YHWH ʿal-kēn yôreh ḥaṭṭāʾîm baḏḏāreḵ): 'Good and upright is the LORD; therefore he instructs sinners in the way.' The declaration ṭôb wĕyāšār ('good and upright') describes YHWH's character. The logical connector ʿal-kēn ('therefore, on account of this') makes the instruction of sinners a consequence of divine character, not human worthiness: YHWH teaches sinners precisely because he is good. The noun ḥaṭṭāʾîm ('sinners') — the same people whose sins are addressed in v.7 — are not excluded from divine guidance; they are its target. This is grace-as-pedagogy.
25.9: yanhēl ʿănāwîm bammišpāṭ wîlammēd ʿănāwîm darkô (yanĕhēl ʿănāwîm bammišpāṭ wîlammēḏ ʿănāwîm darkô): 'He leads the humble in what is right, and teaches the humble his way.' The key word ʿănāwîm ('humble, meek, afflicted') — those who are bent down, whether by external oppression or by internal posture — are the specific recipients of divine guidance. The noun mišpāṭ ('justice, right') as the medium of leading means YHWH guides through his established order of justice, not arbitrary preference. Jesus's beatitude 'blessed are the meek' (Matt 5:5) echoes the Psalter's consistent elevation of the ʿānāw as the one who inherits the earth (Ps 37:11).
25.10: kol-ʾōrĕḥôt YHWH ḥesed wĕʾĕmet lĕnōṣĕrê bĕrîtô wĕʿēdōtāyw (ḵol-ʾōrĕḥôṯ YHWH ḥeseḏ wĕʾĕmeṯ lĕnōṣĕrê ḇĕrîṯô wĕʿēḏōṯāyw): 'All the paths of the LORD are steadfast love and faithfulness, for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies.' The comprehensive claim kol-ʾōrĕḥôt ('all the paths') eliminates exceptions: every way YHWH leads is characterized by ḥesed wĕʾĕmet ('steadfast love and faithfulness'). The qualifier lĕnōṣĕrê bĕrîtô wĕʿēdōtāyw, 'for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies,' identifies the covenant community as the context in which this promise operates — not a universal guarantee but a covenant-shaped assurance.
25.11: lĕmaʿan šimkā YHWH wĕsālaḥtā laʿăwōnî kî rāb hûʾ (lĕmaʿan šimkā YHWH wĕsālaḥtā laʿăwōnî kî rāḇ hûʾ): 'For your name's sake, O LORD, pardon my guilt, for it is great.' The appeal lĕmaʿan šimkā ('for your name's sake') grounds the request in divine honor rather than human claim: the psalmist does not argue his worthiness but YHWH's reputation. The strikingly honest phrase kî rāb hûʾ, 'for it is great' — the guilt's greatness is the very reason for the request. The logic inverts: normally great guilt would disqualify; here it intensifies the appeal. Only a God of extraordinary grace can handle great guilt — and YHWH is that God.
25.12: mî-zeh hāʾîš yĕrēʾ YHWH yôrennû bĕdārek yibḥār (mî-zeh hāʾîš yĕrēʾ YHWH yôrennû bĕḏāreḵ yiḇḥār): 'Who is the man who fears the LORD? Him will he instruct in the way that he should choose.' The rhetorical question mî-zeh hāʾîš, 'who is the man?' recalls Ps 24:3-4 — the entrance liturgy question about qualification. Here the qualifier is yĕrēʾ YHWH ('fearing the LORD') — the foundational orientation from which all wisdom flows (Prov 1:7). The result: yôrennû bĕdārek yibḥār, 'he will instruct him in the way he should choose.' YHWH teaches the God-fearer how to make life's choices — wisdom as the fruit of reverence.
25.13: napšô bĕṭôb tālîn wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ (napšô bĕṭôḇ tālîn wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ): 'His soul shall abide in well-being, and his offspring shall inherit the land.' The promise napšô bĕṭôb tālîn, 'his soul shall lodge in prosperity' — using lûn ('to lodge overnight, rest') suggests the well-being is settled and abiding, not temporary. The generational extension wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ, 'and his offspring shall inherit the land,' draws on the covenantal promise of the land (Gen 12:7; Ps 37:11). The blessing of the God-fearing extends beyond the individual to the next generation. Matt 5:5's 'they shall inherit the earth' universalizes the land promise.
25.14: sôd YHWH lîrēʾāyw ûbĕrîtô lĕhôdîʿām (sôḏ YHWH lîrēʾāyw ûḇĕrîṯô lĕhôḏîʿām): 'The friendship of the LORD is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant.' The noun sôd ('council, intimate circle, secret counsel') — used of the divine council (Jer 23:18; Job 15:8) — here describes the intimate fellowship YHWH offers to the God-fearing: not merely information about the covenant but inclusion in YHWH's own inner circle. The phrase ûbĕrîtô lĕhôdîʿām, 'and he makes his covenant known to them,' is the content of the sôd: covenant-knowledge as the shared secret of divine friendship.
25.15: ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešet raglāy (ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešeṯ raglāy): 'My eyes are ever toward the LORD, for he will pluck my feet out of the net.' The phrase ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH, 'my eyes always toward the LORD,' describes the posture of constant orientation — not periodic turning but sustained gaze. The ground kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešet raglāy, 'for he will pluck my feet out of the net,' uses yāṣāʾ (hiphil, 'to bring out, pluck out') for rescue from the reŝet ('net, snare') — the traps set by enemies (cf. Ps 10:9; 22:20). Trust is gaze; gaze enables rescue.
25.16: pĕnēh-ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî kî-yāḥîd wĕʿānî ʾānî (p̄ĕnēh-ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî kî-yāḥîd wĕʿānî ʾānî): 'Turn to me and be gracious to me, for I am lonely and afflicted.' The double self-description yāḥîd ('alone, solitary') and ʿānî ('afflicted, poor') identifies the psalmist's condition: isolated and beaten down. The imperative pĕnēh ('turn toward me!') — literally the divine face turning — requests the personal attention of YHWH. The word ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!') invokes the ḥānan/ḥesed cluster of divine favor. Loneliness before God is the psalm's deepest pain: not merely suffering but suffering in perceived isolation from the divine.
25.17: ṣārôt lĕbābî hirḥîbû mimmeṣûqôtay hôṣîʾēnî (ṣārôt lĕḇāḇî hirḥîḇû mimmeṣûqôṯay hôṣîʾēnî): 'The troubles of my heart are enlarged; bring me out of my distresses.' The idiom ṣārôt lĕbābî hirḥîbû ('the troubles of my heart have enlarged') describes how distress expands to fill the interior — anxieties that grow until they dominate the entire inner landscape. The imperative hôṣîʾēnî ('bring me out!') from yāṣāʾ ('to go out') is the rescue term — the same verb used in Exod 13:3 for the Exodus. The psalmist prays for a personal exodus from his meṣûqôt ('straits, tight places').
25.18: rĕʾēh ʿonyî waʿămālî wĕśāʾ lĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtāy (rĕʾēh ʿonyî waʿămālî wĕśāʾ lĕḵol-ḥaṭṭōʾṯāy): 'Consider my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins.' The imperative rĕʾēh ('see, consider') requests divine attention to the external (ʿonî, 'affliction') and internal (ʿāmāl, 'toil, trouble') dimensions of suffering. The addition wĕśāʾ lĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtāy, 'and lift off all my sins,' connects suffering to sin — not necessarily causally but covenantally: the psalmist acknowledges that complete deliverance requires both external rescue and internal forgiveness. The verb nāśāʾ ('to lift up, carry away') as the verb for forgiveness captures the removal of the burden (cf. Lev 16:22).
25.19: rĕʾēh ʾôyĕbay kî rabbû wĕśinʾat ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî (rĕʾēh ʾôyĕbay kî rabbû wĕśinʾaṯ ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî): 'Consider how many are my enemies, and with what violent hatred they hate me.' The resumptive rĕʾēh ('consider/see') repeats v.18's opening: look at both the sin (internal) and the enemies (external). The phrase śinʾat ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî ('with hatred of violence they hate me') is a cognate accusative intensifier — the hating is characterized by ḥāmās ('violence, destructive force'). The enemies' hatred is not merely emotional but operational: they intend violence.
25.20: šomrâ napšî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî ʾal-ʾēbôš kî ḥāsîtî bāk (šomrâ napšî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî ʾal-ʾēḇôš kî ḥāsîṯî ḇāḵ): 'Keep my soul and deliver me; let me not be put to shame, for I take refuge in you.' The paired imperatives šomrâ ('guard!') and haṣṣîlēnî ('deliver!') bracket the prayer: constant protection and active rescue. The shame-avoidance ʾal-ʾēbôš recalls v.2 — the psalm is framed by the fear of shame. The ground ḥāsîtî bāk ('I have taken refuge in you') is the basis: since the psalmist has publicly committed to YHWH as refuge, YHWH's honor is bound up with the outcome.
25.21: tōm wĕyōšer yiṣṣĕrûnî kî qiwwîtîkā (ṯōm wĕyōšer yiṣṣĕrûnî kî qiwwîtîkā): 'May integrity and uprightness preserve me, for I wait for you.' The nouns tōm ('completeness, integrity') and yōšer ('uprightness, straightness') function as guardians — the psalmist's own moral character as protective companions. The reason: kî qiwwîtîkā, 'for I wait for you' — covenant faithfulness expressed as patient, taut expectation. The verse restates the psalm's posture: integrity-shaped waiting as the practical form of covenant trust.
25.22: pĕdēh ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mikkōl ṣārôtāyw (p̄ĕḏēh ʾĕlōhîm ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl miḵḵōl ṣārôṯāyw): 'Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles.' The closing verse breaks the acrostic (it is a pê verse appended after the ṣādê verse) and shifts from individual to national concern. The verb pādâ ('redeem, ransom') — one of the key exodus/salvation verbs — applied to Israel as a whole suggests that the individual psalmist's experience of needing rescue mirrors Israel's corporate situation. The move from personal prayer to corporate intercession is characteristic of psalmic theology: the individual's faith is never separable from the community's hope.

26.1: šopĕṭēnî YHWH kî ʾănî bĕtummî hālāktî (šop̄ĕṭēnî YHWH kî ʾănî bĕṯummî hālāḵtî): 'Vindicate me, O LORD, for I have walked in my integrity.' Psalm 26 is a protestation of innocence — a genre related to the oath of clearance in which the accused declares their innocence before God (cf. Job 31). The verb šāpaṭ ('judge, vindicate') is a legal request: not punishment but examination and exoneration. The phrase bĕtummî hālāktî, 'I have walked in my integrity,' uses tōm ('completeness, wholeness') — the same word as Ps 25:21. The accompanying confession ûbaYHWH bāṭaḥtî lōʾ ʾemʿāḍ, 'and in the LORD I trust without wavering,' frames the integrity as covenant-grounded, not self-generated.
26.2: bĕḥānēnî YHWH wĕnassēnî ṣārĕpâ kilĕyôtay wĕlibbî (bĕḥānēnî YHWH wĕnassēnî ṣārĕp̄â ḵilĕyôṯay wĕlibbî): 'Prove me, O LORD, and try me; test my heart and my mind.' The three verbs — bāḥan ('examine'), nāsâ ('test, try'), and ṣārap ('smelt, refine') — all describe metallurgical or judicial investigation. The objects kilĕyôtay ('my kidneys/reins' — the seat of deep feeling) and libbî ('my heart' — the center of will and thought) encompass the whole inner person: both emotion and cognition. The psalmist invites the most thorough possible divine inspection, confident that the examination will vindicate rather than condemn.
26.3: kî ḥasdĕkā lĕneged ʿênāy wĕhithallaktî bĕʾamittekā (kî ḥasdĕḵā lĕnegeḏ ʿênāy wĕhiṯhallakṯî bĕʾămiṯṯeḵā): 'for your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in your faithfulness.' The phrase lĕneged ʿênāy ('before my eyes') — used elsewhere for what one constantly sees and is shaped by — means the psalmist's vision is constituted by YHWH's ḥesed: the divine love is his constant reference point. The hitpael wĕhithallaktî ('I walked about / I conduct myself') from hālak suggests habitual, lifestyle-level behavior — not isolated acts but the ongoing pattern of walking in ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth).
26.4: lōʾ-yāšabtî ʿim-mĕtê-šāwĕʾ (lōʾ-yāšabtî ʿim-mĕtê-šāwĕʾ): 'I do not sit with men of falsehood.' The negative lōʾ-yāšabtî ('I have not sat') followed by the parallel lōʾ ʾābôʾ ('I will not go in') with ʿim nĕʿālamîm ('with those who hide themselves/dissemblers') describe separation from the morally compromised. The company one keeps shapes the character one has: the psalmist's integrity is maintained partly by the associations he avoids. mĕtê-šāwĕʾ ('men of falsehood/vanity') and nĕʿālamîm ('those who cloak themselves, hypocrites') are the two groups he refuses to inhabit.
26.5: śānēʾtî qĕhal mĕrēʿîm wĕʿim rĕšāʿîm lōʾ ʾēšēb (śānēʾṯî qĕhal mĕrēʿîm wĕʿim rĕšāʿîm lōʾ ʾēšēb): 'I hate the assembly of evildoers, and I will not sit with the wicked.' The verb śānēʾ ('to hate') is strong — not merely avoid but actively reject. The noun qāhāl ('assembly, congregation') used for mĕrēʿîm ('evildoers') is a deliberate anti-type to the qāhāl of YHWH's worshippers (v.12). The two assemblies — the evildoers' and the righteous — are mutually exclusive: one cannot inhabit both. The psalmist's claim to innocence rests partly on sustained non-participation in the alternative community.
26.6: ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn kappāy wăʾăsōbbĕbâ ʾet-mizbaḥăkā YHWH (ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn kappāy wăʾăsōḇḇĕḇâ ʾeṯ-mizḇaḥăḵā YHWH): 'I wash my hands in innocence and go around your altar, O LORD.' The verb rāḥaṣ ('to wash') applied to the hands is a ritual purity gesture, but here deployed metaphorically for moral innocence (bĕnikkāyôn, 'in cleanness/innocence'). The Pilate parallel (Matt 27:24) uses the same gesture in reverse: he washes his hands to disclaim responsibility while the crowd accepts blood-guilt. The psalmist's hand-washing is a claim; Pilate's is a denial. The verb sābab ('go around, encircle') describes the processional circumambulation of the altar — active participation in worship, not passive attendance.
26.7: lĕhašmîaʿ bĕqôl tôdâ ûlĕsappēr kol-niplĕʾôtêkā (lĕhašmîaʿ bĕqôl tôḏâ ûlĕsappēr ḵol-nipl̂ĕʾôṯeḵā): 'proclaiming thanksgiving aloud, and telling all your wondrous deeds.' The two infinitives — lĕhašmîaʿ ('to cause to be heard') and lĕsappēr ('to recount, tell') — describe the content of the worshipper's participation: thanksgiving (tôdâ) and narration of niplĕʾôt ('wonders, marvelous deeds'). The tôdâ was both a thanksgiving offering and the liturgical speech accompanying it. The psalmist's worship is verbal proclamation — witness embedded in liturgy.
26.8: YHWH ʾāhabtî mĕʿôn bêtekā ûmĕqôm miškan kĕbôdekā (YHWH ʾāhaḇtî mĕʿôn bêṯeḵā ûmĕqôm miškan kĕḇôḏeḵā): 'O LORD, I love the habitation of your house and the place where your glory dwells.' The declaration ʾāhabtî ('I love') — qal perfect, completed and ongoing — applied to mĕʿôn bêtekā ('the dwelling of your house') expresses the psalmist's deepest affection as being for the sanctuary. The phrase mĕqôm miškan kĕbôdekā, 'the place where your glory dwells,' uses the theological kābôd — the radiant divine presence that filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35) and temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11). Love of the sanctuary is love of the presence it houses.
26.9: ʾal-tʾĕsōp ʿim-ḥaṭṭāʾîm napšî wĕʿim-ʾanšê dāmîm ḥayyāy (ʾal-tʾăsōp ʿim-ḥaṭṭāʾîm napšî wĕʿim-ʾanšê ḏāmîm ḥayyāy): 'Do not sweep away my soul with sinners, nor my life with bloodthirsty men.' The verb ʾāsap ('to gather, sweep away') in negative imperative form — ʾal-tʾĕsōp ('do not gather away') — pictures divine judgment as a gathering-and-sweeping of the wicked. The psalmist's prayer: do not include me in that sweep. The phrase ʾanšê dāmîm ('men of blood') designates murderers and the violent; their distinguishing characteristic is bloodshed. The psalmist's protestation of innocence throughout the psalm is precisely the ground for this request.
26.10: ʾăšer bîdêhem zimmâ wîmînām māleʾâ šōḥad (ʾăšer bîḏêhem zimmâ wîmînām māleʾâ šōḥad): 'in whose hands are evil devices, and whose right hands are full of bribes.' The noun zimmâ ('evil plan, wickedness') describes premeditated wrongdoing — schemes carried in the hands. The right hand (yāmîn) māleʾâ šōḥad, 'full of bribes,' corrupts the justice system: the šōḥad perverts the judge's verdict, converting justice into commerce. This is precisely what Ps 15:5 and Ps 24:4 identified as disqualifying from divine presence — and why the psalmist protests his own innocence: his hands are ʾerḥaṣ bĕnikkāyôn (v.6), not full of bribes.
26.11: waʾănî bĕtummî ʾēlēk pĕdēnî wĕḥonnēnî (waʾănî bĕṯummî ʾēlēḵ p̄ĕḏēnî wĕḥonnēnî): 'But as for me, I walk in my integrity; redeem me and be gracious to me.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but as for me') creates the contrast with the bloodthirsty of vv.9-10. The confession bĕtummî ʾēlēk ('I walk in my integrity') returns to the psalm's opening claim (v.1) — inclusio formed by the same language. The paired imperatives pĕdēnî ('redeem me!') and ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!') place the psalmist's need entirely in YHWH's hands: integrity does not earn rescue; it gives YHWH a basis to act in covenant faithfulness.
26.12: raglî ʿāmĕdâ bĕmîšôr bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăbārēk YHWH (raḡlî ʿāmĕḏâ bĕmîšôr bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăḇārēḵ YHWH): 'My foot stands on level ground; in the great assembly I will bless the LORD.' The phrase raglî ʿāmĕdâ bĕmîšôr, 'my foot stands on level ground,' uses mîšôr ('level plain, evenness') — the same root as yāšār ('upright'). The foot that walked in integrity (v.1) now stands on level ground — a spatial metaphor for moral stability. The vow bĕmaqhēlîm ʾăbārēk YHWH, 'in the great assembly I will bless the LORD,' returns the psalm to the sanctuary of v.8: the individual's protestation of innocence culminates in corporate worship. Integrity leads to praise.

27.1: YHWH ʾôrî wĕyišʿî mimmî ʾîrā YHWH māʿôz ḥayyay mimmî ʾepḥad (YHWH ʾôrî wĕyišʿî mimmî ʾîrā YHWH māʿôz ḥayyay mimmî ʾep̄ḥaḏ): 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?' The psalm opens with a triple divine declaration: YHWH as ʾôr ('light'), yešûʿâ ('salvation'), and māʿôz ('stronghold/refuge'). The light-metaphor (ʾôr) denotes guidance, revelation, and life itself — the opposite of darkness and death (cf. Isa 9:2; John 8:12). The rhetorical questions mimmî ʾîrā / mimmî ʾepḥad ('whom shall I fear? / of whom shall I be afraid?') eliminate every ground for fear: if YHWH is all three, no human power can threaten what matters most.
27.2: biqrōb ʿālay mĕrēʿîm leʾĕkōl ʾet-bĕśārî (biqrōḇ ʿālay mĕrēʿîm leʾĕḵōl ʾeṯ-bĕśārî): 'When evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh.' The verb ʾākal ('eat') applied to flesh — leʾĕkōl ʾet-bĕśārî — is a violent metaphor for total consumption/destruction (cf. Ps 14:4). The enemies are mĕrēʿîm ('evildoers') who approach (qārob, 'draw near') with intent to destroy. The reversal: hēmmâ kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû, 'they stumbled and fell.' The perfects are perfects of confidence — the psalmist narrates the defeat as already accomplished. The approach of enemies results in their own collapse before the one they attacked.
27.3: ʾim taḥăneh ʿālay maḥăneh lōʾ-yîrā libbî (ʾim taḥăneh ʿālay maḥăneh lōʾ-yîrā libbî): 'Though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear.' The hypothetical ʾim ('though, even if') introduces extreme test cases: an encamped army (maḥăneh — the same term for Israel's camp in the wilderness) and war (milḥāmâ) arrayed against the psalmist. The response of the heart (lēb): lōʾ-yîrā ('shall not fear') and lōʾ ʾeṭaḥ ('I will remain confident/trust'). The noun biṣṣōʾt ('in this' — the crisis) is the circumstance that occasions the confidence declaration: not despite the danger but precisely in it.
27.4: ʾaḥat šāʾaltî mēʾēt YHWH ʾôtāh ʾăbaqqēš (ʾaḥat šāʾaltî mēʾēt YHWH ʾôṯāh ʾăḇaqqēš): 'One thing have I asked of the LORD, that will I seek after.' The emphatic ʾaḥat ('one thing') narrows the entire scope of prayer to a single petition — the concentration of all desire into one longing. The petition: šibtî bĕbêt-YHWH kol-yĕmê ḥayyay lĕḥăzôt bĕnoʿam YHWH ûlĕbaqqēr bĕhêkālô, 'to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD and to inquire in his temple.' The three infinitives — šibtî ('dwelling'), lĕḥăzôt ('gazing'), ûlĕbaqqēr ('inquiring') — constitute the fullness of sanctuary life: permanence, contemplation, and seeking. The noʿam YHWH ('beauty/pleasantness of the LORD') is the ultimate object of vision (cf. Ps 16:11; Rev 22:4).
27.5: kî yiṣpĕnēnî bĕsukkōh bĕyôm rāʿâ (kî yiṣp̄ĕnēnî bĕsukkōh bĕyôm rāʿâ): 'for he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble.' The verb ṣāpan (piel, 'to hide, treasure up') pictures YHWH concealing the psalmist in sukkō ('his shelter/booth') — the divine dwelling as protective cover. The second image: yastirēnî bĕsēter ʾohŏlô, 'he will conceal me under the cover of his tent' (the tabernacle as hiding place). The third: yĕrômĕmēnî ʿal-ṣûr ('he will set me high upon a rock') — elevation beyond the reach of enemies. The day of trouble (yôm rāʿâ) does not nullify the confidence of v.1-3 but confirms its necessity.
27.6: wĕʿattâ yārûm rōʾšî ʿal ʾōyĕbay sĕbîbôtay (wĕʿattâ yārûm rōʾšî ʿal ʾōyĕbay sĕḇîḇôṯay): 'And now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me.' The raising of the head (yārûm rōʾšî) is the reversal of the bowing/humiliation the enemies sought. The vow that follows: wĕʾezbeḥâ bĕʾohŏlô zibbĕḥê tĕrûʿâ ʾāšîrâ waʾăzammĕrâ laYHWH, 'I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make melody to the LORD.' The sacrifices of tĕrûʿâ ('shouting, alarm/joy') transform military victory into liturgical offering: the battle's outcome becomes the occasion for worship.
27.7: šmaʿ YHWH qôlî ʾeqrāʾ wĕḥonnēnî waʿănēnî (šmaʿ YHWH qôlî ʾeqrāʾ wĕḥonnēnî waʿănēnî): 'Hear, O LORD, when I cry aloud; be gracious to me and answer me.' The psalm's second half (vv.7-14) shifts from confidence declaration to active prayer — suggesting the psalm was composed in two movements or that the confident first section empowers the petition of the second. The trio šmaʿ ('hear!'), ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!'), and waʿănēnî ('answer me!') are the classic lament imperatives. Even confident trust expresses itself in explicit petition.
27.8: lĕkā ʾāmar libbî baqqĕšû pānāy ʾet-pānêkā YHWH ʾăbaqqēš (lĕḵā ʾāmar libbî baqqĕšû p̄ānāy ʾeṯ-p̄āneḵā YHWH ʾăḇaqqēš): 'You have said, 'Seek my face.' My heart says to you, 'Your face, LORD, do I seek.'' The divine imperative baqqĕšû pānāy ('seek my face!') — whether heard in Scripture, liturgy, or internal conviction — is met with immediate response: ʾeṯ-pānêkā YHWH ʾăbaqqēš ('your face, O LORD, I seek'). The two-part structure of divine invitation and human response is the grammar of prayer: God's call precedes and elicits the seeking. The pānîm ('face') — divine self-disclosure — is both the command's content and the prayer's object.
27.9: ʾal-tastēr pānêkā mimmennî ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ʿabdekā (ʾal-tastēr p̄āneḵā mimmennî ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ʿaḇḏeḵā): 'Hide not your face from me; turn not your servant away in anger.' The negative imperatives — ʾal-tastēr ('do not hide!') and ʾal-taṭ bĕʾap ('do not turn away in anger!') — are prayers against divine wrath and hiddenness. The psalmist has been seeking the face (v.8) and now fears the face might be hidden — the very hiding that Ps 22:1 lamented. The continued address ʿabdekā ('your servant') maintains the relational framework: servant-speech is the human position before the sovereign YHWH. The phrase ʾĕzrātî hāyîtā, 'you have been my help,' appeals to prior experience to ground present petition.
27.10: kî-ʾābî wĕʾimmî ʿăzābûnî waYHWH yaʾasĕpēnî (kî-ʾāḇî wĕʾimmî ʿăzāḇûnî waYHWH yaʾasĕp̄ēnî): 'For my father and my mother have forsaken me, but the LORD will take me in.' The extreme hypothetical — parental abandonment — represents the deepest human betrayal: even the most natural and binding of bonds may fail. The response waYHWH yaʾasĕpēnî, 'but the LORD will take me in/gather me' — from ʾāsap ('gather, receive') — images YHWH as the one who gathers the abandoned child. The statement grounds the appeal of v.9: if even parents might fail, YHWH's faithfulness must not. This is the Psalter's most poignant expression of YHWH as the ultimate parent (cf. Isa 49:15).
27.11: hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ûnaḥēnî bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy (hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ûnaḥēnî bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy): 'Teach me your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path because of my enemies.' The request hôrēnî ('teach me!') from yārâ ('to throw, point, teach') — the root of tôrâ — asks for foundational instruction in divine direction. The phrase bĕʾōraḥ mîšôr ('on a level/even path') echoes Ps 25:21 (tōm) and Ps 26:12 (mîšôr): moral levelness as protection from stumbling. The enemies are the context: lĕmaʿan šôrĕrāy, 'because of my enemies/those who watch me,' who would exploit any deviation.
27.12: ʾal-tittĕnēnî bĕnepeš ṣārāy (ʾal-tittĕnēnî bĕnepeš ṣārāy): 'Give me not up to the will of my adversaries.' The phrase bĕnepeš ṣārāy ('into the desire/appetite of my adversaries') — nepeš as 'appetite, desire' (rather than 'soul') — means: do not let them have what they hunger for, which is my destruction. The motive clause kî qāmû bî ʿēdê šāqer wîpēaḥ ḥāmās, 'for false witnesses have risen against me, and they breathe out violence,' specifies the judicial danger: perjury in court. ʿēdê šāqer ('false witnesses') is a specific violation of the Decalogue (Exod 20:16) — the psalmist faces coordinated legal assault backed by lies.
27.13: lûlēʾ heʾĕmantî lirʾôt bĕṭûb YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm (lûlēʾ heʾĕmantî lirʾôt bĕṭûḇ YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm): 'I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living.' The phrase lûlēʾ ('unless, if not') begins a conditional whose main clause is suppressed — a classical rhetorical aposiopesis: 'If I had not believed... [I would have despaired].' What remains is the content of the faith: lirʾôt bĕṭûb YHWH bĕʾereṣ ḥayyîm, 'to see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living.' The ṭûb YHWH ('goodness of the LORD') is the object of faith-based vision — not merely hoped for in the afterlife but expected in this life. ʾereṣ ḥayyîm ('land of the living') = the present world of embodied existence, not the realm of the dead.
27.14: qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕkā wĕqawwēh ʾel-YHWH (qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕḵā wĕqawwēh ʾel-YHWH): 'Wait for the LORD; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the LORD!' The double imperative qawwēh ... qawwēh ('wait ... wait') — from qāwâ ('to wait with taut, rope-like expectation') — frames the verse as an inclusio of hope. Between the two waits: ḥăzaq wĕyaʾămēṣ libbĕkā ('be strong and let your heart take courage') — the verbs used for Joshua's commissioning (Josh 1:6-9) and David's charge to Solomon (1 Chr 22:13). The courageous heart is not self-manufactured but the result of waiting on YHWH — confidence drawn from the one waited for. The psalm ends not with resolution but with the ongoing posture of expectation.

34.1: ʾăbārĕkâ ʾet-YHWH bĕkol-ʿēt tāmîd tĕhillātô bĕpî (ʾăḇāreḵâ ʾeṯ-YHWH bĕḵol-ʿēṯ tāmîḏ tĕhillāṯô ḇĕp̄î): 'I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth.' Psalm 34 is an acrostic poem (each verse beginning with successive Hebrew letters, ʾālep through tāw), attributed to David's feigning of madness before Achish king of Gath (1 Sam 21:10-15 — the superscription uses 'Abimelech,' possibly a dynastic title for Philistine kings). The cohortative ʾăbārĕkâ ('I will bless') opens with resolve: tāmîd ('continually, always') negates any temporal limitation on praise. The phrase tĕhillātô bĕpî, 'his praise in my mouth,' locates worship in the mouth — the organ of both speech and ingestion, connecting to the later 'taste and see' of v.8.
34.2: baYHWH tithallēl napšî (ḇaYHWH tiṯhallēl napšî): 'My soul makes its boast in the LORD.' The hitpael tiṯhallēl ('boasts, glories') from hālal is reflexive — the soul's self-glorying redirected toward YHWH. The Pauline principle of 1 Cor 1:31 ('let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord') draws on this Psalter tradition (cf. Jer 9:23-24). The phrase yišmĕʿû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśmāḥû, 'the humble will hear and be glad,' introduces the ʿănāwîm as those who listen to testimony of rescue and share vicariously in the joy — the community of the poor who live by hearing good news of others' deliverance.
34.3: gaddĕlû laYHWH ʾittî ûnĕrômĕmâ šĕmô yaḥdāw (ḡaddĕlû laYHWH ʾittî ûnĕrômĕmâ šĕmô yaḥdāw): 'Oh, magnify the LORD with me, and let us exalt his name together!' The plural imperatives gaddĕlû ('make great!') and nĕrômĕmâ ('let us exalt!') invite communal amplification of praise. The adverb yaḥdāw ('together') is covenantal — individual rescue becomes corporate celebration. The 'name' (šēm) that is exalted encompasses YHWH's revealed character: the rescue of David (background of the psalm) becomes a public declaration about who YHWH is.
34.4: dāraštî ʾet-YHWH wĕʿānānî ûmikkol-mĕgûrôtay hiṣṣîlānî (ḏāraštî ʾeṯ-YHWH wĕʿānānî ûmikkol-mĕḡûrôṯay hiṣṣîlānî): 'I sought the LORD, and he answered me and delivered me from all my fears.' The verb dāraš ('to seek, inquire after') is the psalmist's primary act — the seeking that initiated the entire encounter. The reciprocal wĕʿānānî ('and he answered me') shows that seeking activates divine response. The noun mĕgûrôt ('fears, terrors') from gûr ('to sojourn, dread') is unusual — it may connote the alien anxiety of someone in a foreign context (David among Philistines), making the historical backstory theologically loaded: fear of exile rescued by YHWH.
34.5: hibbîṭû ʾēlāyw wĕnāhārû ûpĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥpārû (hibbîṭû ʾēlāyw wĕnāhārû ûp̄ĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥp̄ārû): 'Those who looked to him were radiant, and their faces shall never be ashamed.' The plural hibbîṭû ('they looked') shifts from David's individual experience to the community's: looking toward YHWH has a visible effect — wĕnāhārû ('they were radiant'), from nāhar ('to shine, stream, be radiant'). The shamelessness of their faces — ûpĕnêhem ʾal-yēḥpārû — echoes the Psalter's consistent prayer (e.g., Ps 25:2-3) that those who trust YHWH will not be put to shame. The inversion: looking toward YHWH produces light; looking away produces shame.
34.6: zeh ʿānî qārāʾ wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôtāyw hôšîʿô (zeh ʿānî qārāʾ wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôṯāyw hôšîʿô): 'This poor man cried, and the LORD heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.' The demonstrative zeh ʿānî ('this poor man') is emphatic and personal — David identifies himself as the paradigmatic ʿānî ('afflicted/poor one'). The sequence qārāʾ → šāmaʿ → hôšîʿ ('cried → heard → saved') is the compressed rescue narrative of the entire Psalter. The psalm is not just testimony about YHWH's past action but a pattern: ʿānî + cry → YHWH hears → rescue. Peter applies this pattern to Jesus (1 Pet 5:7).
34.7: ḥōneh malʾak YHWH sābîb lîrēʾāyw wayyĕḥallĕṣēm (ḥōneh malʾak YHWH sāḇîḇ lîrēʾāyw wayyĕḥallĕṣēm): 'The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear him and delivers them.' The participle ḥōneh ('encamps, sets up camp') from ḥānâ applies military encampment language to divine protection: the divine envoy establishes a perimeter. The malʾak YHWH ('messenger/angel of the LORD') in the OT is often indistinguishable from YHWH himself (Gen 16:7-13; 22:11-18; Exod 3:2-6) — an anticipation of the Second Person's mediatorial role. The scope: lîrēʾāyw ('for those who fear him') — the protective encampment is covenant-shaped, not universal. The hiphil wayyĕḥallĕṣēm ('and he delivers them') from ḥālaṣ ('to draw out') describes rescue as extraction from danger.
34.8: ṭaʿămû ûrĕʾû kî ṭôb YHWH (ṭaʿămû ûrĕʾû kî ṭôḇ YHWH): 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good.' The imperatives ṭaʿămû ('taste!') and rĕʾû ('see!') are synesthetic — taste and sight combined invite experiential rather than merely intellectual knowledge of divine goodness. The verb ṭāʿam ('to taste, perceive by eating') makes covenant knowledge bodily: one does not merely learn about YHWH's goodness but experiences it as real as food in the mouth. 1 Peter 2:3 cites this verse: 'if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good' — applied to the community's experience of Christ. The following beatitude ʾašrê haggeber yeḥĕseh-bô, 'blessed is the man who takes refuge in him,' links tasting to ḥāsâ-trust.
34.9: yĕrĕʾû ʾet-YHWH qĕdōšāyw kî ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw (yĕrĕʾû ʾeṯ-YHWH qĕḏôšāyw kî ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw): 'Oh, fear the LORD, you his holy ones, for those who fear him have no lack!' The imperative yĕrĕʾû is directed at qĕdōšāyw ('his holy ones, his consecrated ones') — the covenant community already set apart for YHWH. Fear here (yārēʾ) is not terror but the reverence that orientates all of life toward YHWH. The promise: ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw, 'no lack to those who fear him' — the noun maḥsôr ('want, lack, deficiency') is negated absolutely. Those who fear YHWH have no permanent deficiency; the root of all their needs is met in him.
34.10: kĕpîrîm rāšû wĕrāʿēbû wĕdōrĕšê YHWH lōʾ-yaḥsĕrû kol-ṭôb (kĕp̄îrîm rāšû wĕrāʿēḇû wĕḏōrĕšê YHWH lōʾ-yaḥsĕrû ḵol-ṭôḇ): 'The young lions suffer want and hunger, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing.' The contrast is between kĕpîrîm ('young lions' — the most powerful predators in their prime strength) and dōrĕšê YHWH ('those who seek the LORD'). Young lions — paragons of physical power and hunting ability — still experience rāšû ('become poor') and rāʿēbû ('hunger'). But the YHWH-seekers yaḥsĕrû... kol-ṭôb ('lack no good thing'). Power and provision are not correlated — seeking YHWH, not personal strength, is the ground of sufficiency.
34.11: lĕkû bānîm šimʿû-lî yirʾat YHWH ʾălammēdkem (lĕḵû ḇānîm šimʿû-lî yirʾaṯ YHWH ʾălammēḏkem): 'Come, O children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD.' The shift to wisdom-address — lĕkû bānîm ('come, children') — transforms the psalm from personal testimony to pedagogical discourse: David's rescue becomes a teaching moment. The phrase yirʾat YHWH ʾălammēdkem, 'I will teach you the fear of the LORD,' identifies the fear of YHWH as teachable — a disposition cultivated by instruction and practice, not merely intuited. This verse inaugurates the sapiential center of the psalm (vv.11-14).
34.12: mî-hāʾîš heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ʾōhēb yāmîm lirʾôt ṭôb (mî-hāʾîš heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ʾōhēb yāmîm lirʾôṯ ṭôḇ): 'What man is there who desires life and loves many days, that he may see good?' The rhetorical question mî-hāʾîš ('what man?') creates a universally appealing hook: who does not desire life and good days? The two participles heḥāpēṣ ḥayyîm ('who desires life') and ʾōhēb yāmîm ('who loves days') are universal — the question's answer is everyone. The condition 'to see good' (lirʾôt ṭôb) picks up the 'see' of v.8's synesthetic invitation: seeing good is possible, and the means is covenant wisdom.
34.13: nĕṣōr lĕšônĕkā mērāʿ ûśĕpātêkā middabbēr mirmâ (nĕṣōr lĕšôneḵā mērāʿ ûśĕp̄āṯeḵā middabbēr mirmâ): 'Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking deceit.' The verb nāṣar (qal imperative, 'guard, keep watch') — a military term — applied to the tongue describes speech as a territory to be defended. The contrast between ṭôb ('good') and rāʿ ('evil'), ʾĕmet ('truth') and mirmâ ('deceit'), frames the tongue as the battleground of covenantal integrity. Peter quotes vv.12-16 extensively in 1 Pet 3:10-12, applying the ethical instruction of this psalm to the Christian community's social witness — the passage from Psalter wisdom becomes apostolic exhortation.
34.14: sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb baqqēš šālôm wĕrādĕpēhû (sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb baqqēš šālôm wĕrāḏĕp̄ēhû): 'Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it.' The four imperatives create a complete ethical program: sûr ('turn away') + waʿăśēh ('do') + baqqēš ('seek') + wĕrādĕpēhû ('pursue'). The verb rādap ('pursue') — normally of enemies chasing someone (cf. Ps 7:1) — is applied to šālôm ('peace'): peace must be actively chased, not passively hoped for. The movement from avoidance (sûr mērāʿ) to action (ʿăśēh ṭôb) to pursuit (rādap šālôm) describes the full range of ethical life, corresponding to the OT/NT summary of avoiding evil and doing good (Rom 12:9; 1 Pet 3:11).
34.15: ʿênê YHWH ʾel-ṣaddîqîm wĕʾoznāyw ʾel-šawʿātām (ʿênê YHWH ʾel-ṣaddîqîm wĕʾoznāyw ʾel-šawʿāṯām): 'The eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry.' The double divine attention — ʿênê YHWH ('eyes of the LORD') and ʾoznāyw ('his ears') — ensures complete sensory coverage: YHWH both sees the righteous and hears their cry. Peter cites this verse in 1 Pet 3:12 as the theological ground for the ethical instruction of vv.13-14: righteous conduct is worth maintaining because YHWH is watching and listening. The noun šawʿâ ('cry for help') is the desperate outcry of those in extremity — the same type of cry that initiates rescue throughout the Psalter.
34.16: pĕnê YHWH bĕʿōśê rāʿ lĕhakrît mēʾereṣ zikrām (p̄ĕnê YHWH bĕʿōśê rāʿ lĕhaḵrîṯ mēʾereṣ zikrām): 'The face of the LORD is against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth.' The contrast with v.15 is structurally intentional: YHWH's face (pānîm) is oriented toward the righteous beneficially (v.15) but toward evildoers destructively. The phrase lĕhakrît mēʾereṣ zikrām, 'to cut off their memory from the earth,' invokes the covenant curse of obliterated remembrance (cf. Ps 9:5; Deut 9:14). Memory-erasure is the final form of judgment: not just death but the annihilation of legacy. Peter also quotes this verse, bracketing the moral teaching with divine scrutiny from both sides.
34.17: ṣāʿăqû wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôtām hiṣṣîlām (ṣāʿăqû wĕYHWH šāmaʿ ûmikkol-ṣārôṯām hiṣṣîlām): 'When the righteous cry for help, the LORD hears and delivers them out of all their troubles.' The sequence ṣāʿăqû → šāmaʿ → hiṣṣîlām ('cried → heard → delivered') repeats the pattern of v.6 with generalizing third-person plurals: what happened to David (v.6) is paradigmatic for all the righteous. The noun ṣārôt ('troubles, straits') encompasses every form of distress — and the promise applies to mikkol ('out of all'), without exception. The righteousness that attracts YHWH's hearing is the covenant loyalty described in vv.13-14, not moral perfection.
34.18: qārôb YHWH lĕnišbĕrê-lēb wĕʾet-dakkĕʾê rûaḥ yôšîaʿ (qārôḇ YHWH lĕnišbĕrê-lēḇ wĕʾeṯ-daḵḵĕʾê rûaḥ yôšîaʿ): 'The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.' The two recipients — nišbĕrê-lēb ('broken of heart, shattered in heart') and dakkĕʾê rûaḥ ('crushed of spirit') — describe the person who has experienced catastrophic loss of inner resilience. The adjective dakkāʾ ('crushed, pulverized') from dakāʾ implies being ground down to powder — not merely injured but disintegrated. To such persons YHWH is qārôb ('near') — not distant and requiring approach but already proximate. Jesus's beatitude 'blessed are the poor in spirit' (Matt 5:3) and Isa 61:1's mission to 'bind up the brokenhearted' both draw on this verse's theology.
34.19: rabbôt rāʿôt ṣaddîq ûmikkullām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH (rabbôṯ rāʿôṯ ṣaddîq ûmikkulām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH): 'Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the LORD delivers him out of them all.' The phrase rabbôt rāʿôt ṣaddîq ('many are the troubles of the righteous') is not a complaint but a description — righteous people do suffer, and much. The psalm refuses the false theology that righteousness guarantees comfort. But the counter: ûmikkullām yaṣṣîlennû YHWH ('from all of them the LORD delivers him') — the word kullām ('all of them') makes the rescue scope absolute. The righteous suffer much AND are delivered completely. Both are true; neither cancels the other (cf. 2 Cor 4:8-9; Acts 14:22).
34.20: šōmēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāyw ʾaḥat mēhēnnâ lōʾ nišbārâ (šōmēr ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāyw ʾaḥaṯ mēhēnnâ lōʾ nišbārâ): 'He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken.' The verb šāmar ('guard, keep') applied to ʿaṣmôt ('bones') in the context of v.19's 'many afflictions' — YHWH protects even the skeletal structure of the righteous through suffering. John 19:36 explicitly cites this verse as fulfilled at the crucifixion: the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs (as was customary to hasten death) because he was already dead — John sees in this the fulfillment of Ps 34:20 (and Exod 12:46, the Passover bone-protection). The most literal fulfillment of a Psalter verse in the NT passion narrative.
34.21: tĕmôtēt rāšāʿ rāʿâ wĕśōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû (ṯĕmôṯēṯ rāšāʿ rāʿâ wĕśōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû): 'Affliction will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned.' The verb mātat (hiphil, 'to cause to die') applied to the rāʿâ ('evil') that kills the rāšāʿ ('wicked') is an ironic reversal: evil, which the wicked used against others, destroys them. The phrase śōnĕʾê ṣaddîq yĕʾšāmû, 'those who hate the righteous will be condemned/guilty,' uses the niphal ʾāšam ('to become guilty, bear guilt') — the haters of the righteous are found guilty before YHWH's court. The reversal of vv.19-20 continues: the righteous suffer but are preserved; the wicked triumph but are finally destroyed by their own evil.
34.22: pôdeh YHWH nepeš ʿăbādāyw wĕlōʾ yĕʾšĕmû kol-haḥōsîm bô (p̄ôḏeh YHWH nepeš ʿăḇāḏāyw wĕlōʾ yĕʾšĕmû ḵol-haḥōsîm ḇô): 'The LORD redeems the life of his servants; none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned.' The participle pôdeh ('redeems') from pādâ ('to ransom, redeem') in present tense indicates ongoing activity: YHWH continuously ransoms. The object nepeš ʿăbādāyw ('the life of his servants') encompasses the whole person. The final promise — lōʾ yĕʾšĕmû kol-haḥōsîm bô, 'none who take refuge in him will be condemned' — returns to the ḥāsâ root of v.8 and answers v.21's condemnation of the wicked: the condemned are those who hate the righteous; those who take refuge are never condemned. The acrostic closes with an absolute guarantee of non-condemnation for the covenant community (cf. Rom 8:1).

35.1: rîbâ YHWH ʾet-yĕrîbay lĕḥam ʾet-lōḥāmāy (rîḇâ YHWH ʾeṯ-yĕrîḇay lĕḥam ʾeṯ-lōḥāmāy): 'Contend, O LORD, with those who contend with me; fight against those who fight against me.' Psalm 35 is an imprecatory lament: the psalmist asks YHWH to act as his legal advocate (rîb — 'to contend at law, plead a case') and military champion (lāḥam — 'to fight'). The verb rîb belongs to the legal sphere: a rîb is a formal dispute or lawsuit. The psalmist frames his enemies as parties in a lawsuit against him and asks YHWH to take up his side of the case. The parallel lōḥāmāy ('those who fight against me') adds the military dimension: the legal and military metaphors interweave throughout the psalm, as they do in divine warrior texts (Exod 15; Isa 49:25-26).
35.2: haḥăzēq māgēn wĕṣinnâ wĕqûmâ bĕʿezrātî (haḥăzēq māgēn wĕṣinnâ wĕqûmâ bĕʿezrātî): 'Take hold of shield and buckler and rise for my help!' The imperatives haḥăzēq ('take firm hold!') and qûmâ ('arise!') summon YHWH as divine warrior. The two shields — māgēn (small round shield) and ṣinnâ (large body-covering shield) — represent comprehensive military protection. The phrase qûmâ bĕʿezrātî, 'arise for my help,' uses qûmâ as the liturgical summons to divine action (cf. Pss 7:6; 9:19; 10:12; 27:3). The request is concrete: the warrior-God is to arm himself and stand between the psalmist and his attackers.
35.3: wĕhāreq ḥănît ûsgōr liqrāʾt rōdĕpāy (wĕhāreq ḥănîṯ ûs̱gōr liqrāʾṯ rōḏĕp̄āy): 'Draw out the spear and javelin against my pursuers.' The hiphil hāreq ('draw out, unsheath') applied to the ḥănît ('spear') continues the military arming of YHWH. The phrase ʾĕmōr lĕnapšî yĕšûʿātek ʾānî, 'say to my soul: I am your salvation' — the divine speech-act that transforms the inner person. The requested word is yĕšûʿātek ʾānî ('I am your salvation') — not merely 'I will save you' but an identity declaration: YHWH as the essence of salvation. The psalmist needs not just help but the assurance that his helper is salvation itself.
35.4: yēbōšû wĕyikklĕmû mĕbaqqĕšê napšî (yēḇōšû wĕyikkālĕmû mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî): 'Let them be put to shame and dishonored who seek my life!' The paired jussives — yēbōšû ('let them be ashamed') and yikklĕmû ('let them be dishonored/humiliated') — are imprecatory prayers for the reversal of the enemies' plot. The phrase mĕbaqqĕšê napšî ('those who seek my life') describes those seeking the napšî — the whole person, not merely physical life. The additional pair yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyēḥpĕrû ('let them be turned back and confounded') amplifies the desired reversal: the attackers become the routed.
35.5: yihyû kĕmōṣ lipnê-rûaḥ ûmalʾak YHWH dôḥeh (yihyû kĕmōṣ lip̄nê-rûaḥ ûmalʾak YHWH dôḥeh): 'Let them be like chaff before the wind, with the angel of the LORD driving them away.' The chaff simile (kĕmōṣ lipnê-rûaḥ) pictures weight-lessness before power: the enemy who seemed substantial is revealed as insubstantial before the divine wind. The agent of their dispersal is malʾak YHWH ('the angel/messenger of the LORD') — the divine warrior's emissary who acts as enforcer of YHWH's judgments. The verb dôḥeh (hiphil participle from dāḥâ, 'to push, drive away') describes relentless pressure.
35.6: yĕhî darkām ḥōšek waḥălaqlaqqôt ûmalʾak YHWH rōdĕpām (yĕhî ḏarḵām ḥōšeḵ waḥălaqlaqqôṯ ûmalʾak YHWH rōḏĕp̄ām): 'Let their way be dark and slippery, with the angel of the LORD pursuing them.' The pairing of ḥōšek ('darkness') and ḥălaqlaqqôt ('slippery places') creates hazardous terrain without visible footing: the pursued cannot see where they step and the ground cannot be gripped. The angel of the LORD who was 'driving' (v.5) is now rōdĕpām ('pursuing') — the same verb used of the enemies' pursuit of the psalmist. The hunter becomes the hunted; divine agency reverses the predator-prey relationship.
35.7: kî ḥinnām ṭāmĕnû-lî šaḥat rišetām ḥinnām ḥāpĕrû lĕnapšî (kî ḥinnām ṭāmĕnû-lî šaḥaṯ rišetām ḥinnām ḥāp̄ĕrû lĕnapšî): 'For without cause they hid their net for me; without cause they dug a pit for my life.' The word ḥinnām ('without cause, for nothing, gratuitously') appears twice — a double emphasis on the unprovoked nature of the persecution. The two traps — rešet ('net') and šaḥat ('pit') — represent different modes of ambush. John 15:25 cites the word ḥinnām from this psalm: 'they hated me without cause' (combining Ps 35:19 and 69:4), applying the unprovoked hatred of the psalmist's enemies to the world's hatred of Jesus — the paradigmatic innocent sufferer.
35.8: tĕbôʾêhû šôʾâ lōʾ-yēdaʿ wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕdô (ṯĕḇôʾêhû šôʾâ lōʾ-yēdaʿ wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕḏô): 'Let destruction come upon him when he does not know it; let the net that he hid ensnare him.' The noun šôʾâ ('sudden ruin, catastrophe') — used of the day of devastation (cf. Isa 10:3; Zeph 1:15) — describes the unexpectedness of the wicked's doom: tĕbôʾêhû ... lōʾ-yēdaʿ ('let it come upon him without his knowing'). The lex talionis principle activates: wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕdô, 'let the net he hid catch him' — his own trap becomes his own capture. The planned ambush of v.7 returns on the planner (cf. Pss 7:15; 9:15-16).
35.9: wĕnapšî tāgîl baYHWH tāśîś bîšûʿātô (wĕnapšî ṯāḡîl ḇaYHWH ṯāśîś bîšûʿāṯô): 'Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD, exulting in his salvation.' The waw-consecutive wĕnapšî ('and my soul') signals the consequence of answered prayer: utter joy. The verb gîl ('rejoice, be glad') describes the bodily expression of spiritual elation; śîś ('exult, delight') adds intensity. Both are baYHWH and bîšûʿātô — in YHWH himself and in his yešûʿâ ('salvation'). The joy is not in the rescue alone but in the Rescuer: the act reveals the character of the one who acts, and the soul rejoices in the person discovered.
35.10: kol-ʿaṣmōtay tōʾmarnâ YHWH mî kāmôkā (ḵol-ʿaṣmōṯay ṯōʾmarnâ YHWH mî ḵāmôḵā): 'All my bones shall say, 'O LORD, who is like you?'' The phrase kol-ʿaṣmōtay ('all my bones') makes the entire skeleton — the structural self — a speaking chorus. The doxological question mî kāmôkā ('who is like you?') is the incomparability formula used throughout the OT (Exod 15:11; Pss 71:19; 89:8; Mic 7:18). The following words specify YHWH's incomparability: maṣṣîl ʿānî mēḥāzāq mimmennû wĕʿānî wĕʾebyôn mĕgōzlô, 'delivering the poor from one too strong for him, the poor and needy from one who robs him.' YHWH is uniquely the advocate of the structurally weak against the structurally powerful.
35.11: yĕqûmûn ʿēdê ḥāmās ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtî yišʾālûnî (yĕqûmûn ʿēḏê ḥāmās ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḏaʿtî yišʾālûnî): 'Malicious witnesses rise up; they ask me of things I do not know.' The noun ʿēdê ḥāmās ('witnesses of violence') are perjurers — their testimony is not merely false but violent, designed to destroy. The phrase ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtî yišʾālûnî, 'they ask me about things I do not know,' describes entrapment: questions about deeds never done or knowledge never possessed. The situation connects to the false witnesses against Jesus (Matt 26:60-61) and to the broader persecution motif that runs from the individual psalmist through to the Servant of Isaiah 53.
35.12: yĕšallĕmûnî rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ šĕkôl lĕnapšî (yĕšallĕmûnî rāʿâ taḥaṯ ṭôḇâ šĕḵôl lĕnapšî): 'They repay me evil for good, bereaving my soul.' The inversion rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ ('evil in place of good') is a recognized form of injustice throughout the OT: the ultimate perversion of reciprocity (cf. Prov 17:13; Ps 109:5; Rom 12:17). The noun šĕkôl ('bereavement, loss of children') describes the bereft state of a parent who has lost children — the most profound grief — applied to the napšî ('soul'): the psalmist's inner self is bereaved by the ingratitude of those he helped.
35.13: waʾănî bĕḥolotām lĕbûšî śāq ʿinnêtî batsôm napšî (waʾănî bĕḥolotām lĕḇûšî śāq ʿinnêṯî ḇaṣṣôm napšî): 'But I, when they were sick — I wore sackcloth; I afflicted myself with fasting.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but I') contrasts the enemies' ingratitude (v.12) with the psalmist's prior loyalty. The verbs — lābōš śāq ('wore sackcloth') and ʿinnâ bĕṣôm ('afflicted with fasting') — are mourning rites performed not for himself but for them: he fasted and wept over their illness. The phrase ûtĕpillātî ʿal-ḥêqî tāšûb, 'and my prayer returned to my bosom,' is difficult: either the prayer returned unanswered (possibly), or the psalmist's compassion turned back on himself (i.e., even his intercession for them produced personal grief).
35.14: kĕrēaʿ kĕʾāḥ-lî hiṯhallāktî kĕʾabel-ʾēm qōdēr šāḥôtî (kĕrēaʿ kĕʾāḥ-lî hiṯhallāḵtî kĕʾāḇel-ʾēm qōḏēr šāḥôtî): 'I went about as though I grieved for my friend or my brother; I bowed down in mourning as one who laments his mother.' The similes escalate: kĕrēaʿ ('like a friend') → kĕʾāḥ-lî ('like my own brother') → kĕʾabel-ʾēm ('like one mourning a mother'). The mourning gestures — qōdēr ('darkened, clad in dark garments') and šāḥôtî ('I bowed down') — enact grief publicly. The intensity of the mourning for the enemies' illness makes the enemies' ingratitude (v.12) all the more offensive: David mourned them like family, and they repaid him with violence.
35.15: ûbĕṣalʿî śāmĕḥû wĕneʾespû (ûḇĕṣalʿî śāmĕḥû wĕneʾespû): 'But at my stumbling they rejoiced and gathered.' The noun ṣālaʿ ('limping, stumbling') describes a momentary weakness or defeat — the psalmist's vulnerability. The response: śāmĕḥû ('they rejoiced') and neʾespû ('they gathered') — the gathering of mockers at the scene of another's pain. The phrase nēkîm śĕmûqîm ... lōʾ yādaʿtî, 'smiters whom I did not know gathered against me,' introduces anonymous attackers: not just known enemies but strangers who join in the assault. The social dimension is communal shame: public mockery from an assembled crowd.
35.16: bĕḥanpê lāʿăgê māʿôg ḥāraq ʿālay šinnêhem (bĕḥanp̄ê lāʿăḡê māʿôḡ ḥāraqqû ʿālay šinnêhem): 'Like profane mockers at a feast, they gnash their teeth at me.' The phrase ḥanpê lāʿăgê māʿôg ('godless jesters/mockers at a cake-feast') pictures those who mock at banquets — the context of contemptuous entertainment. The verb ḥāraš šinnayim ('gnash teeth') is the physical expression of rage and contempt (cf. Pss 37:12; 112:10; Lam 2:16). The enemies' enjoyment of the psalmist's suffering is a feast: his pain is their entertainment. The verb māʿôg ('cake, flat cake') may refer specifically to revelers who mock over food — those whose full stomachs enable their cruelty.
35.17: ʾădōnāy kammâ tirʾeh hāšîbâ napšî miššōʾêhem (ʾăḏōnāy kammâ tirʾeh hāšîḇâ napšî miššōʾêhem): 'O Lord, how long will you look on? Rescue my soul from their destruction.' The address ʾădōnāy ('my Lord') combined with kammâ tirʾeh ('how long will you see/watch?') is the lament of divine inaction: YHWH witnesses the injustice but appears to tolerate it. The verb rāʾâ ('to see') in this context means watching without intervening — the most maddening form of non-response. The petition hāšîbâ napšî ('restore my life') from šûb ('to bring back') — as if the soul has been taken or driven away and needs restoration — and yĕḥîdātî mikkĕpîrîm, 'my precious one from the young lions,' uses the same yĕḥîdâ ('precious one') as Ps 22:20.
35.18: ʾôdĕkā bĕqāhāl rāb bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ʾăhallĕlekā (ʾôḏĕḵā bĕqāhāl rāḇ bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ʾăhallĕleḵā): 'I will thank you in the great assembly; in the mighty throng I will praise you.' The vow of public praise (bĕqāhāl rāb, 'in the great congregation') is the psalmist's counter-offering to the enemies' feast (v.16): if they feast on his misfortune, he will make YHWH's deliverance a public feast of praise. The phrase bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ('in a mighty/numerous people') emphasizes the size of the audience — the vindication will be as public as the shame was. The praise is the response that transforms private rescue into communal testimony (cf. Ps 22:25).
35.19: ʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ʾōyĕbay šeqer śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ʾal-yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin (ʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ʾōyĕbay šeqer śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ʾal-yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin): 'Let not those rejoice over me who are wrongfully my foes, nor let those wink the eye who hate me without cause.' The key phrase śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ('those who hate me without cause') parallels v.7's ḥinnām — the unprovoked nature of the hatred is repeated. John 15:25 cites this phrase: 'They hated me without a cause,' applying the psalmist's experience of arbitrary persecution to Jesus. The gesture yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin ('wink the eye') is the gesture of hidden malice — the concealed pleasure of seeing another suffer, expressed through a private eye-movement that signals to confederates while maintaining a public face.
35.20: kî lōʾ šālôm yĕdabbĕrû wĕʿal ragʿê-ʾereṣ diwrê mirmôt yaḥăšōbûn (kî lōʾ šālôm yĕḏabbĕrû wĕʿal raḡʿê-ʾereṣ diḇrê mirmôṯ yaḥăšōḇûn): 'For they do not speak peace, but against those who are quiet in the land they devise words of deceit.' The contrast is between šālôm ('peace' — the content of righteous speech) and diwrê mirmôt ('words of deceit'). The target rĕgê hāʾāreṣ ('the quiet ones of the land') are those who live peaceably, uninvolved in conflict — precisely those who should be unmolested. The enemies target the peaceful: their predation is not retaliatory but predatory.
35.21: wayyarḥîbû ʿālay pîhem ʾāmĕrû heʾāḥ heʾāḥ rāʾătâ ʿênênû (wayyarḥîbû ʿālay pîhem ʾāmĕrû heʾāḥ heʾāḥ rāʾāṯâ ʿênênû): 'They open wide their mouths against me; they say, 'Aha, aha! Our eyes have seen it!'' The verb rāḥab ('to be wide, broad') in the hiphil wayyarḥîbû ('they opened wide') applied to the mouth describes the maximum exposure of mockery — the gaping maw of contempt (cf. Ps 22:13). The exclamation heʾāḥ heʾāḥ ('aha! aha!') is the vocalization of satisfied malice — the sound of enemies witnessing what they desired. The claim rāʾătâ ʿênênû ('our eyes have seen') is triumphant: they have witnessed the psalmist's downfall and now claim confirmation of their judgment against him.
35.22: rāʾîtâ YHWH ʾal-teḥĕraš ʾădōnāy ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî (rāʾîṯā YHWH ʾal-teḥĕraš ʾăḏōnāy ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî): 'You have seen, O LORD; be not silent! O Lord, be not far from me!' The perfect rāʾîtâ ('you have seen') turns the enemies' boast (v.21, 'our eyes have seen') against itself: YHWH has also seen, and his seeing is the ground of hope. The negative imperatives ʾal-teḥĕraš ('do not be silent!') and ʾal-tirḥaq ('do not be far!') pray against two modes of divine abandonment: silence (non-speech) and distance (non-presence). The psalmist wants YHWH to convert his silent seeing into active speech and presence.
35.23: hāʿîrâ wĕhāqîṣâ lĕmišpāṭî ʾĕlōhay waʾdōnāy lĕrîbî (hāʿîrâ wĕhāqîṣâ lĕmišpāṭî ʾĕlōhay waʾḏōnāy lĕrîḇî): 'Awake and rouse yourself for my vindication, for my cause, my God and my Lord!' The imperatives hāʿîrâ ('awake!') and hāqîṣâ ('rouse yourself!') treat YHWH as sleeping — the bold anthropomorphism of Ps 44:23 ('awake! why are you sleeping, O Lord?'). The cause is lĕmišpāṭî lĕrîbî ('for my vindication, for my lawsuit') — legal categories. YHWH is summoned as judge (ʾĕlōhay, 'my God') and advocate (waʾdōnāy, 'my Lord') in a case where the enemies have brought false testimony (v.11).
