35.24: šopĕṭēnî kĕṣidqĕkā YHWH ʾĕlōhāy wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî (šop̄ĕṭēnî ḵĕṣiḏqĕḵā YHWH ʾĕlōhāy wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî): 'Vindicate me, O LORD my God, according to your righteousness, and let them not rejoice over me!' The imperative šopĕṭēnî ('vindicate me, judge me') grounds the request in kĕṣidqĕkā ('according to your righteousness') — the psalmist appeals not to his own merit but to YHWH's covenant character. The prayer against rejoicing — wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ('let them not rejoice over me') — returns to the opening petition (v.4: yiśmĕḥû ʿālay ʾōyĕbay). The framing concern is the enemy's gloating, which would be a public victory for wickedness over covenant righteousness.
35.25: ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām heʾāḥ napšēnû ʾal-yōʾmĕrû billa'ănûhû (ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām heʾāḥ napšēnû ʾal-yōʾmĕrû billa'ănûhû): 'Let them not say in their hearts, 'Aha, our soul's desire!' Let them not say, 'We have swallowed him up.'' The inner speech ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām ('let them not say in their hearts') returns to the enemies' self-talk. The exclamation heʾāḥ napšēnû ('aha, what our soul desired!') reveals the predatory appetite behind the attack: the psalmist's downfall is what the enemies' nepeš ('appetite, desire') wanted. The metaphor billa'ănûhû ('we have swallowed him up') pictures consumption — the psalmist as prey devoured whole.
35.26: yēbōšû wĕyikklĕmû yaḥdāw śĕmēḥê rāʿātî (yēḇōšû wĕyikkālĕmû yaḥdāw śĕmēḥê rāʿāṯî): 'Let them be put to shame and disappointed altogether who rejoice at my calamity.' The verb śāmaḥ ('rejoice') at rāʿātî ('my calamity') identifies the enemies by their pleasure in another's suffering — Schadenfreude as a theological category. The shame they sought to impose on the psalmist is redirected: yēbōšû ('let them be shamed') and yikklĕmû ('let them be disgraced'). The additional prayer yilbĕšû-bōšet ûkĕlimmâ hammitgaddĕlîm ʿālay, 'let those who magnify themselves against me be clothed with shame and dishonor' — clothing is a metaphor for complete encasement in identity: shame becomes their permanent garment.
35.27: yārōnnû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḥăpēṣê ṣidqî (yārōnnû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḥăp̄ēṣê ṣiḏqî): 'Let those who delight in my righteousness shout for joy and be glad.' Against the enemies who delight in calamity (v.26) stand those who delight in ṣidqî ('my righteousness/vindication'): the community that wants righteous outcomes for the righteous. The phrase wĕyōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal YHWH haḥāpēṣ šĕlôm ʿabdô, 'and let them say continually, 'Great is the LORD, who delights in the peace of his servant!'' — the desired outcome of rescue is communal praise. YHWH's delight in šālôm for his servant is the theological assertion that peace for the righteous is not incidental but purposeful divine will.
35.28: ûlĕšônî tehgeh ṣidqātĕkā kol-hayyôm tĕhillātekā (ûlĕšônî ṯehgeh ṣiḏqāṯĕḵā ḵol-hayyôm tĕhillāṯeḵā): 'Then my tongue shall tell of your righteousness and of your praise all the day long.' The concluding vow ûlĕšônî tehgeh ('and my tongue shall murmur/declare') from hāgâ ('to mutter, meditate, declare') — the word used in Ps 1:2 for meditating on Torah — closes the imprecatory psalm with praise. The objects ṣidqātĕkā ('your righteousness') and tĕhillātekā ('your praise') are the content of the ongoing declaration: YHWH's righteous vindication of the innocent becomes the substance of a lifetime of worship. The imprecatory prayer resolves not in violence but in testimony.

36.1: nĕʾum-peša lārāšāʿ bĕqereb libbî (nĕʾum-p̄ešaʿ lārāšāʿ bĕqereb libbî): 'Transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart.' The noun nĕʾum ('oracle, utterance') — normally reserved for prophetic speech (nĕʾum YHWH, 'oracle of the LORD') — is here attributed to peša ('transgression'): sin functions as a false prophet within the wicked person, uttering its own counterfeit oracle. The phrase bĕqereb libbî ('within my heart') is textually difficult — some read libbô ('his heart'): the oracle of transgression lodges in the wicked man's inner life. The result: ʾên paḥad ʾĕlōhîm lĕneged ʿênāyw, 'there is no fear of God before his eyes' (Paul quotes this in Rom 3:18, completing his OT catena of universal sinfulness).
36.2: kî-heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw bĕʿênāyw limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ (kî-heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw bĕʿênāyw limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ): 'For he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated.' The hiphil heḥĕlîq ('to make smooth, flatter') applied reflexively — heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw ('he flatters himself') — describes the self-deceptive sycophancy of the sinner's internal voice: the false oracle of v.1 tells him he is fine. The phrase limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ, 'to find out his iniquity and hate it,' describes the moral investigation that would expose him — but self-flattery prevents this self-knowledge. The wicked man's sin is invisible to himself; the blindness is the sin's deepest effect.
36.3: dibrê pîw ʾāwen ûmirmâ ḥādal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîb (diḇrê p̄îw ʾāwen ûmirmâ ḥāḏal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîḇ): 'The words of his mouth are trouble and deceit; he has ceased to act wisely and do good.' The mouth (peh) is again the diagnostic organ: dibrê ʾāwen ûmirmâ ('words of wickedness and deceit') reveal the heart's corruption (cf. Matt 12:34). The phrase ḥādal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîb, 'he has ceased to act wisely and do good,' uses the perfect ḥādal ('to cease, stop') — the cessation of wisdom and goodness is not a gradual decline but an accomplished break. The wicked man has definitively stopped the two activities that define the righteous: śākal (act wisely) and hêṭîb (do good/act beneficially).
36.4: ʾāwen yĕḥaššōb ʿal-miškābô yityaṣṣēb ʿal-derek lōʾ-ṭôb rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās (ʾāwen yĕḥaššōḇ ʿal-mišḵāḇô yiṯyaṣṣēḇ ʿal-ḏereḵ lōʾ-ṭôḇ rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās): 'He plots trouble on his bed; he sets himself in a way that is not good; he does not reject evil.' The bedroom (miškāb) is where unguarded thought happens — and the wicked man's nocturnal activity is planning ʾāwen ('iniquity, wickedness'). The hitpael yityaṣṣēb ('he stations himself, sets himself') describes deliberate positioning on derek lōʾ-ṭôb ('a way that is not good') — understated irony: it is not merely 'not good' but actively evil. The conclusion rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās, 'evil he does not reject,' completes the portrait: night-scheming, deliberate path-taking, and the refusal to refuse evil. This is the full portrait of the hardened wicked, in contrast to the entering worshippers of Ps 24.
36.5: YHWH baššāmayim ḥasdekā ʾĕmûnātĕkā ʿad-šĕḥāqîm (YHWH baššāmayim ḥasdĕḵā ʾĕmûnāṯĕḵā ʿaḏ-šĕḥāqîm): 'Your steadfast love, O LORD, extends to the heavens; your faithfulness to the clouds.' After the portrait of the wicked (vv.1-4), the psalm pivots sharply to the fullness of divine character. The spatial imagery — baššāmayim ('to the heavens') and ʿad-šĕḥāqîm ('to the clouds') — uses the vertical dimension to express limitlessness: ḥesed ('steadfast love') and ʾĕmûnâ ('faithfulness, reliability') are not bounded attributes but reach upward without ceiling. The contrast is complete: the wicked's reach is to his bed and to wickedness; YHWH's reach is to the sky.
36.6: ṣidqātĕkā kĕhārê ʾēl mišpāṭêkā tĕhôm rabbâ (ṣiḏqāṯĕḵā kĕhārê ʾēl mišpāṭêkā ṯĕhôm rabbâ): 'Your righteousness is like the mountains of God; your judgments are like the great deep.' The two similes create a vertical pair: ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness') compared to hārê ʾēl ('mountains of God,' i.e., mighty/towering mountains) — high and immovable. mišpāṭîm ('judgments') compared to tĕhôm rabbâ ('the great deep') — the primordial abyss whose depth is unfathomable. Both are inaccessible to full human comprehension but utterly reliable: the righteousness and judgment of YHWH are as real and as beyond human measure as the mountains and the ocean depths. Paul's doxology in Rom 11:33 echoes this: 'how unsearchable are his judgments!'
36.7: mah-yāqār ḥasdĕkā ʾĕlōhîm ûbĕnê ʾādām bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā yeḥĕsāyûn (mah-yāqār ḥasdĕḵā ʾĕlōhîm ûḇĕnê ʾāḏām bĕṣēl kĕnāp̄êḵā yeḥĕsāyûn): 'How precious is your steadfast love, O God! The children of man take refuge in the shadow of your wings.' The exclamatory mah-yāqār ('how precious!') from yāqar ('to be precious, costly') applies economic language of extreme value to ḥesed. The image of refuge bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā ('in the shadow of your wings') — the shadow being cool, protective, and intimate — draws on the mother-bird sheltering her young (Deut 32:11; Ps 17:8; 57:1; Matt 23:37). The phrase ûbĕnê ʾādām ('the children of man') universalizes the refuge: it is available to all humanity, not only to Israel, which is the psalm's implicit mission theology.
36.8: yirwĕyûn middešen bêtekā wĕnaḥal ʿădānêkā tašqēm (yirwĕyûn middešen bêṯeḵā wĕnaḥal ʿăḏānêḵā ṯašqēm): 'They feast on the abundance of your house, and you give them drink from the river of your delights.' The verb rāwâ ('to be saturated, drink one's fill') combined with middešen bêtekā ('from the richness/fat of your house') describes the sanctuary meal — the ḥesed of YHWH materialized as rich food. The phrase naḥal ʿădānêkā ('the river of your delights') uses the noun ʿeden — a word connected to 'Eden' (Gen 2:8) and meaning 'luxury, delight.' The river of YHWH's delights is the antithesis of the desert and the image of eschatological abundance (cf. Rev 22:1-2, 'the river of the water of life').
36.9: kî ʿimmĕkā mĕqôr ḥayyîm bĕʾôrkā nirʾeh-ʾôr (kî ʿimmĕḵā mĕqôr ḥayyîm bĕʾôrĕḵā nirʾeh-ʾôr): 'For with you is the fountain of life; in your light do we see light.' The phrase mĕqôr ḥayyîm ('fountain of life') identifies YHWH as the source of all vitality — life does not merely come from YHWH but is located ʿimmĕkā ('with you'). The chiastic bĕʾôrkā nirʾeh-ʾôr ('in your light we see light') is among the most theologically dense phrases in the OT: the light by which we perceive anything is itself derived from divine light. John's prologue echoes this: 'In him was life, and the life was the light of men' (John 1:4). The fountain and the light image together describe YHWH as the ground of both existence (life) and intelligibility (light).
36.10: mĕšōk ḥasdĕkā lĕyōdĕʿêkā wĕṣidqātĕkā lĕyišrê-lēb (mĕšōk ḥasdĕḵā lĕyôḏĕʿêḵā wĕṣiḏqāṯĕḵā lĕyišrê-lēḇ): 'Continue your steadfast love to those who know you, and your righteousness to the upright of heart!' The imperative mĕšōk ('draw out, prolong') from māšak ('to draw, extend') — as in drawing a line — applied to ḥesed asks for its continuation: not a new gift but the extension of an established pattern. The recipients lĕyōdĕʿêkā ('those who know you') and lĕyišrê-lēb ('the upright of heart') define the covenant community by knowledge and integrity — both qualities opposite to the wicked of vv.1-4.
36.11: ʾal-tĕbôʾēnî regel gaʾăwâ wĕyad-rĕšāʿîm ʾal-tĕnidēnî (ʾal-ṯĕḇôʾēnî regel gaʾăwâ wĕyaḏ-rĕšāʿîm ʾal-ṯĕniḏēnî): 'Let not the foot of arrogance come upon me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away.' The spatial language of the wicked — regel gaʾăwâ ('the foot of pride/arrogance') and yad rĕšāʿîm ('the hand of the wicked') — frames the threat as a territorial incursion: arrogance presses down with its foot, wickedness reaches with its hand to displace. The noun gaʾăwâ ('pride') is the sin that fuels the wicked of vv.1-4: the oracle of transgression (v.1) produces the prideful self-flattery (v.2) that enables all subsequent wrongdoing.
36.12: šām nāplû pōʿălê ʾāwen dĕḥû wĕlōʾ-yākĕlû qûm (šām nāplû p̄ōʿălê ʾāwen dĕḥû wĕlōʾ-yāḵĕlû qûm): 'There the evildoers lie fallen; they are thrust down, unable to rise.' The deictic šām ('there!') points to a specific location of judgment — perhaps the place where the wicked fell in past divine action, serving as a sign. The verbs nāplû ('they have fallen'), dĕḥû ('they were thrust down'), and lōʾ-yāḵĕlû qûm ('they are unable to rise') describe the finality of the wicked's collapse: the fall is complete, the fall is passive (thrust down, not merely stumbled), and there is no recovery. Against the living waters of v.9 and the refuge under divine wings of v.7, the wicked have chosen the darkness of their bed-plotting (v.4) — and this is its end. The psalm closes with the scene of the wicked prostrate and immobile, the mirror-image of the righteous 'satisfied with your fat' (v.8).

28.1: צוּרִי אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ מִמֶּנִּי (ṣûrî ʾal-teḥĕrash mimmenî, "my Rock, do not be deaf to me") — ḥārash: to be deaf or silent. Divine silence experienced as deafness — the unanswered prayer feels like a stone wall. The appeal to ṣûr (rock/cliff) is precisely because rocks do not move; the Psalmist asks the immovable one not to be unmovable toward him. נִמְשַׁלְתִּי עִם-יֹורְדֵי בוֹר (nimshaltî ʿim-yôrĕdê bôr, "I will be like those who go down to the pit") — bôr: a cistern, a pit — by extension the grave and underworld. Divine silence = death, because only the living praise God (Ps 30:9).
28.2: בְּנָשְׂאִי יָדַי אֶל-דְּבִיר קָדְשֶׁךָ (bĕnāśĕʾî yāday ʾel-dĕbîr qodshekā, "as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place") — dĕbîr: the inner chamber, the Holy of Holies — the back room of the temple (from dābar: that which is behind). The ritual lifting of hands was a standard prayer posture (1 Tim 2:8). Solomon prays that God would hear prayers directed toward this place even from a distance (1 Kgs 8:29-30).
28.3: הַמְדַבְּרִים שָׁלוֹם עִם-רֵעֵיהֶם (hĕmĕdabbĕrîm shālôm ʿim-rêʿêhem, "who speak peace to their neighbors") — the wicked who use shālôm (peace) as a social greeting while harboring רָעָה בִּלְבָבָם (rāʿāh bilĕbābām, "evil in their hearts"). The heart-speech contrast: social performance of shālôm conceals interior hostility. The two-tongue motif recurs in Ps 12:2-3 and Jas 3:10. The lêb (heart/mind) as the true location of the self, unmasked by God (Ps 7:9).
28.4: תֶּן-לָהֶם כְּפָעֳלָם (ten-lāhem kĕpoʿŏlām, "Pay them back for what they have done") — pōʿal (deed, work — from pāʿal: to work, to do): a different word from maʿăśeh (artistic work); pōʿal focuses on the act as a moral deed. The imprecation follows the lex talionis logic: the return is proportional to the kĕpoʿŏlām (according to their doing). The prayer for divine judgment is not vengeance-seeking but covenant appeal — God is the judge who must respond consistently to wickedness.
28.5: כִּי לֹא יָבִינוּ אֶל-פְּעֻלֹּת יְהוָה (kî lōʾ yābînû ʾel-pĕʿullōt YHWH, "Because they take no account of the LORD's works") — bîn: to perceive, discern, pay attention to — the failure is moral inattention to what is visible. Pĕʿullōt YHWH (the LORD's deeds): the deeds of God in history that demand theological interpretation. יֶהֶרְסֵם וְלֹא יִבְנֵם (yehĕrsêm wĕlōʾ yibnêm, "he will pull them down and not rebuild them") — hāras (to demolish) vs bānāh (to build): total and unreversed demolition.
28.6: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה (bārûk YHWH, "Praise the LORD!") — the abrupt reversal from petition (vv1-5) to praise (vv6-9). כִּי שָׁמַע קוֹל תַּחֲנוּנָי (kî shāmaʿ qôl taḥanûnāy, "for he has heard my cry for mercy") — taḥanûn: supplication, plea for grace (from ḥānan: to be gracious). The perfect tense shāmaʿ (he heard) registers certainty of divine response before it is visibly confirmed — faith that the prayer has been received.
28.7: יְהוָה עֻזִּי וּמָגִנִּי (YHWH ʿuzzî ûmāginnî, "The LORD is my strength and my shield") — ʿōz (strength/power — same word as Ps 8:2 where it also means "praise") and māgên (the small personal shield). וַיַּעֲלֹז לִבִּי (wayyaʿălōz libbî, "my heart leaps for joy") — ʿālaz: to exult, to leap for joy — a physical, exuberant rejoicing (used of the earth rejoicing, Ps 96:12). The joy is bodily, not merely cognitive.
28.8: יְהוָה עֹז-לָמוֹ וּמָעוֹז יְשׁוּעוֹת מְשִׁיחוֹ (YHWH ʿōz-lāmô ûmāʿôz yĕshûʿôt mĕshîḥô, "The LORD is the strength of his people; he is the saving fortress of his anointed one") — mĕshîaḥ: his anointed one. The Davidic king is the LORD's māshîaḥ (Messiah), and the protection of the people flows through the anointed. Māʿôz yĕshûʿôt: "fortress of salvations" — the multiple acts of rescue collected under this image. For the NT, the title māshîaḥ makes this the language of the one through whom all divine saving strength reaches the people.
28.9: וּרְעֵם וְנַשְּׂאֵם עַד-הָעוֹלָם (ûrĕʿêm wĕnassĕʾêm ʿad-hāʿôlām, "be their shepherd and carry them forever") — two parental/pastoral verbs: rāʿāh (to shepherd, to tend) and nāśāʾ (to carry, to bear — as a parent carries a child, Deut 1:31). The combination of shepherding and carrying is the image of the LORD who both leads and physically bears the people. ʿad-hāʿôlām: forever — the eternal scope of the carrying. Jesus as the Good Shepherd who carries the lost sheep home (Luke 15:5) draws on this pastoral register.

29.1: הָבוּ לַיהוָה בְּנֵי אֵלִים (hābû laYHWH bĕnê ʾêlîm, "Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings") — bĕnê ʾêlîm: "sons of God/divine beings" — the heavenly council that surrounds the divine throne (Job 1:6; Ps 82:1, 6). The psalm opens by commanding the divine council to worship YHWH — not Israel, but the celestial assembly itself. This is a remarkable claim about YHWH's supremacy in the heavenly realm.
29.2: הָבוּ לַיהוָה כְּבוֹד שְׁמוֹ (hābû laYHWH kĕbôd shĕmô, "Give the LORD the glory due his name") — kābôd: weight, honor, splendor — the overwhelming presence of God. הִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לַיהוָה בְּהַדְרַת-קֹדֶשׁ (hishtaḥăwû laYHWH bĕhadrat-qōdesh, "Worship the LORD in the splendor of his holiness") — hadrat-qōdesh: a debated phrase; "splendor/glory of holiness" or "holy attire/array." Either way, worship is not casual but appropriately clothed in what holiness requires.
29.3: קוֹל יְהוָה עַל-הַמָּיִם (qôl YHWH ʿal-hammāyim, "The voice of the LORD is over the waters") — the first of seven qôl YHWH (Voice of the LORD) occurrences in vv3-9, functioning as a liturgical refrain. אֵל-הַכָּבוֹד הִרְעִים (ʾêl-hakkābôd hirʿîm, "the God of glory thunders") — ʾêl hakkābôd: "the God of glory" — a unique phrase in the Psalter. Stephen cites it in Acts 7:2 ("The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham"). The title identifies YHWH as the possessor of the divine kābôd (weight/presence) that the divine council was commanded to ascribe to him.
29.4: קוֹל-יְהוָה בַּכֹּחַ (qôl-YHWH bakkōaḥ, "The voice of the LORD is powerful") — kōaḥ: strength, force, power — the raw physical energy of the divine voice (thunder). קוֹל יְהוָה בֶּהָדָר (qôl YHWH behādār, "the voice of the LORD is majestic") — hādār: splendor, majesty, beauty — the aesthetic dimension alongside brute force. These two terms (power + majesty) form the Ugaritic storm-god Baal's vocabulary, here entirely reassigned to YHWH — the theological polemic is embedded in the word-choice.
29.5: קוֹל יְהוָה שֹׁבֵר אֲרָזִים (qôl YHWH shōbêr ʾărāzîm, "The voice of the LORD shatters the cedars") — shābar: to shatter, to break into pieces. The cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê hallĕbānôn) were the largest, most permanent trees in the ancient world — symbols of royal power and permanence (Isa 2:13; Ezek 31:3). If the divine voice shatters what no human force can break, it is omnipotent.
29.6: וַיַּרְקִידֵם כְּמוֹ-עֵגֶל (wayyarqîdêm kĕmô-ʿêgel, "He makes Lebanon skip like a calf") — rāqad: to skip, to dance, to leap (the joy-dance). The mountain-shattering force that breaks cedars causes the mountains to dance like calves. Sîryôn (Sirion): the Phoenician name for Mount Hermon (Deut 3:9). The wild ox (rĕʾêm) was the most uncontrollable animal known in the ancient world — yet it "skips" before the divine voice.
29.7: קוֹל יְהוָה חֹצֵב לַהֲבוֹת אֵשׁ (qôl YHWH ḥōṣêb lahăbôt ʾêsh, "The voice of the LORD strikes with flashes of lightning") — ḥāṣab: to hew, to cut out, to carve — the lightning as if chiseled from the storm. Lahăbôt ʾêsh: "flames of fire" — lightning as fire-flames cut from the sky. The creative/destructive ambiguity: the same verb used for stonecutting is used for the lightning-bolt, as if God is a craftsman cutting fire from the sky.
29.8: קוֹל יְהוָה יָחִיל מִדְבָּר (qôl YHWH yāḥîl midbār, "The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness") — ḥûl: to writhe, to tremble, to be in labor — the same verb used in v9 for the deer writhing in labor. The wilderness (midbār) itself writhes at the divine voice. Midbār qādesh: the wilderness of Kadesh — the southern region through which Israel wandered, now the scene of theophanic power.
29.9: קוֹל יְהוָה יְחוֹלֵל אַיָּלוֹת (qôl YHWH yĕḥôlêl ʾayyālôt, "The voice of the LORD causes the deer to writhe in labor") — yāḥal (polel: to cause to writhe/bring to birth): the divine voice triggers labor contractions in the deer. The same voice that breaks cedars also determines the moment of birthing — control over both the large and the small. וּבְהֵיכָלוֹ כֻּלּוֹ אֹמֵר כָּבוֹד (ûbĕhêkālô kullô ʾōmêr kābôd, "in his temple all cry, 'Glory!'") — the seventh and final qôl YHWH produces the worshiping cry: kābôd (glory). The heavenly assembly responds with the one word that encapsulates all ascription.
29.10: יְהוָה לַמַּבּוּל יָשָׁב (YHWH lammabbûl yāshāb, "The LORD sat enthroned over the Flood") — mabbûl: the Noahic flood — the only use of this specific word in the Psalter. It is the exclusive word Genesis uses for Noah's flood (Gen 6-9), not the generic flood-word (shéṭeph). YHWH was enthroned above the primordial chaos-flood — the creator was not threatened but in sovereign session above it. וַיֵּשֶׁב יְהוָה מֶלֶךְ לְעוֹלָם (wayyêsheb YHWH melek lĕʿôlām, "the LORD sits enthroned as King forever") — the one who sat above the primordial flood is the one who sits forever.
29.11: יְהוָה עֹז לְעַמּוֹ יִתֵּן (YHWH ʿōz lĕʿammô yittên, "The LORD gives strength to his people") — the same ʿōz (strength) that the heavenly beings were commanded to ascribe to YHWH (v1) is now given by YHWH to his people. The divine strength is not hoarded but distributed. יְהוָה יְבָרֵךְ אֶת-עַמּוֹ בַשָּׁלוֹם (YHWH yĕbārêk ʾet-ʿammô bashshālôm, "the LORD blesses his people with peace") — the storm-theophany psalm ends with shālôm (peace, wholeness). The God whose voice shatters cedars and triggers labor blesses his people with peace. The power is the foundation of the peace: only the one mightier than every storm can give true shālôm.

30.1: The superscription reads שִׁיר חֲנֻכַּת הַבַּיִת לְדָוִד (shîr ḥanukkāt habbayit lĕdāwid, "A Song for the Dedication of the Temple, of David") — ḥanukkāh: dedication (from ḥānak: to initiate, to dedicate). The same word as the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah) in John 10:22. The verb אֲרוֹמִמְךָ (ʾărômimkā, "I exalt you") — rûm (hiphil: to lift up, to exalt): the Psalmist "lifts up" God in response to God having "lifted him up" — דִּלִּיתָנִי (dillîtānî, "you have lifted me up") from dālāh: to draw up (as drawing water from a well). The rescue is like drawing someone up from a cistern.
30.2: אֱלֹהַי שִׁוַּעְתִּי אֵלֶיךָ וַתִּרְפָּאֵנִי (ʾĕlōhay shiwwaʿtî ʾêlêkā wattirpāʾênî, "I cried to you for help, and you healed me") — shāwaʿ: to cry for help (same root as the prayer-cry in Ps 28:2); rāpāʾ: to heal. The healing may be literal recovery from illness or a metaphor for deliverance from life-threatening danger (v3 suggests near-death). The divine name ʾĕlōhay (my God) — the first-person possessive relationship as the ground for the cry.
30.3: יְהוָה הֶעֱלִיתָ מִן-שְׁאוֹל נַפְשִׁי (YHWH heʿĕlîtā min-shĕʾôl napshî, "LORD, you brought my soul up from the realm of the dead") — shĕʾôl: the underworld, the comprehensive OT term for the abode of the departed. חִיִּיתַנִי מִיּוֹרְדֵי-בוֹר (ḥiyyîtanî miyôrĕdê-bôr, "you restored my life from among those who descend to the pit") — bôr: cistern/pit (as in Ps 28:1) — a second term for the underworld focused on its depth and darkness. Ḥāyāh (piel: to keep alive, to give life): God preserving the Psalmist from joining the descent.
30.4: זַמְּרוּ לַיהוָה חֲסִידָיו (zammĕrû laYHWH ḥăsîdāyw, "Sing praises to the LORD, O you his faithful ones") — zāmar: to make music with instruments — the word behind mizmôr (psalm). Ḥăsîdîm: the faithful/loyal ones — the recipients of God's ḥesed (covenant love) who in turn are loyal to him. לְזֵכֶר קָדְשׁוֹ (lĕzêker qodshô, "and give thanks to his holy name") — zêker: memory, memorial — the "memory of his holiness" as what is acknowledged in thanksgiving.
30.5: כִּי רֶגַע בְּאַפּוֹ חַיִּים בִּרְצוֹנוֹ (kî regaʿ bĕʾappô ḥayyîm birṣônô, "For his anger lasts only a moment, but his favor lasts a lifetime") — regaʿ: a moment, an instant — the briefest possible time unit. ʾAp (nostril/anger) contrasted with rāṣôn (favor, pleasure, delight). לָעֶרֶב יָלִין בֶּכִי וְלַבֹּקֶר רִנָּה (lāʿereb yālîn bekî wĕlabbōqer rinnāh, "weeping may remain through the night, but joy arrives with the morning") — yālîn: to lodge, to spend the night as a temporary guest. Weeping spends the night but is gone by morning; rinnāh (the ringing shout of joy) comes with dawn.
30.6: וַאֲנִי אָמַרְתִּי בְשַׁלְוִי (waʾănî ʾāmartî bĕshalwî, "When I was prosperous, I said") — shalwāh: ease, tranquility, prosperity. The Psalmist's confession: prosperity breeds presumption. בַּל-אֶמּוֹט לְעוֹלָם (bal-ʾemmôṭ lĕʿôlām, "I will never be shaken") — māṭ: to totter, to be shaken, to slip. The confident claim of permanent stability — precisely the kind of claim that invites divine testing (vv7b-10). The false security of prosperity not grounded in divine favor.
30.7: יְהוָה בִּרְצוֹנְךָ הֶעֱמַדְתָּה לְהַרְרִי-עֹז (YHWH birṣônĕkā heʿĕmadtāh lĕharrî-ʿōz, "By your favor, LORD, you had made my mountain stand firm") — the Psalmist now acknowledges what he failed to see in v6: the stability was not inherent but the product of divine rāṣôn (favor). הִסְתַּרְתָּ פָנֶיךָ הָיִיתִי נִבְהָל (histartā pānêkā hāyîtî nibhāl, "when you hid your face, I was terrified") — the theology of the hidden God: hester pānim (hiding of the face) = YHWH withdrawing active presence. Bāhal (niphal: to be terrified): the consequence of divine withdrawal is existential dismay.
30.8: אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה אֶקְרָא (ʾêlêkā YHWH ʾeqrāʾ, "To you, LORD, I called out") — the natural response to the divine face-hiding is not despair but prayer. Qārāʾ (to call, to cry out): the same call of vv2-3 that was heard and healed. The Psalmist did not turn to other sources of help when the divine face was hidden; he called more urgently to the one who hid it.
30.9: מַה-בֶּצַע בְּדָמִי (māh-beṣaʿ bĕdāmî, "What is gained if I die?") — beṣaʿ: gain, profit (often of commercial gain). The argument from divine interest: what does God gain from the Psalmist's death? הֲיוֹדֶה עָפָר אֱמִתֶּךָ (hăyôdeh ʿāpār ʾĕmittekā, "Can the dust praise you? Can it proclaim your faithfulness?") — ʿāpār (dust): what the dead return to (Gen 3:19). The implicit argument: the living praise; the dead are silent. God's interest in his own glory is best served by keeping the Psalmist alive — not manipulation but the logic of covenant.
30.10: שְׁמַע-יְהוָה וְחָנֵּנִי (shĕmaʿ-YHWH wĕḥonnênî, "Hear me, LORD, and have mercy on me!") — ḥānan: to be gracious, to show mercy/favor (same root as taḥanûn in Ps 28:2). The plea for ḥēn (grace): unearned divine favor. יְהוָה הֱיֵה-עֹזֵר לִי (YHWH hĕyêh-ʿōzêr lî, "LORD, be my helper!") — the simple, direct appeal in the moment of need.
30.11: הָפַכְתָּ מִסְפְּדִי לְמָחוֹל לִי (hāpaktā mispĕdî lĕmāḥôl lî, "You have turned my mourning into dancing") — hāpak: to turn, to overturn, to reverse — the same reversal-word used for the overturning of Sodom (Gen 19:25). Mispēd: mourning, formal lamentation. Māḥôl: circle-dancing (from ḥûl: to whirl in circles) — exuberant communal celebration. פִּתַּחְתָּ שַׂקִּי וַתְּאַזְּרֵנִי שִׂמְחָה (pittaḥtā śaqqî wattĕʾazzĕrênî śimḥāh, "you have removed my sackcloth and clothed me with joy") — śaq: sackcloth — the rough mourning-garment; śimḥāh (joy) as the new garment. Joy as something worn, not merely felt.
30.12: לְמַעַן יְזַמֶּרְךָ כָבוֹד וְלֹא יִדֹּם (lĕmaʿan yĕzammĕrĕkā kābôd wĕlōʾ yiddōm, "so that my spirit may sing your praise and not be silent") — kābôd here may mean "my honor/my soul" (the internal, honored self) rather than "glory." Yiddōm (to be silent, to be still): the counterpart to the silence of death (v9). The rescued soul cannot be silent — silence is for the dead. יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי לְעוֹלָם אוֹדֶךָּ (YHWH ʾĕlōhay lĕʿôlām ʾôdekā, "O LORD my God, I will give you thanks forever") — the closing vow: forever-praise as the response to the forever-favor of v5.

31.1: בְּךָ יְהוָה חָסִיתִי (bĕkā YHWH ḥāsîtî, "In you, LORD, I take refuge") — ḥāsāh: to take refuge, to seek shelter (as under the wings of a parent bird, Ps 57:1). The perfect tense of settled trust: "I have taken refuge and remain there." בְּצִדְקָתְךָ פַלְּטֵנִי (bĕṣidqātĕkā pallĕṭênî, "rescue me by your righteousness") — ṣĕdāqāh: righteousness, covenant-faithfulness. The rescue is grounded in the LORD's own covenantal integrity — not in the Psalmist's merit. God's righteousness means he must rescue those who trust in him, because his covenant commits him to it.
31.2: הַטֵּה אֵלַי אָזְנְךָ מְהֵרָה הַצִּילֵנִי (haṭṭeh ʾêlay ʾāznĕkā mĕhêrāh haṣṣîlênî, "Turn your ear to me; quickly rescue me") — nāṭāh (hiphil: to incline/bend down to hear a faint voice). Mĕhêrāh: quickly, speedily — urgency. לְצוּר-מָעוֹז לְבֵית מְצוּדוֹת לְהוֹשִׁיעֵנִי — the cluster of spatial metaphors: ṣûr (rock/cliff), māʿôz (stronghold), bêt mĕṣûdôt (house of fortresses) — layered protection: rock → fortress → fortified building within the fortress.
31.3: כִּי-סַלְעִי וּמְצוּדָתִי אָתָּה (kî-salʿî ûmĕṣûdātî ʾāttāh, "For you are my rock and my fortress") — selaʿ: the cliff-face of rock (as in Ps 18:2). וּלְמַעַן שִׁמְךָ תַּנְחֵנִי וּתְנַהֲלֵנִי (ûlĕmaʿan shimkā tanḥênî ûtĕnahălênî, "lead me and guide me for your name's sake") — nāḥāh: to guide (as a shepherd leads); nāhal: to lead gently to water (as a shepherd leads to watering-places, Ps 23:2). The double shepherding verbs. Lĕmaʿan shimkā: "for the sake of your name" — God's own reputation as the motivation for his action.
31.4: תּוֹצִיאֵנִי מֵרֶשֶׁת זוּ טָמְנוּ-לִי (tôṣîʾênî mêreshet zû ṭāmĕnû-lî, "Free me from the trap they have secretly set for me") — reshet: a net, a web — the hunting net laid for birds. Ṭāman: to hide, to conceal — the trap is secretly laid; the danger is not visible until it springs. The enemies as hunters who hide their devices.
31.5: בְּיָדְךָ אַפְקִיד רוּחִי (bĕyādĕkā ʾapqîd rûḥî, "Into your hand I commit my spirit") — pāqad (hiphil: to entrust, to deposit for safekeeping — the legal term for depositing valuables into another's custody). Rûaḥ: spirit, breath — the animating life-force, the innermost self. Jesus quotes this exact verse from the cross in Luke 23:46 (LXX: εἰς χεῖράς σου παρατίθεμαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου), making his death the ultimate act of trust in the Father. In Jewish evening prayer this verse was used daily — the surrender of the breath-self to divine keeping through the night. פָּדִיתָה אוֹתִי יְהוָה אֵל אֱמֶת (pādîtāh ʾôtî YHWH ʾêl ʾĕmet, "you have redeemed me, LORD, faithful God") — pādāh: to ransom, to redeem.
31.6: שָׂנֵאתִי הַשֹּׁמְרִים הַבְלֵי-שָׁוְא (śānêtî hashomĕrîm habalê-shāwľ, "I despise those who cling to worthless idols") — shamar (to keep, to cling to — here used for idol-devotion): the same verb for keeping commandments, deployed negatively for idol-adherence. Habalê-shāwľ: "vanities of emptiness" — hebel (breath/vanity — the word of Ecclesiastes) combined with shāwľ (worthlessness). The double-emptiness of idols. וַאֲנִי אֶל-יְהוָה בָּטָחְתִּי — the contrast: idol-clinging vs bāṭaḥ (trusting, leaning-on) that connects to the real.
31.7: אָגִילָה וְאֶשְׂמְחָה בְּחַסְדֶּךָ (ʾāgîlāh wĕʾesmĕḥāh bĕḥasdekā, "I will be glad and rejoice in your steadfast love") — gîl and śāmaḥ: twin verbs of joy (same pairing as Ps 21:1). Ḥesed: covenant-loyalty love. אֲשֶׁר רָאִיתָ אֶת-עָנְיִי יָדַעְתָּ בְּצָרוֹת נַפְשִׁי — rāʾāh: to see; yādaʿ: to know experientially. God not only observed the distress from a distance but knew it from within, entering into the experience of the Psalmist's suffering.
31.8: וְלֹא הִסְגַּרְתַּנִי בְּיַד-אוֹיֵב (wĕlōʾ hisgartanî bĕyad-ʾôyêb, "you did not hand me over to the enemy") — sāgar (hiphil: to deliver over as a prisoner): not handing over to the enemy. הֶעֱמַדְתָּ בַמֶּרְחָב רַגְלָי (heʿĕmadtā bammerhāb raglāy, "you set my feet in a wide-open place") — merehāb again (as in Ps 18:19; 4:1): salvation as liberation from the tight place (ṣar) into the wide space. God sets the feet in the open space — stable footing in an unconstrained environment.
31.9: כִּי צָרָה-לִי עֵינִי כִּי נַפְשִׁי וּבִטְנִי (kî ṣārāh-lî ʿênî kî napshî ûbiṭnî, "my eye wastes with grief — my soul and my body too") — grief affects three registers: ʿayin (eye — what perceives the world), nāpesh (soul — the animating self), and beṭen (belly/body — the physical container). The eye that has wasted (ʿāshash: to shrink, to waste) is the eye that has wept until hollow.
31.10: כִּי-כָלוּ בְיָגוֹן חַיַּי (kî-kālû bĕyāgôn ḥayyay, "For my life is exhausted by grief") — kālāh: to be completed/finished — life consumed by yāgôn (grief, sorrow). כָּשַׁל בַּעֲוֹנִי כֹחִי (kāshal baʿăwōnî kōḥî, "my strength fails because of my iniquity") — kāshal: to stumble, to stagger; ʿāwôn: iniquity, guilt. The confession that moral failure has physical consequences. ʿAṣmôtay ʿāshōshû: "my bones waste away" — the skeleton as deepest structural framework, now deteriorating.
31.11: מִכָּל-צֹרְרַי הָיִיתִי חֶרְפָּה (mikkol-ṣōrĕray hāyîtî ḥerpāh, "Among all my enemies I am an object of scorn") — ḥerpāh: reproach, scorn, shame — the public shame that falls on the defeated and guilty. לִמְיֻדָּעַי מְאֹד — mĕyuddāʿ (those who know me): the people who knew the Psalmist in his prosperity now flee from him. Social abandonment accompanies disgrace — a condition that mirrors Jesus' abandonment at the cross (Luke 23:49: "those who knew him stood at a distance").
31.12: נִשְׁכַּחְתִּי כְּמֵת מִלֵּב (nishkaḥtî kĕmêt millêb, "I am as forgotten as a dead man") — shākaḥ (niphal: to be forgotten): the social death of being forgotten while still alive. הָיִיתִי כִּכְלִי אֹבֵד (hāyîtî kiklî ʾōbêd, "I have become like a shattered clay vessel") — kelî: vessel, instrument; ʾōbêd: perishing, destroyed. The broken pottery that has no further use and is discarded.
31.13: כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי דִּבַּת רַבִּים (kî shāmaʿtî dibbat rabbîm, "I hear the rumors of the many") — dibbāh: a whispering, a defamatory report — the gossip-network. מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב (māgôr missābîb, "terror surrounds me") — māgôr missābîb is a Jeremian phrase (Jer 20:3, 10) — "terror on every side." Jeremiah uses it of himself and ironically gives it as the name of the priest who arrested him. Psalm and prophet share a vocabulary of total encirclement by threat.
31.14: וַאֲנִי עָלֶיךָ בָטַחְתִּי יְהוָה (waʾănî ʿālêkā bāṭaḥtî YHWH, "But I trusted in you, LORD") — the wĕʾănî (but I) counter to all of vv9-13: despite abandonment, wasting, forgotten status, and surrounding terror — the Psalmist's orientation remains. אָמַרְתִּי אֱלֹהַי אָתָּה (ʾāmartî ʾĕlōhay ʾāttāh, "I said, 'You are my God'") — the basic confession of covenant relationship: "my God." In the middle of catastrophe, the Psalmist speaks the relational claim.
31.15: בְּיָדְךָ עִתֹּתָי (bĕyādĕkā ʿittōtāy, "My times are in your hand") — ʿittîm: times, seasons, appointed moments (plural of ʿêt: a fixed time). Every moment of vulnerability and opportunity held in the divine hand. Not fate but covenantal custody: the same hand into which the spirit was committed (v5) now holds all the moments of the life. הַצִּילֵנִי מִיַּד-אֹיְבַי (haṣṣîlênî miyad-ʾōyĕbay, "rescue me from my enemies") — the contest of which hand holds the Psalmist: the enemy's hand or the LORD's.
31.16: הָאִירָה פָנֶיךָ עַל-עַבְדֶּךָ (hāʾîrāh pānêkā ʿal-ʿabdekā, "Make your face shine on your servant") — the priestly blessing of Num 6:25 ("the LORD make his face shine on you"): the illuminated divine face as the image of favorable presence. Contrast v7b: when the divine face was hidden, terror; when it shines, salvation. הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי בְחַסְדֶּךָ (hôshîʿênî bĕḥasdekā, "save me through your steadfast love") — grounded in ḥesed: rescue flows from covenant loyalty, not from the Psalmist's worthiness.
31.17: יֵבֹשׁוּ רְשָׁעִים יִדְּמוּ לִשְׁאוֹל (yêbōshû rĕshāʿîm yiddĕmû lishĕʾôl, "let the wicked be ashamed — let them go silently down to Sheol") — bûsh: to be ashamed, put to shame (the social disgrace of public failure). Dāmam: to be silent — the silence of Sheol (as opposed to the singing of the rescued Psalmist in Ps 30:12). The reversal: the Psalmist, who was shamed and nearly silent, prays that the wicked get what they intended for him.
31.18: תֵּאָלַמְנָה שִׂפְתֵי-שָׁקֶר (tēʾālamĕnāh śiptê-shāqer, "Let the lying lips be struck silent") — ʾālam (niphal: to be silenced, made mute): the lips that spoke the dibbāh (defamatory whispers) of v13 silenced. הַדֹּבְרוֹת עַל-צַדִּיק עָתָק בְּגַאֲוָה וָבוּז — ʿātāq: insolent, arrogant things; gaʾăwāh: pride; bûz: contempt. The attitude behind the speech: contempt for the righteous.
31.19: מָה-רַב טוּבְךָ אֲשֶׁר-צָפַנְתָּ לִּירֵאֶיךָ (māh-rab ṭûbĕkā ʾăsher-ṣāpantā lîrêʾêkā, "How great is your goodness, which you have stored up for those who fear you") — ṭôb: goodness, good things. Ṣāpan: to store up, to hide away for safekeeping — the goodness of God held in reserve like treasure. Lîrêʾêkā: "for those who fear you" — yirʾāh (fear of God) as the qualifier of who receives it. פָּעַלְתָּ לַחֹסִים בָּךְ — ḥāsāh (participle: those who take refuge): the shelter-seekers for whom stored goodness is actualized.
31.20: תַּסְתִּירֵם בְּסֵתֶר פָּנֶיךָ מֵרֻכְסֵי אִישׁ (tastîrêm bĕsêter pānêkā mêrukhsê ʾîsh, "In the shelter of your presence you hide them from human plots") — divine hiddenness now used positively: the sêter pānim (shelter of the face) as protective concealment. The very face whose hiding brought terror (v7) becomes the shelter of those who trust. תִּצְפְּנֵם בְּסֻכָּה מֵרִיב לְשֹׁנוֹת — hidden in the divine sukkāh (tabernacle/booth) from the quarreling of tongues — connecting to the Festival of Booths and divine dwelling among Israel.
31.21: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה כִּי-הִפְלִיא חַסְדּוֹ לִי (bārûk YHWH kî-hipllîʾ ḥasdô lî, "Praise the LORD, for he displayed his wonderful steadfast love to me") — pālāʾ (hiphil: to make wonderful, to act in a marvelous way): ḥesed niphlāʾ — love that operates beyond normal expectation. בְּעִיר מָצוֹר (bĕʿîr māṣôr, "when I was like a man in a besieged city") — māṣôr: siege — the city under siege as the situation of maximum danger. The Psalmist felt encircled (terror surrounds, v13) as a city under siege, yet from within that encirclement, divine love was displayed.
31.22: וַאֲנִי אָמַרְתִּי בְחָפְזִי (waʾănî ʾāmartî bĕḥāpĕzî, "I said in my panic") — ḥāpaz: to flee in haste, to be panicked — a significant word: it appears in Exod 12:11 and Deut 16:3 for the hasty departure from Egypt (bĕḥippāzôn: "in haste"). The Psalmist's panic uses the exodus-departure word — his inner flight from composed trust. נִגְרַזְתִּי מִנֶּגֶד עֵינֶיךָ (nigrăztî minneged ʿênêkā, "I am cut off from your sight") — the feeling of severance from the divine gaze. שָׁמַעְתָּ קוֹל תַּחֲנוּנַי — the truth under the panic: the feeling was wrong; the hearing was real.
31.23: אֶהֱבוּ אֶת-יְהוָה כָּל-חֲסִידָיו (ʾehĕbû ʾet-YHWH kol-ḥăsîdāyw, "Love the LORD, all you his faithful people!") — the move from individual testimony to community summons: the Psalmist's experience becomes the ground of exhortation to all the ḥăsîdîm. יְהוָה שֹׁמֵר אֱמוּנִים (YHWH shōmêr ʾĕmûnîm, "The LORD protects the faithful") — ʾĕmûnîm: the faithful ones (from ʾāman: to be firm, reliable, faithful). God watches over (shāmar) those characterized by ʾemûnāh (faithfulness).
31.24: חִזְקוּ וְיַאֲמֵץ לְבַבְכֶם (ḥizqû wĕyaʾămêṣ lĕbabkem, "Be strong, and let your heart be courageous") — ḥāzaq (to be strong) and ʾāmaṣ (to be bold/courageous) — the same two verbs of the Deuteronomic commission: "Be strong and courageous" (ḥazaq weʾĕmāṣ, Deut 31:6-7; Josh 1:6-9). כָּל-הַמְיַחֲלִים לַיהוָה (kol-hĕmĕyaḥălîm laYHWH, "all you who hope in the LORD!") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation, to hope actively. The Psalm ends with the summons to those who are in the position the Psalmist was in at the beginning — in distress, waiting for the LORD to act.

32.1: מַשְׂכִּיל לְדָוִד (maskîl lĕdāwid) — the superscription identifies this as a maskîl: a teaching/wisdom psalm (from śākal: to be wise, to act prudently). It is the first of the seven traditional "Penitential Psalms." אַשְׁרֵי נְשׂוּי-פֶּשַׁע (ʾashrê nĕśûy-peshaʿ, "Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven") — nāśāʾ (passive participle: lifted, carried away): the transgression (peshaʿ: willful rebellion) is carried away like a burden. This is the language of Isa 53:4-6 (the Servant "bore our transgressions"). כְּסוּי חֲטָאָה (kĕsûy ḥaṭṭāʾt, "whose sin is covered") — kāsāh: to cover, to clothe — the sin hidden as if beneath a garment. Paul quotes vv1-2 in Rom 4:7-8 as proof that the OT itself taught justification by grace through non-reckoning of sin.
32.2: אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם לֹא-יַחְשֹׁב יְהוָה לוֹ עָוֹן (ʾashrê ʾādām lōʾ-yaḥshōb YHWH lô ʿāwôn, "Blessed is the man to whom the LORD does not reckon iniquity") — ḥāshab: to reckon, to account, to calculate (an accounting term). Paul highlights this word in Rom 4:3-8 — it is the same word used for God "reckoning" righteousness to Abraham (Gen 15:6). The three terms of human guilt: peshaʿ (willful rebellion), ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/missing the mark), ʿāwôn (iniquity/guilt). The three terms of divine remedy: carried away, covered, not-reckoned. וְאֵין בְּרוּחוֹ רְמִיָּה (wĕʾên bĕrûḥô rĕmiyyāh, "in whose spirit there is no deceit") — rĕmiyyāh: deceit, guile — the absence of self-deception (contrast v3-4).
32.3: כִּי-הֶחֱשֵׁיתִי בָּלוּ עֲצָמָי (kî-heḥĕshêtî bālû ʿăṣāmāy, "When I kept silent about my sin, my bones wasted away") — ḥāshah (hiphil: to be silent) about the sin. Bālāh: to waste away, to deteriorate (as an old garment wears out). The bones as the structural framework: unconfessed guilt decays the deepest structural level. בְּשַׁאֲגָתִי כָּל-הַיּוֹם (bĕshaʾăgātî kol-hayyôm, "through my constant groaning") — shaʾăgāh: the roaring/groaning of an animal in distress (used of the lion's roar, Prov 19:12). Inarticulate bodily distress — the body expressing what the mouth refuses to confess.
32.4: כִּי יוֹמָם וָלַיְלָה תִּכְבַּד עָלַי יָדֶךָ (kî yômām wālaylāh tikkbad ʿālay yādekā, "Day and night your hand pressed down on me") — kābad: to be heavy — the hand of God as a physical weight pressing on the unconfessed. Day and night without relief. נֶהְפַּךְ לְשַׁד-בְּחַרְבֹנֵי קָיִץ (nehpak lĕshad-bĕḥarbōnê qāyiṣ, "my strength dried up like moisture in the summer heat") — shad: moisture, sap (the liquid vitality of a plant); ḥarbōnê qāyiṣ: "summer drought conditions." Guilt as a drought that desiccates the inner life.
32.5: חַטָּאתִי אוֹדִיעֲךָ (ḥaṭṭāʾtî ʾôdîʿăkā, "I made my sin known to you") — yādaʿ (hiphil: to cause to know): the Psalmist's act of confession makes the known-to-God sin also known from his own side. וַעֲוֹנִי לֹא-כִסִּיתִי (waʿăwōnî lōʾ-kissîtî, "did not hide my iniquity") — no longer self-concealing (contrast v3: the silent hiding). וְאַתָּה נָשָׂאתָ עֲוֹן חַטָּאתִי סֶלָה (wĕʾattāh nāśāʾtā ʿāwôn ḥaṭṭāʾtî selāh, "and you forgave the guilt of my sin") — nāśāʾ: the carrying-away of the iniquity (as in v1). The full arc in one verse: I made known → you carried away. The symmetry of confession and forgiveness.
32.6: עַל-זֹאת יִתְפַּלֵּל כָּל-חָסִיד אֵלֶיךָ (ʿal-zōʾt yitpallêl kol-ḥāsîd ʾêlêkā, "Therefore let every faithful person pray to you") — the experience of vv3-5 becomes warrant for communal instruction: because confession works, every ḥāsîd should pray. לְעֵת מְצֹא (lĕʿêt mĕṣōʾ, "while you may still be found") — the urgency of the temporal window (māṣāʾ: to find — cf. Isa 55:6: "Seek the LORD while he may be found"). רַק לְשֵׁטֶף מַיִם רַבִּים (raq lĕshêṭep māyim rabbîm, "when the great floodwaters surge") — the māyim rabbîm (many waters) as chaos-force: prayer now offers protection when the floods come.
32.7: אַתָּה סֵתֶר לִי (ʾattāh sêter lî, "You are my hiding place") — the sêter (shelter/concealment) used positively (as in Ps 31:20): God as the place of protected hiddenness. Not the hiding of unconfessed sin (v3) but the sheltering of the confessed and forgiven. רָנֵּי פַלֵּט תְּסוֹבְבֵנִי (rannê palêṭ tĕsôbĕbênî, "you surround me with songs of rescue") — rannê palêṭ: "shouts of rescue/deliverance" encircling the Psalmist. The surrounding terror (Ps 31:13) replaced by surrounding songs of rescue.
32.8: אַשְׂכִּילְךָ וְאוֹרְךָ בְּדֶרֶךְ-זוּ תֵלֵךְ (ʾaskîlĕkā wĕʾôrĕkā bĕderek-zû têlêk, "I will instruct you and show you the way you should walk") — the subject shifts: this is now God speaking (or a wisdom teacher). Śākal (hiphil: to give insight, to make wise — the same root as maskîl in the superscription). The maskîl psalm demonstrates its didactic purpose: the God who forgave now instructs. אִיעֲצָה עָלֶיךָ עֵינִי (îʿăṣāh ʿālêkā ʿênî, "I will guide you with my eye on you") — the guiding eye as the attentive regard that keeps the led one on course.
32.9: אַל-תִּהְיוּ כְּסוּס כְּפֶרֶד אֵין הָבִין (ʾal-tihyû kĕsûs kĕpered ʾên hābîn, "Do not be like a horse or mule that lacks understanding") — bîn: understanding (the same word as Ps 28:5: "they take no account"). The animal that cannot self-direct and must be controlled externally contrasts with the person who responds to the divine eye's guidance without requiring the bit (beṣem) and bridle (resen) of external compulsion.
32.10: רַבִּים מַכְאוֹבִים לָרָשָׁע (rabbîm makʾôbîm lārāshāʿ, "The wicked have many sorrows") — the antithetical proverb: many pains (makʾôb: pain, sorrow) for the wicked vs. וְהַבּוֹטֵחַ בַּיהוָה חֶסֶד יְסוֹבְבֶנּוּ (wĕhabbōṭêaḥ baYHWH ḥesed yĕsôbĕbennû, "but steadfast love surrounds those who trust in the LORD") — surrounding ḥesed (covenant love) encircles those who bāṭaḥ (trust/lean). The contrast is spatial: many pains press on the wicked; covenant love encircles the trusting.
32.11: שִׂמְחוּ בַיהוָה וְגִילוּ צַדִּיקִים (śimḥû baYHWH wĕgîlû ṣaddîqîm, "Be glad in the LORD and rejoice, you righteous people!") — śāmaḥ and gîl (the twin joy-verbs). Ṣaddîqîm: the righteous — here, those declared righteous through the confession-and-forgiveness process of vv1-5 (the Pauline reading: the righteous are the forgiven-and-not-reckoned-against). וְהַרְנִינוּ כָּל-יִשְׁרֵי-לֵב (wĕharnînû kol-yishrê-lêb, "Shout for joy, all who are upright in heart!") — yāshar (upright, straight-hearted): those whose inner orientation is without the deceit of v2b — the forgiven who no longer hide.

33.1: רַנְּנוּ צַדִּיקִים בַּיהוָה (rannĕnû ṣaddîqîm baYHWH, "Shout for joy in the LORD, you righteous!") — Psalm 33 has no superscription, making it anonymous — the only anonymous psalm in Book I (Ps 1-41) alongside Ps 1, 2, and 10. In the LXX it is attributed to David. The opening rinnāh (ringing shout of joy) picks up directly from Ps 32:11, continuing the exuberant sound. לַיְשָׁרִים נָאוָה תְהִלָּה (layyĕshārîm nāwāh tĕhillāh, "Praise is beautiful from those who are upright") — nāwāh: fitting, beautiful, appropriate. Praise is the congruous response of the yĕshārîm (upright ones).
33.2: הוֹדוּ לַיהוָה בְּכִנּוֹר (hôdû laYHWH bĕkinnôr, "Praise the LORD with the lyre") — kinnôr: the lyre — David's instrument (1 Sam 16:23), the principal string instrument of ancient Israel. בְּנֵבֶל עָשׂוֹר זַמְּרוּ-לוֹ (bĕnêbel ʿāśôr zammĕrû-lô, "make music with the ten-stringed harp") — nêbel: the harp (larger than the kinnôr); ʿāśôr: "ten" (ten-stringed). The physicality of worship: instruments, voices, hands — all engaged.
33.3: שִׁירוּ-לוֹ שִׁיר חָדָשׁ (shîrû-lô shîr ḥādāsh, "Sing him a new song") — ḥādāsh: new — the new song that corresponds to new saving acts (Ps 96:1; 98:1; 149:1; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9; 14:3). Not novelty for its own sake but the appropriate response to fresh divine action. הֵיטִיבוּ נַגֵּן בִּתְרוּעָה (hêṭîbû naggên bitĕrûʿāh, "play with skill and shout for joy") — yāṭab (hiphil: to do well, to be skilled): excellence in musical execution as part of worship. Tĕrûʿāh: the war-shout/trumpet blast of victory — the same word as the shout at Jericho.
33.4: כִּי-יָשָׁר דְּבַר-יְהוָה (kî-yāshār dĕbar-YHWH, "For the word of the LORD is right and true") — yāshār: straight, right, upright — the divine word as the standard of uprightness. וְכָל-מַעֲשֵׂהוּ בֶּאֱמוּנָה (wĕkol-maʿăśêhû beʾĕmûnāh, "all his works are done in faithfulness") — ʾĕmûnāh: faithfulness, reliability, consistency. The word is straight; the works are faithful. This verse prepares for vv6-9 by establishing that the creative word operates in faithfulness.
33.5: אֹהֵב צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט (ʾōhêb ṣĕdāqāh ûmishpāṭ, "He loves righteousness and justice") — the pair ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness/right-standing) and mishpāṭ (justice/judgment) is the foundational moral vocabulary of the Hebrew prophets (Amos 5:24; Mic 6:8; Isa 5:7). God's love (ʾāhab) for these qualities expresses his character. חֶסֶד יְהוָה מָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ (ḥesed YHWH māleʾāh hāʾāreṣ, "the earth is full of the LORD's steadfast love") — the ḥesed of YHWH fills the earth — not private and limited to Israel but overflowing into the whole created order.
33.6: בִּדְבַר יְהוָה שָׁמַיִם נַעֲשׂוּ (bidĕbar YHWH shāmayim naʿăśû, "The heavens were made by the word of the LORD") — dĕbar YHWH: the word of the LORD — the creative speech of Gen 1 ("and God said…"). The heavens are the product of divine speech. וּבְרוּחַ פִּיו כָּל-צְבָאָם (ûbĕrûaḥ pîw kol-ṣĕbāʾām, "all their host by the breath of his mouth") — rûaḥ pîw: "breath/spirit of his mouth" — the divine breath that is both the wind hovering over the waters (Gen 1:2) and the speech-breath issuing creative words. John 1:1-3 develops this dĕbar YHWH theology christologically: the Word (Logos) through whom all things were made is the pre-existent Son.
33.7: כֹּנֵס כַּנֵּד מֵי הַיָּם (kōnês kannêd mê hayyām, "He gathers the sea's waters like a heap") — kānās: to gather, to pile up; nêd: a heap, a mound. This echoes the Exodus sea-crossing: "The waters piled up; the surging waters stood firm like a wall" (Exod 15:8, the same word nêd). God's creative ordering of the waters is the same power as the Exodus rescue. נֹתֵן בְּאֹצָרוֹת תְּהוֹמוֹת (nōtên bĕʾōṣārôt tĕhômôt, "he stores the deep in reservoirs") — tĕhôm: the deep (Gen 1:2); ʾōṣār: treasury, storehouse. God stores the chaotic deep as if it were treasure in a vault — sovereignty over the primordial chaos.
33.8: יִירְאוּ מֵיְהוָה כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (yîrĕʾû mêYHWH kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Let all the earth fear the LORD") — the universal summons to yirʾāh (reverence/fear): not Israel alone but all the earth. The ground for universal reverence is given in vv6-7: the creator of the heavens and sovereign over the deep deserves universal acknowledgment. מִמֶּנּוּ יָגוּרוּ כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי תֵבֵל (mimmennu yāgûrû kol-yōshĕbê têbêl, "let all who live in the world stand in awe of him") — têbêl: the inhabited world — the specifically human-populated earth.
33.9: כִּי הוּא אָמַר וַיֶּהִי (kî hûʾ ʾāmar wayyehî, "For he spoke, and it came to be") — the fiat-creation formula: ʾāmar (he spoke) and yehî (and it was) — the Gen 1 pattern: "And God said… and there was." The word is causally sufficient for existence: no further mechanism needed. הוּא-צִוָּה וַיַּעֲמֹד (hûʾ-ṣiwwāh wayyaʿămōd, "he commanded, and it stood firm") — ṣiwwāh: to command (the same word for divine commandments); ʿāmad: to stand, to endure. What the divine command calls into being "stands" — the command is the foundation of created permanence.
33.10: יְהוָה הֵפִיר עֲצַת-גּוֹיִם (YHWH hêpîr ʿăṣat-gôyim, "The LORD thwarts the purposes of the nations") — pārar (hiphil: to break, to frustrate, to make void): the divine frustration of human plans. ʿĒṣāh: counsel, plan, strategy. הֵנִיא מַחְשְׁבוֹת עַמִּים (hênîʾ maḥshĕbôt ʿammîm, "he brings the plans of the peoples to nothing") — maḥshāb: thought, plan (from ḥāshab: to reckon — the same accounting root as Ps 32:2). The nations' reckonings are un-reckoned by the LORD; their plans dissolve before the divine purpose (v11).
33.11: עֲצַת יְהוָה לְעוֹלָם תַּעֲמֹד (ʿăṣat YHWH lĕʿôlām taʿămōd, "The counsel of the LORD stands forever") — the same word ʿēṣāh (counsel/purpose) used for the nations' plans in v10, now applied to the divine plan. The contrast is permanence: the nations' ʿēṣāh is frustrated; the LORD's ʿēṣāh stands (ʿāmad) forever — the same verb as v9 ("it stood firm"). מַחְשְׁבוֹת לִבּוֹ לְדֹר וָדֹר (maḥshĕbôt libbô lĕdōr wādōr, "the purposes of his heart through all generations") — the deep interior intention of God persists generation after generation.
33.12: אַשְׁרֵי הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר-יְהוָה אֱלֹהָיו (ʾashrê haggôy ʾăsher-YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw, "Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD") — the ʾashrê beatitude applied to a nation (gôy): collective blessedness. הָעָם בָּחַר לְנַחֲלָה לוֹ (hāʿām bāḥar lĕnaḥălāh lô, "the people he has chosen as his own possession") — bāḥar: to choose (the election vocabulary of Deut 7:6); naḥălāh: inheritance, possession. The chosen people as the LORD's own inheritance. 1 Pet 2:9 applies this election-language to the church: "a chosen people… God's special possession."
33.13: מִשָּׁמַיִם הִבִּיט יְהוָה (mishshāmayim hibbîṭ YHWH, "The LORD looks down from heaven") — nābaṭ (hiphil: to look, to gaze intently): not a casual glance but deliberate, attentive observation from the divine vantage above the heavens. רָאָה אֶת-כָּל-בְּנֵי הָאָדָם (rāʾāh ʾet-kol-bĕnê hāʾādām, "he observes all humanity") — bĕnê hāʾādām: "sons of Adam" — humanity as the descendants of the first human. Universal scope: not Israel specifically but all of Adam's offspring.
33.14: מִמְּכוֹן-שִׁבְתּוֹ הִשְׁגִּיחַ (mimmĕkôn-shībtô hishgîaḥ, "from his dwelling place he surveys") — shāgaḥ (hiphil: to look attentively, to survey): the patient, comprehensive gaze. Mĕkôn-shîbtô: "the established/fixed place of his sitting" — the heavenly throne as the permanent vantage point. אֶל כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ (ʾel kol-yōshĕbê hāʾāreṣ, "all who live on the earth") — every earthly inhabitant within the divine field of vision.
33.15: הַיֹּצֵר יַחַד לִבָּם (hayyōṣêr yaḥad libbām, "he who forms the hearts of all") — yāṣar: to form, to shape (as a potter shapes clay — Gen 2:7: "the LORD God formed the human from the dust"). Yaḥad libbām: "all their hearts together" — the divine potter who formed each individual heart also knows what he has made. הַמֵּבִין אֶל-כָּל-מַעֲשֵׂיהֶם (hammêbîn ʾel-kol-maʿăśêhem, "and considers everything they do") — bîn: to understand, to discern (Ps 28:5; 32:9). The one who made the hearts understands every deed those hearts produce.
33.16: אֵין-הַמֶּלֶךְ נוֹשָׁע בְּרָב-חָיִל (ʾên-hammelek nôshāʿ bĕrab-ḥāyil, "No king is saved by the size of his army") — political theology: military might (ḥāyil: valor, army) does not determine victory. Nôshāʿ (niphal passive: saved/rescued): the king himself is not the saver but the one who needs saving — and the size of his forces does not guarantee his rescue.
33.17: שֶׁקֶר הַסּוּס לִתְשׁוּעָה (sheqer hassûs litshûʿāh, "A horse is a vain hope for victory") — sheqer: falsehood, deception — the horse as a liar in the sense of promising what it cannot deliver. The war-horse (sûs) was the cutting-edge military technology of the ancient world (chariot warfare); Ps 20:7 contrasted horse-trust with name-of-the-LORD trust. וּבְרֹב חֵילוֹ לֹא יְמַלֵּט (ûbĕrōb ḥêlô lōʾ yĕmallêṭ, "its great power cannot bring rescue") — mālat (piel: to cause to escape, to rescue): the horse's great strength cannot produce escape.
33.18: הִנֵּה עֵין יְהוָה אֶל-יְרֵאָיו (hinnêh ʿên YHWH ʾel-yĕrêʾāyw, "The eye of the LORD is on those who fear him") — the divine eye that surveys all humanity (v13-14) is specifically protective toward those who fear him. Yĕrêʾāyw: those who fear him — the relationship vocabulary. לַמְיַחֲלִים לְחַסְדּוֹ (lamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô, "on those who hope in his steadfast love") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation (the same word as the closing line of Ps 31:24); ḥesed: covenant love. The eye is on those whose hope is directed toward the LORD's own ḥesed — not toward their own resources (vv16-17).
33.19: לְהַצִּיל מִמָּוֶת נַפְשָׁם (lĕhaṣṣîl mimmāwet napshām, "to rescue them from death") — the purpose of the divine eye: rescue from death. Nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away, rescue). Nāpesh: the living self — the whole person rescued from death. וּלְחַיּוֹתָם בָּרָעָב (ûlĕḥayyôtām bārāʿāb, "to keep them alive in famine") — concrete provision: survival in famine (rāʿāb). God's providential care is not abstract — it reaches into the physical need for food.
33.20: נַפְשֵׁנוּ חִכְּתָה לַיהוָה (napshênû ḥikkĕtāh laYHWH, "We wait for the LORD") — the communal voice: "our soul has waited" (ḥikkāh: to wait, to be patient — intensive waiting). The individual hope of v18 now becomes the communal confession. עֶזְרֵנוּ וּמָגִנֵּנוּ הוּא (ʿezrênû ûmāginnênû hûʾ, "he is our help and our shield") — ʿezer: helper (Ps 30:10); māgên: shield (Ps 28:7). The communal declaration: "he is" — the predicate of identity, not petition. Already the help; already the shield.
33.21: כִּי-בוֹ יִשְׂמַח לִבֵּנוּ (kî-bô yiśmaḥ libbênû, "Our hearts are glad in him") — śāmaḥ: rejoice, be glad; the gladness is "in him" (bô) — not in circumstances but in the person of the LORD who is the help and shield. כִּי בְשֵׁם קָדְשׁוֹ בָטָחְנוּ (kî bĕshêm qodshô bāṭāḥnû, "for we trust in his holy name") — bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on (the persistent trust-verb of this cluster of psalms). The holy name as the ground of trust.
33.22: יְהִי-חַסְדְּךָ יְהוָה עָלֵינוּ (yĕhî-ḥasdĕkā YHWH ʿālênû, "Let your steadfast love, LORD, rest on us") — the jussive closing prayer: may the ḥesed that belongs to the LORD come to rest on the community. The psalm ends with the same word that saturated it — ḥesed (vv5, 18, 22). The whole psalm is a meditation on divine ḥesed as the ground of creation (v5), the object of hope (v18), and the content of the closing prayer. כַּאֲשֶׁר יִחַלְנוּ לָךְ (kaʾăsher yiḥalnû lāk, "as we have placed our hope in you") — yāḥal: the same hope-waiting of v18 and Ps 31:24. The closing prayer links the ḥesed to the hoping: may covenant love rest on us in proportion to the hope we have placed in you.

37.1: ʾal-titḥar bammĕrēʿîm ʾal-tĕqannēʾ bĕʿōśê ʿawlâ (ʾal-tiṯḥar bammĕrēʿîm ʾal-tĕqannēʾ bĕʿōśê ʿawlâ): 'Fret not yourself because of evildoers; be not envious of wrongdoers.' Psalm 37 is an alphabetic acrostic (each pair of verses beginning with successive Hebrew letters) attributed to David, addressing the classic theodicy problem of the prosperous wicked. The opening double prohibition — ʾal-titḥar ('do not fret') and ʾal-tĕqannēʾ ('do not be envious') — identifies the spiritual danger: not the wicked's prosperity itself but the psalmist's inner reaction to it. The verb ḥārâ ('to burn, be agitated') in the hitpael describes smoldering indignation; qānāʾ ('to be jealous, envious') describes the coveting of another's success. Both responses misread the situation.
37.2: kî kĕḥāṣîr mĕhērâ yibbālû ûkĕyereq dešeʾ yibbōlûn (kî kĕḥāṣîr mĕhērâ yibbālû ûkĕyereq dešeʾ yibbōlûn): 'For they will soon fade like the grass and wither like the green herb.' The conjunction kî ('for') provides the theological reason for not fretting: the wicked's prosperity is temporary. The two similes — kĕḥāṣîr ('like grass') and kĕyereq dešeʾ ('like the green herb/plant') — are temporal metaphors of rapid decay. The hithpalel yibbālû ('they will wither/fade') describes the irreversible process; yibbōlûn ('they will wither') is repeated for emphasis. The comparison to grass is the psalm's foundational theodicy: what appears permanent is categorically transient (cf. Ps 90:5-6; Isa 40:6-8; 1 Pet 1:24).
37.3: beṭaḥ baYHWH waʿăśēh ṭôb šĕkon ʾereṣ ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ (ḇeṭaḥ ḇaYHWH waʿăśēh ṭôḇ šĕḵon ʾereṣ ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ): 'Trust in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and befriend faithfulness.' The psalm's positive prescriptions begin: bāṭaḥ ('trust') and ʿāśâ ṭôb ('do good') are the twin responses to the wicked's prosperity. The promise: šĕkon ʾereṣ ('dwell in the land') — the land-inheritance theme from the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 12:7; 15:7) is restated as the reward of covenant faithfulness. The phrase ûrĕʿēh ʾĕmûnâ, 'shepherd faithfulness / feed on faithfulness,' uses the verb rāʿâ ('shepherd, graze') — the psalmist is to live off divine ʾĕmûnâ as sheep graze on good pasture.
37.4: wĕhitʿannag ʿal-YHWH wĕyittĕn-lĕkā mišʾălôt libbĕkā (wĕhiṯʿannag ʿal-YHWH wĕyittĕn-lĕḵā mišʾălôṯ libbĕḵā): 'Delight yourself in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart.' The hitpael wĕhitʿannag ('delight yourself') from ʿānag ('to be soft, delicate, to luxuriate') describes finding YHWH himself as one's deepest pleasure — the enjoyment of God as the supreme satisfaction. The result: wĕyittĕn-lĕkā mišʾălôt libbĕkā, 'he will give you the petitions of your heart.' The phrase is not a blank check for wish-fulfillment but a promise that when YHWH is one's delight, the heart's desires are shaped by that delight — and YHWH fulfills desires so shaped. The heart that delights in YHWH will not desire what YHWH cannot give.
37.5: gōl ʿal-YHWH darkekā ûbeṭaḥ ʿālāyw wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh (ḡōl ʿal-YHWH ḏarḵeḵā ûḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh): 'Commit your way to the LORD; trust in him, and he will act.' The imperative gōl ('roll!') from gālal ('to roll') — used idiomatically for rolling a burden onto another (Prov 16:3) — describes committing the entire way (derek) to YHWH: the path of one's life handed over completely. The ground: ûbeṭaḥ ʿālāyw, 'and trust in him' — trust is the posture that enables the rolling. The result: wĕhûʾ yaʿăśeh, 'and he will act' — the emphatic subject hûʾ ('he himself') emphasizes that YHWH, not the psalmist, will bring it to pass. Human commitment enables but does not produce the outcome.
37.6: wĕhôṣîʾ kāʾôr ṣidqekā ûmišpāṭekā katssohorayim (wĕhôṣîʾ ḵāʾôr ṣiḏqeḵā ûmišpāṭeḵā katssohorayim): 'He will bring forth your righteousness as the light, and your justice as the noonday.' The hiphil wĕhôṣîʾ ('he will bring forth') uses the light-emergence metaphor: righteousness that is currently hidden or obscured will be revealed. The comparison kāʾôr ('like light') and katssohorayim ('like the noonday') moves from dawn to high noon — the most brilliant, unambiguous illumination. This verse addresses the experience of the righteous person whose innocence is not recognized by the world: YHWH himself will eventually make it as visible as noon light.
37.7: dōm laYHWH wĕhithôlel lô ʾal-titḥar bĕmatzlîaḥ darkô (ḏôm laYHWH wĕhiṯhôlel lô ʾal-tiṯḥar bĕmaṣlîaḥ darkô): 'Be still before the LORD and wait patiently for him; fret not yourself over the one who prospers in his way.' The verb dōm ('be silent, still') is a rare imperative that requires active quieting — the stillness takes effort when circumstances provoke agitation. The hitpael wĕhithôlel lô ('wait in hope for him') from ḥîl ('to writhe, wait') images the taut waiting of one in labor — expectant but not yet relieved. The return of ʾal-titḥar ('fret not') from v.1 (now specifically about the one maṣlîaḥ darkô, 'who prospers in his way') shows the repeated temptation: success without apparent punishment provokes the very fretting the psalm forbids.
37.8: rĕpēh mēʾap wĕʿăzōb ḥēmâ ʾal-titḥar ʾak-lĕhārēaʿ (rep̄ēh mēʾap wĕʿăzōḇ ḥēmâ ʾal-tiṯḥar ʾaḵ-lĕhārēaʿ): 'Refrain from anger and forsake wrath! Fret not yourself; it tends only to evil.' The triple prohibition — rĕpēh ('cease!'), ʿăzōb ('abandon!'), ʾal-titḥar ('do not fret!') — demands a progressive abandonment of reactive emotions. The word ʾap ('anger, nose') and ḥēmâ ('heat, wrath') together capture both the appearance (flared nostrils) and the inner temperature of anger. The final clause ʾak-lĕhārēaʿ, 'it tends only to evil' — the fretting and anger, left to run their course, lead to evildoing. The unfretting psalmist avoids becoming what he condemns in the wicked.
37.9: kî mĕrēʿîm yikkārētûn wĕqōwê YHWH hēmmâ yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ (kî mĕrēʿîm yikkārētûn wĕqōwê YHWH hēmmâ yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ): 'For the evildoers shall be cut off, but those who wait for the LORD shall inherit the land.' The kî ('for') gives the ground for the prohibitions of vv.7-8: the outcome is secure. The verb kārat (niphal, 'be cut off') — used of covenant curses (Gen 17:14; Lev 20:17) — applies to the evildoers. But qōwê YHWH ('those who wait for the LORD') — using the taut-expectation root qāwâ — will yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ('inherit the land'). This verse is the first of five land-inheritance promises in Psalm 37 (vv.9,11,22,29,34), which Jesus universalizes in Matt 5:5: 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.'
37.10: wĕʿôd mĕʿaṭ wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕhitbônanttā ʿal-mĕqōmô wĕʾênennu (wĕʿôḏ mĕʿaṭ wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕhiṯḇônantā ʿal-mĕqōmô wĕʾênennu): 'In just a little while, the wicked will be no more; though you look carefully at his place, he will not be there.' The temporal phrase ʿôd mĕʿaṭ ('yet a little while') frames the wicked's dominance as imminently temporary. The verb wĕhitbônantā (hitpael, 'you will look carefully, consider attentively') describes the careful inspection of the wicked's place (maqôm) — and finding nothing. The disappearance is not merely death but trace-erasure: no evidence of the wicked's tenure remains (cf. the name-blotting of Ps 34:16). Hebrews 10:37 quotes this phrase: 'in just a little while, he who is coming will come and will not delay.'
37.11: waʿănāwîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕhitʿannĕgû ʿal-rōb šālôm (waʿănāwîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕhiṯʿannĕgû ʿal-rōḇ šālôm): 'But the meek shall inherit the land and delight themselves in abundant peace.' The noun ʿănāwîm ('meek, afflicted, humble') — those bent down by circumstance or by posture of humility before God — are contrasted with the rāšāʿ ('wicked'). The land inheritance yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ is their promise. The additional blessing: wĕhitʿannĕgû ʿal-rōb šālôm, 'they will delight themselves in abundant peace' — using the same hitpael of ʿānag from v.4 ('delight in the LORD'), now applied to šālôm in superabundance. Jesus quotes this verse verbatim in Matt 5:5, making the Beatitude a direct citation of the Psalter's wisdom tradition. The land promise is re-territorialized to encompass the whole earth (gē, LXX).
37.12: zōmēm rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûḥōrēq ʿālāyw šinnāyw (zōmēm rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûḥōrēq ʿālāyw šinnāyw): 'The wicked plots against the righteous and gnashes his teeth at him.' The participle zōmēm ('plotting, scheming') from zāmam ('to plan, devise') describes deliberate malice — the wicked's agenda is specifically targeted at (lĕ-) the righteous. The phrase ḥōrēq šinnāyw ('gnashing his teeth') is the bodily expression of rage and contempt, the visible sign of the wicked's inner disposition. The gnashing of teeth as a sign of judgment's approach appears in Matt 8:12; 13:42; 22:13 — the psalmist's image of the wicked's teeth-gnashing becomes, in Jesus's teaching, the eschatological condition of those who refused the kingdom.
37.13: ʾădōnāy yiśḥaq-lô kî rāʾâ kî yābōʾ yômô (ʾăḏōnāy yiśḥaq-lô kî rāʾâ kî yāḇōʾ yômô): 'But the Lord laughs at the wicked, for he sees that his day is coming.' The divine laughter — YHWH yiśḥaq ('laughs, mocks') — uses the same verb as Ps 2:4 where YHWH laughs at the nations conspiring against his anointed. The laughter is not cruelty but the expression of incomprehensible superiority: the wicked's plotting (v.12) is ludicrous from the perspective of one who sees yômô, 'his day.' The 'day' of judgment coming for the wicked makes all his scheming appear as the futile activity of someone who has already lost.
37.14: ḥereb pāṭĕḥû rĕšāʿîm wĕdārĕkû qaštām lĕhappîl ʿānî wĕʾebyôn (ḥereḇ pāṭĕḥû rĕšāʿîm wĕḏārĕḵû qaštām lĕhappîl ʿānî wĕʾeḇyôn): 'The wicked draw the sword and bend their bows to bring down the poor and needy.' The wicked's weapons — ḥereb ('sword') and qešet ('bow') — are deployed against ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ('the poor and needy'): those least able to defend themselves. The phrase lĭṭbōaḥ yišrê-derek, 'to slaughter those who walk uprightly,' adds a third target — the morally upright. The combination: the structural underdog (poor/needy) and the moral exemplar (upright) are the wicked's primary victims. Power exploits vulnerability and virtue simultaneously.
37.15: ḥarbām tābôʾ bĕlibbām wĕqaštôtām tišbārenâ (ḥarḇām ṯāḇôʾ bĕlibbām wĕqaštôṯām tišbārenâ): 'Their sword shall enter their own heart, and their bows shall be broken.' The lex talionis principle operates: ḥarbām ... bĕlibbām, 'their own sword into their own heart' — the weapon aimed at others pierces the wielder. The passive tišbārenâ ('will be broken') — the bows snapping — echoes Ps 37:17's 'arms of the wicked will be broken.' The reversal: the weapons of aggression become instruments of self-destruction. The wicked's armament, far from securing their dominance, becomes the mechanism of their defeat. This is the Psalter's consistent theology of divine poetic justice.
37.16: ṭôb mĕʿaṭ latsaddîq mēhāmôn rĕšāʿîm rabbîm (ṭôḇ mĕʿaṭ latsaddîq mēhāmôn rĕšāʿîm rabbîm): 'Better is the little that the righteous has than the abundance of many wicked.' The comparative ṭôb ... mē- ('better than') structure is the classic wisdom saying of comparative value (cf. Prov 15:16-17; 16:8). The mĕʿaṭ ('little, few') of the righteous is better than the hāmôn ('multitude, wealth, noise') of many wicked — not because poverty is virtue but because the righteous man's little is held in covenant with YHWH and therefore is genuinely satisfying, while the wicked's abundance is precarious and morally contaminated.
37.17: kî zĕrôʿôt rĕšāʿîm tišbārenâ wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH (kî zĕrôʿôṯ rĕšāʿîm tišbārenâ wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH): 'for the arms of the wicked shall be broken, but the LORD upholds the righteous.' The noun zĕrôʿôt ('arms') denotes power and strength — the arms of the wicked that do violence (cf. Ps 10:15) will tišbārenâ ('be broken'). The contrast: wĕsômēk ṣaddîqîm YHWH, 'but the LORD sustains/upholds the righteous.' The participle sômēk (from sāmak, 'to lean upon, support') pictures YHWH as the structural support on which the righteous lean their entire weight. The wicked support themselves by their own strength (which breaks); the righteous are upheld by YHWH (who does not break).
37.18: yôdēaʿ YHWH yĕmê tĕmîmîm wĕnaḥălātām lĕʿôlām tihyeh (yôḏēaʿ YHWH yĕmê ṯĕmîmîm wĕnaḥălāṯām lĕʿôlām tihyeh): 'The LORD knows the days of the blameless, and their heritage will abide forever.' The verb yāḏaʿ ('to know') applied by YHWH to yĕmê tĕmîmîm ('the days of the blameless') is covenantal knowledge: intimate attention and care, not merely information. The word tāmîm ('blameless, complete') — used of Noah (Gen 6:9), Abraham (Gen 17:1), and sacrificial animals without defect — describes moral integrity. The result: naḥălātām lĕʿôlām, 'their inheritance forever' — permanence opposed to the transience of the wicked (v.2).
37.19: lōʾ-yēbōšû bĕʿēt rāʿâ ûbîmê rĕʿābôn yiśbāʿû (lōʾ-yēḇōšû bĕʿēṯ rāʿâ ûḇîmê rĕʿāḇôn yiśbāʿû): 'They are not put to shame in evil times; in the days of famine they have abundance.' The promise lōʾ-yēbōšû ('they will not be ashamed/disappointed') ensures the covenant community is not humiliated even bĕʿēt rāʿâ ('in the time of evil/trouble'). The specific test: bîmê rĕʿābôn ('in days of famine') — the ultimate scarcity test — and yet yiśbāʿû ('they will be satisfied/full'). The pair shame/satisfaction frames the issue: shame is the social experience of failure; fullness is its physical opposite. The blameless will experience both social dignity and physical provision even when circumstances are worst.
37.20: kî rĕšāʿîm yōbēdû wĕʾōyĕbê YHWH kîqar kārîm kālû bĕʿāšān kālû (kî rĕšāʿîm yōḇēḏû wĕʾōyĕḇê YHWH kîqar kārîm kālû ḇĕʿāšān kālû): 'But the wicked shall perish; the enemies of the LORD are like the glory of the pastures — they vanish, like smoke they vanish away.' The antithesis: rĕšāʿîm yōbēdû ('the wicked shall perish/be destroyed') against the heritage of the blameless (v.18). The double simile: kîqar kārîm ('like the prize of the meadows / the splendor of the pastures') — momentarily beautiful — then kālû bĕʿāšān ('they vanish in smoke'). The reduplication kālû ... kālû ('they vanish ... they vanish') is emphatic: total, complete disappearance. The wicked's visible glory is the most transient thing imaginable.
37.21: lōweh rāšāʿ wĕlōʾ yĕšallēm wĕṣaddîq ḥônēn ûnôtēn (lōweh rāšāʿ wĕlōʾ yĕšallēm wĕṣaddîq ḥônēn ûnôtēn): 'The wicked borrows but does not pay back, but the righteous is generous and gives.' The economic behaviour of the two types is their moral signature: the wicked lāwâ ('borrows') but lōʾ yĕšallēm ('does not repay') — the loan becomes a theft. The righteous ḥônēn ('shows grace, is generous') and nôtēn ('gives') — without requiring repayment. The wicked's non-repayment is not merely financial irresponsibility but a moral posture of entitlement; the righteous giving is the embodiment of ḥesed in economic life. Proverbs 19:17 and Luke 6:35 elaborate the theology of generous giving without expectation of return.
37.22: kî mĕbōrākāyw yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ûmĕqullālāyw yikkārētû (kî mĕḇōrāḵāyw yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ ûmĕqullālāyw yikkārētû): 'for those blessed by him shall inherit the land, but those cursed by him shall be cut off.' The covenant categories — blessed (bārak) and cursed (qālal) — from the Mosaic covenant (Deut 27-28) are applied: the blessed inherit the land (the Abrahamic promise renewed at Sinai) and the cursed are cut off (the covenant curse). The land-inheritance for the blessed (mĕbōrākāyw) is the fourth occurrence of this promise in the psalm (vv.9,11,22,29,34), establishing it as the psalm's central hope.
37.23: mēYHWH miṣʿădê-geber kônnānû wĕdarkô yeḥpāṣ (mēYHWH miṣʿăḏê-ḡeḇer kônnānû wĕḏarḵô yeḥp̄āṣ): 'The steps of a man are established by the LORD, when he delights in his way.' The phrase miṣʿădê-geber ('steps of a strong man') refers to each footfall in the journey of life — YHWH kônnānû ('establishes, makes firm') each step. This is divine providence applied to individual biography: not just the broad strokes of history but each step of the specific person is ordered by YHWH. The qualifier wĕdarkô yeḥpāṣ ('and he delights in his way') — YHWH's delight in the person's way is the condition: the steps of the one who delights in YHWH (v.4) are established by YHWH. The two delights are correlated.
37.24: kî yippōl lōʾ-yûṭal kî YHWH sômēk yādô (kî yippōl lōʾ-yûṭal kî YHWH sômēk yāḏô): 'though he fall, he shall not be cast headlong, for the LORD upholds his hand.' The concessive kî yippōl ('though he fall') acknowledges real failure, stumbling, and setback: the psalm is not naively optimistic. The qualification lōʾ-yûṭal ('he will not be hurled down') — from yāṭal ('to cast, throw') — distinguishes a fall that is recovered from a fatal fall. The reason: kî YHWH sômēk yādô, 'for the LORD holds his hand' — the image of a father holding a child's hand while the child stumbles, preventing a catastrophic fall. The stumbling is real; the destruction is prevented.
37.25: naʿar hāyîtî gam-zāqantî wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāb wĕzarʿô mĕbaqqeš-lāḥem (naʿar hāyîtî ḡam-zāqantî wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāḇ wĕzarʿô mĕḇaqqeš-lāḥem): 'I have been young, and now am old, yet I have not seen the righteous forsaken or his children begging for bread.' The personal testimony spans a lifetime: naʿar ... gam-zāqantî ('young ... and now old'). The claim wĕlōʾ-rāʾîtî ṣaddîq neʾĕzāb ('I have not seen the righteous forsaken') is empirical wisdom from lived experience — but it is also a theological claim that requires nuance: the psalmist is not denying that righteous people suffer (v.19's 'evil times') but that YHWH finally abandons them. The observation is a pattern across a long life, not a promise that every moment will be painless.
37.26: kol-hayyôm ḥônēn ûmalweh wĕzarʿô lĕbirākâ (ḵol-hayyôm ḥônēn ûmalweh wĕzarʿô lĕḇirāḵâ): 'He is ever lending generously, and his children become a blessing.' The participle ḥônēn ('showing favor, being generous') combined with malweh ('lending') describes habitual generosity: the righteous person's default economic mode is giving and lending without demanding return. The result wĕzarʿô lĕbirākâ, 'and his seed/children become a blessing,' shows the transgenerational effect: the righteous man's character shapes his children into the same pattern, and they in turn are lĕbirākâ ('for a blessing') to others (cf. Gen 12:2: Abraham 'will be a blessing').
37.27: sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôb ûšĕkon lĕʿôlām (sûr mērāʿ waʿăśēh-ṭôḇ ûšĕḵon lĕʿôlām): 'Turn away from evil and do good; so shall you dwell forever.' The ethical imperative returns to v.3's pattern: sûr mērāʿ ('depart from evil') + ʿāśēh ṭôb ('do good') — the pair appears also in Ps 34:14 and Isa 1:16-17 as the prophetic summary of covenant ethics. The promise: ûšĕkon lĕʿôlām, 'you will dwell forever' — the land-dwelling of v.3 extended to perpetuity. The combination of ethical instruction and land-promise reflects the covenant logic of Deuteronomy: obedience produces land-tenure; disobedience leads to exile.
37.28: kî YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōb ʾet-ḥăsîdāyw lĕʿôlām nišmārû (kî YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōḇ ʾeṯ-ḥăsîḏāyw lĕʿôlām nišmārû): 'For the LORD loves justice; he will not forsake his faithful ones. They are preserved forever.' The ground of covenant faithfulness: YHWH ʾōhēb mišpāṭ ('loves justice/right'). The verb ʾāhab ('love') applied to mišpāṭ means YHWH is constitutively committed to just outcomes — it is not a policy but an affection. The covenantal guarantee: wĕlōʾ-yaʿăzōb ʾet-ḥăsîdāyw ('he will not forsake his faithful ones'). The ḥăsîdîm ('covenant-loyal ones') are preserved (nišmārû) forever. The wicked children in contrast: wĕzeraʿ rĕšāʿîm nikrāt, 'the offspring of the wicked shall be cut off' — the generational reversal of v.26.
37.29: ṣaddîqîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕyiškenû lāʿad ʿālêhā (ṣaddîqîm yîrĕšû ʾāreṣ wĕyiškenû lāʿaḏ ʿālêhā): 'The righteous shall inherit the land and dwell upon it forever.' The fifth land-inheritance promise (vv.9,11,22,29,34) is stated in its most expansive form: lāʿad ʿālêhā, 'forever upon it.' The permanence is absolute and temporal — not a temporary tenure but everlasting dwelling. The righteous (ṣaddîqîm) — defined throughout the psalm by trust, covenant-keeping, and generosity — are the true heirs of the land against which the wicked falsely claim ownership.
37.30: pî-ṣaddîq yehgeh ḥokmâ ûlĕšônô tĕdabbēr mišpāṭ (p̄î-ṣaddîq yehgeh ḥoḵmâ ûlĕšônô ṯĕḏabbēr mišpāṭ): 'The mouth of the righteous utters wisdom, and his tongue speaks justice.' The verb hāgâ ('to mutter, meditate, utter') — used in Ps 1:2 for meditating on Torah — describes the continuous output of the righteous man's mouth: wisdom (ḥokmâ) flows because Torah saturates his inner life. The parallel ûlĕšônô tĕdabbēr mišpāṭ, 'his tongue speaks justice,' connects verbal output with the courtroom: the righteous man's speech produces just outcomes in communal life. Speech and justice are inseparable in covenant ethics.
37.31: tôrat ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô lōʾ timmāʿēd ʾăšūrāyw (ṯôraṯ ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô lōʾ ṯimmāʿēḏ ʾăšūrāyw): 'The law of his God is in his heart; his steps do not slip.' The phrase tôrat ʾĕlōhāyw bĕlibbô ('the Torah of his God in his heart') is the Psalter's internalization motif — Torah not merely studied externally but lodged in the lēb (will-center). This is the Jeremianic 'new covenant' anticipation (Jer 31:33: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts') and the Deuteronomic ideal (Deut 6:6: 'these words shall be on your heart'). The consequence: lōʾ timmāʿēd ʾăšūrāyw, 'his steps do not slip/totter' — internal Torah produces external stability (cf. Ps 119:11).
37.32: ṣōpeh rāšāʿ latsaddîq ûmĕbaqqēš lahămîtô (ṣōp̄eh rāšāʿ laṣṣaddîq ûmĕḇaqqēš lahămîtô): 'The wicked watches for the righteous and seeks to put him to death.'
