The participle ṣōpeh ('watches, lies in wait') describes predatory surveillance — the wicked observing the righteous not with neutral attention but with lahămîtô ('to kill him') as the goal. The surveillance language connects to Ps 10:8-9 (the wicked lurking) and Ps 35:4 (seeking the psalmist's life). The righteous person lives under constant hostile observation by the wicked: their virtue provokes hatred rather than admiration.
37.33: YHWH lōʾ-yaʿazbennû bĕyādô wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāpĕṭô (YHWH lōʾ-yaʿazbennû bĕyāḏô wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāp̄eṭô): 'The LORD will not abandon him to his power or let him be condemned when he is brought to trial.' The divine guarantee lōʾ-yaʿazbennû ('he will not abandon him') directly addresses the wicked's lethal intention (v.32): the wicked watches to kill, but YHWH will not leave the righteous in the wicked's hand (bĕyādô). The legal protection: wĕlōʾ yarširʿennû bĕhiššāpĕṭô, 'he will not condemn him when he is judged' — the court that the wicked manipulate against the righteous (cf. Ps 35:11-12) will not produce a verdict of guilty, because YHWH is the ultimate judge.
37.34: qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ûšĕmōr darkô wîrômĕmĕkā lārešet ʾāreṣ bĕhikkārēt rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh (qawwēh ʾel-YHWH ûšĕmōr ḏarḵô wîrômĕmeḵā lārešeṯ ʾāreṣ bĕhikkārēṯ rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh): 'Wait for the LORD and keep his way, and he will exalt you to inherit the land; you will look on when the wicked are cut off.' The final land-inheritance promise: waiting on YHWH (qawwēh) combined with keeping his way (šĕmōr darkô) produces exaltation and land-tenure. The climactic vision: bĕhikkārēt rĕšāʿîm tirʾeh, 'when the wicked are cut off, you will see it' — the righteous are witnesses to the wicked's end, not victims of it. The psalm's theodicy is resolved in this witnessed reversal.
37.35: rāʾîtî rāšāʿ ʿārîṣ ûmiṯʿāreh kĕʾezrāḥ raʿănān (rāʾîtî rāšāʿ ʿārîṣ ûmiṯʿāreh kĕʾezrāḥ raʿănān): 'I have seen a wicked, ruthless man, spreading himself like a green laurel tree.' The personal testimony rāʾîtî ('I have seen') introduces an anecdote. The wicked man is ʿārîṣ ('ruthless, terrifying') — one who intimidates through power — and miṯʿāreh ('spreading himself') like a native tree (ʾezrāḥ, 'one born on the land, indigenous') that is raʿănān ('luxuriantly green'). The spreading tree is the picture of robust vitality and established dominance: the wicked man appears as well-rooted and flourishing as any tree in its native soil.
37.36: wayyaʿăbōr wĕhinnēh ʾênennu waʾăbaqqĕšēhû wĕlōʾ nimṣāʾ (wayyaʿăḇōr wĕhinnēh ʾênennu waʾăḇaqqĕšēhû wĕlōʾ nimṣāʾ): 'Then I passed by, and behold, he was no more; though I sought him, he could not be found.' The narrative suddenly shifts: wayyaʿăbōr ('and he passed by/over') and wĕhinnēh ʾênennu, 'and behold, he was not.' The conjunction wĕhinnēh ('and behold!') signals the surprise of the absence: the psalmist looked for the previously dominant wicked man — waʾăbaqqĕšēhû ('I sought him') — and found nothing. The impressive tree of v.35 has vanished. The anecdote confirms the psalm's thesis: the wicked's flourishing is as temporary as it is dramatic.
37.37: šĕmār tām ûrĕʾēh yāšār kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm (šĕmār tām ûrĕʾēh yāšār kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm): 'Mark the blameless and behold the upright, for there is a future for the man of peace.' The imperatives šĕmār ('mark, observe!') and rĕʾēh ('see!') invite contemplative attention to the tām ('blameless one') and yāšār ('upright one'). What to observe: kî ʾaḥărît lĕʾîš šālôm, 'for there is a future/end for the man of peace.' The noun ʾaḥărît ('end, latter part, future') — usually referring to the final outcome of a life — is positive for the person of šālôm. The man of integrity has a future; the wicked's 'end' is destruction (v.38). The contrast of ends reveals which life was worth living.
37.38: ûpōšĕʿîm nišmĕdû yaḥdāw ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm nikrātâ (ûp̄ōšĕʿîm nišmĕdû yaḥdāw ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm nikrātâ): 'But transgressors shall be altogether destroyed; the future of the wicked shall be cut off.' The antithesis of v.37: pōšĕʿîm ('transgressors, rebels') nišmĕdû yaḥdāw, 'are destroyed together' — collective annihilation. The ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm ('future/end of the wicked') is nikrātâ ('cut off') — using the same covenant-curse verb as vv.9,22,34. The contrast ʾaḥărît šālôm versus ʾaḥărît rĕšāʿîm is the psalm's sharpest theodicy: the ends of the two lives are qualitatively opposite.
37.39: ûtĕšûʿat ṣaddîqîm mēYHWH māʿûzzām bĕʿēt ṣārâ (ûṯĕšûʿaṯ ṣaddîqîm mēYHWH māʿûzzām bĕʿēṯ ṣārâ): 'The salvation of the righteous is from the LORD; he is their stronghold in the time of trouble.' The verse states explicitly what the entire psalm has assumed: the righteous do not save themselves. tĕšûʿâ ('salvation, deliverance') is mēYHWH ('from the LORD') — its source is exclusively divine. The noun māʿûz ('stronghold, refuge') from ʿāzaz ('to be strong') describes YHWH as a fortified position — the same imagery as Ps 27:1 ('the LORD is the stronghold of my life'). The 'time of trouble' (bĕʿēt ṣārâ) is when the stronghold becomes most necessary — and most evident as sufficient.
37.40: waYHWH yaʿăzĕrēm wayfallĕṭēm yĕfallĕṭēm mērĕšāʿîm wĕyôšîʿēm kî-ḥāsû bô (waYHWH yaʿăzĕrēm wayfallĕṭēm yĕp̄allĕṭēm mērĕšāʿîm wĕyôšîʿēm kî-ḥāsû ḇô): 'The LORD helps them and delivers them; he delivers them from the wicked and saves them, because they take refuge in him.' The four divine actions — yaʿăzĕrēm ('helps them'), wayfallĕṭēm ('delivers them'), yĕfallĕṭēm ('he will deliver them'), wĕyôšîʿēm ('saves them') — are the accumulated deliverances of a full psalm of trust. The final clause kî-ḥāsû bô ('because they take refuge in him') closes the psalm where it began: the foundational act of ḥāsâ-trust (v.3, bĕṭaḥ baYHWH) produces the salvation described. Trust initiates; YHWH completes. The acrostic closes with the exhaustive grammar of rescue.

38.1: YHWH ʾal-bĕqeṣpĕkā tôkîḥēnî ûbăḥămātĕkā tĕyassĕrēnî (YHWH ʾal-bĕqeṣpĕkā ṯôḵîḥēnî ûḇăḥămāṯĕḵā ṯĕyassĕrēnî): 'O LORD, rebuke me not in your anger, nor discipline me in your wrath.' Psalm 38 is one of the seven penitential psalms (with Pss 6, 32, 51, 102, 130, 143) — psalms of confession and prayer for forgiveness. The opening, nearly identical to Ps 6:1, places the psalm in the tradition of prayer for divine restraint of punitive wrath. The two verbs — yākōḥ ('to rebuke, correct') and yāsar ('to discipline, chasten') — are both pedagogical: divine correction is real but potentially excessive. The psalmist does not deny deserving correction but prays that the wrath (qeṣep, ḥēmâ) be moderated: chastening, yes; annihilation, no.
38.2: kî ḥiṣṣêkā nēḥŝtu-bî ûtappîl ʿālay yādekā (kî ḥiṣṣêḵā nēḥŝtu-ḇî ûṯappîl ʿālay yāḏeḵā): 'For your arrows have sunk into me, and your hand has come down on me.' The arrows (ḥēṣ) of divine judgment — a metaphor for the painful circumstances (illness, loss) the psalmist experiences as divine response to sin — have nēḥŝtu ('sunk in, been driven'). The phrase ûtappîl ʿālay yādekā, 'and your hand presses down on me,' uses the hiphil of nāpal ('to fall, cast down') with the intensive sense of heavy pressure. YHWH's hand on the psalmist produces the physical symptoms described in vv.3-8 — the suffering is attributed to divine action, not random misfortune.
38.3: ʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî mippĕnê zaʿmekā ʾên šālôm baʿăṣāmay mippĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾtî (ʾên mĕṯōm biśĕrî mipp̄ĕnê zaʿmeḵā ʾên šālôm baʿăṣāmay mipp̄ĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾṯî): 'There is no soundness in my flesh because of your indignation; there is no health in my bones because of my sin.' The double ʾên ... ʾên ('there is no ... there is no') creates total privation: no mĕtōm ('soundness, wholeness') in the flesh (bāśār), no šālôm ('peace, health') in the bones (ʿeṣem). Both diagnoses are given double causation: mippĕnê zaʿmekā ('because of your indignation') and mippĕnê ḥaṭṭāʾtî ('because of my sin'). The psalmist refuses to separate physical suffering from theological cause: the illness is both the result of sin and the experience of divine displeasure.
38.4: kî ʿăwōnōtay ʿābĕrû rōʾšî kĕmaśśāʾ kābēd yikbĕdû mimmennî (kî ʿăwōnōṯay ʿāḇĕrû rōʾšî kĕmaśśāʾ kāḇēḏ yiḵbĕḏû mimmennî): 'For my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me.' The spatial metaphor ʿābar rōʾš ('pass over the head') describes overwhelming: the iniquities (ʿăwōnōt) have risen above the psalmist's capacity to manage, like floodwaters that submerge. The simile kĕmaśśāʾ kābēd ('like a heavy burden') — a load heavier than the bearer — captures the crushing weight. The verb yiḵbĕdû ('they are heavy, they press') from kābēd ('to be heavy, honored') — the same root as kābôd ('glory/weight') — inverts honor: what should be light (forgiven sin) becomes crushing (unforgiven guilt).
38.5: hibʾîšû nāmaqû ḥabbûrōtāy mippĕnê ʾiwwaltî (hiḇʾîšû nāmaqû ḥabbûrôṯāy mipp̄ĕnê ʾiwwaltî): 'My wounds stink and fester because of my foolishness.' The verb bāʾaš ('to stink, be offensive') in the hiphil hiḇʾîšû ('they have made to stink') — combined with nāmaqû ('they are putrid, have rotted') — describes infected wounds whose stench signals advanced corruption. The word ḥabbûrôt ('wounds, bruises, stripes') are the physical marks; mippĕnê ʾiwwaltî, 'because of my foolishness,' names the source. The noun ʾiwwelet ('foolishness') in Proverbs is the opposite of ḥokmâ ('wisdom') — the fool acts without regard for consequences or covenant, and the festering wounds are the embodied consequence.
38.6: naʿăwêtî šaḥōtî ʿad-mĕʾōd kol-hayyôm qōdēr hibbāltî (naʿăwêṯî šaḥōṯî ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ ḵol-hayyôm qōḏēr hibbālṯî): 'I am utterly bowed down and prostrate; all the day I go about mourning.' The verb ʿāwâ (niphal naʿăwêtî, 'I am bowed, twisted, bent over') captures the physical posture of someone whose weight of suffering prevents standing erect. The intensifiers — šāḥôtî ʿad-mĕʾōd ('bowed down exceedingly') — amplify. The temporal phrase kol-hayyôm ('all the day') eliminates any interval of relief. The participle qōdēr ('darkened, in dark mourning garments') describes the public appearance of grief: the psalmist walks about visibly in mourning, unable to conceal his state.
38.7: kî ḵeślay mālĕʾû niqeh ûʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî (kî ḵeślay māleʾû niqeh ûʾên mĕṯōm biśĕrî): 'For my loins are filled with burning, and there is no soundness in my flesh.' The noun kesālayim ('loins, the lumbar/kidney region') and the verbal niqeh (from nilqad or related — 'burning, inflammation') describe severe internal pain. The repeated ʾên mĕtōm biśĕrî ('no soundness in my flesh') from v.3 — the inclusio of bodily suffering — underscores that the illness is total: no part of the body has escaped the divine correction. The psalmist's entire physical being is the medium of penitential experience.
38.8: pûgôtî wĕnidkêtî ʿad-mĕʾōd šāʾagtî minnehămat libbî (p̄ûḡôṯî wĕniḏkêṯî ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ šāʾaḡtî minnehămaṯ libbî): 'I am feeble and crushed; I groan because of the tumult of my heart.' The verb pûg ('to be numb, feeble, exhausted') combined with dākkâ (niphal niḏkêṯî, 'I am crushed, pulverized') — the same root as dakkāʾ in Ps 34:18 ('crushed in spirit') — describe the inner dissolution of one who has reached physical and emotional exhaustion. The audible response: šāʾagtî ('I roar, groan') minnehămat libbî, 'because of the agitation/turmoil of my heart.' The lion-roar šāʾag applied to human anguish conveys the involuntary, raw vocalisation of extreme distress — what Hezekiah's prayer in Isa 38:14 calls 'chattering like a swallow.'
38.9: ʾădōnāy nĕgdĕkā kol-taʾăwātî wĕʾanḥātî mimmĕkā lōʾ-nistārâ (ʾăḏōnāy neḡḏeḵā ḵol-taʾăwāṯî wĕʾanḥāṯî mimmĕḵā lōʾ-nistārâ): 'O Lord, all my longing is before you; my sighing is not hidden from you.' The divine address ʾădōnāy ('my Lord') in direct speech — after the third-person description of suffering — personalizes the prayer. The phrase nĕgdĕkā kol-taʾăwātî, 'before you is all my longing,' uses neged ('in front of, before') to assert that YHWH sees the psalmist's deepest desire. The groaning (ʾanḥâ) that the heart produces (v.8) is lōʾ-nistārâ ('not hidden') from YHWH — nothing is concealed. The reassurance: even the inarticulate suffering of v.8 reaches God as fully as any formulated prayer.
38.10: libbî sĕḥar koḥî ʿăzābānî wĕʾôr-ʿênay gam-hēm ʾên ʾittî (libbî sĕḥar ḵōḥî ʿăzāḇānî wĕʾôr-ʿênay ḡam-hēm ʾên ʾittî): 'My heart throbs; my strength fails me, and the light of my eyes — it also has gone from me.' The verb sāḥar (here 'throb, race') describes the heart's physical distress — palpitations from illness or anxiety. The phrase kōḥî ʿăzābānî ('my strength has abandoned me') uses ʿāzab ('to leave, forsake') — the same verb used of YHWH's forsaking in Ps 22:1. Physical exhaustion is framed as abandonment. The most poignant element: wĕʾôr-ʿênay gam-hēm ʾên ʾittî, 'even the light of my eyes has left me' — the eyes that once saw light are darkened. Complete sensory and physical failure accompanies the spiritual crisis.
38.11: ʾōhăbay wĕrēʿay minneged negʿî yaʿamōdû ûqĕrôbay mērāḥōq ʿāmādû (ʾōhăḇay wĕrēʿay minnegeḏ negʿî yaʿămōḏû ûqĕrôḇay mērāḥôq ʿāmāḏû): 'My friends and companions stand aloof from my plague, and my nearest kin stand far off.' The social dimension of suffering: ʾōhăbay ('my lovers, those who love me'), rēʿay ('my companions'), and qĕrôbay ('my near ones, relatives') all maintain distance. The phrase minneged negʿî ('from the presence of my plague') — the wound/disease is the reason for their withdrawal — suggests that the psalmist's suffering is visible and repulsive. The spatial opposites yaʿamōdû ('they stand') and mērāḥōq ('at a distance') echo the social abandonment motif of Ps 22:11 and the suffering servant's social isolation in Isa 53:3. The abandonment by intimates is a recognizable form of human suffering that Jesus also experienced (Mark 14:50).
38.12: waynĕqĕšû mĕbaqqĕšê napšî wĕdōrĕšê rāʿātî dibbĕrû hawwôt (waynĕqĕšû mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî wĕḏōrĕšê rāʿāṯî dibbĕrû hawwôṯ): 'Those who seek my life lay their snares; those who seek my hurt speak of ruin and meditate treachery all the day long.' The contrast to the distant friends (v.11): the enemies are actively engaged. mĕbaqqĕšê napšî ('those who seek my life') lay traps (nāqaš, 'to snare'); dōrĕšê rāʿātî ('those who seek my harm') speak hawwôt ('ruination, disaster'). The phrase ûmirmôt kol-hayyôm yehgû, 'and meditate deceit all the day,' uses hāgâ from Ps 1:2 — the daily meditation on Torah inverted into daily scheming of deceit. What the righteous meditates on (Torah) determines character; what the wicked meditates on (mirmâ) determines destruction.
38.13: waʾănî kĕḥērēš lōʾ ʾĕšmaʿ ûkĕ'illēm lōʾ yiptaḥ pîw (waʾănî ḵĕḥērēš lōʾ ʾĕšmaʿ ûḵĕ'illēm lōʾ yip̄taḥ pîw): 'But I am like a deaf man; I do not hear, like a mute man who does not open his mouth.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but I') introduces the psalmist's chosen response to the enemies: silence. The similes kĕḥērēš ('like a deaf one') and kĕ'illēm ('like a mute one') describe a deliberate non-engagement. The psalmist neither hears the enemy's slanders nor responds to them — a profound counterintuitive strategy. Isaiah 53:7 applies a nearly identical posture to the Servant: 'like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.' Matthew 26:63 and 27:14 note that Jesus was silent before his accusers, fulfilling this prophetic-psalmist pattern.
38.14: waʾĕhî kĕʾîš ʾăšer lōʾ šômēaʿ ûʾên bĕpîw tôkāḥôt (waʾĕhî ḵĕʾîš ʾăšer lōʾ šômēaʿ ûʾên bĕp̄îw tôḵāḥôṯ): 'So I have become like a man who does not hear, and in whose mouth are no rebukes.' The continuation of v.13: the psalmist's silence extends to the absence of tôkāḥôt ('rebukes, counter-arguments') in his mouth. The word tôkāḥat is the technical term for the reasoned argument in a legal dispute — the psalmist has no counter-charges, no legal defense, no retaliatory rebuke. This absolute silence before accusation is one of the most remarkable postures in the Psalter. The choice to be silent rather than self-defend reflects trust in YHWH as the true advocate (cf. v.15).
38.15: kî-lĕkā YHWH hôḥāltî ʾattâ taʿăneh ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhāy (kî-lĕḵā YHWH hôḥāltî ʾattâ taʿăneh ʾăḏōnāy ʾĕlōhāy): 'For it is for you, O LORD, that I wait; it is you, O Lord my God, who will answer.' The silence of vv.13-14 is explained here: the psalmist is silent before humans kî ('because') he waits for YHWH to answer. The verb yāḥal (hophal hôḥāltî, 'I wait with hope') from ḥil ('to writhe, wait in tense expectation') indicates patient but urgent hope. YHWH himself — ʾattâ taʿăneh ('you will answer') — is the one who speaks in the courtroom where humans have heard only silence. The logic: be silent before false accusers; wait for the True Judge to answer.
38.16: kî-ʾāmartî pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî bĕmôṭ raglî ʿālay higdîlû (kî-ʾāmartî pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî bĕmôṭ raglî ʿālay higdîlû): 'For I said, 'Only let them not rejoice over me, who boast against me when my foot slips!'' The internal monologue kî-ʾāmartî ('for I said') reveals the psalmist's specific fear: pen-yiśmĕḥû-lî ('lest they rejoice at my expense'). The condition bĕmôṭ raglî ('when my foot slips') — the very stumbling that Ps 37:24 promises YHWH prevents — is here the feared occasion for enemy gloating. The phrase ʿālay higdîlû, 'they magnify themselves against me,' describes arrogant self-assertion at the psalmist's expense. The prayer for non-shame (cf. Pss 25:2; 35:19) underlies the entire penitential complex.
38.17: kî-ʾănî lĕṣelaʿ nākôn ûmaʿkōbî negdî tāmîd (kî-ʾănî lĕṣelaʿ nāḵôn ûmaʿkōḇî neḡḏî ṯāmîd): 'For I am ready to fall, and my pain is ever before me.' The phrase lĕṣelaʿ nākôn ('prepared for limping/stumbling') — the participial nākôn ('prepared, established') with ṣelaʿ ('limping, stumbling') — describes permanent precariousness: the psalmist is perpetually on the verge of collapse, one more failure away from falling. The phrase ûmaʿkōbî negdî tāmîd, 'and my pain is ever before me,' means the suffering is never out of sight: it is the constant foreground of experience, not the background. The word maʿkōb ('pain, anguish') appears in Isaiah 53:3 as the Servant's defining experience: 'a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.'
38.18: kî-ʿăwōnî ʾaggîd ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî (kî-ʿăwōnî ʾaggîd ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî): 'For I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin.' The decisive turn: the psalmist moves from describing suffering to explicit confession. The verb nāgad (hiphil ʾaggîd, 'I will declare, confess') marks the public acknowledgment of ʿăwōnî ('my iniquity'). The parallel ʾeddĕʾag mēḥaṭṭāʾtî, 'I am sorry/troubled because of my sin,' uses the verb dāʾag ('to be anxious, worried, sorrowful') — not mere intellectual acknowledgment but emotional distress over the sin itself. This is the movement of genuine repentance: from suffering → recognition of sin's role → active confession → sorrow. The penitential psalm reaches its theological core.
38.19: wĕʾōyĕbay ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû wĕrabbû śōnĕʾay šeqer (wĕʾōyĕḇay ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû wĕrabbû śōnĕʾay šeqer): 'But my enemies are vigorous and many, and those who hate me wrongfully have multiplied.' The contrast: the psalmist is diminished (vv.3-10), but the enemies are ḥayyîm ʿāṣēmû ('lively, vigorous and strong'). Their number has rabbû ('multiplied') — a qualitative increase in opposition. The phrase śōnĕʾay šeqer ('those who hate me falsely/without cause') returns to the Psalter's recurring motif of unprovoked enmity (cf. Ps 35:19; 69:4 — both cited in John 15:25 for the world's hatred of Jesus).
38.20: wĕmĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ yiśṭĕnûnî taḥat rŏdpî-ṭôb (wĕmĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥaṯ ṭôḇâ yiśṭĕnûnî ṯaḥaṯ rŏḏpî-ṭôḇ): 'Those who render evil for good accuse me because I follow after good.' The specific charge against the psalmist: mĕšallĕmê rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ ('those who repay evil for good') — the same moral inversion as Ps 35:12. The word śāṭan ('to be an adversary, to accuse') appears here in verbal form: yiśṭĕnûnî, 'they act as adversaries against me / they accuse me.' The ground of their accusation: taḥat rŏdpî-ṭôb, 'because I pursue good.' Goodness is the provocation: the wicked cannot tolerate virtue in others because it judges them by contrast.
38.21: ʾal-taʿazbēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî (ʾal-taʿazbēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî): 'Do not forsake me, O LORD! O my God, be not far from me!' The negative imperatives ʾal-taʿazbēnî ('do not forsake me!') and ʾal-tirḥaq ('do not be far!') are the central petition after the long confession and description of suffering. The prayer against divine abandonment (ʿāzab) picks up the physical abandonment of strength (v.10, kōḥî ʿăzābānî) — even the psalmist's own strength has forsaken him; now he prays YHWH will not do the same. The spatial nearness ʾal-tirḥaq echoes Ps 22:11 and Ps 35:22 — the same prayer in the darkest moments of the Psalter.
38.22: ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî ʾădōnāy tĕšûʿātî (ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî ʾăḏōnāy tĕšûʿāṯî): 'Make haste to help me, O Lord, my salvation!' The urgent final petition ḥûšâ ('hurry!') from ḥûš ('to move quickly, hasten') adds temporal urgency: help is needed now. The address ʾădōnāy with the possessive tĕšûʿātî ('my salvation') makes YHWH personally the psalmist's yešûʿâ — salvation personified. The brief ending stands in sharp contrast to the extended suffering of vv.1-20: the long description of distress yields to a two-element prayer — come to my help quickly, you who are my salvation. The compression is itself a form of trust: the psalmist reduces everything to the one thing needed.

39.1: ʾāmartî ʾeśmĕrâ dĕrākay mēḥăṭōʾ bilĕšônî ʾeśmĕrâ lĕpî mazzōm (ʾāmartî ʾeśmĕrâ ḏĕrāḵay mēḥăṭōʾ ḇilĕšônî ʾeśmĕrâ lĕp̄î mazzōm): 'I said, 'I will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue; I will guard my mouth with a muzzle.'' Psalm 39 is attributed to David with a dedication to Jeduthun, one of the chief temple musicians (1 Chr 16:41-42). The opening resolve — ʾeśmĕrâ ('I will guard') applied to both darkay ('my ways') and pî ('my mouth') — describes the determined self-restraint of someone who has learned that careless speech about God's governance leads to sin. The noun mazzôm (or miśmôr, 'muzzle, bridle') is uniquely striking: the mouth is to be as controlled as a bridled horse. The reason: bĕʿôd rāšāʿ lĕnegdî, 'while the wicked are in my presence' — the psalmist restrains speech about divine justice in the hearing of the ungodly, lest his complaint be ammunition for their atheism.
39.2: neʾĕlamtî dûmiyyâ heḥĕšêtî mîṭṭôb ûmaʿkōbî neʿkār (neʾĕlamtî ḏûmiyyâ heḥĕšêtî mîṭṭôb ûmaʿkōḇî neʿkār): 'I was mute and silent; I held my peace to no avail, and my distress grew worse.' The three verbs of silence — neʾĕlamtî ('I was made mute'), dûmiyyâ ('I was still/silent'), heḥĕšêtî ('I kept quiet') — escalate: silence was attempted at every level. The phrase mîṭṭôb ('from good') suggests the silence extended even to good things — he held back not just complaints but anything. The result: ûmaʿkōbî neʿkār, 'and my pain was stirred/increased.' The silence that was meant to manage distress instead intensified it: suppressed anguish becomes more acute. The psalm's opening explains why speech eventually erupts (v.3).
39.3: ḥam-libbî bĕqirbî bĕhaggîgî tîbʿar-ʾēš dibbartî billĕšônî (ḥam-libbî bĕqirḇî bĕhaggîgî ṯîḇʿar-ʾēš dibbartî ḇillĕšônî): 'My heart became hot within me. As I mused, the fire burned; then I spoke with my tongue.' The heart's heat — ḥam-libbî ('my heart became hot') — describes suppressed anguish building to overflow. The verb hāgâ (bĕhaggîgî, 'as I mused/meditated') is the meditation root of Ps 1:2 — but here meditation intensifies rather than calms: tîbʿar-ʾēš, 'the fire burned.' Fire is the image of contained energy suddenly beyond control: the discipline of vv.1-2 fails, and dibbartî billĕšônî ('I spoke with my tongue') — the resolution breaks. The psalmist speaks — and what he speaks is the meditation on mortality in vv.4-6.
39.4: hôdîʿēnî YHWH qiṣṣî ûmiddat yāmay mah-hîʾ ʾēdĕʿâ meh-ḥādēl ʾānî (hôḏîʿēnî YHWH qiṣṣî ûmiḏḏaṯ yāmay mah-hîʾ ʾēḏĕʿâ meh-ḥāḏēl ʾānî): 'O LORD, make me know my end and what is the measure of my days; let me know how fleeting I am!' The prayer for self-knowledge hôdîʿēnî ('make me know!') is unusual: the psalmist asks YHWH to reveal his mortality — qiṣṣî ('my end'), middat yāmay ('the measure of my days'). The phrase meh-ḥādēl ʾānî, 'how transient/failing I am,' uses ḥādal ('to cease, fail, stop') — the psalmist as someone who will simply stop. The prayer for mortality-awareness is not morbid self-pity but a request for the theological insight that Ps 90:12 calls wisdom: 'teach us to number our days.'
39.5: hinnēh ṭĕpāḥôt nātattâ yāmay wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin nĕgdekā (hinnēh ṭĕp̄āḥôṯ nāṯattâ yāmay wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin neḡḏeḵā): 'Behold, you have made my days a few handbreadths, and my lifetime is as nothing before you. Surely all mankind stands as a mere breath!' The handbreadth (ṭepaḥ) — roughly 3 inches, the smallest unit of measurement — reduces the span of a life to a trivial interval. The phrase wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin nĕgdekā, 'my lifespan is as nothing before you,' uses the comparison to divine eternity: sub specie aeternitatis, any human life is negligible in duration. The conclusion: ʾak kol-hebel kol-ʾādām niṣṣāb selah, 'surely every man standing is but breath.' The word hebel ('breath, vapor, vanity') — the central term of Ecclesiastes — makes human existence as transient as an exhaled breath. The Selah invites meditation on the weight of this reality.
39.6: ʾak bĕṣelem yithallak-ʾîš ʾak-hebel yehĕmāyûn yiṣbōr wĕlōʾ-yēdaʿ mî-yōsĕpēm (ʾaḵ bĕṣelem yiṯhallak-ʾîš ʾaḵ-heḇel yehĕmāyûn yiṣḇōr wĕlōʾ-yēḏaʿ mî-yōsĕp̄ēm): 'Surely a man goes about like a shadow! Surely for nothing they are in turmoil; man heaps up wealth and does not know who will gather.' The phrase bĕṣelem yithallak-ʾîš ('a man goes about like a shadow/image') uses ṣelem ('image, shadow') — the same word as 'image of God' (bĕṣelem ʾĕlōhîm, Gen 1:26-27) — here ironically: humanity made in the image is itself a shadow. The turmoil of wealth-accumulation: yiṣbōr wĕlōʾ-yēdaʿ mî-yōsĕpēm, 'he piles up and does not know who will gather it.' Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) directly engages this wisdom.
39.7: wĕʿattâ mah-qiwwîtî ʾădōnāy tôḥaltî lĕkā hîʾ (wĕʿattâ mah-qiwwîtî ʾăḏōnāy tôḥaltî lĕḵā hîʾ): 'And now, O Lord, for what do I wait? My hope is in you.' The transitional wĕʿattâ ('and now') marks the pivot from the meditation on mortality (vv.4-6) to its theological conclusion: if human life is hebel and human wealth is uncertain, what can the psalmist wait for? The answer: tôḥaltî lĕkā hîʾ ('my hope is in you — it is you'). The emphatic hîʾ ('it is') makes YHWH the exclusive content of hope: not health, not prosperity, not longevity — YHWH himself. The tôḥalt root (from yāḥal, 'wait/hope') identifies the type of hope: taut, expectant, rope-like — a hope that pulls toward its object.
39.8: mikkol-pĕšāʿay haṣṣîlēnî ḥerpat nābāl ʾal-tĕśîmēnî (mikḵol-p̄ĕšāʿay haṣṣîlēnî ḥerpat nāḇāl ʾal-tĕśîmēnî): 'Deliver me from all my transgressions. Do not make me the scorn of the fool!' The two petitions: haṣṣîlēnî ('deliver me') from pĕšāʿay ('my transgressions/rebellions') — deliverance from sin's consequences. And: ʾal-tĕśîmēnî ḥerpat nābāl, 'do not make me an object of reproach for the fool' — the nābāl ('moral fool' of Ps 14:1) who denies God's governance would use the psalmist's suffering as evidence that righteousness does not pay. The prayer against becoming fodder for practical atheism joins personal deliverance to theological witness.
39.9: neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾeptaḥ pî kî ʾattâ ʿāśîtā (neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾep̄taḥ pî kî ʾattâ ʿāśîṯā): 'I am mute; I do not open my mouth, for it is you who have done it.' The silence of vv.1-2 returns: neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾeptaḥ pî ('I am mute, I do not open my mouth'). But now the reason is given: kî ʾattâ ʿāśîtā, 'for you have done it.' The suffering is acknowledged as divine action — the same theology as Ps 38:2-3 but now stated simply and without accusation. The psalmist does not complain to others about what God has done; silence before humans is the response to the recognition that divine sovereignty governs all that happens. This is the silent submission of Job 1:21 ('the LORD gave, the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD').
39.10: hāsēr mēʿālay nigʿekā mittignûrat yādĕkā ʾănî kālîtî (hāsēr mēʿālay niḡʿeḵā mittignûraṯ yāḏĕḵā ʾănî ḵālîṯî): 'Remove your stroke from me; I am spent by the hostility of your hand.' The imperative hāsēr ('remove, turn away!') from sûr applied to nigʿekā ('your blow/stroke') — the divine affliction of v.2 that is experienced as YHWH's hand. The phrase mittignûrat yādĕkā ('from the hostility/contention of your hand') — tigra' may mean 'blow/combat/opposition' — pictures the divine hand pressing down. The result: ʾănî kālîtî, 'I am spent/consumed' — from kālâ ('to be finished, consumed'). The psalmist is at the limit of his capacity to bear the divine correction.
39.11: bĕtôkāḥôt ʿal-ʿāwōn yissartā ʾîš wattemes kaʿāš ḥămûdô ʾak hebel kol-hāʾādām selah (bĕṯôḵāḥôṯ ʿal-ʿāwōn yissartā ʾîš wattemes kaʿāš ḥămûdô ʾaḵ heḇel ḵol-hāʾāḏām selah): 'When you discipline a man with rebukes for sin, you consume like a moth what is dear to him; surely all mankind is a mere breath!' The metaphor of moth consumption — kāʿāš ḥămûdô ('like a moth, what is dear to him') — images the silent, invisible destruction of what is most precious: the moth consumes from within, leaving the outward form intact but the substance eaten away. Divine discipline for sin operates similarly: gradually consuming the things one holds dear. The Selah again punctuates the conclusion ʾak hebel kol-hāʾādām, 'surely all humanity is a breath' — reinforcing the transience established in v.5.
39.12: šimʿâ tĕpillātî YHWH wĕšawʿātî haʾăzînâ ʾel-dimʿātî ʾal-teḥeraš (šimʿâ ṯĕp̄illāṯî YHWH wĕšawʿāṯî haʾăzînâ ʾel-dimʿāṯî ʾal-teḥeraš): 'Hear my prayer, O LORD, and give ear to my cry; hold not your peace at my tears.' The petition summons three divine senses: hearing for the prayer (tĕpillâ), attentive listening for the cry (šawʿâ), and responding to the tears (dimʿâ). The phrase ʾal-teḥeraš ('do not be silent!') makes the tears themselves a form of prayer — even inarticulate weeping demands divine response. The theological ground: kî gēr ʾānōkî ʿimmĕkā tôšāb kĕkol-ʾăbōtāy, 'for I am a sojourner with you, a guest, as all my fathers were.' The psalmist's identity as a gēr ('sojourner, alien') before YHWH provides the basis for his appeal — sojourners depend entirely on their host's hospitality (cf. Heb 11:13: 'strangers and pilgrims on the earth').
39.13: hāšaʿ mimmennî wĕʾabligâ bĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî (hāšaʿ mimmennî wĕʾaḇliḡâ ḇĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî): 'Look away from me, that I may smile again, before I depart and am no more!' The closing request is striking: hāšaʿ mimmennî ('look away from me!') — previously the psalmist prayed for God to look at him (v.12); now he prays for divine gaze to relent. The divine attention has been experienced as the source of his affliction (v.10-11); a momentary lifting of that gaze would allow recovery. The purpose wĕʾabligâ ('that I might brighten/recover a smile') from bālag ('to smile, brighten') — even a brief respite before the end. The phrase bĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî, 'before I go and am no more,' is the psalm's most poignant phrase — the awareness of mortality pressing close. The psalm ends not with resolution but with the raw prayer of one who expects death soon and wants a moment's relief before it comes.

40.1: qawwōh qiwwîtî YHWH wayyēṭ ʾēlay wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî (qawwōh qiwwîtî YHWH wayyēṭ ʾēlay wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî): 'I waited patiently for the LORD; he inclined to me and heard my cry.' The cognate infinitive absolute qawwōh qiwwîtî ('I waited, waited!') is intensified repetition — the waiting was sustained, thorough, emphatic. This is the taut expectation of qāwâ ('to hope with expectation'), not passive resignation. The divine response: wayyēṭ ʾēlay ('he inclined toward me') — the anthropomorphism of a king bending to hear a petition — and wayyišmaʿ šawʿātî ('he heard my cry'). The psalm opens with answered prayer and works backward and forward from this center of rescue.
40.2: wayyaʿălēnî mibbôr šāʾôn mîṭîṭ hayyāwēn wayyāqem ʿal-selaʿ raglāy kônnēn ʾăšûrāy (wayyaʿălēnî mibbôr šāʾôn mîṭîṭ hayyāwēn wayyāqem ʿal-selaʿ raglāy ḵônnēn ʾăšûrāy): 'He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, making my steps secure.' The spatial rescue from bôr šāʾôn ('the pit of roaring/destruction') and ṭîṭ hayyāwēn ('the miry clay') to selaʿ ('a rock') is the psalm's central metaphor: from chaotic instability to stable ground. The word bôr ('pit') is used for the grave/Sheol in Ps 28:1; 30:3 — the rescue is from the edge of death. The rock (selaʿ) appears throughout the Psalter as the divine attribute of stability (Ps 18:2, 31); here it is the literal place where the delivered one stands.
40.3: wayyittēn bĕpî šîr ḥādāš tĕhillâ lēʾlōhênû yirʾû rabbîm wĕyîrāʾû wĕyibṭĕḥû baYHWH (wayyittēn bĕp̄î šîr ḥādāš ṯĕhillâ lēʾlōhênû yirʾû rabbîm wĕyîrāʾû wĕyiḇṭĕḥû ḇaYHWH): 'He put a new song in my mouth, a song of praise to our God. Many will see and fear, and put their trust in the LORD.' The divine gift of šîr ḥādāš ('a new song') — the song that rescue produces is qualitatively new: it cannot be the old song because the situation is transformed. The phrase wayyittēn bĕpî ('he placed in my mouth') makes the song a divine endowment, not self-generated praise. The missionary effect: yirʾû rabbîm ('many will see') → wĕyîrāʾû ('they will fear') → wĕyibṭĕḥû baYHWH ('they will trust in the LORD'). Rescue generates testimony which generates fear of God which generates trust: a chain of effect from one person's deliverance to the expansion of the covenant community.
40.4: ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer śām YHWH miṭlaḥtô wĕlōʾ-pānâ ʾel-rĕhābîm wĕśāṭê kāzāb (ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer śām YHWH miṭlaḥtô wĕlōʾ-p̄ānâ ʾel-rĕhābîm wĕśāṭê ḵāzāb): 'Blessed is the man who makes the LORD his trust, who does not turn to the proud, to those who go astray after a lie!' The beatitude ʾašrê ('blessed!') marks the transition from personal testimony to universal instruction. The one who makes YHWH miṭlaḥtô ('his trust') — the object of utter reliance — is blessed. The negative contrasts: lōʾ-pānâ ('he does not turn') toward rĕhābîm ('the proud, arrogant') or śāṭê kāzāb ('those who go off after a lie'). The lie is the false alternative to YHWH — any competing object of trust that promises what only YHWH can give.
40.5: rabbôt ʿāśîtā ʾattâ YHWH ʾĕlōhay niplĕʾōtêkā wĕmaḥšĕbōtêkā ʾēlênû (rabbôṯ ʿāśîṯā ʾattâ YHWH ʾĕlōhay niplĕʾōṯeḵā wĕmaḥšĕḇōṯeḵā ʾēlênû): 'You have multiplied, O LORD my God, your wondrous deeds and your thoughts toward us; none can compare with you!' The declaration rabbôt ʿāśîtā ('you have done many things') introduces the theme of divine abundance: the niplĕʾôt ('wonders, marvelous deeds') and maḥšĕbôt ('thoughts, plans') toward ʾêlênû ('us') are countless. The phrase ʾên-ʿārak ʾēlêkā ('none can compare to you') is the incomparability formula — YHWH's acts are without analogue. The attempt to declare them: ʾaggîdâ wădabbĕrâ ʿāṣĕmû missappēr ('if I would declare and speak of them, they are too many to count') — the exhaustion of language before divine abundance.
40.6: zebaḥ ûminḥâ lōʾ-ḥāpaṣtā ʾoznayim kārîtā lî ʿōlâ waḥăṭāʾâ lōʾ šāʾāltā (zeḇaḥ ûminḥâ lōʾ-ḥāp̄aṣtā ʾoznayim kārîtā lî ʿōlâ waḥăṭāʾâ lōʾ šāʾāltā): 'In sacrifice and offering you have not delighted; ears you have dug for me; burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.' The declaration that YHWH does not ḥāpaṣ ('delight in') the four sacrifice types — zebaḥ (peace offering), minḥâ (grain offering), ʿōlâ (burnt offering), ḥaṭāʾâ (sin offering) — is prophetically radical, echoing Isa 1:11; Amos 5:21-22; Mic 6:6-8. The remarkable alternative: ʾoznayim kārîtā lî, 'ears you have dug for me' (MT) — possibly meaning 'you have created me as one who hears/obeys.' The LXX reads instead 'a body you have prepared for me' (sōma katērtisō moi), and Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes the LXX version, applying it to the incarnation: Christ's body is the true offering that replaces animal sacrifice.
40.7: ʾāz ʾāmartî hinnēh bāʾtî bimĕgillat-sēper kātûb ʿālay (ʾāz ʾāmartî hinnēh ḇāʾṯî ḇimĕgillat-sēper kāṯûḇ ʿālay): 'Then I said, 'Behold, I have come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me.'' The phrase hinnēh bāʾtî ('behold, I have come') is the response to the inadequacy of sacrifices: the psalmist comes in person, in obedience. The scroll (mĕgillat-sēper) contains what is kātûb ʿālay ('written about me') — a mysterious scroll that prescribes the speaker's role. Hebrews 10:7 applies this to Christ: 'Then I said, 'Behold, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written of me in the scroll of the book.'' The scroll that prescribes Christ's obedience encompasses the entire OT witness to the Messiah — the Psalter's voice becomes the Messiah's self-declaration at the incarnation.
40.8: laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā ʾĕlōhay ḥāpaṣtî wĕtôrātĕkā bĕtôk mēʿāy (laʿăśôṯ rĕṣôneḵā ʾĕlōhay ḥāp̄aṣtî wĕṯôrāṯeḵā bĕṯôḵ mēʿāy): 'I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart.' The psalmist's (and in Heb 10, Christ's) self-description: laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā ḥāpaṣtî, 'to do your will I delight' — the ḥāpaṣ ('delight') that YHWH does not find in animal sacrifice (v.6) is found here, in the person who delights in obedience. The parallel: wĕtôrātĕkā bĕtôk mēʿāy, 'and your Torah is in my inner parts/bowels' — Torah internalized in the very viscera (mēʿayim), not merely studied. This is the Jeremianic new covenant (Jer 31:33) realized in the individual who offers obedience rather than animal blood. In Christ, this becomes the perfect incarnate obedience that accomplishes what no sacrifice could.
40.9: biśśartî ṣedeq bĕqāhāl rāb hinnēh śĕpātay lōʾ ʾeklaʾ YHWH ʾattâ yādāʿtā (biśśarṯî ṣeḏeq bĕqāhāl rāḇ hinnēh śĕp̄āṯay lōʾ ʾeḵlāʾ YHWH ʾattâ yāḏāʿtā): 'I have told the glad news of righteousness in the great congregation; behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, O LORD.' The verb bāśar (piel biśśartî, 'to bring good news, evangelize') — the root of bĕśôrâ ('good news, gospel') — makes the psalmist's public testimony explicitly an act of evangelism: the ṣedeq ('righteousness/vindication') is proclaimed as glad tidings to the qāhāl rāb ('the great assembly'). The witness is complete: lōʾ ʾeklaʾ śĕpātay ('I have not restrained my lips'). The LXX uses euēngelisamēn ('I evangelized') — the same word-group as NT euaggelion ('gospel').
40.10: lōʾ-kissîtî ṣidqātĕkā bĕtôk libbî ʾĕmûnātĕkā ûtĕšûʿātĕkā ʾāmartî lōʾ-kiḥadtî ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā lĕqāhāl rāb (lōʾ-kissîṯî ṣiḏqāṯeḵā bĕṯôḵ libbî ʾĕmûnāṯeḵā ûṯĕšûʿāṯeḵā ʾāmartî lōʾ-ḵiḥaḏtî ḥasdĕḵā waʾămiṯṯeḵā lĕqāhāl rāḇ): 'I have not hidden your righteousness within my heart; I have spoken of your faithfulness and your salvation; I have not concealed your steadfast love and your faithfulness from the great assembly.' The five divine attributes publicly declared — ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness'), ʾĕmûnâ ('faithfulness'), yĕšûʿâ ('salvation'), ḥesed ('steadfast love'), ʾĕmet ('truth/faithfulness') — are the content of the testimony. The double negation lōʾ kissîtî ... lōʾ kiḥadtî ('I have not hidden ... I have not concealed') frames the proclamation as total, leaving nothing of YHWH's character unexpressed. The lĕqāhāl rāb ('to the great assembly') makes the testimony fully public.
40.11: ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-tiklaʾ raḥămêkā mimmennî ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā tāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî (ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-ṯiḵlāʾ raḥămeḵā mimmennî ḥasdĕḵā waʾămiṯṯeḵā ṯāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî): 'As for you, O LORD, you will not restrain your mercy from me; your steadfast love and your faithfulness will ever preserve me.' The transition: the psalmist declared God's character publicly (vv.9-10); now he prays that character toward himself. The verb kālaʾ ('restrain, hold back') — used in v.9 for the psalmist's non-restraint of lips — is now inverted in prayer: ʾattâ YHWH lōʾ-tiklaʾ raḥămêkā ('you, O LORD, do not restrain your mercies from me'). The dual guardians ḥasdĕkā waʾămittĕkā ('your steadfast love and your faithfulness') tāmîd yiṣṣĕrûnî ('always preserve me') — these divine attributes are the relentless pursuers of Ps 23:6's 'goodness and mercy shall follow me.'
40.12: kî ʾăpāpûnî rāʿôt ʿad-ʾên mispar tĕʾăzûqûnî ʿăwōnōtay wĕlōʾ-yākolltî lirʾôt ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôt rōʾšî (kî ʾăp̄āp̄ûnî rāʿôṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispar ṯĕʾăzûqûnî ʿăwōnōṯay wĕlōʾ-yāḵoltî lirʾôṯ ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôṯ rōʾšî): 'For evils have encompassed me beyond number; my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see; they are more than the hairs of my head; my heart fails me.' The psalm pivots from praise (vv.1-11) to lament (vv.12-17). The evils (rāʿôt) that encompass (ʾāpap, 'surround, engulf') are ʿad-ʾên mispar ('without number'). The iniquities (ʿăwōnōt) that tĕʾăzûqûnî ('have overtaken, seized') — from ʾāzaq ('to seize') — are ʿāṣĕmû miśśaʿărôt rōʾšî, 'more than the hairs of my head.' The heart's failure — wĕlibî ʿăzābānî ('my heart has forsaken me') — returns to the language of Ps 38's bodily collapse. Vv.12-17 appear again in Psalm 70 as a standalone prayer for help, confirming its independent liturgical use.
40.13: rĕṣēh YHWH lĕhaṣṣîlēnî YHWH lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ (rĕṣēh YHWH lĕhaṣṣîlēnî YHWH lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ): 'Be pleased, O LORD, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me!' The double imperative — rĕṣēh ('be pleased, be willing!') and ḥûšâ ('hurry!') — addresses both YHWH's will and his timing. The verb rāṣâ ('to be pleased with, accept favorably') asks YHWH to lĕhaṣṣîlēnî ('deliver me') — not from duty but from willing favor. The urgency ḥûšâ lĕʿezrātî, 'hurry to my help,' returns to Ps 38:22's identical closing. The phrase recurs in Ps 70:1 and shapes the ending of the Psalter's Daily Prayer tradition. In the liturgy it becomes the opening of the Hours of prayer (Deus in adiutorium meum intende).
40.14: yēbōšû wĕyaḥpĕrû yaḥdāw mĕbaqqĕšê napšî lispôtāh yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăpēṣê rāʿātî (yēḇōšû wĕyaḥp̄ĕrû yaḥḏāw mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî lisp̄ôṯāh yissōḡû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăp̄ēṣê rāʿāṯî): 'Let them be put to shame and disappointed altogether who seek to snatch away my life; let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt.' The imprecatory jussives — yēbōšû ('let them be ashamed'), yaḥpĕrû ('let them be confounded'), yissōgû ʾāḥôr ('let them be turned back'), yikkālĕmû ('let them be disgraced') — cover both the inner shame and the outward retreat of enemies. The phrase mĕbaqqĕšê napšî lispôtāh, 'those who seek to snatch away my life/throat,' uses lispôt ('to sweep away, snatch') — the violence of consumption imagery. The parallel ḥăpēṣê rāʿātî ('those who delight in my harm') echoes Ps 35:26.
40.15: yāšōmmû ʿal-ʿēqeb buštatām hāʾōmĕrîm lî heʾāḥ heʾāḥ (yāšōmmû ʿal-ʿēqeḇ buštatām hāʾōmĕrîm lî heʾāḥ heʾāḥ): 'Let them be appalled because of their shame who say to me, 'Aha, Aha!'' The prayer yāšōmmû ('let them be desolate/appalled') — from šāmam ('to be astonished, desolate, horrified') — asks that the enemies' shame become the occasion for their own horror. The exclamation heʾāḥ heʾāḥ ('aha! aha!') — which the enemies cry at the psalmist's suffering (Ps 35:21 also) — will rebound on those who uttered it: their gloating becomes their own shame.
40.16: yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû bĕkā kol-mĕbaqqĕšêkā yōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal YHWH ʾōhăbê tĕšûʿātekā (yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḇeḵā ḵol-mĕḇaqqĕšêḵā yōʾmĕrû ṯāmîd yiḡḏal YHWH ʾōhăḇê ṯĕšûʿāṯeḵā): 'May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; may those who love your salvation say continually, 'Great is the LORD!'' The counter-prayer for the covenant community: yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû ('let them rejoice and be glad') — the same verbs used of the hoped-for individual joy in Ps 40:9. The community of mĕbaqqĕšêkā ('those who seek you') and ʾōhăbê tĕšûʿātekā ('those who love your salvation') join in the permanent liturgy yigdal YHWH ('great is the LORD!'). The enemies say heʾāḥ (v.15); the community says yigdal YHWH — the contrasting communal speech defines the two assemblies.
40.17: waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾădōnāy yaḥăšōb lî ʿezrātî ûmĕpalṭî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhay ʾal-tĕʾaḥar (waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾeḇyôn ʾăḏōnāy yaḥăšōḇ lî ʿezrātî ûmĕp̄alṭî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhay ʾal-ṯĕʾaḥar): 'As for me, I am poor and needy, but the Lord takes thought for me. You are my help and my deliverer; do not delay, O my God!' The final verse holds the entire psalm in miniature: the psalmist is ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ('poor and needy') — the Psalter's most vulnerable pair — yet ʾădōnāy yaḥăšōb lî, 'the Lord thinks of me / takes me into account.' The verb ḥāšab ('to think, plan, account') suggests divine deliberation and intention directed toward the psalmist: YHWH calculates for him. The self-designations — ʿezrātî ('my help') and mĕpalṭî ('my deliverer') — are the psalmist's titles for YHWH. The closing prayer ʾal-tĕʾaḥar ('do not delay!') from ʾāḥar ('to tarry') urgently requests what was already promised. The psalm ends with the believer's most characteristic posture: needy, known, and waiting.

41.1: אַשְׁרֵי מַשְׂכִּיל אֶל-דָּל (ʾashrê maskîl ʾel-dāl, "Blessed is the one who cares for the vulnerable") — dāl: thin, weak, poor — the one who is diminished (from dālal: to dangle, to be brought low). This is the physically and socially vulnerable person, not the spiritually poor (ʾānî). Maskîl here is not the psalm-type but the verb: "who acts wisely/attentively toward" the vulnerable. The wisdom of the caring person is the reciprocating structure: the LORD will deliver that person in the day of trouble — care given produces care received.
41.2: יְהוָה יִשְׁמְרֵהוּ וִיחַיֵּהוּ (YHWH yishmĕrêhû wîḥayyêhû, "The LORD protects and preserves his life") — shāmar: to keep, to guard; ḥāyāh (piel: to keep alive, to give life). אַשַּׁר בָּאָרֶץ (ʾushshar bāʾāreṣ, "he is regarded as blessed in the land") — ʾāshar (pual: to be called/regarded as blessed) — a passive form of the ʾashrê root. The social recognition of blessedness follows from the divine protection. וְאַל-תִּתְּנֵהוּ בְּנֶפֶשׁ אֹיְבָיו (wĕʾal-tittĕnêhû bĕnepesh ʾōyĕbāyw, "you will not hand him over to his enemies' desire") — nepesh (soul/desire): the enemies' deep appetite for his destruction is thwarted.
41.3: יְהוָה יִסְעָדֶנּוּ עַל-עֶרֶשׂ דְּוָי (YHWH yisʿādennû ʿal-ʿereś dĕwāy, "The LORD supports him on his bed of illness") — sāʿad: to support, to sustain (as a crutch or prop — the word used for bread "sustaining the heart" in Gen 18:5). ʿEreś: a couch, bed; dĕwāy: illness, languishing (from dāwāh: to be faint, sick — the same root as the "sick with love" in Song 2:5). כָּל-מִשְׁכָּבוֹ הָפַכְתָּ בְחָלְיוֹ (kol-mishkābô hāpaktā bĕḥolyô, "in his sickness you bring him back to health") — literally "you have turned/transformed all his bed in his illness" — the bed of illness reversed.
41.4: אֲנִי-אָמַרְתִּי יְהוָה חָנֵּנִי (ʾănî-ʾāmartî YHWH ḥonnênî, "As for me, I said, 'LORD, have mercy on me'") — the shift to the Psalmist's own cry: ḥānan: to be gracious, to show unearned favor. רְפָאָה נַפְשִׁי כִּי-חָטָאתִי לָךְ (rĕpāʾāh napshî kî-ḥāṭāʾtî lāk, "heal my soul, for I have sinned against you") — rāpāʾ: to heal; nāpesh: the whole animated self. The confession that the sickness may be connected to sin — not as a theological claim that all sickness is punishment, but as the Psalmist's personal acknowledgment of a possible link. The prayer is for healing of the nāpesh (the inner person), not merely the body.
41.5: אוֹיְבַי יֹאמְרוּ רַע לִי (ʾôyĕbay yōʾmĕrû raʿ lî, "My enemies speak wickedly against me") — literally "speak evil of me/to me." מָתַי יָמוּת וְאָבַד שְׁמוֹ (mātay yāmût wĕʾābad shĕmô, "When will he die and his name vanish?") — māwet: death; ʾābad: to perish, to be destroyed. The enemies' desire: not merely his death but the obliteration of his shēm (name/reputation) — the erasure of his social existence. In the ancient world, the continuity of one's name was the nearest equivalent to immortality.
41.6: וְאִם-בָּא לִרְאוֹת שָׁוְא יְדַבֵּר (wĕʾim-bāʾ lirʾôt shāwľ yĕdabbêr, "When one of them comes to visit me, he mouths empty words") — shāwľ: emptiness, worthlessness, vanity — the visit is a social performance concealing hostile intelligence-gathering. לִבּוֹ יִקְבָּץ-אָוֶן לוֹ (libbô yiqbaṣ-ʾāwen lô, "while inwardly filing away material against me") — qābaṣ: to gather, to collect; ʾāwen: trouble, iniquity, harmful intention. The heart as the storage chamber for accumulated ammunition against the sick person.
41.7: יַחַד עָלַי יִתְלַחֲשׁוּ כָּל-שֹׂנְאָי (yaḥad ʿālay yitlaḥăshû kol-śōnĕʾāy, "All who hate me whisper together against me") — lāḥash: to whisper, to mutter (originally of the snake's hiss — the same root used for the whisperer/serpent of Gen 3:1). Yaḥad: together, as a unified conspiracy. עָלַי יַחְשְׁבוּ רָעָה לִי (ʿālay yaḥshĕbû rāʿāh lî, "they are plotting disaster for me") — ḥāshab: to think, to devise, to calculate (the accounting word — planning evil as deliberate design).
41.8: דְּבַר-בְּלִיַּעַל יָצוּק בּוֹ (dĕbar-bĕliyaʿal yāṣûq bô, "Something incurable has seized him") — bĕliyaʿal: the chaos/worthlessness force (as in Ps 18:4 — Belial). The phrase "a word/thing of Belial is poured into him" — the illness as the hostile force of chaotic destruction fastened on him. וַאֲשֶׁר שָׁכַב לֹא-יוֹסִיף לָקוּם (waʾăsher shākab lōʾ-yôsîp lāqûm, "he will never get up from where he is lying") — the enemies' confident prediction: this is terminal. The language of the lying-down of death.
41.9: גַּם-אִישׁ שְׁלוֹמִי אֲשֶׁר-בָּטַחְתִּי בוֹ (gam-ʾîsh shĕlômî ʾăsher-bāṭaḥtî bô, "Even my closest friend, whom I trusted") — ʾîsh shĕlômî: "man of my peace/wholeness" — the one with whom one shares shālôm (covenant wholeness). Bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on. אוֹכֵל לַחְמִי הִגְדִּיל עָלַי עָקֵב (ʾōkêl laḥmî higdîl ʿālay ʿāqêb, "who shared my bread, has turned against me") — ʾōkêl laḥmî: "one who eats my bread" — table-fellowship as the covenant bond; bread-sharing was the sign of covenant relationship (Gen 31:54; Exod 18:12). ʿĀqêb: heel — "lifted up the heel against me." Jesus applies this verse to Judas in John 13:18 ("the one who eats my bread has turned against me"), making the betrayal the fulfillment of this pattern.
41.10: וְאַתָּה יְהוָה חָנֵּנִי וַהֲקִימֵנִי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ḥonnênî wahăqîmênî, "But you, LORD, have mercy on me and restore me") — the turn from the enemies' confident prediction (he will never rise) to the prayer for rising. Qûm (hiphil: to cause to rise, to set upright): the counterword to the lying-down of v8. וַאֲשַׁלְּמָה-לָּהֶם (waʾăshallĕmāh-lāhem, "so that I may give them what they deserve") — shālam (piel: to repay, to complete — the same root as shālôm). The imprecation: repayment in full to those who violated the covenant of peace.
41.11: בְּזֹאת יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-חָפַצְתָּ בִּי (bĕzōʾt yādaʿtî kî-ḥāpaṣtā bî, "By this I know that you are pleased with me") — ḥāpēṣ: to delight in, to take pleasure in (the covenant-pleasure word of Ps 18:19; 5:4). The sign of divine pleasure: כִּי לֹא-יָרִיעַ אֹיְבִי עָלָי (kî lōʾ-yārîaʿ ʾōyĕbî ʿālay, "my enemy has not triumphed over me") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout in triumph, to raise the victory cry). The enemy's silence is read as the sign of God's favorable attention.
41.12: וַאֲנִי בְּתֻמִּי תָּמַכְתָּ בִּי (waʾănî bĕtummî tāmaktā bî, "As for me, you hold me up in my integrity") — tōm: integrity, completeness, blamelessness (from tāmam: to be complete — the root of tāmîm). Tāmak: to hold, to support. וַתַּצִּיבֵנִי לְפָנֶיךָ לְעוֹלָם (wattaṣṣîbênî lĕpānêkā lĕʿôlām, "and place me before your face forever") — yāṣab (hiphil: to set upright, to station) — permanently positioned before the divine face, the place of covenant nearness and honor.
41.13: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵהָעוֹלָם וְעַד הָעוֹלָם (bārûk YHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾêl mêhāʿôlām wĕʿad hāʿôlām, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting") — this verse is the closing doxology of Book I of the Psalter (Ps 1-41), not part of Psalm 41 itself. Each of the five books of the Psalter closes with a similar doxological formula (see also Ps 72:18-19; 89:52; 106:48; 150:6). Mêhāʿôlām wĕʿad hāʿôlām: "from the age/hidden-one to the age/hidden-one" — the vast expanse of time in both directions, enclosing all temporal existence. אָמֵן וְאָמֵן (ʾāmên wĕʾāmên, "Amen and Amen") — the double liturgical affirmation: ʾāmên (truly so, let it stand firm — from ʾāman: to be firm, reliable, faithful). The congregation's ratification of the doxology.

42.1: כְּאַיָּל תַּעֲרֹג עַל-אֲפִיקֵי-מָיִם (kĕʾayyāl taʿărōg ʿal-ʾăpîqê-māyim, "As a deer longs for flowing streams") — ʾayyāl: a male deer/stag (the feminine ʾayyālāh also possible; some texts have the feminine panting; both genders used). ʿĀrag: to long for, to pant (possibly an onomatopoeia of the panting breath; used only here and Joel 1:20 of animals). ʾĂpîqîm: river-channels, watercourses (the irrigation channels of Ps 1:3). כֵּן נַפְשִׁי תַעֲרֹג אֵלֶיךָ אֱלֹהִים (kên napshî taʿărōg ʾêlêkā ʾĕlōhîm, "so my soul longs for you, O God") — the simile equates nāpesh (the animating self, the whole person) with the thirsting deer. The longing is whole-person, not merely intellectual or emotional.
42.2: צָמְאָה נַפְשִׁי לֵאלֹהִים לְאֵל חָי (ṣāmĕʾāh napshî lêʾlōhîm lĕʾêl ḥāy, "My soul thirsts for God, for the living God") — ṣāmēʾ: to thirst (the physical, urgent thirst of dehydration). ʾÊl ḥāy: the living God — the one who is characterized by life (ḥayyîm), in contrast to the idols who are dead (mêtîm). John 7:37-38 echoes this thirst-language: "If anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink." מָתַי אָבוֹא וְאֵרָאֶה פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (mātay ʾābôʾ wĕʾêrāʾeh pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm, "When will I come and stand in God's presence?") — literally "see the face of God" — the pilgrim desire to appear before God in the sanctuary (Exod 23:17). The exile from the temple is the exile from the face of God.
42.3: הָיְתָה-לִּי דִמְעָתִי לֶחֶם יוֹמָם וָלָיְלָה (hāyĕtāh-llî dimʿātî leḥem yômām wālaylāh, "My tears have been my food day and night") — dimʿāh: tears (tear-drops); leḥem: bread, food. The tears replace the bread — grief replaces nourishment. The inversion of the hunger-satisfaction cycle: instead of eating to live, one weeps without end. בֶּאֱמֹר אֵלַי כָּל-הַיּוֹם אַיֵּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (beʾĕmōr ʾêlay kol-hayyôm ʾayyêh ʾĕlōhêkā, "while people keep saying to me all day long, 'Where is your God?'") — the repeated taunt (ʾayyêh: where?): the enemies interpret the Psalmist's suffering as proof of divine absence or divine abandonment. This is the same taunt in Ps 79:10 and Ps 115:2.
42.4: אֵלֶּה אֶזְכְּרָה וְאֶשְׁפְּכָה עָלַי נַפְשִׁי (ʾêlleh ʾezkĕrāh wĕʾeshpĕkāh ʿālay napshî, "These things I remember as I pour out my soul") — shāpak: to pour out (as water is poured from a vessel). The nāpesh poured out — the whole self emptied in grief (as in Ps 62:8: "pour out your hearts to him"; and Lam 2:19). כִּי אֶעֱבֹר בַּסָּךְ (kî ʾeʿĕbōr bassāk, "how I used to walk with the crowd, leading them in procession") — sāk: the sheltering canopy of the festival crowd, the festal procession with its protective multitude. The memory of leading the pilgrimage procession to the house of God, now inaccessible.
42.5: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי (māh-tishtôḥăḥî napshî, "Why are you downcast, O my soul?") — the refrain (also v11; 43:5): the Psalmist addresses himself in the second person — a kind of inner dialogue or self-counsel. Shāḥāh (hithpolel: to bow down, to be bent over, to be downcast) — the soul bowed under the weight of grief. וּמַה-תֶּהֱמִי עָלָי (ûmāh-tehĕmî ʿālay, "Why so disturbed within me?") — hāmāh: to roar, to make a loud sound, to be in tumult — the inner noise of distress. יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm, "Put your hope in God") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation. The self-counsel: direct the tumult toward God rather than turning inward.
42.6: עָלַי נַפְשִׁי תִשְׁתּוֹחָח (ʿālay napshî tishtôḥāḥ, "My soul is cast down within me") — resuming the self-address: the soul that was told to hope (v5) is still downcast. עַל-כֵּן אֶזְכָּרְךָ מֵאֶרֶץ יַרְדֵּן וְחֶרְמוֹנִים (ʿal-kên ʾezkārĕkā mêʾereṣ yardên wĕḥermônîm, "therefore I remember you from the land of Jordan, the heights of Hermon") — the physical distance from Jerusalem specified: the Jordan region and Hermon (the north) — far from the temple, recalling God from a distance. Memory as the substitute for physical presence when access is denied.
42.7: תְּהוֹם-אֶל-תְּהוֹם קוֹרֵא (tĕhôm-ʾel-tĕhôm qôrêʾ, "Deep calls to deep") — tĕhôm: the primordial deep (Gen 1:2: "the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the tĕhôm"). The deep calls to the deep — disaster summons disaster in a chain reaction of overwhelming force. לְקוֹל צִנּוֹרֶיךָ (lĕqôl ṣinnôrêkā, "at the roar of your waterfalls") — ṣinnôr: a waterspout, a channel (possibly the torrential channels through which floodwaters pour). כָּל-מִשְׁבָּרֶיךָ וְגַלֶּיךָ עָלַי עָבָרוּ (kol-mishbārêkā wĕgallêkā ʿālay ʿābārû, "all your waves and breakers have rolled over me") — mishbār: a breaker (where waves break on rocks); gal: a wave. Both owned by "you" — these are God's waves, not random chaos. The same language Jonah uses in his prayer from the fish's belly (Jon 2:3).
42.8: יוֹמָם יְצַוֶּה יְהוָה חַסְדּוֹ (yômām yĕṣawweh YHWH ḥasdô, "By day the LORD directs his steadfast love") — the counter-movement within the flood: while the depths overwhelm, by day the LORD commands (ṣāwāh: to command, to issue an order) his ḥesed (covenant loyalty). וּבַלַּיְלָה שִׁירֹה עִמִּי (ûbalaylāh shîrô ʿimmî, "at night his song is with me") — even through the night of suffering, the divine song (shîr) accompanies. תְּפִלָּה לְאֵל חַיַּי (tĕpillāh lĕʾêl ḥayyāy, "a prayer to the God of my life") — ʾêl ḥayyāy: the God of my life/lives — the same epithet as v2 (ʾêl ḥāy), now possessively attached: "my" life-God.
42.9: אוֹמְרָה לְאֵל סַלְעִי לָמָה שְׁכַחְתָּנִי (ʾômĕrāh lĕʾêl salʿî lāmāh shĕkahttānî, "I say to God my rock, 'Why have you forgotten me?'") — selaʿ: the rock/cliff-face (as in Ps 18:2; 31:3). לָמָּה-קֹדֵר אֵלֵךְ (lāmmāh-qōdêr ʾêlêk, "Why do I walk in mourning?") — qādar: to be dark, to mourn (from the darkened appearance of the mourner's face). בְּלַחַץ אוֹיֵב (bĕlaḥaṣ ʾôyêb, "under the oppression of the enemy") — laḥaṣ: pressure, oppression (from lāḥaṣ: to press, to squeeze). The enemy as a force that presses and constricts.
42.10: בְּרֶצַח בְּעַצְמוֹתַי חֵרְפוּנִי (bĕreṣaḥ bĕʿaṣmôtay ḥêrĕpûnî, "As if a sword were cutting through my bones, my enemies taunt me") — reṣaḥ: a killing blow, a shattering stroke (from rāṣaḥ: to murder, to slay). The taunt is experienced as a physical blow to the skeleton. Ḥārap: to taunt, to reproach, to shame (the same word as ḥerpāh in Ps 31:11). בְּאָמְרָם אֵלַי כָּל-הַיּוֹם אַיֵּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ — the same taunt as v3: "Where is your God?" — the repetition showing the relentless nature of the social assault.
42.11: The refrain for the second time: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי — "Why are you downcast, O my soul?" The repetition of the identical refrain (also in 43:5) argues strongly that Psalms 42 and 43 are one literary unit. The refrain is not despair — it is the Psalmist's deliberate self-interruption, refusing to let grief have the final word: יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים כִּי-עוֹד אוֹדֶנּוּ יְשׁוּעֹת פָּנָיו (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm kî-ʿôd ʾôdennû yĕshûʿōt pānāyw, "Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him — my Savior and my God") — yĕshûʿōt pānāyw: "salvations of his face" — the face as the source of saving presence.

43.1: Psalm 43 has no superscription — unlike every other psalm in Book II — confirming that Psalms 42-43 form a single composition. שָׁפְטֵנִי אֱלֹהִים וְרִיבָה רִיבִי (shāpĕṭênî ʾĕlōhîm wĕrîbāh rîbî, "Vindicate me, O God, and plead my cause") — shāpaṭ: to judge, to vindicate (in a legal dispute, the judge who decides in one's favor vindicates); rîb: a legal dispute, a lawsuit. Rîbāh rîbî: "plead my dispute/case" — the Psalmist brings God to court as his advocate against the hostile nation. מֵאִישׁ-מִרְמָה וְעַוְלָה פַלְּטֵנִי (mêʾîsh-mirmāh wĕʿawlāh pallĕṭênî, "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me") — mirmāh: deceit, treachery; ʿawlāh: injustice, perversity — the twin character of the enemy.
43.2: כִּי-אַתָּה אֱלֹהֵי מָעוּזִּי (kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhê māʿûzzî, "For you are the God who is my stronghold") — māʿôz: fortress, stronghold (as in Ps 28:8). לָמָה זְנַחְתָּנִי (lāmāh zĕnaḥtānî, "why have you rejected me?") — zānaḥ: to reject, to cast away (the word for covenant rejection — Jer 7:29; Ps 44:9). The paradox: the God who is the stronghold has also apparently rejected. לָמָּה-קֹדֵר אֶתְהַלֵּךְ (lāmmāh-qōdêr ʾethalêk, "Why do I go about mourning?") — the same qādar (to be dark, to go about in mourning) as Ps 42:9 — the two psalms share vocabulary as well as refrain.
43.3: שְׁלַח-אוֹרְךָ וַאֲמִתְּךָ (shĕlaḥ-ʾôrĕkā waʾămittĕkā, "Send out your light and your truth") — the two divine guides personified: ʾôr (light) and ʾĕmet (truth/faithfulness) sent like advance envoys to lead the Psalmist back to God's holy mountain. Light and truth as the conditions for the journey to divine presence. יְנַחוּנִי (yĕnaḥûnî, "let them lead me") — nāḥāh: to guide, to lead (the shepherd-guide verb of Ps 31:3). The two divine attributes personified as guides — prefiguring the Johannine language of the Spirit of truth who leads into all truth (John 16:13).
43.4: וְאָבוֹאָה אֶל-מִזְבַּח אֱלֹהִים (wĕʾābôʾāh ʾel-mizbah ʾĕlōhîm, "Then I will approach the altar of God") — the mizbêaḥ (altar): the place of sacrifice and access, the point where the human and divine meet. אֶל-אֵל שִׂמְחַת גִּילִי (ʾel-ʾêl śimḥat gîlî, "to God who is my greatest joy") — śimḥat gîlî: "the joy of my exultation/dancing" — gîl (the physical, dancing joy) combined with śimḥāh (rejoicing). God himself is the content of the joy, not merely its source. וְאוֹדְךָ בְכִנּוֹר אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהָי (wĕʾôdĕkā bĕkinnôr ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhāy, "I will praise you with the harp, O God my God") — the vow of praise with the kinnôr (lyre/harp — David's instrument) once the pilgrimage is restored.
43.5: The third and final occurrence of the refrain: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי — "Why are you downcast, O my soul?" — closing the literary unit of Psalms 42-43 with the identical words of 42:5 and 42:11. The three-part refrain structures the whole composition as a progressive self-counsel: each recurrence after a descent into lament is the act of self-interruption — the Psalmist refuses to end on despair. The final refrain has the same hope-turn as the previous two: יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm, "Put your hope in God") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation. The last word is not despair but directed hope.

44.1: Psalm 44 is the first of the communal laments — the "we" of the nation rather than the "I" of the individual. אֱלֹהִים בְּאָזְנֵינוּ שָׁמַעְנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm bĕʾāznênû shāmaʿnû, "O God, we have heard with our own ears") — bĕʾāznênû: "with our ears" — the auditory reception of tradition. אֲבוֹתֵינוּ סִפְּרוּ-לָנוּ (ʾăbôtênû sippĕrû-lānû, "our ancestors have told us what you accomplished") — sāpar (piel: to recount, to narrate — the word behind mizmôr and the ongoing declaration of Ps 19:1). The historical tradition as oral narrative passed from generation to generation.
44.2: אַתָּה יָדְךָ גּוֹיִם הוֹרַשְׁתָּ (ʾattāh yādĕkā gôyim hôrashtā, "With your own hand you drove out the nations") — yārash (hiphil: to dispossess, to drive out): the Conquest vocabulary. וַתִּטָּעֵם (wattiṭṭāʿêm, "and planted our ancestors") — nāṭaʿ: to plant (as trees are planted) — the ancestors as a planted community in their land. תָּרַע לְאֻמִּים וַתְּשַׁלְּחֵם (tārǎʿ lĕʾummîm wattĕshallĕḥêm, "you crushed the peoples and expelled them") — the two-stage action: crush (rāʿaʿ: to shatter, to break in pieces) then expel (shālaḥ: to send away, to release).
44.3: כִּי לֹא בְחַרְבָּם יָרְשׁוּ אָרֶץ (kî lōʾ bĕḥarbām yārĕshû ʾāreṣ, "For they did not win the land by their own sword") — the theological interpretation of the Conquest: not by their own military prowess. וּזְרוֹעָם לֹא-הוֹשִׁיעָה לָּמוֹ (ûzĕrôʿām lōʾ-hôshîʿāh llāmô, "and their own arm did not deliver them") — zĕrôaʿ: arm (the symbol of human strength and military power). כִּי-יְמִינְךָ וּזְרוֹעֲךָ (kî-yĕmînĕkā ûzĕrôʿĕkā, "it was your right hand and your arm") — the same word zĕrôaʿ (arm) now applied to God: the divine arm accomplished what the human arm could not. וְאוֹר פָּנֶיךָ כִּי רְצִיתָם (wĕʾôr pānêkā kî rĕṣîtām, "and the light of your face, because you were favorable toward them") — rāṣāh: to be favorable, to take pleasure in — the divine pleasure as the ultimate reason for the conquest.
44.4: אַתָּה-הוּא מַלְכִּי אֱלֹהִים (ʾattāh-hûʾ malkî ʾĕlōhîm, "You are my King, O God") — the shift from the communal recitation to the first-person singular: the individual or royal voice within the communal psalm. צַוֵּה יְשׁוּעוֹת יַעֲקֹב (ṣawwêh yĕshûʿôt yaʿăqōb, "decree victories for Jacob") — ṣāwāh (piel: to command, to decree): the divine word of command that actualizes salvation. Yĕshûʿôt (plural: salvations — multiple acts of rescue).
44.5: בְּךָ צָרֵינוּ נְנַגֵּחַ (bĕkā ṣārênû nĕnaggêaḥ, "Through you we push back our enemies") — nāgaḥ: to gore, to push (as a bull uses its horns). בְּשִׁמְךָ נָבוּס קָמֵינוּ (bĕshimkā nābûs qāmênû, "in your name we trample those who rise against us") — bûs: to trample with the feet. Both verbs are animal-strength images — the horn-push and the hoof-trample — applied to the community's combat, but "through you" and "in your name" locate the power in the divine name, not in the community's own strength.
44.6: כִּי לֹא בְקַשְׁתִּי אֶבְטָח (kî lōʾ bĕqashtî ʾebṭāḥ, "For I do not trust in my bow") — qeshet: a bow — the primary long-range weapon. Bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on (the recurring trust-verb). The explicit renunciation of military self-reliance — the bow as the symbol of personal martial capacity.
44.7: כִּי הוֹשַׁעְתָּנוּ מִצָּרֵינוּ (kî hôshaʿtānû miṣṣārênû, "But you have saved us from our enemies") — yāshaʿ (hiphil: to cause to be saved, to rescue): God as the active saver — the hiphil causative shows God bringing salvation into being, not merely permitting it. וּמְשַׂנְאֵינוּ הֱבִישׁוֹתָ (ûmĕśanĕʾênû hĕbîshôtā, "and disgraced those who hate us") — bûsh (hiphil: to cause to be ashamed): God shaming the enemies — the reversal of the taunt.
44.8: בֵּאלֹהִים הִלַּלְנוּ כָל-הַיּוֹם (bêʾlōhîm hillalnû kol-hayyôm, "In God we have boasted all day long") — hālal (piel: to praise, to boast about — the same root as hallĕlûyāh). The community's continuous boasting is entirely "in God" (bêʾlōhîm) — not in their own history or identity. Selāh marks the transition to the lament that follows.
44.9: אַף-זָנַחְתָּ וַתַּכְלִימֵנוּ (ʾap-zānaḥtā wattaklîmênû, "But you have cast us off and brought us to disgrace") — the shocking reversal: the same God who saved (v7) has now rejected (zānaḥ: to cast away — the covenant-rejection word). Kālam (hiphil: to bring to shame, to disgrace): public humiliation. וְלֹא-תֵצֵא בְּצִבְאֹתֵינוּ (wĕlōʾ-têṣêʾ bĕṣibʾôtênû, "and have not gone out with our armies") — the divine warrior who fought with Israel (v3) is no longer accompanying the armies. The divine desertion of the military host.
44.10: תְּשִׁיבֵנוּ אָחוֹר מִנִּי-צָר (tĕshîbênû ʾāḥôr minnî-ṣār, "You made us retreat before the enemy") — shûb (hiphil: to cause to turn back): not voluntary retreat but forced turning. Ṣār: the adversary (the same ṣar root as distress — the enemy as the tightening force). וּמְשַׂנְאֵינוּ שָׁסוּ לָמוֹ (ûmĕśanĕʾênû shāsû lāmô, "and our enemies plundered us") — shāsāh: to plunder, to despoil — taking what belongs to the defeated.
44.11: תִּתְּנֵנוּ כְּצֹאן מַאֲכָל (tittĕnênû kĕṣōʾn maʾăkāl, "You handed us over like sheep for slaughter") — ṣōʾn: the flock; maʾăkāl: food — the sheep as food for those who devour them. The passive submission of the defenseless flock. This verse anticipates Ps 44:22, which Paul quotes directly. וּבַגּוֹיִם זֵרִיתָנוּ (ûbaggôyim zêrîtānû, "and scattered us among the nations") — zārāh: to scatter, to winnow (as grain is scattered to the winds): the diaspora-scattering as divine action.
44.12: תִּמְכֹּר-עַמְּךָ בְלֹא-הוֹן (timkōr-ʿammĕkā bĕlōʾ-hôn, "You sold your people for a pittance") — mākar: to sell — the commercial vocabulary applied shockingly to God's people. Hôn: wealth, something of value. "Without wealth" — sold for nothing, the sale making no profit. The degradation is total: not even a fair price for the defeat.
44.13: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ חֶרְפָּה לִשְׁכֵנֵינוּ (tĕśîmênû ḥerpāh lishkênênû, "You have made us the object of mockery to our neighbors") — ḥerpāh: reproach, scorn (the same word as Ps 31:11 — the individual's shame now the community's). לַעַג וָקֶלֶס לִסְבִיבוֹתֵינוּ (laʿag wāqeles lisĕbîbôtênû, "a laughingstock and a source of scorn to those around us") — laʿag: mockery, derision; qeles: scorn, ridicule (only here and Ezek 36:4 — rare word). The community encircled by the laughter of their neighbors.
44.14: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ מָשָׁל בַּגּוֹיִם (tĕśîmênû māshāl baggôyim, "You have made us a byword among the nations") — māshāl: a proverb, a byword (to "become a māshāl" means to be a cautionary tale — Deut 28:37 threatens this as a covenant curse). מְנוֹד-רֹאשׁ בַּלְאֻמִּים (mĕnôd-rōʾsh ballĕʾummîm, "a wagging of heads among the peoples") — the body-language of contempt: the head-wagging that signals disdain or pitying dismissal (Jer 18:16; Lam 2:15).
44.15: כָּל-הַיּוֹם כְּלִמָּתִי נֶגְדִּי (kol-hayyôm kĕlimmātî negdî, "All day long my disgrace is before me") — kĕlimmāh: disgrace, shame — related to kālam (to be disgraced, v9). וּבֹשֶׁת פָּנַי כִּסָּתְנִי (ûbōshet pānay kissātĕnî, "and shame has covered my face") — bōshet: shame; kāsāh: to cover (the same verb as the "covered sin" of Ps 32:1, but now shame rather than sin is what covers). The face as the site of honor/shame — covered with shame rather than shining with the divine face-light.
44.16: מִקּוֹל מְחָרֵף וּמְגַדֵּף (miqqôl mĕḥārêp ûmĕgaddêp, "On account of the taunting voice of the one who reviles and blasphemes") — ḥārap (piel: to taunt, to revile); gādap (piel: to blaspheme, to revile — particularly of speaking against God). The voices of the enemies who simultaneously mock the people and blaspheme God. מִפְּנֵי אוֹיֵב וּמִתְנַקֵּם (mippĕnê ʾôyêb ûmitnakkêm, "because of the enemy who seeks revenge") — nāqam (hithpael: to avenge oneself, to seek revenge): the vindictive purpose behind the attack.
44.17: כָּל-זֹאת בָּאַתְנוּ וְלֹא שְׁכַחֲנוּךָ (kol-zōʾt bāʾatnû wĕlōʾ shĕkaḥănûkā, "All this has come upon us, yet we have not forgotten you") — the central theological claim of the psalm: the suffering is not the result of covenant infidelity (contrast Deut 28 where disaster follows apostasy). Shākaḥ: to forget — the covenant violation of forgetting God. The community denies this. וְלֹא-שִׁקַּרְנוּ בִּבְרִיתֶךָ (wĕlōʾ-shiqarnû bibrîtekā, "and we have not been unfaithful to your covenant") — shāqar: to be false, to deal falsely, to lie — the covenant (the bĕrît) not violated. This is the psalm of innocent suffering — the lament that cannot explain its pain by appeal to guilt.
44.18: לֹא-נָסוֹג אָחוֹר לִבֵּנוּ (lōʾ-nāsôg ʾāḥôr libbênû, "Our heart has not turned away") — nāsag: to turn back, to retreat (the same verb as "retreat" in v10, now applied to the heart rather than the army — the inward loyalty contrasted with the external military defeat). וַתֵּט אֲשֻׁרֵינוּ מִנִּי-אָרְחֶךָ (wattêṭ ʾăshurênû minnî-ʾārĕḥekā, "and our steps have not strayed from your path") — nāṭāh: to deviate, to turn aside. The path (ʾōraḥ) as the way of covenantal life — not abandoned.
44.19: כִּי דִכִּיתָנוּ בִּמְקוֹם תַּנִּים (kî dikkîtānû bimqôm tannîm, "Yet you have crushed us in the haunt of jackals") — dākāh (piel: to crush, to shatter — as the bones are crushed). Tannîm: jackals or sea-monsters (the same word appears as both — here contextually the jackals of the wilderness, the animals that inhabit desolate, destroyed places). וַתְּכַס עָלֵינוּ בְּצַלְמָוֶת (wattĕkas ʿālênû bĕṣalmāwet, "and covered us with the shadow of death") — ṣalmāwet: deep darkness, shadow of death (Ps 23:4 — the valley of ṣalmāwet). The total encirclement by lethal darkness.
44.20: אִם-שָׁכַחְנוּ שֵׁם אֱלֹהֵינוּ (ʾim-shākaḥnû shêm ʾĕlōhênû, "If we had forgotten the name of our God") — the conditional self-examination: "if we had done X..." — the ʾim (if) is an oath-structure in Hebrew: "if we had forgotten… [then let the punishment stand, but we haven't]." וַנִּפְרֹשׂ כַּפֵּינוּ לְאֵל זָר (wanniprōś kappênû lĕʾêl zār, "or spread our hands in prayer toward a foreign god") — pāraś kappayim: to spread/extend the palms in prayer (the upward gesture of supplication). ʾÊl zār: a strange/foreign god.
44.21: הֲלֹא אֱלֹהִים יַחֲקָר-זֹאת (hălōʾ ʾĕlōhîm yaḥăqār-zōʾt, "Would God not discover this?") — ḥāqar: to search out, to examine thoroughly — the divine forensic examination that reveals everything hidden. כִּי-הוּא יֹדֵעַ תַּעֲלֻמוֹת לֵב (kî-hûʾ yōdêaʿ taʿălumôt lêb, "For he knows the hidden things of the heart") — taʿălumôt: hidden things, secrets (from ʿālam: to conceal). God knows what is concealed even from human self-examination — the argument is: if we had sinned in secret, God would know. The fact of suffering does not imply the fact of hidden sin.
44.22: כִּי-עָלֶיךָ הֹרַגְנוּ כָל-הַיּוֹם (kî-ʿālêkā hōragnû kol-hayyôm, "Yet for your sake we face death all day long") — the key theological claim: the suffering is "for your sake" (ʿālêkā) — not because of unfaithfulness but because of faithfulness. נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ כְּצֹאן טִבְחָה (neḥshābnû kĕṣōʾn ṭibḥāh, "we are counted as sheep destined for slaughter") — ḥāshab: to be reckoned, to be counted (the accounting root of Ps 32:2) — reckoned as slaughter-sheep. Paul quotes this verse verbatim in Romans 8:36 to describe the condition of believers facing persecution, then immediately follows with: "No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us." The psalm's suffering-for-faithfulness becomes the NT's theology of cross-bearing discipleship.
44.23: עוּרָה לָמָּה תִישַׁן אֲדֹנָי (ʿûrāh lāmmāh tîshān ʾădōnāy, "Wake up! Why are you sleeping, O Lord?") — ʿûr (qal imperative: to awake, to rouse oneself). The remarkable anthropomorphism: the LORD asleep — not a theological claim that God sleeps (Ps 121:4 explicitly denies this: "He who watches over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep") but the experiential language of a community in extremis. The prayer uses the only vocabulary available to describe the feeling of divine inaction. הָקִיצָה אַל-תִּזְנַח לָנֶצַח (hāqîṣāh ʾal-tiznaḥ lāneṣaḥ, "Rise up! Do not reject us forever!") — qîṣ: to awake; zānaḥ: to reject (as in v9). The fear that the rejection might be permanent (lāneṣaḥ: forever).
44.24: לָמָּה-פָנֶיךָ תַסְתִּיר (lāmmāh-pānêkā tastîr, "Why do you hide your face?") — hester pānim (hiding of the face — as in Ps 30:7; 31:17): the divine concealment experienced as absence. תִּשְׁכַּח עָנְיֵנוּ וְלַחֲצֵנוּ (tishkaḥ ʿānyênû wĕlaḥăṣênû, "Why do you ignore our affliction and our oppression?") — ʿonyī: affliction, poverty, humiliation (from ʿānāh: to be bent/bowed down); laḥas: oppression (as in Ps 42:9). Both words describe the crushing of a people under superior force.
44.25: כִּי שָׁחָה לֶעָפָר נַפְשֵׁנוּ (kî shāḥāh leʿāpār napshênû, "For our soul is brought down to the dust") — shāḥāh: to bow, to sink down (the same root as tishtôḥăḥî in Ps 42:5 — the downcast soul); ʿāpār: dust (as in Ps 30:9 — the dust of death). דָּבְקָה לָאָרֶץ בִּטְנֵנוּ (dābĕqāh lāʾāreṣ biṭnênû, "our body clings to the ground") — dābaq: to cling, to stick fast — the intimate bodily contact with the ground in utter prostration.
44.26: קוּמָה עֶזְרָתָה לָּנוּ (qûmāh ʿezrātāh llānû, "Rise up and help us!") — qûm (imperative: rise!) — the same imperative used for divine waking in v23 but now with the purpose of help (ʿezer). וּפְדֵנוּ לְמַעַן חַסְדֶּךָ (ûpĕdênû lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā, "Deliver us for the sake of your steadfast love") — pādāh: to redeem, to ransom (as in Ps 31:5). Lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā: "for the sake of your covenant-loyalty" — the final appeal grounds the prayer not in the community's merit (which has been affirmed) but in God's own covenant character. The psalm ends not with self-justification but with appeal to divine ḥesed.

45.1: רָחַשׁ לִבִּי דָּבָר טוֹב (rāḥash libbî dābār ṭôb, "My heart is overflowing with a beautiful theme") — rāḥash: a hapax legomenon (appears only here) — to bubble up, to overflow, to boil over (like a pot). The heart as the vessel from which the beautiful word pours. אֹמֵר אָנִי מַעֲשַׂי לְמֶלֶךְ (ʾōmêr ʾānî maʿăśay lĕmelek, "I address my poem to the king") — a wedding ode for the king; maʿăśay: my work(s), my compositions. לְשׁוֹנִי עֵט סֹפֵר מָהִיר (lĕshônî ʿêṭ sōpêr māhîr, "my tongue is the pen of a skilled scribe") — the tongue as a writing instrument, the poet as a skilled copyist (māhîr: swift, expert). The superscription: "A Song of Loves" (shîr yĕdîdōt) — yĕdîdāh: beloved, darling (the same root as yĕdîdîm, the covenant love-name).
45.2: יָפְיָפִיתָ מִבְּנֵי אָדָם (yāpyāpîtā mibbĕnê ʾādām, "You are more handsome than any other man") — yāpāh (doubled for intensity): the most beautiful among Adam's sons. הוּצַק חֵן בְּשִׂפְתוֹתֶיךָ (hûṣaq ḥên bĕśiptôtêkā, "grace is poured into your lips") — yāṣaq (hophal: to be poured out): grace (ḥên) as liquid poured into the king's speech. עַל-כֵּן בֵּרַכְךָ אֱלֹהִים לְעוֹלָם (ʿal-kên bêrakĕkā ʾĕlōhîm lĕʿôlām, "therefore God has blessed you forever") — the beauty and gracious speech are not independent gifts but expressions of the divine blessing of the anointed king.
45.3: חֲגוֹר-חַרְבְּךָ עַל-יָרֵךְ גִּבּוֹר (ḥăgôr-ḥarbĕkā ʿal-yārêk gibbôr, "Strap your sword on your thigh, O champion") — ḥāgar: to gird, to belt; gibbôr: the mighty warrior-hero. הוֹדְךָ וַהֲדָרֶךָ (hôdĕkā wahădārekā, "your glory and splendor") — hôd and hādār: the divine glory-vocabulary of Ps 21:5, now applied to the warrior-king. The warrior's military accoutrements are described in the language of divine splendor — the king as the earthly bearer of divine glory.
45.4: וַהֲדָרְךָ צְלַח רְכַב (wahădārkā ṣĕlaḥ rĕkab, "Ride out victoriously in your splendor") — ṣālaḥ: to prosper, to succeed (the cavalry image). עַל-דְּבַר-אֱמֶת וְעַנְוָה-צֶדֶק (ʿal-dĕbar-ʾĕmet wĕʿanwāh-ṣedeq, "for the sake of truth, humility, and righteousness") — the three foundations of the ideal kingship: ʾĕmet (truth/faithfulness), ʿanwāh (meekness/humility — the same word used of Moses in Num 12:3 and of the meek who inherit the earth in Matt 5:5), and ṣedeq (righteousness). וְתוֹרְךָ נוֹרָאוֹת יְמִינֶךָ (wĕtôrĕkā nôrāʾôt yĕmînekā, "let your right hand achieve awesome deeds") — yārah (hiphil: to teach/direct — but here the sense is "to show, to point out"): the right hand teaching awesome things through the deeds of war.
45.5: חִצֶּיךָ שְׁנוּנִים עַמִּים תַּחְתֶּיךָ יִפְּלוּ (ḥiṣṣêkā shĕnûnîm ʿammîm taḥtêkā yippĕlû, "Your arrows are sharp, bringing peoples down beneath you") — shānān: to sharpen (the whetted arrows of the divine warrior, now the king's arrows). Yippĕlû taḥtêkā: "they fall beneath you" — the posture of the defeated before the victor (Ps 18:38-39). בְּלֵב אוֹיְבֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ (bĕlêb ʾôyĕbê hammelek, "they pierce into the hearts of the king's enemies") — the arrows strike to the heart, not merely the body's surface.
45.6: כִּסְאֲךָ אֱלֹהִים עוֹלָם וָעֶד (kisʾăkā ʾĕlōhîm ʿôlām wāʿed, "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever") — one of the most theologically contested verses in the OT. The king is addressed as ʾĕlōhîm (God). Three main readings: (1) "Your throne is God's throne forever" — the ʾĕlōhîm as a genitive of possession; (2) "God is your throne forever" — ʾĕlōhîm as predicate; (3) the Psalmist directly addresses the king as ʾĕlōhîm — applying divine language to the anointed representative.
