The shepherd-flock metaphor (Ps 23) inverted: the shepherd's anger is destroying his own flock.
74.2: zĕkor ʿēdātekhā qānîtā qedem — remember your congregation which you acquired long ago. Qānāh (to acquire, create, possess) — the verb of creation theology (Gen 14:19,22; Prov 8:22); the congregation is God's own created possession. The rhetorical strategy: remind God of what he has already invested. gāʾaltā shēbet naḥălātekhā — which you redeemed as the tribe of your heritage. Gāʾal (kinsman-redemption) — the Exodus is the great redemption that grounds all subsequent appeals for rescue.
74.3: hārîmāh pĕʿāmêkhā lĕmashuʾôt nēṣaḥ — direct your steps to the perpetual ruins. The lament invites God to come and see what has been done to his sanctuary — to walk through the wreckage. kol-hērēaʿ ʾôyēb baqqōdesh — all the evil the enemy has done in the sanctuary. The sanctuary (qōdesh) is the place that belongs to God; its desecration is therefore a direct affront to divine honor, not merely a national disaster.
74.4: shāʾăgû ṣōrĕrêkhā bĕqereb môʿădêkhā — your foes have roared in the midst of your assembly place. The môʿēd (appointed meeting place, the sanctuary where God meets with Israel) is filled with the roar (shāʾag — the lion's roar) of enemies. śāmû ʾôtōtām ʾōtôt — they have set up their own signs as signs. The enemy's military standards (ʾōtôt) replace the divine signs (ʾōtôt) — the same word is used of YHWH's signs in the Exodus (Exod 10:1-2). Replacing divine signs with human signs is the defining sacrilege.
74.5: yiwwādaʿ kĕmēbîʾ lĕmāʿĕlāh — it seemed as if someone was raising axes. A difficult verse; the image is of wood-cutters attacking the carved temple panels — the very skilled labor that built the temple (1 Kgs 6) reversed in destruction. The craftsman who raised his axe to carve is invoked to image the vandal who raises his axe to destroy.
74.6: pittûḥêhā yaḥad bĕkashîl wĕkêlappôt yahaʾlômûn — its carved wood they broke down with hatchets and hammers. Pittûḥîm (carved works — the intricate temple paneling described in 1 Kgs 6:29-36) battered with kêlappôt (hammers, mauls). The detail makes the lament concrete: specific sacred objects destroyed by specific violent tools. The specificity is itself a form of testimony — witness to what was done.
74.7: shillĕḥû bāʾēsh miqdāshêkhā — they set your sanctuary on fire. The burning of the temple (miqdāsh) — the most extreme act of desecration (2 Kgs 25:9). lāʾāreṣ ḥillĕlû mishkan shĕmêkhā — they profaned to the ground the dwelling place of your name. Mishkan shĕmêkhā (dwelling place of your name) — the Deuteronomic theology of the divine name dwelling in the chosen place (Deut 12:5,11) makes this profanation an attack on the divine name itself, not merely on a building.
74.8: ʾāmĕrû bĕlibbām nînām yaḥad — they said in their hearts, 'We will oppress them altogether'. The enemies' interior speech — their deliberate program of total destruction. śārĕpû kol-môʿădê-ʾêl bāʾāreṣ — they burned all the meeting places of God in the land. Môʿădê-ʾêl (meeting places of God) — possibly the regional high places or local sanctuaries, or the proto-synagogues of exilic communities; the burning extends beyond Jerusalem to every place of assembly.
74.9: ʾôtōtênû lōʾ rāʾînû — we do not see our signs. The divine signs (ʾōtôt) — the Exodus miracles and the ongoing prophetic word — are absent. The word plays against v4: the enemy's signs have replaced the divine signs. ʾên-ʿôd nābîʾ — there is no longer a prophet. The cessation of prophetic word is the most devastating element of the lament: without a prophet there is no access to the divine will, no word of interpretation, no promise of duration. wĕlōʾ-ʾittānû yōdēaʿ ʿad-māh — and there is none among us who knows how long. Not even the duration of the silence is known — the silence is total.
74.10: ʿad-māttay ʾĕlōhîm yĕḥārēph ṣār — how long, O God, will the enemy mock? Ḥāraph (to reproach, revile, challenge) — the enemy's taunting is a theological challenge: if God cannot protect his sanctuary and his people, his name is dishonored. yĕnāʾēṣ ʾôyēb shĕmĕkhā lāneṣaḥ — shall the enemy revile your name forever? The divine reputation becomes the argument for divine action — God must act for his own name's sake (cf. Ezek 36:22-23).
74.11: lāmmāh tāshîb yādĕkhā wîmînekhā — why do you hold back your hand, your right hand? The yad yāmîn (right hand) is the hand of divine power — the Exodus weapon-hand (Exod 15:6,12). miqqereb ḥêqĕkhā kallēh — from the midst of your bosom, destroy! God's power is folded in his lap like a concealed weapon — the plea is for him to draw and act. The image pictures divine restraint as physical containment that must be released.
74.12: wĕʾĕlōhîm malkî miqqedem — yet God is my King from of old. The pivot of the lament — the move from complaint to the basis of appeal. The antiquity of God's kingship (miqqedem: from ancient times, from the beginning) provides historical ground for current petition. pōʿēl yĕshûʿôt bĕqereb hāʾāreṣ — working salvation in the midst of the earth. Past salvations are the pattern for future ones; what God has done in history he can do again.
74.13: ʾattāh phōrartā vĕʿozzĕkhā yām — you divided the sea by your might. The Exodus sea-crossing narrated in creation-mythological terms: shabbartā rāʾshê tannînîm ʿal-hammāyim — you shattered the heads of the sea monsters on the waters. Tannîn (sea dragon, chaos monster) echoes Canaanite mythology (Lothan/Leviathan, the serpent with seven heads in Ugaritic texts). YHWH's defeat of the sea monsters is both historical (Exodus) and cosmological (creation ordering). If God defeated the chaos of Egypt and the primordial sea, he can defeat Babylon.
74.14: ʾattāh rāṣaṣtā rāʾshê liwyāthān — you crushed the heads of Leviathan. Liwyāthān (Leviathan — from lāwāh: to coil, twist) is the primordial sea creature of chaos (Job 41; Isa 27:1). The plural heads echo the seven-headed dragon of Ugaritic mythology. tittĕnennû maʾăkhāl lĕʿam lĕṣiyyîm — you gave him as food for the creatures of the wilderness. The chaos monster becomes food — an ironic reversal. YHWH's sovereignty over chaos is absolute: even Leviathan feeds others at his disposal.
74.15: ʾattāh bāqaʿtā maʿyān wānaḥal — you split open springs and wadis. The water-from-the-rock (Exod 17; Num 20) and the Jordan crossing (Josh 3) stand behind this verse — YHWH produces water in the desert and stops water in the river. ʾattāh hôbashtā naharôt ʾêtān — you dried up ever-flowing streams. Nĕharôt ʾêtān (ever-flowing, perennial rivers) — YHWH stops what cannot naturally stop. Both miracles demonstrate mastery over water in its full range of behaviors.
74.16: lĕkhā yôm ʾaf-lĕkhā lāylāh — yours is the day, yours also the night. Creation theology: day and night belong to YHWH — neither is the domain of rival powers or foreign deities. ʾattāh hăkhînôtā māʾôr wāshāmesh — you established the light and the sun. The sun is not a deity (contra Egypt's Ra) but a created instrument under YHWH's control. The creation confession forms the ground for the appeal to the Creator to act on behalf of his creation.
74.17: ʾattāh hiṣṣabtā kol-gebûlôt ʾāreṣ — you established all the boundaries of the earth. Gebûl (boundary, border) — God as the one who sets the limits of nations (cf. Deut 32:8; Acts 17:26). qayiṣ wāḥōrep ʾattāh yĕṣartām — summer and winter, you made them. The seasonal boundaries — another dimension of divine ordering. All of vv13-17 establish the Creator's credentials for the specific petition of vv18-23: the one who ordered creation can restore the sanctuary.
74.18: zĕkhār-zōʾt ʾôyēb ḥērap YHWH — remember this, O LORD, how the enemy has mocked. The appeal shifts from creation theology to divine reputation: the enemy's taunting is directed against YHWH, not merely against Israel. wĕʿam nābāl nēʾăṣû shĕmekā — and a foolish people has despised your name. Nābāl (fool — the one who lives as if God does not exist, cf. Ps 14:1) — the enemy's mockery is practical atheism. What is at stake is the divine name.
74.19: ʾal-tittēn lĕḥayyat nefesh tôrekhā — do not deliver the soul of your dove to the wild beasts. Tôr (turtledove) — a gentle, vulnerable bird used in sacrifice (Lev 1:14; 5:7; Luke 2:24), here as a metaphor for Israel. The turtledove is helpless against predators — the image of Israel's radical vulnerability without divine protection. ḥayyat ʿăniyyêkhā ʾal-tishkāḥ lāneṣaḥ — do not forget the life of your poor forever. The two terms tôrekhā (your dove) and ʿăniyyêkhā (your poor) equate the nation with the poor — those who have nothing but God.
74.20: habbēṭ labberît — have regard for the covenant. The covenant (berît) is the final ground of appeal — not Israel's merit but the divine commitment. kî māleʾû maḥăshakkê-ʾereṣ neʾôt ḥāmās — for the dark places of the land are full of the habitations of violence. Maḥăshakkê-ʾereṣ (the dark places of the land) — the caves and hiding places of the exilic condition; darkness as the condition of the covenant people when the covenant seems forgotten.
74.21: ʾal-yāshob dāk niklām — let not the downtrodden be put to shame. Dāk (crushed, oppressed — from dākhāh: to crush) — those already beaten down must not also face the shame of unanswered prayer. ʾānî wĕʾebyôn yĕhallĕlû shĕmekhā — let the poor and needy praise your name. Their future praise is the purpose of present intervention — the community's worship is contingent on divine rescue. The appeal to God's desire to be praised is itself a motive for action.
74.22: qûmāh ʾĕlōhîm rîbāh rîbekhā — arise, O God, plead your own cause. The repeated root rîb (legal dispute, lawsuit) — both noun and verb: rîbāh rîbekhā (plead your plea, fight your fight). The appeal reverses the ordinary legal frame: God is not merely petitioned to take Israel's side but called to defend his own case against those who dishonor him. God is plaintiff, not merely judge. zĕkhor ḥerpātekhā minnî-nābāl kol-hayyôm — remember how the fool mocks you all day long.
74.23: ʾal-tishkāḥ qôl ṣōrĕrêkhā — do not forget the clamor of your foes. The final appeal: God must not forget the noise of those who oppose him. shaʾăwat qāmêkhā ʿōlāh tāmîd — the tumult of those who rise against you goes up continually. Tāmîd (continually) — the unceasing noise of opposition to God is the psalm's final word, hanging without resolution, awaiting divine response. The lament ends in suspension — a theological posture of waiting that trusts the God of v12 to act.

75.1: hôdînû lĕkhā ʾĕlōhîm hôdînû — we give thanks to you, O God; we give thanks. The doubled hiphil of yādāh (to confess, acknowledge, thank) creates liturgical emphasis — thanks given and given again. wĕqārôb shĕmekā — your name is near. Qārôb (near, close) as a theological declaration: the divine name is not distant or inaccessible but close enough to invoke. The nearness of the name is the reason for thanks, not the occasion for it.
75.2: kî ʾeqqaḥ môʿēd — when I choose the appointed time. The voice shifts from community (v1) to God himself (vv2-5) — a divine speech embedded in the psalm. Môʿēd (appointed time, set season — the same word for the sacred calendar of Lev 23) — God controls when judgment falls. ʾănî mêshārîm ʾeshpōṭ — I will judge with perfect equity. Mêshārîm (uprightnesses, level places — plural abstract noun) — justice rendered with complete, undeflecting fairness across every dimension.
75.3: nĕmōgîm ʾereṣ wĕkhol-yōshĕbêhā — when the earth totters and all its people with it. Māmag (to melt, dissolve, totter) — the earth losing its structural integrity. ʾānōkî tikkantî ʿammûdêhā selāh — it is I who hold its pillars firm. The hiphil of kûn (to establish, fix, make firm) — God maintaining the structural supports of creation against dissolution. ʿAmmûdêhā (its pillars) — the cosmological framework of the earth (cf. 1 Sam 2:8; Ps 104:5). The selah pause marks the weight of the claim: universal chaos does not shake the one who sustains all things.
75.4: ʾāmartî lahalĕlîm ʾal-tāhōlû — I say to the arrogant: Stop your boasting. Hōlēl (arrogant, boastful — from hālal, the root of hallĕlûyāh — praise — turned into its degenerate opposite: vain boasting). wĕlārĕshāʿîm ʾal-tārîmû qāren — and to the wicked: do not raise your horn. The horn (qeren) is the animal's weapon and status symbol — raised high means asserting dominance. The divine speech commands the gesture of self-elevation to stop.
75.5: ʾal-tārîmû lammārôm qarnĕkhem — do not raise your horn on high. The repetition of the horn-command (v4b) intensifies with lammārôm (on high, toward the heights) — not just raised but raised toward heaven — the posture of those who challenge God himself. tĕdabbĕrû bĕṣawwāʾr ʿātāq — do not speak with an arrogant neck. Ṣawwāʾr ʿātāq (arrogant/stiff neck) — ʿātāq (bold, insolent, stiff — used of the neck that refuses to bow) in this construction. Speaking with a stiff neck is the posture of those who will not bow before the divine judge of v2.
75.6: kî lōʾ mimmôṣāʾ ûmimmaʿărāb wĕlōʾ mimmidbar — for advancement comes neither from east nor west, nor from the wilderness of the south. The directional inventory (east, west, wilderness/south) is a merism for everywhere — the negative is absolute: no human quarter, no political alliance, no geographic advantage produces the exaltation the wicked seek by raising their horns. The verse omits the north — traditionally the direction of the divine throne (Isa 14:13) — because v7 supplies the answer: elevation comes from God alone.
75.7: kî-ʾĕlōhîm shōpēṭ — but God is the judge. The participial form shōpēṭ (the one who judges — ongoing, characteristic action, not a one-time event) — judging is what God does by nature. zeh yashpîl wĕzeh yārîm — he brings one person down and raises another up. The hiphil of shāpal (to bring low) and rûm (to raise) — both vertical movements are divine prerogative. The wicked's raised horn (vv4-5) and the righteous person's lifted horn (v10) are explained by this sovereign divine action.
75.8: kî khôs bĕyad-YHWH wĕyayin ḥāmar — for in the hand of the LORD there is a cup of foaming wine. Kôs (cup) as the vessel of divine judgment — a major prophetic motif: Isa 51:17,22; Jer 25:15-17; Ezek 23:31-33; Hab 2:16; Rev 14:10; 16:19. Yāyin ḥāmar (wine that ferments, foams) — spiced wine, full strength. wayyaṣṣēq mizzeh wĕkol-rĕshāʿê ʾāreṣ yimṣûʾ yishĕtû ʾet-shĕmārêhā — he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it to the very dregs. Shĕmārîm (dregs, sediment at the bottom) — the bitterest and most potent part. The wicked must drink every last drop.
75.9: waʾănî ʾaggîd lĕʿôlām — but as for me, I will declare this forever. The voice returns to the psalmist in a vow of perpetual proclamation. The contrast with the wicked's fate (v8) is stark: they drain the cup of wrath; the Psalmist sings forever. ʾăzammĕrāh lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb — I will sing praises to the God of Jacob. ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) — not a generic deity but the God of covenant history, the one who chose the unlikely son over the elder (Gen 25:23), whose faithfulness to Jacob is the basis for faithfulness now.
75.10: wĕkhol-qarnê rĕshāʿîm ʾăgaddēaʿ — all the horns of the wicked I will cut down. Gādaʿ (to cut off, hew down — used of felling trees and vines) — the horn imagery of vv4-5 reaches its resolution. Who speaks? The divine voice of vv2-5 resuming, or the psalmist confidently declaring what God will do? The ambiguity is likely intentional — the psalmist so identifies with the divine purpose that the distinction dissolves. tĕrômnāh qarnôt ṣaddîq — the horns of the righteous shall be lifted high. The contrast is complete: wicked horns cut down, righteous horns raised — the divine inversion of v7 enacted in full.

76.1: nôdāʿ bîhûdāh ʾĕlōhîm — God is known in Judah. The niphal of yādaʿ (to be known, to make oneself known) — the same root as the covenant-knowledge formula. bĕyisrāʾêl gādôl shĕmô — his name is great in Israel. The pairing of Judah and Israel is a merism for the whole people — the divine fame is national. The victory celebrated in vv3-6 is YHWH's public demonstration of this greatness; the hymn opens by naming the result before explaining the cause.
76.2: wayhî bĕshālēm sukkô — his tabernacle is in Salem. Shālēm (Salem) is the ancient name for Jerusalem (Gen 14:18; Heb 7:2) — its connection to shālôm (wholeness, peace) is lexically transparent. Sukkô (his booth/tabernacle — from sukkāh, the temporary dwelling, used of the Feast of Booths) — the warrior-God dwells in the city he defends. ûmĕʿônātô bĕṣiyyôn — his dwelling place is in Zion. Mĕʿônāh (dwelling place, den) — the permanent habitation that makes Salem/Zion the location of divine judgment.
76.3: shāmmāh shibbēr rishpê-qāshet — there he shattered the flashing arrows. Risphê qāshet (flames/sparks of the bow — fiery arrows) — the image combines fire and combat. Shibbar (piel of shābar: to shatter, smash to pieces) — the weapons of war broken at the source, in the presence of God at his sanctuary. māgēn wĕḥereb ûmilḥāmāh selāh — the shield, the sword, and all the weapons of war. The catalog (bow-arrows, shield, sword) covers the full range of ancient warfare — nothing escaped the divine judgment. There (shāmmāh, at Zion) is the location of total disarmament.
76.4: nāʾôr ʾattāh ʾaddîr mêharrê-ṭāref — you are glorious and majestic, more than the everlasting mountains of plunder. Ṭāref (prey, plunder, what a predator tears) — the mountains of plunder are either the lair-mountains of predators or the accumulated trophies of powerful nations. YHWH is more majestic than either. The comparison is deliberately jarring: the greatest thing one can imagine (prey-mountains, the pinnacle of military success) cannot approach the divine splendor.
76.5: ʾeshshôlĕlû ʾabbîrê lēb — the valiant warriors have been stripped of their plunder. ʾAbbîr lēb (mighty of heart — those of formidable courage) — the ironic reversal: those who came to plunder are themselves stripped (shālal). nāmû shĕnātām — they have fallen into the sleep of death. Nûm (to sleep, to slumber) as a euphemism for death — the mighty warriors permanently put to sleep. wĕlōʾ-māṣĕʾû khol-ʾanshê-ḥayil ʾet-yĕdêhem — not one soldier could lift a hand. Even the reflexive act of raising a hand in defense was beyond them — a total incapacity imposed by divine judgment.
76.6: mîgaʿarātĕkhā ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb — at your rebuke, O God of Jacob. Gaʿar (to rebuke, to thunder at — used of the divine rebuking of the sea in Ps 104:7; 106:9; Nah 1:4) — the divine rebuke is the audible power that shatters armies. The same rebuke that divided the sea at the Exodus here destroys the war machine. nirdāam wākkerekeb wāssûs — both chariot and horse fell into a dead sleep. Rādam (to be in a deep stupor, a death-like sleep — the same root as Jonah 1:5's nirdām) — the chariot force cast into helpless unconsciousness. The chariot was ancient warfare's decisive weapon; its defeat signals total military reversal.
76.7: ʾattāh nôrāʾ ʾattāh — but you — you alone are to be feared. The pronoun ʾattāh (you) appears twice for emphasis — an insistent identification of who, exactly, inspires fear. Nôrāʾ (fearful, awesome — niphal participle of yārēʾ) — applied to God at Sinai (Deut 10:17) and to the angel of YHWH. ûmî-yaʿămōd lĕphānêkhā mēʾāz ʾappekā — who can stand before you when your anger is kindled? The rhetorical question expects no answer — the defeated armies of vv5-6 are the empirical demonstration. The question frames divine wrath not as capricious but as a self-evident power before which nothing can stand.
76.8: mishāmayim hishmaʿtā mišpāṭ — from heaven you pronounced judgment. Hishmaʿtā (hiphil of shāmaʿ: you caused to be heard, you announced) — judgment is audible, announced from the heavens and heard on earth. ʾāreṣ yārĕʾāh wĕshāqāṭāh — the earth feared and stood still. Earth personified as a witness to the divine verdict: it heard and froze in awe. The earth's response mirrors the proper human response that the invaders refused to give — they came with horns raised (Ps 75:4-5); the earth itself bowed.
76.9: bĕqûm-lammišpāṭ ʾĕlōhîm — when God arose to judge. The divine arising (qûm) is the standard idiom for God moving from apparent inactivity into decisive intervention (cf. Ps 7:6; 68:1; 74:22). lĕhôshîaʿ kol-ʿanwê-ʾāreṣ selāh — to save all the humble of the earth. The ʿānāw (the humble, the meek, the lowly — those who have bent their necks in contrast to the stiff-necked of Ps 75:5) — those with no human recourse. The selah marks the theological center: God's judgment serves his saving purpose for the vulnerable.
76.10: kî-ḥămat ʾādām tôdekhā — surely human fury will bring you praise. Ḥēmāh (fury, burning anger — the hot rage of the enemy) becomes an occasion for YHWH's praise — the paradox of divine sovereignty transforming human wrath into divine glorification. shĕʾērît ḥēmōt taḥgōr — whatever wrath remains you will restrain. Tāḥgōr (you will gird on, restrain — from ḥāgar: to gird about, as a warrior girds on a weapon) — God wearing the remaining human wrath as a controlled girdle. Both the wrath unleashed and the wrath restrained serve the divine purpose.
76.11: nidrû wĕshallĕmû laYHWH ʾĕlōhêkhem — make vows to the LORD your God and keep them. The victory hymn turns to exhortation: the proper response to witnessed divine power is vow-making and vow-keeping. kol-sĕbîbāyw yōbîlû shay lammôrāʾ — let all the surrounding nations bring tribute to the one who is to be feared. Shay (tribute, gift — a rare term, cf. Isa 18:7; Ps 68:29) — the surrounding nations, seeing the invader's defeat, are called to bring tribute to the mōrāʾ (the one who inspires fear). The national humiliation of the aggressor is the universal invitation.
76.12: yibṣōr rûaḥ nĕgîdîm — he cuts off the spirit of princes. Bāṣar (to cut off, to harvest — used of cutting grapes) — the breath/spirit (rûaḥ) of princes snipped like a cluster. Nāgîd (prince, leader — one set before others) — those in positions of earthly authority, whose authority and breath are equally in God's hands. nôrāʾ lĕmalkê-ʾāreṣ — he is fearsome to the kings of the earth. The psalm closes with nôrāʾ (fearsome) — the same word centered in v7. Kings feared by their subjects are themselves made to fear the God who cuts off the breath of princes.

77.1: qôlî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm wĕʾeṣʿāqāh — I cried out to God with my full voice. The cohortative ʾeṣʿāqāh (I will/let me cry out) — not a quiet prayer but a loud, urgent cry. Ṣāʿaq (to cry out) is the word used of Israel's cry from Egypt (Exod 2:23; 3:7) — the Exodus register is activated from the psalm's opening verse. qôlî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm wĕhaʾăzîn ʾēlāy — I cried to God, and he listened. The hiphil of ʾāzan (to give ear, to listen attentively) — God inclined his ear. The second half of v1 already contains the resolution the body of the psalm struggles to reach.
77.2: bĕyôm ṣārātî ʾădōnāy dārāshtî — in the day of my trouble I sought the Lord. Dārash (to seek, to inquire — the same verb of seeking God at a sanctuary or oracle) — distress drives purposeful seeking. yādî laylāh niggĕrāh wĕlōʾ tāpûg — through the night my hand was outstretched without ceasing. Nāgar (to flow out, be poured out — used of water) — the hand stretched out in prayer, poured out through the night without flagging. māʾănāh hinnāḥēm nafshî — my soul refused to be comforted. The niphal of nāḥam (to be comforted) refused — a deliberate rejection of lesser comforts, holding out for God alone, which paradoxically prolongs the distress.
77.3: ʾezkĕrāh ʾĕlōhîm wĕʾehĕmāyāh — I thought of God and I groaned. The paradox: remembering God at this stage produces groaning rather than comfort (the therapeutic remembering of vv11-20 comes later, after the crisis reaches its lowest point). Hāmāh (to murmur, roar, groan — used of the churning sea and of tumultuous crowds) — the inner noise of unresolved distress. ʾāśîḥāh wĕtiʿaṭṭēph rûḥî selāh — I poured out my grief until my spirit grew faint. ʿāṭaph (hithpael: to wrap oneself up, to grow faint from exhaustion) — the spirit collapsing under unresolved grief.
77.4: ʾāḥaztā shĕmurôt ʿênāy — you kept my eyes wide open through the night. The subject shifts to God: ʾāḥaztā (you held, you grasped — from ʾāḥaz: to seize, take hold) — God himself holding the eyelids open. The insomnia is not merely psychological but divinely caused: God is keeping the Psalmist awake. This makes God the agent of the very suffering the Psalmist brings to him in complaint — the same move as Ps 73:14 (God who afflicts his own). niphʿamtî wĕlōʾ ʾădabbēr — I was too troubled to speak. Pāʿam (to beat, trouble, agitate — used of Pharaoh's troubled spirit, Gen 41:8) — agitated beyond articulation.
77.5: ḥishshabtî yāmîm miqqedem — I think about the days of old. Ḥāshab (to reckon, compute, think deliberately — the accountant's word) — purposeful recollection rather than nostalgic wandering. shĕnôt ʿôlāmîm — the years of long ago. ʿôlām (hidden, of long ago, ancient) — the years whose significance requires excavation, the buried history of divine faithfulness. The movement from personal crisis (vv1-4) toward historical memory (vv5-6, then vv11-20) begins here — the therapeutic arc of the psalm.
77.6: ʾezkĕrāh nĕgînātî ballāylāh — I recall my song in the night. Nĕgînāh (song, melody — from nāgan: to play a stringed instrument) — the Psalmist recalls his former worship song, the night song that once came naturally and now does not. ʿim-lĕbābî ʾāśîḥāh — I ponder in my heart. Śîaḥ (to muse, meditate — the same root as the meditation of Ps 119:15,23) — deliberate inward rumination. wîḥaqqēr rûḥî — my spirit searches deeply. Ḥāqar (to search out, probe — used of thorough legal investigation) — the spirit conducting a thorough examination of its own condition.
77.7: hălĕʿôlāmîm yiznaḥ ʾădōnāy — will the Lord reject us forever? Zānaḥ (to reject, push away — the same verb as Ps 74:1; 44:9) — the communal lament vocabulary applied to personal experience. The series of questions in vv7-9 form a theodicy catechism, each attacking a specific divine attribute: permanence (v7), faithfulness in steadfast love (v8), grace and compassion (v9). wĕlōʾ-yōsîph lirṣôt ʿôd — will he never again be pleased with us? Rāṣāh (to be pleased, accept favorably — the technical word for divine acceptance of a sacrifice, Lev 1:4) — will divine acceptance never return?
77.8: heʾāphes lāneṣaḥ ḥasdô — has his steadfast love ceased once and for all? Ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) — the foundational divine attribute of Exod 34:6-7. Āphēs (to cease, come to an end — used of armies that cease, of hope that ends) — the question is not whether God can cease to love but whether the silence is permanent. gāmar ʾōmer lĕdōr wādōr — has his promise failed for every generation to come? ʾŌmer (word, promise, speech) — the divine word that was the foundation of covenant confidence. Has the word gone silent for all future generations?
77.9: hăshākah ḥannôt ʾêl — has God forgotten to show grace? Ḥānan (to show grace, to be gracious — the divine attribute proclaimed at Sinai, Exod 34:6: ḥannûn wĕraḥûm) — the question strips the attribute from God and holds it up as a challenge. ʾim-qāphāṣ bĕʾap raḥămāyw selāh — has he locked away his compassion in his anger? Qāphaṣ (to shut, close — a rare verb suggesting a snapping shut) — the raḥămîm (compassions/mercies, from reḥem: womb, the maternal depths) clamped shut by anger. The selah marks the nadir of the questioning.
77.10: waʾōmar ḥallôtî hîʾ — then I said: this is the sickness that is killing me. The most exegetically contested verse in the psalm. Ḥallôtî (my sickness, my wounding — from ḥālāh: to be sick, wounded) — the Psalmist names his condition. shĕnôt yĕmîn ʿelyôn — the years of the right hand of the Most High. Two main readings: (1) the right hand of the Most High has changed — the crisis thought that God's saving power has withdrawn; (2) the years of the Most High's right hand are past — the golden years of divine action seem over. Either reading makes v10 the pivot: the crisis-thought about God's apparent withdrawal is immediately followed by I will remember (v11). The Psalmist does not resolve the crisis by argument but by therapeutic memory of the Exodus.
77.11: ʾezkōr maʿallĕlê-YAH — I will call to mind the works of the LORD. Maʿallēl (deed, work — from ʿālal: to act, to deal with) — the specific, concrete acts of God in history. The name YAH (the shortened form of YHWH, found especially in the Psalter and in hallĕlûyāh) — personal, intimate. kî-ʾezkĕrāh miqqedem pilelĕkhā — yes, I will remember your wonders from of old. The cohortative ʾezkĕrāh (I will remember) is a deliberate act of will against the current despair — the Psalmist chooses to move from questioning (vv7-9) to remembering.
77.12: wĕhāgîtî bĕkhol-poʿlekā — I will meditate on all your works. Hāgāh (to murmur, meditate — Ps 1:2's word for sustained Torah meditation) — the same contemplative practice turned from individual crisis toward historical acts of God. ûbĕʿălîlôtekhā ʾāśîḥāh — and think carefully about everything you have done. The ʾāśîḥāh (meditative contemplation — the same word used of distress in v3) is now redirected: the interior groaning becomes productive meditation on redemptive history. Memory is the therapy.
77.13: ʾĕlōhîm baqqōdesh darkekā — O God, your path runs through what is holy. Baqqōdesh darkekā — the divine way is through the holy place; the sanctuary is where God's way is made known. The sanctuary as the place of divine revelation recurs from Ps 73:17 — it is where the eschatological perspective becomes visible, where crisis is met with memory. mî-ʾêl gādôl kĕʾĕlōhîm — what god anywhere is as great as our God? The incomparability formula — no divine competitor approaches YHWH in the acts now about to be rehearsed.
77.14: ʾattāh hāʾêl ʿōśēh pheleʾ — you are the God who performs wonders. Pheleʾ (wonder, extraordinary deed — Exod 15:11 in the Song of the Sea: nôrāʾ tĕhillōt ʿōśēh pheleʾ, awesome in praises, working wonders). The Exodus doxology vocabulary begins consciously here — the Psalmist is entering the Song of the Sea tradition. hôdaʿtā bāʿammîm ʿuzzekā — you have declared your power among the nations. The Exodus was a public, international event — a display before all peoples (cf. Exod 9:16; Josh 2:10), not a private national experience.
77.15: gāʾaltā bizzĕrôaʿ ʿammekā — with your strong arm you redeemed your people. Gāʾal (kinsman-redemption) + zĕrôaʿ (the arm, the outstretched arm) — the full Exodus formula compressed into four Hebrew words. The combination recalls the Deuteronomic summaries (Deut 5:15; 26:8). bĕnê-yaʿăqōb wĕyôsēph selāh — the descendants of Jacob and Joseph. The unusual dual mention of Jacob and Joseph — perhaps including both southern and northern traditions (Joseph = Ephraim/Manasseh), embracing the whole people in the Exodus memory.
77.16: rāʾûkhā mayim ʾĕlōhîm rāʾûkhā mayim yāḥîlû — the waters saw you, O God; the waters saw you and recoiled. Rāʾāh (to see) repeated — the waters as sentient witnesses to the divine presence. Yāḥîlû (they writhed, were in anguish — from ḥûl: to writhe as in labor-pain) — the Red Sea experiencing the anguish of a woman in childbirth at God's approach. ʾaf yirgezu tĕhōmôt — the very depths were shaken. Tĕhôm (the deep, the primordial ocean — Gen 1:2) trembling before the Creator — the Exodus crossing narrated in creation-battle terms.
77.17: zōrĕmû mayim ʿābôt — the clouds poured down rain. Zāram (to pour out in a torrent — violent, sudden rain) — the storm-theophany begins. qôl nātĕnû shĕḥāqîm — the sky resounded. Shĕḥāqîm (the thin upper air — from shāḥaq: to grind fine) — the upper atmosphere resonating with the divine voice. ʾaf-ḥăṣāṣêkhā yithallākû — your arrows flashed out in every direction. Ḥēṣ (arrow) — divine lightning as divine arrows, combining the warrior and storm images in one theophany.
77.18: qôl raʿămĕkhā baggalgal — your thunder rolled through the churning heavens. Galgal (wheel, whirlwind, rolling thing — the wheel-sound of thunder rolling across the vault of heaven). hēʾîrû bĕrāqîm tēbēl — your lightning illuminated the world. Tēbēl (the inhabited earth — the whole world with its peoples) lit up by divine lightning. rāgĕzāh wattiʿash hāʾāreṣ — the earth trembled and quaked. The two earthquake verbs (rāgaz: to quake with agitation; raʿash: to tremble violently) combine Sinai vocabulary (Exod 19:18) with the Exodus crossing into one cosmic event.
77.19: bayyām darkekā ûshbîlekhā bĕmayim rabbîm — your path went through the sea, your way through the great deep. The Exodus crossing narrated as divine theophany — God's way passes through the sea, through the primordial chaos-waters (mayim rabbîm: the great waters, Ps 29:3). wĕʿiqbôtekhā lōʾ nōdāʿû — but your footprints could not be found. ʿĀqēb (heel, footprint, track) — God left no traceable footprint in the sea. The divine transcendence expressed as invisibility: God acts decisively in history, parts the sea, leads the people — and leaves no trace that can be pinned down, analyzed, or reduced to a natural phenomenon. He cannot be controlled.
77.20: nāḥîtā kattsōʾn ʿammekā — you guided your people like a flock. Nāḥāh (to lead, guide — specifically of divine leading in the wilderness, Exod 13:21; 15:13; Deut 32:12) + ṣōʾn (flock) — the shepherd image closing the Exodus retrospective. bĕyad-mōsheh wĕʾahărōn — through the leadership of Moses and Aaron. The psalm ends not with triumphant theophany but with human instruments of divine guidance. The personal crisis of vv1-9 is answered not with a direct divine address but with the reminder that God has always led through human intermediaries. The Psalmist stands in that tradition — the God who led then continues to lead now.

68.1: The superscription assigns Psalm 68 to David as both a psalm (mizmôr) and a song (shîr). The opening words are a direct quotation of the ark-setting-out formula from Num 10:35: יָקוּם אֱלֹהִים יָפוּצוּ אוֹיְבָיו (yāqûm ʾĕlōhîm yāpûṣû ʾôyĕbāyw, "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered"). In Numbers the formula was spoken by Moses each time the ark moved through the wilderness; its citation here signals that Psalm 68 is a processional hymn accompanying the ark into the sanctuary. Qûm (to arise — the standing-up from rest of a king about to act): God roused to march. וְיָנוּסוּ מְשַׂנְאָיו מִפָּנָיו (wĕyānûsû mĕśānʾāyw mippānāyw, "let those who hate him flee before him") — the enemies in full flight before the advancing divine presence. The ark was the visible throne of the invisible God; when it moved, the enemies' flight was anticipated as already accomplished.
68.2: Two similes for the dissolution of the wicked: כְּהִנְדֹּף עָשָׁן תִּנְדֹּף (kĕhindōp ʿāshān tindōp, "as smoke is dispersed by the wind, disperse them") — nādap: to blow away, to scatter (the insubstantial enemy driven off by a gust). כְּהִמֵּס דּוֹנַג מִפְּנֵי-אֵשׁ (kĕhimmês dônag mippĕnê-ʾêsh, "as wax melts before fire") — māsas (niphal: to melt, to dissolve): wax before fire is the image of swift, irreversible dissolution. Both similes are chosen for their speed: smoke is gone in a gust; wax melts at the fire's approach. The enemies are already as insubstantial as smoke, already as fugitive as wax. יֹאבְדוּ רְשָׁעִים מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (yōʾbĕdû rĕshāʿîm mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm, "so let the wicked perish before God") — ʾābad: to be destroyed (as in Ps 1:6 — the way of the wicked perishes).
68.3: וְצַדִּיקִים יִשְׂמְחוּ יַעַלְצוּ לִפְנֵי אֱלֹהִים (wĕṣaddîqîm yiśmĕḥû yaʿalṣû lipnê ʾĕlōhîm, "But let the righteous be glad and rejoice before God") — two joy verbs: śāmaḥ (to be glad — the general term) and ʿālaṣ (to exult, to leap with joy — the more physical, dancing delight). וְיָשִׂישׂוּ בְשִׂמְחָה (wĕyāśîśû bĕśimḥāh, "let them celebrate with great joy") — śîś (to exult greatly — Isa 61:10: "I will greatly rejoice in the LORD"). Three joy terms accumulate: the righteous response to God's arising is an escalating, irrepressible delight. Contrast with the enemies' three-stage dissolution (smoke, wax, perishing).
68.4: שִׁירוּ לֵאלֹהִים זַמְּרוּ שְׁמוֹ (shîrû lêʾlōhîm zammĕrû shĕmô, "Sing to God, sing praises to his name") — the double call to song: shîr (vocal song) and zāmar (instrumental praise). סֹלּוּ לָרֹכֵב בָּעֲרָבוֹת (sōllû lārōkêb bāʿărābôt, "exalt him who rides upon the ʿărābôt") — sālal: to heap up, to lift up, to make a highway for. Rōkêb bāʿărābôt: "rider on the ʿărābôt" — "the heavens" (cf. Deut 33:26). This epithet directly parallels the Ugaritic divine title rkb ʿrpt ("rider on the clouds") applied to Baal in Canaanite literature — the Psalmist claims that title exclusively for YHWH, performing a polemical displacement of the storm-god. בְּיָהּ שְׁמוֹ (bĕYāh shĕmô, "the LORD [JAH] is his name") — Yāh: the contracted form of YHWH (יָהּ), appearing independently only here and in the hallĕlû-Yāh formula. The short divine name asserts the identity of the heavens-rider with Israel's covenant God.
68.5: אֲבִי יְתוֹמִים וְדַיַּן אַלְמָנוֹת (ʾăbî yĕtômîm wĕdayyān ʾalmānôt, "a father of orphans and a defender of widows") — two divine titles for those without natural protectors: ʾāb (father) to the yātôm (orphan — the fatherless); dayyān (judge/defender/advocate — from dîn: to govern, to plead a cause, to defend in court) for the ʾalmānāh (widow — the one without a husband-protector). Together they define the divine character as the advocate for the legally and socially voiceless — the theme of Exod 22:22-24; Deut 10:18; Isa 1:17. אֱלֹהִים בִּמְעוֹן קָדְשׁוֹ (ʾĕlōhîm bimʿôn qodshô, "God in his holy dwelling") — from the holy dwelling above, God attends to those below who have no earthly advocate.
68.6: אֱלֹהִים מוֹשִׁיב יְחִידִים בַּיְתָה (ʾĕlōhîm môshîb yĕḥîdîm baytāh, "God gives the isolated a home to belong to") — yāḥîd: solitary, isolated, alone (the single one without family network). Môshîb (hiphil participle: the one who settles, who causes to dwell): God as the divine settlor who plants the isolated into a household. מוֹצִיא אֲסִירִים בַּכּוֹשָׁרוֹת (môṣîʾ ʾăsîrîm bakkôshārôt, "he brings prisoners out into freedom") — bakkôshārôt: possibly "with singing/with prosperity" (from kāshar: to prosper) — the liberation accompanied by flourishing. אַךְ סוֹרְרִים שָׁכְנוּ צְחִיחָה (ʾak sôrĕrîm shākĕnû ṣĕḥîḥāh, "but the rebellious dwell in a parched land") — sôrēr: the stubborn rebel (Deut 21:18); ṣĕḥîḥāh: a scorched, sun-baked waste — the habitat that mirrors the spiritual condition of those who resist the settling God.
68.7: אֱלֹהִים בְּצֵאתְךָ לִפְנֵי עַמֶּךָ (ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣêtĕkā lipnê ʿammekā, "O God, when you went out before your people") — the theophany-march begins: God going before Israel (Exod 13:21-22; Num 10:33). בְּצַעְדְּךָ בִישִׁימוֹן סֶלָה (bĕṣaʿdĕkā bîshîmôn selāh, "when you marched through the wilderness") — ṣāʿad: to march, to stride (the measured military step of a warrior); yĕshîmôn: the desolate wilderness (Num 21:20). The phrase closely parallels the Song of Deborah (Judg 5:4-5): "LORD, when you went out from Seir... the earth trembled." Both texts preserve memory of the same wilderness march. Selāh marks the pause before the theophany description.
68.8: אֶרֶץ רָעָשָׁה אַף-שָׁמַיִם נָטְפוּ (ʾereṣ rāʿāshāh ʾap-shāmayim nāṭĕpû, "the earth shook, and the heavens poured down rain") — rāʿash: to quake, to tremble (the seismic response to divine approach — Ps 77:18; Judg 5:4); nāṭap: to drip, to pour down. מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים זֶה סִינַי (mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm zeh sînay, "at the presence of God — even Sinai itself") — the parenthetical identification: Sinai is the paradigm-theophany, and all else trembles by reference to it. מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾêl, "at the presence of God, the God of Israel") — the full divine title anchors the cosmic trembling in covenant history: this is not generic deity but Israel's covenant God.
68.9: גֶּשֶׁם נְדָבוֹת תָּנִיף אֱלֹהִים (geshem nĕdābôt tānîp ʾĕlōhîm, "You sent an abundant rain, O God") — nĕdābôt: freewill-offerings, voluntary gifts (from nādab: to give freely without compulsion — as in Exod 25:2: the freewill offering for the tabernacle). The rain described as a nĕdābāh is a divine gift given out of liberality, not obligation. Nûp (hiphil: to wave, to shake loose): God scattering the rain from the clouds. נַחֲלָתְךָ וְנִלְאָה אַתָּה כוֹנַנְתָּהּ (naḥălātĕkā wĕnilʾāh ʾattāh kônantāh, "you restored your weary inheritance") — nālaʾ: to be weary, exhausted; kûn (polel: to establish firmly, to restore): God re-establishing the exhausted inheritance. The "inheritance" (naḥălāh) is the covenant people — weary from the wilderness and needing divine restoration.
68.10: חַיָּתְךָ יָשְׁבוּ-בָהּ (ḥayyātĕkā yāshĕbû-bāh, "Your congregation settled in the land") — ḥayyāh: a community, a living group (the living community of the covenant — from ḥāyāh: to live). תָּכִין בְּטוֹבָתְךָ לֶעָנִי אֱלֹהִים (tākîn bĕṭôbātĕkā leʿānî ʾĕlōhîm, "in your goodness, O God, you provided for the needy") — kûn (hiphil: to establish, to prepare, to provide — as a host sets a table for a guest): God's goodness (ṭôbāh) as the instrument of provision for the ʿānî (the needy, the afflicted — the one who is bent down by want). The land settlement is not merely a political event but an act of divine provision for those who depended entirely on God's goodness.
68.11: אֲדֹנָי יִתֶּן-אֹמֶר (ʾădōnāy yittên-ʾōmer, "The Lord gave the word") — ʾōmer: a word, a saying (the divine proclamation that sets the victory in motion). הַמְבַשְּׂרוֹת צָבָא רָב (hamĕbaśśĕrôt ṣābāʾ rāb, "great was the army of women who announced the victory") — bāśar (piel feminine plural participle: women announcing/heralding good news): the mĕbaśśĕrôt are women heralds of victory (as in Exod 15:20-21 where Miriam leads the women in the victory song; 1 Sam 18:6-7 where women with timbrels celebrate David's victories). Ṣābāʾ rāb: a great army of female heralds — the victory announcement is carried by an army of women. The divine word given; the women's proclamation as the immediate response.
68.12: מַלְכֵי צְבָאוֹת יִדֹּדוּן יִדֹּדוּן (malkê ṣĕbāʾôt yiddōdûn yiddōdûn, "The kings of armies fled in haste") — the doubled yiddōdûn marks the frantic speed of the kings' flight. וּנְוַת-בַּיִת תְּחַלֵּק שָׁלָל (ûnĕwat-bayit tĕḥallêq shālāl, "she who stayed at home shared in the plunder") — nĕwat-bayit: "the one who stays at the house" — the woman at home, not on the battlefield, yet given a share in the plunder. This is the custom of Num 31:27: the spoil divided between fighters and the rest of the community. Even those who did not fight receive God's victory-gift.
68.13: The verse is notoriously difficult. אִם-תִּשְׁכְּבוּן בֵּין שְׁפַתָּיִם (ʾim-tishkĕbûn bên shĕpattayim, "even if you once lay among the sheepfolds") — shĕpattayim: possibly sheepfolds, saddlebags, or the two sides of something — the word's meaning is uncertain. The imagery suggests a former low or resting state. כַּנְפֵי יוֹנָה נֶחְפָּה בַכֶּסֶף (kanpê yônāh neḥpāh bakkesep, "the wings of a dove covered with silver") — the dove with silver-covered wings: possibly Israel emerging from disgrace into the glory of deliverance (Ps 74:19 where the dove represents the community). The contrast is between resting in lowly sheepfolds and the subsequent shining of silver-and-gold wings. וְאֶבְרוֹתֶיהָ בִּירַקְרַק חָרוּץ (wĕʾebrôtêhā bîraqraq ḥārûṣ, "her feathers with yellow gold") — yrq-yrq: reduplication for gleaming yellowish-green; ḥārûṣ: refined gold. The image of a dove whose plain feathers have been transformed by divine victory into gleaming gold — glory following humiliation.
68.14: בְּפָרֵשׂ שַׁדַּי מְלָכִים בָּהּ (bĕpārêś shadday mĕlākîm bāh, "When the Almighty scattered kings throughout the land") — Shadday: the Almighty (the patriarchal divine name — Gen 17:1; 28:3); pāraś: to spread out, to scatter. תַּשְׁלֵג בְּצַלְמוֹן (tashleg bĕṣalmôn, "it snowed on Zalmon") — shālag (hiphil: to snow — the only occurrence of the hiphil of shālag); Ṣalmôn: a dark or shaded mountain (near Shechem — Judg 9:48). The sudden whitening of the dark mountain may image the transformation of the enemy-landscape by divine power — the darkness of enemy territory blanched white by the Almighty's intervention.
68.15: הַר-אֱלֹהִים הַר-בָּשָׁן (har-ʾĕlōhîm har-bāshān, "The mountain of God is like the mountain of Bashan") — an exclamation addressed to the Bashan range (including Hermon — 9,232 feet), the most impressive mountain system in the region. הַר-גַּבְנֻנִּים הַר-בָּשָׁן (har-gabnunnîm har-bāshān, "a high, many-peaked mountain, like the mountain of Bashan") — gabnunnîm: many-ridged, jagged-peaked (a word for the serrated ridge-line of a great mountain range). The verse sets up the contrast to follow: Bashan is magnificent, many-peaked, and seemingly the natural choice for a divine dwelling — yet God chose differently.
68.16: לָמָּה תְּרַצְּדוּן הָרִים גַּבְנֻנִּים (lāmmāh tĕraṣṣĕdûn hārîm gabnunnîm, "Why do you look with envy, O many-peaked mountains?") — rāṣad: to look askance, to eye with jealousy (a rare verb — the mountains personified as looking with competitive envy at Zion). הָהָר חָמַד אֱלֹהִים לְשִׁבְתּוֹ (hāhār ḥāmad ʾĕlōhîm lĕshibtô, "This is the mountain that God desired for his dwelling") — ḥāmad: to desire, to covet, to delight in (the same root as the Tenth Commandment's lōʾ taḥmōd). God's sovereign desire chose Zion — the smaller, less dramatic hill — over the imposing Bashan range. אַף-יְהוָה יִשְׁכֹּן לָנֶצַח (ʾap-yĕhwāh yishkōn lāneṣaḥ, "the LORD will dwell there forever") — the permanence of the divine choice: Zion as the eternal dwelling, not by geological majesty but by divine election (Deut 7:7-8: God chose Israel not because of its greatness but because of love).
68.17: רֶכֶב אֱלֹהִים רִבֹּתַיִם אַלְפֵי שִׁנְאָן (rekeb ʾĕlōhîm ribbōtayim ʾalpê shinʾān, "The chariots of God are twenty thousands, thousands upon thousands") — ribbōtayim: the dual of ten-thousand = twenty thousand; ʾalpê shinʾān: "thousands upon thousands" (the exact meaning of shinʾān is debated: possibly "repeated," "of angels," or "of the terrifying"). The massive enumeration of the divine army dwarfs any human force. אֲדֹנָי בָם סִינַי בַּקֹּדֶשׁ (ʾădōnāy bām sînay baqqōdesh, "the Lord is among them — as in Sinai, in the holy place") — the key theological claim: the same divine presence that appeared at Sinai in the wilderness is now present in Zion's sanctuary. Sinai-holiness and Zion-holiness are continuous; the God of the wilderness theophany has brought his throne permanently to Jerusalem.
68.18: The most theologically freighted verse in the psalm and the one cited in the NT. עָלִיתָ לַמָּרוֹם שָׁבִיתָ שֶּׁבִי (ʿālîtā lammārôm shābîtā shebî, "You have ascended to the heights, leading a train of captives") — ʿālāh: to ascend; shābāh shebî: the cognate-accusative construction "you led captivity captive" (the absolute internal object — emphasizing the completeness and finality of the victory). The image is of the victorious king ascending to his throne after battle, bringing the conquered enemies as the trophy of his triumph. לָקַחְתָּ מַתָּנוֹת בָּאָדָם (lāqaḥtā mattānôt bāʾādām, "you have received tribute from men") — the MT reads "received/taken" (lāqaḥtā): the victorious king receives tribute. Eph 4:8 quotes this with "he gave gifts to men" (ἔδωκεν δόματα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις) — reversing the direction. Paul's reading either follows a targum tradition or applies a midrashic technique: the ascended Christ distributes to the church what he received; the "giving" is the Spirit's gifts given through the ascended Lord. The reversal is deliberate: Christ did not ascend to hoard the tribute but to distribute the Spirit as gifts. אַף סוֹרְרִים לִשְׁכֹּן יָהּ אֱלֹהִים (ʾap sôrĕrîm lishkōn Yāh ʾĕlōhîm, "yes, even the rebellious — that the LORD God might dwell there") — the extraordinary extension: the victory benefits even the formerly rebellious.
68.19: בָּרוּךְ אֲדֹנָי יוֹם יוֹם (bārûk ʾădōnāy yôm yôm, "Blessed be the Lord, who day after day carries our burdens for us") — the distributive repetition yôm yôm (day day = every single day): the divine carrying is not a one-time rescue but a continuous, daily burden-bearing. יַעֲמָס-לָנוּ הָאֵל יְשׁוּעָתֵנוּ סֶלָה (yaʿămās-lānû hāʾêl yĕshûʿātênû selāh, "the God of our salvation carries our loads") — ʿāmas: to load, to carry a burden as a beast of burden carries a pack (Isa 46:1-4: "I have carried you from before your birth... I will carry and will save"). God compared to a beast of burden lovingly carrying what his people cannot carry themselves. The warrior-God of the processional is also the daily burden-bearer. Selāh.
68.20: הָאֵל לָנוּ אֵל לְמוֹשָׁעוֹת (hāʾêl lānû ʾêl lĕmôshāʿôt, "Our God is the God of deliverances") — môshāʿôt: plural of salvation/deliverance: multiple deliverances, the accumulated repertoire of divine rescues. The God of one salvation would be remarkable; the God of deliverances (plural) is the God of a track record. וְלֵיהוִה אֲדֹנָי לַמָּוֶת תּוֹצָאוֹת (wĕlayĕhwāh ʾădōnāy lammāwet tôṣāʾôt, "and to the LORD, the Lord, belong the ways of escape from death") — tôṣāʾôt: exits, escape routes (from yāṣāʾ: to go out). Even from death — the ultimate closure — God has exits. The plural tôṣāʾôt suggests multiple escape routes that God holds: he who controls even death's exits controls everything.
68.21: אַךְ-אֱלֹהִים יִמְחַץ רֹאשׁ אֹיְבָיו (ʾak-ʾĕlōhîm yimḥaṣ rōʾsh ʾôyĕbāyw, "But God will crush the head of his enemies") — māḥaṣ: to shatter, to smash (as in Num 24:17: the star from Jacob "shall crush the foreheads of Moab"; Ps 110:6: "he will shatter heads over the wide earth"). קָדְקֹד שֵׂעָר מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּאֲשָׁמָיו (qodqōd śēʿār mithallêk bāʾăshāmāyw, "the proud scalp of those who persist in their guilt") — qodqōd: the crown/top of the head; śēʿār: hairy (the flowing hair of the unashamed proud — possibly the warrior's long hair). Mithallêk bāʾăshāmāyw: "walking about in their guilt" — those who continue in their offenses without turning. The crushing is directed specifically at the proud who persist in guilt rather than repenting.
68.22: אָמַר אֲדֹנָי מִבָּשָׁן אָשִׁיב (ʾāmar ʾădōnāy mibbāshān ʾāshîb, "The Lord declared, 'I will bring them back from Bashan'") — the divine speech: bringing back the enemy from Bashan (the far territory east of the Jordan — a general term for the remote places of enemy retreat). אָשִׁיב מִמְּצֻלוֹת יָם (ʾāshîb mimmĕṣûlôt yām, "I will bring my people back from the depths of the sea") — the two extreme geographies: the high territory of Bashan and the depths of the sea. No enemy refuge is beyond God's reach; no oppressed people too deeply submerged to be retrieved. The motif echoes Amos 9:2-3: no depth or height places anyone beyond God's hand.
68.23: לְמַעַן תִּמְחַץ רַגְלְךָ בְּדָם (lĕmaʿan timḥaṣ raglĕkā bĕdām, "so that your foot will be stained in the blood of your enemies") — a graphic battle image: the victory so complete that the returning warrior's foot is bloodied by the field of the slain. לְשׁוֹן כְּלָבֶיךָ מֵאֹיְבִים מִנֵּהוּ (lĕshôn kĕlābêkā mêʾōyĕbîm minnehû, "and the tongues of your dogs will have their share") — the dogs licking the blood of the battlefield slain (as in 1 Kgs 22:38; 21:19). The image does not celebrate cruelty but the completeness of divine vindication over the enemy: even the scavenging dogs are fed by the thoroughness of the victory.
68.24: רָאוּ הֲלִיכוֹתֶיךָ אֱלֹהִים (rāʾû hălîkôtêkā ʾĕlōhîm, "People have witnessed your processions, O God") — hălîkāh: procession, march (from hālak: to walk/go). The psalm shifts from narrative of past victories to the present liturgical procession. הֲלִיכוֹת אֵלִי מַלְכִּי בַקֹּדֶשׁ (hălîkôt ʾêlî malkî baqqōdesh, "the processions of my God, my King, into the holy place") — the processional into the sanctuary: the ark brought into the Jerusalem temple, carrying with it the memory of all the Sinai and wilderness marches now culminating in permanent holy dwelling. The worshiper's personal claim: "my God, my King."
68.25: קִדְּמוּ שָׁרִים אַחַר נֹגְנִים (qiddĕmû shārîm ʾaḥar nōgĕnîm, "The singers lead the procession; behind them come the musicians") — qādam (piel: to go before, to lead): the singers at the head; nōgĕnîm: players of stringed instruments (from nāgan: to play — 1 Sam 16:16-18 for David playing for Saul). בְּתוֹךְ עֲלָמוֹת תּוֹפֵפוֹת (bĕtôk ʿălāmôt tôpĕpôt, "among them, young women playing tambourines") — ʿălāmôt: young women (as in 1 Sam 18:6-7; Exod 15:20: Miriam and the women with timbrels); tôpēp: to beat the tambourine. The procession composition: male singers leading, then string players, with young women with timbrels among them — the full worship orchestra in procession.
68.26: בְּמַקְהֵלוֹת בָּרְכוּ אֱלֹהִים (bĕmaqhêlôt bārĕkû ʾĕlōhîm, "Bless God in the great assembly") — maqhêlôt: congregations, choral assemblies (from qāhal: the assembled congregation). The call to corporate blessing (bārak): the assembled multitude joining in the doxology. אֲדֹנָי מִמְּקוֹר יִשְׂרָאֵל (ʾădōnāy mimmĕqôr yiśrāʾêl, "praise the LORD, all you who come from the fountain of Israel") — māqôr: spring, fountain, source (Israel's patriarchal origin — the fountain from which all Israel flows). The comprehensive summons: all who trace their origin to the source of Israel are called to join the assembly-blessing.
68.27: שָׁם בִּנְיָמִן צָעִיר רֹדֵם (shām binyāmîn ṣāʿîr rōdêm, "There is Benjamin, the youngest, leading the way") — ṣāʿîr: the youngest, the smallest (the tribe of Benjamin, the last-born son of Jacob). Benjamin paradoxically leads despite being the smallest. The tribes named in the processional: שָׂרֵי יְהוּדָה רִגְמָתָם (śārê yĕhûdāh rigmātām, "the princes of Judah in their bright company") — rigmāh: a heap, a crowd (a gathered throng). Judah, Zebulun, Naphtali — a representative sample of the northern and southern tribes together, united in the processional worship. The tribal gathering in Jerusalem is the enacted unity of what will be divided under Solomon's successors.
68.28: צִוָּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ עֻזֶּךָ (ṣiwwāh ʾĕlōhêkā ʿuzzekā, "Your God has commanded your strength") — ṣāwāh: to command, to ordain (God having already ordained and established the people's strength — the strength is not self-generated but divinely decreed). עוּזָּה אֱלֹהִים זוּ פָּעַלְתָּ לָּנוּ (ʿûzzāh ʾĕlōhîm zû pāʿaltā llānû, "show your strength, O God, in what you have already begun for us") — the petition: not for new power but for the continuation and display of what God has already done. The perfect pāʿaltā ("what you have done") grounds the imperative ʿûzzāh ("show strength") — past acts are the basis for present appeal.
68.29: מֵהֵיכָלֶךָ עַל-יְרוּשָׁלִָם (mêhêkālekā ʿal-yĕrûshālāim, "Because of your temple in Jerusalem") — the temple as the address from which tribute is commanded: the temple's presence in Jerusalem is the magnet drawing the kings. לְךָ יוֹבִילוּ מְלָכִים שָׁי (lĕkā yôbîlû mĕlākîm shāy, "kings will bring their gifts to you") — shāy: a gift, tribute (a rare word — Isa 18:7: "At that time tribute will be brought to the LORD of hosts"). The eschatological vision: the nations' kings bringing their tribute to Jerusalem's temple — developed in Isa 60:3 and Rev 21:24-26.
68.30: גְּעַר חַיַּת קָנֶה (gĕʿar ḥayyat qāneh, "Rebuke the beast that lurks in the reeds") — gāʿar: to rebuke (the authoritative command of the sovereign); ḥayyat qāneh: "the beast of the reeds" — likely the hippopotamus or crocodile (symbols of Egypt — cf. Ps 74:14: the Leviathan of Egypt; Ezek 29:3: the great dragon lying in the Nile). עֲדַת אַבִּירִים בְּעֶגְלֵי עַמִּים (ʿădat ʾabbîrîm bĕʿeglê ʿammîm, "the herd of bulls among the peoples' young") — the foreign powers as bulls and calves: the strong nations surrounding weak ones. מִתְרַפֵּס בְּרַצֵּי-כָסֶף (mitrappês bĕraṣṣê-khāsep, "until they submit with pieces of silver") — rāpas (hithpael: to trample oneself down, to grovel, to prostrate): forced submission with tribute. בִּזַּר עַמִּים קְרָבוֹת יֶחְפָּצוּ (bizzar ʿammîm qĕrābôt yeḥpāṣû, "scatter the peoples who delight in war") — the divine rebuke scattering the war-delighting nations.
68.31: יֶאֱתָיוּ חַשְׁמַנִּים מִנִּי מִצְרָיִם (yeʾĕtāyû ḥashmannîm minnî miṣrāyim, "Envoys will come from Egypt") — ḥashmannîm: dignitaries, princes, envoys (a rare word — possibly from an Egyptian loan-word; the nobles who make diplomatic submission). Egypt as the paradigm-oppressor making voluntary submission to God. כּוּשׁ תָּרִיץ יָדָיו לֵאלֹהִים (kûsh tārîṣ yādāyw lêʾlōhîm, "Ethiopia will quickly extend her hands to God") — rûṣ (hiphil: to cause to run, to stretch out quickly): Cush/Ethiopia extending hands in submission and worship. Isaiah 18:7 and 45:14 develop the same vision. The two great African powers of the ancient world submitting to Israel's God: the eschatological horizon of the psalm's processional.
68.32: מַמְלְכוֹת הָאָרֶץ שִׁירוּ לֵאלֹהִים (mamlaḵôt hāʾāreṣ shîrû lêʾlōhîm, "Sing to God, O kingdoms of the earth") — the summons broadens to all kingdoms: not Israel alone but the mamlaḵôt hāʾāreṣ. The processional that began with the ark on Zion now encompasses the whole earth. זַמְּרוּ אֲדֹנָי סֶלָה (zammĕrû ʾădōnāy selāh, "sing praises to the Lord, Selah") — zāmar: to praise with instruments; Selāh marks the pause before the final doxological section.
68.33: לָרֹכֵב בִּשְׁמֵי שְׁמֵי-קֶדֶם (lārōkêb bishĕmê shĕmê-qedem, "to him who rides upon the ancient heavens") — shĕmê qedem: "the heavens of old/antiquity" — the primordial heavens (Deut 33:27: "The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms"). The rider on the ʿărābôt (v4) is now the rider on the ancient heavens — both eternity (time) and the highest heavens (space) are under his sovereignty. הֵן יִתֵּן בְּקוֹלוֹ קוֹל עֹז (hên yittên bĕqôlô qôl ʿōz, "listen, he thunders with his mighty voice") — qôl ʿōz: the powerful voice (Ps 29:3-9 — the voice of the LORD over the waters, shattering cedars). The thunder as the divine speech addressed to all the earth.
68.34: תְּנוּ עֹז לֵאלֹהִים (tĕnû ʿōz lêʾlōhîm, "Ascribe power to God") — nātan ʿōz: to "give" power — not that God receives power from human declaration, but that the assembly acknowledges and ascribes to God the power that is already his (Ps 29:1-2: "Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength"). עַל-יִשְׂרָאֵל גַּאֲוָתוֹ (ʿal-yiśrāʾêl gaʾăwātô, "his splendor is over Israel") — gaʾăwāh: majesty, splendor (the divine majesty — Exod 15:7: "In the greatness of your majesty you overthrow your adversaries"). God's majesty displayed specifically over Israel — the chosen people as the theater of divine splendor. וְעֻזּוֹ בַּשְּׁחָקִים (wĕʿuzzô bashĕḥāqîm, "and his strength is in the heavens") — shĕḥāqîm: the sky-dust, the clouds/heavens (from shāḥaq: to rub fine).
68.35: נוֹרָא אֱלֹהִים מִמִּקְדָּשֶׁיךָ (nôrāʾ ʾĕlōhîm mimmiqddāshêkā, "God is awe-inspiring from his sanctuary") — nôrāʾ: fearful, awesome (as in Ps 65:5 — the God of awesome deeds). The processional psalm ends where it began — at the sanctuary, the place of God's dwelling. אֵל יִשְׂרָאֵל הוּא נֹתֵן עֹז וְתַעֲצֻמוֹת לָעָם (ʾêl yiśrāʾêl hûʾ nōtên ʿōz wĕtaʿăṣumôt lāʿām, "the God of Israel is the one who gives strength and power to the people") — taʿăṣumôt: mighty forces, powers (from ʿāṣam: to be strong — the abstract noun for concentrated power). The God asked to "show your strength" (v28) is revealed as the one who gives strength and power to those who worship. בָּרוּךְ אֱלֹהִים (bārûk ʾĕlōhîm, "Blessed be God") — the closing doxology bookending v19's "Blessed be the Lord."

69.1: Psalm 69 is the second most cited individual psalm in the NT (after Ps 22), with at least eight distinct quotations or allusions. The opening is the urgent lament-cry. הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי אֱלֹהִים כִּי בָאוּ מַיִם עַד-נָפֶשׁ (hôshîʿênî ʾĕlōhîm kî bāʾû mayim ʿad-nāpesh, "Save me, O God, for the waters have come up to my soul/throat") — hôshîʿa (hiphil imperative: save! deliver!): the urgent cry naming the LORD as the only rescuer. Bāʾû mayim ʿad-nāpesh: "the waters have come to the nāpesh" — the nāpesh is both "soul" and "throat" (the life-breath in the throat): water up to the throat is the moment before drowning. The liturgical tune (shôshannîm: "to the tune of Lilies") frames this urgent lament within Israel's worship practice.
69.2: טָבַעְתִּי בִּיוֹן מְצוּלָה (ṭābaʿtî bîyôn mĕṣûlāh, "I am sinking in the deep mud") — ṭābaʿ: to sink, to be submerged (as in Exod 15:4: Pharaoh's chariots "sank" in the Red Sea); yāwen: miry clay, swamp-mud (the sucking mud that holds the foot — also Ps 40:2 where God drew the Psalmist out of it). Mĕṣûlāh: the deep, the abyss. מַיִם עָמְקִים שִׁבֹּלֶת שְׁטָפַתְנִי (mayim ʿāmĕqîm shibbolet shĕṭāpatni, "the deep waters flood over me") — shibbōlet: a flowing stream, a flood-current (the same word as the "Shibboleth" test of Judg 12:6). The image: the Psalmist sinking in the swamp-mud while the flood-current sweeps over him — no ground underfoot and no escape above.
69.3: יָגַעְתִּי בְקָרְאִי נִחַר גְּרוֹנִי (yāgaʿtî bĕqārʾî niḥar gĕrônî, "I am exhausted from calling out; my throat is raw") — yāgaʿ: to be weary, to be worn out; nāḥar: to be raw, to be parched (the scorched, burned-out throat from continuous crying). כָּלוּ עֵינַי מְיַחֵל לֵאלֹהָי (kālû ʿênay mĕyaḥêl lêʾlōhāy, "my eyes have given out while waiting for my God") — kālāh: to be consumed, to fail (the eyes that have scanned the horizon for God's arrival until they are burned out — Ps 119:82: "my eyes fail from searching your promise"). Mĕyaḥêl (piel participle: waiting with hope — from yāḥal: to wait in hope): the exhaustion comes not from despair but from sustained, hoping expectation. Voice and eyes are both spent, yet the waiting continues.
69.4: רַבּוּ מִשַּׂעֲרוֹת רֹאשִׁי שֹׂנְאַי חִנָּם (rabbû miśśaʿărôt rōʾshî śōnĕʾay ḥinnām, "Those who hate me without cause outnumber the hairs of my head") — ḥinnām: without cause, for nothing, gratuitously. John 15:25 quotes this word directly: "They hated me without a cause" — Jesus applies this verse to himself, identifying himself as the righteous sufferer of Ps 69. אֲשֶׁר לֹא-גָזַלְתִּי אָז אָשִׁיב (ʾăsher lōʾ-gāzaltî ʾāz ʾāshîb, "what I did not steal, I must now restore") — a forensic expression: being compelled to pay back (shûb: to return, to restore) what one did not take (gāzal: to rob). The Psalmist bears the punishment for wrongs he did not commit. The NT applies this to Christ's substitutionary suffering: bearing the debt of sins not his own.
69.5: אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ לְאִוַּלְתִּי (ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh yādaʿtā lĕʾiwwaltî, "O God, you know my foolishness") — ʾiwwelet: folly (the moral recklessness — from ʾĕwîl: the fool). The Psalmist makes no claim to innocence before God: the "without cause" hatred of v4 exists even though the Psalmist has genuinely sinned. וְאַשְׁמוֹתַי מִמְּךָ לֹא-נִכְחָדוּ (wĕʾashmôtay mimmĕkā lōʾ-niḵḥādû, "and my sins are not concealed from you") — ʾashmāh: guilt, offense; kāḥad (niphal: to be concealed). The transparency before God — the Psalmist knows that his own guilt is visible to the judge even as he protests that the enemy's hatred is groundless. Both are simultaneously true.
69.6: אַל-יֵבֹשׁוּ בִי קֹוֶיךָ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה צְבָאוֹת (ʾal-yēbōshû bî qōwêkā ʾădōnāy yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt, "Let not those who hope in you be disgraced because of me, O Lord GOD of hosts") — the Psalmist's concern is not merely for himself but for those who share his trust in God: his public failure would publicly discredit all who trust the same God. Qāwāh: to hope, to wait with expectation. The full threefold divine name (Lord YHWH of hosts) is the highest possible divine authority. אַל-יִכָּלְמוּ בִי מְבַקְשֶׁיךָ אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (ʾal-yikkālĕmû bî mĕbaqshêkā ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾêl) — kālam: to be put to shame publicly (as opposed to bôsh — kālam has the nuance of being made to look foolish before others). The seekers of God would be publicly humiliated if the psalmist's suffering were read as divine abandonment.
69.7: כִּי-עָלֶיךָ נָשָׂאתִי חֶרְפָּה (kî-ʿālêkā nāśāʾtî ḥerpāh, "For your sake I have borne disgrace") — nāśāʾ: to carry, to bear (the load-bearing verb — Isa 53:4 uses the same verb: the Servant "bore our griefs"). Ḥerpāh: reproach, disgrace, the public shame of the accused. The motivation: the disgrace is borne "for your sake" (ʿālêkā) — the suffering is vicarious in character; it comes because of the Psalmist's identification with God. כִּסְּתָה כְלִמָּה פָּנָי (kissĕtāh kĕlimmāh pānāy, "humiliation has covered my face") — the face — the seat of personal honor in ancient Near Eastern culture — covered/buried under humiliation.
69.8: מוּזָר הָיִיתִי לְאֶחָי (mûzār hāyîtî lĕʾeḥāy, "I have become a stranger to my brothers") — zār: a stranger, a foreigner (one who is not at home). The estrangement from one's own family as a result of identification with God is the deepest social wound: the covenant community that should be the place of belonging becomes the place of alienation. וְנָכְרִי לִבְנֵי אִמִּי (wĕnokrî libnê ʾimmî, "an outsider to my own mother's children") — nokrî: the foreign one, the one not of the household (a stronger term than zār). John 7:5 notes that Jesus's brothers did not believe in him — the NT applies this estrangement directly to Christ.
69.9: כִּי-קִנְאַת בֵּיתְךָ אֲכָלָתְנִי (kî-qinʾat bêtĕkā ʾăkālatni, "Zeal for your house has consumed me") — qinʾāh: zeal, jealousy (the passionate, exclusive devotion — as in Num 25:11 where Phinehas's "zeal" turned back God's wrath). ʾĀkal: to eat, to consume (the fire-like consuming of zeal — Exod 24:17: "the LORD appeared... like a consuming fire"). John 2:17 quotes this verse when Jesus cleanses the temple: "His disciples remembered that it was written, 'Zeal for your house will consume me.'" The switch from aorist ("has consumed") to future ("will consume") in John reflects the eschatological intensification — the temple action is the beginning of a consuming that ends at the cross. וְחֶרְפּוֹת חוֹרְפֶיךָ נָפְלוּ עָלָי (wĕḥerpôt ḥôrpêkā nāplû ʿālay, "and the insults directed at you have landed on me") — Rom 15:3 applies this to Christ: "the reproaches of those who reproached you fell upon me."
69.10: וָאֶבְכֶּה בַצּוֹם נַפְשִׁי (wāʾebkeh baṣṣôm napshî, "When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting") — bākāh: to weep; ṣôm: fasting (the self-humbling discipline — Joel 2:12); ʿinnāh nāpesh: to humble/afflict the soul (the technical language of the Day of Atonement — Lev 16:31; 23:27). וַתְּהִי לַחֲרָפוֹת לִי (wattĕhî laḥărāpôt lî, "that became a source of shame for me") — the reversal: the acts of piety (weeping, fasting) that should have won social sympathy instead became the material for reproach. The righteous disciplines made the Psalmist a spectacle.
69.11: וָאֶתְּנָה לְבוּשִׁי שָׂק (wāʾetĕnāh lĕbûshî śāq, "I dressed in sackcloth") — śaq: sackcloth, the coarse garment of mourning and penitence (2 Sam 3:31; Isa 37:1). The sackcloth signals the Psalmist's internal state to the community — the visible mourning garment worn as a communal communication of grief. וָאֱהִי לָהֶם לְמָשָׁל (wāʾĕhî lāhem lĕmāshāl, "and I became a laughingstock to them") — māshāl: a proverb, a byword (as in Deut 28:37; 1 Kgs 9:7 — becoming a "byword" means becoming the negative illustration everyone cites). The mourner mocked: the sackcloth meant to elicit sympathy confirms in the observers' minds that the Psalmist is a lost cause.
69.12: יָשִׂיחוּ בִי יֹשְׁבֵי שָׁעַר (yāśîḥû bî yōshĕbê shāʿar, "Those who sit at the city gate gossip about me") — yāśaḥ: to speak, to meditate aloud; yōshĕbê shāʿar: "those who sit at the gate" — the civic and judicial center of ancient cities (Ruth 4:1-11; Prov 31:23). The very place where legal proceedings happen — the seat of official life — has become the place where the Psalmist is discussed as a public scandal. וּנְגִינוֹת שׁוֹתֵי שֵׁכָר (ûnĕgînôt shôtê shêkār, "and I have become the subject of the drunkards' songs") — nĕgînāh: a song, a musical piece. From the city gate's judiciary to the tavern's drunkards, the whole social spectrum has made him the object of derision.
69.13: וַאֲנִי תְפִלָּתִי-לְךָ יְהוָה (waʾănî tĕpillātî-lĕkā yĕhwāh, "But as for me, my prayer is directed to you, LORD") — the contrastive waʾănî (but I, as for me): despite the universal mockery (vv11-12), the Psalmist's orientation remains fixed on YHWH alone. עֵת רָצוֹן אֱלֹהִים (ʿêt rāṣôn ʾĕlōhîm, "at a time of your favor, O God") — ʿêt rāṣôn: the acceptable/favorable time (Isa 49:8: "In the time of favor I have answered you" — Paul cites Isa 49:8 in 2 Cor 6:2: "Now is the acceptable time"). בְּרָב-חַסְדְּךָ עֲנֵנִי בֶּאֱמֶת יִשְׁעֶךָ (bĕrāb-ḥasdĕkā ʿănênî beʾĕmet yishʿekā, "O God, in your great steadfast love, answer me in the truth of your salvation") — the twin grounds of appeal: the vastness of divine ḥesed (as in Ps 51:1) and the faithfulness (ʾemet) of divine salvation. The covenant-reliability that makes God's salvation trustworthy, not merely possible.
69.14: הַצִּילֵנִי מִטִּיט וְאַל-אֶטְבָּעָה (haṣṣîlênî miṭṭîṭ wĕʾal-ʾeṭbāʿāh, "Rescue me from the mire and do not let me sink") — ṭîṭ: mud, mire (the same swamp-mud of v2); ṭābaʿ (now negated in petition): the opening image is petitioned against — what I am sinking in, rescue me from. אִנָּצְלָה מִשֹּׂנְאַי (ʾinnāṣĕlāh miśśōnĕʾay, "let me be saved from those who hate me") — nāṣal (niphal: to be rescued — the passive petition when the Psalmist cannot rescue himself). וּמִמַּעֲמַקֵּי-מָיִם (ûmimmaʿămaqqê-māyim, "and from the deep waters") — maʿămaqqîm: depths (as in Ps 130:1: "Out of the depths I cry to you, LORD"). The petition covers both the swamp below (mire) and the flood above (deep waters).
69.15: אַל-תִּשְׁטְפֵנִי שִׁבֹּלֶת מַיִם (ʾal-tishṭĕpênî shibbolet mayim, "Let not the flood sweep me away") — shāṭap: to flood over, to sweep away; shibbōlet: the stream-current (as in v2). וְאַל-תִּבְלָעֵנִי מְצוּלָה (wĕʾal-tiblāʿênî mĕṣûlāh, "let not the depths swallow me") — bālaʿ: to swallow, to engulf (as in Num 16:30-32: the earth "swallowed" Korah). וְאַל-תֶּאְטַר-עָלַי בְּאֵר פִּיהָ (wĕʾal-teʾṭar-ʿālay bĕʾêr pîhā, "let not the pit close its mouth on me") — ʾāṭar: to close (the shutting of the pit's mouth above the one thrown in — Gen 37:24: Joseph thrown into the pit). Three petitions covering the three dangers: flood, depth, and the closure of Sheol.
69.16: עֲנֵנִי יְהוָה כִּי-טוֹב חַסְדֶּךָ (ʿănênî yĕhwāh kî-ṭôb ḥasdekā, "Hear me, LORD, for your steadfast love is good") — the ground of appeal: the goodness of divine ḥesed. Ṭôb ḥesed: "good steadfast love" — the goodness is both a quality of the love and a statement about the character of a God whose love exceeds rather than just meets obligation (compare Ps 63:3: "ḥesed is better than life"). כְּרֹב רַחֲמֶיךָ פְּנֵה אֵלָי (kĕrōb raḥămêkā pĕnêh ʾêlay, "in your great compassion, turn toward me") — raḥămîm: the plural of deep compassion (from reḥem: the womb — the maternal tenderness; Isa 49:15). The plural intensifies: not a single act of compassion but the full reservoir of divine motherly tenderness turned toward the drowning petitioner.
69.17: וְאַל-תַּסְתֵּר פָּנֶיךָ מֵעַבְדֶּךָ (wĕʾal-tastêr pānêkā mêʿabdekā, "Do not turn your face away from your servant") — sātar pānîm: "to hide the face" (the technical term for divine withdrawal — Ps 13:1; 27:9; 44:24 — the experience of divine absence). כִּי-צַר-לִי מַהֵר עֲנֵנִי (kî-ṣar-lî mahêr ʿănênî, "I am in great distress — answer me quickly") — ṣar: the narrow/tight place, the anguish of having no room. Mahêr: quickly (the adverb of urgency — not that God's timing is wrong but that the situation is desperate). The "quickly" of the drowning person to the rescuer: not a demand but a description of extremity.
69.18: קָרְבָה אֶל-נַפְשִׁי גְאָלָהּ (qorbāh ʾel-napshî gĕʾālāh, "Come near to my soul and rescue it") — qārab: to draw near; gāʾal: to redeem, to act as the gōʾêl (the kinsman-redeemer who restores the lost family member — Ruth 4:4-6; Lev 25:25). God petitioned to come near and exercise kinsman-redemption on the drowning soul. לְמַעַן אֹיְבַי פְּדֵנִי (lĕmaʿan ʾôyĕbay pĕdênî, "set me free because of my enemies") — pādāh: to ransom, to redeem by payment. The "because of" (lĕmaʿan) is remarkable: the enemies' existence is itself the argument for rescue — God's character is vindicated when the righteous sufferer is rescued from those who wrongly persecute him.
69.19: אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ חֶרְפָּתִי וּבָשְׁתִּי וּכְלִמָּתִי (ʾattāh yādaʿtā ḥerpātî ûbāshtî ûkĕlimmātî, "You know my disgrace, my shame, and my humiliation") — three terms for public dishonor accumulating: ḥerpāh (disgrace/reproach — v9), bōshet (shame — the state of being put to shame), kĕlimmāh (humiliation — the being-made-to-look-foolish of v6). God knows all three dimensions of the Psalmist's public destruction. נֶגְדְּךָ כָּל-צוֹרְרָי (negdĕkā kol-ṣôrĕrāy, "all my enemies are in your sight") — neged: before, in plain sight of. The appeal: if God sees it all, let him act on what he sees.
69.20: חֶרְפָּה שָׁבְרָה לִבִּי (ḥerpāh shābrāh libbî, "Disgrace has broken my heart") — shābar: to break, to shatter (as in Ps 34:18: "The LORD is near to the brokenhearted" — nishbĕrê lêb). The reproach (ḥerpāh) as the breaking-agent for the heart: the sustained public dishonor has shattered the inner center. וָאֲנוּשָׁה (wāʾănûshāh, "and I am in anguish") — ʾānash: to be mortally ill, to be beyond recovery (Jer 17:9: "the heart is ʾānûsh — desperately sick"; Jer 30:12: a wound that is incurable). וָאֲקַוֶּה לָנוּד וָאַיִן (wāʾăqawweh lānûd wāʾayin, "I looked for someone to show compassion, but there was none") — nûd: to nod in sympathy, to show compassion (Job 2:11: "they went to comfort and console him"). Wayyāyin: and there was none — the absolute absence of any human comforter. The isolation is complete.
69.21: The most cited verse of Psalm 69 in the Passion narratives. וַיִּתְּנוּ בְּבָרוּתִי רֹאשׁ (wayyittĕnû bĕbārûtî rōʾsh, "They put bitter gall in my food") — bārût: food, bread (the sustaining meal); rōʾsh: gall, head-poison (a bitter, poisonous plant — Amos 6:12: "wormwood"). They put poison in what was meant to sustain. וְלִצְמָאִי יַשְׁקוּנִי חֹמֶץ (wĕliṣmāʾî yashqûnî ḥōmeṣ, "and when I was thirsty, they offered me vinegar to drink") — ḥōmeṣ: vinegar, sour wine (acidic, undrinkable as a refreshment). Matt 27:34 (gall mixed with wine given to Jesus before crucifixion) and Matt 27:48/John 19:29 (vinegar on a sponge at the cross) fulfill this verse. John 19:28 explicitly notes that Jesus said "I thirst" "so that the scripture would be fulfilled" — a direct citation of this verse's thirst-and-vinegar scene.
69.22: יְהִי-שֻׁלְחָנָם לִפְנֵיהֶם לְפָח (yĕhî-shulḥānām lipnêhem lĕpāḥ, "Let their table be set as a snare before them") — shulḥān: a table (the meal, the place of fellowship and covenant); pāḥ: a bird-trap, a snare. The table — the symbol of hospitality and covenant fellowship — becomes a trap. וְלִשְׁלוֹמִים לְמוֹקֵשׁ (wĕlishlômîm lĕmôqêsh, "and what was meant for their prosperity become a trap") — shālôm (plural: prosperity, peace); môqêsh: a snare (Ps 64:5). Romans 11:9-10 quotes vv22-23 directly, applying them to Israel's hardening: their table (Torah, temple, covenant privilege) has become a trap through rejection of the Messiah. Paul's application is the ultimate reversal: what was given for salvation has been used for stumbling.
69.23: תֶּחְשַׁכְנָה עֵינֵיהֶם מֵרְאוֹת (teḥshaknahnāh ʿênêhem mêrĕʾôt, "Let their eyes be darkened so they cannot see") — ḥāshak: to be dark, to grow dark (Ps 18:28: "you lighten my darkness"; Isa 5:30: "the light is dark in the clouds"). The darkened eyes of those who refused to see now unable to see at all: the judicial punishment fitting the sin of willful blindness. Romans 11:10 quotes: "Let their eyes be darkened so that they cannot see." וּמָתְנֵיהֶם תָּמִיד הַמְעַד (ûmātnêhem tāmîd hamʿad, "and let their bodies tremble without ceasing") — mātên: the loins/hips (the center of physical strength); maʿad (hiphil: to cause to shake): the shaking of the loins as physical strength fails. Double judgment: vision darkened, physical strength failing — the complete debility of those from whom God withdraws.
69.24: שְׁפָךְ-עֲלֵיהֶם זַעְמֶךָ (shĕpōk-ʿălêhem zaʿmekā, "Pour out your wrath upon them") — shāpak zaʿam: the pouring out of divine wrath (Jer 10:25: "Pour out your wrath on the nations"; Rev 16: the seven bowls of wrath poured out). Zaʿam: wrath, indignation (the burning anger that scorches what it falls on — distinct from ʾap: the flaring nostril-anger). וַחֲרוֹן אַפְּךָ יַשִּׂיגֵם (waḥărôn ʾappĕkā yaśśîgêm, "let your fierce anger seize them") — ḥărôn ʾap: the burning of the nostrils (Exod 32:12); nāśag (hiphil: to overtake, to catch up with — as a pursuing force catches the fleeing). The imprecatory petition: the enemies' actions warrant the judgment now called down upon them.
69.25: תְּהִי-טִירָתָם נְשַׁמָּה (tĕhî-ṭîrātām nĕshammāh, "Let their camp become desolate") — ṭîrāh: encampment, fortified settlement; shāmam (niphal: to be desolate, to be devastated). בְּאָהֳלֵיהֶם אַל-יְהִי יֹשֵׁב (bĕʾōhŏlêhem ʾal-yĕhî yōshêb, "let no one live in their dwelling") — the total emptying of the dwellings. Acts 1:20 quotes this verse directly with reference to Judas Iscariot: "For it is written in the Book of Psalms, 'Let his homestead become desolate, and let there be no one to live in it.'" Peter applies the judicial prayer of the persecuted righteous to the specific betrayer of Jesus, identifying Judas as the embodiment of the enemies of the anointed sufferer.
69.26: כִּי-אַתָּה אֲשֶׁר-הִכִּיתָ רָדָפוּ (kî-ʾattāh ʾăsher-hikkîtā rādāpû, "For they have harassed the one whom you have afflicted") — nākāh (hiphil: to strike, to smite): God himself has struck the Psalmist (permitted or caused the affliction — Isa 53:4: "we esteemed him stricken, smitten by God"). The enemies pile on the one already struck by God — the double wound of divine affliction and human persecution. וְאֶל-מַכְאוֹב חֲלָלֶיךָ יְסַפֵּרוּ (wĕʾel-makʾôb ḥălālêkā yĕsappêrû, "and they gossip about the suffering of those you have wounded") — makʾôb: pain, anguish (from kāʾab: to be pained — Isa 53:3-4: the Servant who was "a man of sorrows"). The mockers take the divine wounding as the occasion for gossip rather than compassion.
69.27: תְּנָה-עָוֹן עַל-עֲוֹנָם (tĕnāh-ʿāwōn ʿal-ʿăwōnām, "Pile their guilt on top of their guilt") — the imprecatory petition: nātan ʿāwōn ʿal ʿāwōn: "give iniquity upon their iniquity" — the judicial principle of compounding guilt for compounded sin. They who persecuted the afflicted one have added offense upon offense; let the accounting reflect the accumulation. וְאַל-יָבֹאוּ בְּצִדְקָתֶךָ (wĕʾal-yābōʾû bĕṣidqātekā, "and do not let them share in your vindication") — ṣĕdāqāh: righteousness, vindication (the divine declaration of right — Isa 46:13). Let them have no share in the acquittal that the righteous sufferer receives.
69.28: יִמָּחוּ מִסֵּפֶר חַיִּים (yimmāḥû missêper ḥayyîm, "Let them be erased from the scroll of the living") — māḥāh (niphal: to be wiped out, to be erased — Exod 32:32-33 where Moses asks to be "blotted out" of God's book). Sêper ḥayyîm: "the scroll of the living" — the divine register of the living (Exod 32:32; Isa 4:3; Luke 10:20). Revelation 3:5 echoes this verse in the reverse: "I will not blot out his name from the book of life." וְעִם-צַדִּיקִים אַל-יִכָּתֵבוּ (wĕʿim-ṣaddîqîm ʾal-yikkātêbû, "let them not be listed among the righteous") — exclusion from both registers: the scroll of life and the company of the righteous. The two registers are related: to be in the book of life is to be among the righteous; exclusion is double.
