69.29: וַאֲנִי עָנִי וְכֹאֵב (waʾănî ʿānî wĕkōʾêb, "But I am afflicted and in pain") — the contrastive return to the individual sufferer after the imprecatory section: the enemies in their guilt; the Psalmist in his affliction and pain. ʿĀnî: afflicted, lowly; kōʾêb: in pain (from kāʾab: the same word as the Servant's "sorrows" in Isa 53). יְשׁוּעָתְךָ אֱלֹהִים תְּשַׂגְּבֵנִי (yĕshûʿātĕkā ʾĕlōhîm tĕśaggĕbênî, "may your salvation, O God, lift me up") — śāgab (piel: to set on high, to lift to safety — Ps 59:1; 91:14). The salvation lifts the lowly to a high secure place — the rescue from the mire of vv2-3 accomplished.
69.30: אֲהַלְלָה שֵׁם-אֱלֹהִים בְּשִׁיר (ʾăhallĕlāh shêm-ʾĕlōhîm bĕshîr, "I will praise the name of God with a song of praise") — the psalm pivots from lament and imprecation to vow and praise: the personal testimony of the rescued sufferer (as in Ps 22:22: "I will tell of your name to my brothers"). Hālal (piel: to praise lavishly — the root of hallĕlû-Yāh). וַאֲגַדְּלֶנּוּ בְתוֹדָה (waʾăgaddĕlennû bĕtôdāh, "I will honor him with thanksgiving") — gādal (piel: to make great, to magnify); tôdāh: the thanksgiving offering (Ps 50:23: "The one who offers the sacrifice of thanksgiving glorifies me"). The magnifying of God is the Psalmist's act of making God's greatness visible and audible to others.
69.31: וְתִיטַב לַיהוָה מִשּׁוֹר פָּר (wĕtîṭab laYHWH mishôr par, "This will please the LORD more than an ox or a bull") — yāṭab (qal: to be good, to be pleasing): the praise-song will please God more than the largest and most expensive animal sacrifices. מַקְרִן מַפְרִיס (maqrîn maphrîs, "with its horns and hoofs") — qeren: horn (the sign of mature strength and full value in a sacrificial animal); pāras: hoof (split hoof — the clean-animal marker of Lev 11:3). The most valuable possible sacrifice: a mature bull with full horns and clean hoofs. The Psalmist claims that heartfelt praise and thanksgiving exceed this. Ps 50:14 and 51:17 develop the same tradition: "The sacrifice acceptable to God is a broken spirit."
69.32: רָאוּ עֲנָוִים יִשְׂמָחוּ (rāʾû ʿănāwîm yiśmāḥû, "The afflicted will see it and rejoice") — ʿānāw: the humble, the meek, the afflicted (the same social class as the Psalmist — those who have no earthly recourse). When they see the Psalmist's rescue, they rejoice: the personal testimony of salvation is the communal encouragement of all who suffer similarly. דֹּרְשֵׁי אֱלֹהִים וִיחִי לְבַבְכֶם (dōrshê ʾĕlōhîm wîḥî lĕbabkem, "those of you who seek God, may your hearts be renewed") — dārash: to seek diligently (Ps 34:4; Amos 5:4: "seek me and live"); ḥāyāh (qal: may your hearts live) — the living of the heart as the opposite of the broken heart (v20). The assembly encouraged by one rescued person's testimony.
69.33: כִּי-שֹׁמֵעַ אֶל-אֶבְיוֹנִים יְהוָה (kî-shōmêaʿ ʾel-ʾebyônîm yĕhwāh, "For the LORD listens to the needy") — ʾebyôn: the needy, the destitute (from ʾābāh: to desire, to want — the one in want). YHWH hears specifically what no human hears — the cry of the poorest. וְאֶת-אֲסִירָיו לֹא בָזָה (wĕʾet-ʾăsîrāyw lōʾ bāzāh, "he does not look down on those he has bound") — ʾāsar: to bind (his prisoners, his bound ones — those whose affliction comes as if they were prisoners in God's custody); bāzāh: to despise. God does not despise the very ones he has allowed to suffer: their suffering is not evidence of divine contempt but of divine pedagogy. Ps 22:24 expresses the same truth: "he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one."
69.34: יְהַלְלוּהוּ שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (yĕhallĕlûhû shāmayim wāʾāreṣ, "Let heaven and earth give him praise") — the cosmic praise (as in Ps 148 — the entire creation summoned). יַמִּים וְכָל-רֹמֵשׂ בָּם (yammîm wĕkol-rōmêś bām, "the seas and every living thing in them") — rōmêś: everything that creeps/moves (Gen 1:21-26). The scope: heaven, earth, and sea — the three zones of creation all summoned to praise the God who hears the needy. The personal lament psalm broadens at its close to cosmic dimensions: the individual's rescue is an event of universal significance.
69.35: כִּי אֱלֹהִים יוֹשִׁיעַ צִיּוֹן (kî ʾĕlōhîm yôshîaʿ ṣiyyôn, "For God will save Zion") — the eschatological horizon: the individual's rescue (v29) is a sample of Zion's rescue. Yôshîaʿ (hiphil imperfect: will save — the future certainty based on the divine character). וְיִבְנֶה עָרֵי יְהוּדָה (wĕyibneh ʿārê yĕhûdāh, "and rebuild the cities of Judah") — the rebuilding of destroyed cities (Isa 54:3; 61:4). וְיָשְׁבוּ שָׁם וִירֵשׁוּהָ (wĕyāshĕbû shām wîrêshûhā, "people will live there and take possession of it") — the restoration of the covenant people to their land as the conclusive sign of divine salvation.
69.36: וְזֶרַע עֲבָדָיו יִנְחָלוּהָ (wĕzeraʿ ʿăbādāyw yinḥālûhā, "The children of his servants will inherit it") — zeraʿ: seed, offspring (the covenant continuity through generations — Gen 17:7-8: "your seed after you"); nāḥal: to inherit (the covenantal transfer of the land from generation to generation). וְאֹהֲבֵי שְׁמוֹ יִשְׁכְּנוּ-בָהּ (wĕʾōhăbê shĕmô yishkĕnû-bāh, "and those who love his name will live there") — the defining characteristic of the inheritors: not ethnic descent alone but love of the divine name (Ps 5:11: "let all who love your name rejoice in you"). The psalm that began in drowning waters ends with the seed of servants dwelling securely in an inherited land — the complete reversal of the lament's situation.

70.1: Psalm 70 is virtually identical to Ps 40:13-17, condensed into a standalone emergency petition. The superscription adds לְהַזְכִּיר (lĕhazzkîr, "to bring to remembrance") — the hiphil infinitive of zākar: to remember. This marks it as a liturgical piece for "bringing [the worshiper's situation] to remembrance" before God (as in the ʾazkārāh, the memorial portion of the grain offering — Lev 2:2). The superscription frames the whole psalm as an act of presenting an urgent case to the divine court. The compression relative to Ps 40:13-17 (where the parallel passage follows extended thanksgiving) signals a standalone emergency use: extracted from its longer context and deployed as an urgent cry on its own. אֱלֹהִים לְהַצִּילֵנִי יְהוָה לְעֶזְרָתִי חוּשָׁה (ʾĕlōhîm lĕhaṣṣîlênî yĕhwāh lĕʿezrātî ḥûshāh, "O God, hurry to deliver me! O LORD, hurry to help me!") — two divine names paired with the single urgent command ḥûshāh (hurry! from ḥûsh: to hasten, to move quickly): the cry for speed from the one who cannot wait.
70.2: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיַחְפְּרוּ מְבַקְשֵׁי נַפְשִׁי (yēbōshû wĕyaḥpĕrû mĕbaqshê napshî, "Let those who seek my life be put to shame and confusion") — bôsh: to be put to shame; ḥāpar: to be confounded, to be humiliated (to dig or scratch the ground in embarrassment — the image of the defeated person unable to meet anyone's gaze). יִסֹּגוּ אָחוֹר וְיִכָּלְמוּ חֲפֵצֵי רָעָתִי (yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăpêṣê rāʿātî, "let those who delight in my harm be turned back and put to disgrace") — sûg: to turn back, to withdraw, to be made to retreat (Isa 42:17: they "shall be turned back in utter shame"); kālam: the public humiliation (Ps 69:6-7). Three words for defeat in sequence: shame (bôsh), confusion (ḥāpar), public disgrace (kālam). The enemies' three-fold humiliation mirrors the Psalmist's three-fold dishonor of Ps 69:19.
70.3: יָשׁוּבוּ עַל-עֵקֶב בָּשְׁתָּם (yāshûbû ʿal-ʿêqeb bāshtām, "Let them be turned back in their shame") — shûb ʿal-ʿêqeb: "to be turned back on the heel" — the reversal of the direction of march (the army that advanced is turned backward on its own heels). הָאֹמְרִים הֶאָח הֶאָח (hāʾōmĕrîm heʾāḥ heʾāḥ, "those who say, 'Aha! Aha!'") — the mocking exclamation heʾāḥ (as in Ps 35:21: "Aha! Aha! Our eyes have seen it!"; Ezek 26:2: "Aha, the gate of the peoples is broken"). The repeated exclamation captures the gloating of spectators at the Psalmist's downfall. The very exclamation that expressed their delight becomes the occasion of their own reversal.
70.4: יָשִׂישׂוּ וְיִשְׂמְחוּ בְּךָ כָּל-מְבַקְשֶׁיךָ (yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû bĕkā kol-mĕbaqshêkā, "But let all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you") — the contrast: while the enemies are turned back in shame, those who seek God are turned forward in joy. Śîś and śāmaḥ: the two joy verbs of Ps 68:3 — accumulated delight. וְיֹאמְרוּ תָמִיד יִגְדַּל אֱלֹהִים אֹהֲבֵי יְשׁוּעָתֶךָ (wĕyōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal ʾĕlōhîm ʾōhăbê yĕshûʿātekā, "let those who love your salvation say always, 'God be magnified!'") — gādal (qal jussive: let God be made great/magnified): the doxological cry of those who love salvation — not "I am safe" but "God is great." Tāmîd: continually, always (Ps 34:1: "I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth").
70.5: וַאֲנִי עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן (waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, "But I am poor and needy") — the contrastive waʾănî (but I) as in Ps 69:29. ʿĀnî (afflicted/humble) and ʾebyôn (destitute/needy): the twin designations of the person who has no earthly resources and therefore turns entirely to God. In Ps 40:17 (the parallel) the same closing verse: the poverty is the qualification for trust, not the obstacle to it. אֱלֹהִים חוּשָׁה-לִּי (ʾĕlōhîm ḥûshāh-llî, "hurry to me, O God!") — the same ḥûshāh as v1: the psalm closes with the same urgent command that opened it. The emergency is maintained through to the final word — no resolution within the psalm itself, only urgent petition. עֶזְרִי וּמְפַלְּטִי אַתָּה (ʿezrî ûmĕpallĕṭî ʾattāh, "You are my help and my deliverer") — ʿēzer: help (the ʿezer of Gen 2:18 — the essential help); pallaṭ (piel participle: the one who causes escape, the liberator — from pālaṭ: to escape). יְהוָה אַל-תְּאַחַר (yĕhwāh ʾal-tĕʾaḥar, "O LORD, do not delay!") — ʾāḥar: to delay, to tarry. The final petition: opened with "hurry" (ḥûshāh), closed with "do not delay" (ʾal-tĕʾaḥar) — the same urgency in different words. The compressed Psalm 70 maintains emergency pitch from first word to last, unlike the extended Ps 40 from which it was drawn.

78.1: haʾăzînû ʿammî tôrātî — listen, O my people, to my instruction. Haʾăzîn (hiphil imperative of ʾāzan: to give ear, to incline the ear — a posture of attentive reception) opens this long historical poem with the register of wisdom instruction. Tôrāh here is not the Mosaic law specifically but instruction, teaching — the same word used for a teacher's guidance (Prov 1:8; 3:1). The poem functions as a wisdom meditation on history, not a law code.
78.2: ʾeptĕḥāh bĕmāshāl pî — I will open my mouth with a parable. Māshāl (parable, wisdom saying, comparison) + ḥîdôt (riddles, dark sayings, things requiring interpretation). Matthew 13:35 cites this verse as fulfilled in Jesus's parabolic teaching: the long-hidden mysteries of Psalm 78 are the very things Jesus opens in parable form. Ḥîdāh (riddle, enigma) — a truth that requires effort to decode, not merely information to receive.
78.3: ʾăsher shāmaʿnû wĕnēdāʿēm — what we have heard and come to know. The oral tradition — shāmaʿ (to hear, the primary mode of Torah reception in a pre-literate culture) and yādaʿ (to know by experience, to understand through relationship). waʾăbôtênû sipperû-lānû — what our ancestors passed down to us. The chain of transmission is already two generations deep before the current speaker — hearing the history of YHWH is itself a historical act.
78.4: lōʾ nĕkhahēd mibĕnêhem — we will not conceal from their children. Kāḥad (to conceal, hide) — the deliberate decision against suppression. lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn mĕsappĕrîm tĕhillôt YHWH — we will tell the next generation of the LORD's praiseworthy deeds. Tĕhillôt (praises, acts worthy of praise) — the history of YHWH's acts is itself a form of his praise. Telling history truthfully is an act of worship.
78.5: wayyāqem ʿēdût bĕyaʿăqōb wĕtôrāh śām bĕyisrāʾêl — he established a formal testimony in Jacob and ordained a law in Israel. ʿĒdût (testimony, decree — the formal covenant stipulation, cf. Ps 19:7; 25:10) — the divine establishment of the transmission requirement. The parents' obligation to teach is not cultural preference but divinely ordained covenant structure. ʾăsher ṣiwwāh ʾet-ʾăbôtênû lĕhôdîʿām lĕbĕnêhem — which he commanded our ancestors to teach their children.
78.6: lĕmaʿan yēdĕʿû dôr ʾaḥărôn bānîm yiwwāledû — so that the generation to come would know — even children not yet born. The purpose clause extends the transmission chain forward into theoretical infinity: children not yet conceived are already the intended audience of the teaching. The phrase bānîm yiwwāledû (children who will be born) — future generations already within the scope of the divine teaching mandate.
78.7: wĕyāśîmû bĕʾĕlōhîm kislām — so they would place their confident hope in God. Kesel (confidence, hope — from a root meaning thickness, fatness; the solid, well-founded confidence of one who is not naively hopeful but substantially grounded) — not mere optimism but trust founded on historical evidence. wĕlōʾ yishkĕḥû maʿallĕlê ʾēl — and would not forget what God has done. The entire transmission chain serves the goal of non-forgetting — what Psalm 78 will show is precisely how catastrophic forgetting is.
78.8: wĕlōʾ yihyû kĕʾăbôtām dôr sôrēr ûmôreh — and would not be like their ancestors — a stubborn and rebellious generation. Sôrēr ûmôreh (stubborn and rebellious) is the technical phrase of Deut 21:18-21 for the incorrigible son who faces execution — applied here to a whole generation. dôr lōʾ-hēkhîn libbô wĕlōʾ-neʾĕmānāh ʾet-ʾēl rûḥô — a generation that did not fix its heart on God and whose spirit was not faithful to him. Unfaithfulness is first a matter of the heart's orientation, then behavior follows.
78.9: bĕnê ʾeprāyim nôshĕqê rômê-qāshet — the men of Ephraim, though armed with bows. The opening of the Ephraim indictment — Ephraim (the dominant northern tribe) is the paradigm of failure throughout this psalm, setting up the contrast with Judah's election (v68). hāpĕkhû bĕyôm qĕrāb — turned back when the day of battle came. The hiphil of hāphak (to turn, reverse) — armed warriors who reversed course in battle. The specific event is debated (the conquest? a particular battle?) but the theological meaning is clear: military equipment without covenant faithfulness produces failure.
78.10: lōʾ shāmĕrû berît ʾĕlōhîm — they failed to keep God's covenant. The covenant (berît) as the governing framework — Ephraim's military failure in v9 is explained theologically here. ûbĕtôrātô māʾănû lālākheth — and refused to live by his instruction. Māʾan (to refuse — a deliberate, volitional rejection, not mere neglect). The law as the locus of covenant faithfulness: refusing the Torah is refusing the covenant.
78.11: wayyishkĕḥû ʿălîlôtāyw — they forgot what he had done. Shākhah (to forget) — the central sin of this psalm. The verb appears repeatedly (vv11, 42; and the command not to forget in vv7, 35) — forgetting YHWH's acts is not innocent lapse but covenant unfaithfulness. wĕniplĕʾôtāyw ʾăsher herʾām — and the wonders he had shown them. The wonders (niplĕʾôt) were shown (herʾām, hiphil: caused them to see) — the audience was present, the deeds were public, the forgetting is inexcusable.
78.12: negĕd ʾăbôtām ʿāśāh pheleʾ — he performed remarkable deeds in plain sight of their ancestors. Negĕd (before, in front of, openly) — the miracles were not private or hidden but performed publicly. bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim śĕdēh-ṣōʿan — in the land of Egypt, in the region of Zoan. Ṣōʿan (Zoan, the Egyptian city near where Israel lived during the bondage — Tanis/Avaris in the Nile Delta) is the historical anchor: these were real events at a real location.
78.13: bāqaʿ yām wayyaʿăbîrēm — he split the sea and brought them through. Bāqaʿ (to split, cleave — the same verb for the splitting of the rock in v15) — the primary act of the Exodus. wayyaṣṣēb-māyim kĕmô-nēd — he made the water stand in walls. Nēd (heap, mound — used of the wall of water in Exod 15:8; Josh 3:13) — the water piled up in a solid heap, defying its own nature at God's command.
78.14: wayyanhēm beʿānān yômām — in the daytime he led them with a cloud. Nāhāh (to lead, guide) — the divine leading through both day and night elements. wĕkhol-hallaylāh bĕʾôr ʾesh — and all night long with a column of fire. The cloud (protection from the Egyptian sun) and fire (light and warmth at night) are the twin provisions of the wilderness: the natural needs of a desert journey met by supernatural means.
78.15: yĕbaqquaʿ ṣurîm bammidbār — he split open rocks in the wilderness. The same verb bāqaʿ (to split, v13) now applied to rocks — the God who split the sea also splits rock. wayyasht kĕtĕhōmôt rabbāh — and gave them water to drink as from the great deep. Tĕhôm rabbāh (the great deep — the primordial subterranean ocean of Gen 1:2; 7:11) — the water from the rock is not merely a miraculous spring but draws on the same cosmic deep that YHWH controls at creation.
78.16: wayyôṣiʾ nōzĕlîm miṣṣûr — he brought streams out of the rock. Nōzĕlîm (flowing streams — from nāzal: to flow, trickle) — not a single gush but sustained flowing water. wayyôred kĕnahărôt māyim — and made the water flow down in rivers. Nāhār (river — the word for a substantial, navigable waterway) — what came from desert rock was not a trickle but a river. The extravagance of divine provision is the point.
78.17: wayyôsîpû ʿôd laḥăṭōʾ-lô — yet they kept on sinning against him. The waw-consecutive with yāsaph (to add, to continue) — they added sin upon sin, not a single lapse but a continuing pattern. lamrôt ʿelyôn baṣṣiyyāh — provoking the Most High in the wilderness. Mārah (to be rebellious, to provoke by opposition) + ʿelyôn (Most High) — the specific name emphasizes the audacity: provoking the highest authority in the driest place, surrounded by the evidence of miraculous provision.
78.18: waynassû ʾēl bĕlibbām — they deliberately tested God in their hearts. Nāsāh (to test, to put to the proof — the same verb as in the Temptation narratives, Matt 4:7 citing Deut 6:16) — testing God is not innocent inquiry but insolent demanding. lishʾōl-ʾōkhel lĕnapshām — demanding food to satisfy their cravings. Nepeš (soul, appetite) — the demand is for physical satisfaction. The test is whether God's provision was sufficient or whether they could impose their own agenda on it.
78.19: waydabbĕrû bĕʾĕlōhîm — they spoke against God. The preposition bĕ (in, against) with the speaking verb indicates hostile speech — speaking at or against God, not to him. ʾāmĕrû hayûkhal ʾēl laʿărōkh shulḥān bammidbār — they said, 'Can God really lay out a meal here in the wilderness?' Shulḥān (table — the piece of furniture for formal eating, not just food) — the question demands not mere sustenance but a proper table, a served meal. The irony is that God had already provided the cloud, fire, and water; the question treats all of it as insufficient.
78.20: hēn hikkāh-ṣûr wayyāzûbû māyim — look, he hit a rock and water poured out. The accusative hēn (behold, look) — the concession before the challenge. They acknowledge the water miracle: wayyāzûbû māyim (and water flowed abundantly). hagam-leḥem yûkhal tēt ʾim-yākhîn sheʾēr lĕʿammô — but can he also supply bread? Can he provide meat for his whole people? The challenge escalates from the miraculous already accomplished to a demand for more. The unanswered question is immediately answered by God's action in vv23-28.
78.21: lākhēn shāmaʿ YHWH wayyitʿabbār — therefore the LORD heard what they said and became furious. ʿAbar (hithpael: to be provoked to fury — used of floodwaters overflowing; divine anger as an overflowing) — God heard the hostile speech of v19 and responded with commensurate anger. wĕʾesh niśśĕqāh bĕyaʿăqōb — a fire broke out against Jacob. The fire of divine judgment (Num 11:1-3) — literal fire at the borders of the camp when the same complaint arose.
78.22: kî lōʾ heʾĕmînû bĕʾĕlōhîm — because they refused to believe in God. The hiphil of ʾāman (to trust, to place firm confidence in) — belief is trust-commitment, not intellectual assent. The diagnosis of the sin: the demand for bread and meat was a symptom of fundamental distrust. wĕlōʾ bāṭĕḥû bîshûʿātô — and put no trust in his power to save. Yĕshûʿāh (salvation, the capacity to rescue) — they had seen the Exodus and doubted God's willingness/ability to save them in the wilderness.
78.23: wayyĕṣaw shĕḥāqîm mimmaʿal — yet he had already commanded the clouds above. The command-verb ṣiwwāh (to command) — even while angry (v21), God had already issued the provision order. The divine provision is not earned by Israel's subsequent faith; it precedes their complaint. wĕdaltê shāmayim pātāḥ — and opened the doors of heaven. Daltê shāmayim (the doors/gates of heaven) — the heavenly storehouses opened, the same image as in Gen 7:11 for the floodgates and Mal 3:10 for the windows of heaven.
78.24: wayyamṭēr ʿălêhem mān leʾĕkhōl — he had rained down manna for them to eat. The verb māṭar (to rain) applied to the manna — provision from the sky, like rain, but unlike rain. wĕdĕgan shāmayim nātan lāmô — and had given them grain from heaven. Dĕgan shāmayim (grain of heaven) — heavenly bread contrasted with earthly grain. John 6:31 quotes this phrase in the Bread of Life discourse: Jesus is the true bread from heaven, of which the manna was a type.
78.25: leḥem ʾabbîrîm ʾākhal ʾîsh — human beings ate the food of angels. ʾAbbîrîm (the mighty ones — the term used for powerful warriors and, by extension in some traditions, for heavenly beings/angels, cf. LXX's bread of angels in the Septuagint rendering here). The manna is described as food of the supernatural realm. ṣēdāh shālah lāhem lāśābăʿ — he sent them abundant provisions. Śābēaʿ (abundance, fullness — satisfaction beyond adequacy) — not starvation-rations but lavish supply.
78.26: yassēʿ qādîm bashshāmāyim — he caused an east wind to blow through the sky. The east and south winds are the instruments of the quail-provision (cf. Num 11:31 where the wind brings quail from the sea). Nāsaʿ (hiphil: to cause to set out, to make journey) — wind as a divine agent dispatched on a mission. waynahēg bĕʿuzzô tēmān — by his power he drove the south wind. God driving the wind as a charioteer drives horses — creation obeys its Creator's commands.
78.27: wayyamṭēr ʿălêhem kĕʿāphār shĕʾēr — he rained meat on them as thick as dust. ʿĀphār (dust — the comparison is to the innumerable grains of dust, not to anything negative). ûkĕḥôl yammîm ʿôph kānāph — and birds with wings as numerous as grains of sea sand. The comparison to sand of the sea (the Abrahamic promise vocabulary, Gen 22:17) is ironic: the quantity promised to Abraham's descendants is here applied to the birds sent to feed them.
78.28: wayyappēl bĕqereb maḥănēhû — he dropped it right in the middle of their camp. Nāpal (hiphil: to cause to fall, to drop) — the quail not driven to the edges requiring effort to gather but dropped in the center of the camp, immediately accessible. sābîb lĕmishkĕnōtāyw — all around where they lived. The provision surrounds their dwellings — an image of enveloping, excessive generosity.
78.29: wayyōʾkĕlû wayyisbĕʾû meʾōd — they ate and were completely satisfied. Śābaʿ meʾōd (were completely satisfied — satisfied to excess, absolutely full) — God gave exactly what the craving demanded and more. wĕtaʾăwātām yābîʾ lāhem — he had given them exactly what they wanted. Taʾăwāh (craving, desire — the same word used of the Tree of the Knowledge in Gen 3:6) — their craving was satisfied. But the satisfaction of craving does not produce faithfulness; it sets up the catastrophe of v30-31.
78.30: lōʾ-zārû mitaʾăwātām — they had not turned from their craving. The verb zûr (to be a stranger to, to be estranged from) — they had not become estranged from their craving; the desire persisted even while being satisfied. ʿôd ʾokhlām bĕphîhem — the food was still in their mouths. The chilling timing — the meat still between their teeth when judgment fell (cf. Num 11:33 literally). Satiation did not produce gratitude; it produced no movement of the heart at all.
78.31: wĕʾap ʾĕlōhîm ʿālāh bāhem — when God's wrath came down on them. ʾAph (nostril, anger — the breathing-wrath of God) rising against them. wayyahărōg bĕmishmannêhem — and slew the strongest among them. Mishman (the fat ones, the choice ones — the best of the people, those in prime physical condition). ûbĕḥûrê yisrāʾêl hikhriʿ — and struck down the young men of Israel. Kāraʿ (to cause to kneel, to bring down) — the strong are specifically targeted: divine judgment falls on capacity, not just weakness.
78.32: bĕkhol-zōʾt ḥāṭĕʾû ʿôd — despite all of this, they went on sinning. The summarizing bĕkhol-zōʾt (despite all of this — through all the plagues, the provision, the judgment) — the pattern continues. wĕlōʾ heʾĕmînû bĕniplĕʾôtāyw — and refused to believe even his miracles. The miracles were not hidden but public; disbelief is not ignorance but willful refusal. Faith should have been the natural response to what they witnessed; its absence is moral failure.
78.33: waykhal bĕhebel yĕmêhem — so he made their days dissolve into emptiness. Hebel (breath, vapor, emptiness — Qoheleth's signature word) — the wasted potential of their lives reduced to vapor. ûshĕnôtām babbĕhālāh — and their years end in sudden fright. Bĕhālāh (terror, sudden dread — from bāhal: to terrify, to agitate) — not peaceful old age but years that end in shock. The wilderness generation died without reaching the goal; their years were emptied of purpose by their own unfaithfulness.
78.34: ʾim-hăragām ûdĕrāshûhû — whenever he struck them down, they would search for him. The conditional ʾim marking a repeated pattern: each strike produces a temporary seeking. Dārash (to seek, to inquire — the word of serious, purposeful inquiry) — but the sequel shows it is crisis-driven, not genuine. wĕshābû wĕshiḥărû ʾēl — they would turn around and sincerely seek God. Shāḥar (to seek eagerly in the morning, to seek earnestly — from shaḥar: dawn) — the urgency of early-morning seeking. But the sincerity is apparent, not real, as v35-37 show.
78.35: wayyizkĕrû kî ʾĕlōhîm ṣûrām — they recalled that God was their rock. Ṣûr (rock) — the crisis-memory of God's attribute as fortress and foundation. wĕʾēl ʿelyôn gōʾălām — and the Most High God was their redeemer. Gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer) — the memory of the Exodus redemption returns under pressure. The titles Rock and Redeemer are exactly what Psalm 77 closed with; the memory is accurate. But memory and transformation are not the same thing.
78.36: waypattûhû bĕphîhem — but they only said what he wanted to hear. Pittāh (piel of pāthāh: to entice, to flatter, to deceive with words — the same word used of the seductive enticement in Prov 1:10; 7:21) — they flattered God with their confession-speech. ûbilshônām yĕkazzĕbû-lô — they spoke to him with lying tongues. Kāzab (to lie, to be deceitful) — the repentance-speech was deceptive. Crisis produces the words of repentance without the reality of it.
78.37: wĕlibbām lōʾ-khên ʿimmô — their hearts were not truly directed toward him. Kēn (right, fixed, directed — the heart in the right position, oriented toward God) — a diagnosis: the heart-orientation never changed. wĕlōʾ neʾĕmĕnû bĕbĕrîtô — they were not faithful to his covenant. ʾĀman (niphal: to be established, faithful, reliable) — the covenant requires consistent faithfulness, not crisis-driven performance. External words without internal transformation do not satisfy the covenant.
78.38: wĕhûʾ raḥûm yĕkhappēr ʿāwōn — yet he, being full of compassion, covered over their guilt. The theological pivot of the entire psalm: wĕhûʾ (but he — the emphatic pronoun marking the divine counter to human failure). Raḥûm (compassionate — from reḥem: womb; the deep, inward, maternal compassion). Yĕkhappēr (he covers/atones — the piel of kāpar, the technical term for atonement) — guilt covered, not merely overlooked. wĕlōʾ-yashḥît wĕhirbāh lĕhāshîb ʾappô — and did not destroy them; again and again he turned back his anger. The repeated restraint — not once but habitually, structurally. Divine compassion is not a single exception but a pattern that constitutes God's character.
78.39: wayyizkōr kî-bāśār hēmmāh — he remembered that they were only flesh. Bāśār (flesh — the creaturely, mortal material of human existence) — God's memory of the creature's constitution is the theological basis for the compassion of v38. This is not low expectations but covenant grace: the Creator factors in the creature's weakness. rûaḥ hōlēkh wĕlōʾ yāshûb — a passing wind that does not come back. Rûaḥ (spirit, wind, breath) that passes and does not return — the full Qoheleth meditation on human transience (cf. Eccl 1:4-6) compressed into one clause. God's anger is restrained by his awareness of human mortality.
78.40: kammāh yamrûhû bammidbār — how many times they defied him in the wilderness. Kammāh (how many — an exclamation of enumerable but uncounted frequency). Mārah (to rebel, to be bitter — the same root as Miriam/Marah, the bitter water of Exod 15:23) — the wilderness rebellions are numerous enough to resist counting. yaʿaṣîbûhû bishshĕmāmāh — and caused him grief in the wasteland. ʿĀṣab (to hurt, to cause grief, to wound with sorrow — used of human grief and of divine grief: Gen 6:6) — the Psalmist does not hesitate to ascribe genuine emotional pain to God at Israel's rebellion.
78.41: wayyāshûbû waynassû ʾēl — they kept turning back and testing God. The waw-consecutive marks the recurring cycle: return → test again. wĕqĕdôsh yisrāʾêl hitʾimmârû — pressing against the Holy One of Israel. Hitʾammēr (hithpael of ʾāmar: to press against, to provoke by insolent speech — a rare form) — pushing against the limits of the Holy One. Qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl (Holy One of Israel) is Isaiah's characteristic divine epithet — its use here may reflect the Isaianic tradition or share its origin in Asaphite worship.
78.42: lōʾ zākhĕrû ʾet-yādô — they forgot what his hand had done. Zākar (to remember — here negated: not to remember) — the hand of God, the instrument of Exodus power. yôm ʾăsher-pādām miṣṣār — and the day he rescued them from their oppressor. Pādāh (to redeem/rescue by payment or force) — the redemption day of the Exodus. The forgetting of the Exodus is the root cause of the rebellion in Canaan; if the Exodus is remembered, its implications for trust in the wilderness follow naturally.
78.43: ʾăsher-śām bĕmiṣrāyim ʾôtōtāyw — how he had displayed his miraculous signs in Egypt. ʾŌt (sign — the Exodus plagues as divine signs, cf. Exod 10:1-2: the signs were intended so that the story would be told to future generations). ûmôphĕtāyw biśdēh-ṣōʿan — and his wonders in the region of Zoan. Môphēt (wonder, portent — paired with ʾôt throughout Deuteronomy's plague summaries: Deut 6:22; 7:19; 26:8). The Zoan location anchors the wonders in history.
78.44: wayyahăpōkh lĕdām yĕʾōrêhem — he turned their rivers into blood. Hāphak (to turn, overturn, convert) — the first plague (Exod 7:17-21). Yĕʾōr (the Nile — the Egyptian term for the river, not the standard Hebrew nāhār) — Egypt's life-giving river turned lethal. wĕnōzĕlêhem bilti yishĕtāyûn — so that they were unable to drink from their streams. The inversion of the rock-water provision (v15-16): God can give water from rock or take water from rivers.
78.45: yĕshallēaḥ bāhem ʿārōb wayyōkhlēm — he sent swarms of flies to devour them. ʿĀrōb (swarms — possibly a mixture of insects, traditionally flies) — the fourth plague (Exod 8:20-32). ûṣĕphardēaʿ wattishshĕḥîtēm — and frogs that brought ruin upon them. Ṣĕphardēaʿ (frogs — the second plague, Exod 8:1-15). The order here is not strictly Exodus order — the Psalm rearranges for rhetorical rather than chronological effect.
78.46: wayyittēn lĕḥāsîl yĕbûlām — he handed their harvest over to the locust. Ḥāsîl (locust — the devouring stage of the locust, from ḥāsal: to consume, finish off). wîgîʿām lāʾarbeh — and their labor to the swarming caterpillar. ʾArbeh (the swarming locust — the eighth plague, Exod 10:1-20). The pairing of two locust-type insects emphasizes total agricultural devastation: whatever survived the first was finished by the second.
78.47: yaharōg babbārād gaphnam — he struck their vines down with hail. The seventh plague (Exod 9:13-35). Bārād (hail — the destructive ice storm that devastated Egyptian agriculture). wĕshiqmôtām bĕḥanāmāl — and their sycamore trees with killing frost. Shiqmāh (sycamore-fig tree — a resilient tree important to Egyptian agriculture and to the poor as a food source). Ḥanāmāl (killing frost, blight — rare word) — even the hardiest trees were destroyed.
78.48: wayyasgēr labbārād bĕʿîrām — he surrendered their cattle to the hail. Sāgar (to shut up, surrender to — used of handing prisoners to their captors; God hands the Egyptian livestock to the hail as an executioner). ûmiqnêhem lārĕshāphîm — and their flocks to lightning strikes. Rĕsheph (flame, lightning bolt, burning fever — a divine fire-messenger) — the livestock killed by the electrical component of the hailstorm.
78.49: yĕshallēaḥ bām ḥărôn ʾappô — he unleashed his fierce anger upon them. Ḥărôn ʾap (the burning of the nostrils — fierce, blazing anger) — the full force of divine anger. ʿebrāh wāzaʿam wĕṣārāh mishlaḥat malʾăkhê rāʿîm — his wrath, indignation, and distress — a company of destroying messengers. Four terms for divine anger (ḥărôn + ʿebrāh + zaʿam + ṣārāh) accumulate. Malʾăkhê rāʿîm (messengers/angels of evil) — the destroying angels as divine instruments, distinct from God himself but serving his punitive purpose.
78.50: yĕpallēs nātîb lĕʾappô — he made a clear way for his wrath. Pālas (piel: to weigh out, level, make a clear path) — God smoothing the road for his own anger to travel without obstacle. lōʾ-ḥāśak mimmāwet napshām — he did not withhold their lives from death. Ḥāśak (to withhold, spare) — no sparing, no restraint. wĕḥayyātām laddeber higîr — but surrendered them to the plague. Deber (plague, pestilence) — the fifth plague was specifically against livestock (Exod 9:1-7), but the cumulative effect is death throughout Egypt.
78.51: wayyak kol-bĕkhôr bĕmiṣrāyim — he killed all the firstborn in Egypt. The climactic tenth plague — the one that finally broke Pharaoh's resistance. rēʾshît ʾônîm bĕʾohŏlê ḥām — the prime of their strength in the dwellings of Ham. Rēʾshît ʾônîm (the firstfruits of their strength — the best offspring, the concentrated vigor of procreation). ʾOhŏlê ḥām (the tents/dwellings of Ham) — Egypt as the land of Ham, one of Noah's sons (Gen 10:6). The ethnic designation reminds: what falls is not just a political power but a family of humanity that opposed God's redemptive purpose.
78.52: wayyassēʿ kattsōʾn ʿammô — but he brought his own people out like sheep. The contrast with v51 is immediate: Egypt's prime is killed; Israel is led like a shepherd leads a flock. Nāsaʿ (to cause to journey, to lead) — the same root as the wilderness journeys of Numbers. waynah'gēm kammidbār kaʿēder — and led them through the wilderness like a flock. ʿĒder (flock, drove of livestock) — Israel is led through the wilderness as a whole flock, not scattered stragglers.
78.53: wayyanhēm lābeṭaḥ wĕlōʾ phāḥādû — he guided them in safety so that they had nothing to fear. Bāṭaḥ (securely, in safety — the state of being unafraid because adequately protected). Pāḥad (to be in dread, to be terrified) — negated: the divine leading produced freedom from fear. wĕʾet-ʾôyĕbêhem kissāh hayyām — the sea swallowed their enemies. The pursuing Egyptian army covered by the sea — the crossing secured not just Israel's exit but the permanent removal of the threat.
78.54: waybîʾēm ʾel-gĕbûl qodshô — he brought them to his sacred territory. Gĕbûl qodshô (the border/territory of his holiness — the land of Canaan as the sacred space where YHWH's presence would dwell). har-zeh qānĕtāh yĕmînô — to the mountain that his right hand had won. Qānāh (to acquire, create, possess — the same creation verb as Gen 14:19,22) — Zion as the mountain YHWH acquired through the conquest, not merely occupied. The right hand as the instrument of both Exodus power (Exod 15:6) and Conquest victory.
78.55: waygārash mippenêhem gôyim — he expelled the nations from before them. Gārash (to drive out, expel) — the Conquest as divine expulsion, not human military achievement. wayyappîlēm bĕḥebel naḥălāh — assigned their land as an inheritance by lot. Ḥebel (measuring line — the land surveyed by rope and distributed by lot; the same word used in Ps 16:6 for the pleasant lines of David's inheritance). wayyashken bĕʾohŏlêhem shibtê yisrāʾêl — and made the tribes of Israel to dwell in their territory. Settlement is the completion of the salvation arc that began with the Exodus.
78.56: waynassû wayyamrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhîm ʿelyôn — nevertheless they tested and defied the Most High God. The cycle resumes — Canaan does not break the pattern of wilderness rebellion. wĕʿēdôtāyw lōʾ shāmārû — and refused to keep his commandments. ʿĒdôt (testimonies, the covenant stipulations) — the same term as v5's establishment of the covenant structure. Having received the land, they refuse the law that governs it.
78.57: wayyissōgû wayyibgĕdû kaʾăbôtām — they turned back and acted faithlessly, just as their ancestors had. Sûg (to move back, to apostatize) — the movement is backward, away from God. nehepĕkhû kĕqeshet remiyyāh — they bent away like a defective bow. Qeshet remiyyāh (a deceitful/defective bow — one that bends in the wrong direction when drawn, sending the arrow off-target). The image captures the combination of outward form (they look like a bow, a weapon of God's army) and internal failure (they misfire when it counts).
78.58: wayyakʿîsûhû bĕbāmôtām — they made him angry with their hilltop shrines. Kāʿas (to provoke to anger, to irritate — often used of idolatry's effect on God). Bāmāh (high place — the unauthorized cult site on hills, the persistent target of prophetic and Deuteronomic criticism). ûbiphĕsîlêhem yaqnîʾûhû — and roused his jealousy with their carved idols. Qinʾāh (jealousy, zealous possessiveness — the emotion of a spouse discovering infidelity). The covenant is a marriage; idolatry is adultery.
78.59: shāmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm wayyitʿabbār — when God heard all this, he was incensed. ʿAbar (hithpael: to overflow with fury — as in v21 at the wilderness complaint). wayyimʾas meʾōd bĕyisrāʾêl — he thoroughly rejected Israel. Māʾas meʾōd (rejected intensely — the combination of the rejection verb with the intensifier meʾōd: very much, to a great degree) — not a mild disapproval but comprehensive rejection of what they had become.
78.60: wayyittōsh mishkān-shilōh — he abandoned the tabernacle at Shiloh. Nāṭash (to forsake, abandon — used of a field left fallow) — the dramatic abandonment of Shiloh, the central sanctuary before Jerusalem. Shilōh was the site of the tabernacle during the Judges period (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1:3) until the ark was captured in 1 Samuel 4. ʾōhel shikkēn bāʾādām — the tent he had established among his people. The tent God himself placed among his people — now abandoned.
78.61: wayyittēn lashshĕbî ʿuzzô — he allowed his strength to go into exile. ʿŌz (strength — a common epithet for the ark, cf. 1 Sam 4:21-22: kābôd departed from Israel when the ark was taken). wĕtiphʾartô bĕyad-ṣār — and his glory into enemy hands. Tiphʾāret (glory, splendor — parallel to ʿōz as paired terms for the ark). God himself handed his glory to the enemy — the ark capture is not a defeat of God but a divine concession of the symbol of his presence. The profound paradox: YHWH uses the enemy to judge his own people.
78.62: wayyasgēr laḥereb ʿammô — he surrendered his people to the sword. Sāgar (to shut up, surrender — the same verb as v48's surrender of Egyptian cattle to the hail). ûbĕnaḥălātô hitʾabbār — and turned against his inheritance in anger. ʾAbar (hithpael: overflow with fury) again — the same burning anger, now directed against Israel rather than Egypt. YHWH's inheritance (naḥălāh) has become the object of his own judgment.
78.63: baḥûrāyw ʾākĕlāh ʾesh — fire devoured their young men. The fire of war consuming the youth — the immediate cost of the Philistine battle (1 Sam 4:10-11). ûbĕtûlōtāyw lōʾ hullālû — and their young women received no marriage praise. Hālal (pual: to be praised, to have wedding songs sung over them) — the young women left unmarried because the young men were killed. The absence of wedding songs is a sign of social death; the generation that should have married and continued the people is extinguished.
78.64: kōhănāyw bĕḥereb nāphalû — their priests were cut down by the sword. Hophni and Phinehas (1 Sam 4:11) — the specific historical referent behind this verse. wĕʾalmĕnōtāyw lōʾ tibkênāh — their widows could not even weep. The wives of the slain priests could not complete the mourning rite — perhaps because they died too (Phinehas's wife died in childbirth upon hearing the news, 1 Sam 4:19-20), or because the scale of disaster overwhelmed the capacity to mourn. The absence of weeping is the deepest marker of catastrophe.
78.65: wayyîqaṣ kĕyāshēn ʾădōnāy — then the Lord awoke as if from sleep. The metaphor of God waking is startling — it implies that during the Shiloh period and ark-capture, God appeared to be sleeping (cf. Ps 44:23; Elijah's taunt of Baal in 1 Kgs 18:27). kĕgibbôr mithronān miyyāyin — like a warrior who rises vigorous from wine. The simile is deliberately bold: a warrior rousing from wine is re-energized, aggressive, formidable. God's action against the Philistines (1 Sam 5-7) is described in the register of warrior-arousal.
78.66: wayyak ṣārāyw ʾāḥôr — he drove his enemies back. The Philistines driven back — the plagues of 1 Sam 5 (tumors, mice) forcing them to return the ark. ḥerpat ʿôlām nātan lāmô — and put them to shame that will not end. Ḥerpat ʿôlām (everlasting reproach/shame) — the permanent disgrace of the nation that dared capture the ark of YHWH. The shame is not a moment but a status.
78.67: wayyimʾas bĕʾōhel yôsēph — he refused the tent of Joseph. The theological move of the psalm's climax: not merely punishing Israel but selecting among the tribes. Māʾas (to reject, refuse) applied to the tent of Joseph — the Shiloh tradition was associated with the Joseph tribes (Ephraim/Manasseh). ûbeshēbeṭ ʾeprāyim lōʾ bāḥār — and did not choose the tribe of Ephraim. The Ephraim failure introduced in v9 is now the theological basis for the tribal rejection. Ephraim's military failure was symptomatic of a deeper election-failure.
78.68: wayyibḥar ʾet-shēbeṭ yĕhûdāh — instead he chose the tribe of Judah. Bāḥar (to choose — the election verb of Deuteronomy, used of the divine choice of Israel, of Jerusalem, of the Levites, and now of Judah over Ephraim). ʾet-har ṣiyyôn ʾăsher ʾāhēb — Mount Zion, which he loved. ʾĀhēb (he loved — the divine love as the basis for election, not merit or size as in Deut 7:7-8). The choice of Zion is a love-choice.
78.69: wayyiben kĕmô-rāmîm miqdāshô — he built his sanctuary like the heights of heaven. Rāmîm (heights, the elevated places — the heavens) — Zion's temple built to cosmic proportions, like heaven itself. kĕʾereṣ yĕsādāh lĕʿôlām — like the earth that he has set in place forever. The sanctuary is as permanent as the earth's foundations — the comparison grounds Zion's permanence in creation-permanence. (The subsequent destruction of the temple in 587 BCE is why Ps 74 and 79 exist; this claim is covenant-eschatological, not empirical-political.)
78.70: wayyibḥar bĕdāwid ʿabdô — he chose David his servant. Bāḥar (the election verb) applied for the third time in four verses — Judah chosen (v68), Zion chosen (v68), David chosen (v70). The three choices are a single divine movement: tribe → city → king. wayyiqqāḥēhû mimmikmĕrôt ṣōʾn — and took him from the sheep pens. Mikhlāʾ ṣōʾn (sheep pens/enclosures) — the specific occupation of David before his anointing (1 Sam 16:11). The deliberate pastoral origin of the king.
78.71: mēʾăḥar ʿālôt hĕbîʾô — from tending the nursing ewes he brought him. ʿālôt (nursing ewes — the most demanding of the shepherd's flock, requiring special care for both ewe and lamb). David's competence with the most vulnerable sheep is the sign of his qualification for the most demanding of pastoral roles — shepherding the people. lirʿôt bĕyaʿăqōb ʿammô ûbĕyisrāʾêl naḥălātô — to shepherd Jacob his people, and Israel his inheritance. The transition from literal shepherd to metaphorical shepherd is explicit: the same verb (rāʿāh: to shepherd, tend) governs both the sheep and the nation.
78.72: wayrĕʿēm kĕtōm lĕbābô — he tended them with the integrity of his heart. Tōm lēbāb (completeness/integrity of heart — undivided, whole-hearted attention) — the internal quality of the shepherd's care. ûbitbûnat kappāyw yanḥēm — and guided them with the skill of his hands. Tĕbûnāh (understanding, skillfulness — the cognitive component of practical competence) applied to the hands — the intelligent, experienced work of the hands. Heart-integrity and hand-skill are the twin qualifications of the ideal shepherd-king, answering the indictment of the earlier generations who had neither.

79.1: ʾĕlōhîm bāʾû gôyim bĕnaḥălātekhā — O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance. Naḥălāh (inheritance, possession) — the land is God's property, not Israel's; the invasion is a property violation against God, not merely a national disaster. ṭimmĕʾû ʾet-hêkhal qodshekā — they have defiled your holy temple. Ṭāmēʾ (piel: to defile, make ritually impure) — the same defilement language used of corpse-impurity in Num 19 applied to the sanctuary. The temple cannot function when defiled; God's dwelling place is rendered uninhabitable.
79.2: nātĕnû ʾet-nĕblat ʿăbādêkhā maʾăkhāl lĕʿôph hashshāmāyim — they have given the corpses of your servants as food to the birds of the heavens. Nĕblāh (corpse, carcass — the term for an unburied body, often used of animals; its application to humans marks the utter dehumanization). Unburied corpses were the supreme disgrace in ancient Mediterranean culture (cf. Tobit's dangerous act of burying corpses; Antigone's resistance). bĕśar ḥăsîdêkhā lĕḥayyat ʾāreṣ — the flesh of your godly ones to the beasts of the earth. Ḥāsîd (the pious, the faithful, the covenant-loyal ones) — those who embodied covenant loyalty are now carrion. The implicit appeal: God's own faithful deserve better.
79.3: shāpĕkhû dāmām kĕmayim sĕbîbôt yĕrûshālāim — they have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem. Shāphak dām (to pour out blood — the language of murder and of sacrifice, sometimes overlapping) + kĕmayim (like water — the comparison emphasizes both quantity and the casual disregard, as though blood were as cheap as water). wĕʾên qôbēr — and there was no one to bury the dead. The absence of burial is the final detail of shame — the dead lie exposed because there are no survivors with the capacity or freedom to bury them.
79.4: hāyînû ḥerpāh lishkhēnênû — we have become a reproach to our neighbors. Ḥerpāh (reproach, disgrace — the social-shame vocabulary of failed covenant loyalty) — national defeat is experienced as public humiliation before neighboring peoples. laʿag wāqelesleṣ lisĕbîbôtênû — a mockery and derision to those around us. Two terms for ridicule (laʿag: mockery, contemptuous laughter; qelesleṣ: derision, scoffing) — the nations surrounding Israel find the defeat not tragic but comic, a confirmation of their low estimation of YHWH's power.
79.5: ʿad-māh YHWH tĕʾĕnap lāneṣaḥ — how long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? The ʿad-māh (how long?) formula is the communal lament's cry for divine action — not a complaint that God is wrong but a plea that the duration of his silence/anger is unbearable. tiʿar kaʾesh qinʾātekhā — will your jealousy burn like fire? Qinʾāh (jealousy — the divine jealousy provoked by Israel's covenant-breaking now burning against Israel; the psalmist is aware that the destruction is covenantally earned). The appeal is not that God is unjust but that the punishment is sufficient.
79.6: shĕphōkh ḥămātĕkhā ʾel-haggôyim ʾăsher lōʾ yĕdāʿûkhā — pour out your wrath on the nations that do not know you. Shāphak ḥĕmāh (to pour out fury) — the same cup-of-wrath imagery (cf. Ps 75:8; Jer 10:25 quotes this almost verbatim). The argument: if the jealousy-anger is burning (v5), redirect it toward those who deserve it — the nations that never acknowledged YHWH. wĕʿal mamlakyôt ʾăsher bĕshimĕkhā lōʾ qārāʾû — and on the kingdoms that do not call on your name. The criterion is worship — those who do not call on the divine name versus Israel who does (or should).
79.7: kî ʾākal ʾet-yaʿăqōb — for they have devoured Jacob. ʾĀkal (to eat, devour — used of nations devouring Israel as a predator devours prey, cf. Jer 10:25 where this verse is nearly quoted). wĕʾet-nāwēhû heshammû — and laid waste his homeland. Nāweh (home, pasture, pleasant habitation) — Jacob's pleasant home reduced to shāmam (desolation). The homeland as both habitat and theological inheritance is destroyed.
79.8: ʾal-tizkār-lānû ʿăwōnōt rîshōnîm — do not hold our former iniquities against us. Rîshōnîm (former, earlier) — possibly the sins of previous generations (ancestral guilt as the cause of the current punishment), or the psalmist's own past sins. The prayer is explicitly penitential — unusual in communal laments which more often assert innocence. mahēr yĕqaddĕmûnû raḥămêkhā — let your compassion come quickly to meet us. Qādaph (piel: to come before, to meet — to intercept on the way) — the compassion racing ahead of further punishment to intercept the people before they are destroyed. kî dallônû meʾōd — for we are utterly brought low. Dal (thin, weak, impoverished) — the confession of total destitution.
79.9: ʿozrênû ʾĕlōhê yishʿēnû ʿal-dĕbar kĕbôd-shĕmekā — help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name. ʾĔlōhê yishʿēnû (God of our salvation) — the name invoked contains the plea; the God whose name is salvation is asked to act consistently with his own name. The motivation is explicit: ʿal-dĕbar kĕbôd-shĕmekā (for the sake of the glory of your name) — not Israel's merit but divine reputation. wĕhaṣṣîlênû wĕkhappēr ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾtênû lĕmaʿan shĕmekā — deliver us and forgive our sins, for your name's sake. The name-argument appears twice — the governing motivation throughout: God's action in history displays his character to the watching nations.
79.10: lāmmāh yōʾmĕrû haggôyim ʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêhem — why should the nations say, 'Where is their God?' The taunt of the nations (cf. Ps 42:3,10; 115:2; Joel 2:17) — the defeat of Israel is interpreted internationally as the defeat of YHWH. The question ʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêhem (where is their God?) is the practical atheism of military victory: if YHWH cannot protect his people, he has no effective power. yiwwādaʿ baggôyim lĕʿênênû niqmat dam-ʿăbādêkhā hashshāphûkh — before our eyes, let the avenging of your servants' shed blood be known among the nations. The vengeance for blood is a covenant obligation (Gen 9:5-6; Deut 32:43) — God owes the blood of his servants an answer.
79.11: tābôʾ lĕphānêkhā ʾanqat ʾāsîr — let the groaning of the prisoner come before you. ʾAnqāh (groaning — the low, involuntary sound of one in pain or imprisonment; used of oppressed Israel in Egypt, Exod 2:24) — the prisoners' groaning as wordless prayer reaching the divine ears. kĕgōdel zĕrôʿăkhā hôtēr bĕnê tĕmûtāh — according to your great power, preserve those condemned to die. Bĕnê tĕmûtāh (sons of death — those legally sentenced to die, or those who will certainly die; a legal idiom for the condemned) — the appeal is for divine commutation of the death sentence imposed by the enemy. Zĕrôaʿ (arm) — the Exodus arm (Ps 77:15) invoked again.
79.12: wĕhāshēb lishkhēnênû shebʿātayim ʾel-ḥêqām — return to our neighbors sevenfold into their bosom the reproach. Shebʿātayim (sevenfold — not literally seven times but the full intensified repayment, as in the Cain-Lamech sequence of Gen 4:15,24) — the complete, comprehensive vengeance that exceeds proportional retaliation. Ḥêq (bosom, lap — to pour into the bosom is to give someone what they deserve, cf. Isa 65:6-7). ḥerpātām ʾăsher ḥērĕphûkhā ʾădōnāy — the reproach with which they have taunted you, O Lord. The reproach is directed at YHWH, not merely at Israel — the motivation for its repayment is again divine honor.
79.13: waʾănaḥnû ʿamĕkhā wĕṣōʾn marʿîtekhā — then we your people, the sheep of your pasture. The closing self-identification: ʿamĕkhā (your people) + ṣōʾn marʿîtekhā (the flock of your pasture) — both the national covenant language and the shepherd-flock metaphor of Ps 78:52,71-72. The nation that was led from Egypt like a flock claims the same identity in exile: still the shepherd's flock. nôdeh lĕkhā lĕʿôlām lĕdôr wādôr nĕsappēr tĕhillātekhā — will give you thanks forever; from generation to generation we will recount your praise. The promised praise provides a future purpose for present rescue: God rescues so that his people can praise. The generational praise-chain of Ps 78:1-8 returns as the goal toward which Ps 79 points.

86.1: haṭṭēh YHWH ʾoznĕkhā ʿănēnî — turn your ear toward me, O LORD, and answer me. The hiphil of nāṭāh (to incline, stretch) applied to God's ear — the physical gesture of one leaning in to hear. kî-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾānî — for I am poor and needy. The double-term formula ʿānî wĕʾebyôn (poor and destitute) recurs throughout the Psalter (Ps 40:17; 70:5; 109:22) as the basis for divine aid — absolute need as the only qualification for prayer.
86.2: shĕmor nafshî kî-ḥāsîd ʾānî — preserve my soul, for I am devoted to you. Ḥāsîd (faithful, covenant-loyal, pious — from ḥesed: the one who embodies covenant love) — the Psalmist's self-identification as a ḥāsîd is both a claim and an appeal: God's ḥesed should protect the one who embodies ḥesed. hôshaʿ ʿabdĕkhā ʾattāh ʾĕlōhay habboṭēaḥ ʾēlêkhā — save your servant who trusts in you, O my God. Three self-identifications (devoted, servant, trusting) build the case for divine response.
86.3: ḥonnēnî ʾădōnāy kî ʾēlêkhā ʾeqrāʾ kol-hayyôm — be gracious to me, O Lord, for I cry out to you all day long. Ḥānan (to be gracious — the divine attribute of Exod 34:6 that recurs in v15). The frequency of prayer (kol-hayyôm: all day long) is itself an argument for a response — persistent prayer makes an implicit claim on divine attentiveness. Psalm 86 is notably constructed from phrases borrowed from many other psalms; v3 echoes Ps 56:1; 57:1.
86.4: śammēaḥ nefesh ʿabdekā — gladden the soul of your servant. Śāmaḥ (hiphil: to cause to rejoice, to make glad) — the petition is not just for rescue but for joy: full salvation includes gladness, not merely safety. kî ʾēlêkhā ʾădōnāy nafshî ʾeśśā — for to you, O Lord, I lift up my soul. Lifting the soul (nefesh) to God — the gesture of complete surrender and reorientation of one's center of gravity toward God (cf. Ps 25:1; 143:8).
86.5: kî-ʾattāh ʾădōnāy ṭôb wĕsallāḥ — for you, O Lord, are good and forgiving. Sallāḥ (forgiving — a qal adjective from sālaḥ: to forgive) occurs only here in the entire OT as an attribute of God — everywhere else sālaḥ is a verb. The nominal form makes forgiveness a permanent divine characteristic, not just an occasional divine act. wĕrab-ḥesed lĕkhol-qōrĕʾêkhā — abounding in steadfast love for all who call on you. Rab-ḥesed (great in steadfast love) — the phrase from Exod 34:6 (wĕrab-ḥesed wĕʾemet) cited directly. All who call — not just Israel.
86.6: haʾăzînāh YHWH tĕphillātî — give ear to my prayer, O LORD. The hiphil of ʾāzan (to give ear) — a second request for divine attentiveness, completing the frame opened in v1. wĕhaqshîbāh bĕqôl taḥănûnôtāy — listen to the voice of my plea for mercy. Taḥănûn (plea for grace/mercy — from ḥānan: to be gracious) — the prayer as an appeal to divine grace, not a claim of divine obligation.
86.7: bĕyôm ṣārātî ʾeqrāʾekā — on the day of my trouble I call to you. The connection between crisis and prayer: the day of trouble (ṣārāh) is the day of calling. kî taʿănēnî — for you answer me. The certainty is grammatically present tense — not a hope but a known fact drawn from experience. The prayer is made precisely because the answer is certain, not speculatively.
86.8: ʾên-kāmôkhā bāʾĕlōhîm ʾădōnāy — there is no one like you among the gods, O Lord. The incomparability formula (ên kāmôkhā) acknowledges the existence of other divine claimants while asserting their absolute inferiority to YHWH (cf. Exod 15:11; Ps 35:10; 89:6). wĕʾên kĕmaʿăśêkhā — and no works compare to yours. The incomparability extends to deeds, not just being — YHWH's actions are uniquely powerful.
86.9: kol-gôyim ʾăsher ʿāśîtā yābôʾû wĕyishtaḥăwû lĕphānêkhā ʾădōnāy — all the nations you have made will come and bow before you, O Lord. The petition moves from personal crisis to eschatological vision: all nations — precisely because YHWH made them (ʾăsher ʿāśîtā) — will ultimately worship their Creator. wîkhabbĕdû lishmekhā — and they will glorify your name. The universal worship envisioned in Ps 72:11; 87:4-6 surfaces here as grounds for present prayer.
86.10: kî-gādôl ʾattāh wĕʿōśēh niplāʾôt — for you are great and you do wonders. Niplāʾôt (wonders — the Exodus vocabulary of Ps 77:14; 78:4) — the divine greatness expressed in specific historical acts. ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm lĕbaddekā — you alone are God. Lĕbaddekā (you alone — the exclusive particle) — a direct monotheistic confession, not merely incomparability but uniqueness. No other being is ʾĕlōhîm in the same sense.
86.11: hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ʾăhalēkh baʾămittekhā — teach me your way, O LORD, that I may walk in your truth. Hôrēnî (hiphil imperative of yārāh: to teach, direct — the same root as tôrāh) — the request is for ongoing guidance in the divine path, not merely information. yaḥēd lĕbābî lĕyirʾāh shĕmekā — give me an undivided heart to reverence your name. Yāḥad lēbāb (to make the heart one, undivided) — hapax in this form; the undivided heart is the antithesis of double-mindedness (Jas 1:8). The Shema's call to love with the whole heart (Deut 6:5) as a personal petition.
86.12: ʾôdekhā ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhay bĕkhol-lĕbābî — I will thank you, O Lord my God, with my whole heart. Bĕkhol-lĕbābî (with all my heart) — the fulfillment of the petition of v11 (undivided heart): the whole heart now offered in praise. waʾăkhabĕdāh shimĕkhā lĕʿôlām — I will glorify your name forever. The temporal scope extends the personal praise to eternity — the individual's commitment is to permanent, unceasing glorification.
86.13: kî-ḥasdĕkhā gādôl ʿālay — for your steadfast love toward me is great. Gādôl ʿālay (great over/toward me) — the divine ḥesed measured not in abstract but in its personal weight, felt as a presence. wĕhiṣṣaltā nafshî mishshĕʾôl taḥtiyyāh — you have rescued my soul from the lowest depths of Sheol. Taḥtiyyāh (lowest, undermost — the superlative of taḥtî: the lowest level of Sheol) — the deepest pit of the underworld. The rescue is past (perfect: hiṣṣaltā) — the Psalmist testifies to a completed deliverance.
86.14: ʾĕlōhîm zēdîm qāmû-ʿālay — O God, arrogant men have risen up against me. Zēdîm (arrogant ones, presumptuous — those who act with overweening pride against God and neighbor). waʿădat ʿārîṣîm biqqĕshû nafshî — a band of ruthless men seeks my life. ʿĀrîṣ (violent, ruthless, a tyrant). wĕlōʾ śāmûkhā lĕnegdām — they do not keep you before them. The defining sin of the adversary: God is not lĕnegdām (before/opposite them) — they live as practical atheists, with no divine reference point (cf. Ps 10:4; 36:1).
86.15: wĕʾattāh ʾădōnāy ʾēl-raḥûm wĕḥannûn — but you, O Lord, are a compassionate and gracious God. The direct citation of Exod 34:6 — the Mosaic self-revelation of YHWH's character at Sinai. The full formula there is raḥûm wĕḥannûn ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab-ḥesed wĕʾemet (compassionate and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness). Ps 86:5 cited ṭôb wĕsallāḥ wĕrab-ḥesed; now v15 cites the core attributes directly. This Exodus 34 formula is the most-cited divine self-description in the OT (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 103:8; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jon 4:2).
86.16: pĕnēh ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî — turn to me and be gracious to me. The second round of petitions: pānāh (to turn toward — the opposite of the divine hiding-of-face) + ḥānan (to be gracious). tĕnāh ʿuzzĕkhā lĕʿabdekā — give your strength to your servant. ʿŌz (strength, power) as the divine gift: the Psalmist does not already possess what is needed; it must be given. wĕhôshîʿāh lĕben-ʾămātekhā — and save the son of your maidservant. Ben-ʾāmāh (son of the maidservant) — the idiom for the household-born slave, fully belonging to the household from birth. The self-description claims a hereditary membership in YHWH's household.
86.17: ʿăśēh-ʿimmî ʾôt lĕṭôbāh — show me a sign of your favor. ʾôt (sign — a visible marker of divine intention or presence, as in the Exodus signs) requested as confirmation of favor (ṭôbāh: goodness). wĕyirʾû śōnĕʾay wĕyēbōshû — so that those who hate me will see and be ashamed. The enemies' shame is the social reversal that confirms divine favor — not vengeance for its own sake but vindication that makes God's commitment publicly visible. kî-ʾattāh YHWH ʿăzartanî wĕniḥamtanî — because you, O LORD, have helped me and comforted me. The psalm closes with past-tense certainty: the help is already received in the confidence of faith.

87.1: yĕsûdātô bĕharrê qōdesh — his foundation is on the holy mountains. The subject (yĕsûdātô: his foundation, that which he has founded) is initially ambiguous — whose foundation? Context makes it Zion/Jerusalem. Harrê qōdesh (holy mountains — plural) may include all the ridges of the Jerusalem hill system, or may be a superlative plural for the supremely holy mountain. The foundation metaphor establishes permanence: what is founded on mountains does not shift.
87.2: ʾōhēb YHWH shaʿărê ṣiyyôn mikkol mishkĕnôt yaʿăqōb — the LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwelling places of Jacob. ʾĀhēb (loves — the divine love as the basis for Zion's election, as in Ps 78:68). Shaʿărê ṣiyyôn (gates of Zion — the gates as the most public, civic part of the city; what YHWH loves is not just the sanctuary but the whole city in its public life). The comparison with all the other dwelling places of Jacob makes the preference explicit and total.
87.3: nĕkhabbādôt mĕdubbār bākh ʿîr hāʾĕlōhîm selāh — glorious things are spoken of you, O city of God. Nĕkhabbādôt (glorious things — niphal participle of kābad: weighty, honored things, things befitting the divine kābôd) — the oracle about Zion carries the weight of divine glory. ʿÎr hāʾĕlōhîm (city of God) — the title that makes Jerusalem not merely a capital but a theological entity. The selah pause before the astonishing v4.
87.4: ʾazkîr rahab ûbābel lĕyōdĕʿāy — among those who acknowledge me I will name Rahab and Babylon. Rahab (the mythological chaos dragon, used as a poetic name for Egypt, cf. Ps 89:10; Isa 30:7; 51:9) — Egypt and Babylon, the two great enemies of Israel's history, are named in YHWH's register. hinnēh pĕleshet wĕṣōr ʿim-kûsh zeh yullad-shām — behold — Philistia, Tyre, and Ethiopia: this one was born there. Five nations (Egypt, Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, Ethiopia) representing the known world — all its great powers — enrolled in Zion's birth register. Zeh yullad-shām (this one was born there) is the phrase inscribed next to each nation's name, granting them Zion-citizenship regardless of their geographic birth. The OT's boldest universalist vision: spiritual re-birth in Zion that transcends ethnic origin.
87.5: wĕlĕṣiyyôn yēʾāmar ʾîsh wĕʾîsh yullad bāh — of Zion it will be said, 'This one and that one were born in her'. The repeated birth formula (yullad bāh: born in her) applies now to every individual — not just the great nations but each person enrolled. wĕhûʾ yĕkhônĕnehā ʿelyôn — and the Most High himself will establish her. Yĕkhônēn (piel of kûn: to establish, make firm) — divine agency is the source of Zion's permanence; her stability is not political but theological, guaranteed by ʿelyôn (the Most High).
87.6: YHWH yispōr bikhtôb ʿammîm — when the LORD records the enrollment of the peoples. Sāphar (to count, to record, to write in a register) — the divine census-taking. Bikhtôb ʿammîm (in the writing/register of the peoples) — a heavenly population register in which the nations are enrolled. zeh yullad-shām selāh — this one was born there. The registration formula repeated — every enrolled person has the same notation: Zion-born. The selah marks the astonishment of the vision.
87.7: wĕshārîm kĕḥōlĕlîm kol-maʿyānay bākh — singers and dancers alike say, 'All my springs of life are in you'. Maʿyān (spring, source of water — used of living water sources in Song 4:15; Ps 84:6) — Zion as the source of all life's vitality. The springs are plural (maʿyānay: my springs) but all in Zion — all the sources of life converge in the city of God. The psalm closes not with a theological statement but with a personal affirmation of Zion as the totality of one's life-source.

88.1: YHWH ʾĕlōhê yĕshûʿātî — O LORD, God of my salvation. Even in the darkest psalm, the address names God as ʾĕlōhê yĕshûʿātî (God of my salvation) — the title contains the entire hope that the psalm cannot find. yôm-ṣāʿaqtî ballaylāh negdekhā — I cry out to you day and night. Yôm and laylāh together (day and night) — unbroken, continuous prayer. The contrast with v18's conclusion (darkness as companion) is complete: prayer fills both day and night, yet receives no answer presented in the psalm.
88.2: tābôʾ lĕphānêkhā tĕphillātî — let my prayer come before you. Bôʾ lĕphānêkhā (to come before you — to gain audience, to reach the divine presence) — the prayer as a traveler seeking admission to God's court. haṭṭēh ʾoznĕkhā lĕrinnātî — turn your ear toward my cry. Rinnāh (cry, shout — can be of joy or of distress; here clearly the latter) — the ringing cry of acute distress seeking divine attention.
88.3: kî-śābiʿāh bĕrāʿôt nafshî — for my soul is saturated with troubles. Śābaʿ (to be satisfied, sated — used normally of having one's fill of food or good things) here applied ironically to rāʿôt (evils, troubles) — the soul has been filled to capacity with evil, as a container that can take no more. wĕḥayyay lishʾôl higgîʿû — and my life draws near to Sheol. The verb nāgaʿ (to touch, reach) — life has made contact with Sheol's boundary.
88.4: nĕḥshabtî ʿim-yôrĕdê bôr — I am reckoned among those who go down to the pit. Ḥāshab (niphal: to be counted, reckoned among) — the social/legal accounting: the Psalmist has been placed in the category of the dying/dead while still alive. Bôr (pit — the underworld, Sheol's concrete image). hāyîtî kĕgeber ʾên-ʾāyāl — I have become like a man with no strength at all. ʾĀyāl (strength, vigor — from ʾayil: ram, the symbol of physical power) — utterly drained.
88.5: bammētîm ḥophshî — like one cast loose among the dead. Ḥophshî (free, released — the word for a freed slave) — the paradox: among the dead one is free from life's obligations and relationships, but this freedom is the freedom of total abandonment. kĕmô ḥălālîm shōkĕbê qeber ʾăsher lōʾ zĕkhartām ʿôd — like the slain who lie in the grave — those you no longer remember, cut off from your hand. The terrifying clause: those in the grave are those lōʾ zĕkhartām ʿôd (whom you no longer remember). In Hebrew thought, divine memory sustains existence; to be forgotten by God is to cease to exist in any meaningful sense.
88.6: shattanî bĕbôr taḥtiyyôt — you have placed me in the lowest pit. The hiphil of shît (to place, set) — God is explicitly the agent of this placement, not merely an observer. bĕmaḥăshakkîm bimĕṣôlôt — in utter darkness, in the depths below. Maḥăshak (darkness, the dark place — the characteristic condition of Sheol) + mĕṣôlāh (deep place, abyss — used of the depths of the sea in Exod 15:5; Mic 7:19). The imagery of pit + darkness + depths combines all Sheol metaphors.
88.7: ʿālay sāmĕkhāh ḥămātekhā — your wrath presses down hard on me. Sāmak (qal: to lean upon, press down — the same verb used of laying hands on a sacrifice; here God's wrath is the weight pressing down on the Psalmist). wĕkhol-mishbārêkhā ʿinnîtā selāh — and you have battered me with all your waves. Mishbār (breaker, crashing wave — from shābar: to break) — the same word as the breakers of the sea (Ps 42:7; 93:3). Divine wrath experienced as a battering sea. The selah marks a moment of overwhelming weight.
88.8: hirḥaqtā mĕyuddaʿay mimmennî — you have pushed my companions far away from me. The hiphil of rāḥaq (to remove to a distance) — God as the agent of social isolation. shattanî tôʿēbôt lāmô — you have made me repulsive to them. Tôʿēbāh (abomination, something repulsive) — the Psalmist has become the thing his companions find morally/ritually unacceptable. kāluʾ wĕlōʾ ʾēṣēʾ — I am sealed in and cannot get out. Kālaʾ (niphal: to be shut up, imprisoned) — trapped in both the isolation of illness and the approaching tomb.
88.9: ʿênî dāʾăbāh minnî ʿōnî — my eye wastes away with grief. Dāʾab (to pine, waste away — the slow, sustained wasting of grief, not the sudden shock of acute loss). qĕrāʾtîkhā YHWH kol-hayyôm shēṭaḥtî ʾēlêkhā khappāy — O LORD, I call to you every day; I spread out my hands toward you. The spread hands (kappayim: palms) in supplication — the physical gesture of prayer, maintained despite receiving no visible answer. The persistence of prayer against the silence is itself an act of faith.
88.10: hălammētîm taʿăśeh pheleʾ — do you perform wonders for the dead? The series of questions in vv10-12 are not truly interrogative — they expect the answer 'no' in Sheol-theology, but the question is whether YHWH might break the rule for the Psalmist. Pheleʾ (wonder — the Exodus miracle word) applied to death: can the God who works wonders in history also work them in death? ʾim-rĕphāʾîm yāqûmû yôdûkhā selāh — can the departed arise and praise you? Rĕphāʾîm (the departed spirits, the shades of the dead — a term for Sheol's inhabitants). The selah marks the unanswerable weight of the question.
88.11: hăyĕsuppār baqqeber ḥasdekā — is your steadfast love announced in the grave? The ḥesed that the Psalmist knows from experience — is it communicated in the grave? ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bāʾăbadon — is your faithfulness spoken of in Abaddon? ʾĂbadon (destruction, the realm of the dead — from ʾābad: to perish; appears in Job 26:6; 28:22; Prov 15:11; 27:20; Rev 9:11 as Apollyon). The question pairs ḥesed and ʾĕmûnāh (steadfast love and faithfulness) — the two great covenant attributes — against Sheol and Abaddon.
88.12: hăyiwwādaʿ baḥōshek pilʾekā — will your wonders be known in the darkness? Ḥōshek (darkness — the characteristic condition of Sheol, as in v6). wĕṣidqātĕkhā bĕʾereṣ nĕshiyyāh — will your righteousness be heard of in the land of forgetting? ʾEreṣ nĕshiyyāh (the land of forgetting) — a unique name for Sheol found only here in the OT. Nĕshiyyāh (from shākah: to forget) — Sheol as the place where memory is lost, where the dead forget and are forgotten. The cosmic geography of oblivion.
88.13: waʾănî ʾēlêkhā YHWH shiwwaʿtî — but I, O LORD, cry out to you. The contrast particle waʾănî (but I) — against all the rhetorical questions (which expect the answer: no, God does not act in Sheol), the Psalmist continues to pray. ûbaboqer tĕphillātî tĕqaddĕmekā — in the morning my prayer comes before you. Qāddam (to come before, to meet — to intercept) — the morning prayer rushing ahead to meet God before the day's silence descends again. The morning prayer as the daily resumption of hope.
88.14: lāmāh YHWH tiznaḥ nafshî — O LORD, why do you reject my soul? Zānaḥ (to reject, cast off — the communal lament verb of Ps 74:1; 79:5) applied to the individual soul. tastîr pānêkhā mimmennî — why do you hide your face from me? The two great divine absences compressed into one verse: rejection (zānaḥ) and hidden face (hester pānim). These are the standard vocabulary of divine abandonment — the Psalmist names what is happening with theological precision.
88.15: ʿānî waggōwēaʿ minnōʿar — I have been afflicted and near death since my youth. The duration of the suffering is not a recent crisis but a lifelong condition. nāśāʾtî ʾêmêkhā ʾāphûnāh — I suffer your terrors; I am overcome. ʾêmāh (terror, dread — the overwhelming fear produced by the divine presence in its terrifying aspect). ʾāphûnāh (I am overcome, I am helpless — possibly from phûg: to grow numb, be powerless) — the terror has left him paralyzed.
88.16: ʿālay ʿābĕrû ḥărônêkhā — your fierce wrath has swept over me. ʿĀbar (to pass over — as a flood sweeps over its banks; the divine anger as an inundating force). biʿûtêkhā ṣimmĕtûnî — your terrors have destroyed me. Biʿûtîm (terrors — from bāʿat: to terrify, overwhelm; the same root as the Psalmist's paralysis in v15). Ṣāmat (to put an end to, silence, destroy — used of the destruction of the wicked in Ps 18:40; 94:23) — now applied to the righteous sufferer.
88.17: ʾibbĕnî khammayim kol-hayyôm — they surround me like water all day long. The terrors personified as surrounding waters — the drowning metaphor that the sea-imagery of v7 anticipates. hiqqîpû ʿālay yaḥad — they close in on me together. Nāqaph (to close in, encircle — used of encircling enemies, Job 1:5; Isa 29:1) — a coordinated encirclement with no exit point.
88.18: hirḥaqtā mimmennî ʾōhēb wārēaʿ — you have put far from me those who love me and my friends. The complete desolation of relationship: ʾōhēb (lover, one who loves) and rēaʿ (friend, companion) both removed by divine action. mĕyuddaʿay maḥshākh — my companions are in darkness. The final words of the psalm. Unlike every other lament psalm, Psalm 88 ends without resolution, without a vow of praise, without a turn to confidence. The only companion remaining is darkness (maḥshākh). This is the Psalter's record of the experience of unresolved suffering — a permission to name what is, without forcing a liturgical resolution that has not come.

89.1: ḥasdê YHWH lĕʿôlām ʾāshîrāh — I will sing of the LORD's steadfast love forever. The opening declaration sets ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) as the psalm's governing word — it appears more than a dozen times across the 52 verses. lĕdōr wādōr ʾôdîaʿ ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bĕphî — with my mouth I will proclaim your faithfulness to every generation. The double temporal scope (forever + every generation) frames what becomes in vv38-51 an agonized question: where is the ḥesed that was proclaimed?
89.2: kî-ʾāmartî ʿôlām ḥesed yibbāneh — for I declared, your steadfast love is built to stand forever. Yibbāneh (niphal of bānāh: to be built, to be established — the passive form: it is being built, is under construction that will never stop) — ḥesed as an edifice God is perpetually constructing. shāmayim tākhîn ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bāhem — your faithfulness is established in the heavens themselves. Tākhîn (hiphil of kûn: to establish firmly) — the divine faithfulness as permanent as the heavens: what the heavens are to the physical cosmos, faithfulness is to the moral cosmos.
89.3: kārattî berît libbĕḥîrî — I have made a covenant with my chosen one. The divine speech begins (vv3-4) — God quotes himself in the context of the Davidic covenant. Bāḥar (to choose — the election verb of Deuteronomy; David chosen as YHWH chose Israel). nishbaʿtî lĕdāwid ʿabdî — I have sworn an oath to David my servant. The covenant is secured by divine oath — not merely promise but sworn commitment, which makes it the most binding form of divine communication (cf. Ps 89:35; Heb 6:17-18).
89.4: ʿad-ʿôlām ʾăkhîn zarʿekā — I will make your line endure forever. Zāraʿ (seed, offspring, line — the Abrahamic promise vocabulary of Gen 12:7; 13:15) applied to David: the Davidic covenant inherits the Abrahamic seed-promise. ûbānîtî lĕdōr-wādōr kissĕʾăkhā selāh — and build your throne for every generation to come. Bānāh (to build) — the same verb as v2's ḥesed yibbāneh: the steadfast love is built by building the throne. The selah marks the weight of the oath just recited.
89.5: wĕyôdû shāmayim pilʾĕkhā YHWH — the heavens praise your wonders, O LORD. From covenant declaration (vv3-4) to cosmic worship (vv5-7). Pilʾĕkhā (your wonders — the Exodus word, now applied to heavenly wonder-works). ʾap-ʾĕmûnātĕkhā biqhal qĕdōshîm — your faithfulness is declared in the congregation of the holy ones. Qĕhal qĕdōshîm (the congregation/assembly of the holy ones — the heavenly council of divine beings) — the faithfulness proclaimed on earth (v1-2) is simultaneously declared in the heavenly realm.
89.6: kî mî bashshaḥaq yaʿărōkh laYHWH — for who in all the heavens can be compared with the LORD? Shaḥaq (the thin upper air, the clouds — the highest stratum of the visible sky, where the divine beings dwell). yidmeh laYHWH bibnê ʾēlîm — who among the heavenly beings is like the LORD? Bĕnê ʾēlîm (sons of God/divine beings — the heavenly council members, cf. Ps 29:1; 82:1). The incomparability formula of v8 (= Ps 86:8) applied to the heavenly council: YHWH is incomparable even among divine beings.
89.7: ʾêl naʿărāṣ bĕsôd-qĕdōshîm rabbāh — a God greatly feared in the assembly of the holy ones. Naʿărāṣ (niphal of yārēʾ: held in awe, feared — the passive: one before whom awe is experienced). wĕnôrāʾ ʿal-kol-sĕbîbāyw — and awesome above all who surround him. The council members who surround YHWH (as in Isa 6's seraphim, or the council of 1 Kgs 22:19-23) experience awe in his presence — divine beings awed by the one they serve.
89.8: YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt mî-khāmôkhā ḥăsîn YAH — LORD God of hosts, who is as powerful as you, LORD? ʾĔlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt (God of hosts — the military title of divine sovereignty over all heavenly and earthly armies). Ḥāsîn (mighty, strong — hapax in this form; the superlative of divine power). wĕʾĕmûnātĕkhā sĕbîbôtekhā — your faithfulness wraps around you on every side. Divine faithfulness not merely as an attribute but as a surrounding environment — ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/reliability) as the atmosphere that surrounds the divine throne.
89.9: ʾattāh môshēl bĕgeʾût hayyām — you govern the surging sea. Gēʾûth (surging, swelling — the proud rising of waves, from gāʾāh: to be exalted, to surge) — the sea in its most threatening aspect. bĕśôʾ gālāyw ʾattāh tĕshabbĕḥēm — when its waves rear up, you calm them. Shābah (piel: to calm, still — used of Jesus's stilling of the storm in Mark 4:39, perhaps deliberately). YHWH's mastery over the chaos-sea continues the creation-battle theology of Ps 74:13-14.
89.10: ʾattāh dikkîtāh kĕḥālāl rāhab — you crushed Rahab like a slaughtered carcass. Rāhab (the chaos dragon — here as a name for Egypt, as in Ps 87:4; Isa 30:7; 51:9) crushed (dākhāh: to crush, break down) like a ḥālāl (one slain, a corpse). bizrôaʿ ʿuzzĕkhā pizzartā ʾôyĕbêkhā — with your mighty arm you scattered your enemies. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) — the Exodus arm, now pictured scattering the chaos-forces as it scattered Pharaoh's army.
89.11: lĕkhā shāmayim ʾaf-lĕkhā ʾāreṣ — the heavens belong to you; the earth also is yours. The creation claim: heavens and earth are YHWH's by right of creation, not by conquest. tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh ʾattāh yĕsadtām — you founded the world and everything in it. Tēbēl (the inhabited world) + mĕlōʾāh (its fullness — everything filling it). The double claim grounds every subsequent assertion of YHWH's sovereignty.
89.12: ṣāphôn wĕyāmîn ʾattāh bĕrāʾtām — north and south — you created them. All four compass directions implied by the pairing of north (ṣāphôn) and south (yāmîn: literally, the right hand — south). tābôr wĕḥermôn bĕshimĕkhā yĕrannēnû — Tabor and Hermon sing for joy in your name.
