The two great mountains — Tabor in the west (the hill of Jezreel valley) and Hermon in the far northeast — as personified worshipers, representing the entire land in its geographic extent singing to its Creator.
89.13: lĕkhā zĕrôaʿ ʿim-gĕbûrāh — your arm is mighty. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) returns — consistently the emblem of Exodus power. tāʿōz yādĕkhā tārûm yĕmînekhā — your hand is strong; your right hand is raised high. The three body-parts of divine power: arm, hand, right hand. All three in elevated, active position — the warrior's ready posture.
89.14: ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ mĕkhôn kissĕʾekā — righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne. Mĕkhôn (foundation, established base — from kûn: to be fixed, established) — the divine throne rests on ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ (righteousness and justice): these are not policies God implements but the structural support of his rule. ḥesed wĕʾemet yĕqaddĕmû phānêkhā — steadfast love and faithfulness lead the way before you. Qāddam pānim (to go before the face) — ḥesed and ʾemet as heralds preceding the divine king. Four divine attributes together form the cosmic government architecture.
89.15: ʾashrê hāʿām yôdĕʿê tĕrûʿāh — blessed is the people who know the joyful shout. Tĕrûʿāh (the battle cry, the festive shout, the sound of the shophar — used of the war cry in Josh 6:5,20, of the worship-shout in Ezra 3:12-13, and of the shophar blast of the new moon festival) — those who know YHWH's war-shout/worship-cry are blessed. YHWH bĕʾôr-pānêkhā yĕhallēkhûn — they walk, LORD, in the light of your face. ʾôr pānîm (the light of the face — the Aaronic blessing's yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēlêkhā, Num 6:25) — the people who know the divine shout walk in the light of divine favor.
89.16: bĕshimĕkhā yĕgîlûn kol-hayyôm — they rejoice in your name all day long. Gîl (to rejoice — the active, physical joy of dancing; more bodily than śāmaḥ) in the divine name all day long. ûbĕṣidqātĕkhā yārûmû — and are lifted up by your righteousness. The divine righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh) as the lifting force — the blessed people are elevated not by their own merit but by God's righteous intervention.
89.17: kî-tiphʾeret ʿuzzāmô ʾāttāh — for you are the splendor of their strength. Tiphʾeret (splendor, glory, beauty) — the beauty that makes their strength beautiful comes from God. ûbirṣônĕkhā tārûm qarnênû — by your favor our strength is raised high. Rāṣôn (favor, goodwill — the divine favor as the agent of horn-exaltation; cf. Ps 75's horn imagery). The community's strength is raised by divine favor, not by military capability.
89.18: kî laYHWH māginnênû — for our shield belongs to the LORD. Māgēn (shield — the small hand-shield, the personal protection) — Israel's protective shield is YHWH's property, not their own. wĕliqĕdôsh yisrāʾêl malkênû — our king is the Holy One of Israel. Qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl (Holy One of Israel — the Isaianic epithet, as in Pss 71:22; 78:41) — the king of Israel is not a human sovereign but the Holy One. This sets up the Davidic oracle: the human king of vv19-37 is the vice-regent of this divine king.
89.19: ʾāz dibbartā bĕḥāzôn laḥăsîdêkhā — you spoke in a vision to your faithful servants. Ḥāzôn (vision — the prophetic vision in which God's word comes; cf. 2 Sam 7's Nathan oracle which is the background for this Psalm). wayyōʾmer shiwwîtî ʿēzer ʿal-gibbôr — and said, I have given help to a warrior. Gîbbôr (warrior, mighty man) — David the fighting man. The divine help precedes the human achievement; military prowess is sustained from above.
89.20: māṣāʾtî dāwid ʿabdî — I have found David my servant. The election formula māṣāʾ (to find — not the result of a search but of an encounter: God found David as one finds an unexpected treasure) + ʿabdî (my servant — the highest honor title in the OT, borne by Moses, Abraham, the Prophets, and here David). bĕshemen qodshî māshaḥtîhû — with my holy oil I have anointed him. The anointing with shemen qōdesh (holy oil — the sacred anointing oil of Exod 30:22-32) — the physical act that transferred divine Spirit and commission.
89.21: ʾăsher yādî tikkôn ʿimmô — my hand will uphold him steadily. Kûn (hiphil: to make firm, establish steadily) — the divine hand as a steadying support, not merely an occasional intervention. ʾap-zĕrōʿî tĕʾammĕṣennû — my arm will give him strength. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) channeling strength into the king — David's military power is the Exodus arm directed through a human king.
89.22: lōʾ-yashîʾ ʿālaw ʾôyēb — no enemy will overpower him. Naśāʾ (hiphil: to exact tribute from, to oppress, to overpower) — no enemy will be able to extract what they want from him. ûben-ʿawlāh lōʾ yĕʿannennû — no wicked person will oppress him. The double negative of vv22-23 establishes the invulnerability of the anointed: both external enemies and internal troublemakers are blocked from oppressing the one God upholds.
89.23: wĕkhattôtî miqqĕdāmāyw ṣārāyw — I will shatter his enemies before his eyes. The hiphil of kāthat (to crush, pound — the mortar-and-pestle violence of crushing) — God acts as the warrior whose crushing power precedes the king into battle. ûmĕśanʾāyw ʾĕgōp — and defeat all who hate him. Nāgaph (hiphil: to strike, beat, defeat in battle — the plague verb of Exod 12:27 now applied to David's enemies).
89.24: wĕʾĕmûnātî wĕḥasdî ʿimmô — my faithfulness and steadfast love will be with him. The two covenant attributes (ʾĕmûnāh and ḥesed) travel with David as divine companions — they are not merely qualities God has but presences God sends with his anointed. ûbishmî tārûm qarnô — through my name his strength will be raised high. Horn-exaltation (cf. Ps 75:10; 89:17) through the divine name — the king's power is named, attributed, and sustained by YHWH's own reputation.
89.25: wĕśamtî bayyām yādô — I will extend his dominion to the sea. Yādô (his hand — as a symbol of rule and sovereignty) placed in the sea: the king's authority reaching to the horizon. ûbannĕhārôt yĕmînô — and his right hand over the rivers. The pairing of sea and rivers as the boundaries of universal dominion echoes Ps 72:8's Messianic scope. What Ps 72 prays for the ideal king, Ps 89 establishes as divine covenant promise.
89.26: hûʾ yiqrāʾēnî ʾābî ʾāttāh — he will call out to me, 'You are my Father'. ʾĀbî ʾāttāh (my Father, you) — the most intimate address. The Davidic king adopts the filial relationship with YHWH. This royal father-son language is the background for the NT's Christological use of divine sonship: what is true of the Davidic king in a representative sense is true of Jesus in its full ontological sense. ʾēlî wĕṣûr yĕshûʿātî — my God, and the Rock who saves me. The two titles from the Psalter's own vocabulary of trust (God + Rock of salvation) placed in the mouth of the anointed king.
89.27: ʾap-ʾănî bĕkhôr ʾettĕnēhû — I will appoint him as my firstborn. Bĕkhôr (firstborn — the one with the double-portion inheritance, the primary heir, the one who stands first before God). The Davidic king is to have the status of firstborn before YHWH — which Israel as a whole was called (Exod 4:22: bĕnî bĕkhōrî yisrāʾêl). ʿelyôn lĕmalkê-ʾāreṣ — the highest of all the kings of the earth. ʿElyôn (the highest) — the same word used of God as Most High now applied to the Davidic king's status among earthly kings.
89.28: lĕʿôlām ʾeshmar-lô ḥasdî — I will maintain my steadfast love for him forever. Shāmar ḥesed (to keep/maintain steadfast love — the covenant-keeping idiom) with lĕʿôlām (forever) — the duration of the ḥesed matches the duration of the throne (v4: forever). ûberîtî neʾĕmenet lô — and my covenant with him will be secure. Neʾĕmenet (niphal participle of ʾāman: reliable, faithful, established) — the covenant is structurally sound, not subject to failure from God's side.
89.29: wĕśamtî lāʿad zarʿô — his line will continue forever. Lāʿad (for ever, for the long duration) — the seed/line (zāraʿ) enduring through time. wĕkhissĕʾô kîmê shāmāyim — and his throne will last as long as the heavens above. Kîmê shāmāyim (like the days of the heavens) — the heavens as the measuring rod of the throne's duration. What endures as long as the physical universe endures does not end within history.
89.30: ʾim-yaʿăzĕbû bānāyw tôrātî — if his descendants abandon my instruction. The conditional clauses (vv30-32) are the covenant's discipline provisions — comparable to the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy. The ḥesed is unconditional (v28); the temporal experience of covenant blessing is conditional on obedience. ûbĕmishpāṭay lōʾ yēlēkhû — and refuse to live by my rulings. The two covenant-obligations: Torah and mišpāṭ (rulings, decisions — the case-law applications of Torah).
89.31: ʾim-ḥuqqōtay yĕḥallēlû — if they break my decrees. Ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal: to profane, to treat as common what is sacred — the same word as the profaning of the temple in Ps 74:7). wĕmiṣwōtay lōʾ yishmōrû — and fail to keep my commandments. The four terms across vv30-31 (tôrāh, mišpāṭ, ḥuqqôt, miṣwōt) cover the full vocabulary of Mosaic covenant stipulations — the discipline clauses are Deuteronomically comprehensive.
89.32: wĕphāqadtî bĕshebeṭ pishʿām — then I will punish their rebellion with a rod. Pāqad (to visit, to reckon with — the divine accounting verb used of both blessing and judgment). Shebeṭ (rod — the shepherd's staff used for discipline, cf. Ps 2:9; 2 Sam 7:14: God will be a father to David and discipline with a human rod). ûbingāʿîm ʿăwōnām — and their sin with blows. Negaʿ (blow, stroke, plague — the word for divine affliction that runs from Exod 11:1 through Ps 38:11) — the discipline is painful but not fatal to the covenant.
89.33: wĕḥasdî lōʾ-ʾāphîr mĕʿimmô — but I will not withdraw my steadfast love from him. ʾĀphar (hiphil: to break off, dissolve, annul) — the ḥesed cannot be dissolved. wĕlōʾ-ʾashqer bĕʾĕmûnātî — or betray my faithfulness. Shāqar (to deal falsely, to betray a trust) — negated: YHWH will not be false to his faithfulness. The divine attributes cannot be separated from divine action; what God is structurally, he does historically.
89.34: lōʾ-ʾăḥallēl bĕrîtî — I will not break my covenant. Ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal: to profane, to treat as common) — the same verb as the Davidic descendants' possible profaning of the statutes (v31). God will not do to his own covenant what the rebellious descendants might do to his statutes. ûmôṣāʾ śĕphātay lōʾ ʾăshanneh — or change what I have spoken. Môṣāʾ śĕphātayim (what goes out from the lips) — the spoken word once uttered is binding; the divine speech cannot be recalled or revised.
89.35: ʾaḥat nishbaʿtî bĕqodshî — I have sworn by my own holiness once and for all. ʾAḥat (once — the single, unrepeatable oath) + bĕqodshî (by my holiness) — God swears by his own holiness because there is nothing higher by which to swear (Heb 6:13). The holiness of God is the ultimate guarantee: if the oath fails, the divine holiness itself would be compromised. ʾim-lĕdāwid ʾăkhazzēb — I will not deceive David. Kāzab (to lie, deceive) negated — the oath sworn on divine holiness cannot deceive.
89.36: zarʿô lĕʿôlām yihyeh — his descendants will endure forever. The seed (zāraʿ) given permanence parallel to the throne. wĕkhissĕʾô kashshemesh negdî — his throne will last as long as the sun shines before me. Negdî (before me — in my sight, under my watching eye). The sun as the standard of permanence: the throne endures as long as the sun in YHWH's sight.
89.37: kĕyārēaḥ yikkôn lĕʿôlām — like the moon it will be established forever. The moon as the second celestial witness to permanence — the two great lights of Gen 1:16 both testify to the covenant's duration. wĕʿēd bashshaḥaq neʾĕmān selāh — that faithful witness set in the heavens. ʿĒd neʾĕmān (faithful witness) — the moon itself as a reliable witness to the covenant. The selah marks the theological climax of the covenant celebration — immediately followed by the devastating reversal of v38.
89.38: wĕʾattāh zānachtā wattimʾās — but now you have cast off and rejected him. The abrupt turn: wĕʾattāh (but you — the same emphatic pronoun as Ps 73:23's pivot, now in reverse direction). Zānaḥ (to cast off — Ps 74:1; 79:5's lament word) + māʾas (to reject) — the two rejection verbs together. hitʾabbartā ʿim-mĕshîḥekā — you are enraged against your anointed king. Mĕshîaḥ (the anointed one — the specific Davidic title; the Messiah). The psalm's entire covenant celebration becomes the indictment: the God who swore that oath on his holiness appears to have broken it.
89.39: niʾartā berît ʿabdekā — you have repudiated the covenant with your servant. Nāʾar (piel: to spurn, reject with contempt — to shake off as one shakes off something disgusting) — applied to the covenant. ḥillaltā lāʾāreṣ nizrô — you have thrown his crown into the dust. Ḥillēl (piel: to profane) — the crown (nāzar: the diadem, the consecrated crown) thrown to the ground. The divine holiness-oath of v35 is implicitly invoked: if God profanes the covenant and the crown, his own holiness is at stake.
89.40: pāraṣtā kol-gĕdērōtāyw — you have broken down all his walls. Pāraṣ (to break through, make a breach) — the defensive walls of the kingdom breached. śamtā miḇṣārāyw meḥittāh — and reduced his fortifications to rubble. Miḇṣār (fortification, stronghold) — the military infrastructure of the kingdom made into meḥittāh (terror, ruin — what causes fright).
89.41: shāsahû kol-ʿōbĕrê dārekh — all who pass by strip him of what he has. Shāsāh (to plunder, strip bare) — the king whose throne was sworn to last as long as the heavens (v29) is now stripped by passersby. hāyāh ḥerpāh lishkhēnāyw — he has become a mockery to his neighbors. Ḥerpāh (reproach, mockery — the shame vocabulary of Ps 79:4 at the destruction of Jerusalem). The king who should have been the highest of earthly kings (v27) is a mockery to neighbors.
89.42: hărîmōtā yĕmîn ṣārāyw — you have strengthened the hand of his enemies. The right hand of the enemies raised — the same right hand that God stretches out in salvation (v13) is here raised in the enemy's favor. hiśśaḥtā kol-śōnĕʾāyw — you have let all who hate him celebrate. Śāmaḥ (to rejoice) in the enemies — the joy that should belong to Israel (vv16) belongs to those who hate.
89.43: ʾap-tāshîb ṣûr ḥarbô — you have blunted his sword in battle. Shûb ṣûr ḥereb (to turn back the edge of the sword — to make it dull, to reverse its cutting power). wĕlōʾ hăqēmōtô bammilḥāmāh — and failed to hold him up when he fought. Qûm (hiphil: to establish, support) — God who upheld the Davidic king (v21: hand steadily upholding) has not upheld him in battle.
89.44: hishbattā miṭṭohŏrô — you have stripped away his glory. Ṭohar (purity, brightness, splendor) — the king's royal radiance removed. wĕkhissĕʾô lāʾāreṣ miggaretāh — and hurled his throne down to the ground. The throne sworn to stand as long as the heavens (v29) hurled to the ground — the contrast with the covenant's permanence is complete and devastating.
89.45: hiqṣartā yĕmê ʿălûmāyw — you have shortened the days of his prime. ʿĂlûmîm (youth, prime years — from ʿālam: young manhood, the vigorous years) — the king's best years cut short. heʿaṭîtāhû bûshāh selāh — and wrapped him in shame. Heʿāṭāh (to wrap, to clothe) — shame (bôshāh) worn as a garment (cf. Ps 73:6's pride-as-necklace). The selah marks the nadir of the catalog of disasters.
89.46: ʿad-māh YHWH tistātēr lāneṣaḥ — how long, O LORD? Will you hide yourself away forever? The ʿad-māh (how long?) of communal lament (Ps 74:10; 79:5) applied to the Davidic crisis. tiʿar kāʾesh ḥămātekā — how long will your anger blaze like fire? The divine anger as fire — the same image as Ps 79:5's jealousy burning. The question is not why God is angry but how long the anger can continue given the oath of v35.
89.47: zĕkōr-ʾănî māh-ḥāled — remember how short my span of life is! Ḥeled (span of life, lifetime — the brief duration of human existence; cf. Ps 39:5-6). The human-transience argument: God must act now because the Psalmist's life is short. ʿal-māh shāwĕʾ bārāʾtā kol-bĕnê-ʾādām — for what emptiness you have made all the children of man! Shāwĕʾ (emptiness, vanity — the Qoheleth word hebel in a different form) — if the covenant fails, human existence under it is vanity.
89.48: mî geber yiḥyeh wĕlōʾ yirʾeh māwet — what man can go on living and never see death? The universal human condition invoked as pressure: every human dies, so the anointed king under the covenant needs divine action before his death concludes the opportunity. yĕmalleṭ nafshô miyyad-shĕʾôl selāh — who can rescue his life from the grip of the grave? Shĕʾôl (the underworld) — from whose hand no human rescue is possible (Ps 49:7-9). The selah marks the unanswerable weight of mortality.
89.49: ʾayyēh ḥăsādêkhā hāriʾshōnîm ʾădōnāy — Lord, where is your steadfast love of old? The key appeal of the lament section: ḥăsādîm (the plural of ḥesed — the accumulated acts of steadfast love from ancient times, hāriʾshōnîm: the former ones). The plural ḥăsādîm includes all the covenant actions from the Exodus through David. nishbaʿtā lĕdāwid baʾĕmûnatekā — which in your faithfulness you swore to David? The oath of v35 invoked directly — the ʾĕmûnāh that was sworn is the basis for the appeal.
89.50: zĕkōr ʾădōnāy ḥerpat ʿăbādêkhā — remember, Lord, the mocking your servants endure. Zākar (to remember — the covenant memory-appeal) directed toward the mocking (ḥerpāh) of the divine servants. śĕʾētî bĕḥêqî kol-rabbîm ʿammîm — how I bear in my heart the taunts of all those nations. ḥêq (bosom, lap — what one carries close to oneself) — the Psalmist carries the nations' taunts in his own heart as a personal burden.
89.51: ʾăsher ḥērĕphû ʾôyĕbêkhā YHWH — with which your enemies, LORD, have taunted. The enemies of the Davidic king identified as enemies of YHWH — the attack on the anointed is an attack on the one who anointed him. ʾăsher ḥērĕphû ʿîqĕbôt mĕshîḥekā — mocking every step of your anointed king. ʿîqĕbôt (steps, footprints — every step the anointed takes is mocked). The psalm ends with the anointed king walking under constant derision — the lament is complete, without resolution, holding the tension between the oath of v35 and the reality of vv38-51.
89.52: bārûkh YHWH lĕʿôlām ʾāmēn wĕʾāmēn — blessed be the LORD forever! Amen and Amen. The doxology closing Book III of the Psalter (Pss 73-89) — identical in function to the doxologies of Books I (Ps 41:13), II (Ps 72:18-19), IV (Ps 106:48), and V (Ps 150). The double ʾāmēn (so be it — the congregational affirmation of what has been prayed) closes not the lament of Ps 89 but the entire third book. The lament of Ps 89 is not resolved within the psalm; the Book III doxology does not resolve it either — it holds the tension open for the entire Psalter to address in Books IV-V.

90.1: tĕphillāh lĕmōsheh ʾîsh hāʾĕlōhîm — A Prayer of Moses, the man of God. The attribution to Moses makes Psalm 90 unique — the only Mosaic psalm in the Psalter. ʾÎsh hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God) is the title for prophets who speak God's word (Deut 33:1; 1 Kgs 13:1; 2 Kgs 4:7). ʾădōnāy māʿôn ʾattāh hāyîtā llānû bĕdōr wādōr — Lord, you have been our dwelling place in every generation. Māʿôn (home, habitation, dwelling place — the permanent residence of God as Israel's refuge across all generations; cf. Deut 33:27: mĕʿônāh ʾĕlōhê qedem, the eternal God is your dwelling). The psalm anchors the fleeting human life (vv3-10) against the eternal divine habitation.
90.2: bĕṭerem hārîm yullādû — before the mountains were born. Yālad (to bear, give birth) applied to the mountains — the creation of the mountains described as a birth event, personifying the earth's formation as parturition. wattĕḥôlēl ʾereṣ wĕtēbēl — or you had formed the earth and the world. Ḥûl (polel: to writhe in labor, bring forth in birth-pangs) — earth and inhabited world brought forth in a cosmic labor. ûmēʿôlām ʿad-ʿôlām ʾattāh ʾēl — from age to age you are God. The frame: creation events (mountains, earth) bracket the eternal divine being. God predates his own creation — the pre-creation God who will outlast it.
90.3: tāshēb ʾĕnôsh ʿad-dakkāʾ — you turn human beings back to dust. Dakkāʾ (a crushed thing, powder, dust — from dākāʾ: to crush) — humanity returned to the crushed, pulverized state of pre-formation. The reversal of the creation-act of Gen 2:7 (wayyîṣer YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hāʾādām ʿāpār: from dust taken, to dust returned, Gen 3:19). wattōʾmer shûbû bĕnê-ʾādām — and you say, 'Return, O children of Adam!' The divine word that reverses creation — shûbû (return) being the opposite of the forming act. Bĕnê-ʾādām (sons of Adam/humanity) — the collective solidarity of all human beings with the first human who was returned to dust.
90.4: kî ʾelep shānîm bĕʿênêkhā kĕyôm ʾetmôl kî yaʿăbōr — for a thousand years in your sight are like yesterday after it has passed. The divine perspective on time: a thousand years (ʾelep shānîm) as yesterday in retrospect (ʾetmôl kî yaʿăbōr: yesterday after it has gone by — i.e., past and therefore experienced as short). wĕʾashmûrāh balllāylāh — or like a single watch in the night. ʾAshm ûrāh (a watch — the ancient division of the night into three or four watches, each 3-4 hours). A thousand years equals the briefest human unit of experienced time — not that time is unreal but that the divine relationship to it is utterly other (cf. 2 Pet 3:8's use of this verse).
90.5: zĕramtām shēnāh yihyû — you sweep them away like a flood; they are like a dream. Zāram (to pour out in a torrent, sweep away — used of a rainstorm-flood; the sudden overwhelming inundation). Shēnāh (sleep) — human generations like a dream that dissolves on waking; no substance persisting after the divine gaze. baqqōqer kĕḥāṣîr yaḥălōph — in the morning they are like grass that sprouts. ḥāṣîr (grass, fresh vegetation — the type of rapid, ephemeral growth) that pushes up in the morning dew. The grass metaphor of Isa 40:6-8: all flesh is grass.
90.6: baqqōqer yāṣîṣ wĕḥālāph — in the morning it blossoms and shoots up. Yāṣîṣ (to blossom, flourish — the brief flowering of the grass) + ḥālap (to pass on, to shoot up quickly). lāʿereb yĕmôlēl wĕyābēsh — by evening it is mown down and withers. Mālal (polal: to be cut off, mowed down) + yābēsh (to dry up, wither) — the complete life-cycle of grass compressed into a single day: morning blooming to evening withering. The same imagery in Isa 40:7-8 applied to nations; in Ps 90 applied to human generations.
90.7: kî-khālînû bĕʾappekhā — for we are brought to an end by your anger. Kālāh (niphal: to be finished, brought to completion/end) — the divine anger as the agent that brings human existence to its terminus. ûbăḥămātĕkhā nibbhālnû — by your fury we are overwhelmed/terrified. Bāhal (niphal: to be dismayed, terrified, overwhelmed — the panic of sudden shock). The pairing of ʾap (anger, the flared nostril) and ḥēmāh (fury, hot wrath) — the two Hebrew terms for divine anger in increasing intensity.
90.8: shattāh ʿăwōnōtênû lĕnegdekhā — you have placed our sins before you. Shît lĕneged (to set before, display in plain sight) — the divine perspective sees what is hidden to others: the sins are placed lĕnegedekhā (before your face, in your presence). ʿălûmēnû lîʾôr pānêkhā — our hidden faults in the full light of your presence. ʿAlûm (hidden thing, secret fault — from ʿālam: to conceal) — what is concealed from human sight is exposed in the divine light (ʾôr pānêkhā: the light of your face). The divine omniscience as the context for human mortality.
90.9: kî khol-yāmênû pānû bĕʿebrātekhā — all our days slip away under your wrath. Pānāh (to turn away, to pass away — as a day turns and is gone). killînû shānênû kĕmô-hegeh — we bring our years to an end like a sigh. Hegeh (a sound, murmur, groan — from hāgāh: to murmur, meditate; the low sound of sighing, or possibly the growl of a lion, or the cooing of a dove — the onomatopoeic word for a brief, soft sound). Human years brought to an end with a murmur — not a shout but a barely audible exhalation. The restraint of the image makes mortality more, not less, poignant.
90.10: yĕmê-shĕnôtênû bāhem shĭbʿîm shānāh — the span of our years is seventy. The first statement of a human lifespan as a defined quantity — shĭbʿîm shānāh (70 years) + wĕʾim bigĕbûrōt shĕmônîm shānāh (and if strong, 80 years). These are not averages but ideals — the maximum ordinarily achievable without special divine extension. wĕrāhbām ʿāmāl wāʾāwen — but even then they contain only toil and grief. ʿĀmāl (toil, trouble, grievous labor) + ʾāwen (trouble, iniquity, emptiness) — the substance of the extended years. kî-gāz ḥîsh wannaʿûphāh — for they pass quickly and we fly away. Gāz (to cut off, pass quickly — the rapid passing); ʿûph (to fly away — as a bird takes sudden flight).
90.11: mî-yôdēaʿ ʿōz ʾappekhā — who truly grasps the force of your anger? The rhetorical question: who has really reckoned with the weight (ʿōz: strength, force) of the divine anger? ûkĕyirʾātekhā ʿebrātekhā — who fears your wrath as it deserves? Ûkĕyirʾātekhā (according to/equal to your being feared) — the fear the divine wrath is owed but not paid. The implication: no one fully grasps it, which makes the plea of v12 (wisdom to count our days) all the more necessary.
90.12: limnôt yāmênû kēn hôdaʿ — teach us to count our days aright. Limnôt (to count, reckon, number) — not mere arithmetic but moral reckoning: to assign each day its true weight knowing its finitude. Hôdaʿ (hiphil imperative of yādaʿ: teach, make known) — the wisdom-petition addressed to YHWH. wĕnābîʾ lĕbab ḥokhĕmāh — that we may acquire a wise heart. Lĕbab ḥokhĕmāh (a heart of wisdom) — wisdom located in the heart (the seat of understanding and decision), arrived at by the discipline of mortality-awareness. The psalm's intellectual core: the proper response to human finitude is not despair but wisdom.
90.13: shûbāh YHWH ʿad-māthāy — turn back, LORD! How long will this go on? The communal lament imperative — shûb (return, relent — the opposite of divine turning-away) + ʿad-māthāy (how long? — the standard lament formula). wĕhinnāḥēm ʿal-ʿăbādêkhā — have mercy on your servants! Nāḥam (niphal: to relent, be moved to compassion — the divine turning from judgment to mercy). The pivot from the meditation on mortality to petition: having stared into the reality of human finitude (vv3-11) and sought wisdom (v12), now the community cries out for divine relenting.
90.14: śabĕʿênû babbōqer ḥasdekā — fill us in the morning with your steadfast love. Śābaʿ (hiphil: to satisfy, fill — the same word used of Ps 88:3 where the soul was filled with troubles; now the petition is to be filled instead with ḥesed). The morning as the time of grace: just as the grass springs up in the morning (v6), let the divine steadfast love spring up first. ûnĕrannĕnāh wĕnishmaḥāh bĕkhol-yāmênû — so that we may celebrate and rejoice through all our days. The goal: that the ḥesed filling the morning will sustain joy through the entire brief human lifespan.
90.15: śammĕḥênû kiymôt ʿinnîtānû — give us gladness to match the days you have made us suffer. The petition for proportional gladness — joy equal in measure to the suffering: kiymôt (like the days of) the affliction (ʿinnîtānû: you humbled/afflicted us). shĕnôt rāʾînû rāʿāh — joy equal to the years we have known trouble. The request is not for suffering to be denied but for it to be matched and ultimately overcome by gladness — a balance-of-years theology that assumes God can and will compensate.
90.16: yērāʾeh ʾel-ʿăbādêkhā phāʿŏlekā — let your work appear to your servants. Phōʿal (work, deed — the divine action in history) — the petition that the divine work become visible again to those who serve God. wahădarĕkhā ʿal-bĕnêhem — and your splendor shine on their children. Hādār (splendor, majesty, honor) — the divine glory that was seen in the Exodus and the wilderness now sought for the next generation (bĕnêhem: their children). The generational hope: the parents who suffered will not see the full manifestation, but their children will.
90.17: wîhî nōʿam ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû ʿālênû — may the favor of the LORD our God rest upon us. Nōʿam (pleasantness, grace, beauty, favor — from nāʿēm: to be pleasant, beautiful; cf. Naomi's name). The closing petition: the divine pleasantness resting on the community as its permanent atmosphere. ûmaʿăśēh yādênû kônĕnāh ʿālênû — establish the work of our hands. Kôn (hiphil: to establish firmly, make permanent) — the double petition with the doubly stated refrain: the human work (maʿăśēh yādênû) given permanence by divine action. The psalm closes where it opened: the permanent God asked to give permanence to what human hands create in their brief span.

91.1: yōshēb bĕsēter ʿelyôn bĕṣēl shadday yitlônān — whoever lives in the shelter of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty. Sēter (shelter, hiding place, secret place — from sātar: to hide) + ʿelyôn (Most High — the Canaanite epithet for the supreme deity, adopted as a title for YHWH). Bĕṣēl (in the shadow — the cool shade cast by a protecting presence) + shadday (the Almighty — the patriarchal name of God, especially prominent in Genesis and Job). The two divine titles (ʿelyôn and shadday) together invoke the God who is both supremely exalted and near enough to cast a sheltering shadow.
91.2: ʾōmar laYHWH maḥsî ûmĕṣûdātî ʾĕlōhay ʾebaṭaḥ-bô — I will say to the LORD, 'You are my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust.' Maḥseh (refuge — the place of shelter in storm or danger) + mĕṣûdāh (fortress, stronghold — the military fortification; from ṣûd: to hunt, to surround). The personal profession: ʾĕlōhay (my God) + ʾebaṭaḥ-bô (I trust in him) — the shift from third-person description (v1) to first-person confession, making the general promise personal.
91.3: kî hûʾ yaṣṣîlĕkhā mippah yāqûsh — he will rescue you from the hunter's trap. Naṣal (hiphil: to rescue, deliver — the Exodus deliverance verb) from the pah yāqûsh (fowler's snare — the trap laid for birds). The snare metaphor: the enemies and their plans as a fowler's trap into which the trusting person might fall but from which YHWH delivers. middeber hawwôt — and from every deadly disease. Deber (pestilence, plague — one of the Exodus plagues, here personified as a threat from which YHWH rescues).
91.4: bĕʾebrātô yāsekh lākh wĕtaḥat kĕnāphāyw teḥseh — he will shelter you under his feathers; you will find refuge beneath his wings. ʾEbrāh (feathers, pinions — the large flight feathers of a bird's wing) + kĕnāphāyw (his wings) — the dual metaphor of the mother bird protecting her young (cf. Deut 32:11's eagle metaphor; Ps 17:8; 36:7; Ruth 2:12). ṣinnāh wĕsōḥērāh ʾĕmûnātô — his faithfulness is your shield and armor. Ṣinnāh (large shield — the full body-shield) + sōḥērāh (encircling protection, buckler — a round shield or the protective encirclement itself). Divine faithfulness (ʾĕmûnāh) as body-armor — the reliability of God's character as the believer's defense.
91.5: lōʾ tîrāʾ mippahad lāylāh — you will not fear the terror that comes in the night. Pahad (dread, terror — the sudden overwhelming fear) that comes lāylāh (in the night — the time of darkness and vulnerability). mēḥēṣ yāʿûph yômām — nor the arrow that flies in the daylight. The four threats in vv5-6 span the dimensions of danger: night-terror, daylight-arrow (visible enemy), dark-pestilence, noon-destruction — every time and every kind of threat.
91.6: middeber bāʾōphel yahălōkh — nor the disease that moves through the dark. Deber (pestilence) that yahălōkh bāʾōphel (walks in darkness — the darkness of night). miqqeṭeb yāshûd ṣohŏrāyim — or the plague that strikes at noon. Qeṭeb (destruction, a destroying power — used in Deut 32:24 of pestilence; Isa 28:2 of storm-devastation) + ṣohŏrāyim (noon, midday — the blazing heat of noon). The qeṭeb ṣohŏrāyim became in the Septuagint the daimonion mesembrinon (the noonday demon) — the medieval spiritual tradition's identification of acedia (spiritual torpor) with this noon-striking plague.
91.7: yippōl mişşidĕkhā ʾelep ûrĕbābāh mîmînekhā — a thousand may fall beside you, ten thousand at your right hand. The military imagery: battlefield casualties surrounding the protected one. ʾElep (thousand) + rĕbābāh (ten thousand — the highest number word in Hebrew; cf. the song of 1 Sam 18:7). ʾēlekhā lōʾ yiggāsh — but it will not reach you. Nāgaʿ (to touch, reach, strike) — the destruction that strikes ten thousand will not make contact with the protected one. Not a claim of physical invincibility but of covenant protection amid universal mortality.
91.8: raq bĕʿênêkhā tabbîṭ — you will only watch with your own eyes. Raq (only, merely) — the sole action: watching with open eyes. wĕshillumāt rĕshāʿîm tirʾeh — and see the fate of the wicked. Shillumāh (retribution, recompense — from shālem: the paying back what is owed) of the wicked — the trusting person is a spectator to the judgment that falls on those who do not trust. The position is not active participation in the judgment but witness to it.
91.9: kî-ʾattāh YHWH maḥsî — because you have made the LORD your refuge. The kî (because) gives the reason for the protection of vv5-8: the ground is the decision made — YHWH chosen as refuge (maḥseh: the hiding place). ʿelyôn śamtā mĕʿônekhā — the Most High your dwelling place. Māʿôn — the same word as Ps 90:1 (God as the dwelling place) — here the human makes the divine dwelling place their own habitation. The symmetry: God is humanity's dwelling (Ps 90:1), and the Most High is the trusting person's dwelling (91:9).
91.10: lōʾ-tĕʾunneh ʾēlêkhā rāʿāh — no evil will happen to you. ʾĀnan (pual: to befall, happen to — used of bad things that meet you). wĕnega lōʾ-yiqrab bĕʾāhŏlekhā — and no plague will come close to your tent. Negaʿ (blow, plague, affliction) + bĕʾāhŏlekhā (your tent — the domestic, dwelling space). The protection extends from the person to the household. This is the protective covenant promise that undergirds the psalm — not a guarantee of absolute physical immunity but of the covenant shelter of divine care.
91.11: kî malʾākhāyw yĕṣawweh-lākh lishmorĕkhā bĕkhol-dĕrākhêkhā — for he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways. The divine commissioning of angels for personal protection — ṣāwāh (to command) applied to the angels' assignment. Bĕkhol-dĕrākhêkhā (in all your ways — not just the religious ways but every path). This verse and v12 are quoted by the adversary in Matt 4:6 / Luke 4:10-11 in the temple-pinnacle temptation — not a misquotation but a misapplication: the text promises protection in the normal paths of life, not in a spectacular act of self-endangerment to force God's hand.
91.12: ʿal-kappayim yiśśāʾûnekhā — they will carry you on their hands. Kappayim (palms of the hands) — the angels bearing the protected person in their open hands, preventing a fall. pen-tiggōph bāʾeben raglekhā — lest your foot strike against a stone. Nāgaph (to strike, stumble) — the protection from even ordinary stumbling. The extreme care described (angels carrying in their palms, preventing foot from touching a stone) expresses the totality of divine protection — it is this total protection that makes the adversary's misuse in Matt 4 a distortion: the promise covers the ordinary paths of life, not a theatric leap from the temple parapet.
91.13: ʿal-shaḥal wāphethen tidrokh — you will tread on the lion and the cobra. Shaḥal (young lion — the vigorous prime lion) + pethen (cobra, the Egyptian cobra — the most deadly serpent known to ancient Israelites). tirmos kĕphîr wĕtannîn — you will trample the young lion and the great serpent. Kĕphîr (young lion) + tannîn (sea dragon, great serpent — the chaos monster of Gen 1:21; Ps 74:13; Isa 51:9). The treading on dangerous creatures echoes Gen 3:15 (enmity between the seed and the serpent) and is echoed in Luke 10:19 (Jesus granting authority to tread on serpents). The dangerous creatures represent all powers hostile to the protected one.
91.14: kî bî ḥāshaq waʾăpalleṭēhû — 'Because he clings to me in love,' declares the LORD, 'I will rescue him'. The divine first-person speech (vv14-16) — God speaking directly about the trusting person. Ḥāshaq (to cling to, be attached to in love — the devoted attachment that cannot be separated; used of Israel's election, Deut 7:7). ʾăśaggĕbēhû kî yādaʿ shĕmî — I will set him beyond reach, because he truly knows who I am. Śāgab (piel: to set high and out of reach) — exaltation as protection. Yādaʿ shĕmî (he knows my name) — intimate personal knowledge of the divine name and character as the basis for the divine response.
91.15: yiqrāʾēnî wĕʾeʿĕnēhû — when he calls on me I will answer. The covenant prayer-promise: calling and answering as the basic transaction of relationship. ʿimmô-ʾānôkhî bĕṣārāh — I will be with him in his trouble. ʿImmô ʾānôkhî (I myself am with him) — the divine presence with the sufferer in the midst of the trouble (ṣārāh: distress, straits), not removal from it. ʾăḥallĕṣēhû waʾăkhabbĕdēhû — I will rescue him and give him honor. Ḥālaṣ (hiphil: to rescue, deliver — to pull free from danger) + kābēd (piel: to honor, give weight to — the bestowal of the divine honor on the one who clings to God).
91.16: ʾōrekh yāmîm ʾaśbîʿēhû — I will give him a long life. ʾōrekh yāmîm (length of days — the covenantal long life of Deut 11:9; Prov 3:16) — the divine gift of life's full measure. wĕʾarʾēhû bishûʿātî — and let him see my saving power. Rāʾāh bishuāʿāh (to see the salvation) — the final covenant gift: witnessing the divine salvation in its fullness. The psalm closes with God's own declaration — the divine voice directly promising what the entire psalm has described from the human side.

92.1: mizmôr shîr lĕyôm hashshabbāt — A Psalm. A Song for the Sabbath day. The only psalm with this specific Sabbath title — its placement in the Psalter and its content (the divine work celebrated, the wicked's apparent flourishing addressed, the righteous ultimately vindicated) fit the Sabbath's theological scope: the day that celebrates the goodness of completed creation and anticipates its eschatological fulfillment. ṭôb lĕhôdôt laYHWH — it is good to give thanks to the LORD. Ṭôb (good) — the creation-goodness word of Gen 1, applied to the act of thanksgiving. ûlĕzammēr lĕshimĕkhā ʿelyôn — to sing praises to your name, O Most High. Zāmar (to make music, to sing praise — often with instruments) to ʿelyôn (the Most High — the God who governs all).
92.2: lĕhaggîd baqqōqer ḥasdekā — to declare your steadfast love each morning. Ḥesed announced in the morning — the morning as the time of covenant renewal and the experience of fresh grace (cf. Lam 3:22-23). wĕʾĕmûnātĕkhā ballêlôt — and your faithfulness through every night. The pairing: ḥesed for the morning (the active covenant love) + ʾĕmûnāh for the night (the reliability that sustains through darkness). The full day covered by the two divine attributes — the Sabbath as the day when both are proclaimed.
92.3: ʿălê-ʿāśôr wăʿălê-nābel — to the music of the ten-stringed instrument and the lute. ʿĀśôr (ten-stringed instrument) + nābel (lute, harp — a large, pear-shaped stringed instrument). ʿălê higgāyôn bĕkhinnôr — and the rippling of the lyre. Higgāyôn (a murmuring, rippling sound — possibly a specific type of music, or the low melodious sound of the lyre; the word also appears in Ps 9:16 as a musical direction). The three instruments create a full ensemble of praise — string instruments covering different registers of sound.
92.4: kî śimmahătanî YHWH bĕphāʿŏlekā — you have made me glad, LORD, by what you have done. The personal testimony (śimmahătanî: you made me glad — the same verb as the petition of Ps 90:14-15). Bĕphāʿŏlekā (by your deed/work) — the divine action as the source of human gladness. bĕmaʿăśēh yādêkhā ʾărannēn — I sing for joy over all that your hands have made. Rānan (to shout for joy — the loud, ringing cry of joy) at the divine handiwork. The Sabbath context: the work of YHWH's hands that calls forth joy is the completed creation rested from on the seventh day.
92.5: māh-gādĕlû maʿăśêkhā YHWH — how great are your works, LORD! The exclamation of wonder at the divine deeds — māh-gādĕlû (how great! — the exclamatory māh). mĕʾōd ʿāmĕqû maḥshĕbōtekhā — how utterly deep are your thoughts! ʿĀmaq (to be deep — the depth that cannot be fathomed from below). Maḥshābāh (thought, plan, design — the divine cognitive activity that produces the observed works). The works are visible; the thoughts behind them are deep beyond human comprehension (cf. Isa 55:8-9; Rom 11:33).
92.6: ʾîsh-baʿar lōʾ yēdāʿ ûkhĕsîl lōʾ-yābîn ʾet-zōʾt — a senseless person cannot grasp it, and a fool does not understand this. Baʿar (brutish, stupid — one who behaves like an animal, without reason; cf. Ps 73:22). Kĕsîl (fool — the morally/intellectually deficient person who rejects wisdom). The two types of incomprehension: the brutish senselessness that cannot see, and the deliberate foolishness that refuses to understand. What they cannot grasp is elaborated in v7 — the mystery of the wicked's apparent success.
92.7: biphroaḥ rĕshāʿîm kĕmô ʿēśeb — the wicked may spring up like grass. Pāraḥ (to blossom, spring up — the growth verb applied to the wicked as to grass). The reversal of the righteous-as-tree image (vv12-14): the wicked flourish like grass — quickly and visibly. wayyāṣîṣû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — and every evildoer may flourish. Yāṣîṣ (to blossom brightly — cf. Ps 90:6 where grass blossoms in the morning). lĕhishshāmĕdām ʿădê-ʿad — but they are headed for eternal ruin. ʿAdê-ʿad (forever, into the furthest future) — the flourishing is temporary, the destruction permanent. The temporal contrast: quick grass-flourishing vs. forever-destruction.
92.8: wĕʾattāh mārôm lĕʿôlām YHWH — while you, LORD, are exalted on high forever. Mārôm (on high — the elevated place of divine reign) + lĕʿôlām (forever). The contrast with the grass of v7: the wicked flourish briefly and perish eternally; YHWH is exalted eternally. The spatial height (mārôm) of divine governance is the frame within which the apparent flourishing of the wicked is correctly evaluated.
92.9: kî hinnēh ʾōyĕbêkhā YHWH — yes, your enemies, LORD. The kî-hinnēh (for behold) draws attention dramatically to what follows. kî-hinnēh ʾōyĕbêkhā yōʾbēdû — your enemies will certainly perish. ʾĀbad (to perish, be destroyed) — the enemies of YHWH will perish. yitpārĕdû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — all who do evil will be scattered. Pārad (niphal: to be scattered, separated) — the dissolution of the apparently cohesive group of evildoers. The repetition of kî hinnēh twice in v9 gives a rhythmic emphasis to the certainty of the outcome.
92.10: wattārēm kirʾêm qarnî — but you have raised my strength like the horn of a wild ox. Rĕʾēm (the wild ox — the aurochs, Bos primigenius, the immense untameable wild cattle of the ancient Near East; cf. Num 23:22; Deut 33:17; Job 39:9-12). The horn of the wild ox as the image of natural, unconstrained power — not the trained strength of a domestic ox but the raw power of the wild. ballōtî bĕshemen raʿănān — I have been anointed with fresh oil. Raʿănān (fresh, green, lush — the quality of newly pressed oil still full of vitality). The anointing with fresh oil may be literal (festive anointing of Ps 23:5) or metaphorical for divine blessing and refreshment.
92.11: wattabbēṭ ʿênî bĕshûrāy — my eyes have seen the downfall of my enemies. Shûr (those who watch/lurk against me — from shārar: to be hostile, to watch with hostile intent). The eye-witness to the enemies' downfall — the watching-of-destruction echoed from Ps 91:8. baqāmîm ʿālay mĕrēʿîm tishmaʿnāh ʾoznāy — my ears have heard the ruin of those who rose against me. The double sensory testimony: eyes see and ears hear the fate of those who rose against (qûm ʿālay) the Psalmist.
92.12: ṣaddîq kattāmār yiphrāḥ — the righteous person flourishes like the palm tree. Tāmār (date palm — Phoenix dactylifera, the tall, stately palm whose fruit comes from near its crown, living for centuries; producing fruit into old age). The contrast with the wicked's grass-flourishing (v7): the wicked are like grass (quick, ephemeral), the righteous are like palm trees (tall, perennial, productive into old age). kĕʾerez ballĕbānôn yiśgeh — and grows tall like a cedar of Lebanon. ʾErez ballĕbānôn (cedar of Lebanon — the great mountain cedar, symbol of grandeur and permanence). Two trees representing the two most majestic forms of botanical life known to ancient Israel.
92.13: shĕtûlîm bĕbêt YHWH — planted in the house of the LORD. Shātûl (transplanted, planted — the passive participle: these are not self-seeded wild growths but deliberately planted trees). bĕḥaṣrôt ʾĕlōhênû yaphriʾû — they will flourish in the courts of our God. The planting location: bêt YHWH (house of YHWH) + ḥaṣrôt ʾĕlōhênû (courts of our God). The righteous flourish precisely because they are planted in proximity to God — the source of their life. The temple courts as the garden where God's people thrive.
92.14: ʿôd yĕnûbûn bĕśêbāh — in old age they still produce fruit. Nûb (to bring forth fruit — the productive bearing of the tree). ʾbĕśêbāh (in grey-haired old age — the silvery hair of the aged). The contrast with human transience in Ps 90: here the righteous remain fruitful in old age, like the palm whose fruit-bearing continues for decades. dĕshēnîm wĕraʿănannîm yihyû — they remain vigorous and full of life. Dāshēn (fat, well-nourished, flourishing — the full health that comes from proper nourishment) + raʿănān (fresh, green — the word for the fresh oil of v10). Vitality maintained through old age as the covenant outcome.
92.15: lĕhaggîd kî-yāshār YHWH — to declare that the LORD is just. The final function of the righteous: they bear witness (higgîd: to declare, make known) to the divine justice. Their own flourishing is itself the testimony. ṣûrî wĕlōʾ-ʿawlātāh bô — my rock, and there is nothing wrong in him. Ṣûr (rock — the bedrock on which the trusting person stands, cf. Deut 32:4). ʿAwlāh (injustice, wrong, perversity) — its negation: lōʾ-ʿawlātāh bô (no injustice in him). The closing confession declares the full vindication of YHWH's justice despite the apparent flourishing of the wicked in v7 — the Sabbath psalm ends with the divine righteousness as the bedrock reality.

93.1: YHWH mālākh gēʾût lābēsh — the LORD reigns! He is clothed with majesty. YHWH mālākh (the LORD reigns/has become king) — the enthronement proclamation formula that opens the cluster of enthronement psalms (93, 95-99). Whether mālākh is timeless-present (always reigns) or a punctual past (has just become king in a liturgical ceremony) has been much debated; the proclamation asserts both eternal sovereignty and its immediate recognition. Gēʾût lābēsh (clothed in majesty — from gāʾāh: to rise, be exalted; the swelling, surging of divine glory). lābēsh YHWH ʿōz hitʾazzār — the LORD is dressed in strength and has girded himself. Hitʾazzār (hitpael of ʿāzar: to gird oneself — the warrior's preparation). ʾap-tikkôn tēbēl bal-timmôṭ — the world is firmly established — it cannot be shaken. Tikkôn (niphal of kûn: to be established, fixed) — creation's stability as the consequence of divine sovereignty.
93.2: nākhôn kissĕʾăkhā mēʾāz — your throne has been established from ancient times. Nākhôn (established, fixed — the same root as tikkôn in v1) — the divine throne's stability mirrors the world's stability: both fixed by the same sovereign. Mēʾāz (from of old, from long ago — the deep past; cf. Mic 5:2's mîqedem mîmê ʿôlām). mēʿôlām ʾāttāh — you yourself are from everlasting. Mēʿôlām (from eternity, from the indefinitely ancient past) — YHWH's own existence predates the throne he occupies and the world it governs; the most compressed statement of divine eternity in the psalm.
93.3: nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt YHWH nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt qôlām — the great waters have lifted up their voice, LORD; the rushing floods have lifted up their voice. The triple repetition of nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt (the rivers/floods have lifted up) mimics the rising surging of waters — the anaphora performs the flood's insistent swelling. yiśśĕʾû nĕhārôt dākyām — and their crashing waves. Dākî (crashing, pounding waves — from dākhāh: to crush, pound). The chaos-waters making themselves heard — the cosmic challenge to divine sovereignty that v4 answers.
93.4: miqqōlôt mayim rabbîm — mightier than the roar of many waters. Mayim rabbîm (many waters — the chaotic primordial flood; cf. Rev 17:1 where many waters are the nations) — the most powerful force in the ancient Near Eastern cosmological imagination. ʾaddîrîm mishbĕrê-yām — mightier than the waves of the sea. Mishbār (breaker, crashing wave) of the sea — the sea in its most violent aspect. ʾaddîr bammarôm YHWH — the LORD on high is mighty. ʾAddîr (mighty, majestic — the great-power word) placed both at the beginning (waters mighty) and the end (LORD mighty) — the seas' power compared to YHWH's. YHWH's power in the high place (bammarôm) exceeds both. The cosmic battle is over before it begins.
93.5: ʿēdōtekhā neʾĕmnû mĕʾōd — your decrees are completely trustworthy. ʿĒdâh (testimonies, decrees — the divine statutes as witness to YHWH's character; cf. Ps 19:7). Neʾĕmnû (niphal of ʾāman: they are reliable, trustworthy — the root of ʾāmēn). The moral order (ʿēdôt: the divine testimony-law) matches the physical order (the world established, v1) — both secured by the same divine sovereignty. lĕbêtekhā naʾăwāh-qōdesh YHWH lĕʾōrekh yāmîm — holiness befits your house, LORD, forevermore. Nāʾāh (to be fitting, to become, to adorn) — holiness (qōdesh) adorns/befits the divine house as gēʾût adorns YHWH in v1. The psalm closes with the moral attribute (holiness) answering the cosmic attribute (majesty): the King who defeats the chaos-waters is also the Holy One whose house is adorned with holiness.

94.1: ʾēl-nĕqāmôt YHWH ʾēl nĕqāmôt hôphîaʿ — O LORD, God of vengeance — God of justice, arise and shine forth! Nĕqāmôt (plural of nĕqāmāh: retribution, vengeance — from nāqam: to avenge, execute retributive justice). The plural may intensify (great retribution) or refer to multiple acts of just retribution. The term is not personal revenge but the legal execution of justice — YHWH as the impartial judge who balances the scales. Hôphîaʿ (hiphil imperative of yāphaʿ: to shine forth, appear in splendor — the request for the divine judicial appearance to become visible).
94.2: hinnāśēʾ shōpheṭ hāʾāreṣ — rise up, Judge of the earth. Nāśāʾ (niphal imperative: rise up — the judge taking his seat of authority). Shōphēṭ hāʾāreṣ (Judge of the earth) — the title Abraham used in his intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:25: hashōphēṭ kol-hāʾāreṣ lōʾ yaʿăśeh mishpāṭ). hāshēb gĕmûl ʿal-gēʾîm — pay back to the proud what they deserve. Gĕmûl (recompense, what is owed — the return that matches the deed) on the gēʾîm (proud, arrogant ones — from gāʾāh: to swell, rise high).
94.3: ʿad-māthay rĕshāʿîm YHWH ʿad-māthay rĕshāʿîm yaʿălōzû — how long, LORD, will the wicked — how long will the wicked rejoice in triumph? The doubled ʿad-māthay (how long? how long?) — the urgency of the theodicy question expressed through repetition. ʿĀlaz (to exult, rejoice triumphantly — often the celebration of an unexpected victory). The wicked's exulting is the affront: they should be silent before the divine judge, but instead they celebrate.
94.4: yabbîʿû yĕdabbĕrû ʿātāq — they spew proud words without end. Nābaʿ (hiphil: to pour out, gush forth — the uncontrolled outpouring of speech). ʿātāq (proud, arrogant things — words of presumptuous arrogance, the speech of those who think they are beyond accountability). yitʾammĕrû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — all who do evil brag without restraint. ʾĀmar (hitpael: to show oneself great, boast) — the evil-doers' self-display as the antithesis of fearing God.
94.5: ʿammĕkhā YHWH yĕdakĕʾû — they crush your people, LORD. Dākhāʾ (piel: to crush, pound — the same root as Ps 90:3's dakkāʾ: crushed dust). The victims identified: ʿammĕkhā (your people) — which means the oppression is not only against the weak but against YHWH himself. wĕnaḥălātĕkhā yĕʿannû — and oppress those who belong to you. Naḥălāh (inheritance, property — Israel as YHWH's own possession). To oppress God's naḥălāh is to violate divine property — the crime is against the owner of the people, not only against the people.
94.6: ʾalmānāh wĕgēr yahărōgû — they murder the widow and the foreigner. ʾAlmānāh (widow) + gēr (resident alien, foreigner — the protected classes of Israelite law: Exod 22:21-24; Deut 24:17-22; Lev 19:9-10). These are precisely the ones the Mosaic law most explicitly protects. wîtômîm yĕrāṣĕḥû — and kill the fatherless. Yātôm (orphan) — the third member of the classic triad (widow, orphan, stranger) that appears throughout the prophets as the test of social justice (Isa 1:17; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10; Mal 3:5).
94.7: wayyōʾmĕrû lōʾ yirʾeh-YAH — and they say, 'The LORD never sees it'. Yirʾeh-YAH (the LORD does not see) — the theological premise of the oppressor: practical atheism, the denial of divine observation. wĕlōʾ-yābîn ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb — the God of Jacob pays no attention. Bîn (to understand, perceive — judicial discernment). The ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) — the covenant God of the patriarchs, who should above all perceive the suffering of his people, is claimed by the oppressor not to notice. The psalmist's counter-argument follows in vv9-11.
94.8: bînû bōʿărîm bāʿām — grasp this, you most senseless among the people. Bîn (imperative: understand, discern) directed at the bōʿărîm (the brutish/senseless ones — cf. Ps 92:6: ʾîsh-baʿar). ûkhĕsîlîm māthay taskîlû — fools, when will you become wise? Kĕsîlîm (fools — the morally obtuse). The rhetorical address directly to the oppressors, inviting them to use the faculty of reason they are demonstrating they lack.
94.9: hănōṭaʿ ʾōzen hălōʾ yishmaʿ — the one who made the ear — does he not hear? The qal wahomer (from minor to major) argument: the ear's Creator must be able to do what the ear can do. Nāṭaʿ (to plant, to form — the delicate craftsmanship of forming the ear). If a human craftsman understands the instrument he has crafted, how much more the divine craftsman who created hearing itself. ʾim-yōṣēr ʿayin hălōʾ yabbîṭ — the one who formed the eye — does he not see? The parallel: the eye's Formation (yāṣar: the potter's crafting of the eye's form) implies the ability to see. The argument from creation to Creator's capacities.
94.10: hăyōsēr gôyim hălōʾ yôkhîaḥ — the one who corrects the nations — will he not call them to account? Yāsar (to discipline, correct, instruct — the instructive discipline that brings nations to order). If YHWH disciplines entire nations, how could he fail to call individual oppressors to account? hămĕlammēd ʾādām daʿat — the one who teaches all people. Limmēd (piel: to teach) — the divine pedagogy directed toward all humanity (ʾādām: the collective human being).
94.11: YHWH yōdēaʿ maḥshĕbôt ʾādām kî-hēmmāh hebel — the LORD knows human beings' thoughts — that they are nothing but vapor. The divine knowledge maḥshĕbôt ʾādām (the thoughts/plans of humanity) — YHWH knows them precisely and has evaluated them: hēmmāh hebel (they are vapor, emptiness — the Qoheleth word for the insubstantial transience of all human projects). This verse is quoted by Paul in 1 Cor 3:20 (kurios ginōskei tous dialogismous tōn sophōn: the Lord knows the reasonings of the wise, that they are futile) — applied to the futility of human wisdom compared to divine wisdom. The oppressors' plans are known to God and evaluated as hebel.
94.12: ʾashrê haggeber ʾăsher-tĕyassĕrennû YAH — blessed is the man whom you discipline, LORD. ʾAshrê (blessed — the beatitude formula; cf. Ps 1:1) + yāsar (to discipline, instruct): blessing comes through discipline. ûmitôrātekhā tĕlammĕdennû — and instruct from your teaching. Tôrāh (teaching, instruction — the Torah as the divine curriculum through which YHWH instructs his people). The disciplined person is blessed not despite the hardship but through it — the instruction given through suffering.
94.13: lĕhashqîṭ lô mîmê rāʿ — giving him rest in days of trouble. Hashqîṭ (hiphil of shāqaṭ: to give rest, to make quiet — the resting-from-trouble that the disciplined person receives). ʿad yikkāreh lārāshāʿ shāḥat — until a pit is dug for the wicked. The temporary nature of the trouble: the disciplined righteous person rests while the wicked's pit is being prepared. Shāḥat (pit — both literal and metaphorical: the grave/destruction that awaits).
94.14: kî lōʾ-yiṭōsh YHWH ʿammô — the LORD will not abandon his people. Nāṭash (to abandon, forsake, cast off — the action of leaving without looking back). wĕnaḥălātô lōʾ yaʿăzōb — or desert his inheritance. ʿĀzab (to abandon, leave) — the two verbs together (nāṭash + ʿāzab) reinforce the covenantal commitment: YHWH will not leave his naḥălāh (cf. Deut 4:31; 31:6; Josh 1:5). The confidence grounding the lament of vv3-11 and the petition of v1-2.
94.15: kî-ʿad-ṣedeq yāshûb mishpāṭ — for justice will return to the righteous. Shûb (to return — justice reverting to its proper place with the righteous after its apparent absence). wĕʾaḥărāyw kol-yishĕrê-lēb — and all who are upright in heart will pursue it. Yāshār-lēb (upright of heart — the opposite of the hardened heart of v8; the person whose inner compass is aligned correctly). The return of justice creates a rally of the upright — they pursue what has returned.
94.16: mî-yāqûm lî ʿim-mĕrēʿîm — who will stand up for me against the wicked? The personal lament-question: who will rise as an advocate against the mĕrēʿîm (those who do evil — piel participle of rāʿaʿ: to do harm)? mî-yityaṣṣēb lî ʿim-pōʿălê ʾāwen — who will take my side against evildoers? Yāṣab (hitpael: to station oneself, take a position as an advocate or champion). The implied answer: only YHWH, as vv17-19 confirm.
94.17: lûlê YHWH ʿezrātāh lî — if the LORD had not been my help. Lûlê (if not — the conditional of disaster: what would have happened without divine intervention). kimaʿaṭ shākhĕnāh dûmāh nafshî — I would very soon have lain in the silence of death. Dûmāh (silence — the silence of death; cf. Ps 115:17: lōʾ hammētîm yĕhallĕlû-YAH wĕlōʾ kol-yōrĕdê dûmāh: not the dead praise the LORD, not those going down to silence). The narrow escape: kimaʿaṭ (almost, nearly) — the Psalmist was almost in the silence of death but for YHWH's intervention.
94.18: ʾim-ʾāmartî māṭāh raglî — when I felt my foot giving way. Māṭāh (to be inclined, to slip — the foot losing its footing). The moment of crisis described with simplicity: the foot slipping is enough to indicate the totality of the threat. ḥasdĕkhā YHWH yisʿādēnî — your steadfast love, LORD, held me steady. Sāʿad (to support, uphold, prop up — the prop placed under the slipping foot). The divine ḥesed as the stabilizing force under the failing foot — the covenant love as the practical ground underfoot.
94.19: bĕrōb śarʿappay bĕqirbî — when anxious thoughts multiply inside me. Śarʿappîm (anxious thoughts, disquieting thoughts — from śāraph: to divide, split; the inner splitting of anxiety; hapax in this form). The internal experience: the multiplication of worrying thoughts within (bĕqirbî: inside me). tanhûmekhā yĕshaʿăshĕʿû nafshî — your comfort brings delight to my soul. Tanḥûm (consolation, comfort — from nāḥam: to comfort, console). Shaʿashuaʿ (to delight, to bring exquisite pleasure — a rare intensive form: the divine comfort not merely quieting the anxiety but producing delight in the soul).
94.20: hăyĕḥābrĕkhā kissēʾ hawwôt — can a corrupt throne have any fellowship with you? Ḥābar (to be associated with, have fellowship/partnership with) — can the throne of hawwôt (ruin, destruction, malicious plans — the corrupt power that enacts harm) have any association with YHWH? The implied answer is no — YHWH cannot be in covenant with or complicit in corrupt governance. yōṣēr ʿāmāl ʿălê-ḥōq — one that enacts harm through its own laws. ʿĀmāl (trouble, injustice) enacted ʿălê-ḥōq (by/through statute, through official legislation) — the oppression that uses legal forms to perpetrate injustice is what the psalm specifically addresses: structural evil embedded in law.
94.21: yāgôddû ʿal-nefesh ṣaddîq — they join forces against the lives of the righteous. Gādad (hitpolel: to band together, mass against — the organized assault of a group). wĕdām nāqî yarshîʾû — and condemn the innocent to death. Dām nāqî (innocent blood — the blood of the guiltless) condemned (rāshāʿ: piel: to declare guilty, to condemn as guilty — the judicial condemnation of the blameless). The corruption of the legal system reaches its apex: innocent blood condemned through legal process.
94.22: wayhî YHWH lî lĕmiśgāb — but the LORD is my strong fortress. Miśgāb (high fortress, elevated refuge — from śāgab: to be high, inaccessible; cf. Ps 91:14 where the trusting person is placed beyond reach). The turn from complaint to confidence: wĕʾĕlōhay lĕṣûr maḥsî — and my God is the rock where I take shelter. Ṣûr maḥsî (rock of my refuge) — the two bedrock images (fortress + rock) combined as the divine basis of security against the corrupt power of v20-21.
94.23: wayyāsheb ʿălêhem ʾet-ʾôwnām — he will pay them back for their sin. Shûb ʿāl (hiphil: to bring back upon — the divine recoil of their ʾāwen [iniquity, evil] back onto those who committed it). ûbĕrāʿātām yaṣmîtēm — and destroy them for their evil. Ṣāmat (hiphil: to cut off, silence, destroy completely — used of the wicked's destruction, cf. Ps 18:40; 88:16). yaṣmîtēm YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — the LORD our God will wipe them out. The closing reiteration of the divine name: the God who was invoked in v1 as the God of retributions delivers the retribution at the psalm's close.

95.1: lĕkhû nĕrannĕnāh laYHWH — come, let us sing joyfully to the LORD. Rānan (to give a ringing shout of joy — the loud, exuberant communal cry that opens worship). The cohortative nĕrannĕnāh (let us shout/sing) is the liturgical call to corporate worship. nārîʿāh lĕṣûr yishʿênû — let us shout aloud to the Rock who saves us! Rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout in triumph/worship — the blast of joy). Ṣûr yishʿênû (Rock of our salvation) — the title from Ps 89:26 where the anointed king calls YHWH ṣûr yĕshûʿātî; here applied to the community's corporate salvation.
95.2: nĕqaddĕmāh phānāyw bĕtôdāh — let us come before him with thanksgiving. Qādam pānim (to come before the face — to approach the divine presence as a subject approaches a king). Tôdāh (thanksgiving — the specific sacrifice/act of thanksgiving in Israelite worship, from yādāh: to acknowledge, confess, thank). bizkĕmîrôt nārîaʿ lô — and celebrate him with songs of praise. Mĕzimmôt (psalms, songs — the musical compositions of praise offered in the temple). The two modes of approach: thanksgiving and song.
95.3: kî ʾēl gādôl YHWH ûmelek gādôl ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm — for the LORD is a great God, a great King greater than all other gods. The grounding kî (for/because) gives the reason for worship: YHWH's divine identity. ʾĒl gādôl (great God) + melek gādôl (great King) — the double title acknowledges YHWH's divine nature and sovereign rule simultaneously. ʿAl-kol-ʾĕlōhîm (over all the gods) — the incomparability formula positioning YHWH over all other divine claimants, as in Ps 86:8; 89:6-7.
95.4: ʾăsher bĕyādô meḥqĕrê-ʾāreṣ — the deepest places of the earth are in his hand. Meḥqār (deep recesses, hidden depths — from ḥāqar: to search out) of the earth — the inaccessible underground depths. wĕtôʿăphôt hārîm lô — and the mountain peaks belong to him. Tôʿāphôt (heights, peaks — the extreme high points) of the mountains. The vertical sovereignty: from the deepest underground (meḥqĕrê-ʾāreṣ) to the highest peaks (tôʿăphôt hārîm) — the full vertical range of the created order belongs to YHWH.
95.5: ʾăsher-lô hayyām wĕhûʾ ʿāśāhû — the sea is his — he made it. Ownership grounded in creation: lô (his) because ʿāśāhû (he made it). The sea that the chaos-waters of Ps 93:3-4 threatened to overwhelm YHWH's rule belongs to him as its maker — possession precedes and defeats the chaos-threat. wĕyabbāshet yādāyw yāṣārû — his own hands shaped the dry land. Yāṣar (to form, shape — the potter's forming; cf. Gen 2:7 where YHWH forms the man from dust). The dry land formed by YHWH's hands — the same hands that hold the depths (v4). All the earth is his handiwork.
95.6: bōʾû nishṭaḥăweh wĕnikhrāʿāh — come, let us bow down and worship. The second call to worship (bōʾû: come — cf. v1's lĕkhû: come), now shifting from joyful shouting (vv1-2) to reverent prostration (shāḥāh: to bow low, prostrate oneself in worship) and kneeling (kāraʿ: to kneel). nibrĕkhāh liphnê-YHWH ʿōśênû — let us kneel before the LORD who made us. ʿĒśânû (who made us — from ʿāśāh: the same verb as v5 where YHWH made the sea). The congregation as YHWH's handiwork alongside the sea and dry land.
95.7: kî hûʾ ʾĕlōhênû waʾănaḥnû ʿam marʿîtô wĕṣōʾn yādô — for he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, the flock he tends. The covenant identity: ʾĕlōhênû (our God) + ʿammô (his people) in the classical covenant formula. Ṣōʾn yādô (the flock of his hand) — the sheep held in God's hand, cared for with his own hand (cf. Ezek 34:31's good shepherd promise). hayyôm ʾim-bĕqōlô tishmaʿû — today — if only you would hear his voice. Hayyôm (today — the urgent, unrepeatable present moment that the divine voice occupies). The psalm pivots abruptly from worship-invitation to covenant-warning. The ʾim (if) marks the conditional threshold.
95.8: ʾal-taqshû lĕbabĕkhem kimrîbāh — do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah. Qāshāh (hiphil: to make hard, to harden — the deliberate stiffening of the heart against the divine voice). Mĕrîbāh (Meribah — the place of quarreling, from rîb: to contend; Exod 17:7; Num 20:13 where the people contended with God over water). kĕyôm massāh bammidebār — as on that day at Massah in the wilderness. Massāh (testing — from nāsāh: to test; Exod 17:7 where the location was named both Massah and Meribah). The wilderness generation as the paradigmatic warning against hardening.
95.9: ʾăsher nissûnî ʾăbôtêkhem — your ancestors put me to the test. Nāsāh (to test, to prove — the dangerous reversal: the creature testing the Creator). bĕḥānûnî gam-rāʾû phāʿălî — and challenged me, even after seeing all I had done. Bāḥan (to test, examine — a second verb for testing, as if the first were insufficient to express the audacity). The aggravating circumstance: gam-rāʾû phāʿălî (they also saw my works) — the testing happened despite (gam: also, even) the visual evidence of YHWH's miraculous deeds. Testing after seeing is the supreme ingratitude.
95.10: ʾarbaʿîm shānāh ʾāqûṭ bĕdôr — for forty years I was provoked by that generation. Qûṭ (qal: to loathe, be disgusted with — from a root related to deep revulsion; used here of God's attitude toward the wilderness generation). This is an unusually intense verb for divine reaction — not simply anger but something closer to moral nausea at persistent rebellion. wāʾōmar ʿam tōʿê-lēbāb hēm — and declared, 'This people's heart keeps wandering'. Tāʿāh (to wander, err, stray — as a sheep straying from the path; cf. Ps 119:176). The diagnosis: erring heart (tōʿê-lēbāb) — not occasional failure but a structural disposition to wander. wĕhēm lōʾ-yādĕʿû dĕrākhāy — they have never learned my ways. Yādaʿ (to know — covenant-knowing that includes relational fidelity). They have not internalized the divine ways despite forty years of experience with them.
95.11: ʾăsher-nishbaʿtî bĕʾappî — so in my anger I swore. Nishbaʿ (niphal of shābaʿ: to swear an oath) bĕʾappî (in my anger — the oath sworn in the moment of divine wrath). ʾim-yĕbōʾûn ʾel-mĕnûḥātî — 'They will never enter my rest.' Mĕnûḥāh (rest, resting-place — from nûaḥ: to rest, settle; the concept covers both the physical land-rest of Canaan, Gen 2:2's divine Sabbath rest, and the eschatological rest toward which both point). The ʾim before the oath is the negative oath-formula in Hebrew: ʾim yĕbōʾûn = they shall certainly not enter. The epistle to the Hebrews (Heb 3:7-4:11) reads this verse as describing a rest that remains available — the wilderness generation's exclusion does not exhaust the promise of rest; the hayyôm of v7 is still the door to enter it.

80.1: The superscription assigns Psalm 80 to Asaph and specifies the tune shôshannîm ʿêdût (Lilies of the Covenant). The opening address is unique in the Psalter: רֹעֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל הַאֲזִינָה (rōʿēh yiśrāʾêl hāʾăzînāh, "O Shepherd of Israel, give ear!") — rōʿēh: the participle of rāʿāh (to shepherd/feed/tend) used as a divine title (Gen 48:15 — Jacob: "the God who has been my shepherd all my life"; Ps 23:1). נֹהֵג כַּצֹּאן יוֹסֵף (nōhêg kaṣṣōʾn yôsêp, "you who lead Joseph like a flock") — nāhag: to lead, to drive (the shepherd driving the flock — Ps 78:52). Joseph stands for the northern tribes (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin — v2). יֹשֵׁב הַכְּרוּבִים הוֹפִיעָה (yōshêb hakkĕrûbîm hôpîʿāh, "you who are enthroned above the cherubim, shine forth!") — the ark-presence language (1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2). Hôpîʿ (hiphil: to shine forth in blazing light — Deut 33:2): the cherubim-enthroned God summoned to shine for his flock.
80.2: לִפְנֵי אֶפְרַיִם וּבִנְיָמִן וּמְנַשֶּׁה (lipnê ʾeprayim ûbinyāmîn ûmĕnashsheh, "before Ephraim and Benjamin and Manasseh") — the three Rachel tribes grouped together in the wilderness march behind the ark (Num 10:21-24). The petition calls the Shepherd to march at the front of his flock again, as at the Exodus. עוֹרְרָה אֶת-גְּבוּרָתְךָ וּלְכָה לִישֻׁעָתָה לָּנוּ (ʿôrĕrāh ʾet-gĕbûrātĕkā ûlĕkāh lîshûʿātāh llānû, "stir up your might and come to save us!") — ʿûr (piel: to rouse from inactivity — Ps 7:6: "rise up against the rage of my enemies"). The petition treats God as a sleeping or resting warrior who must be awakened to battle.
80.3: אֱלֹהִים הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ וְהָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה (ʾĕlōhîm hăshîbênû wĕhāʾêr pānêkā wĕniwwāshêʿāh, "Restore us, O God; let your face shine on us, that we may be saved!") — the first occurrence of the three-part refrain that structures the whole psalm (repeated at vv7 and 19 with escalating divine titles). Shûb (hiphil: to cause to return/restore — dense with covenant meaning: both physical return and inner restoration of the relationship). Hāʾêr pānêkā: "let your face shine" — the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25: yāʾêr YHWH pānāyw ʾêlêkā). The shining divine face is both the sign of favor and the source of salvation.
80.4: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת עַד-מָתַי עָשַׁנְתָּ בִּתְפִלַּת עַמֶּךָ (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt ʿad-mātay ʿāshantāh bĕtĕpillat ʿammekā, "O LORD God of hosts, how long will you smolder against your people's prayers?") — ʿāshantāh: second-person qal perfect of ʿāshan (to smoke, to smolder — Ps 74:1; Deut 29:20). God's anger described as smoke. The extraordinary claim: even the people's prayers provoke the divine anger rather than appeasing it. ʿAd-mātay (how long?) — the standard lament-time challenge (Ps 13:1-2; 74:10; 79:5).
80.5: הֶאֱכַלְתָּם לֶחֶם דִּמְעָה (heʾĕkhaltām leḥem dimʿāh, "you have fed them with the bread of tears") — dimʿāh: tears (from dāmaʿ: to weep). The covenant meal subverted: instead of the feast of Ps 23:5 or the honey of Deut 32:13-14, God gives bread made of tears. וַתַּשְׁקֵמוֹ בִּדְמָעוֹת שָׁלִישׁ (wattashqêmô bĕdĕmāʿôt shālîsh, "and you gave them tears to drink in full measure") — shālîsh: a measure of capacity — either a triple portion or a standard measure (either way, abundance is what was meant to sustain). The one who gave water from the rock (Exod 17) now gives tears in measured abundance; the wilderness provision inverted.
80.6: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ מָדוֹן לִשְׁכֵנֵינוּ (tĕśîmênû mādon lishkênênû, "you make us an object of contention for our neighbors") — mādon: strife, contention (from dîn: to judge/contend). Israel has become the disputed object that neighbors fight over as a bone of contention, not as a prize. וְאֹיְבֵינוּ יִלְעֲגוּ-לָמוֹ (wĕʾôyĕbênû yilʿăgû-lāmô, "and our enemies mock us") — lāʿag: to mock, to deride (Ps 79:4; 44:13-14). The nations meant to see Israel as proof of YHWH's power instead see proof of the Shepherd's abandonment of his flock.
80.7: The refrain at its midpoint with a fuller divine title: אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt hăshîbênû, "Restore us, O God of hosts") — ṣĕbāʾôt (of hosts/armies — YHWH as commander of the heavenly army) escalates the appeal: if God is Lord of all armies, why are enemies mocking his people? וְהָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה — the refrain unchanged: face-shining as the singular petition. The structural repetition is itself the liturgical act of persistence before God that the psalm dramatizes.
80.8: גֶּפֶן מִמִּצְרַיִם תַּסִּיעַ (gepen miMMiṣrayim tassîaʿ, "you uprooted a vine from Egypt") — nāsaʿ (hiphil: to uproot, to move camp): the Exodus as agricultural transplanting. The vine-allegory of Israel is a major prophetic tradition: Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21 ("I planted you as a choice vine — how then did you turn wild?"); Ezek 17; 19. תְּגָרֵשׁ גּוֹיִם וַתִּטָּעֶהָ (tĕgārêsh gôyim wattittāʿehā, "you drove out the nations and planted it") — gāresh: to drive out, expel (the same verb for Adam's expulsion from Eden — Gen 3:24). The vintner who cleared the land and planted with care is the same God whose face the Psalmist begs to shine (v7).
80.9: פִּנִּיתָ לְפָנֶיהָ (pinnîtā lĕpānêhā, "you cleared the ground before it") — pinnāh (piel: to clear away, remove obstacles — stone-clearing before planting). וַתַּשְׁרֵשׁ שָׁרָשֶׁיהָ וַתְּמַלֵּא-אָרֶץ (wattashrêsh shārāshêhā wattĕmallêʾ-ʾāreṣ, "it took deep root and filled all the land!") — shāresh (piel: to take root — denominative from shoresh: root). The transplanted vine established itself and expanded to fill the land — the Conquest and the settlement under David and Solomon at its maximum extent.
80.10: כָּסּוּ הָרִים צִלָּהּ (kāssû hārîm ṣillāh, "the mountains were covered with its shade") — a vine's shadow covering mountains: hyperbolic growth imagery for the empire at its maximum extent. וַעֲנָפֶיהָ אַרְזֵי-אֵל (waʿănāpêhā ʾarzê-ʾêl, "its branches were like the cedars of God") — ʾarzê-ʾêl: the divine superlative — the cedars of Lebanon (the tallest trees of the ancient Near East — 1 Kgs 5:6; Ps 104:16). Vine-branches reaching the scale of Lebanon's cedars: the empire of David-Solomon at its most imposing.
80.11: תְּשַׁלַּח קְצִירֶהָ עַד-יָם (tĕshallah qĕṣîrehā ʿad-yām, "it sent its branches to the sea") — the western border (the Mediterranean). וְאֶל-נָהָר יוֹנְקוֹתֶיהָ (wĕʾel-nāhār yônqôtêhā, "and its shoots to the River") — hannāhār: the Euphrates (the eastern boundary — Gen 15:18; Deut 11:24). The vine at its maximum extent reaches sea to river — the full territorial promise of Abraham's covenant. This is the vineyard at its height before the allegory turns to devastation.
80.12: לָמָּה פָּרַצְתָּ גְדֵרֶיהָ (lāmmāh pāraṣtā gĕdêrehā, "why have you broken down its walls?") — pāraṣ: to breach (2 Kgs 25:10). The gardener who planted the vine is the same one who has torn down its protective walls — the lament confronts the theodicy directly. וְאָרוּהָ כָּל-עֹבְרֵי דָרֶךְ (wĕʾārûhā kol-ʿōbĕrê dārek, "so that all who pass along the road pluck its fruit!") — ʾārah: to gather/pluck fruit. Without walls, the vineyard is exposed to random exploitation by anyone passing — the opposite of the careful cultivation of the vintner.
80.13: יְכַרְסְמֶנָּה חֲזִיר מִיָּעַר (yĕkharsĕmennāh ḥăzîr miyyāʿar, "the boar from the forest ravages it") — kārasam: to gnaw, to root up (the rooting behavior of a wild pig that tears up vineyards from below, uprooting the roots themselves). Ḥăzîr miyyāʿar: the wild boar from the forest — not a thief stealing fruit but a beast destroying the root system. וְזִיז שָׂדַי יִרְעֶנָּה (wĕzîz śāday yirʿennāh, "and the wild creatures of the field feed on it") — zîz: the swarming wildlife (Ps 50:11). The boar and the field-creatures together represent the foreign nations: large powers that uproot and small opportunists that graze what remains.
80.14: אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת שׁוּב-נָא (ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt shûb-nāʾ, "Return, we pray, O God of hosts!") — shûb + nāʾ (entreating particle): the verb that described Israel's restoration (v1) now turned toward God: "you turn, return!" הַבֵּט מִשָּׁמַיִם וּרְאֵה (habbêṭ mishshāmayim ûrĕʾêh, "look down from heaven, see!") — nābaṭ (hiphil: to look down from a height). וּפְקֹד גֶּפֶן זֹאת (ûpĕqōd gepen zōʾt, "and tend this vine!") — pāqad: to visit, attend to (the word covers both "to punish" and "to care for with blessing" — context demands: give the attending care of a gardener).
80.15: וְכַנָּה אֲשֶׁר-נָטְעָה יְמִינֶךָ (wĕkhannāh ʾăsher-nāṭĕʿāh yĕmînekā, "the stock your right hand planted") — kānāh: a root-stock, the base of a vine from which shoots grow (a rare agricultural term). Yĕmîn: the right hand of power (Ps 17:7; 20:6). God planted the vine with his own right hand — the vintner's personal investment is the basis of the appeal. וְעַל-בֵּן אִמַּצְתָּ לָּךְ (wĕʿal-bên ʾimmaṣtā llāk, "and the son you made strong for yourself") — ben: son/branch (possibly both — the shoot of the vine and the royal son). ʾĀmēṣ (piel: to make strong, establish). The "son" designation introduces a royal dimension developed in v17.
80.16: שְׂרֻפָה בָאֵשׁ כְּסוּחָה (śĕrûpāh bāʾêsh kĕsûḥāh, "it is burned with fire and cut down!") — sāḥāh: to cut away, prune (catastrophic pruning — the opposite of the careful tending requested in v14). Burned and cut: both the vine's surface and its roots destroyed. מִגַּעֲרַת פָּנֶיךָ יֹאבֵדוּ (miggaʿărat pānêkā yōʾbêdû, "may they perish at the rebuke of your face!") — gaʿārah: the divine rebuke (Ps 76:6 — the rebuke that put Sennacherib's army to sleep; Ps 18:15 — the rebuke that exposed the sea-bottom). The Psalmist turns the divine rebuke from its current target (the vine/Israel) toward the enemies who burned and cut.
80.17: תְּהִי-יָדְךָ עַל-אִישׁ יְמִינֶךָ עַל-בֶּן-אָדָם (tĕhî-yādĕkā ʿal-ʾîsh yĕmînekā ʿal-ben-ʾādām, "let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man!") — ʾîsh yĕmînekā: the man at God's right hand (the position of supreme honor — Ps 110:1). Ben-ʾādām: son of man/son of Adam (used in Ps 8:4 for human frailty — here with "whom you made strong for yourself"). In Dan 7:13 the eschatological figure who comes before the Ancient of Days receives universal dominion; Jesus's self-designation as the Son of Man activates this cluster. The vine's son/shoot (v15) is now the royal ben-ʾādām at God's right hand. אִמַּצְתָּ לָּךְ (ʾimmaṣtā llāk, "whom you have made strong for yourself") — same verb and object as v15, forming an inclusion: the vine's strength and the Son of Man's strength are the same divine action.
80.18: וְלֹא-נָסוֹג מִמֶּךָּ (wĕlōʾ-nāsôg mimmekā, "then we will not turn back from you!") — nāsôg: to retreat, to turn back (Ps 44:18: "we have not departed from you"; Ps 78:57: the ancestors turned away like a faithless bow). The vow: if God restores the vine through the son/man, Israel will not retreat from the restored covenant. תְּחַיֵּנוּ וּבְשִׁמְךָ נִקְרָא (tĕḥayyênû ûbĕshimĕkā niqrāʾ, "give us life, and we will call on your name!") — ḥāyāh (piel: to give life — the quickening of the dead vine). To call on the divine name is the defining act of covenant worship (Gen 4:26; 12:8).
80.19: The final refrain completes its progression: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt hăshîbênû, "Restore us, O LORD God of hosts!") — the fullest divine title: YHWH + ʾĕlōhîm + ṣĕbāʾôt.
