The refrain's three occurrences build: v3 = ʾĕlōhîm; v7 = ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt; v19 = YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt — escalating toward the full covenant name. הָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה — the petition unchanged: restoration does not come through Israel's achievement but through the return of the divine face.

81.1: Psalm 81 is a festal psalm set to the gittît tune (Ps 8; 84 — possibly from Gath or a harvest/winepress instrument). הַרְנִינוּ לֵאלֹהִים עוּזֵּנוּ (harninû lêʾlōhîm ʿuzzênû, "Sing aloud to God our strength!") — rānan: to ring out, to shout in joy (the piercing sound of festival exuberance). ʿUzzênû: "our strength" — the divine name replaced by an epithet of power (Ps 46:1). הָרִיעוּ לֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (hārîʿû lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb, "shout for joy to the God of Jacob!") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to raise a war-cry/shout of joy — the ambiguous root covers both). The God of Jacob (Gen 28:13-16) — the covenant made at Bethel with the wanderer who wrestled until blessing came.
81.2: שְׂאוּ-זִמְרָה וּתְנוּ-תֹף (śĕʾû-zimrāh ûtĕnû-tōp, "raise a song, beat the tambourine!") — tōp: the hand-drum/tambourine (Miriam's instrument at the Exodus — Exod 15:20; the women greeting David — 1 Sam 18:6). כִּנּוֹר נָעִים עִם-נָבֶל (kinnôr nāʿîm ʿim-nābel, "the sweet lyre with the harp") — kinnôr nāʿîm: "pleasant/sweet lyre" (the same root as David's epithet nĕʿîm zĕmirôt yiśrāʾêl — "the sweet singer of Israel" — 2 Sam 23:1). The full instrumental ensemble assembled for the festival.
81.3: תִּקְעוּ בַחֹדֶשׁ שׁוֹפָר בַּכֵּסֶה לְיוֹם חַגֵּנוּ (tiqĕʿû baḥōdesh shôpār bakkesse lĕyôm ḥaggênû, "blow the shofar at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day!") — baḥōdesh: at the new moon (the beginning of the month); bakkesse: at the fullness/covering (the full moon — the 15th of the month). The combination identifies either the Feast of Tabernacles (Tishri 1 = new moon; Tishri 15 = full moon) or the Passover cycle. The shôpār (ram's horn) is blown at both new moons (Num 10:10) and major festivals (Lev 23:24).
81.4: כִּי חֹק לְיִשְׂרָאֵל הוּא מִשְׁפָּט לֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (kî ḥōq lĕyiśrāʾêl hûʾ mishpāṭ lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb, "for it is a statute for Israel, a rule given by the God of Jacob") — ḥōq: a fixed ordinance (the decrees of the sacred calendar — Lev 23). Mishpāṭ: a determined ruling. The two terms emphasize fixity (ḥōq) and authority (mishpāṭ): this feast is neither optional nor human invention but divinely inscribed in the covenant calendar.
81.5: עֵדוּת בִּיהוֹסֵף שָׂמוֹ בְּצֵאתוֹ עַל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (ʿêdût bîhôsêp śāmô bĕṣêtô ʿal-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim, "he established it as a decree in Joseph when he went out over the land of Egypt") — the pronoun in bĕṣêtô (when he went out) is grammatically ambiguous: it could refer to Joseph rising to power, to Israel departing Egypt at the Exodus, or to God going out against Egypt in judgment (Exod 11:4). All three readings are defensible. שְׂפַת לֹא-יָדַעְתִּי אֶשְׁמָע (śĕpat lōʾ-yādaʿtî ʾeshmaʿ, "I heard a voice I had not known") — the dramatic shift into first person: the Psalmist (or personified Israel) says "I hear a language I did not know." This marks the transition into the embedded divine speech that runs through v16.
81.6: Divine speech begins. הֲסִירוֹתִי מִסֵּבֶל שִׁכְמוֹ (hăsîrôtî missibbel shikmô, "I removed the burden from his shoulder") — sibbel: the burden-basket (Exod 1:11 — Israel forced to carry burdens in Egypt; Exod 2:11 — Moses "saw their burdens"). Shikmo: his shoulder (the load-bearing joint). כַּפָּיו מִדּוּד תַּעֲבֹרְנָה (kappāyw middûd taʿăbōrnāh, "his hands were freed from the basket") — dûd: a large pot/basket for carrying clay bricks. The Exodus liberation described in terms of physical release from forced labor — hands freed from the brick-basket grounds the festival command in lived memory.
81.7: בַּצָּרָה קָרָאתָ וָאֲחַלְּצֶךָּ (baṣṣārāh qārāʾtā wāʾăḥallĕṣekā, "in your distress you called, and I rescued you!") — ṣārāh: the narrow/distressed situation (Ps 18:6). Ḥālaṣ (piel: to draw out, liberate — pulling from a tight place). אֶעֶנְךָ בְּסֵתֶר רַעַם (ʾeʿenkā bĕsêter raʿam, "I answered you from the secret/hidden place of thunder") — sêter raʿam: the dark storm-cloud from which YHWH spoke at Sinai (Exod 19:16-20; 20:18-21). אֶבְחָנְךָ עַל-מֵי מְרִיבָה סֶלָה (ʾebḥānĕkā ʿal-mê mĕrîbāh selāh, "I tested you at the waters of Meribah!") — Exod 17:7 and Num 20:13: where Israel tested God and God tested Israel (Ps 95:8-9). Selāh.
81.8: שְׁמַע עַמִּי וְאָעִידָה בָּךְ (shĕmaʿ ʿammî wĕʾāʿîdāh bāk, "listen, O my people, while I admonish you!") — ʿûd (hiphil: to testify against, to warn, to admonish — as a covenant witness). The divine speech becomes a covenant lawsuit: God as the witness against Israel's faithlessness. יִשְׂרָאֵל אִם-תִּשְׁמַע-לִי (yiśrāʾêl ʾim-tishmaʿ-lî, "O Israel, if you would only listen to me!") — the conditional (Deut 28 pattern): what follows are covenant terms and their consequences, not mere exhortation.
81.9: לֹא-יִהְיֶה בְךָ אֵל זָר (lōʾ-yihyeh bĕkā ʾêl zār, "there must be no foreign god among you!") — ʾêl zār: strange/foreign god (Deut 32:12). וְלֹא תִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לְאֵל נֵכָר (wĕlōʾ tishtaḥăweh lĕʾêl nêkār, "you must not bow down to any alien god!") — nêkār: the foreign, alien (Ruth 2:10). Two words for foreignness (zār and nêkār) applied to idols — restating the First Commandment in different language.
81.10: אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ הַמַּעַלְךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (ʾānōkî yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhêkā hammmaʿalkā mêʾereṣ miṣrāyim, "I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt!") — verbatim citation of the Decalogue preamble (Exod 20:2; Deut 5:6). The divine speech in the psalm quotes itself — the God who established Sinai re-presents it in the festal setting. הַרְחֶב-פִּיךָ וַאֲמַלְאֵהוּ (harḥeb-pîkā waʾămalʾêhû, "open your mouth wide, and I will fill it!") — like a parent bird feeding nestlings who open their mouths wide to receive (Ps 104:27-28). The wide-open mouth is the posture of receptive dependence — contrasted with the people's actual behavior in v11.
81.11: וְלֹא-שָׁמַע עַמִּי לְקוֹלִי (wĕlōʾ-shāmaʿ ʿammî lĕqôlî, "but my people did not listen to my voice") — the heartbreak of divine speech: the invitation of v10 refused by the very people addressed as "my people" (ʿammî — the covenant-ownership word). Lōʾ-shāmaʿ: the Shema inverted. וְיִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא-אָבָה לִי (wĕyiśrāʾêl lōʾ-ʾābāh lî, "Israel would not submit to me!") — ʾābāh: to be willing, to consent (Isa 1:19: "if you are willing and obedient, you shall eat the good of the land"). Israel's "not-willing" is the active rejection of the feast God offered.
81.12: וָאֲשַׁלְּחֵהוּ בִּשְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (wāʾăshallĕḥêhû bishŏrîrût libbām, "so I gave them over to their stubborn hearts") — shālaḥ: to send, to release, to let go (releasing them to their own trajectory). Shĕrîrût lêb: stubbornness/hardness of heart (Deut 29:18; Jer 3:17; 7:24). Paul's parallel in Rom 1:24: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός ("God gave them over") — the same judicial action: abandonment-to-consequence as punishment for willful rejection. יֵלְכוּ בְּמוֹעֲצוֹתֵיהֶם (yêlĕkû bĕmôʿăṣôtêhem, "to follow their own devices") — môʿēṣāh: counsel, deliberate plan (from yāʿaṣ: to counsel). Stubbornness generates its own counsels; God's release is to let the self-generated counsel run its course.
81.13: לוּ עַמִּי שֹׁמֵעַ לִי (lû ʿammî shōmêaʿ lî, "if only my people would listen to me!") — lû: the particle of unfulfilled wish (as in Num 14:2: "if only we had died in Egypt!" — the contrary-to-fact wishful). God expressing a divine wish that Israel had listened — genuine divine longing over uncollected faithfulness. יִשְׂרָאֵל בִּדְרָכַי יְהַלֵּכוּ (yiśrāʾêl bîdrākhay yĕhallêkû, "if only Israel would walk in my ways!") — dĕrākhay: my ways (the halakah — the walk-path of covenantal life). The divine lament over forfeited blessing.
81.14: כִּמְעַט אוֹיְבֵיהֶם אַכְנִיעַ (kimĕʿaṭ ʾôyĕbêhem ʾakhnîaʿ, "I would quickly subdue their enemies!") — kimĕʿaṭ: "as a small thing, soon, quickly" — subduing enemies would be a small matter for God. Kānaʿ (hiphil: to bring low, to subdue — Josh 7:12-13; Judg 4:23). וְעַל-צָרֵיהֶם אָשִׁיב יָדִי (wĕʿal-ṣārêhem ʾāshîb yādî, "and turn my hand against their foes!") — shûb yād: to turn one's hand against — the divine hand employed against Israel's enemies instead of against Israel. The counterfactual: IF Israel listened → THEN enemies quickly subdued.
81.15: מְשַׂנְאֵי יְהוָה יְכַחֲשׁוּ-לוֹ (mĕśanʾê yĕhwāh yĕkhaḥăshû-lô, "those who hate YHWH would cringe before him!") — kāḥash (piel: to cringe, to fawn in feigned submission — as a conquered party): the enemies who hated God would come crawling. וִיהִי עִתָּם לְעוֹלָם (wîhî ʿittām lĕʿôlām, "and Israel's time would have endured forever!") — ʿittām: their appointed time of blessing/wellbeing. The reward of obedience: Israel's peace fixed, not subject to the rise-and-fall cycles of the Judges.
81.16: וַיַּאֲכִלֵהוּ מֵחֵלֶב חִטָּה (wayyaʾăkhîlêhû mêḥêleb ḥiṭṭāh, "I would have fed you with the finest of the wheat!") — ḥêleb ḥiṭṭāh: "the fat/richest of the wheat" (the very finest grain — ḥêleb is the word for the best portion offered in sacrifice: Lev 3:16-17). וּמִצּוּר דְּבַשׁ אַשְׂבִּיעֶךָ (ûmiṣṣûr dĕbash ʾasbîʿekā, "and with honey from the rock I would satisfy you!") — dĕbash miṣṣûr: honey from the rock (Deut 32:13 — wild honeybees nesting in rock clefts; the wilderness providing abundance in an unexpected place). The conditional feast: had Israel listened, the finest wheat and rock-honey would have been their portion — the wilderness provisions multiplied to their ultimate form.

82.1: Psalm 82 is the most theologically concentrated psalm in the collection — the divine council under judgment. אֱלֹהִים נִצָּב בַּעֲדַת-אֵל בְּקֶרֶב אֱלֹהִים יִשְׁפֹּט (ʾĕlōhîm niṣṣāb baʿădat-ʾêl bĕqereb ʾĕlōhîm yishpōṭ, "God has taken his place in the divine assembly; in the midst of the gods he holds judgment!") — niṣṣāb (niphal of nāṣab: standing firmly, taking the presiding judge's position). ʿĂdat-ʾêl: the council/congregation of God (the divine council — the bĕnê ʾĕlōhîm of Gen 6:2; Job 1:6; 2:1; Ps 89:6-7: heavenly beings attending the divine sovereign). Bĕqereb ʾĕlōhîm yishpōṭ: "in the midst of the gods he holds court" — the participial yishpōṭ (ongoing characteristic judging). Whether the ʾĕlōhîm are human judges or divine beings is the central interpretive question: the death-like-Adam of v7 makes greater sense if addressed to beings who considered themselves immortal.
82.2: עַד-מָתַי תִּשְׁפְּטוּ-עָוֶל (ʿad-mātay tishpĕṭû-ʿāwel, "how long will you go on judging unjustly?") — ʿad-mātay: the "how long" of lament, now in the divine mouth. ʿāwel: injustice, moral perversity (what is crooked). The divine council members who were meant to execute divine justice have done the opposite. וּפְנֵי רְשָׁעִים תִּשְׂאוּ-סֶלָה (ûpĕnê rĕshāʿîm tiśʾû-selāh, "showing partiality to the wicked! Selah.") — nāśāʾ pānîm: "to lift the face" (giving preferential treatment — the exact language Deut 10:17 uses for what God does NOT do: "who does not lift faces"). Selāh.
82.3: שִׁפְטוּ-דַל וְיָתוֹם עָנִי וָרָשׁ הַצְדִּיקוּ (shiptû-dal wĕyātôm ʿānî wārāsh haṣdîqû, "defend the poor and fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute!") — four terms for the socially vulnerable: dal (the weak/economically depleted), yātôm (the orphan — without a father-protector), ʿānî (the afflicted), rāsh (the poor). Haṣdîq (hiphil: to declare righteous, to vindicate in court): the positive mandate — the council's job was to be advocates for the voiceless. This is the agenda of Isa 1:17; Mic 6:8; and the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:6).
82.4: פַּלְּטוּ-דַל וְאֶבְיוֹן מִיַּד רְשָׁעִים הַצִּילוּ (palĕṭû-dal wĕʾebyôn miyad rĕshāʿîm haṣṣîlû, "rescue the poor and needy; save them from the grip of the wicked!") — pālaṭ (piel: to cause to escape, to rescue from the narrow place); nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away to safety). Two rescue verbs paired with two victim terms. Miyad rĕshāʿîm: "from the hand of the wicked" — the hand as the instrument of power and oppression. This is the divine council's actual mandate: to liberate the powerless from the grip of the powerful.
82.5: לֹא יָדְעוּ וְלֹא יָבִינוּ בַּחֲשֵׁכָה יִתְהַלָּכוּ (lōʾ yādĕʿû wĕlōʾ yābînû baḥăshêkāh yithallākû, "they have no knowledge and no understanding; they walk about in darkness!") — the triple failure: no knowledge (lōʾ yādĕʿû), no understanding (lōʾ yābînû), walking in darkness (baḥăshêkāh). The council-members who should dispense divine wisdom are themselves without wisdom and in darkness. יִמּוֹטוּ כָּל-מוֹסְדֵי אָרֶץ (yimmôṭû kol-môsĕdê ʾāreṣ, "all the foundations of the earth are shaken!") — môsĕdîm: foundations (Ps 18:15; Job 38:4). The moral failure of the heavenly council reverberates cosmically: when justice fails at the heavenly level, the earth's foundations themselves become unstable (Prov 2:21-22; Isa 24:18-19).
82.6: אֲנִי-אָמַרְתִּי אֱלֹהִים אַתֶּם וּבְנֵי עֶלְיוֹן כֻּלְּכֶם (ʾănî-ʾāmartî ʾĕlōhîm ʾattem ûbĕnê ʿelyôn kullĕkem, "I declared, 'You are gods, sons of the Most High, every one of you!'") — the divine "I" recalling a prior declaration: God himself conferred the status ʾĕlōhîm and bĕnê ʿelyôn (sons of the Most High) on the council-members. This is the foundation from which v7's reversal is so devastating. Jesus cites this verse in John 10:34-35: "Is it not written in your Law, 'I said, you are gods'? If he called them gods to whom the word of God came — and Scripture cannot be broken — how can you say that the one the Father consecrated and sent into the world is blaspheming by saying 'I am God's Son'?" The argument moves from the lesser to the greater: if the divine council received "gods," how much more the one God consecrated and sent.
82.7: אָכֵן כְּאָדָם תְּמוּתוּן וּכְאַחַד הַשָּׂרִים תִּפֹּלוּ (ʾāken kĕʾādām tĕmûtûn ûkĕʾaḥad haśśārîm tippōlû, "nevertheless, you will die like the man [Adam]/like human beings, and fall like any fallen ruler!") — ʾāken: "nevertheless, however" — the adversative that shatters the status of v6. Kĕʾādām: "like Adam" or "like a human" — the polysemy is likely intentional: both readings operate simultaneously. To die like Adam is to share the curse of Gen 3:19 ("you are dust, and to dust you shall return") — the beings designated "gods" will share the fate of the one who grasped for divine status and received mortality instead. Kĕʾaḥad haśśārîm tippōlû: "like one of the rulers you will fall" — Isa 14:12-15 (the fall of the king of Babylon). The ultimate theodicy: the unjust divine council will be subject to death itself.
82.8: קוּמָה אֱלֹהִים שָׁפְטָה הָאָרֶץ (qûmāh ʾĕlōhîm shāpĕṭāh hāʾāreṣ, "Rise up, O God, judge the earth!") — the Psalmist's voice returns for the first time since v1. Qûm (the warrior's arising — Ps 68:1: "let God arise, let his enemies be scattered"): the call for God to actively judge replaces the failed council. כִּי-אַתָּה תִנְחַל בְּכָל-הַגּוֹיִם (kî-ʾattāh tinḥal bĕkhol-haggôyim, "for all the nations belong to you!") — nāḥal: to inherit, to take possession. The psalm ends on a massive claim: not just Israel but all the nations are God's inheritance. The unjust sub-rulers replaced by the one just Sovereign who owns every nation — the universal scope resolves the crisis of the psalm.

83.1: Psalm 83 is the final Asaph psalm and the longest. אֱלֹהִים אַל-דֳּמִי-לָךְ אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ וְאַל-תִּשְׁקֹט אֵל (ʾĕlōhîm ʾal-dŏmî-lāk ʾal-teḥĕrash wĕʾal-tishqōṭ ʾêl, "O God, do not remain silent; do not hold your peace or be still, O God!") — three synonymous verbs of divine inaction: dāmam (to be silent, to stand still); ḥārash (to be deaf/silent — Isa 42:14: a woman who has been quiet in labor); shāqaṭ (to be at rest, undisturbed — the tranquility of the uninvolved). The triple negation is the urgency of the lament: three different kinds of divine non-action are all pleaded against simultaneously.
83.2: כִּי-הִנֵּה אוֹיְבֶיךָ יֶהֱמָיוּן (kî-hinneh ʾôyĕbêkā yehĕmāyûn, "For behold, your enemies are in an uproar!") — hāmāh: to roar, to be in tumult (used of the sea — Ps 46:3; 65:7 — and of roaring enemy nations — Ps 46:6). וּמְשַׂנְאֶיךָ נָשְׂאוּ רֹאשׁ (ûmĕśanʾêkā nāśĕʾû rōʾsh, "those who hate you have raised their heads!") — nāśāʾ rōʾsh: to lift the head (the gesture of arrogant self-assertion — Judg 8:28; Ps 110:7). The enemies are described as those who hate God — their opposition to Israel is opposition to the God of Israel.
83.3: עַל-עַמְּךָ יַעֲרִימוּ סוֹד (ʿal-ʿammĕkā yaʿărîmû sôd, "they devise cunning plans against your people!") — ʿāram (hiphil: to be crafty, to devise schemes — the same root as the "shrewd/crafty" serpent of Gen 3:1). Sôd: a secret council (Jer 23:18 — the divine sôd in which the true prophet stands). The enemies have their own counter-sôd against God's people. וְיִתְיָעֲצוּ עַל-צְפוּנֶיךָ (wĕyiṯyāʿăṣû ʿal-ṣĕpûnêkā, "and scheme against your treasured ones!") — ṣĕpûnîm: the hidden/treasured ones (from ṣāpan: to hide, to treasure — Col 3:3: "your life is hidden with Christ in God").
83.4: אָמְרוּ לְכוּ וְנַכְחִידֵם מִגּוֹי (ʾāmĕrû lĕkû wĕnakhḥîdêm miggôy, "they say, 'Come, let us wipe them out as a nation!'") — kāḥad (hiphil: to destroy completely, to annihilate — to cause to cease to exist). The genocidal intention: to remove Israel from the category of gôy (nation). וְלֹא-יִזָּכֵר שֵׁם-יִשְׂרָאֵל עוֹד (wĕlōʾ-yizzākêr shêm-yiśrāʾêl ʿôd, "let the name of Israel be remembered no more!") — zākar (niphal: to be remembered). The goal: not merely military defeat but complete obliteration of memory — the erasure of Israel's name from human consciousness.
83.5: כִּי נוֹעֲצוּ לֵב יַחְדָּו (kî nôʿăṣû lêb yaḥdāw, "for they conspire together with one accord!") — yāʿaṣ: to counsel, to scheme. Yaḥdāw: together, in unified concert. What the Shema calls Israel to do toward God (to love with the whole heart), the nations are doing against him. עָלֶיךָ בְּרִית יִכְרֹתוּ (ʿālêkā bĕrît yikrōtû, "they have made a covenant against you!") — kārat bĕrît: to cut a covenant (the standard treaty formula). The nations have made a formal alliance directed against God himself — Ps 2:2: the kings of the earth conspiring against YHWH and his Anointed.
83.6: אָהֳלֵי אֱדוֹם וְיִשְׁמְעֵאלִים מוֹאָב וְהַגְרִים (ʾōhŏlê ʾĕdôm wĕyishmaʿêlîm môʾāb wĕhagrîm, "the tents of Edom and the Ishmaelites, Moab and the Hagrites") — the first four of a 10-nation coalition. Edom (descended from Esau — Israel's twin, carrying the deepest fratricidal betrayal: Obad 10-14; Ps 137:7); Ishmaelites (descendants of Ishmael — the traders who carried Joseph to Egypt — Gen 37:25); Moab (east of the Dead Sea — descended from Lot, fighting Israel from Balak onward — Num 22-24); Hagrites (descendants of Hagar — northern Arabian tribe: 1 Chr 5:10,19-20). Each carries a genealogical relationship with Israel making the betrayal doubly bitter.
83.7: גְּבַל וְעַמּוֹן וַעֲמָלֵק פְּלֶשֶׁת עִם-יֹשְׁבֵי צוֹר (gĕbal wĕʿammôn waʿămālêq pĕleshet ʿim-yōshĕbê ṣôr, "Gebal and Ammon and Amalek, Philistia with the inhabitants of Tyre!") — Gebal (Phoenician Byblos, or Edomite Gebalene); Ammon (east of the Jordan, descended from Lot — Judg 10-11; 2 Sam 10); Amalek (the perpetual wilderness enemy — Exod 17:8-16; Deut 25:17-19); Philistia (the coastal confederation — primary antagonist in Judges-Samuel); Tyre (the great Phoenician trading city — Ezek 26-28). The geographic sweep covers east, south, north, and west.
83.8: גַּם-אַשּׁוּר נִלְוָה עִמָּם (gam-ʾashshûr nilwāh ʿimmām, "Assyria too has joined them!") — lāwāh (niphal: to join oneself, to attach — the same root as Levi's name). Assyria's attachment would date the setting to the 8th-7th centuries BCE when Assyria dominated the region. הָיוּ זְרוֹעַ לִבְנֵי-לוֹט סֶלָה (hāyû zĕrôaʿ libnê-lôṭ selāh, "they lend their strength to the children of Lot!") — zĕrôaʿ: the arm/strength (Ps 77:15; Isa 52:10). Assyria's massive military arm strengthening the smaller regional powers. The children of Lot (Moab and Ammon — v6) endorsed by the ancient Near East's superpower. Selāh marks completion of the 10-nation inventory.
83.9: עֲשֵׂה-לָהֶם כְּמִדְיָן כְּסִיסְרָא כְיָבִין בְּנַחַל קִישׁוֹן (ʿăśêh-lāhem kĕmidyān kĕsîsĕrāʾ kĕyābîn bĕnaḥal qîshôn, "Do to them as you did to Midian, as to Sisera and Jabin at the river Kishon!") — three historical precedents: Midian (the defeat of 135,000 enemy soldiers under Gideon — Judg 7-8; divine intervention that turned the enemies against each other); Sisera (the Canaanite general whose army was overwhelmed at the Kishon in the rain — Judg 4-5); Jabin (the king of Hazor behind Sisera — Judg 4:2). The Kishon — a wadi near Megiddo, Judg 5:21: "the torrent Kishon swept them away." Three situations where God alone won the battle are the template for the present petition.
83.10: נִשְׁמְדוּ בְעֵין-דֹּאר (nishĕmĕdû bĕʿên-dōʾr, "they were destroyed at En-dor!") — shāmad (niphal: to be annihilated). En-dor: near the Jezreel valley at the foot of Mount Moreh, near Gideon's battlefield (not the En-dor of the witch — 1 Sam 28:7). הָיוּ דֹמֶן לָאֲדָמָה (hāyû dōmen lāʾădāmāh, "they became dung for the ground!") — dōmen: dung, manure (Jer 8:2; 9:22 — "they shall be like dung on the surface of the ground"). The bodies of the powerful coalition become fertilizer — the ultimate reversal of their ambition to wipe Israel from the earth.
83.11: שִׁיתֵמוֹ נְדִיבֵימוֹ כְּעֹרֵב וְכִזְאֵב (shîtêmô nĕdîbêmô kĕʿōrêb wĕkhizʾêb, "make their nobles like Oreb and Zeeb!") — Oreb (Raven) and Zeeb (Wolf): the two princes of Midian captured and executed by the Ephraimites at the Jordan during Gideon's pursuit — Judg 7:25 ("they killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb, and Zeeb at the wine press of Zeeb"). וְכִזְבַח וּכְצַלְמֻנָּע כָּל-נְסִיכֵימוֹ (wĕkhizbaḥ ûkĕṣalmunnāʿ kol-nĕsîkêmô, "and all their princes like Zebah and Zalmunna!") — the two kings of Midian whom Gideon personally executed as blood-avengers for his brothers — Judg 8:5-21. All four Midianite leaders are paradigms of God's power to eliminate the most dangerous enemies.
83.12: אֲשֶׁר אָמְרוּ נִירְשָׁה לָּנוּ אֵת נְאוֹת אֱלֹהִים (ʾăsher ʾāmĕrû nîrĕshāh llānû ʾêt nĕʾôt ʾĕlōhîm, "who said, 'Let us seize for ourselves the pastures of God!'") — nāʾāh: pastureland, the pleasant meadows (Ps 23:2: "green pastures"). Nîrĕshāh: we will inherit/possess (from yārash: the covenantal inheritance term). The coalition's specific goal: not just to defeat Israel but to take possession of the land promised to Abraham. God's land claimed by those who reject his covenant (Lev 25:23: "the land is mine").
83.13: אֱלֹהַי שִׁיתֵמוֹ כַגַּלְגַּל כְּקַשׁ לִפְנֵי-רוּחַ (ʾĕlōhay shîtêmô khagalgal kĕqash lipnê-rûaḥ, "O my God, make them like whirling dust, like chaff before the wind!") — galgal: a wheel, something rolling — specifically the round, hollow-stemmed tumbleweed that breaks off at the root in autumn and rolls across the landscape driven by wind (Isa 17:13: "like rolling things before the wind"; Job 21:18). Qash: dry straw/chaff (the winnowed lightweight residue — Ps 1:4; Isa 40:24). The coalition that threatened permanent erasure of Israel is itself to be erased without trace.
83.14: כְּאֵשׁ תִּבְעַר-יָעַר וּכְלַהֲבָה תְּלַהֵט הָרִים (kĕʾêsh tibʿar-yāʿar ûkĕlahăbāh tĕlahêṭ hārîm, "as fire consumes the forest, as the flame sets the mountains ablaze!") — two fire images for thoroughness: bāʿar (to burn/consume — the complete combustion that does not stop until everything combustible is gone); lāhaṭ (piel: to flame fiercely, to scorch — the setting-ablaze of the mountains). The fire imagery prepares for v15's petition: "pursue them with your storm and terrify them with your tempest."
83.15: כֵּן תִּרְדְּפֵם בְּסַעֲרֶךָ (kên tirdĕpêm bĕsaʿărekā, "so pursue them with your storm!") — sāʿar: the violent storm-wind (the whirlwind that took Elijah — 2 Kgs 2:1; YHWH's answer to Job — Job 38:1). וּבְסוּפָתְךָ תְבַהֲלֵם (ûbĕsûpātĕkā tĕbahălêm, "and terrify them with your tempest!") — sûpāh: a tornado (Prov 1:27; Isa 17:13). Bāhal (piel: to terrify, to throw into sudden panic — Josh 10:10; 1 Sam 7:10). The petition connects to Sisera's precedent: the Kishon river storm panicked his chariots (Judg 5:20-21: "the stars fought from heaven... against Sisera").
83.16: מַלֵּא פְנֵיהֶם קָלוֹן (mallêʾ pĕnêhem qālôn, "fill their faces with shame!") — qālôn: dishonor, disgrace (the public opposite of kābôd/glory — Prov 3:35). וִיבַקְשׁוּ שִׁמְךָ יְהוָה (wîbaqshû shimĕkā yĕhwāh, "that they may seek your name, O LORD!") — the pivotal purpose clause: the shame of v16 has a redemptive goal. The enemies are shamed so that they will seek the divine name. Bāqash shêm: to seek the name (acknowledging and pursuing the God who has acted — Isa 55:6: "seek the LORD while he may be found"). The imprecation has a missionary ambition: judgment as the path to the enemies' conversion.
83.17: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיִבָּהֲלוּ עֲדֵי-עַד (yēbōshû wĕyibbāhălû ʿădê-ʿad, "let them be put to shame and dismayed forever!") — bôsh: to be put to shame; bāhal (niphal: to be terrified, thrown into disarray). ʿĂdê-ʿad: "forever, unto eternity" — the temporal absolute for those who do not repent. וְיַחְפְּרוּ וְיֹאבֵדוּ (wĕyaḥpĕrû wĕyōʾbêdû, "let them be disgraced and perish!") — ḥāpar: to be confounded (to dig/scratch in the dust in humiliation); ʾābed: to perish. This verse covers those who refuse the redemptive shame of vv16,18 — the two tracks: shame-leading-to-seeking or shame-leading-to-perishing.
83.18: וְיֵדְעוּ כִּי-אַתָּה שִׁמְךָ יְהוָה לְבַדֶּךָ עֶלְיוֹן עַל-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (wĕyêdĕʿû kî-ʾattāh shimĕkā yĕhwāh lĕbaddĕkā ʿelyôn ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ, "let them know that you alone, whose name is the LORD, are the Most High over all the earth!") — the goal of the entire imprecatory psalm: not Israel's vengeance but universal knowledge (yādaʿ) of who YHWH is. Lĕbaddĕkā: "you alone" (Deut 32:12). ʿElyôn: the Most High (Gen 14:18-22; Dan 7:18,22). The psalm that began with the coalition plotting to erase Israel's name ends with the petition that the nations know the divine name — the eschatological reversal of Ps 2: conspiracy against God ends in the nations' recognition of his sovereignty.

84.1: Psalm 84 is one of the finest poems in the entire Psalter — a pilgrim's psalm from the sons of Korah, set to the gittît melody (Ps 8; 81). מַה-יְּדִידוֹת מִשְׁכְּנוֹתֶיךָ יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת (mah-yĕdîdôt mishkĕnôtêkā yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt, "How lovely are your dwelling places, O LORD of hosts!") — yĕdîdôt: loveliness, belovedness (the plural abstract noun from yādîd: beloved/lovely — the same root related to love/affection). Mishkĕnôtêkā: your dwelling places (plural of the mishkān — the Tabernacle/Dwelling: Num 24:5). The exclamatory mah (how!) frames the opening as a cry of wonder, not a description. The Psalmist has not yet arrived — still on the road, burning with longing for a place he loves more than anywhere on earth.
84.2: נִכְסְפָה וְגַם-כָּלְתָה נַפְשִׁי לְחַצְרוֹת יְהוָה (niksĕpāh wĕgam-kāltāh napshî lĕḥaṣrôt yĕhwāh, "my soul longs, yes, faints for the courts of the LORD!") — two verbs of extreme desire: kāsap (niphal: to pale/grow pale with longing — the whitening of intense desire: Gen 31:30: "you longed intensely for your father's house"); kālāh (to be consumed, to fail — Ps 119:81: "my soul kāltāh for your salvation"). The soul is consumed with longing until it fails. לִבִּי וּבְשָׂרִי יְרַנְּנוּ אֶל-אֵל חָי (libbî ûbĕśārî yĕrannĕnû ʾel-ʾêl ḥāy, "my heart and my flesh cry out for the living God!") — the whole person (heart = inner; flesh = body) crying simultaneously. ʾÊl ḥāy: the living God (Ps 42:2; 2 Kgs 19:4,16).
84.3: גַּם-צִפּוֹר מָצְאָה בַיִת וּדְרוֹר קֵן לָהּ (gam-ṣippôr māṣĕʾāh bayit ûdĕrôr qên lāh, "even the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself!") — ṣippôr: small bird/sparrow (the most ordinary, inexpensive bird — Matt 10:29: "are not two sparrows sold for a penny?"); dĕrôr: the swallow or swift (a migratory bird that travels thousands of miles). Both birds have found their home; the Psalmist is still on the pilgrim road. The irony is tender and painful: the least of birds nests in the sanctuary; the Psalmist who loves it most cannot yet be there. אֲשֶׁר-שָׁתָה אֶפְרֹחֶיהָ אֶת-מִזְבְּחוֹתֶיךָ (ʾăsher-shātāh ʾephrōḥêhā ʾet-mizbĕḥôtêkā, "where she may lay her young — even your altars, O LORD of hosts, my King and my God!") — the bird nests at the very altars: the holiest point of the sanctuary. The contrast between the bird's effortless belonging and the Psalmist's aching distance is the psalm's central tension.
84.4: אַשְׁרֵי יוֹשְׁבֵי בֵיתֶךָ עוֹד יְהַלְלוּךָ סֶּלָה (ʾashrê yôshĕbê bêtekā ʿôd yĕhallĕlûkā selāh, "Blessed are those who dwell in your house; they are ever singing your praise! Selah.") — the first of three ʾashrê beatitudes in Psalm 84 (vv4, 5, 12). ʾAshĕrê: "how blessed!" — the plural of ʾōsher (blessing/happiness). The Levites and priests who lived in the temple courts: yôshĕbê bêtekā (those who dwell in the house). They are ever (ʿôd: still, continuously) praising. The Psalmist on the road looks at permanent residents and pronounces them blessed. The beatitude structure sets up the psalm's progress: dwellers (v4), pilgrims (v5), trusters (v12). Selāh.
84.5: אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם עוֹז-לוֹ בָךְ מְסִלּוֹת בִּלְבָבָם (ʾashrê ʾādām ʿōz-lô bāk mĕsillôt bibbābām, "Blessed is the man whose strength is in you, in whose heart are the pilgrim roads!") — the second ʾashrê: not the temple-resident but the active pilgrim. ʿŌz lô bāk: whose strength is in you (Ps 46:1 — not in his own stamina). Mĕsillôt bibbābām: "pilgrimage roads are in their heart" — mĕsillāh: a raised highway (Isa 40:3: "prepare the way of the LORD, make straight a highway"). The roads exist first in the interior: the longing for Zion creates the pilgrimage. The Psalms of Ascent (120-134) are the liturgical expression of those interior roads.
84.6: עֹבְרֵי בְּעֵמֶק הַבָּכָא מַעְיָן יְשִׁיתוּהוּ (ʿōbĕrê bĕʿêmeq habbākhāʾ maʿyān yĕshîtûhû, "as they pass through the valley of Baka, they make it a place of springs!") — ʿêmeq habbākhāʾ: the "valley of Baka" — bākhāʾ may refer to a balsam/mastic tree (2 Sam 5:23-24; the trees in whose rustle David heard God's movement) or be related to bākāh (to weep) — "the valley of weeping/tears." Both readings are defensible. The transformation: they make it a spring — yāshat: to establish. The weeping valley becomes a spring by the pilgrims' passing through. גַּם-בְּרָכוֹת יַעְטֶה מוֹרֶה (gam-bĕrākhôt yaʿṭeh môreh, "the early rain also covers it with pools!") — môreh: the early October-November rain (Joel 2:23).
84.7: יֵלְכוּ מֵחַיִל אֶל-חָיִל (yêlĕkû mêḥayil ʾel-ḥāyil, "they go from strength to strength!") — ḥayil: strength, valor (Ruth 2:1; 3:11: "a woman of valor"). From ḥayil to ḥayil: each stage of the pilgrimage depletes and then replenishes — the pilgrim does not weaken but strengthens with each day toward Zion. The movement is paradoxical: the difficult valley (v6) produces not weakness but deeper resources. יֵרָאֶה אֶל-אֱלֹהִים בְּצִיּוֹן (yērāʾeh ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣiyyôn, "each one appears before God in Zion!") — rāʾāh (niphal: the cultic term for appearing before the divine presence — Exod 34:23: "three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD").
84.8: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת שִׁמְעָה תְפִלָּתִי (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt shimmĕʿāh tĕpillātî, "O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer!") — the fullest possible divine title (YHWH + ʾĕlōhîm + ṣĕbāʾôt — as in Ps 80:19): invoked as the pilgrim makes his mid-journey petition. הַאֲזִינָה אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב סֶלָה (hāʾăzînāh ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb selāh, "give ear, O God of Jacob!") — Jacob who walked the roads from Beersheba to Haran and from Haran to Peniel — the pilgrim-patriarch is the guarantor of God's ear for traveling worshipers. Selāh.
84.9: רְאֵה אֱלֹהִים מָגִנֵּנוּ (rĕʾêh ʾĕlōhîm māginnênû, "Behold our shield, O God!") — māgên: shield (the divine covering — Gen 15:1: "I am your shield"; Ps 3:3; 18:2). וְהַבֵּט פְּנֵי מְשִׁיחֶךָ (wĕhabbêṭ pĕnê mĕshîḥekā, "look on the face of your anointed!") — mĕshîaḥ: anointed one (the Messiah — the anointed king consecrated by oil: 1 Sam 16:13; 2 Sam 22:51; Ps 2:2; 132:10). Since the whole people is making pilgrimage to appear before God (v7), the prayer for the king is the prayer for the nation's representative. The divine looking at the anointed one's face is the counterpart to the divine face shining on Israel (Ps 80:3,7,19).
84.10: כִּי טוֹב-יוֹם בַּחֲצֵרֶיךָ מֵאָלֶף (kî ṭôb-yôm baḥăṣêrêkā mêʾālep, "for a single day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere!") — the comparison is superlative: not "better than a thousand bad days" but "better than a thousand days anywhere else." The arithmetic of divine presence upends all human calculation. בָּחַרְתִּי הִסְתּוֹפֵף בְּבֵית אֱלֹהַי (bāḥartî histôpêp bĕbêt ʾĕlōhay, "I would rather stand at the threshold of the house of my God!") — histôpêp: to stand at the threshold, to be a doorkeeper (from sap: threshold/sill). The lowest status role in the sanctuary — simply standing at the door — preferred to comfort in the wicked's tents. מִדּוּר בְּאָהֳלֵי-רֶשַׁע (middûr bĕʾōhŏlê-reshaʿ, "than dwell comfortably in the tents of the wicked!") — even the permanent residence of the wicked cannot compete with the lowest post at the divine door.
84.11: כִּי שֶׁמֶשׁ וּמָגֵן יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (kî shemesh ûmāgên yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm, "for the LORD God is a sun and shield!") — shemesh (sun) is a rare divine epithet — God is more commonly likened to light, fire, and glory than to the sun itself (the solar disk was a pagan worship object: Ezek 8:16 — men with their backs to the temple worshipping the sun). The epithet is deliberately adopted against this background: the sun-worshippers' object of veneration is, in fact, YHWH's function — warmth, light, orientation, life. The pairing with māgên (shield) covers both positive provision (light/warmth) and protective covering. לֹא יִמְנַע-טוֹב לַהֹלְכִים בְּתָמִים (lōʾ yimnaʿ-ṭôb lahōlĕkhîm bĕtāmîm, "no good thing does he withhold from those who walk with integrity!") — tāmîm: wholeness, blamelessness (from tāmam: to be complete — the walk not split between YHWH and idols).
84.12: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם בֹּטֵחַ בָּךְ (yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾashrê ʾādām bōṭêaḥ bāk, "O LORD of hosts, blessed is the one who trusts in you!") — the third and climactic ʾashrê beatitude of the psalm. The progression: v4 blessed are the dwellers (in the presence); v5 blessed is the pilgrim (on the way); v12 blessed is the truster (anywhere). The third beatitude is the most expansive: any ʾādām (any person) whose posture is trust (bāṭaḥ: to lean upon, to place one's weight on — Ps 22:4-5; Prov 3:5). The psalm that began with extraordinary longing for the place of God's presence ends by locating blessing in the relationship of trust — a move from physical presence to interior posture.

85.1: Psalm 85, a Korah psalm, moves between recollection of past restoration and petition for present renewal. רָצִיתָ יְהוָה אַרְצֶךָ שַׁבְתָּ שְׁבִית יַעֲקֹב (rāṣîtā yĕhwāh ʾarṣekā shab̈tā shĕbît yaʿăqōb, "O LORD, you showed favor to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob!") — rāṣāh: to be pleased with, to accept favorably (Day of Atonement language — Lev 1:4; Isa 40:2: "her iniquity is pardoned"). Shab̈tā shĕbît: the cognate accusative — "you restored the restoration of Jacob" (idiom for returning from captivity — Deut 30:3; Jer 30:3; Job 42:10). The psalm's first three verses all use completed-action perfects — describing divine acts as done, laying them as the foundation for petition.
85.2: נָשָׂאתָ עֲוֹן עַמֶּךָ כִּסִּיתָ כָל-חַטָּאתָם סֶלָה (nāśāʾtā ʿăwōn ʿammekā kissîtā kol-ḥaṭṭāʾtām selāh, "you forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered over all their sin! Selah.") — two paired verbs: nāśāʾ ʿāwōn: "to lift/bear iniquity" (as the scapegoat bears Israel's sins — Lev 16:22; as the Servant of Isa 53:12 "bore the sin of many"). Kissāh ḥaṭṭāʾāh: "to cover sin" (Ps 32:1: "blessed is the one whose sin is covered!" — Rom 4:7 cites Ps 32:1 directly). The two great acts of atonement: lifting and covering — both perfect-tense completed divine acts. Selāh.
85.3: אָסַפְתָּ כָל-עֶבְרָתֶךָ הֱשִׁיבוֹתָ מֵחֲרוֹן אַפֶּךָ (ʾāsaptā kol-ʿebrātekā hĕshîbôtā mêḥărôn ʾappekā, "you withdrew all your wrath; you turned away from your fierce anger!") — ʿebrāh: overflow of wrath (the noun from ʿābar: to overflow); ḥărôn ʾap: the burning of the nostrils (Exod 32:12). Both were gathered in (ʾāsap: to collect, withdraw) and turned back. This third completed-action verse is the memory that makes petition in vv4-7 possible: God who has done all this can do it again.
85.4: שׁוּבֵנוּ אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׁעֵנוּ (shûbênû ʾĕlōhê yishʿênû, "restore us again, O God of our salvation!") — the first petition: qal imperative of shûb: "cause us to return/restore us." The same verb that described Israel's physical return (shab̈tā shĕbît — v1) now turned back to God: cause the inner and national restoration that the physical return did not fully achieve. ʾĔlōhê yishʿênû: "God of our salvation" — the divine title that stakes the petition on God's own character as savior. וְהָפֵר כַּעַסְךָ עִמָּנוּ (wĕhāpêr kaʿăśekā ʿimmānû, "and set aside your displeasure against us!") — pārar (hiphil: to break, to annul, to cause to cease — used of breaking a covenant or decree).
85.5: הַלְעוֹלָם תֶּאֱנַף-בָּנוּ (halĕʿôlām teʾĕnap-bānû, "will you be angry with us forever?") — ʾānap: to be angry (Deut 1:37; 4:21; 9:20). The interrogative hal (surely not?) expects a negative answer. תִּמְשֹׁךְ אַפְּךָ לְדֹר וָדֹר (timmĕshōk ʾappĕkā lĕdōr wādōr, "will you extend your anger through every generation?") — māshak: to draw out, to prolong (to pull something along — as water drawn from a well). Anger drawn through generations contrasts with Exod 34:6 (the divine character: "slow to anger" — ʾerek ʾappayim, literally "long-nostriled"). The petition presses the divine character against the present divine anger.
85.6: הֲלֹא-אַתָּה תָּשׁוּב תְּחַיֵּנוּ (hălōʾ-ʾattāh tāshûb tĕḥayyênû, "will you not revive us again, so that your people may rejoice in you?") — the interrogative-expectant: hălōʾ ʾattāh presses the divine identity as the one who revives. Ḥāyāh (piel: to give life, to revive — 2 Kgs 13:21: the dead man touching Elisha's bones "revived and stood"; Hos 6:2: "on the third day he will raise us up"). Tĕḥayyênû: "revive us" — the resurrection language used for national renewal and, in the prophets, for eschatological resurrection. וְעַמְּךָ יִשְׂמְחוּ-בָךְ (wĕʿammĕkā yiśmĕḥû-bāk, "that your people may rejoice in you!") — joy in God as the fruit of revival: the returning life expresses itself in rejoicing in the divine person, not merely in changed circumstances.
85.7: הַרְאֵנוּ יְהוָה חַסְדֶּךָ וְיֶשְׁעֲךָ תִּתֶּן-לָנוּ (harʾênû yĕhwāh ḥasdekā wĕyeshʿăkā titten-lānû, "Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation!") — rāʾāh (hiphil: to show, cause to see — to make the divine ḥesed visible and demonstrable). Ḥasdekā: your steadfast love (Lam 3:22). Yeshʿăkā: your salvation (the noun from yāshaʿ: to save — as in the name Jesus/Yeshua). The paired petition: make the love visible + give the salvation. The two are inseparable: showing the ḥesed and giving salvation are the same divine action.
85.8: אֶשְׁמְעָה מַּה-יְדַבֵּר הָאֵל יְהוָה (ʾeshĕmĕʿāh mah-yĕdabbêr hāʾêl yĕhwāh, "I will listen to what God the LORD says!") — a dramatic turn: the Psalmist falls silent from his petitions and adopts the prophetic listening posture. ʾEshĕmĕʿāh (cohortative: I will listen — the deliberate act of oriented attention): as Samuel (1 Sam 3:9-10: "speak, for your servant is listening"), as Habakkuk (Hab 2:1: "I will stand at my watchpost and listen"). כִּי-יְדַבֵּר שָׁלוֹם אֶל-עַמּוֹ וְאֶל-חֲסִידָיו (kî-yĕdabbêr shālôm ʾel-ʿammô wĕʾel-ḥăsîdāyw, "for he will speak peace to his people and to his faithful ones!") — shālôm: comprehensive wholeness, covenant-restoration (Isa 52:7: "how beautiful... 'Your God reigns'"). וְאַל-יָשׁוּבוּ לְכִסְלָה (wĕʾal-yāshûbû lĕkhislāh, "only let them not return to folly!") — kĕsîlāh: folly (from kĕsîl: the fool who refuses wisdom — Prov 1:32; 3:35). The peace is conditional: do not return to the foolishness of idolatry and covenant-breaking.
85.9: אַךְ-קָרוֹב לִירֵאָיו יִשְׁעוֹ (ʾak-qārôb lîrêʾāyw yishʿô, "surely his salvation is near to those who fear him!") — qārôb: near, close (the divine salvation described as spatially imminent — Ps 34:7). לִשְׁכֹּן כָּבוֹד בְּאַרְצֵנוּ (lishkōn kābôd bĕʾarṣênû, "so that glory may dwell in our land!") — kābôd: the divine glory (the kābôd YHWH that filled the tabernacle — Exod 40:34-35 — and the temple — 1 Kgs 8:11 — and departed in the crisis of Ezek 10-11). The petition: may the glory that departed return and dwell (shākan). Ezek 43:1-5 promises its return. John 1:14 declares fulfillment: "the Word... tabernacled (ἐσκήνωσεν) among us, and we saw his glory (δόξαν)."
85.10: חֶסֶד-וֶאֱמֶת נִפְגָּשׁוּ צֶדֶק וְשָׁלוֹם נָשָׁקוּ (ḥesed-wĕʾĕmet nipgāshû ṣedeq wĕshālôm nāshāqû, "steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss!") — the most celebrated verse of Psalm 85, one of the great theological lines of the Psalter. Four divine covenant attributes personified as figures who meet and embrace. Nifgash (niphal of pāgash: to meet, to encounter — as in Exod 4:24 where YHWH "met" Moses): ḥesed (covenant love) and ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth — the two paired throughout: Ps 89:14; Gen 24:27) meeting in greeting. Nāshaq: to kiss (Gen 29:11; 45:15): ṣedeq (righteousness — the divine right-making, the justification) and shālôm (peace — the wholeness of restored relationship) kissing. Luther saw this as a prophecy of the incarnation: "Where could these four attributes meet except at the cross, where love and faithfulness met in the sacrifice of the Son, and righteousness and peace kissed in the reconciliation of God and humanity?" The verse is not allegory — it is the covenant vision of reality restored.
85.11: אֱמֶת מֵאֶרֶץ תִּצְמָח (ʾĕmet mêʾereṣ tiṣmaḥ, "faithfulness springs up from the earth!") — ṣāmaḥ: to sprout, to spring up (the agricultural verb for vegetation breaking through — Isa 45:8: "let righteousness spring up from the earth"). ʾĔmet: faithfulness/truth rising from the ground — the covenant faithfulness of the human community responding to the divine character. וְצֶדֶק מִשָּׁמַיִם נִשְׁקָף (wĕṣedeq mishshāmayim nishqāp, "and righteousness looks down from heaven!") — nishqāp (niphal of shāqap: to look down from a height — Num 21:20; Gen 26:8). Heaven and earth in exchange: faithfulness rising from below; righteousness gazing down from above — the mutual meeting of human and divine in covenant fulfillment.
85.12: גַּם-יְהוָה יִתֵּן הַטּוֹב (gam-yĕhwāh yittên haṭṭôb, "yes, the LORD will give what is good!") — haṭṭôb: the good (with the definite article: the specific covenantal good that belongs to restoration — Ps 84:11: "no good thing does he withhold"). וְאַרְצֵנוּ תִּתֵּן יְבוּלָהּ (wĕʾarṣênû tittên yĕbûlāh, "and our land will yield its harvest!") — yĕbûl: the produce, the yield (Lev 26:4,20; Deut 11:17 — harvest as the sign of covenant blessing or its absence). The restoration of v1 (God showed favor to the land) now extends to productive abundance: the land gives back what the curse had withheld.
85.13: צֶדֶק לְפָנָיו יְהַלֵּךְ (ṣedeq lĕpānāyw yĕhallêk, "righteousness will march before him!") — ṣedeq personified as the divine advance-guard: the one who goes before the divine warrior to prepare the way (as the ark went before Israel — Num 10:33; Josh 3:6). The image of divine righteousness as forerunner/herald: Isa 58:8 ("your righteousness will go before you"); Isa 45:8 ("let righteousness spring up from the earth"). Paul's declaration in Rom 1:17 that the righteousness of God is being revealed (ἀποκαλύπτεται δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ) in the gospel echoes this precise imagery — the advance of divine ṣedeq into human history. וְיָשֵׂם לְדֶרֶךְ פְּעָמָיו (wĕyāśêm lĕderek pĕʿāmāyw, "and prepare a path for his steps!") — pĕʿāmîm: footsteps, the rhythmic advance of God's march into his restored world. Righteousness clears the road and lays the path for the divine arrival — the psalm that began by remembering a past restoration ends looking forward to the final arrival of God himself, preceded by his righteousness as herald.

48.1: shîr mizmôr libnê-qōraḥ — a Song, a Psalm of the sons of Korah. The double title (shîr: song for singing + mizmôr: psalm for instrumental accompaniment) is unusual — both vocal and instrumental performance indicated. gādôl YHWH ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd — great is the LORD and greatly to be praised. Gādôl (great) + mĕhullāl (passive participle of hālal: the one who is greatly praised). The incomparability formula applied to Zion-worship. bĕʿîr ʾĕlōhênû har qodshô — in the city of our God, on his holy mountain. The praise is not abstract — it is geographically located: in the city, on the mountain.
48.2: yĕphēh nôph śĕmōaḥ kol-hāʾāreṣ — Mount Zion, beautiful in its height, the joy of all the earth. Nôph (elevation, height — the aesthetic quality of the mount's height, not merely its altitude). Śĕmōaḥ kol-hāʾāreṣ (joy of all the earth — the source of joy radiating to the whole world). har-ṣiyyôn yarkĕtê ṣāphôn — Mount Zion, on the heights of the north. Yarkĕtê ṣāphôn (far recesses/heights of the north) is the most theologically loaded phrase: ṣāphôn is also the name of Mount Zaphon (Jebel el-Aqra, north of Ugarit) — the mountain where Baal held his court in Canaanite mythology. The psalm maps YHWH's Zion onto the cosmic mountain of the gods, displacing Baal: what was true of the pagan divine mountain is now true, and more truly true, of Zion. qiryat melek rāb — the city of the great King. Melek rāb (great King — the title for the supreme sovereign over other kings; cf. Ps 47:2; Matt 5:35).
48.3: ʾĕlōhîm bĕʾarmmĕnôteyhā nôdāʿ lĕmiśgāb — God has shown himself to be a fortress within her citadels. ʾArmmĕnôt (citadels, palace-towers — the most heavily fortified buildings of an ancient city). Nôdāʿ lĕmiśgāb (niphal: he has made himself known as a fortress) — the divine self-disclosure as protective strength occurring within the city's own strongholds. The defensive architecture of Zion testifies to the divine presence that makes it impregnable.
48.4: kî-hinnēh hammĕlākhîm nôʿădû — for look — the kings assembled. Nôʿad (niphal of yāʿad: to meet by appointment, to assemble — a deliberate, planned coalition). The kings who appear to be Zion's existential threat. ʿābĕrû yaḥdāw — they came against her together. The united advance — maximum military threat. The next verse (v5) reveals that their united power dissolves on sight of Zion.
48.5: hēmmāh rāʾû kēn tāmāhû — they saw it and were bewildered. Tāmah (to be astonished, confounded — the stunned reaction when one sees something that defeats all expectation). nibhălû nibbĕhālû — they were struck with terror and fled. The intensified niphal (nibhălû) emphasizes the sudden panic. The alliteration in the Hebrew (nibhălû nibbĕhālû: they were terrified / they fled in terror) mimics the frantic flight. The military coalition collapses at the sight of the city — without the need for any described battle.
48.6: rĕʿādāh ʾăḥāzātam shām — trembling took hold of them there. Rĕʿādāh (trembling, shaking — the involuntary physical response to terror). Shām (there — in that very place where they came to conquer). ḥîl kĕyôledāh — pain like that of a woman giving birth. Ḥîl (writhing, labor-pain — the intense, overwhelming physical pain of childbirth; a standard OT image for the paralysis that overcomes the apparently powerful in the moment of divine judgment, cf. Isa 13:8; Jer 4:31; Mic 4:10). The warriors' trembling reduces them to the helplessness of a woman in labor — the height of military irony.
48.7: bĕrûaḥ qādîm tĕshabbēr ʾoniyyôt tarshîsh — with the east wind you shatter the ships of Tarshish. Rûaḥ qādîm (the east wind — the dry, powerful sirocco that blows in from the desert; used of the divine weapon in Exod 14:21, Ps 78:26, Ezek 27:26). Tarshîsh (Tarshish — possibly Tartessus in Spain or another distant trading port; the ships of Tarshish are the grandest ocean-going vessels of antiquity, symbol of commercial wealth and power). YHWH's east wind shatters even the greatest human vessels — the same power that routes armies (vv4-6) also commands the sea.
48.8: kaʾăsher shāmaʿnû kēn rāʾînû — as we have heard, so we have seen. The conjunction of heard tradition and personal experience — the community's historical memory (shāmaʿnû: we have heard, the passed-down testimony) confirmed in their own sight (rāʾînû: we have seen). bĕʿîr-YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt bĕʿîr ʾĕlōhênû — in the city of the LORD of hosts, in the city of our God. The double identification of the city (Lord of hosts, Our God) emphasizes its divine ownership. ʾĕlōhîm yĕkhônĕnehā ʿad-ʿôlām selāh — God establishes it forever. Kôn (piel: to establish firmly, make permanent) + ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) — the permanence of Zion as the divine guarantee against all the kings who assembled.
48.9: dimmînû ḥasdekā ʾĕlōhîm bĕqereb hêkhālekhā — we have meditated on your steadfast love, O God, within your temple. Dimmāh (piel: to picture, meditate on, liken — the root is related to dāmāh: likeness, image). In the temple the community meditates — pictures, ponders, contemplates — the divine ḥesed. The temple as the space for theological reflection on God's covenant love, not merely for ritual performance.
48.10: kĕshimĕkhā ʾĕlōhîm kēn tĕhillātekhā — as your name reaches, O God, so does your praise. The proportionality of name and praise: wherever the divine name reaches, there praise follows — the geographical spread of YHWH's reputation is matched by the geographical spread of worship. ʿal-qaṣwê-ʾāreṣ — to the ends of the earth. The universal scope: from Zion (v1) to the ends of the earth (v10) — the psalm moves outward. ṣedeq māleʾāh yĕmînekhā — your right hand is full of righteousness. The divine right hand (yāmîn) — the hand of action and power — full of ṣedeq (righteousness, justice). What the hand is full of is what it distributes.
48.11: yiśmaḥ har-ṣiyyôn tāgēlanāh bĕnôt yĕhûdāh — let Mount Zion rejoice; let the towns of Judah be glad. The call to rejoicing: the mountain itself (har-ṣiyyôn) and the daughter-villages of Judah (bĕnôt yĕhûdāh: daughters of Judah = the surrounding towns). lĕmaʿan mishpāṭêkhā — because of your righteous judgments. Mishpāṭ (judgment/decision — the righteous verdicts) as the reason for joy: the divine justice displayed in the defeat of the kings is cause for communal celebration.
48.12: sōbbû ṣiyyôn wĕhaqîphûhā — walk all around Zion, circle her. The liturgical procession around the city — a pilgrimage act of inspection and praise. siprû migdālêyhā — count her towers. Māgdāl (tower — the watchtowers and defensive towers of the city wall). The counting is not military inventory but liturgical attention — seeing the city's defenses as testimony to divine protection.
48.13: shîtû libbĕkhem lĕḥêlāh — mark her walls carefully. Shît lēb (to set the heart toward — to give careful attention to; the idiom for deliberate, attentive regard). Ḥêl (rampart, fortification wall — the outer defensive earthwork). passĕgû ʾarmmĕnôteyhā — tour her citadels. Pāsag (to survey, look over carefully — a rare verb; to pass between and inspect). lĕmaʿan tĕsappĕrû lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn — that you may tell the next generation. The purpose of the inspection: to remember what you have seen and transmit it to the next generation. The tour of Zion's defenses becomes a pedagogical act — what you mark and survey today you can narrate to your children.
48.14: kî zeh ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhênû ʿôlām wāʿed — for this God is our God forever and ever. The climactic confession — zeh (this one — demonstrative, pointing to the God whose power has just been celebrated) is ʾĕlōhênû (our God). The Zion-theology grounded in covenant identity. hûʾ yĕnahăgênû ʿalmût — he will guide us until the very end. Nāhag (to guide, lead — the shepherd's or caravan-guide's leading). ʿAlmût (the word is ambiguous: it can mean ʿad-mût = until death = forever; or ʿal-mût = over/beyond death = past the death-threshold). Most read it as 'forever' (unto death = all one's life), but the consonantal text admits a reading of 'beyond death' — a faint ray of post-mortem hope within the Zion psalm.

49.1: shimĕʿû-zōʾt kol-hāʿammîm — hear this, all peoples! The universal address of a wisdom psalm — not just Israel but kol-hāʿammîm (all the peoples). haʾăzînû kol-yōshĕbê ḥāled — listen, all who live in the world. Ḥāled (the world, the temporal world — from a root meaning duration; the world as the sphere of transient time; cf. Ps 17:14: mēḥeled ḥelqām, their portion in this life). The audience is as wide as humanity itself, because the psalm's subject (mortality and wealth's impotence before death) is universal.
49.2: gam-bĕnê ʾādām gam-bĕnê-ʾîsh — the humble and the great alike. Bĕnê ʾādām (sons of Adam — the common person, humanity in its generic form) + bĕnê ʾîsh (sons of a man of rank — the distinguished, the notable). The Hebrew idiom distinguishes commoner from aristocrat — both are addressed. yaḥad ʿāshîr wĕʾebyôn — the rich and the poor together. Yaḥad (together — they are unified in the audience, as they will be unified in death, v12). The wisdom that follows applies to all without distinction.
49.3: pî yĕdabbēr ḥokhĕmôt — my mouth will speak wisdom. The wisdom-genre self-identification: the speaker announces he will offer ḥokhĕmôt (wisdom — plural of ḥokhĕmāh: wisdoms, wisdom-speech in all its forms). wĕhagût libbî tĕbûnôt — and the reflection of my heart will be understanding. Hagût (meditation, reflection — from hāgāh: to murmur, to meditate) of the lēb (heart = the center of thought and understanding). Tĕbûnôt (understandings — plural: multiple facets of understanding). The heart's meditation as the source of what the mouth will speak — wisdom is not imported but grown internally.
49.4: ʾaṭṭeh lĕmāshāl ʾoznî — I bend my ear to a proverb. Māshāl (proverb, wisdom-saying — the generic term for wisdom-literature; from māshal: to rule, to be like). The act of bending the ear (nāṭāh ʾōzen) to a proverb — listening to receive wisdom. ʾeptaḥ bĕkhinnôr ḥîdātî — I will open my riddle with the harp. Ḥîdāh (riddle, dark saying, enigmatic wisdom — the indirect wisdom that requires insight to unpack; from ḥûd: to propose a riddle, cf. Samson's riddle, Judg 14:12-19). The pairing of māshāl and ḥîdāh signals the highest wisdom-register (cf. Ps 78:2). The harp (kinnôr) as the instrument of prophetic inspiration (1 Sam 10:5; 2 Kgs 3:15).
49.5: lāmmāh ʾîrāʾ bîmê rāʿ — why should I be afraid in evil days? The existential question the psalm addresses: what is the ground of fearlessness when evil days come? ʿăwōn ʿăqēbay yĕsûbbênî — when the guilt of my accusers closes in around me. ʿĂqēb (heel — those who trip at the heel, who pursue closely from behind; from ʿāqab: to supplant, to overtake from behind). The accusers (or the treacherous) surrounding the Psalmist. The answer to why not to fear occupies the rest of the psalm: because wealth cannot ransom, and death makes all equal, trust in God transcends the threat.
49.6: haboṭĕḥîm ʿal-ḥêlām — those who trust in their wealth. Bāṭaḥ ʿal (to trust in, rely upon — placing one's weight on). Ḥayil (wealth, strength, resources — the accumulated capital in all its forms). ûbĕrōb ʿoshĕrām yithallālû — and boast of their great riches. Hālal (hitpael: to boast, to praise oneself — the same root as hallelujah inverted: self-praise instead of God-praise). The object of the self-boasting is ʿōsher (riches, wealth). The irony: those who will not escape death (vv7-12) are those who trust in wealth and boast of riches.
49.7: ʾāḥ lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh ʾîsh — truly no one can by any means ransom another person. The emphatic negation: ʾāḥ lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh (surely no/by no means shall one ransom) — the infinitive absolute (phādōh) + imperfect (yiphĕdeh) expresses the categorical impossibility. Phādāh (to ransom, to buy back from danger/obligation/death — the redemption verb of the Exodus, Ps 130:8). The ransom that no human can pay for another. lōʾ-yittēn lĕʾĕlōhîm kophĕrô — or give God a payment for their life. Kōpher (the ransom price — the substitute payment that averts death, as in Exod 30:12). No ransom sufficient to God can be offered by a human.
49.8: wĕyāqar pidyon nafshām — for the price of their life is too costly. Pidyon (ransom — from phādāh) + yāqār (too costly, too high — beyond affordability). wĕḥādal lĕʿôlām — and no payment will ever be enough. Ḥādal (to cease, to give up — the payment must be abandoned as an impossible task). The economic metaphor: the ransom price exceeds all possible human resources; it must be given up as unachievable forever.
49.9: wîḥî-ʿôd lāneṣaḥ — that one could live on forever. The goal that the ransom would achieve — continued life — is declared impossible. lōʾ yirʾeh hashshāḥat — and not see the pit. Shāḥat (the pit — the grave, Sheol as a pit; the destination of every unransomed life). No humanly-funded ransom can prevent the grave.
49.10: kî yirʾeh ḥăkhāmîm yāmûtû — he sees that even the wise die. The empirical observation: the wise (ḥăkhāmîm) die like everyone else — wisdom does not provide exemption from death. yaḥad kĕsîl wābāʿar yōbēdû — the fool and the senseless alike perish. Kĕsîl (fool — the morally obtuse) + bāʿar (brute, the senseless person of animal-like behavior, cf. Ps 92:6). All three — wise, fool, brute — perish together. wĕʿāzĕbû lāʾăḥērîm ḥêlām — and leave their wealth behind for others. The wealth (ḥayil) that was trusted (v6) and boasted of cannot be carried into death — it passes to others (ʾăḥērîm: strangers).
49.11: qirbām bāttêmô lĕʿôlām — in their hearts they imagined their homes would last forever. Qereb (inner parts, heart — what they thought inside). Their homes (bāttêmô: their houses) imagined as permanent. mishkĕnōtām lĕdōr wādōr — their dwelling places to all generations. The fantasy of dynastic permanence — houses and estates passing through all generations. qārĕʾû bishĕmôtām ʿălê ʾădāmôt — they called their lands after their own names. The act of naming the land after oneself — the ancient equivalent of leaving one's name on a building or institution — as a gesture of claiming permanence that death will defeat.
49.12: wĕʾādām bîqār bĕlî-yālîn — man in all his honor will not last overnight. Bîqār (in honor, in dignity — the splendor of wealth and position). Lōʾ yālîn (will not spend the night — will not remain through even a single night of time; the brevity expressed by a single night's lodging). nidmeh kabbe-hēmôt nidĕmû — he is like the animals that perish. Dāmāh (to be like, to resemble) + bĕhēmôt (animals, cattle) — the human without divine connection is likened to beasts that simply die and are gone. This refrain appears again at v20, with one significant variation.
49.13: zeh darkām kĕsel lāmô — this is the fate of those who are confident in themselves. Kesel (foolish confidence, self-reliance — from the root of kĕsîl: the fool who trusts in the wrong thing). wĕʾaḥărêhem bĕphêhem yirṣû selāh — and after them people applaud their talk. Those who come after them (ʾaḥărêhem) approve of their path (bĕphêhem yirṣû: they find pleasure in their words). The fool's path gets inherited and continued by others who find it appealing. The selah marks a moment for the listener to absorb this cycle of folly.
49.14: kaṣṣōʾn lashshĕʾôl shattû — like sheep they are headed for Sheol. The driving of sheep: shātat (to drive, appoint) — they are herded toward Sheol as sheep are driven to their destination. māwet yirʿēm — death will be their shepherd. Rāʿāh (to shepherd, to pasture) — Death as shepherd is the psalm's darkest inversion: the God who is shepherd (Ps 23:1; 80:1) is here replaced by Death as the shepherd of those who trust in wealth. wîrdû-bām yeshārîm laboqer — and the upright will have dominion over them in the morning. The eschatological reversal at the morning: the yeshārîm (the upright ones) rule over the formerly proud rich. wĕṣûrām lĕbattôt sheʾôl — their bodies will decay in Sheol, with no home left for them. The end of the household fantasy of v11: no home (zĕbul: dwelling, lordly residence) remains.
49.15: ʾakh-ʾĕlōhîm yiphĕdeh nafshî miyyad-shĕʾôl — but God will ransom my soul from the grip of Sheol. The pivot verse — ʾakh (but, surely — the contrast particle). Everything vv7-9 said was impossible (lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh: none can ransom another), God will do: yiphĕdeh (he will ransom). The vocabulary is exact: the impossible human ransom becomes the possible divine ransom. kî yiqqāḥēnî selāh — for he will take me to himself. Lāqaḥ (to take) — the word used of Enoch's divine taking (wayyiqqah ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm, Gen 5:24) and of Elijah's translation (2 Kgs 2:1-11). The verb carries the weight of those two precedents: being taken by God is the vocabulary of post-mortem rescue. The selah marks this as the most weighty statement in the psalm.
49.16: ʾal-tîrāʾ kî-yaʿăshîr ʾîsh — do not be frightened when someone becomes rich. The practical application of vv7-15: the command not to fear (yārēʾ) the enrichment of another. kî-yirbbeh kĕbôd bêtô — when the splendor of their household grows. Kābôd (glory, splendor — here applied to a household's impressive accumulation). The apparently threatening success of the wealthy person is the context of fear — the psalm counsels the right response.
49.17: kî lōʾ bĕmôtô yiqqaḥ hakkōl — for when they die they take nothing with them. The reason not to fear: at death, all the accumulated wealth (hakkōl: everything) is left behind. lōʾ-yērēd ʾaḥărāyw kĕbôdô — their glory will not follow them down. The reversal: the glory (kābôd) that was growing (v16) will not descend with the person into Sheol — it stays in the world above and passes to others (v10: lāʾăḥērîm).
49.18: kî-nafshô bĕḥayyāyw yĕbārek — though in their lifetime they counted themselves fortunate. Bārak nafshô (to bless oneself — to congratulate oneself, to pronounce oneself well-off; the self-congratulation of the prosperous). wĕyôdûkhā kî-têṭîb lākh — and people praised them for prospering. Yādāh (hiph: to praise) when things go well (kî-têṭîb lākh: because it is good for you). The social praise of prosperity: people praise the successful as long as success lasts.
49.19: tābôʾ ʿad-dôr ʾăbôtāyw — they will go to join their ancestors. The inevitable reunion with the dead — the same Sheol that received their forefathers receives them. ʿad-neṣaḥ lōʾ yirʾû-ʾôr — and never again see the light. ʾôr (light — the light of the living world, of divine favor; cf. Ps 36:9: bĕʾôrĕkhā nirʾeh-ʾôr). The loss of light is the definitive characteristic of Sheol (cf. Ps 88:12; Job 10:21-22). The self-praising, wealth-trusting person goes into permanent darkness.
49.20: wĕʾādām bîqār wĕlōʾ yābîn nidmeh kabbe-hēmôt nidĕmû — man in all his honor, but without understanding, is like the animals that perish. The refrain returns from v12 with one variation: v12 has bĕlî-yālîn (will not remain/lodge) but v20 has wĕlōʾ yābîn (without understanding). The final verse specifies what makes human honor equivalent to animal death: the absence of bîn (understanding/discernment — the wisdom-faculty). Honor without understanding ends in animal-death. V15's divine ransom is the alternative for those who do understand.

50.1: ʾēl ʾĕlōhîm YHWH dibbēr — the Mighty One, God, the LORD, has spoken. The solemn triple invocation of the divine name — ʾēl (the generic name for deity, the powerful one) + ʾĕlōhîm (the generic plural divine name) + YHWH (the covenant personal name). This judicial formula (cf. Josh 22:22: ʾēl ʾĕlōhîm YHWH) convenes the divine court. Psalm 50 is the first of the Asaphite psalms (50, 73-83). wayyiqrāʾ ʾāreṣ mimmizerāḥ-shemesh ʿad-mĕbōʾô — and summons the earth from east to west. The divine summons of the entire earth as witness to the covenant lawsuit (rîb) — from sunrise to sunset, the universal scope of the judicial proceeding.
50.2: mîṣiyyôn miklal-yōphî ʾĕlōhîm hôphîaʿ — out of Zion, perfect in beauty, God has shone forth. Miklal-yōphî (perfection of beauty — the superlative of Zion's beauty; cf. Lam 2:15: ʾăsher ʾāmĕrû kĕlîlat yōphî of fallen Jerusalem). Hôphîaʿ (hiphil of yāphaʿ: to shine forth — the blazing appearance of the divine judge from Zion; the same verb as Ps 94:1 where YHWH is asked to shine forth in judgment).
50.3: yābôʾ ʾĕlōhênû wĕʾal-yeḥĕrash — our God comes and will not stay silent. Ḥāresh (to keep silent, to hold one's peace — the accused calling on the judge to be silent; here YHWH will not remain silent). ʾēsh lĕphānāyw tōʾkhēl — fire devours before him. Fire as the divine judicial presence (cf. Exod 3:2; 19:18; Deut 4:24; Ps 97:3). ûsĕbîbāyw niśʿărāh mĕʾōd — and a fierce storm rages around him. Niśʿar (niphal: to be violently stormy — the storm-theophany surrounding the divine judge). The God who comes is neither neutral nor passive — fire and storm attend his arrival.
50.4: yiqrāʾ ʾel-hashshāmayim mēʿāl wĕʾel-hāʾāreṣ — he calls out to the heavens above and to the earth. The witnesses summoned: heaven and earth — the two parties in the cosmic covenant (cf. Deut 30:19: hāʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm ʾet-hashshāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ: I call heaven and earth to witness against you). lādîn ʿammô — to judge his people. Dîn (to judge — to enter into judicial proceedings against). The covenant lawsuit: YHWH is prosecuting Israel, not an external enemy.
50.5: ʾispû-lî ḥăsîday — bring together my faithful people. Ḥāsîd (the faithful, covenant-loyal ones — those who embody ḥesed). kōrĕtê bĕrîtî ʿălê-zābaḥ — those who sealed covenant with me by sacrifice. Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant — the standard covenant formula; covenants were ratified by cutting animals in two, as in Gen 15:10-18; Jer 34:18-20). The faithful are defined by their covenantal relationship — they sealed the covenant with sacrifice. Now the sacrifices are the subject of the lawsuit.
50.6: wayyaggîdû shāmayim ṣidqô — the heavens declare his righteousness. The heavens as witnesses now become witnesses to the divine righteousness — the cosmic court validates YHWH's justice. kî-ʾĕlōhîm shōphēṭ hûʾ selāh — for God himself is the judge. The selah after this declaration — God is the judge, both plaintiff and judge in the covenant lawsuit. The pause lets the weight of that identity settle before the divine speech begins.
50.7: shimĕʿāh ʿammî waʾădabbērāh — listen, my people, and I will speak. The divine speech begins — YHWH addressing ʿammî (my people: the covenant relationship intact even in the lawsuit). yisrāʾēl wĕʾāʿîdāh bākh — I will testify against you, O Israel. ʿûd (hiphil: to testify solemnly, to warn — the judicial testimony function). ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhêkhā ʾānōkhî — I am God, your God. The self-identification formula — exactly the form of the Ten Commandments' preface (ʾānōkhî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkhā, Exod 20:2). The covenant relationship is the basis for the lawsuit, not its repudiation.
50.8: lōʾ ʿal-zĕbāḥêkhā ʾôkhîḥekā — my rebuke is not over your sacrifices. The lawsuit is not a critique of offering sacrifices per se — they have been brought (wĕʿôlōtekhā lĕnegdî tāmîd: your burnt offerings are before me continually). The complaint is not the absence of sacrifice but the misunderstanding of its purpose.
50.9: lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ mibbêtekhā phār — I will not take from your household a bull. The divine refusal of literal animal sacrifice as if God needed the animals — phār (bull — the most valuable sacrificial animal). mimdibĕrōtekhā ʿattûdîm — or goats from your pens. Dibrāh (fold, flock-enclosure) + ʿattûd (male goat — the leader of the flock). Both the largest and the most valuable animals are declared unnecessary as gifts to God.
50.10: kî-lî khol-ḥaytô-yāʿar — for every animal of the forest belongs to me. The ownership argument: YHWH already owns all the animals (as creator and sovereign), so the gift of an animal adds nothing to his holdings. behēmôt bĕʾalpê hārîm — the cattle on a thousand hills. The totality of domesticated and wild animals — all belong to YHWH. The sacrificial system cannot transfer to God what he already possesses.
50.11: yādaʿtî kol-ʿôph hārîm — I know every bird in the hills. Yādaʿ (to know — comprehensive divine knowledge of every creature). wĕzîz śādeh ʿimmādî — everything that moves in the open fields is mine. Zîz (moving thing, wild creature — possibly insects or small animals). All of creation's mobile life is with YHWH (ʿimmādî: with me). Nothing can be given to God as a gift since nothing is not-God's to begin with.
50.12: ʾim-ʾerʿab lōʾ-ʾōmar lākh — if I were hungry, I would not tell you. The absurd hypothetical pressed to make the point: if hunger were even possible for YHWH, he would not need to ask the worshiper — he could supply himself. kî-lî tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh — for the world and all it contains is mine. Tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh (the inhabited world and its fullness — the creation-claim formula of Ps 24:1). What the worshiper offers comes from what already belongs to YHWH — the gift returns to its source.
50.13: hăʾōkhal bĕśar ʾabbîrîm — do I eat bull meat? The anthropomorphic assumption corrected: God does not eat the flesh of the sacrifices. wĕdam ʿattûdîm ʾeshteh — do I drink the blood of goats? The blood, which was specifically prohibited for Israelite consumption (Lev 17:10-14), is also not consumed by YHWH. The physical substance of animal sacrifice accomplishes nothing by transfer of matter to the divine.
50.14: zebah lĕʾĕlōhîm tôdāh — bring God a thank-offering as your sacrifice. The positive instruction — the alternative to misunderstood animal sacrifice. Tôdāh (thanksgiving, the thank-offering — from yādāh: to acknowledge/praise; the specific sacrificial genre in which the worshiper gives thanks for a completed divine rescue). wĕshallēm lĕʿelyôn nĕdārêkhā — and keep your vows to the Most High. Shālem nĕdārim (to fulfill/complete vows) — the ethical dimension: the sacrifice YHWH wants is keeping promises. ʿElyôn (the Most High) in the place of ʾĕlōhîm — the divine sovereign whose vows cannot be ignored.
50.15: ûqrāʾēnî bĕyôm ṣārāh — call on me when trouble comes. The covenant prayer-promise: the sacrifice God accepts is the prayer of the distressed person. ʾăḥalleṣkā wattĕkhabbĕdēnî — I will rescue you, and you will honor me. The reciprocal: divine rescue produces human honor of God. The true sacrificial cycle: prayer-rescue-praise, not animal-offering-divine satisfaction.
50.16: wĕlārāshāʿ ʾāmar ʾĕlōhîm — but to the wicked God says. The second part of the lawsuit turns from the misunderstanding of sacrifice (vv8-15) to the misuse of covenant language by those whose lives contradict it. māh-lĕkhā lĕsappēr ḥuqqāy — what right do you have to quote my laws? Sāphar ḥuqqay (to recount/recite my statutes) — those who recite the covenant law but do not keep it. wattisśāʾ bĕrîtî ʿălê-phîkhā — or to take my covenant on your lips? Nāśāʾ ʿălê-pheh (to take on the lips — to verbalize covenant language without moral correspondence).
50.17: wĕʾattāh śānēʾtā mûsār — you despise correction. Mûsār (discipline, instruction, correction — the educative action of God that the wicked reject). wattashlikhēhû dĕbāray ʾaḥărêkhā — and flung my words aside. Shalakh ʾaḥărāyw (to throw behind oneself — to dismiss contemptuously). The covenant words of God treated as unwanted refuse thrown over the shoulder.
50.18: ʾim-rāʾîtā gannāb wattirṣ ʿimmô — when you see a thief you go along with him. Rāṣāh ʿimmô (to be pleased with him, to approve — active complicity with theft). wĕʿim-mĕnāʾăphîm ḥelqĕkhā — and you make yourself at home with adulterers. The explicit listing of covenant-violations: complicity in theft and adultery (the seventh and eighth commandments reversed).
50.19: phîkhā shālaḥtā bĕrāʿāh — you let your mouth run loose into evil. Shālaḥ pheh bĕ (to send/let loose the mouth into) — the unrestrained mouth directed into evil speech. ûleshônĕkhā taṣmîd mirmmāh — and your tongue is busy weaving lies. Ṣāmad (to attach, join closely — the tongue joined to deceit as a craftsman works at a loom). Mirmāh (deceit, treachery — the crafted lie).
50.20: tēshēb bĕʾāḥîkhā tĕdabbēr — you sit and talk against your own brother. Yāshab + dibber bĕ (to sit and speak against — the posture of settled slander, deliberate and comfortable). bĕben-ʾimmĕkhā titten dōphî — you slander your mother's son. Ben-ʾēm (son of your mother — the closest familial bond, the full sibling) is the one slandered (dōphî: blemish, slander). The covenant violations cut through the most intimate human bonds.
50.21: ʾēlleh ʿāśîtā wĕhehĕrashttî — you did all these things, and I said nothing. The divine silence interpreted by the wicked as divine approval — the dangerous theological mistake. dimmîtā heyôt-ʾehyeh kāmôkhā — you concluded I must be just like you. Dimmāh (to think, imagine — to picture in the mind) + heyôt-ʾehyeh kāmôkhā (that I would be like you). The theological diagnosis: anthropomorphism in the deepest sense — projecting human moral character onto God, concluding that the silent God shares the worshiper's values. ʾôkhîḥăkhā wĕʾerkhāh lĕʿênêkhā — but now I rebuke you and lay everything out before your eyes. The divine silence is broken — the judicial speech (v7) delivers its full rebuke.
50.22: bînû-nāʾ zōʾt shōkĕḥê ʾĕlōhîm — take this seriously, you who have forgotten God. Shākhah ʾĕlōhîm (to forget God — the covenant failure of Deut 6:12; 8:11-14). pen-ʾeṭrōph wĕʾên maṣṣîl — or I will tear you apart, and no one will rescue you. Tāraph (to tear as a lion tears prey — the predatory divine judgment on those who forget God). The urgency: no rescuer will be available once the divine tearing begins.
50.23: zōbēaḥ tôdāh yĕkhabbĕdānnî — whoever brings thanksgiving as their sacrifice glorifies me. The closing positive statement (cf. v14): the tôdāh-sacrifice honors God precisely because it acknowledges dependence rather than attempting to obligate God through material gifts. wĕśām dārekh ʾarʾennû bĕyeshaʿ ʾĕlōhîm — to the one who walks a right path I will show the salvation of God. Śām dārekh (one who orders/prepares the way — who walks in the prepared path of the covenant) receives the divine salvation (yeshaʿ ʾĕlōhîm) as its natural outcome. The lawsuit closes not with condemnation but with the open door of thanksgiving and right-path-walking.

51.1: ḥānnēnî ʾĕlōhîm kĕḥasdekā — have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love. The triple ground of the petition: ḥesed (steadfast love) + raḥămîm (compassion, tender mercies — from reḥem: the womb; the visceral compassion of a mother for her child) + ḥesed again in expanded form. The three divine attributes are offered as the only basis the Psalmist can appeal to — not innocence but divine character. kĕrōb raḥămêkhā meḥē pĕshāʿay — in your great compassion, blot out my transgressions. Māḥāh (to blot out, wipe away — as ink is wiped from a tablet; the record of pĕshāʿîm (rebellious acts — the most willful category of sin, from pāshaʿ: to rebel, to transgress deliberately against authority) erased from the divine ledger.
51.2: harbeḥ kabbĕsênî mēʿăwōnî — scrub away my guilt completely. Kābaś (to wash by pressing, kneading, treading — the vigorous washing that requires physical effort; the laundering of heavily soiled cloth, not a gentle rinse). The intensive washing vocabulary: harbeḥ kabbĕsênî (wash me greatly — the adverb harbeḥ intensifying the already intense verb). ûmēḥaṭṭāʾtî ṭahărênî — and purify me from my sin. Ṭāhēr (piel: to declare clean, to purify — the priestly verdict of purification). Ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin — the missing of the mark, the deviation from the path).
51.3: kî-pĕshāʿay ʾănî ʾēdāʿ — for I know my rebellious acts. The acknowledgment of full awareness — the Psalmist cannot plead ignorance. wĕḥaṭṭāʾtî negdî tāmîd — and my sin is before me at every moment. Negdî tāmîd (before me always — continuously present to the conscience). The sin that was committed at a specific moment (the David/Bathsheba crisis) has become an unrelenting presence in the sinner's self-awareness.
51.4: lĕkhā lĕbaddekā ḥāṭāʾtî wĕhārāʿ bĕʿênêkhā ʿāśîtî — against you — you alone — have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight. Lĕkhā lĕbaddekā (to you, to you alone — the exclusive particle lĕbad) emphasizes the vertical dimension of sin: the sin against Bathsheba and Uriah is simultaneously and ultimately a sin against God. Not that David did not sin against humans, but that the divine offense is primary. lĕmaʿan tiṣdaq bĕdabbĕrekhā — so that you are proved right when you speak. Ṣādaq (qal: to be righteous, be in the right) when he speaks his verdict — the confession validates the divine judgment. Paul cites this in Rom 3:4 (hina an dikaiōthēs en tois logois sou: that you might be justified in your words) as establishing the universal sinfulness that only divine justification can address.
51.5: hēn bĕʿāwôn ḥôlāltî — surely I was brought forth into guilt. Ḥôlāltî (polal of ḥûl/ḥîl: to be brought forth in labor-pangs — to be born). ʿĀwôn (iniquity, guilt — the state of crooked relationship to God's standard). The Psalmist traces his present sin back to his condition at birth — solidarity with human sinfulness. ûbĕḥēṭ yĕḥemmatanî ʾimmî — and in sin my mother conceived me. Not a statement that conception or birth are sinful acts, but that the human condition from its first moment is marked by the sinful state of humanity that precedes any individual act.
51.6: hēn ʾĕmet ḥāpaṣtā battûḥôt — you desire truth deep inside a person. Battûḥôt (in the innermost parts — the hidden interior of a person; from tāḥaḥ: to be beneath/below). ʾĕmet (truth, reliability — the covenant faithfulness). God's requirement is not exterior performance but interior truthfulness. ûbĕsātûm ḥokhĕmāh tôdîʿēnî — in the hidden place you are teaching me wisdom. Sātûm (the covered, hidden place — the innermost secret; from sātar: to hide). The divine instruction in the hidden interior: wisdom taught in the secret place, corresponding to the truth required there.
51.7: tĕḥaṭṭĕʾênî bĕʾēzôb wĕʾeṭhār — cleanse me with hyssop, and I will be clean. ʾĒzôb (hyssop — the small, fragrant herb used in ritual purification for those with skin disease or corpse-impurity: Lev 14:4-7, 49-52; Num 19:6, 18). The hyssop dipped in the blood of a bird or the water of purification was used to sprinkle the unclean. Here the Psalmist applies the imagery to his inner moral condition. tĕkabbĕsênî ûmishsheleg ʾalbîn — wash me, and I will be whiter than snow. The comparison to snow (sheleg) sets the highest possible standard of whiteness — snow is the purest white in the natural world (cf. Isa 1:18: ʾim-yihyû ḥăṭāʾêkhem kashĕnî kĕsheleg yalbînû).
51.8: tashmiʿênî śāśôn wĕśimḥāh — let me hear the sounds of joy and gladness. The petition to hear joy and gladness as the divine response to forgiveness — the senses restored to their proper objects. tāgēlanāh ʿăṣāmôt dikkîtāh — let the bones you have crushed be glad. Dākhāh (piel: to crush — the same root as dakkāʾ in Ps 90:3). The bones as the deepest physical structure — divine judgment reaches to the skeletal level. The crushed bones that are glad when forgiveness comes express the physicality of guilt and its relief.
51.9: hastēr pānêkhā mēḥăṭāʾāy — look away from my sins. Haster pānim (to hide the face — to look away from, not to see). The unusual request: the divine face hidden from the sins rather than from the sinner (normally, hester pānim from the person is the experience of abandonment; here it is requested that God look away from the sin record). wĕkhol-ʿăwōnōtay meḥē — wipe out all my guilt. Māḥāh again (cf. v1) — the complete erasure of all iniquity.
