The renewal of youth as the culmination of the participial string: forgiving, healing, redeeming, crowning, satisfying — all together constitute the restoration of the whole person.
103.6: ʿōśeh ṣĕdāqôt YHWH ûmishpāṭîm lĕkhol-ʿăshûqîm — the LORD acts with righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed. Ṣĕdāqôt (righteous acts — the plural indicating multiple acts of justice). ʿĀshûq (oppressed — passive participle of ʿāshaq: to squeeze/oppress, those crushed by injustice). The movement from the personal (vv1-5: benefits for the individual soul) to the social (v6: justice for the oppressed). YHWH's ḥesed expressed in both personal healing and social justice.
103.7: yôdîaʿ dĕrākhāyw lĕmōsheh libnê yisrāʾēl ʿalîlôtāyw — he revealed his ways to Moses and his mighty deeds to the people of Israel. Dārakh (ways — the characteristic patterns of divine action) revealed to Moses specifically — the Sinai self-revelation of Exod 33-34, where Moses asked to know God's ways (hôdîʿênî nā ʾet-dĕrākekhā, Exod 33:13). ʿAlîlāh (mighty deed, act — the recorded powerful acts) to the whole people. Moses received the why (the ways); Israel received the what (the acts).
103.8: raḥûm wĕḥannûn YHWH ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab-ḥāsed — the LORD is compassionate and gracious, patient in anger and overflowing with steadfast love. The direct citation of the Mosaic self-revelation formula of Exod 34:6 (the middôt — divine attributes). This is the text Moses received when he asked to know God's ways (v7). The four attributes: raḥûm (compassionate — from reḥem: womb), ḥannûn (gracious — giving grace freely), ʾerek ʾappayim (long-nostrilled: slow to anger — the nostril-flaring of rage restrained), rab-ḥesed (great in steadfast love). The most cited divine self-description in the OT (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jon 4:2).
103.9: lōʾ-lāneṣaḥ yārîb wĕlōʾ lĕʿôlām yiṭṭôr — he does not bring charges forever, nor does he hold on to his anger without end. Rîb (to contend, bring a legal charge — the covenant-lawsuit verb; cf. Ps 50's divine lawsuit). Nāṭar (to keep, maintain — to hold a grudge permanently). The temporally bounded nature of divine anger: lāneṣaḥ (forever) and lĕʿôlām (to the age) both negated. YHWH's anger is real but not eternal — the divine patience eventually resolves in forgiveness, not in permanent prosecution.
103.10: lōʾ khĕḥăṭāʾênû ʿāśāh lānû wĕlōʾ khăʿăwōnōtênû gāmal ʿālênû — he has not treated us as our sins deserve, nor paid us back measure for measure for our iniquities. The double negation: lōʾ… lōʾ — neither sins-proportional treatment (ḥăṭāʾênû: our sins) nor iniquities-proportional repayment (ʿăwōnōtênû gāmal: our iniquities measured back). Gāmal (to deal fully with, to recompense exactly) — the exact recompense that divine mercy withholds. The psalm does not claim there is no divine judgment but that divine mercy prevents full judicial recompense.
103.11: kî kigbōaḥ shāmayim ʿal-hāʾāreṣ gābar ḥasdô ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — for as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love for those who fear him. The spatial metaphor for the immeasurable: the height of the heavens above the earth is the maximum visible distance (it cannot be measured from below). Gābar (to be mighty, prevail — the divine ḥesed is mighty, prevailing in measure). The comparison with the immeasurable does not quantify the ḥesed — it declares it beyond quantification.
103.12: kirhōq mizrāḥ mimmāʿărab hirḥîq mimmennû ʾet-pĕshāʿênû — as far as east is from west, that is how far he has removed our transgressions from us. The east-west axis is directional, not circular: north and south eventually meet (one could travel far enough north to arrive in the south), but east and west never meet — one can travel east forever without arriving west. The infinite distance in the east-west direction is the image for the complete removal of transgressions (pĕshāʿîm: willful rebellious acts). Hirḥîq (hiphil: to remove to a distance — to place far away).
103.13: kĕraḥēm ʾāb ʿal-bānîm rāḥam YHWH ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — just as a father is tender toward his children, so the LORD is tender toward those who fear him. Rāḥam (piel: to show deep compassion — from reḥem: the womb). The father's compassion for his children as the analogy for divine compassion — not the womb-compassion of a mother (though that imagery also appears in Isa 49:15) but the protective, tender compassion of a father. The familial metaphor for divine-human relationship grounding the forgiveness of vv9-12.
103.14: kî-hûʾ yādaʿ yiṣrênû zākhûr kî-ʿāphār ʾănaḥnû — for he knows exactly what we are made of; he has not forgotten that we are dust. Yāṣar (to form, shape — the potter's word; YHWH knows our formation because he formed us, Gen 2:7). Zākhûr (a passive participle: he is one who remembers — the ongoing divine awareness). ʿĀphār (dust — from Gen 2:7: wayyîṣer YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hāʾādām ʿāphār min-hāʾădāmāh; and Gen 3:19: ʿāphār ʾattāh wĕʾel-ʿāphār tāshûb). The divine compassion (v13) grounded in the divine knowledge of human frailty: God's mercy is not naïve but precisely calibrated to what we actually are.
103.15: ʾĕnôsh kĕḥāṣîr yāmāyw kĕṣîṣ haśśādeh kēn yāṣîṣ — as for mortals, their days are like grass; they bloom like a wildflower of the field. ʾĔnôsh (mortal man — the word emphasizing human frailty, from a root meaning to be sick/weak; contrast with ʾādām or ʾîsh). Ḥāṣîr (grass — cf. Ps 90:5-6; Isa 40:6-8). Ṣîṣ haśśādeh (wildflower of the field — the seasonal flower of the Palestinian landscape, beautiful and brief). The grass and wildflower together represent beauty + transience.
103.16: kî rûaḥ ʿābĕrāh-bô wĕʾênennû — the wind sweeps over it and it vanishes. Rûaḥ (wind — here the hot sirocco that desiccates vegetation rapidly). ʿĀbar (to pass over — the wind's passage is enough to kill the grass). wĕlōʾ-yakkîrennû ʿôd mĕqômô — and the place where it stood no longer knows it. The personification of māqôm (place): the place no longer recognizes the person who stood there. The completeness of mortality — not just the person disappears but their location forgets them. Compare with the permanence of divine ḥesed in the next verse.
103.17: wĕḥesed YHWH mēʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — but the steadfast love of the LORD is from age to age over those who fear him. The pivot: the brevity of human life (vv15-16) set against the eternal divine ḥesed. Mēʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from age to age — from everlasting to everlasting): the temporal scope of ḥesed is the exact opposite of human transience. wĕṣidqātô libnê bānîm — and his righteousness reaches to their grandchildren. The divine righteousness reaching to generations (bĕnê bānîm: sons of sons/grandchildren) — the covenantal faithfulness that outlasts individual lives and passes through family lines.
103.18: lĕshōmĕrê bĕrîtô ûlĕzōkhĕrê phiqquddāyw laʿăśôtām — to those who hold to his covenant and keep his commands in mind to live by them. The two conditions defining those who receive the eternal ḥesed: shāmar bĕrît (keeping the covenant — the active maintenance of covenant relationship) + zākar phiqqûdîm laʿăśôt (remembering commands in order to do them — the doing as the purpose of the remembering). The ethical and relational dimensions together: covenant identity + obedient action.
103.19: YHWH bashshāmayim hēkhîn kissĕʾô ûmalkûtô bakkōl māshālāh — the LORD has set up his throne in the heavens; his kingdom governs everything that exists. Kûn (hiphil perfect: he has established firmly — the divine throne is already established, not being established). Malkût (kingdom, sovereignty) governing bakkōl (over everything — the universal scope). The theological climax: before calling on the cosmic community to praise (vv20-22), the basis is stated — YHWH's universal sovereignty. Every created entity called to bless is within his governance.
103.20: bārekû YHWH malʾākhāyw gibbōrê kōaḥ — bless the LORD, you his angels — his mighty warriors. The outward expansion of blessing: from the single soul (nafshî, vv1-2) now to the angels (malʾākhîm: messengers, divine council members). Gibbōrê kōaḥ (mighty in strength — the angels as powerful beings, cf. Ps 29:1's bĕnê ʾēlîm). ʿōśê dĕbārô lishmoaʿ bĕqôl dĕbārô — who carry out his commands, swift to obey his voice. ʿĀśāh dābār (to carry out his word/command) — obedience as the defining activity of the angels. Lishmoaʿ bĕqôl dĕbārô (to hear/obey the voice of his word) — hearing as the prelude to obedience.
103.21: bārekû YHWH kol-ṣĕbāʾāyw mĕshārĕtāyw ʿōśê rĕṣônô — bless the LORD, all his armies — all who serve him and carry out his purposes! Ṣĕbāʾîm (armies, hosts — the divine armies of heaven, giving YHWH his title ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt: God of hosts). Mĕshārĕtāyw (his servants/ministers — those who serve in the divine court). Rāṣôn (his pleasure, will, purpose) done: the angels carry out divine purposes rather than their own. The second circle of blessing: the angelic armies.
103.22: bārekû YHWH kol-maʿăśāyw bĕkhol-mĕqōmôt memshaltô — bless the LORD, everything he has made, in every place his reign extends. The third circle: all of creation (kol-maʿăśāyw: all his works) in every place his dominion extends. The universal blessing of the Creator by all his creatures — the creation-wide doxology. bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH — bless the LORD, O my soul! The return to v1: the psalm that expanded from one soul to all creation returns to the single soul. The cosmic praise collapses back into the individual's personal blessing — showing that the single soul's praise participates in and echoes the cosmic praise of all creation.

104.1: bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH YHWH ʾĕlōhay gādaltā mĕʾōd — bless the LORD, O my soul! O LORD my God, how great you are. The self-address of Ps 103:1 opens the creation hymn. Gādaltā mĕʾōd (you are very great — the exclamatory recognition of divine greatness in the face of creation's scope). hôd wĕhādār lābāshtā — robed in honor and majesty. Hôd (splendor, majesty — the radiant glory of the divine presence) + hādār (honor, ornamental beauty). The divine Creator clothed in the qualities of creation's own beauty.
104.2: ʿōṭeh-ʾôr kaśśalmāh — wrapped in light as in a robe. Light is not created (as in Gen 1:3 where it is the first created thing) but worn as a garment — the divine self-clothing in light precedes creation. The light is the outward expression of the divine glory, the garment that makes YHWH visible. nôṭeh shāmayim kayyĕrîʿāh — spreading out the heavens like a tent curtain. Yĕrîʿāh (tent curtain — the fabric panels of a tent). The heavens stretched like tent fabric by the divine hand — the image of a powerful deity pitching a cosmic tent (cf. Isa 40:22: hantôṭeh kaddôq shāmayim).
104.3: hameqāreh bammayim ʿăliyyôtāyw — he sets the beams of his upper chambers on the waters. Qārāh (to set beams — the laying of the ceiling beams of a building) on the waters above the firmament (Gen 1:7's waters above). The divine palace above the heavenly waters — the upper rooms (ʿăliyyôt) of the cosmic palace. haśśām ʿābîm rĕkhûbô hamehallek ʿal-kanpê-rûaḥ — he mounts the clouds as his chariot; he travels on the wings of the wind. ʿĀb (thick cloud) as divine chariot — the storm-cloud as the vehicle of the divine warrior (cf. Ps 68:4, 33; Isa 19:1). Kanpê-rûaḥ (wings of the wind) — the wind as the speed of divine movement.
104.4: ʿōśeh malʾākhāyw rûḥôt mĕshārĕtāyw ʾēsh lōhēṭ — he makes the winds his messengers and flashing fire his servants. The deliberate ambiguity: malʾākhāyw rûḥôt can be read as either (a) he makes his angels into winds (the angels are the winds) or (b) he makes the winds his messengers/angels. Both readings are grammatically valid. Heb 1:7 reads this in sense (a) — angels as winds — applying it to the Son's superiority over angels. The psalm's point in either case: the natural forces (wind, fire) are divine servants, not independent powers or deities.
104.5: yāsad-ʾereṣ ʿal-mĕkhônêhā — he fixed the earth on its foundations. Yāsad (to found, lay the foundation — the architect's act of establishing the base). Mĕkhôn (foundation, established base — from kûn: to be fixed). bal-timmôṭ ʿôlām wāʿed — so that it will never be shaken. Mûṭ (to shake, totter — the destabilization of what is fixed). The double temporal scope: ʿôlām wāʿed (forever and ever). The earth's stability as a consequence of divine founding — it holds because of the divine action that established it.
104.6: tĕhôm kallib-bûsh kissîtô — you covered it with the deep like a cloak. Tĕhôm (the deep — the primordial ocean of Gen 1:2; the deep that covered the pre-creation earth). Libûsh (clothing, garment) — the deep draped over the earth like a cloak (echoing the divine light-clothing of v2: YHWH wears light; the earth is clothed in the deep). ʿal-hārîm yaʿamdû māyim — the waters towered above the mountains. The primordial state: waters covering even the mountain peaks — the pre-creation condition before the divine ordering of v7-9.
104.7: min-gĕʿārātĕkhā yānûsûn — at your rebuke they retreated. Gāʿar (to rebuke — the commanding voice that drives away; cf. Ps 106:9 where the sea is rebuked at the Exodus). The waters flee at the divine rebuke — the chaos-ordering by divine voice. min-qôl raʿamĕkhā yēḥāpēzûn — at the sound of your thunder they fled in haste. Raʿam (thunder — the divine voice in the storm) causing the waters to flee in terror (ḥāpaz: to be in hasty flight). The creation of order is described in the language of a theophany — the storm-God commanding the chaotic waters.
104.8: yaʿălû hārîm yērĕdû bĕqāʿôt ʾel-mĕqôm zeh yāsadtā lāhem — the mountains rose and the valleys sank to the places you had set for them. The topographic ordering: mountains rising, valleys descending — the creation of the varied landscape. Mĕqôm zeh yāsadtā lāhem (the place you founded for them) — each geological feature placed exactly where YHWH designed it. The creative act as precise architectural intentionality.
104.9: gĕbûl śamtā bal-yaʿăbōrûn — you drew a boundary they cannot cross. Gĕbûl (boundary, border — the territorial limit set by divine decree) + lōʾ yaʿăbōrûn (they shall not cross it — the permanent prohibition on the chaos-waters). bal-yĕkhassûn lĕkhassôt hāʾāreṣ — so the waters will never again engulf the earth. The covenant promise echoing the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:11-15): the waters will not again cover the earth. The creation-order is protected by divine decree — the chaos does not simply retreat but is permanently bounded.
104.10: hamekhallēaḥ maʿyānîm bannĕḥālîm — he sends springs into the valleys. The transition from creation's ordering (vv5-9) to creation's provision (vv10-18): water in the right place (springs in valleys, not covering mountains) becomes the source of life. Maʿyān (spring of water) sent into nĕḥālîm (valleys, wadis — the dry riverbeds that fill with spring water). bên hārîm yĕhallēkhûn — they run between the hills. The springs becoming streams flowing through the hill country — the water system threading through the created landscape.
104.11: yashqû kol-ḥaytô śādāy — giving water to every wild animal. Shāqāh (to water, give drink) — the springs providing water to all the wild animals. yishbĕrû pĕrāʾîm ṣĕmāʾām — the wild donkeys slake their thirst there. Pereh (wild donkey/onager — the untameable wild ass of the open steppe; Job 39:5-8 describes its freedom). The wild, uncatchable creature finds its thirst quenched by the Creator's springs — even the wildest animals are provided for.
104.12: ʿălêhem ʿôph hashshāmayim yishkôn — along their banks the birds of the sky make their homes. Shākan (to dwell, tabernacle — the verb of the divine dwelling among Israel; here the birds of the sky dwelling along the streams). mibbên ʿŏphāyim yittĕnû-qôl — and sing among the branches. The birdsong among the branches (ʿŏphāyim: branches, foliage) as part of the created order's praise — the birds' song as creation's voice.
104.13: mashqeh hārîm mēʿăliyyôtāyw — from his upper chambers he sends rain down the mountains. ʿĂliyyôt (upper chambers — the same divine palace-rooms of v3 from which the cloud-chariot departs). The rain comes from the divine palace above the clouds — the water cycle as divine provision from the heavenly dwelling. mippĕrî maʿăśêkhā tiśbaʿ hāʾāreṣ — the earth is filled by what he provides. Mippĕrî maʿăśêkhā (from the fruit of your works) — the earth satisfied by the fruit of the divine creative activity. Creation's abundance is always the ongoing result of divine action, not an independent system.
104.14: maṣmîaḥ ḥāṣîr labbĕhēmāh wĕʿēśeb laʿăbōdat hāʾādām — he makes grass grow for the cattle and plants for people to tend. Ṣāmaḥ (hiphil: to cause to sprout — the divine causation of vegetative growth). Ḥāṣîr labbĕhēmāh (grass for the cattle) + ʿēśeb laʿăbōdat hāʾādām (vegetation for the labor/service of humanity — the agricultural plants that human work cultivates). lĕhôṣîʾ leḥem min-hāʾāreṣ — so they can bring food out of the ground. The partnership: God causes growth, humans labor, bread comes from the ground. Divine and human action together in the provision of food.
104.15: wĕyayin yĕśammah lĕbab-ʾĕnôsh — wine that lifts the human heart. Śāmaḥ lēbāb (to gladden the heart) — wine as a created good, explicitly approved by the Creator as something that produces the gladness of the human heart. lĕhahălîl pānîm mishshāmen — oil that makes the face glow. Hālal pānîm (to make the face shine — the radiant, health-full face) from the oil. wĕleḥem lĕbab-ʾĕnôsh yisʿād — and bread that gives strength to the human heart. Sāʿad (to support, sustain — bread as the fundamental support of the body and the heart). The three gifts (wine, oil, bread) represent the full range of agricultural provision — festive, cosmetic, and nutritive.
104.16: yiśbĕʿû ʿăṣê YHWH ʾarzê lĕbānôn ʾăsher nāṭāʿ — the trees of the LORD drink deeply — the great cedars of Lebanon that he himself planted. ʿĂṣê YHWH (the trees of the LORD — trees belonging to YHWH by his planting) = the cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê lĕbānôn). Nāṭaʿ (to plant — the divine planting of the great trees). The magnificent cedars are not self-seeded but divinely planted — God as the forester who planted the greatest trees on earth. They are yiśbĕʿûn (satisfied/well-watered) — provided for as YHWH provides for all living things.
104.17: ʾăsher shām ṣippārîm yĕqannēnû — there the birds build their nests. The great cedars as habitat — the magnificent trees become the homes of the birds. Qānan (to build a nest — the nesting act). ḥăsîdāh bĕrôshîm bêtāh — the stork finds her home in the cypress trees. Ḥăsîdāh (the stork — from ḥesed: the stork known for its loyalty/devotion to its young; the bird named for covenant-love). Bĕrôsh (cypress or juniper). Even the stork — whose name carries the root of covenant loyalty — is provided for in the divinely-planted trees.
104.18: hārîm haggĕbōhîm layye-ʿēlîm — the rugged mountains belong to the wild goats. Yaʿēl (mountain goat/ibex — the wild goat adapted to the steep rocky terrain). sĕlāʿîm maḥseh lashshĕphannîm — the rocky crags shelter the rock badgers. Shāphān (rock badger/hyrax — the small mammal that lives in rock crevices). The full range of habitats — tall cedars, mountains, rocky crags — each provided with its appropriate inhabitants. The ecosystems of creation as divine arrangement, not random distribution.
104.19: ʿāśāh yārēaḥ lĕmôʿădîm — he appointed the moon to mark the seasons. Môʿēd (appointed time, season, festival — the calendar system). The moon created specifically for the purpose of marking the calendar (lĕmôʿădîm: for the appointed seasons). shemesh yādaʿ mĕbôʾô — the sun knows exactly when to set. Yādaʿ mābôʾ (knows its setting — the sun as an obedient servant that knows its time). Both luminaries created for their functions, not worshiped as deities — the radical demythologization of the astral powers in Israelite cosmology (cf. Gen 1:14-18 where they are named merely greater light and lesser light).
104.20: tāshēt ḥōshekh wayĕhî lāylāh — you bring on darkness and it becomes night. The divine causing of night — YHWH as the one who sets (shît) the darkness into place. Night is not the absence of divine activity but a divinely appointed time. bô-tirmōś kol-ḥaytô-yāʿar — when all the forest creatures stir. Rāmaś (to creep, crawl, move about — especially of the smaller animals). The night as the time appointed for the forest animals' activity — the nocturnal ecosystem as part of the divine design.
104.21: hakkĕphîrîm shōʾăgîm lataref — the young lions roar as they hunt. Kĕphîr (young lion — the lion in its prime hunting strength). Shāʾag (to roar — the hunting roar of the lion). ûlĕbaqshēsh mēʾēl ʾākĕlām — looking to God for their food. Bāqash mēʾēl (seeking from God) — even the lion's hunting is described as seeking food from God. The predator's hunger and the act of hunting are within the providential framework of the Creator's provision. Not a theological problem but a theological observation: the whole creation, including its predatory activity, is encompassed in divine providence.
104.22: tizraḥ hashshemesh yēʾāsēphûn — when the sun comes up, they slip away. The transition at sunrise: the nocturnal hunters return to their lairs. ʾāsaph (niphal: to be gathered, to withdraw — the lions withdrawing as the sun rises). wĕʾel-mĕʿônōtām yirĕbāṣûn — and stretch out in their lairs. Rābaṣ (to crouch, lie down — the resting posture of an animal in its den). The daily rhythm of animal life governed by the sun's rising and setting — the clock set by the Creator in v19 regulates the whole ecosystem.
104.23: yēṣēʾ ʾādām lĕphōʿŏlô wĕlaʿăbōdātô ʿădê-ʿāreb — then people go out to their work and labor on until the evening. ʾĀdām (humanity — the human sphere of activity). Phōʿal (work, labor) + ʿăbōdāh (service, labor — the human labor to which people go out at sunrise). The human labor-day framed within the same creation order that governs the lions' night — humans and lions share the same divinely ordered world, each active in its appointed time.
104.24: māh-rabbû maʿăśêkhā YHWH kullām bĕḥokhĕmāh ʿāśîtā — LORD, what a multitude of works you have made! You crafted them all in wisdom. Māh-rabbû (how many! — the exclamatory response to creation's abundance). Bĕḥokhĕmāh ʿāśîtā (you made all of them in wisdom — ḥokhĕmāh as the divine design-principle behind creation; cf. Prov 8:22-31 where Wisdom is personified as present at creation). māleʾāh hāʾāreṣ qinyanekā — the earth is full of what belongs to you. Qinyan (acquisition, possession, creature — what God has acquired/created). The earth as YHWH's full possession.
104.25: zeh hayyām gādôl ûrĕḥab yādāyim — and here is the sea — vast and wide. Zeh (this — the demonstrative pointing as if to say: look at that). Gādôl ûrĕḥab yādāyim (great and wide of hands/dimensions — the spatial immensity of the sea). shām remes wĕʾên mispār ḥayyôt qĕṭannôt ʿim-gĕdōlôt — teeming with creatures beyond counting, great and small alike. Remes (swarming, creeping things — the creatures that swarm in the sea). ʾên mispār (no counting — the innumerable abundance). Great and small together: the ecosystem's full range of scale.
104.26: shām ʾoniyyôt yĕhallēkhûn lĕwiyātān zeh-yāṣartā lĕśaḥeq-bô — the ships sail there, and there is Leviathan, which you formed to frolic in the deep. Lĕwiyātān (Leviathan — the chaos dragon of Job 41, Ps 74:14, Isa 27:1; the sea-monster that represents primordial chaos in the ancient Near Eastern imagination). Here the psalm radically domesticates the chaos monster: zeh-yāṣartā lĕśaḥeq-bô (this one you formed to play in it). Leviathan is not an enemy to be defeated (as in Ps 74:14 or the Ugaritic Baal mythology) but a creature YHWH formed (yāṣar: the potter's forming) specifically to play (śāḥaq: to sport, play) in the sea. The most feared creature in ancient mythology becomes God's pet — the ultimate demythologization of chaos.
104.27: kullām ʾēlêkhā yĕśabbērûn lātēt ʾokhĕlām bĕʿittô — all of these look to you to give them their food when they need it. Śābar ʾēl (to look/wait expectantly toward — the posture of patient expectation directed toward God). All the creatures of vv10-26 — wild donkeys, birds, storks, ibex, lions, sea creatures, Leviathan itself — all looking to YHWH for their food. Bĕʿittô (in its time — the providential timing of the divine provision). Creation is not self-sustaining but continuously dependent on the Creator.
104.28: tittēn lāhem yilqôṭûn tiphttaḥ yādĕkhā yiśbĕʿûn ṭôb — when you give, they gather it in; when you open your hand, they are satisfied with good. Yalqaṭ (to gather — as grain is gathered from the field). Pātaḥ yād (to open the hand — the gesture of generous giving; cf. Ps 145:16: the divine open hand satisfying the desire of every living thing). Śābaʿ ṭôb (to be satisfied with good) — the same satisfaction sought in human prayer answered in the ongoing divine provision to all creatures.
104.29: tastîr pānêkhā yibbāhēlûn — if you hide your face, they are terrified. Sātar pānim (to hide the face — the divine withdrawal of attention and favor; cf. Ps 88:14; 102:2). When the divine face is hidden, the creatures are terrified (bāhal: to be dismayed, thrown into panic). tōsēph rûḥām yigwāʿûn wĕʾel-ʿăphārām yāshûbûn — if you take back their breath, they die and return to dust. Rûaḥ (breath, spirit) given or taken by God — the divine in-breathing and out-taking as the generator and terminator of life. ʿĂphār (dust — returning to Gen 3:19's dust). The Creator's breath sustains; its withdrawal ends.
104.30: tishlaḥ rûḥăkhā yibbārēʾûn ûtĕḥaddēsh pĕnê ʾădāmāh — when you send out your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the surface of the earth. Shālaḥ rûaḥ (to send out the Spirit/breath) + bārāʾ (to create — the Genesis 1 creation verb). The continuous creation: YHWH sending out his Spirit continually creates new life. Tĕḥaddēsh pĕnê ʾădāmāh (you renew the face of the earth) — the renewal of the created surface. The Spirit of Gen 1:2 (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm mĕraḥephet ʿal-pĕnê hammāyim) as the ongoing creative force in creation, not merely a past event.
104.31: yĕhî khĕbôd YHWH lĕʿôlām yiśmaḥ YHWH bĕmaʿăśāyw — may the glory of the LORD last forever; may the LORD take delight in what he has made! Kĕbôd YHWH lĕʿôlām (the glory of YHWH forever) — the prayer that the divine glory, the weight and presence of God, endure eternally. Yiśmaḥ YHWH bĕmaʿăśāyw (may YHWH take delight/rejoice in his works) — echoing Gen 1:31 (wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-kol-ʾăsher ʿāśāh wĕhinnēh ṭôb mĕʾōd: God saw all he had made and it was very good). The petition that the divine delight in creation continue — a relational prayer asking that the Creator-creature relationship of pleasure persist.
104.32: hammaббîṭ lāʾāreṣ wattirʿad yiggaʿ bĕhārîm wĕyeʿĕshānû — he looks at the earth and it shakes; he touches the mountains and they pour out smoke. The theophanic power: divine looking causes earthquakes; divine touching causes volcanic eruption. The same power that ordered creation (vv5-9) can unmake its structures with a glance. The Creator's power over creation is not merely historical but perpetual and immediate.
104.33: ʾāshîrāh laYHWH bĕḥayyāy ʾăzammĕrāh lĕʾĕlōhay bĕʿôdî — I will sing to the LORD as long as I live; I will sing praise to my God for as long as I draw breath. Bĕḥayyāy (while I live — the full span of the Psalmist's life dedicated to praise). Bĕʿôdî (while I yet am — while I exist, while there is still breath). The personal vow: the lifetime of the individual Psalmist devoted to singing and praise. The cosmic creation hymn narrows at the end to the single human voice committed to praise for life.
104.34: yĕʿerab ʿālāyw śîḥî ʾānōkhî ʾeśmaḥ baYHWH — may my thoughts about him be sweet to him, as I find my joy in the LORD. ʿĀrēb (to be sweet, pleasing — the flavor-word for something delightful). Śîaḥ (meditation, reflection — the murmured meditation of Ps 77:3, 6; 104:34). The prayer that the Psalmist's meditation be pleasing (ʿārēb) to YHWH — the inner life offered as an acceptable offering. ʾĔśmaḥ baYHWH (I will rejoice in the LORD) — the personal joy grounded in the Creator who has just been celebrated across 33 verses.
104.35: yittammû ḥaṭṭāʾîm min-hāʾāreṣ ûrĕshāʿîm ʿôd ʾênam — let sinners vanish from the earth and let the wicked exist no longer! The jarring petition at the creation hymn's end — the one discordant note in the celebration of created order. Sinners (ḥaṭṭāʾîm) and the wicked (rĕshāʿîm) are the creatures who mar what has been celebrated as beautiful and good. The prayer for their removal is not vindictive but ecological — creation's perfection requires the removal of those who desecrate it. bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH hallĕlûyāh — bless the LORD, O my soul! Hallelujah! The return to the opening self-address (as in Ps 103:22) and the first hallĕlûyāh (praise-YHWH) in the Psalter — a compound formed from hallelû (praise ye!) + YAH (the abbreviated divine name). The creation hymn ends with the first hallelujah, launching the hallelujah psalms that increasingly dominate Psalms 105-150.

107.1: הוֹדוּ לַיהוָה כִּי-טוֹב כִּי לְעוֹלָם חַסְדּוֹ (hôdû layhwāh kî-ṭôb kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô, "Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever!") — the hallel formula (Ps 100:5; 106:1; 118:1,29; 136 — where it forms the refrain after every verse). Hôdû: acknowledge, give thanks (from yādāh: to extend the hand in acknowledgment — both to confess and to praise). Kî-ṭôb: because he is good (not merely because of his benefits). Kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô: because his steadfast love endures forever — the unbounded duration of the covenant commitment that makes giving thanks not merely appropriate but safe: the God thanked will not withdraw. Psalm 107 is the only psalm to open with the hallel formula and then follow it with four extended narrative strophes.
107.2: יֹאמְרוּ גְּאוּלֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר גְּאָלָם מִיַּד-צָר (yōʾmĕrû gĕʾûlê yĕhwāh ʾăsher gĕʾālām miyyad-ṣār, "Let those the LORD has redeemed declare it — those he bought back from the grip of the enemy!") — gĕʾûlê yĕhwāh: the redeemed of the LORD (from gāʾal: the kinsman-redeemer word — the family obligation to buy back a relative from slavery or debt — Lev 25:25,47-55; Ruth 3-4; Isa 41:14; 43:1,14). This is not a commercial purchase but a covenantal rescue by the divine next-of-kin. Miyyad-ṣār: from the hand of the adversary/oppressor (the grip of the enemy treated as a physical clench from which the redeemed must be pried). The psalm summons this specific community — the redeemed — as its primary narrators.
107.3: וּמֵאֲרָצוֹת קִבְּצָם מִמִּזְרָח וּמִמַּעֲרָב מִצָּפוֹן וּמִיָּם (ûmêʾărāṣôt qibbĕṣām mimmizrāḥ ûmimmaʿărāb miṣṣāpôn ûmiyyām, "gathered in from every land — from east and west, from north and south!") — the four compass directions (east, west, north, and yām = the sea/south in Israel's orientation where the west = the Mediterranean and "south" was often specified as sea). The exile-return motif: God gathered scattered Israel from the whole world (Isa 43:5-6: "I will bring your offspring from the east and gather you from the west; I will say to the north, 'Give them up'"; Deut 30:3-5). The geographical comprehensiveness grounds the prayer for any exile from any direction.
107.4: תָּעוּ בַמִּדְבָּר בִּישִׁימוֹן דָּרֶךְ עִיר מוֹשָׁב לֹא מָצָאוּ (tāʿû bammidbar bîshîmôn dārek ʿîr môshāb lōʾ māṣāʾû, "Some wandered in the desert waste, finding no road to a city where they could live!") — tāʿû: they wandered (the word for going astray — sheep without a shepherd: Isa 53:6: "we all like sheep have gone astray"). Bammidbar bîshîmôn: in the wilderness, in the desert/waste (the doubled synonyms emphasizing the uninhabited, trackless character of the place). Tōhû in Hebrew: desert-waste (the word of Gen 1:2 for the pre-creation formlessness — the wanderers are in a pre-civilizational void where there is no city, no road, no môshāb/habitation). The Exodus wilderness is the paradigm.
107.5: רְעֵבִים גַּם-צְמֵאִים נַפְשָׁם בָּהֶם תִּתְעַטָּף (rĕʿêbîm gam-ṣĕmêʾîm napshām bāhem titʿaṭṭāp, "Hungry and thirsty, their soul grew faint within them!") — rĕʿêbîm gam-ṣĕmêʾîm: hungry and also thirsty — the two fundamental physical needs in the wilderness. Napshām bāhem titʿaṭṭāp: "their soul fainted within them" (ʿāṭap: to be weak, to faint — Ps 61:2; 77:3; Lam 2:11,19: souls fainting in the city streets). The nefesh (soul/life-breath) is reduced to fainting — the vital force nearly extinguished. This physical extremity is the trigger for the cry of v6.
107.6: וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יַצִּילֵם (wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yaṣṣîlêm, "Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he rescued them from their distresses!") — the first occurrence of the cry-response pattern that repeats in all four strophes (vv6/13/19/28). Ṣāʿaq: to cry out (the urgent, loud cry of distress — Exod 2:23: "the Israelites groaned and cried out"; 8:12: Moses cried out). Baṣṣar lāhem: in their tight/narrow place (the distress of being squeezed — Ps 18:6; 102:2). Mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yaṣṣîlêm: from their distresses he rescued them — immediate, specific, effective rescue.
107.7: וַיַּדְרִיכֵם בְּדֶרֶךְ יְשָׁרָה לָלֶכֶת אֶל-עִיר מוֹשָׁב (wayyadrîkhêm bĕderek yĕshārāh lāleket ʾel-ʿîr môshāb, "He guided them on a straight path to a city where they could settle!") — hidrîk (hiphil of dārak: to tread a path — to walk, but here causatively: to cause to walk, to guide). The one who guides the blind on a road they did not know (Isa 42:16). Bĕderek yĕshārāh: on a straight/level path (the opposite of the trackless waste of v4). ʿÎr môshāb: a city of habitation — the specific answer to the specific need of v4 (finding "no road to a city"). The divine guidance creates the very road and destination that was utterly absent.
107.8: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם (yôdû layhwāh ḥasdô wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām, "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his steadfast love and for his wonders done for the children of man!") — the first refrain (repeated verbatim at vv15, 21, 31 — the structural spine of the psalm). Yôdû: let them give thanks (the same root as hôdû in v1). Ḥasdô: his steadfast love (the covenant-faithfulness). Nipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām: his wonders for the sons of Adam/humanity (the acts that exceed human comprehension — Exod 15:11; Ps 77:11). The refrain connects each rescue to the same two divine attributes: the character (ḥesed) and the actions (nipĕlāʾôt).
107.9: כִּי-הִשְׂבִּיעַ נֶפֶשׁ שֹׁקֵקָה וְנֶפֶשׁ רְעֵבָה מִלֵּא-טוֹב (kî-hisbîaʿ nefesh shōqēqāh wĕnefesh rĕʿêbāh millêʾ-ṭôb, "For he gives what the hungry soul longs for and fills the starving soul with good things!") — the explanatory kî-clause expanding the refrain with the specific act. Nefesh shōqēqāh: the longing/thirsting soul (shāqaq: to rush, to long — the intense craving of the dehydrated). Hisbîaʿ (hiphil: to satisfy to the full — the same verb as Ps 17:15: "I shall be satisfied when I awake"; Ps 22:26: the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied). Millêʾ-ṭôb: filled with good things — Luke 1:53 echoes this: "he has filled the hungry with good things and the rich he has sent away empty."
107.10: יֹשְׁבֵי חֹשֶׁךְ וְצַלְמָוֶת אֲסִירֵי עֳנִי וּבַרְזֶל (yōshĕbê ḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet ʾăsîrê ʿŏnî ûbarzel, "Some sat in darkness and the shadow of death, bound in misery and iron chains!") — the second strophe (vv10-16): the prisoners. Ṣalmāwet: shadow of death (the Hebrew compound of ṣēl/shadow and māwet/death — the darkest darkness imaginable, the gloom of the underworld entrance — Job 3:5; 10:21-22; 24:17; Ps 23:4). This word appears in Isa 9:1 ("people who walked in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, light has dawned") — quoted in Matt 4:16 as Jesus's coming to Galilee. ʾĂsîrê ʿŏnî ûbarzel: bound in affliction and iron — the double constraint of suffering and physical chains.
107.11: כִּי-מָרוּ אִמְרֵי אֵל וַעֲצַת עֶלְיוֹן נָאָצוּ (kî-mārû ʾimrê ʾêl waʿăṣat ʿelyôn nāʾāṣû, "for they had defied the commands of God and rejected the plan of the Most High!") — the explanation of the imprisonment: willful rebellion against the divine word. Mārāh: to be contentious, to rebel (Num 20:24; Deut 1:26,43: Israel's rebellion in the wilderness). ʾImrê ʾêl: the words/commands of God. ʿĂṣat ʿelyôn nāʾāṣû: they spurned/despised the counsel of the Most High (nāʾaṣ: to treat with contempt — Num 14:11: "how long will they treat me with contempt?"). The connection between sin and bondage is explicit: the darkness is the consequence of defiance. But the refrain's rescue (v14) happens despite the cause.
107.12: וַיַּכְנַע בֶּעָמָל לִבָּם כָּשְׁלוּ וְאֵין עֹזֵר (wayyakhnаʿ beʿāmāl libbām kāshĕlû wĕʾên ʿōzêr, "So he wore them down with grinding toil; they stumbled and fell, with no one to help!") — kānaʿ (hiphil: to humble, to subjugate — the divine bringing-low as discipline): God employed their own consequence to break their resistance. Beʿāmāl: in/through toil/misery (the labor that is also suffering — Eccl 1:3; 2:10). Kāshĕlû wĕʾên ʿōzêr: they stumbled and there was no helper — the consequence of the defiance of v11 is isolation: the one who rejected divine counsel finds no human counsel available either.
107.13: וַיִּזְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יוֹשִׁיעֵם (wayyizʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yôshîʿêm, "Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he saved them from their distresses!") — the second occurrence of the cry-and-rescue pattern. Note the variation: v6 uses yaṣṣîlêm (he rescued/snatched away) while v13 uses yôshîʿêm (he saved — yāshaʿ: the verb behind yĕshûʿāh/salvation and the name Jesus). The two verbs are synonymous in rescue-language but yāshaʿ carries the additional freight of the Exodus salvation (Exod 14:30: "the LORD saved Israel that day") and the messianic name.
107.14: יוֹצִיאֵם מֵחֹשֶׁךְ וְצַלְמָוֶת וּמוֹסְרוֹתֵיהֶם יְנַתֵּק (yôṣîʾêm mêḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet ûmôsĕrôtêhem yĕnattêq, "He led them out of the darkness and the shadow of death and broke their bonds apart!") — yôṣîʾ (hiphil: to bring out — the Exodus verb: Exod 13:3,9,14,16: "the LORD brought you out"). From ḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet (the darkest dark of v10) into freedom. Yĕnattêq: he snapped/broke apart (nātaq: to tear loose, to sever — Acts 2:24 quotes Ps 16:10 with the same semantic field for the resurrection). The iron chains of v10 are broken by the one who does wonders.
107.15: The second refrain, identical to v8: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם (yôdû layhwāh ḥasdô wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām) — "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his steadfast love and for his wonders done for the children of man!" The refrain's exact repetition after each rescue is the psalm's liturgical genius: the same God who rescued the wanderers from the desert (v4) rescued the prisoners from the darkness. The identical words applied to different situations assert a single unified character behind all the rescues.
107.16: כִּי-שִׁבַּר דַּלְתוֹת נְחֹשֶׁת וּבְרִיחֵי בַרְזֶל גִּדֵּעַ (kî-shibbir daltôt nĕḥōshet ûbĕrîḥê barzel giddêaʿ, "For he has smashed the gates of bronze and snapped the iron bars!") — the kî-clause explaining how the rescue happened. Daltôt nĕḥōshet: the bronze/copper gates (the gates of the ancient dungeon or enemy city — Ps 9:13: "you who lift me from the gates of death"). Isa 45:2 uses this image for Cyrus: "I will break down the gates of bronze and cut through the bars of iron" (the Persian king as the LORD's instrument for releasing captives). The messianic application: the gates of death shattered (Matt 16:18: "the gates of Hades will not prevail").
107.17: אֱוִלִים מִדֶּרֶךְ פִּשְׁעָם וּמֵעֲוֹנֹתֵיהֶם יִתְעַנּוּ (ʾĕwîlîm midderek pishʿām ûmêʿăwōnōtêhem yitʿannû, "Some were fools who made themselves sick by the path they chose, suffering because of their own sins!") — the third strophe (vv17-22): the sick. ʾĔwîlîm: fools (from ʾĕwil: the dense/thick fool who lacks moral sense — Prov 1:7; 10:8; distinct from kĕsîl/fool-by-choice). Midderek pishʿām: because of the path of their transgressions (peshaʿ: the covenant-breaking transgression, the deliberate rebellion — Isa 53:5: "he was wounded for our transgressions"). The sick suffer because of their own choices: the wisdom tradition connecting moral and physical health (though vv1-3's geographical gathering suggests this strophe has a broader application than individual punishment).
107.18: כָּל-אֹכֶל תְּתַעֵב נַפְשָׁם וַיַּגִּיעוּ עַד-שַׁעֲרֵי מָוֶת (kol-ʾōkhel tĕtaʿêb napshām wayyaggîʿû ʿad-shaʿărê māwet, "Their soul could not bear any food, and they were approaching the very gates of death!") — tĕtaʿêb: it loathes, abominates (the absolute revulsion of the severely ill who cannot eat — the same word for the loathing of what is detestable in Lev 11:11,13). Shaʿărê māwet: the gates of death (death conceived as a city or underworld with gates — Ps 9:13; Job 38:17: "have the gates of death been shown to you?"; Isa 38:10: Hezekiah's lament). The gates not yet entered but imminent — the most extreme medical crisis the ancient world knew.
107.19: The third cry-and-rescue: וַיִּזְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יוֹשִׁיעֵם — identical to v13 (same strophe-formula). The repetition of the identical cry in the third strophe with a different affliction (sickness vs imprisonment) reinforces the psalm's universalism: the same cry works across all categories of suffering. The one who sent them into the prison of their folly (v17) is also the one who receives their cry and rescues.
107.20: יִשְׁלַח דְּבָרוֹ וְיִרְפָּאֵם וִימַלֵּט מִשַּׁחִיתוֹתָם (yishlah dĕbārô wĕyirpāʾêm wîmallêṭ mishshaḥîtôtām, "He sent his word and healed them and pulled them back from the pit of destruction!") — yishlah dĕbārô: he sent his word (the active, personified word dispatched as a divine agent — Ps 33:6: "by the word of the LORD the heavens were made"; Isa 55:11: "my word shall not return to me empty"). The Word sent to heal anticipates John 1:1,14 and the healing ministry of the Word made flesh (Matt 8:16: "he cast out the spirits with a word and healed all who were sick"). Shaḥîtôtām: their pits/destructions (the plural of shaḥat: pit, corruption — used for both the physical grave and moral destruction — Ps 16:10: "you will not let your Holy One see corruption"; the resurrection connection).
107.21: The third refrain: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם — repeated verbatim. The fourfold structural use of this refrain is itself a theological act: the same praise-formula covers the rescue of wanderers, prisoners, sick, and sailors. No rescue is too small or too specific for the grand ḥesed; no sufferer falls outside the category of "children of man" for whom the wonders are done.
107.22: וְיִזְבְּחוּ זִבְחֵי תוֹדָה וִיסַפְּרוּ מַעֲשָׂיו בְּרִנָּה (wĕyizbĕḥû zibĕḥê tôdāh wîsappĕrû maʿăśāyw bĕrinnāh, "Let them offer thanksgiving sacrifices and tell of his deeds with songs of joy!") — zibĕḥê tôdāh: thanksgiving sacrifices (Lev 7:12-15 — the specific offering of the tôdāh feast, eaten the same day, shared with family and the poor, accompanied by leavened and unleavened bread). The recovery from sickness calls for the most communal and joyful sacrifice. Bĕrinnāh: with ringing joy (the shout of glad exuberance — Ps 100:2). The rescue is not merely private: it becomes public proclamation (sappĕrû maʿăśāyw: narrate/recount his deeds — the word for the storytelling that preserves the memory of divine acts).
107.23: יוֹרְדֵי הַיָּם בָּאֳנִיּוֹת עֹשֵׂי מְלָאכָה בְּמַיִם רַבִּים (yôrĕdê hayyām bāʾŏniyyôt ʿōśê mĕlākhāh bĕmayim rabbîm, "Some went down to the sea in ships, plying their trade on the wide and open sea!") — the fourth strophe (vv23-32): the sailors. Yōrĕdê hayyām: those who go down to the sea (the idiom for seafaring — "going down" because ships were launched from harbor to the lower sea-surface). ʿŌśê mĕlākhāh: doing their work/trade (the word mĕlākhāh covers both skilled craftsmanship and occupational work — used for the work of the tabernacle builders). Bĕmayim rabbîm: in the great waters (the sea as the mayim rabbîm of Ps 29:3; 93:4 — the cosmic chaos-waters that only YHWH controls).
107.24: הֵמָּה רָאוּ מַעֲשֵׂי יְהוָה וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו בִּמְצוּלָה (hêmmāh rāʾû maʿăśê yĕhwāh wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw bimĕṣûlāh, "They witnessed what the LORD does — his wonders in the depths!") — maʿăśê yĕhwāh: the works of the LORD (the acts of creation and sustaining — Ps 8:3; 19:1; 104:24). Bimĕṣûlāh: in the deep (the abyss of the ocean — Exod 15:5: "they sank into the depths like a stone"; Ps 69:2,15: the deep waters). The sailors are involuntary witnesses to the divine power in the most fearsome natural environment.
107.25: וַיֹּאמֶר וַיַּעֲמֵד רוּחַ סְעָרָה וַתְּרוֹמֵם גַּלָּיו (wayyōʾmer wayyaʿămêd rûaḥ sĕʿārāh wattĕrômêm gallāyw, "He spoke and a storm-wind rose, whipping the sea into waves!") — wayyōʾmer: "and he said/spoke" — the bare divine speech as the cause of the storm (as in Gen 1: God speaks and things happen). Rûaḥ sĕʿārāh: a storm-wind (the same compound in Job 38:1; Ezek 1:4 — the divine storm from which God speaks). Wattĕrômêm gallāyw: and it lifted up its waves (the storm raising the sea into towering waves — Job 38:8-11: "who shut in the sea with doors?"). The storm is directly commanded by God and directly stilled by him in v29 — the episode of Jonah 1 and the disciples' storm (Matt 8:23-27; Mark 4:35-41) are explicitly designed as fulfillments of this pattern.
107.26: יַעֲלוּ שָׁמַיִם יֵרְדוּ תְהוֹמוֹת נַפְשָׁם בְּרָעָה תִתְמוֹגָג (yaʿălû shāmayim yêrĕdû tĕhômôt napshām bĕrāʿāh titmmôgāg, "They heaved up to the sky and plunged back down to the depths; their soul was melting away in terror!") — the graphic description of being tossed in a storm from the sailors' perspective: sky one moment, abyss the next. Tĕhômôt: the deeps/tehom (Gen 1:2 — the primordial deep; Ps 77:16; 104:6). Napshām bĕrāʿāh titmmôgāg: "their soul melted away in their trouble" (māgag: to melt, to dissolve — Josh 2:11: Rahab says "our hearts melted" at Israel's approach). The physical motion (sky/depths) produces psychological dissolution.
107.27: יָחוֹגּוּ וְיָנוּעוּ כַּשִּׁכּוֹר וְכָל-חָכְמָתָם תִּתְבַּלָּע (yāḥôggû wĕyānûʿû kashshikkôr wĕkhol-ḥokhmātām titbāllāʿ, "They lurched and stumbled like drunken men, and all their skill/wisdom was consumed!") — yāḥôggû: to reel, to stagger (from ḥāgag: the circular festival dance; here the uncontrolled staggering of a drunk or seasick man). ḥokhmātām titbāllāʿ: "all their wisdom was swallowed up" (bālaʿ: to swallow — Num 16:30-32: the earth opened and swallowed Korah's company; Exod 7:12: Aaron's staff swallowed the magicians' staffs). The sailors' skill and wisdom — the very things that qualified them to work "in great waters" (v23) — are swallowed by the chaos. Human competence exhausted; the cry (v28) becomes the only remaining option.
107.28: The fourth cry: וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם וּמִמְּצוּקֹתֵיהֶם יוֹצִיאֵם (wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem ûmimmĕṣûqōtêhem yôṣîʾêm) — the fourth occurrence of the cry-rescue pattern, now using yôṣîʾ (he brought out — the Exodus verb of vv13-14). The cry comes after all human competence is exhausted (v27: wisdom swallowed). The pattern is deliberately structured: rescue does not come until all self-reliance is eliminated.
107.29: יָקֵם סְעָרָה לִדְמָמָה וַיֶּחֱשׁוּ גַּלֵּיהֶם (yāqêm sĕʿārāh lîdĕmāmāh wayyeḥĕshû gallêhem, "He calmed the storm to a stillness, and the waves fell silent!") — yāqêm sĕʿārāh lîdĕmāmāh: "he raised the storm to a stillness" — the unusual idiom: the storm is "raised/established" (qûm) into (lĕ) stillness (dĕmāmāh). The same God who said "he spoke and a storm-wind rose" (v25) now speaks the storm into silence. Dĕmāmāh: the stillness, hush (1 Kgs 19:12: the "still small voice" — literally "voice of fine/thin silence" — qôl dĕmāmāh daqqāh — after the storm). Mark 4:39: "he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, 'Peace! Be still!' And the wind ceased and there was a great calm."
107.30: וַיִּשְׂמְחוּ כִי-יִשְׁתֹּקוּ וַיַּנְחֵם אֶל-מְחוֹז חֶפְצָם (wayyiśmĕḥû kî-yishtōqû wayyanhêm ʾel-mĕḥôz ḥepṣām, "They rejoiced as the sea grew calm, and he guided them into the harbor they had longed for!") — mĕḥôz ḥepṣām: the harbor of their longing/desire (a hapax — mĕḥôz appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible; ḥêpeṣ: delight, desire — the harbor they most wanted to reach). The rescue is not merely survival but the attainment of the original goal: the sailors set out to reach a destination (v23: working their trade) and the rescue brings them exactly there. Grace restores the original design.
107.31: The fourth and final refrain: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם — the last of the fourfold liturgical punctuation. Having covered desert wanderers, prisoners, sick, and sailors — the full human range of suffering — the psalm now turns in vv32-43 to a wisdom epilogue that interprets the fourfold pattern cosmically.
107.32: וִירוֹמְמוּהוּ בִּקְהַל-עָם וּבְמוֹשַׁב זְקֵנִים יְהַלְלוּהוּ (wîrômĕmûhû biqhal-ʿām ûbĕmôshab zĕqênîm yĕhallĕlûhû, "Let them lift up his name in the great assembly and honor him in the council of the elders!") — the public dimension of rescue: the worship of the individual rescued is to be proclaimed biqhal-ʿām (in the congregation of the people — the full covenant assembly). Môshab zĕqênîm: the session of the elders (the judicial and leadership council — the equivalent of the city-gate assembly). Rescue generates testimony; testimony generates corporate praise. The fourfold rescue is not complete until it reaches the congregational gathering.
107.33: יָשֵׂם נְהָרוֹת לְמִדְבָּר וּמֹצָאֵי מַיִם לְצִמָּאוֹן (yāśêm nĕhārôt lĕmidbar ûmōṣāʾê mayim lĕṣimmāʾôn, "He turns rivers into desert and springs of water into parched ground!") — the wisdom epilogue begins (vv33-43): the cosmic pattern of divine reversal. God can turn fertility into barrenness. Nĕhārôt lĕmidbar: rivers into wilderness (Isa 50:2: "I rebuke the sea and make it dry"; Ps 66:6). The productive river system (civilization's lifeblood) made into trackless desert. The pattern establishes divine sovereignty over both agricultural fertility and barrenness.
107.34: אֶרֶץ פְּרִי לִמְלֵחָה מֵרָעַת יֹשְׁבֵי בָהּ (ʾereṣ pĕrî limlêḥāh mêrāʿat yōshĕbê bāh, "fertile land into a barren salt flat — for the wickedness of those who live there!") — ʾereṣ pĕrî limlêḥāh: the land of fruit/produce into salt/salt flat. Mĕlêḥāh: saltland (Gen 14:3: the Salt Sea; Deut 29:23: the divine curse makes the land "like Sodom and Gomorrah... sulphur and salt, burned-out"; Zeph 2:9: Moab like Sodom). The connection to Sodom is explicit: moral wickedness produces geographic desolation. This is the positive analogue of the cursed ground (Gen 3:17-19).
107.35: יָשֵׂם מִדְבָּר לַאֲגַם-מַיִם וְאֶרֶץ צִיָּה לְמֹצָאֵי מָיִם (yāśêm midbar laʾăgam-mayim wĕʾereṣ ṣiyyāh lĕmōṣāʾê māyim, "But he can also turn the desert into pools of water and dry ground into flowing springs!") — the reversal of vv33-34: what God can make barren, he can also make fertile. Midbar laʾăgam-mayim: the wilderness into a pool/lake (Isa 41:18: "I will make the wilderness a pool of water and the dry land springs of water"). The same sovereign power that judges wickedness with barrenness also rescues the hungry with fertility (Isa 35:1-7: the messianic transformation of the desert into a garden).
107.36: וַיּוֹשֶׁב שָׁם רְעֵבִים וַיְכוֹנְנוּ עִיר מוֹשָׁב (wayyôsheb shām rĕʿêbîm waykônĕnû ʿîr môshāb, "He brings the hungry to live there, and they build a city to call home!") — the same vocabulary as v4: the wanderers who found "no road to a city where they could live" (ʿîr môshāb) now build one in the transformed wilderness. The first strophe's rescue (vv4-9) is re-enacted at the cosmic/agricultural level: God turns the desert that the wanderers could not cross into the very city they could not find. The divine reversal is not abstract but creates human habitation.
107.37: וַיִּזְרְעוּ שָׂדוֹת וַיִּטְּעוּ כְרָמִים וַיַּעֲשׂוּ פְּרִי תְבוּאָה (wayyizrĕʿû śādôt wayyiṭṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm wayyaʿăśû pĕrî tĕbûʾāh, "They plant fields and vineyards that bring in an abundant crop!") — the agricultural acts that civilization requires: planting fields and vineyards (the grain and fruit that together represent food and drink — Deut 8:8). Pĕrî tĕbûʾāh: fruit of produce/harvest (the abundant yield that exceeds subsistence). The newly settled community creates the agricultural civilization that was impossible in the desert of v4. Creation's mandate (Gen 1:28: "be fruitful and multiply") fulfilled in the transformed wilderness.
107.38: וַיְבָרְכֵם וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד וּבְהֶמְתָּם לֹא יַמְעִיט (waybārĕkhêm wayyirbû mĕʾōd ûbĕhemtām lōʾ yamʿîṭ, "He blesses them and they grow in number, and he keeps their herds from shrinking!") — waybārĕkhêm wayyirbû mĕʾōd: "he blessed them and they multiplied greatly" — the creation blessing of Gen 1:22,28 ("be fruitful and multiply") enacted in historical time. Ûbĕhemtām lōʾ yamʿîṭ: "and their cattle he does not diminish" — the livestock as the measure of agricultural blessing (Gen 13:2; Deut 7:13-14). Divine blessing expressed in demographic and pastoral abundance.
107.39: וַיִּמְעֲטוּ וַיָּשֹׁחוּ מֵעֹצֶר רָעָה וְיָגוֹן (wayyimʿăṭû wayyāshōḥû mêʿōṣer rāʿāh wĕyāgôn, "But when they are cut down and humbled through oppression, calamity, and grief!") — the reversal returns: the same community that multiplied (v38) can be reduced (mĕʿaṭ: to be few/small — Lev 26:22: "your animals will be few"). ʿŌṣer: oppression, constraint; rāʿāh: calamity/evil; yāgôn: grief/sorrow. The three negative forces that collapse the abundance of vv36-38. The historical pattern: the blessing of settlement followed by oppression (Judges cycle).
107.40: שֹׁפֵךְ בּוּז עַל-נְדִיבִים וַיַּתְעֵם בְּתֹהוּ לֹא-דָרֶךְ (shōpêkh bûz ʿal-nĕdîbîm wayyatʿêm bĕtōhû lōʾ-dārek, "he pours contempt on rulers and sends them wandering in pathless deserts!") — bûz: contempt, dishonor (the opposite of kābôd/honor — Prov 12:8; the divine valuation applied inversely). ʿAl-nĕdîbîm: on princes/nobles/generous ones — those who had status and power. Wayyatʿêm bĕtōhû lōʾ-dārek: he makes them wander in pathless wasteland (tōhû: the same formlessness-waste as Gen 1:2 and v4). The leaders who built the city (v36) are returned to the desert by the same divine sovereignty that created the city. Job 12:24 quotes this verse almost verbatim.
107.41: וַיְשַׂגֵּב אֶבְיוֹן מֵעוֹנִי וַיָּשֶׂם כַּצֹּאן מִשְׁפָּחוֹת (wayyĕśaggêb ʾebyôn mêʿônî wayyāśem kaṣṣōʾn mishpāḥôt, "but he raises the poor person up from their misery and makes their family grow like a flock!") — the simultaneous reversal: while the nobles wander in the wasteland (v40), the poor person is elevated. ʾEbyôn: the desperately poor/needy. Śāgab (piel: to set on high, to lift to safety — Ps 59:1; 91:14; Prov 29:25: "whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe"). Kaṣṣōʾn mishpāḥôt: like a flock, families (from the shepherd-flock image of v3's gathering). The divine reversal runs simultaneously: nobles down, poor up. Hannah's song (1 Sam 2:4-8) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:51-53) are the clearest developments of this pattern.
107.42: יִרְאוּ יְשָׁרִים וְיִשְׂמָחוּ וְכָל-עַוְלָה קָפְצָה פִּיהָ (yirʾû yĕshārîm wĕyiśmāḥû wĕkhol-ʿawlāh qāpĕṣāh pîhāh, "The righteous see all this and rejoice, while every mouth of wickedness is stopped!") — yĕshārîm: the upright/straight ones (those whose inner orientation is aligned with the divine character — Ps 33:1; 97:11). Their joy is epistemological: they see and understand what is happening in the divine reversal. ʿAwlāh qāpĕṣāh pîhāh: "wickedness stops its mouth" (qāpaṣ: to close suddenly, to snap shut — the mouth of the wicked has no more to say when the divine vindication is plain). Rom 3:19: "every mouth may be stopped and the whole world held accountable to God."
107.43: מִי-חָכָם וְיִשְׁמָר-אֵלֶּה וְיִתְבּוֹנְנוּ חַסְדֵי יְהוָה (mî-ḥākhām wĕyishmār-ʾêlleh wĕyitbônĕnû ḥasdê yĕhwāh, "Let those who are wise take note of all this and reflect carefully on the steadfast love of the LORD!") — the wisdom epilogue's climactic verse. Mî-ḥākhām: "who is wise?" — the rhetorical summons to wisdom (Hos 14:9: "who is wise? Let them understand these things" — the virtually identical verse at the end of Hosea; Eccl 8:1: "who is like the wise man?"). Wĕyishmār-ʾêlleh: and will keep/observe these things (the word shāmar covers both watching carefully and keeping as commandment). Wĕyitbônĕnû ḥasdê yĕhwāh: and ponder/understand the steadfast loves of the LORD (ḥasdê: the plural of ḥesed — the many expressions of the one covenant character). The whole psalm was a demonstration of the divine ḥasdê in action; wisdom is the capacity to see and understand what the four strophes have displayed.

108.1: Psalm 108 is a deliberate composite: vv1-5 = Ps 57:7-11 and vv6-13 = Ps 60:5-12. The combination is not accidental — it pairs the individual's confident praise (Ps 57, written by David in a cave fleeing Saul) with the national lament over defeat (Ps 60, written after a battle). נָכוֹן לִבִּי אֱלֹהִים אָשִׁירָה וַאֲזַמְּרָה אַף-כְּבוֹדִי (nākhôn libbî ʾĕlōhîm ʾāshîrāh waʾăzammĕrāh ʾap-kĕbôdî, "My heart is ready/established, O God; I will sing and praise you with all my glory!") — nākhôn libbî: my heart is established/firm (kônen: the word for permanent establishment — Ps 57:7: the same opening). ʾAp-kĕbôdî: yes, even my glory (the kābôd — the weightiest part of the person, the soul-identity — Ps 7:5; 30:12: "that my glory may sing to you and not be silent").
108.2: עוּרָה הַנֵּבֶל וְכִנּוֹר אָעִירָה שָּׁחַר (ʿûrāh hannêbel wĕkhinnôr ʾāʿîrāh shaḥar, "Awake, harp and lyre! I will be the one to rouse the dawn!") — ʾāʿîrāh shaḥar: "I will awaken/arouse the dawn" — a remarkable reversal of the normal order where the dawn awakens the sleeper. The worshipper rises before dawn to worship, and by singing awakens the dawn itself. The commitment to praise is so fierce that it precedes the most natural orientation-marker of the day (Ps 57:8: the same phrase). Nêbel wĕkhinnôr: the harp and lyre (the large and small stringed instruments of the temple praise ensemble).
108.3: אוֹדְךָ בָעַמִּים יְהוָה וַאֲזַמֶּרְךָ בַּלְאֻמִּים (ʾôdĕkā bāʿammîm yĕhwāh waʾăzammĕrekā ballĕʾummîm, "I will praise you, O LORD, among all peoples and sing your praises among the nations!") — the universal scope of David's intended praise: not in the congregation alone but bāʿammîm (among the peoples) and ballĕʾummîm (among the nations). Paul cites the parallel verse Ps 57:9 in Rom 15:9: "I will praise you among the Gentiles" — demonstrating that the inclusion of the Gentiles in the messianic age was anticipated in David's praise-intention.
108.4: כִּי-גָדוֹל מֵעַל-שָׁמַיִם חַסְדֶּךָ וְעַד-שְׁחָקִים אֲמִתֶּךָ (kî-gādôl mêʿal-shāmayim ḥasdekā wĕʿad-shĕḥāqîm ʾămittekā, "For your steadfast love towers above the heavens, and your faithfulness reaches to the skies!") — the divine character measured against the largest things available for comparison: ḥesed (steadfast love) exceeds the heavens (mêʿal-shāmayim: above/beyond the sky); ʾĕmet/ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness) reaches to the clouds (shĕḥāqîm: the clouds/upper atmosphere). The covenant attributes are literally unmeasurable — they exceed the largest spatial categories. Ps 57:10 has the same verse.
108.5: רוּמָה עַל-שָׁמַיִם אֱלֹהִים וְעַל כָּל-הָאָרֶץ כְּבוֹדֶךָ (rûmāh ʿal-shāmayim ʾĕlōhîm wĕʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā, "Be lifted high above the heavens, O God! Let your glory fill the whole earth!") — the petition: let the divine kābôd that already exceeds the heavens (v4) be manifest so that the whole earth sees it. Rûmāh: be exalted/raised (the qal imperative — a request to God to make his own exaltation visible). Wĕʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā: and over all the earth your glory. The same prayer as Isa 6:3 (the whole earth is full of his glory) asked to be realized in historical experience.
108.6: לְמַעַן יֵחָלְצוּן יְדִידֶיךָ הוֹשִׁיעָה יְמִינְךָ וַעֲנֵנִי (lĕmaʿan yêḥālĕṣûn yĕdîdêkā hôshîʿāh yĕmînĕkā waʿănênî, "Save your beloved ones — rescue us by your mighty hand and answer our prayer!") — yĕdîdêkā: your beloved/loved ones (yādîd: beloved — the same root as David's alternate name Jedidiah — 2 Sam 12:25; Ps 45:1: the Beloved King; Ps 84:1: the lovely/beloved dwelling). The beloved community cries for rescue. Hôshîʿāh yĕmînĕkā: save with your right hand — the same right hand that will "make enemies a footstool" (Ps 110:1).
108.7: אֱלֹהִים דִּבֶּר בְּקָדְשׁוֹ אֶעְלֹזָה אֲחַלְּקָה שְׁכֶם וְעֵמֶק סֻכּוֹת אֲמַדֵּד (ʾĕlōhîm dibbêr bĕqōdshô ʾeʿlōzāh ʾăḥallĕqāh shĕkhem wĕʿêmeq sukkôt ʾămaddêd, "God has spoken from his holy place: 'I will celebrate; I will apportion Shechem and measure out the valley of Succoth.'") — the divine oracle begins: bĕqōdshô (from his holiness/holy place — the sanctuary or the divine character of holiness from which the oracle comes). ʾĔʿlōzāh: I will exult (the victorious king's triumph shout). Shechem (central Canaan, near Nablus) and Succoth (in Transjordan) are named as the divine king's domains — territories on both sides of the Jordan representing the full extent of the promised land.
108.8: לִי גִלְעָד לִי מְנַשֶּׁה וְאֶפְרַיִם מָעוֹז רֹאשִׁי יְהוּדָה מְחֹקְקִי (lî gilʿād lî mĕnashsheh wĕʾeprayim māʿôz rōʾshî yĕhûdāh mĕḥōqĕqî, "Gilead belongs to me; Manasseh belongs to me; Ephraim is the helmet on my head; Judah is my royal scepter!") — the divine king's territorial sovereignty declared through possession formulae. Māʿôz rōʾshî: the strength/fortress of my head (the helmet — Ephraim as the military power protecting the royal head). Mĕḥōqĕqî: my lawgiver/scepter (Judah as the seat of royal authority — Gen 49:10: "the scepter shall not depart from Judah... until tribute comes to him"). The tribal territories become metaphors for the divine king's own equipment.
108.9: מוֹאָב סִיר רַחְצִי עַל-אֱדוֹם אַשְׁלִיךְ נַעֲלִי עֲלֵי-פְלֶשֶׁת אֶתְרוֹעָע (môʾāb sîr raḥṣî ʿal-ʾĕdôm ʾashlîk naʿălî ʿălê-pĕleshet ʾetroʿāʿ, "Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I toss my sandal; over Philistia I raise a victory shout!") — the three traditional enemies of Israel subjected to symbolic gestures of contempt. Sîr raḥṣî: washbasin/pot for washing (the vessel used to wash feet — a menial, domestic image: Moab the great neighboring kingdom reduced to a servant's foot-washing bowl). ʾAshlîk naʿălî: I throw my sandal (to throw a sandal was either an act of possession-taking — Ruth 4:7-8 — or contemptuous dismissal — "this is only worth the throw of a shoe"). Philistia receives the victory-shout of the conquering warrior.
108.10: מִי יֹבִלֵנִי עִיר מִבְצָר מִי נָחַנִי עַד-אֱדוֹם (mî yôbilênî ʿîr mibṣār mî nāḥanî ʿad-ʾĕdôm, "Who will take me into the strong, walled city? Who will guide me all the way to Edom?") — the transition from divine oracle (vv7-9) back to human prayer: if God has declared these territories his, who will actually lead the human king into them? ʿÎr mibṣār: the fortified city (the inaccessible stronghold — Ps 60:9: the same question). The gap between the divine promise and the human reality creates the space of petition.
108.11: הֲלֹא אַתָּה אֱלֹהִים זְנַחְתָּנוּ וְלֹא-תֵצֵא אֱלֹהִים בְּצִבְאוֹתֵינוּ (hălōʾ ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm zĕnaḥtānû wĕlōʾ-têṣêʾ ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣibʾōtênû, "God, have you not turned your back on us? Why do you no longer march out with our armies?") — zānah: to reject, to spurn (the covenant-abandonment word — Ps 44:9,23; Lam 3:31: "the Lord will not cast off forever"). Lōʾ-têṣêʾ bĕṣibʾōtênû: you do not go out with our armies (God as the divine warrior who marched at the head of Israel's army — Exod 13:21; Judg 4:14; 2 Sam 5:24). The divine oracle of vv7-9 is believed — the sovereignty of God over the nations is confessed — but the present military reality contradicts it. The psalm holds the tension without resolving it.
108.12: הָבָה-לָּנוּ עֶזְרָת מִצָּר וְשָׁוְא תְּשׁוּעַת אָדָם (hābāh-llānû ʿezrāt miṣṣār wĕshāwĕʾ tĕshûʿat ʾādām, "Come to our aid against the enemy — human help amounts to nothing!") — shāwĕʾ tĕshûʿat ʾādām: "vain/emptiness is the salvation of man" (shāwĕʾ: emptiness, vanity — the word for the name taken in vain, Exod 20:7; the vanity of Ecclesiastes is hebel but shāwĕʾ denotes deliberate falseness/uselessness). Ps 60:11: the identical phrase. Human military assistance is not merely insufficient but fundamentally unreliable — the word "vain" suggests something that promises but does not deliver. The petition for divine help is thus grounded in the acknowledged bankruptcy of human alternatives.
108.13: בֵּאלֹהִים נַעֲשֶׂה-חָיִל וְהוּא יָבוּס צָרֵינוּ (bêʾlōhîm naʿăśeh-ḥāyil wĕhûʾ yābûs ṣārênû, "With God we will fight with courage, for he is the one who will crush our enemies underfoot!") — the psalm's final confidence: bêʾlōhîm naʿăśeh-ḥāyil (with/through God we will do valiantly — ḥayil: valor, military strength). Not instead of human action but through human action empowered by God. Yābûs ṣārênû: "he will trample our adversaries" (bûs: to tread underfoot — Ps 44:5; Mic 7:19; Mal 4:3). Ps 60:12: the identical confidence. The composite psalm ends where it must: not in human calculation but in divine victory.

109.1: Psalm 109 is the most extended imprecatory psalm, attributed to David. Its central interpretive question: are vv6-20 (1) the Psalmist's curses against his enemies, or (2) a quotation of what the enemies have said against the Psalmist? The singular in v6 (after the plurals of vv2-5) and the shift back in v20 ("let this be the reward of my accusers") support reading (1), but the extended length and specificity of the curses lend plausibility to (2). אֱלֹהֵי תְהִלָּתִי אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ (ʾĕlōhê tĕhillātî ʾal-teḥĕrash, "O God — the one I praise — do not be silent!") — ʾĕlōhê tĕhillātî: "God of my praise" — the divine identity declared as the subject of the Psalmist's ongoing praise (Ps 22:25: "my praise is from you"; Deut 10:21: "he is your praise"). To address God as "God of my praise" while being attacked by enemies is the psalm's opening paradox: the God who is praised has been silent. ʾAl-teḥĕrash: do not be deaf/silent (Ps 28:1; 83:1: the same plea).
109.2: כִּי פִי רָשָׁע וּפִי-מִרְמָה עָלַי פָּתָחוּ (kî pî rāshāʿ ûpî-mirmāh ʿālay pātāḥû, "For the mouths of the wicked and the treacherous are opened wide against me!") — pî rāshāʿ: the mouth of the wicked (the instrument of the attack — not physical violence but verbal); pî-mirmāh: the mouth of deceit (mirmāh: deception, treachery — Ps 52:2-4). Two mouths opened against one person: the attack is verbal and coordinated. דִּבְּרוּ אִתִּי לְשׁוֹן שָׁקֶר (dibbĕrû ʾittî lĕshôn shāqer, "they have spoken against me with a lying tongue!") — lĕshôn shāqer: a lying tongue (Prov 6:17: one of the seven things YHWH hates). The conflict is fundamentally forensic/verbal: the Psalmist is being accused with false words.
109.3: וְדִבְרֵי שִׂנְאָה סְבָבוּנִי וַיִּלָּחֲמוּנִי חִנָּם (wĕdibrê sinʾāh sĕbābûnî wayyillāḥăṣûnî ḥinnām, "They encircle me with hateful words and fight against me for no reason!") — dibrê sinʾāh sĕbābûnî: words of hatred have surrounded me (the encirclement of hostile speech — Ps 22:12,16: "bulls surround me"). Wayyillāḥăṣûnî ḥinnām: they attack/fight against me without cause (ḥinnām: without reason, freely, for nothing — Ps 35:7,19; 69:4; John 15:25: "they hated me without reason" quotes the ḥinnām of the Psalms). The groundlessness of the hatred makes it more unbearable and more recognizably satanic in character.
109.4: תַּחַת-אַהֲבָתִי יִשְׂטְנוּנִי וַאֲנִי תְפִלָּה (taḥat-ʾahăbātî yiśṭĕnûnî waʾănî tĕpillāh, "In exchange for my love they have become my adversaries, though I am prayer itself!") — taḥat-ʾahăbātî: in place of/in return for my love (the return of hatred for love — Ps 35:12: "they repaid me evil for good"). Yiśṭĕnûnî: they act as my śāṭān — the same root as the legal-court accuser and the cosmic adversary. Waʾănî tĕpillāh: "but I am prayer" — perhaps the most concentrated self-description in the Psalter: the Psalmist does not merely say "I pray"; he says his entire existence is prayer. In response to the verbal attack, prayer is his only posture and his total identity.
109.5: וַיָּשִׂימוּ עָלַי רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה וְשִׂנְאָה תַּחַת אַהֲבָתִי (wayyāśîmû ʿālay rāʿāh taḥat ṭôbāh wĕsinʾāh taḥat ʾahăbātî, "They have paid me back evil for good and hatred in place of my love!") — the double inversion restated with parallelism: evil for good, hatred for love. The chiastic relationship between love (v4) and hatred (vv4-5) is the psalm's moral center: the Psalmist loved, was attacked; the enemies hated, had been loved. This sets up the question of whether the imprecation that follows is justice or excess. Prov 17:13: "if anyone returns evil for good, evil will not depart from their house."
109.6: הַפְקֵד עָלָיו רָשָׁע וְשָׂטָן יַעֲמֹד עַל-יְמִינוֹ (hapqêd ʿālāyw rāshāʿ wĕśāṭān yaʿămōd ʿal-yĕmînô, "Set a wicked man to judge him; let an accuser take his place at his right hand!") — v6 begins the disputed section (vv6-20). The singular "him" after the plural "they" of vv2-5 suggests either a primary ringleader is targeted or the whole section is a quotation of what the enemies said about the Psalmist. Śāṭān: the legal-court accuser (the noun śāṭān in its pre-apocalyptic meaning: a human or divine adversary who makes accusations — Job 1-2; Zech 3:1-2). ʿAl-yĕmînô: at his right hand (the place of the legal advocate — Ps 16:8; 110:5; Ps 109:31 inverts this: God stands at the right hand of the poor). The accuser usurps the advocate's position.
109.7: בְּהִשָּׁפְטוֹ יֵצֵא רָשָׁע וּתְפִלָּתוֹ תִּהְיֶה לַחֲטָאָה (bĕhishshāpĕṭô yêṣêʾ rāshāʿ ûtĕpillātô tihyeh laḥăṭāʾāh, "When he faces judgment, let him be condemned; let even his prayer be turned against him!") — yêṣêʾ rāshāʿ: "may he come out guilty" (the verdict in the divine court: rāshāʿ as the guilty verdict, as opposed to ṣaddîq/righteous). Ûtĕpillātô tihyeh laḥăṭāʾāh: "and let his prayer become sin" — the most terrifying of all the imprecations: not merely the prayers unanswered but the prayers themselves becoming the source of condemnation. Prayer turned into iniquity is what happens when one who has rejected God's counsel continues to pray as if the relationship is intact (Prov 28:9: "if one turns away from hearing the law, even his prayer is an abomination").
109.8: יִהְיוּ-יָמָיו מְעַטִּים פְּקֻדָּתוֹ יִקַּח אַחֵר (yihyû-yāmāyw mĕʿaṭṭîm pĕquddātô yiqqaḥ ʾaḥêr, "Let his life be cut short; let someone else take his position!") — yimĕʿaṭṭîm: let his days be few (the opposite of the blessing of long life — Ps 91:16). Pĕquddātô yiqqaḥ ʾaḥêr: "let another take his office/oversight" (pĕquddāh: appointment, office, oversight — from pāqad: to visit, to appoint). This verse is the one Peter quotes in Acts 1:20 of Judas Iscariot: "his office let another take" — the Greek ἐπισκοπή translates pĕquddāh. The apostolic vacancy is filled by citing the Davidic imprecation as prophetic of the betrayer's end and replacement.
109.9: יִהְיוּ-בָנָיו יְתוֹמִים וְאִשְׁתּוֹ אַלְמָנָה (yihyû-bānāyw yĕtômmîm wĕʾishtô ʾalmānāh, "Let his children be left without a father and his wife become a widow!") — the most socially devastating consequence of a man's death in the ancient world: the widowed wife with no legal protector and the fatherless children exposed to exploitation. The Law specifically protected these groups (Exod 22:22-24; Deut 10:18; 14:29; 27:19) — their vulnerability made them objects of divine concern. The imprecation turns the enemy into the very social category his own wickedness (v16: "he hounded the poor and needy") exploited.
109.10: וְנוֹעַ יָנוּעוּ בָנָיו וְשִׁאֵלוּ וְדָרְשׁוּ מֵחָרְבוֹתֵיהֶם (wĕnôaʿ yānûʿû bānāyw wĕshiʾêlû wĕdārĕshû mêḥoorbôtêhem, "Let his children wander and beg, searching for bread far from their ruined homes!") — nôaʿ yānûʿû: the doubled verb intensifies the wandering (the restless, directionless movement of the homeless — as Cain: Gen 4:12,14: "a fugitive and wanderer"). ḥārbôtêhem: their ruins (the destroyed house from which they must now search for food). The children must beg where the father once had power.
109.11: יְנַקֵּשׁ נוֹשֶׁה לְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ וְיָבֹזּוּ זָרִים יְגִיעוֹ (yĕnaqqêsh nōsheh lĕkhol-ʾăsher-lô wĕyābōzzû zārîm yĕgîʿô, "Let creditors take everything he owns; let foreigners carry off all he has worked for!") — nōsheh: the creditor (from nāshāh: to lend at interest — Deut 15:2; 24:11). The enemy who exploited the poor (v16) now faces the ultimate economic reversal: seized by the creditor, looted by foreigners. Yĕgîʿô: the product of his toil (all the labor-value he accumulated).
109.12: אַל-יְהִי-לוֹ מֹשֵׁךְ חָסֶד וְאַל-יְהִי חוֹנֵן לִיתוֹמָיו (ʾal-yĕhî-lô mōshêk ḥesed wĕʾal-yĕhî ḥônên lîtômmāyw, "Let no one show him loyal kindness or take pity on the children he left behind!") — the social exclusion: mōshêk ḥesed (one who extends steadfast love — māshak: to draw out, to extend; ḥesed: the covenant loyalty that creates obligation to help). The refusal of ḥesed to the one who showed no ḥesed (v16) is the lex talionis of social obligation. ḥônên lîtômmāyw: one who is gracious to his orphans — even the children receive no grace, because the father's wickedness has exhausted the community's social capital toward his household.
109.13: יְהִי-אַחֲרִיתוֹ לְהַכְרִית בְּדוֹר אַחֵר יִמַּח שְׁמָם (yĕhî-ʾaḥărîtô lĕhakhrit bĕdôr ʾaḥêr yimmah shĕmām, "Let his line come to an end; let his family name be wiped out in the next generation!") — ʾaḥărît: the latter end/posterity (the future generation that carries a family's name forward). Lĕhakhrit: to be cut off (kārat: the covenantal cutting-off — excision from Israel's community — Lev 17:10,14; the most severe covenantal sanction). Yimmah shĕmām: their name will be wiped out (māḥāh: to blot out — Exod 17:14: "I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek"; Deut 9:14; 25:19). The ultimate fate the Psalmist of v107:43 calls wise people to observe is here invoked against the enemy.
109.14: יִזָּכֵר עֲוֹן אֲבֹתָיו אֶל-יְהוָה וְחַטַּאת אִמּוֹ אַל-תִּמָּח (yizzākêr ʿăwōn ʾăbōtāyw ʾel-yĕhwāh wĕḥaṭṭat ʾimmô ʾal-timmāḥ, "Let the LORD keep his fathers' guilt on record and never wipe out his mother's sin!") — the covenant principle of generational accountability (Exod 20:5: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me"). The Psalmist asks that the guilty man's family guilt remain on the divine ledger rather than be forgiven. The fathers' ʿāwōn (iniquity) and the mother's ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/sin-offering) — both formal covenant terms — are to remain unblotted.
109.15: יִהְיוּ נֶגֶד-יְהוָה תָּמִיד וְיַכְרֵת מֵאֶרֶץ זִכְרָם (yihyû neged-yĕhwāh tāmîd wĕyakhret mêʾereṣ zikhram, "Let all those sins stand always before the LORD, until he wipes out their very memory from the earth!") — neged-yĕhwāh tāmîd: always before the LORD (the divine ledger never cleared — the guilt before the divine gaze without respite of forgiveness). Wĕyakhret mêʾereṣ zikhram: "so that he may cut off their memory from the earth" — zikrām: their remembrance/memorial (the opposite of the enduring name that the righteous receive — Ps 112:6: "the righteous will be remembered forever"). Memory cut off = the most complete form of judgment.
109.16: יַעַן אֲשֶׁר לֹא זָכַר עֲשׂוֹת חָסֶד וַיִּרְדֹּף אִישׁ-עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן (yaʿan ʾăsher lōʾ zākhar ʿăśôt ḥesed wayyirdōp ʾîsh-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, "For he never thought to show loyal kindness, but hounded the poor, the needy, and the brokenhearted to the point of killing them!") — lōʾ zākhar ʿăśôt ḥesed: he did not remember to do ḥesed (covenant-loyalty-in-action toward the vulnerable — Mic 6:8: "do justice, love kindness"). Wayyirdōp ʾîsh-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn: he pursued/hounded the poor and needy man. Dākaʾ lêb (in the full verse): the brokenhearted (Ps 34:18; 51:17: "a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise"). The irony: he crushed the very people God specifically protects, which is why God specifically crushes him.
109.17: וַיֶּאֱהַב קְלָלָה וַתְּבוֹאֵהוּ וְלֹא-חָפֵץ בִּבְרָכָה וַתִּרְחַק מִמֶּנּוּ (wayyeʾĕhab qĕlālāh wattĕbôʾêhû wĕlōʾ-ḥāpêṣ bibrākhāh wattirḥaq mimmennu, "He loved to curse — let curses come to him! He took no pleasure in blessing — let blessing stay far from him!") — the principle of moral gravity: what you love finds you. ʾĀhab qĕlālāh: he loved cursing (qālal: the root meaning to make light, to treat as contemptible, to curse). Wĕlōʾ-ḥāpêṣ bibrākhāh: he had no delight in blessing (the word ḥāpêṣ — delight — is the same word used for the LORD's delight in his own steadfast love, Ps 5:4; Isa 53:10). His choices have structured the universe of his reception: cursing-seeking finds cursing; blessing-rejecting cannot receive blessing.
109.18: וַיִּלְבַּשׁ קְלָלָה כְּמַדּוֹ וַתָּבֹא כַמַּיִם בְּקִרְבּוֹ וְכַשֶּׁמֶן בְּעַצְמוֹתָיו (wayyilbash qĕlālāh kĕmaddô wattābōʾ kammayim bĕqirbô wĕkashshemen bĕʿaṣmôtāyw, "He wrapped himself in curses like a coat; let that curse seep into his body like water and into his bones like oil!") — the garment-of-curses image is uniquely powerful: cursing is not merely something he did but something he clothed himself with, making it his identity-garment. Kammayim bĕqirbô: like water into his inner being (water permeating the body — unstoppable absorption). Kashshemen bĕʿaṣmôtāyw: like oil into his bones (oil penetrating the deepest structural material — the ancient image of deep absorption). The curse, having been his voluntary garment, now soaks into his physiology.
109.19: תְּהִי-לוֹ כְּבֶגֶד יַעְטֶה וּלְמֵזַח תָּמִיד יַחְגְּרֶהָ (tĕhî-lô kĕbeged yaʿṭeh ûlĕmêzaḥ tāmîd yaḥgĕrehā, "Let it cling to him like the clothing he wears, like a belt strapped on every single day!") — the curse as permanent daily clothing: the garment (beged) pulled on and the belt (mêzaḥ) cinched daily. Tāmîd: always, continually. If he chose cursing as his daily practice and identity, let his identity-garment be the curse itself — inseparable from him as the clothes he wears and the belt that defines his figure. The metaphor of garments recurs in the psalm (v18: the coat of curses; v29: the accusers draped in shame).
109.20: זֹאת פְּעֻלַּת שֹׂטְנַי מֵאֵת יְהוָה וְהַדֹּבְרִים רָע עַל-נַפְשִׁי (zōʾt pĕʿullat śōṭĕnay mêʾêt yĕhwāh wĕhaddōbĕrîm rāʿ ʿal-napshî, "Let the LORD repay my accusers with all of this — those who speak evil against me!") — śōṭĕnay: my adversaries/accusers (śāṭān turned into a verb-noun for the human adversaries — the same root as v6). This verse returns to the perspective of the Psalmist addressing God about his enemies (plural again — as in vv2-5), suggesting that vv6-19 were indeed the Psalmist's own imprecations. Mêʾêt YHWH: from YHWH (the source of the repayment — not human vengeance but divine justice). The Psalmist hands the verdict to God.
109.21: וְאַתָּה יְהוִה אֲדֹנָי עֲשֵׂה-אִתִּי לְמַעַן שְׁמֶךָ כִּי-טוֹב חַסְדְּךָ הַצִּילֵנִי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ʾădōnāy ʿăśêh-ʾittî lĕmaʿan shĕmekā kî-ṭôb ḥasdĕkā haṣṣîlênî, "But you, O Lord GOD, deal with me for the sake of your own name; your steadfast love is good — deliver me!") — the great turn: from imprecation to petition. Wĕʾattāh: "but you" — the pivoting contrast (as in Ps 102:12). The Psalmist does not appeal to his own righteousness (contrast: v20's imprecation rests on the enemies' wickedness) but to the divine character: lĕmaʿan shĕmekā (for the sake of your name — Ps 23:3; 25:11; 31:3; the divine honor at stake in the rescue of the one who bears the divine name). Kî-ṭôb ḥasdĕkā: for your steadfast love is good — the same theology as Ps 107:1.
