109.22: כִּי-עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן אָנֹכִי וְלִבִּי חָלַל בְּקִרְבִּי (kî-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾānōkhî wĕlibbî ḥālal bĕqirbî, "For I am poor and needy, and my heart is deeply wounded within me!") — ʿānî wĕʾebyôn: poor and needy (the classic pair of the socially vulnerable whom the enemy hounded — v16 — and whom God specifically protects — v31). By describing himself as ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, the Psalmist identifies with the very class the enemy victimized, and claims the divine protection specifically promised to them. Ḥālal bĕqirbî: my heart is pierced/wounded within me (ḥālal: to be pierced, to be slain — the word for the mortally wounded — Isa 53:5: "he was pierced for our transgressions").
109.23: כְּצֵל-כִּנְטוֹתוֹ נֶהֱלָכְתִּי נִנְעַרְתִּי כָּאַרְבֶּה (kĕṣêl-kintōṭô nehĕlākhtî ninʿartî kāʾarbeh, "I am fading like an evening shadow, brushed away like a locust!") — kĕṣêl-kintōṭô: like a shadow at its decline (the long evening shadow that fades rapidly — Ps 102:11; 144:4). Ninʿartî kāʾarbeh: shaken off like a locust (the locust swept away by the wind or brushed off a person — Nah 3:17: "like the locust swarm that settles in the hedges on a cold day" and then vanishes when warmed). Both images convey impermanence and the impossibility of holding on.
109.24: בִּרְכַּי כָּשְׁלוּ מִצּוֹם וּבְשָׂרִי כָּחַשׁ מִשָּׁמֶן (birkay kāshĕlû miṣṣôm ûbĕśārî kāḥash mishshāmen, "My knees buckle from fasting; my body has wasted away, lean and gaunt!") — miṣṣôm: from fasting (the fasting of the Psalmist's prayer life — v4: "I am prayer"). The voluntary fast intensifying intercession has physically depleted him. Kāḥash mishshāmen: become lean/wasted without fat (kāḥash: to grow lean, to be gaunt; mishshāmen: from being oily/full — the fat, prosperous body reduced to emaciation). The intercessor's physical state mirrors his spiritual condition: depleted but not destroyed.
109.25: וַאֲנִי הָיִיתִי חֶרְפָּה לָהֶם יִרְאוּנִי יְנִיעוּן רֹאשָׁם (waʾănî hāyîtî ḥerpāh lāhem yirʾûnî yĕnîʿûn rōʾshām, "I am the object of their scorn; when they catch sight of me, they shake their heads!") — ḥerpāh: a reproach, the object of public derision (Ps 22:6: "a reproach of men and despised by the people"). Yirʾûnî yĕnîʿûn rōʾshām: "they see me and wag their heads" — the gesture of contemptuous dismissal (2 Kgs 19:21; Lam 2:15: the passers-by hissing and shaking their heads at Jerusalem's ruins). Ps 22:7 uses the same phrase for the righteous sufferer, and Matt 27:39 uses it for the mockers at the crucifixion — another thread connecting the Davidic sufferer to the Passion.
109.26: עָזְרֵנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי כְחַסְדֶּךָ (ʿāzĕrênî YHWH ʾĕlōhay hôshîʿênî kĕḥasdekā, "Help me, O LORD my God; save me in keeping with your steadfast love!") — the direct, simple petition after the extended description of suffering (vv22-25). ʿĀzar: to help (the fundamental petition for divine assistance — Ps 121:2: "my help comes from the LORD"). Kĕḥasdekā: according to/in keeping with your steadfast love (the measure of the rescue is the measure of the divine character — not proportional to the Psalmist's merit but to the divine ḥesed's immensity).
109.27: וְיֵדְעוּ כִּי-יָדְךָ זֹּאת אַתָּה יְהוָה עֲשִׂיתָהּ (wĕyêdĕʿû kî-yādĕkā zōʾt ʾattāh YHWH ʿăśîtāh, "Let them see that your hand is behind this — that you, LORD, are the one who has acted!") — the doxological purpose of the rescue: the witnesses must know (yādaʿ) that this is God's hand, not a natural reversal or a human achievement. Yādĕkā zōʾt: this is your hand (the divine fingerprint on the event — Exod 14:31: after the sea crossing, Israel saw the great work that the LORD did and feared him). The rescue serves the universal knowledge of God's identity and action.
109.28: יְקַלְלוּ-הֵמָּה וְאַתָּה תְבָרֵךְ קָמוּ וַיֵּבֹשׁוּ וְעַבְדְּךָ יִשְׂמָח (yĕqallĕlû-hêmmāh wĕʾattāh tĕbārêk qāmû wayyêbōshû wĕʿabdĕkā yiśmāḥ, "Let them curse — you will bless. When they rise against me, let them be shamed; but let your servant be glad!") — the great counterpoint: their cursing vs. God's blessing. The Psalmist does not ask for the curses to stop but for divine blessing to overwhelm them. Qāmû wayyêbōshû: when they arise, let them be shamed (the reversal of the attackers' rise). Wĕʿabdĕkā yiśmāḥ: "but your servant will be glad" — the final state: shame for the attackers, joy for the servant. Rom 12:14 echoes the ethic: "bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse."
109.29: יִלְבְּשׁוּ שׂוֹטְנַי כְּלִמָּה וְיַעֲטוּ כַמְעִיל בָּשְׁתָּם (yilbĕshû śōṭĕnay kĕlimmāh wĕyaʿăṭû khameʿîl bāshtām, "Let my accusers be dressed in disgrace and wrapped in their own shame like a mantle!") — the garment-imagery returns (inverting vv18-19): the enemy wrapped himself in curses as a coat; let the enemies be wrapped in shame as a garment. Kĕlimmāh: disgrace, dishonor (the public humiliation of exposure). Kameʿîl bāshtām: their shame as a robe/mantle (meʿîl: the outer garment — the most visible item of clothing). The image is the exact reversal of the royal robe: where honor should be, shame is draped. The psalm's final word on the enemies: clothed in what they deserve.
109.30: אוֹדֶה יְהוָה מְאֹד בְּפִי וּבְתוֹךְ רַבִּים אֲהַלְלֶנּוּ (ʾôdeh YHWH mĕʾōd bĕpî ûbĕtôk rabbîm ʾăhallennû, "With my voice I will give the LORD great thanks; I will praise him among the gathered crowd!") — the psalm moves from petition and imprecation to vow of praise. ʾôdeh YHWH mĕʾōd: I will thank/praise the LORD greatly (mĕʾōd: exceedingly, greatly — the intensity of the thanksgiving matches the intensity of the suffering described). Bĕtôk rabbîm: in the midst of many (the congregation — Ps 22:22,25: "I will tell your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you"). Public praise as the proper response to personal rescue.
109.31: כִּי-יַעֲמֹד לִימִין אֶבְיוֹן לְהוֹשִׁיעַ מִשֹּׁפְטֵי נַפְשׁוֹ (kî-yaʿămōd lîmîn ʾebyôn lĕhôshîaʿ mishshōpĕṭê napshô, "For he stands at the right hand of the poor person, to rescue him from all who would condemn him!") — the closing verse is the great inversion. V6: the enemy placed an accuser at the Psalmist's right hand to condemn him. V31: God stands at the right hand of the ʾebyôn (the poor/needy) to rescue him from those who condemn his soul. The accuser occupies the advocate's position (v6); God occupies the advocate's position (v31). The one who prosecutes is opposed by the divine defender. This verse became the ground for Christian confidence in Christ as advocate (1 John 2:1: "we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous") — standing at the right hand where the accuser tried to stand.

105.1: hodu lYHWH qiru bishmo — three Hiphil imperatives open the psalm: hodu (give thanks/acknowledge, from yadah), qiru (call upon), hodihu (make known). The triad frames the psalm's purpose: communal proclamation to the nations (baammim). Ps 105 is a historical recital of covenant faithfulness; 1 Chr 16:8 quotes vv 1-15 at the ark's installation, rooting this liturgy in Israel's worship.
105.2: sihu bkhol-niplootayw — sihu (reflect/meditate/speak, Qal imperative of siah) ranges from inner meditation to outspoken recitation. The object niplootayw (his wondrous acts, Niphal participle of palah) denotes acts so astonishing they exceed human capacity — the word family includes what is 'too difficult' for man but not for God (Gen 18:14).
105.3: hithallelu bshem qodshow — hithallelu is Hithpael of halal, the root of hallelujah: reflexive boasting in the LORD. The command targets the inward community (ybaqqshe YHWH, those who seek the LORD) rather than outsiders. Joy (yismhu) flows from seeking, not from circumstances.
105.4: dirshu YHWH vuzzow — darash (seek/inquire) and baqqesh (seek/search) are near-synonyms deployed in parallel, intensifying the call. uzzow (his strength/power) stands in apposition to phanayw (his face/presence) — to seek God's face is to seek his active power, not merely information about him. bkhol-et (at all times) removes seasonal limits on the pursuit.
105.5: zikru niplootayw asher asah — the Qal imperative zikru (remember) is not passive nostalgia but active liturgical recall. Three objects follow: niplootayw (wonders), mophetayw (signs/portents), and mishpte-phihu (judgments of his mouth). The third term is striking — God's spoken verdicts are as much his deeds as the physical plagues.
105.6: zera avraham avdow — the addressees are identified through two covenant lineages: zera avraham (seed of Abraham, covenant-descent language from Gen 12-17) and bne yaaqov bchirayw (sons of Jacob, his chosen ones). bachar (election) is the key verb of this psalm: God does not merely acknowledge Israel but actively selects them. eved (servant) for Abraham is an honorific of intimate covenantal relationship.
105.7: hu YHWH elohenu — the emphatic pronoun hu (he, himself) asserts exclusive identity: this same LORD is our God. mishpatayw bkhol-haaretz (his judgments/justice throughout all the earth) — what might seem a private national covenant carries universal jurisdiction. The combination of particular covenant and universal sovereignty is characteristic of Israel's theology.
105.8: zachar briytow leolam — zachar (remember) used of God is covenantal action, not mere cognition: his remembering activates faithfulness (cf. Gen 8:1, Exod 2:24). dabar tziwwah leelep dor (the word he commanded to a thousand generations) — thousand is not a precise count but an idiom of incalculable duration, echoing the fifth commandment's promise (Deut 5:10).
105.9: asher karat et-avraham — karat brit (cut a covenant) preserves the ancient rite of covenant ratification through animal division (Gen 15:9-18). The oath sworn to Isaac (nishba lyitzchaq) adds a second generational anchor, showing the covenant is not a one-time event but a promise renewed across the patriarchal line.
105.10: wayyaamideha lyaaqov lchok — heeemid (caused to stand, Hiphil of amad) conveys establishment, durability. chok (statute/binding decree, from chaqaq, to inscribe) describes the covenant as a carved-in obligation — not a revocable policy. brit olam (everlasting covenant) for Israel echoes the same phrase used for Noah (Gen 9:16) and Abraham (Gen 17:7, 13, 19).
105.11: lcha ettten et-eretz knaaan — the direct speech of God embedded in historical recital. chevel nachalatckem — chevel (rope/measuring line, then the portion measured by it) is the idiom for the allotted territorial inheritance. The surveyor's rope (chevel) divides tribal portions (cf. Josh 17:14; Mic 2:4-5); to receive a chevel is to receive a deeded inheritance.
105.12: bheyotam mte mispar — mte mispar (men of number) is an idiom for a small, countable group — few enough that you could number them (cf. Deut 4:27; Jer 44:28). kmat wgarim bah (almost few and sojourners in it) — ger (sojourner) had no legal land rights, making God's gift of the land all the more striking.
105.13: wayyithhallkhu migoy el-goy — hithhallek (Hithpael of halakh) describes repeated, ongoing movement — the patriarchs were perpetually mobile, never settled. The repetition of the preposition (migoy...el-goy, mimmamlacha...el-am) underscores the precariousness of their situation as stateless wanderers among empires.
105.14: lo-hinniach adam leoshqam — hinniach (Hiphil of nuach) means to permit or allow; God did not allow any man to oppress them. wayyochach alaychem mlakhim — yakhah (Hiphil, to rebuke/reprove) used of God correcting kings on behalf of the powerless patriarchs: Pharaoh (Gen 12:17), Abimelech (Gen 20:3-7). Kings are answerable to the covenant God.
105.15: al-tigeu bimshichai — mashiach (anointed) applied to the patriarchs is exceptional — they hold no formal office, yet God treats them as his consecrated ones. nbiyay (my prophets) identifies Abraham especially (Gen 20:7: 'he is a prophet'). The warning protects those who carry the promise; to harm them is to assault the covenant itself.
105.16: wayyiqra raav al-haaretz — the verb qara (call/summon) applied to the famine is theologically charged: the famine is not a natural disaster but a divine summons. kol-matteh-lechem shabar — matteh-lechem (staff of bread) is the idiom for the food supply (Lev 26:26; Ezek 4:16; 5:16): God breaks the very structural support of nutrition, channeling all provision through Joseph.
105.17: shalach liphneyhem ish — yosef — the syntax is deliberate: shalach (he sent) appears before Joseph's name, reframing the sale by his brothers as divine mission. leved nimkar (who was sold as a slave) — the passive voice (nimkar, Niphal of makar) distances the psalmist from fraternal guilt while naming the historical fact. Joseph's slavery is the vehicle of covenant advance.
105.18: annu bakevel raglow — anah (Piel, to afflict/humble) is the intensive form used for Egypt's later affliction of Israel (Exod 1:12). barzel baah naphshow (iron entered his soul) — a vivid idiom: the iron fetters penetrate not just the body but the person (nephesh). The image suggests that Joseph internalized the affliction — the prison was not merely external confinement but entered his whole being.
105.19: imrat YHWH tzrapphathu — tzaraph (to smelt/refine metals) applied to the divine word: the word of God that Joseph had received tested and purified him across years of waiting. The time of suffering (ad-et bo devaro, until the time his word came) is recast as a metallurgical process — affliction as the furnace that proves genuine faith.
105.20: shalach melek wayyattirehu — natar (Piel, to release/let go) and pattach (Piel, to open/set free) in parallel describe the reversal of Joseph's imprisonment. The structural echo with v17 is intentional: God sent Joseph into slavery (v17); now the king sends to release him. Both sendings are under divine governance.
105.21: samo adon lveytow — adon (lord/master) and mosher (ruler) bracket Joseph's authority. bkhol-qinyanow (all his possessions/acquisitions) — qinyan (from qanah, acquire) is an economic term for accumulated assets. Pharaoh's entire estate is placed under the man who had nothing — the reversal is total.
105.22: lesor sarayw bnaphshow — the irony is pointed: Joseph (asur, bound, v18) now binds (asar) princes at his pleasure (bnaphshow). uzqenayw yechakkem (he taught wisdom to his elders) — chakhem Piel (cause to be wise): the former prisoner educates Egypt's ruling class. The schema of humiliation-then-exaltation that recurs through Scripture is on full display.
105.23: wayyavo yisrael mitzrayim — yisrael here is Jacob the patriarch (the same man as in v6), making explicit that the nation's descent begins with one man's journey. eretz-cham (land of Ham) is the archaic poetic name for Egypt, tracing the Egyptians' ancestry to Noah's son Ham (Gen 10:6). The name appears only in the Psalms (78:51; 105:23, 27; 106:22).
105.24: wayyepher et-amow meod — parah (to be fruitful) echoes the creation mandate (Gen 1:28) and the patriarchal promises (Gen 17:20; 28:3). The language is deliberate: Israel in Egypt is fulfilling the Adamic commission even in exile. wayyaamtzehu mitzarayw (he made them stronger than their adversaries) — the very growth that alarmed Egypt was divinely engineered.
105.25: haphak libbam lisno amow — haphak (to turn/overturn) is the verb of Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction (Gen 19:21, 25). Here God turns hearts toward hatred. The theological claim is stark: Pharaoh's hostility is neither accidental nor autonomous but part of God's larger plan to create the crisis that will demonstrate his power. Cf. Exod 4:21; 9:12 (hardening).
105.26: shalach moshe avdow aharon asher bachar-bow — shalach (he sent) for the third time (Joseph v17; Moses here). The sending pattern shows the psalmist's theology: God's human agents are always sent ones. bachar-bow (whom he chose in him) — bachar (election) applied to Aaron alongside Moses; both prophet and priest are elected instruments.
105.27: samu-bam divrey ototayw — a difficult construction: lit. 'they set among them words of his signs.' The phrase indicates that Moses and Aaron displayed and enacted the signs, or that the signs themselves were communicative acts — words spoken in deed. mophetim beretz cham links to the historical summary in Ps 78:43-51 and the tradition's consistent use of ot + mophet as a fixed pair for Exodus miracles.
105.28: shalach choshech wayyachashik — the order of plagues here is not Exodus chronological: darkness (ninth plague) comes first, framing all the others. welo-maru et-devaro (and they did not rebel against his word) — the subject is Moses and Aaron, not Egypt: they remained obedient throughout. The psalmist commends the messengers alongside the miraculous acts.
105.29: haphak et-meyhem ldam — haphak again (vv25, 29): God who turned hearts toward hatred now turns water to blood. The repetition may be intentional — the same overturning power operates in the moral realm (hearts) and the physical realm (water). wayamet et-dghatam (killed their fish) — fish were a dietary staple in Egypt (Num 11:5), making this a fundamental economic and food security crisis.
105.30: sharats artzam tzphardim — sharats (to swarm, teem) echoes the creation language of Gen 1:20, where the waters swarm with living creatures. The frogs overrunning Egypt mock the creation order: proliferation that should be blessing becomes plague. chadre malchehem (the inner rooms of their kings) — the plague penetrates even the most protected spaces of power.
105.31: amar wayyavo arov — amar (he spoke/said) without any specific speech content: the bare divine word is enough to summon the plague. The minimalism is theological — creation and judgment both proceed by dabar alone. kinnim (gnats/lice) and arov (swarms of insects) represent the relentless multiplication of judgment.
105.32: natan gishmeyhem barad — natan (he gave) inverts the blessing idiom: what God gives is hail instead of rain. Rain is gift; hail is judgment wearing rain's form. esh lehavot beartzam (flashing fire in their land) — fire within hail (Exod 9:23-24) is a paradox of simultaneous opposing elements, signaling that normal nature is suspended under divine judgment.
105.33: wayyak gaphnnam uteenatam — hikah (to strike down) is the same verb later used for the firstborn (v36). The progression of plagues strikes agricultural infrastructure: vines, fig trees, then all trees. wayyshaber etz gevulam (he shattered the trees of their border) — shabar (break/shatter) is the word for breaking idols and crushing enemies; nature itself is shattered by the plague.
105.34: amar wayyavo arbeh — again the bare amar (he spoke and locusts came). Two locust species: arbeh (mature locust, the common word) and yeleq (young locust, a less-developed stage) — together they cover the full life cycle of the locust swarm, leaving nothing unconsumed.
105.35: wayyochal kol-esev beartzam — the totality of consumption is emphasized: kol (all) appears twice. esev (vegetation) and peri admatam (fruit of their soil) are the categories of Ps 104's creation abundance — what God provides in creation, the locust plague devours. The land becomes anti-creation.
105.36: wayyak kol-bchor beartzam — the tenth plague strikes the pinnacle of biological priority. reshit kol-onam (firstfruits of all their vigor) — on (vigor/generative power) appears in Jacob's deathbed poem (Gen 49:3) and Deut 21:17 for the firstborn's special claim. Egypt's firstborn are their generational wealth and social capital — both are struck simultaneously.
105.37: wayyotziem bkeseph vzahav — the Exodus verb par excellence: yatzah (bring out). Israel departs loaded with silver and gold (Exod 12:35-36), the plundering of Egypt that fulfills God's promise to Abraham (Gen 15:14). wein bshvataw kosher (not one stumbling/weak among their tribes) — after 400 years of slave labor, no one lacks the strength to march. A final miracle in itself.
105.38: samach mitzrayim btzeetam — the reversal of v24's dynamic: Egypt that once feared Israel's growth now rejoices at their departure. ki-naphal pachadam aleyhem (for the dread of them had fallen upon them) — pachad (sudden terror/dread) is the same word for the awe that falls on surrounding nations during conquest (Gen 35:5; Exod 15:16). Egypt's relief is a backhanded testimony to Israel's God.
105.39: paras anan lmasach — masach (curtain/screen) is the tabernacle term for the veil (Exod 26:36; 35:12). The cloud as covering uses the same word — wilderness protection is structurally homologous to the tabernacle's inner sanctum. God shelters his people with the same conceptual fabric that shields his glory. wesh lehair layilah — the fire pillar continues the day-night totality of provision.
105.40: shaaal wayyave slav — shahal (to ask/request) recalls Israel's demanding petition (Num 11:4-6, 31-34). God gives the quail but at terrible cost there (Num 11:33) — the psalmist omits the accompanying judgment, focusing on the provision. lechem shamayim yasviem (bread of heaven satisfied them) — the manna is bread of heaven (lechem shamayim, cf. Exod 16:4; John 6:32-33), a category of provision exceeding earthly agriculture.
105.41: patach tzur wayyazuvu mayim — patach (to open) applied to a rock makes the stone porous like a spring. The tradition is attested at Rephidim (Exod 17) and Meribah (Num 20); Paul sees one spiritual rock following Israel (1 Cor 10:4). halchu btziyyot nahar (they flowed in dry places like a river) — the desert is transformed into a riverine landscape: barrenness becomes abundance.
105.42: ki-zachar et-dvar qodsho — the structural keystone: zachar (he remembered) echoes v8 where the same verb established the psalm's premise. The entirety of the Joseph narrative, the plagues, the wilderness provision — all of it flows from a single act: God's covenantal memory. dvar qodsho (his holy word/promise) — the promise to Abraham is holy because it carries God's own character.
105.43: wayyotzi amow bsasson — sasson (exultation/rejoicing) and rinnah (ringing cry of joy, jubilant shout) are among the most festive Hebrew terms. rinnah denotes the kind of shout that bursts out involuntarily — the Exodus departure is not a solemn retreat but an eruption of joy. amow (his people) and bhirayw (his chosen ones) — election produces belonging, and belonging produces exultation.
105.44: wayyitten lahem artzot goyim — the land gift is framed in terms of prior labor: waamal leummim yirashu (they inherited the labor of peoples). Israel's inheritance is not vacant land but developed territory — farms, cities, vineyards already planted (Deut 6:10-11). The gift includes the fruits of others' labor, an unmerited grace underscoring that the land is entirely God's to give.
105.45: baavur yishmru chuqqayw — baavur (so that, in order that) reveals the telos of the entire narrative: all of creation-election-rescue-provision culminates in a people who keep Torah. chuqqayw (his statutes) and toratayw (his instructions) frame covenant obedience as the goal of redemption, not its precondition. The psalm closes with hallelu-Yah — paired with Ps 106's opening and closing doxologies to frame Book IV's great historical meditation.

106.1: halleluyah — hodu lYHWH ki-tov ki leolam chasdo — the liturgical refrain shared with Pss 107:1; 118:1; 136:1 (where it appears 26 times). In Ps 106 it frames a catalog of failures — the refrain's stability is precisely the answer to the instability of the history it introduces. The community praises despite the record of betrayal because YHWH's chesed endures leolam (forever).
106.2: mi-ymallel gevurot YHWH — malal Pi. (to speak out fully, declare) in interrogative form: who is able? The question is rhetorical — no one can fully recount God's mighty acts. yashmi kol-tehillato (make heard all his praise) — the incompleteness of any human doxology is itself a doxological acknowledgment: God exceeds all praise.
106.3: ashre shomre mishpat osse tzedaqah bkhol-et — the ashre formula (blessed/happy) introduces the ethical condition against which the following confession of failure will be measured. bkhol-et (at every time) is the standard the nation chronically fails — the psalm will document how the 'every time' became 'almost never.' The beatitude sets up the tragic irony of vv6-46.
106.4: zkhoreni YHWH birtzon amekha — a sudden shift to first-person singular, unique in this communal psalm. The individual voice lodges a personal petition within the corporate lament. ratzon (favor/pleasure/goodwill) is the positive divine disposition toward the elect: the petitioner asks to be included in that which God already intends for his people.
106.5: lirot btvuvat bhirekhah — bachir (chosen one) continues the election theme from Ps 105. The three purposes (lirot, lsimchah, lhithallel) — to see, to share joy, to boast/praise with the inheritance — show that individual salvation is inseparable from communal participation in God's purposes. To be saved is to be incorporated into the worshiping community.
106.6: chatanu im-avotenu — the three-verb confession: chata (miss the mark/sin), avah (bend/act crookedly, iniquity), rasha (act wickedly) — the moral spectrum from inadvertent failure to deliberate rebellion. im-avotenu (along with/together with our fathers) — the solidarity is not merely historical comparison but genuine corporate identification: we sinned, they sinned, the community is one continuous body.
106.7: lo-hiskilu niphleotekhah — hiskil (Hiphil of sakal, give attention to/understand) — the failure was cognitive as well as volitional. Ps 105 commanded sihu (reflect) on the wonders; the fathers at the sea did not. lo-zakhru (did not remember) — the antithesis of zachar that drove Ps 105. wayyamru al-yam-suph (they rebelled at the Red Sea) — the rebellion occurred at the very site of the miracle.
106.8: wayyoshiem lmaan shmo — the decisive theological pivot: God's motivation is not Israel's merit but his own name/reputation. lmaan shmo (for the sake of his name) is the most common stated reason for divine action toward rebellious Israel (Ezek 20:9, 14, 22; 36:22). The name is God's character made public; to act for his name's sake is to act from who he is, not from who Israel is.
106.9: wayyighar byam-suph — gaar (to rebuke/threaten) is the term for God's command to chaos waters (Ps 104:7; Nah 1:4). At the Red Sea, God treats the sea as a chaos power to be subdued — the Exodus has cosmic dimensions. wayyhalekh bacharvot (he walked through the dry places) — halakh of the sea itself: the water that was obstacle becomes pathway.
106.10: wayyoshiem miyad soneh — sane (hating enemy) and gaal (to redeem as kinsman) in the same verse: the rescue from enemy is framed in family-law terms. goel (kinsman redeemer) acts when a family member is enslaved or dispossessed — God acts for Israel as their nearest relative, with full legal standing.
106.11: wayykhasu-mayim tzareyhem — kasah (to cover/overwhelm) with water: the army that tried to cover the Israelites in slavery is itself covered by the sea. lo-notar meyhem echad (not one of them remained) — the totality of judgment echoes Exod 14:28. The same waters that carried Israel become the tomb of their enemies.
106.12: wayyaaminu bidhvarayw wayyashiru tehillato — the moment of faith and song (Exod 14:31-15:1). aman Hiphil (to trust, believe, rely on) — the Song of the Sea is Israel's high-water mark of faith in the Pentateuch. The tragedy is that vv13ff. show how quickly that faith collapsed. The Song is not the culmination of a stable faith but a peak before rapid descent.
106.13: meheru shakhchu maasoyw — meher (quickly, with haste) attached to shakhah (forget) is a devastating indictment: the forgetting was not gradual but precipitous. lo-chakku leatzato (they did not wait for his counsel) — chakah (to wait patiently, tarry) is the discipline they lacked; the wilderness tested precisely this. Impatience and forgetting are twin roots of rebellion.
106.14: wayyitaou taavah bamidbar — avah intensified as noun-verb pair (wayyitaou taavah) — an internal accusative construction: they craved a craving. The place Kibroth-hattaavah (Num 11:34: graves of craving) memorializes this. nasah el biyyshimon (tested God in the wasteland) — the wilderness as a proving ground cuts both ways: God tests Israel, and Israel improperly tests God.
106.15: wayyitten lahem sheellatam — God grants the demand (sheelah, request). wayyshallach razown bnaphsham — razown (leanness/wasting) is the terrible gift inside the granted wish: the quail comes with a wasting sickness in their beings (nephesh). God's judgment here is a 'yes' — the most fearsome divine response to impatient prayer.
106.16: wayyqanneu lmoshe bamachaneh — qanah (to be jealous/zealous) for the priesthood of Moses and Aaron. The irony runs deep: Phinehas will later demonstrate righteous qinna (zeal) in v30, reversing the selfish jealousy of v16. qedosh YHWH (the holy one of the LORD) — Aaron's title; to envy him is to challenge the divine order of holiness.
106.17: tiptach-eretz watibbela datan — the earth opened (patach) and swallowed (bala): judgment takes the form of the earth consuming those who rebelled against the LORD's appointed leader (Num 16:30-33). The naming of Dathan without Korah follows a tradition also seen in Deut 11:6 — Korah's line survived (Num 26:11) and composed psalms.
106.18: wattivaar-esh beadatam — baar (to burn/blaze) — fire from the LORD consumed 250 men who offered incense (Num 16:35). The edah (congregation/company) destroyed by fire stands as a warning against usurping the priestly office; fire that purifies the altar consumes those who approach it without authorization.
106.19: yaasu-egel bchorev — the Golden Calf (Exod 32) is located at Chorev (Deuteronomic name for Sinai), emphasizing that it occurred at the mountain of the law itself — not in absence of revelation but in the very shadow of it. wayyishtachavu lmassechah (they bowed down to a cast/molten image) — massechah (cast metal image) denotes the manufacturing process: they made what they worshiped.
106.20: wayyamiru et-kevodam btavnit shor ochel esev — mur (to exchange/substitute, commercial barter term) makes the trade explicit: kavod (glory, the divine Presence) was exchanged for a grass-eating ox. The grotesque specificity — a working animal — measures the magnitude of the descent. Jer 2:11 applies this language to all of Israel's idolatry.
106.21: shakhchu el moshiam — moshia (Savior, Hiphil participle of yasha) — the title for Israel's personal divine Deliverer is forgotten within weeks of the Exodus events. The verb shakhah (forget) — the counterpoint to zachar — indicts the heart's capacity for self-erasure of even the most extraordinary experiences.
106.22: niphleot beretz-cham noraot al-yam-suph — niphleot (wonders, Niphal participle of palah) and noraot (fearsome acts, Niphal participle of yare) frame the Red Sea crossing from two angles: astonishing and awe-inducing. These are the precise acts just forgotten in v21 — the forgetting is not of distant abstractions but of recent overwhelming experience.
106.23: lule moshe vchiro amad babedek — bedeq (breach in a fortification wall) appears here and Ezek 22:30; a technical military term for a gap through which the enemy pours. Moses stands in the breach, blocking the flow of divine wrath. lehashiv chamato (to turn his anger back) — Moses's intercession is physically imaged as plugging the gap in a wall under siege.
106.24: wayyimasu beretz chemdah — maas (to despise/reject) is a loaded word: used for rejecting God's kingship (1 Sam 8:7), for rejecting the word (1 Sam 15:23), for God's rejection of Saul (1 Sam 16:1). To despise the eretz chemdah (the desirable/lovely land) — the object of 400 years of promise — is to reject the covenant itself. lo-heeeminu lidhvaro echoes v12's momentary faith: the faith of the Song of the Sea had not endured to the border of Canaan.
106.25: wayyrganu beoholeyhem — ragan (to grumble/murmur, Niphal) carries a particularly negative connotation: secret malicious muttering (Deut 1:27). The private (beoholeyhem, in their tents) nature of the complaint makes it more insidious — a conspiracy of complaint rather than open dialogue. lo shameu bqol YHWH — deafness to the divine voice is the definitive covenantal failure.
106.26: wayyisa yadow lahem — nasa yad (to lift the hand) is the oath-swearing gesture (Gen 14:22; Exod 6:8); here turned against Israel: God swears an oath of judgment (Num 14:28-35). The same posture that seals covenant blessing now seals covenant consequence. The structure is deeply ironic: God's oath-raising hand was first raised to swear blessing; now raised to swear judgment.
106.27: whhaphil zaram bagoyim ulzarotam bearatzot — naphal Hiphil (to fell/cast down) and zarah (to scatter/winnow) — the diaspora language used here anticipates the Babylonian exile by centuries. The wilderness generation's failure is the paradigm for exile; the scattering into the nations is the ultimate covenant sanction (Lev 26:33; Deut 4:27; 28:64).
106.28: wayyitzamdu lbaal peor — tzamad (to bind/yoke/attach, Niphal) is a powerful metaphor: Israel is harnessed to Baal of Peor as an ox is yoked to a plow — unable to move independently. wayyochlu zivche metim (they ate sacrifices of the dead) — metim (dead ones) refers to the dead gods who cannot speak or act; to eat their sacrificial meat is to commune with nothingness.
106.29: wayyakhisu bemalleleyhem — kaas (to vex/provoke to anger, cause grief) — the Baal Peor apostasy actively provokes. wayyiphrats bam hamagepah (a plague burst through among them) — parats (to burst/break through): the plague broke through the defenses — the same idiom as a military breach in a wall.
106.30: wayyaamod phinehas wayplallel — pallal Piel is a rare verb, cognate to mishpat (judgment): Phinehas intervened/executed judgment through the physical act of Num 25:7-8. He stood (amad) where Moses stood in v23's breach — both interceptions stop divine wrath, one by prayer and one by action. wattaatzar hammagepah — atzar (to restrain, shut) — the same verb used for shutting the womb (Gen 20:18) and stopping rain (1 Kgs 17:1): divine judgment is shut by righteous intervention.
106.31: wayyechasher low ltzedaqah ldor wador ad-olam — chashav (to reckon/count/credit) is the Gen 15:6 verb used for Abraham's faith being credited as righteousness. The echo is deliberate: Phinehas's zealous act is credited (chashav) as righteousness in exactly the same vocabulary as the paradigmatic faith of the father of the faithful. James 2's discussion of faith and works lives in the tension between these two uses of chashav.
106.32: wayyaqtzipu al-me merivah — qatzaph (to become angry, provoke wrath) at the waters of Merivah (strife-waters, from riv, contend). wayyera lmoshe baavoram (it went badly for Moses because of them) — the psalmist exonerates Moses: the harm came to him because of them. Moses suffers collateral damage from Israel's rebellion, making his exclusion from Canaan a tragedy rather than a simple personal punishment.
106.33: ki-himru et-rucho wayyevatte bsphathayw — marar (to make bitter, Hiphil himru) is the root of Miriam's name and of Naomi's self-renaming (Ruth 1:20). The people made Moses's spirit bitter. batta (to speak rashly, hapax in this form) describes Moses's fatal speech — the words spoken in frustration at Meribah (Num 20:10-11) that cost him entry to the land. The sin of the tongue is the culminating effect of the people's embittering.
106.34: lo-hishmidu et-haamim asher amar lahem YHWH — failure to execute the cherem (devoted destruction) command. The disobedience is not presented as mercy but as defection — the nations not driven out become the source of the assimilation catalogued in vv35-39. Partial obedience plants the seeds of total corruption.
106.35: wayyitaarvu bagoyim wayyilmdu maaseyhem — arav (to intermingle, mix together) — Israel is blended into the nations like dye into water, losing its distinctive character. lamad (to learn) — the wrong curriculum: they learned the nations' practices (maaaseyhem) rather than teaching the nations YHWH's ways. The direction of influence is completely reversed from the original mandate.
106.36: wayyavdu et-atzabbeyhem wayyihyu lahem lmoqesh — atzav (idol/grievous image, from atzav, to hurt/pain) — an idol is a hurt-maker. moqesh (trap/snare) fulfills the warning of Exod 23:33; Josh 23:13: the gods of the nations left in place would become snares. The trajectory from intermarriage (v35) to idol service (v36) to child sacrifice (vv37-38) follows the logic of unchecked assimilation.
106.37: wayyizbechu et-bnehem veet-bnotehem lashedim — shedim (demons) appears only here and Deut 32:17 in the Hebrew Bible. The Akkadian cognate shedu originally denoted protective spirits; the psalmist uses the term with bitter irony — what purports to protect Israel's children is actually destroying them. Child sacrifice as the terminus of idolatry: what began as worship ends as murder.
106.38: wayyishpkhu dam naqi — shaphak dam (to pour out/shed blood) is the idiom for murder (Gen 9:6; Ezek 22:3-4). naqi (innocent, guiltless) — children bear no guilt. wattechanneph haaretz baddamim (the land was profaned by the bloodshed) — chaneph (to be profane/polluted) describes moral defilement of the land itself; the land bears the bloodguilt and becomes unlivable (Num 35:33-34).
106.39: wayyitmeu bmaaseyhem wayyiznu bmalleleyhem — two defilement verbs: tame Niphal (to become ritually/morally unclean) and zanah (to act as a harlot, spiritual adultery). The Prophets consistently use marital infidelity as the metaphor for idolatry (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16; 23) — the covenant is a marriage, and idolatry is adultery against YHWH. The defilement accumulates until the land expels its inhabitants (Lev 18:24-28).
106.40: wayyichar-aph YHWH baamow wayyetaev et-nachalatow — charah aph (his nose burned) is the standard Hebrew idiom for burning anger: the flared nostrils of rage. taav Hiphil (to cause abhorrence, make abominable) — God comes to find his own inheritance loathsome. The emotional register here is as raw as anywhere in the Psalter: YHWH abhors his own people. The covenant love remains, but the experience of covenant life has become revolting to both parties.
106.41: wayyitnem byad-goyim — the defeat formula: to be given into the hand of enemies is the covenantal curse language of Deut 28:25, Judg 2:14, 6:1. wayyimshlu vahem soneyhem (those who hated them ruled over them) — sane (hate, the same root as v10's persecutors at the Red Sea) — the deliverer from haters has now handed them to haters. The cycle is vicious and deliberate.
106.42: wayyilchatzum oyveyhem wayyikanaou tachat yadam — lachatz (to oppress/crush) is Egypt's verb (Exod 3:9): Israel now suffers the Egyptian experience from its neighbors. kanah Niphal (to be humbled/subdued) — the same root as knaaan (Canaan): they are brought down like Canaanites in the land promised to them. The irony is complete.
106.43: paamim rabboth yatzilem vhemah yamru beatzato wayyamokhu baavonam — paamim rabboth (many times): the Judges cycle compressed into three words. makhakh (to sink down, collapse, be brought low) in the final clause is the terminus of the cycle: sin then servitude then cry then rescue then sin then collapse. baavonam (in/through their iniquity) — iniquity is both the cause and the weight that collapses them.
106.44: wayyar batzar lahem bshomeo et-rinnatam — the turning point: God sees (raah) and hears (shamaa). rinnah here denotes the distress-cry (contrast v43's joyful rinnah at departure from Egypt). The same ringing-shout word that described exultant departure is now the anguished cry of the oppressed. God responds to both registers of rinnah.
106.45: wayyizchor lahem britow wayyinachem krob chasadayw — the psalm's climactic verse. zachar brit (remember the covenant) — the same action that opened Ps 105's recital (v8) now closes Ps 106's lament: God's memory of the covenant is the one stable anchor in the entire turbulent history. nacham Niphal (to be moved with compassion, to relent) — the divine relenting is motivated by krob chasadayw (the abundance/greatness of his steadfast loves) — the plural chasadim denotes the fullness of covenantal faithfulness, the accumulated weight of every promise.
106.46: wayyitten otam lrachamim liphne kol-shovehem — rachamim (compassion, womb-mercy, plural of rechem) — God gives them to the compassion of their captors. The term rachamim, denoting the tenderest form of love (maternal womb-mercy), appears in the most unexpected place: the hearts of enemy captors. God can make oppressors show mercy (cf. Gen 39:21 — same construction for Joseph in prison). The exile is softened from within by the same rachamim that drives YHWH himself.
106.47: hoshienu YHWH elohenu wqabtzenu min-hagoyim — the psalm's climactic prayer: hoshia (save) and qabatz (gather/assemble) — the two great exile-reversal verbs (cf. Deut 30:3-4; Isa 43:5; 54:7). The purpose stated: lhodot lshem qodshekhah (to give thanks to your holy name) — the goal of ingathering is not national restoration for its own sake but restored worship. The same hodu that opened Ps 105 (v1) is the telos of the prayer here: the cycle of praise continues.
106.48: barukh YHWH elohey yisrael min-haolam vad haolam — the Book IV doxology, closing Pss 90-106. vaamar kol-haam amen — the congregational response amen (truly, so be it — from aman, the same root as faith/trust in v12 and Gen 15:6) is the community's ratification of all that has been recounted. The paired Pss 105-106 present the single covenant story from both sides — divine faithfulness and human failure — and the doxology pronounces them together as ground for blessing. halleluyah closes both psalms as the only adequate response to the whole.

118.1: hodu lYHWH ki-tov ki leolam chasdo — the great hallel refrain (shared verbatim with Ps 106:1, 107:1, 136:1 passim) opens and closes the psalm (v29 = v1), creating a liturgical inclusio. Ps 118 is the climactic psalm of the Egyptian Hallel (Pss 113-118) sung at Passover; Jesus and the disciples sang it after the Last Supper (Matt 26:30; Mark 14:26). The refrain is not mere formula — ki-tov ki leolam chasdo asserts that YHWH's chesed (covenantal faithfulness) is the permanent structural feature of reality.
118.2: yomar-na yisrael ki leolam chasdo — the first of three liturgical circles: Israel the nation. yomar-na (let ... say, jussive + entreaty particle na) — a call-and-response format, with the congregation repeating the refrain. The na particle makes it an urgent invitation, not merely a command.
118.3: yomru-na veyt-aharon ki leolam chasdo — the second circle: the priestly house of Aaron, the liturgical specialists whose role is precisely to proclaim the divine name. That they too confess leolam chasdo shows the refrain is not casual piety but cultic confession at the highest level.
118.4: yomru-na yirey YHWH ki leolam chasdo — the third and widest circle: yirey YHWH (those who fear the LORD) — a category that extends beyond ethnic Israel to include God-fearers and proselytes (cf. Ps 115:11; 135:20). The concentric structure (Israel → Aaron → fearers) moves from the ethnically particular to the religiously universal.
118.5: min-hammetzer qarati Yah anani vammerchav Yah — the thematic hinge of the psalm's personal testimony. metzer (narrow straits/distress, from tzarar, to squeeze/bind) is the physical sensation of being compressed by crisis. merchav (broad/spacious place, from rachav, wide) is the experiential opposite — rescue is felt as release from constriction into expanse. The contraction Yah (short form of YHWH) appears twice, emphasizing divine directness. The pattern metzer → merchav is repeated across the Psalter as the primary rescue schema.
118.6: YHWH li lo ira mah-yaaseh li adam — YHWH li (the LORD is for/on my side) — the preposition le expresses personal advocacy: YHWH takes the speaker's side. lo ira (I will not fear) — the negative jussive of yare. mah-yaaseh li adam (what can a person do to me?) — the rhetorical question that grounds fearlessness in divine advocacy. Heb 13:6 cites this with the added phrase 'the Lord is my helper' — drawing on v7 to complete the thought.
118.7: YHWH li bozray vaani ereh bsoneay — bozray (among my helpers, Qal participle plural of azar + preposition be + 1s suffix) — YHWH is classified among those who help, but as the decisive one. vaani ereh bsoneay (and I will look/see regarding those who hate me) — raah used for watching the downfall of enemies: the psalmist will outlast them to see their ruin. Cf. Ps 54:9; 59:10.
118.8: tov lachsot bYHWH mivtitoach baadam — chasah (to take refuge/shelter, seek protection) is the active seeking of a defender; batach (to trust/rely on) is passive reliance. The distinction is subtle but real: shelter is obtained, trust is extended. adam (mortal person, the common human) is the comparison — not sinful necessarily, but inherently limited. The proverb form (tov...min, better...than) is wisdom genre.
118.9: tov lachsot bYHWH mivtitoach bindivim — the parallel proverb escalates from adam (ordinary person) to ndivim (nobles/princes/generous ones) — those with actual power and resources. If trusting ordinary humans is foolish, trusting powerful humans is doubly so. ndivim shares the root of ndavah (freewill offering) — generosity and nobility are related concepts in Hebrew thought.
118.10: kol-goyim svavuni bshem YHWH ki amilam — svavuni (they surrounded me, Qal perfect of savav) — the encirclement of enemies. bshem YHWH (in the name of the LORD) — not by military strength but by invoking the divine name. amilam (I will cut them off, Hiphil of mul) — the same verb used for circumcision: to cut off the foreskin, then extended to cutting off enemies. The triple repetition of bshem YHWH ki amilam across vv10-12 creates a liturgical drumbeat of divine name-power.
118.11: sabbuni gam-svavuni bshem YHWH ki amilam — the two synonyms sabbuni and svavuni (both from savav, to surround) with gam (also/even) emphasize the completeness of encirclement — there was no escape route. The repetition of bshem YHWH ki amilam (second of three) builds the psalm's drumbeat to its climax in v12.
118.12: svavuni kadvurim doachu kesh qotzim bshem YHWH ki amilam — two similes for the enemies: kadvurim (like bees) — a swarm, numerous and stinging; doachu kesh qotzim (they blazed out as fire of thorns) — dry-brush fire burns intensely but briefly. The enemies are numerous and fierce but transient. The third and final repetition of bshem YHWH ki amilam closes the strophe: the name has triumphed where numbers and ferocity could not.
118.13: dachoh dchitani linpol vaYHWH azarani — the infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (dachoh dchitani) emphasizes the violence of the push: 'pushed hard to fall.' The subject of dchitani is unstated — enemies collectively, or personified Death. vaYHWH azarani — the adversative vav (but/and then) marks the divine reversal at the crisis point. The structure perfectly captures the moment: maximum pressure, then divine rescue.
118.14: ozi vzimrat Yah vayhi-li liyshvuah — verbatim from Exod 15:2 (the Song of the Sea) and Isa 12:2. The deliberate quotation is a theological claim: this individual's rescue recapitulates the paradigmatic national rescue. oz (strength/might), zimrah (song/praise) — the two responses to salvation are strength and song. Yah (the contracted divine name) appears twice in the verse, a poetic doubling of divine identity.
118.15: qol rinnah viyshvuah baoholey tzaddiqim ymin YHWH ossah chayil — rinnah (ringing shout of joy, cf. Ps 106:44) — the shout that bursts out involuntarily from overwhelming relief. baoholey tzaddiqim (in the tents of the righteous) — the wilderness community imagery: the righteous are those in covenant relationship, living in tents as Israel did in the desert. ymin YHWH ossah chayil (the right hand of the LORD does valiantly/mightily) — yamin (right hand) is the hand of oath, covenant, and power; chayil (valor, strength, army) is the warrior-God idiom.
118.16: ymin YHWH romamah ymin YHWH ossah chayil — the right-hand theme expands into a strophe: romamah (is exalted/raised high) — God's right hand is elevated in the posture of victory and blessing (cf. Exod 15:6, 12). The repetition (ymin YHWH three times across vv15-16) is liturgical emphasis; in temple worship, the right hand was the side of divine blessing.
118.17: lo amut ki echyeh vasapper maasey Yah — the bold defiance of death as outcome. lo amut ki echyeh (I will not die but I will live) — the contrast between mut (death) and chayah (life) is stark. The purpose of survival: asapper maasey Yah (I will recount the deeds of Yah) — sapar (to count/recount, declare narratively) — witness-bearing as the reason for rescue. Life is given so that God's acts can be proclaimed. This verse underlies the NT logic of resurrection-as-witness (Acts 2:24-36).
118.18: yassor yissrani Yah velammavet lo ntanani — the infinitive absolute + finite verb (yassor yissrani) emphasizes the severity of the discipline: 'the LORD has indeed disciplined me severely.' yasar (to discipline/chastise, the parental correction word) — same root as Prov 3:11-12 (cited Heb 12:5-11). velammavet lo ntanani (but to death he has not given me) — the limit on divine discipline is death: even maximum chastening stops short of permanent destruction. The verse is the theological balance between v17's defiance and v13's near-fall.
118.19: pitchu-li shaare-tzedeq avo-vam odeh Yah — the processional gate liturgy begins. pitchu-li (open for me, imperative plural to the gatekeepers). shaare-tzedeq (gates of righteousness) — either the gates through which righteous persons enter, or the gates that righteousness itself constitutes. avo-vam odeh Yah (I will enter them and give thanks to Yah) — entering the temple gates is the act of thanksgiving; the liturgical space makes the gratitude concrete.
118.20: zeh-hashaar laYHWH tzaddiqim yavou vo — the gatekeeper's response: zeh-hashaar (this is the gate) — the demonstrative makes it specific. laYHWH (belonging to/of the LORD) — the gate is his, not the institution's. tzaddiqim yavou vo (the righteous shall enter through it) — the gate defines and identifies the righteous: those who enter this way are thereby shown to be tzaddiqim. The gate is also the teaching: who enters here and how they enter shows what righteousness is.
118.21: odekha ki anitani vatehi-li liyshvuah — the individual thanksgiving that follows gate entry. anitani (you answered me, Qal perfect of anah) — divine response to the metzer-cry of v5. vatehi-li liyshvuah (and you became for me salvation) — the salvation formula used across the Psalter: YHWH is not just a helper but becomes salvation itself for the speaker.
118.22: even maasu habonim haytah lerosh pinnah — the most NT-quoted OT verse. even (stone); maasu (they rejected/despised, Qal perfect of maas — the same rejection-word for Saul and idols); habonim (the builders, Qal participle plural of banah). lerosh pinnah (for the head of the corner) — the cornerstone or capstone, the structurally indispensable stone placed either at the base (load-bearing) or at the apex (keystone of an arch). The paradox: what professional builders discarded becomes the architecturally decisive element. NT citations: Matt 21:42; Mark 12:10; Luke 20:17; Acts 4:11; 1 Pet 2:7; cf. Eph 2:20. Jesus himself applies this to his rejection and resurrection (Matt 21:42). The 'builders' who rejected him are the religious leaders; the exaltation of the cornerstone is the resurrection.
118.23: meat YHWH haytah zot hi niphlet beeyneynu — meat YHWH (from the LORD this was) — divine agency behind the reversal: the builders' rejection is not the end of the story; YHWH's act makes the rejected stone the cornerstone. niphlet (it is marvelous/wonderful, Niphal of palah) — the same root as niphleot throughout the historical psalms: an act so extraordinary it exceeds ordinary human expectations. beeyneynu (in our eyes) — the reversal is visible and publicly acknowledged.
118.24: zeh-hayyom asah YHWH nagilah venismekhah vo — hayyom with the definite article: 'this the day' — not a generic day but the specific day of YHWH's redemptive act. Liturgically, every Passover enacts 'this day,' and Christians apply it to every Lord's Day (the day of resurrection). nagilah (let us spin/leap for joy, cohortative of gil) and venismekhah (and let us be glad, cohortative of samach) — two cohortative invitations to full-body joyful response. gil is the more intense physical word (leaping, spinning); samach is the settled gladness.
118.25: ana YHWH hoshiah-na ana YHWH hatzlichah-na — ana (O LORD, a particle of earnest appeal). hoshiah-na contracted in Aramaic tradition to 'hosanna' — the shout of the crowds at the Triumphal Entry (Matt 21:9; Mark 11:9-10; Luke 19:38; John 12:13). The na particle (entreaty, urgency) transforms the imperative into a plea: 'please save.' hatzlichah-na (grant success/prosper, please) — tzalach (to succeed, prosper, advance) — the second petition shows rescue and flourishing are expected together. Historically a plea; in the Triumphal Entry narrative, a messianic acclamation.
118.26: baruch haba bshem YHWH berakhanukem mivet YHWH — the priestly blessing from within the temple: baruch haba bshem YHWH (blessed is the one who comes in the name of the LORD) — the crowd's greeting at the Triumphal Entry (Matt 21:9; 23:39; Luke 19:38; John 12:13). bshem YHWH (in the name of the LORD) — the representative, authorized agent who acts under divine commission. berakhanukem mivet YHWH (we bless you from the house of the LORD) — the priests inside the temple bless the approaching worshiper. Jesus quotes this psalm back at Jerusalem in Matt 23:39 as a future eschatological condition: 'you will not see me again until you say...'
118.27: el YHWH wayyaer lanu isru-chag baavotim ad-qarnot hamizbeach — el YHWH wayyaer lanu (God is the LORD and he has made his light shine on us) — an allusion to the Aaronic benediction (Num 6:25: 'the LORD make his face shine on you'). isru-chag (bind the festival/sacrificial animal) — asar (bind, the same root as in Joseph's binding, v18 of Ps 105). baavotim (with cords/thick ropes) — avot (twisted cords). ad-qarnot hamizbeach (to the horns of the altar) — the horns were the points at the four corners of the altar where blood was applied (Exod 29:12; Lev 4:7). The binding of the sacrifice to the altar horns resonates typologically with the Akedah (Gen 22, where Abraham bound Isaac) and with the cross.
118.28: eli attah veodekha elohi aromimekha — the personal climax of individual testimony. The double eli (my God) with two different response-verbs: odekha (I will thank/praise you) and aromimekha (I will exalt you, Polel of rum, to lift high). The affirmation 'you are my God' is the covenant formula at its most personal — not an abstract theological claim but a relational declaration. The Polel of rum (cause to be high, exalt) is an intensive form: the speaker actively lifts God high through praise.
118.29: hodu lYHWH ki-tov ki leolam chasdo — verbatim repetition of v1: the liturgical inclusio that frames the entire psalm. The closing refrain is not anticlimactic but retrospective: everything between v1 and v29 — the encirclement by nations, the near-fall, the rescue, the rejected-stone-becomes-cornerstone reversal, the hosanna, the processional entry — is now understood as evidence that ki-tov ki leolam chasdo (he is good; his steadfast love endures forever). The personal testimony and the cosmic claim are identical.

111.1:
hallĕlûyāh — the psalm opens with the liturgical interjection that will frame the entire Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113–118). The term is a third-person plural imperative fused with the divine name: hallĕlû (praise, pl.) + yāh (the LORD). It stands outside the acrostic structure proper, which begins with ʾôdeh yhwh.

ʾôdeh yhwh bĕkhol-lēbāb — ʾôdeh is Hiphil of yādāh (to cast, throw), which came to mean "acknowledge" then "thank/praise." The phrase bĕkhol-lēbāb (with the whole heart) sets the register: total personal engagement, not rote liturgy. Ps 111 and 112 are twin acrostic poems — each of the 22 half-lines begins with successive Hebrew letters (aleph through taw).
111.2: gĕdōlîm maʿăśê yhwh — the acrostic continues: gimel opens this half-line. dĕrûšîm lĕkhol-ḥepṣêhem — dĕrûšîm is a passive participle of dāraš (to seek, study, inquire) — the works of the LORD are "being studied/sought out" by those who delight in them. This is not passive contemplation but active investigation; the same verb is used of studying Torah (Ezra 7:10).
111.3: hōd-wĕhādār pĕʿullātô — hōd (splendor, majesty) and hādār (honor, glory, adornment) are often paired for the royal appearance of kings; here applied to YHWH's deeds. wĕṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — his righteousness "stands forever" — the participle ʿōmedet conveys ongoing, present standing. This phrase recurs word-for-word in Ps 112:3 and 112:9 applied to the righteous person — the mirroring is deliberate.
111.4: zēkher ʿāśāh lĕniplĕʾōtāyw — "he made a memorial/remembrance for his wonderful works." zēkher (memorial, remembrance) is the word behind commemorative ritual — Passover, the feasts, and later Eucharist all operate as zēkher. ḥannûn wĕraḥûm yhwh — the same paired attributes as Exod 34:6, the foundational divine self-disclosure at Sinai. Ps 111 takes these attributes and distributes them through the psalm; Ps 112 applies them to the righteous person.
111.5: ṭeref nātan lîrēʾāyw — ṭeref literally means torn prey — what a wild animal has seized and torn. Used here for the provision God gives to those who fear him; the raw word choice suggests generous, abundant feeding, not measured rations. yizkōr lĕʿōlām bĕrîtô — "he will be mindful forever of his covenant" — zākar (remember) in the OT is never merely cognitive; divine remembering means divine action on behalf of the covenant.
111.6: kōaḥ maʿăśāyw higgîd lĕʿammô — "the power of his works he has declared to his people" — higgîd (Hiphil of nāgad, to make clear, declare) implies formal proclamation; the Exodus is the paradigm. lātēt lāhem naḥălat gôyim — to give them the nations' inheritance/possession. naḥălāh (inheritance, possession) carries the kinship/family estate meaning — the land given to Israel is a family grant from the divine father, not merely a military conquest.
111.7: maʿăśê yādāyw ʾĕmet ûmišpāṭ — "the works of his hands are faithfulness (ʾĕmet) and justice (mišpāṭ)" — the two together form a hendiadys for covenant order. ʾĕmet = truth/faithfulness (relational reliability); mišpāṭ = right judgment/justice (legal reliability). neʾĕmānîm kol-piqûdāyw — "all his precepts are trustworthy" — neʾĕmānîm = Niphal participle of ʾāman (to be firm, reliable) — the cognate of ʾāmēn.
111.8: sĕmûkhîm lāʿad lĕʿôlām — "upheld/supported for ever and ever" — sāmak means to lean on, support, prop up; the precepts of YHWH are themselves load-bearing structures. ʿăśûyim bĕʾĕmet wĕyāšār — "done/made in faithfulness and uprightness" (yāšār = straight, right, upright).
111.9: pĕdût šālaḥ lĕʿammô — "redemption (pĕdût) he sent to his people" — pĕdût is related to pādāh (to ransom, redeem by payment), distinct from gĕʾullāh (kinship redemption). The verb šālaḥ (send, commission) gives the redemption a missional character — it was dispatched. ṣiwwāh-lĕʿōlām bĕrîtô — "he commanded his covenant forever" — ṣiwwāh (command) used with the covenant is striking: the covenant is not merely offered but decreed. qādôš wĕnôrāʾ šĕmô — "holy and awesome/fearful is his name" — the psalm's climax before the concluding wisdom formula.
111.10: rēʾshît ḥokhmāh yirʾat yhwh — "the beginning/chief part of wisdom is the fear of the LORD." rēʾshît can mean either "beginning" (first step, entry point) or "the best/foremost part" (as in firstfruits, Gen 1:1). Both meanings apply: fearing the LORD is where wisdom starts and also its highest expression. This clause appears nearly verbatim in Prov 9:10 and (slightly varied) in Prov 1:7, marking Ps 111 as part of the wisdom tradition. The acrostic ends with taw in the final word tĕhillātô (his praise): the whole alphabet praises him.

112.1: hallĕlûyāh ʾašrê-ʾîš yārēʾ ʾet-yhwh — Ps 112 opens with the same hallĕlûyāh as Ps 111, then immediately echoes Ps 111's closing formula: "blessed is the man who fears the LORD" mirrors "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (111:10). The two psalms are a diptych: Ps 111 describes YHWH's character; Ps 112 maps those same attributes onto the righteous person who fears him. bĕmiṣwōtāyw ḥāpēṣ mĕʾōd — "who greatly delights in his commandments" — ḥāpēṣ (to take pleasure in) is the same word as Ps 111:2 ("those who find pleasure in them"). The acrostic begins anew with aleph.
112.2: gibbôr bāʾāreṣ yihyeh zarʿô — "mighty/powerful in the land will be his seed" — gibbôr is the word for warrior, hero (used of Boaz in Ruth 2:1). Here it denotes the social standing and influence the righteous man's descendants will carry. dôr yĕšārîm yĕbōrāk — "the generation of the upright will be blessed" — yĕšārîm (the upright) = those aligned with covenant order.
112.3: hôn-wāʿōšer bĕbêtô — "wealth and riches are in his house" — the wisdom tradition presents material blessing as a regular outcome of covenant faithfulness, without denying that the righteous sometimes suffer. wĕṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — "and his righteousness stands forever" — verbatim repetition from Ps 111:3 (there said of YHWH's work). The transfer is deliberate: the righteous person shares in a righteousness with the permanence of YHWH's own.
112.4: zāraḥ baḥōšek ʾôr layyĕšārîm — "there rises/shines in the darkness a light for the upright" — zāraḥ is the verb for sunrise; it images a dawn breaking on those sitting in darkness. ḥannûn wĕraḥûm wĕṣaddîq — in Ps 111:4 these three attributes (gracious, compassionate, righteous) describe YHWH; here they describe the righteous person. The structural mirroring is the psalm's point: the one who fears YHWH takes on the divine character.
112.5: ṭôb-ʾîš ḥônēn ûmalweh — "it goes well with a man who shows favor and lends" — ḥānan (to show favor, be gracious) in the Qal is the human equivalent of what YHWH does in divine grace. The wisdom tradition consistently praises generosity: Ps 37:26 says "he is always generous and lends." yĕkhalkēl dĕbārāyw bĕmišpāṭ — "he manages his affairs with justice/right judgment" — the righteous person's practical dealings are ordered by mišpāṭ.
112.6: kî-lĕʿôlām lōʾ yimmôṭ — "for to eternity he will not be shaken/moved" — māṭaṭ (to waver, totter) is the same verb used of cosmic shaking (Ps 46:6); the righteous person has the stability that belongs to the foundations of the world. lĕzēkher ʿôlām yihyeh ṣaddîq — "in everlasting remembrance will be the righteous" — to be remembered is to have one's deeds still operating; to be forgotten is a form of death.
112.7: miššĕmûʿāh rāʿāh lōʾ yîrāʾ — "of evil report he will not be afraid" — the righteous person is not destabilized by catastrophic news because his foundation is not circumstantial. nākôn libbô bāṭûaḥ bayhwāh — "his heart is established/firm, trusting in the LORD" — nākôn (Niphal of kûn, to be established, fixed) describes a settled, prepared heart. bāṭûaḥ (trusting, secure) is the disposition that makes fearlessness possible.
112.8: sāmûk libbô lōʾ yîrāʾ — "his heart is supported/braced; he will not fear" — sāmak (to lean on, support, prop) — from the same root as Ps 111:8. The heart propped up by God is the heart that will not fear. ʿad ʾăšer yirʾeh bĕṣārāyw — "until he sees (what happens to) his enemies" — the righteous person will outlast those arrayed against him.
112.9: pizzar nātan lāʾebyônîm — "he scattered, he gave to the poor" — pizzar (Piel of pāzar) means to scatter, disperse, spread abroad — a vivid image of open-handed, non-calculated giving. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Cor 9:9 when arguing for generous Christian giving: "He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever." Paul applies this OT description of the righteous person to the generous believer. ṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — the third occurrence of this clause across the two psalms (111:3, 112:3, 112:9), now said of the generous giver.
112.10: rāšāʿ yirʾeh wĕkāʿas — "the wicked will see and be vexed/angry" — the final letter of the acrostic (taw) is in taʾăwat rĕšāʿîm (the desire of the wicked). šinnāyw yaḥărōq wĕnāmās — "he will gnash his teeth and melt away" — ḥārak (to grind teeth, used of hostility in Ps 37:12) and māsas (to melt, dissolve). taʾăwat rĕšāʿîm tōʾbēd — "the desire of the wicked will perish" — the closing antithesis to v6's "everlasting remembrance": the wicked leave no lasting trace.

113.1: hallĕlû ʿabdê yhwh hallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh — the triple repetition of hallĕlû opens Ps 113 with liturgical intensity; this is the first of the Egyptian Hallel psalms (113–118) sung at Passover. The address is ʿabdê yhwh (servants of the LORD) — corporate Israel understood as YHWH's household. šēm yhwh (the name of the LORD) — to praise the name is to praise the character and presence as revealed, not merely a label. This distinction matters: the nations will eventually praise this name (Ps 117; Rom 15:11).
113.2: yĕhî šēm yhwh mĕbōrāk mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "may the name of the LORD be blessed from now and until forever" — the jussive yĕhî (let it be) frames this as prayer, not merely description. The temporal span mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from this moment and unto eternity) appears also in Ps 115:18 and 121:8, making it a standard formula for eschatological praise.
113.3: mimmizraḥ-šemeš ʿad-mĕbōʾô mĕhullāl šēm yhwh — "from the rising of the sun to its setting, praised is the name of the LORD" — spatial universality (east to west) complements the temporal universality of v2. The passive participle mĕhullāl (praised, being praised) suggests an already-existing reality: the name is universally worthy of praise as a present fact. Mal 1:11 echoes this: YHWH declares that his name is great among the nations from east to west.
113.4: rām ʿal-kol-gôyim yhwh ʿal-haššāmayim kĕbôdô — "the LORD is high above all nations; above the heavens is his glory" — rām (Qal participle of rûm, to be high) is simple in Hebrew but sweeping in claim: YHWH stands above every political and religious sovereignty. Even the heavens, the highest created reality, are below his glory. The transcendence established here (vv4-5) makes the condescension of vv6-9 all the more radical.
113.5: mî kayhwāh ʾĕlōhênû haggābihî lāšābet — "who is like the LORD our God, who is enthroned so high (haggābihî lāšābet)?" — the rhetorical question expects no answer. haggābihî is the Hiphil participle of gābah (to be high, to raise high): he who makes his seat high. The idiom "high to sit/dwell" emphasizes his enthronement at the apex of creation — precisely the one who then bends down in vv6-9.
113.6: hammašpîlî lirʾôt baššāmayim ûbāʾāreṣ — "who lowers himself to look at the heavens and the earth" — hammašpîlî (Hiphil participle of šāpal, to be low, humble) — God must lower himself to look even at the heavens. The condescension is absolute: the one enthroned above heaven bends down so far that even the sky is below his sight-line. This framing makes vv7-9 theologically radical: his attention to the poor is an act of infinite descent.
113.7: mĕqîmî mēʿāpār dāl mēʾašpōt yārîm ʾebyôn — "raising the poor from the dust, lifting the needy from the ash heap" — the vocabulary is nearly identical to 1 Sam 2:8 (Hannah's song). The ʾašpōt (ash heap, dunghill) in ancient villages was the rubbish heap outside the village where the destitute scavenged — a specific social location, not a metaphor. Ps 113 deliberately evokes Hannah's song, connecting YHWH's saving action with the OT pattern of reversal (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth). Luke 1:52-53 (the Magnificat) continues this pattern.
113.8: lĕhôšîbî ʿim-nĕdîbîm ʿim nĕdîbê ʿammô — "to seat him with princes, with the princes of his people" — nādîb (prince, noble, willing giver) has dual meaning: nobility by birth and liberality of spirit. The God who lifts the poor from the ash heap seats them in the company of the nobility. This is not social revolution through violence but divine reversal through grace — the same pattern appears in the Magnificat and in Jesus' beatitudes.
113.9: mōšîbî ʿăqeret habbayit ʾēm-habbānîm śĕmēḥāh — "who gives the barren one of the house a home, a joyful mother of children" — ʿăqeret (barren woman, from ʿāqar, to root up/hamstring) — one who has been uprooted from the social role of motherhood. Her transformation into ʾēm-habbānîm śĕmēḥāh (a joyful mother of children) closes the psalm on the note of reversal. The barren woman's transformation is the paradigm of all the divine reversals in the Psalter.

114.1: bĕṣēʾt yiśrāʾēl mimmimṣrāyim bêt yaʿăqōb mēʿam lōʿēz — "when Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of lōʿēz" — lōʿēz (from lāʿag, to mock, stammer, speak unintelligibly) describes Egypt as a place of foreign/incomprehensible speech, a domain linguistically alien to Israel. The choice of this word marks Egypt as beyond the covenant world. The LXX renders it barbarophōnou (barbarous-speaking people), the etymological source of the Greek word "barbarian."
114.2: hāyĕtāh yĕhûdāh lĕqodšô yiśrāʾēl mamšĕlôtāyw — "Judah became his sanctuary, Israel his dominion/kingdom" — the verb hāyĕtāh (became, feminine — the country is meant, not the tribe) marks transformation: the land was not always holy but became so through election. The divine name does not appear in this psalm until v7 — everything happens as nature reacting to an unnamed force, building dramatic suspense.
114.3: hayyām rāʾāh wayyānōs haywardēn yissōb lĕʾāḥôr — "the sea saw and fled; the Jordan turned back/reversed" — the Red Sea crossing (Exod 14) and Jordan crossing (Josh 3-4) are compressed into one dramatic moment. The verb yānûs (to flee, run away) — the sea is terrified and runs; yissōb lĕʾāḥôr (to turn back to the rear) — the Jordan's flow is reversed. No Moses, no Joshua is named — only creation's immediate response to divine presence.
114.4: hehārîm rāqĕdû kĕʾêlîm gĕbāʿôt kĕbnê-ṣōʾn — "the mountains leaped/danced like rams, the hills like young lambs" — rāqad (to skip, leap, dance; the same verb in Eccl 3:4 for dancing) applied to mountains achieves the psalm's signature comic-majestic effect. The simile domesticates the mountains into a flock responding to their shepherd — and sets up the interrogation of vv5-6.
114.5: mah-lĕkā hayyām kî tānûs haywardēn tissōb lĕʾāḥôr — "what happened to you, O sea, that you fled? O Jordan, that you turned back?" — the psalm now speaks directly to the sea and the Jordan as if they could answer. The rhetorical questions (vv5-6) probe the cause of vv3-4, building dramatic suspense before the answer in v7.
114.6: hehārîm tirqĕdû kĕʾêlîm gĕbāʿôt kĕbnê-ṣōʾn — "you mountains, why did you leap like rams? You hills, like young lambs?" — the same simile as v4, now turned interrogative. The repetition creates a chiastic structure: vv3-4 describe → vv5-6 interrogate → v7 answers. No answer is given to the sea or the mountains; the answer comes as a command to the earth itself.
114.7: mippĕnê ʾādôn ḥûlî ʾāreṣ mippĕnê ʾĕlôah yaʿăqōb — "from before the Lord (ʾādôn), writhe in anguish O earth; from before the God of Jacob" — the divine name appears for the first time in v7. ḥûl (to writhe, to be in anguish) is the verb used for a woman in labor (Isa 26:17; Jer 22:23); the earth's response to YHWH's presence is birth-pangs, not merely trembling. The title ʾĕlôah yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) emphasizes covenant particularity: the universal Lord is specifically Israel's covenant God.
114.8: hahōpekhî haṣṣûr ʾăgam-māyim ḥallāmîš lĕmaʿyĕnô-māyim — "the one who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water" — a participial relative clause describing God's characteristic action. The reference is to the water-from-rock miracle at Meribah and Massah (Exod 17:1-7; Num 20:2-13). Paul allegorically identifies this rock with Christ in 1 Cor 10:4 ("the spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Christ"). ḥallāmîš (flint, the hardest stone) heightens the miracle: not ordinary rock but flint becomes a flowing spring.

115.1: lōʾ lānû yhwh lōʾ lānû kî-lĕšimĕkā tēn kābôd — "not to us, O LORD, not to us — but to your name give glory" — the double lōʾ lānû (not to us... not to us) is emphatic self-abnegation; the psalm opens by disavowing any claim to divine credit. The glory belongs to the divine name, grounded in two covenant attributes: ʿal-ḥasdĕkā ʿal-ʾămittĕkā — "for your steadfast love (ḥesed) and your faithfulness (ʾĕmet)" — the two pillars of covenantal divine character (Exod 34:6).
115.2: lāmmāh yōʾmĕrû haggôyim ʾayyēh-nāʾ ʾĕlōhêhem — "why should the nations say, 'Where then is their God?'" — the taunt may reflect military defeat or the challenge of Babylonian exile. The nations' challenge is theological: a God who cannot be seen in a cult statue seems powerless by ancient Near Eastern standards. The psalm's response (vv3-8) is that YHWH's invisibility is not weakness but transcendence — and that visible idols are themselves the problem.
115.3: wĕʾĕlōhênû baššāmayim kōl ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh — "but our God is in the heavens; all that he pleases he has done" — the contrast is absolute: the nations ask "Where?" and the answer is "in the heavens" — beyond all spatial localization. kōl ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh (all that he pleases he has done) — divine sovereignty over all events, contrasted with the impotence of idols that cannot even see or speak.
115.4: ʿăṣabbêhem kesef wĕzāhāb maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām — "their idols are silver and gold — the work of human hands" — ʿăṣabbîm (idols, from ʿāṣab, to fashion/shape; also related to grief/pain) — the word choice for "idols" carries a derogatory connotation. The critique "work of human hands" is the core of the OT idol polemic: the material precedes the maker. Compare Isa 44:9-20 (the craftsman who burns half a tree for warmth and makes an idol with the other half) and Jer 10:1-16.
115.5: peh-lāhem wĕlōʾ yĕdabbērû ʿênayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yirʾû — "they have mouths but do not speak; eyes they have but do not see" — the systematic dismemberment of the idol's sense organs (vv5-7) proceeds through mouth, eyes, ears, nose, hands, feet, and throat. The irony is double: the idol has the form of human sense organs that YHWH gave to humans, but the form is hollow — none of them function. The idol is a parody of the human being made in God's image, possessing the form but not the nĕšāmāh (breath of life).
115.6: ʾoznayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yišmāʿû ʾap lāhem wĕlōʾ yĕrîḥûn — "ears they have but do not hear; a nose they have but do not smell" — the idol's nose is functionless in both biological and cultic terms: it cannot smell the sacrifices offered to it. The contrast with YHWH is implicit: YHWH smells Noah's sacrifice (Gen 8:21: wayyāraḥ yhwh ʾet-rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ). The idol's organ exists but cannot engage with what is brought to it.
115.7: yĕdêhem wĕlōʾ yĕmîšûn raglêhem wĕlōʾ yĕhallēkû lōʾ-yehgû bigrônām — "their hands but they cannot feel; their feet but they cannot walk; they do not make a sound from their throat" — māšaš (to feel, grope) — the hands lack tactile sensation. hāgāh (to mutter, meditate, make a sound) — used of the growl of a lion, the mourning of a dove, the murmur of Torah meditation (Ps 1:2). The idol's throat produces nothing — no growl, no word, no breath. The polemic ends with this most basic absence.
115.8: kĕmôhem yihyû ʿōśêhem kōl ʾăšer-bōṭēaḥ bāhem — "like them will be those who make them — all who trust in them" — the idol polemic reaches its theological apex: worshippers become what they worship. If the idol is a non-seeing, non-hearing, non-breathing thing, the worshipper who trusts in it becomes correspondingly deaf, blind, and spiritually lifeless. This principle of worship-as-formation runs counter to the fear of the LORD in Ps 111-112, where the one who fears YHWH takes on YHWH's own character.
115.9: yiśrāʾēl bĕṭaḥ bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "Israel, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the sudden shift from description to direct address (v9) is a liturgical pivot, probably call-and-response between cantor and congregation. The triple refrain (vv9/10/11) addresses Israel, the house of Aaron, and those who fear the LORD — the three groups present in the temple court. ʿezer (help) and māgēn (shield) are the two primary metaphors for divine protection: active assistance and defensive covering.
115.10: bêt ʾahărōn bĕṭĕḥû bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "house of Aaron, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the bêt ʾahărōn (house of Aaron) = the priestly families. Their trust is placed in the same source as all Israel: the LORD, not their priestly status or ritual expertise. The identical refrain drives home that there is one source of help and protection for all.
115.11: yirʾê yhwh bĕṭĕḥû bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "those who fear the LORD, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the third group, yirʾê yhwh (those who fear the LORD), may refer to Gentile proselytes or God-fearers in the temple court (cf. Acts 10:2, 13:16), expanding the circle beyond ethnic Israel. Together the triple refrain says: whatever your status, your help is not in idols but in the living God.
115.12: yhwh zĕkhārānû yĕbārēk — "the LORD has remembered us; he will bless" — zĕkhārānû (he has remembered us) — divine remembering always entails divine acting; it is not a cognitive event but a covenant event. The past tense grounds the future blessing: because he has already remembered, the threefold blessing that follows is certain. The triple yĕbārēk (he will bless) echoes the triple refrain of vv9-11.
115.13: yĕbārēk yirʾê yhwh haqqĕṭannîm ʿim-haggĕdōlîm — "he will bless those who fear the LORD, the small together with the great" — the pairing haqqĕṭannîm ʿim-haggĕdōlîm (the small together with the great) emphasizes that the blessing is not status-based. The fear of the LORD is democratizing: it places the small and the great on the same footing before the divine patron.
115.14: yōsēp yhwh ʿălêkem ʿălêkem wĕʿal-bĕnêkem — "may the LORD increase you — you and your children" — yāsap (to add, increase) — the blessing is multigenerational increase, harking back to the Abrahamic promise (pārāh wĕrābāh, be fruitful and multiply, Gen 1:28; 9:1). The Hiphil jussive: "may he cause you to increase."
115.15: bĕrûkhîm ʾattem layhwāh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "blessed are you by/from the LORD, who made heaven and earth" — the divine title ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) appears also in Ps 121:2 and 124:8. The idol critique (vv4-8) makes this doxology sharper: unlike idols made by human hands, Israel is blessed by the one who made all that is.
115.16: haššāmayim šāmayim layhwāh wĕhāʾāreṣ nātan libnê-ʾādām — "the heavens — heavens — belong to the LORD, but the earth he has given to the children of man" — the repeated haššāmayim šāmayim is emphatic: the upper realm is reserved for YHWH alone. But the earth is given (nātan, perfect tense — permanent grant) to bĕnê-ʾādām (children of Adam/humanity). This vertical division undergirds v17: the dead in Sheol are in a third space — neither YHWH's heaven nor humanity's proper earth.
115.17: lōʾ hāmētîm yĕhalĕlû-yāh wĕlōʾ kol-yōrĕdê dûmāh — "the dead do not praise the LORD, nor any who go down to silence" — dûmāh (silence, the realm of the dead) — Sheol as the land of silence. This is one of the most direct OT statements about the condition of the dead: they cannot praise.
