The same conviction appears in Ps 6:5 ("in death there is no remembrance of you; in Sheol who will give you thanks?") and Ps 88:10-12. This is not a denial of afterlife per se but an affirmation that Sheol is outside the covenant space of praise. Heb 2:12 (citing Ps 22:22) presents Christ's resurrection as precisely what reverses this: the risen Christ praises in the assembly, opening what Sheol had closed.
115.18: waʾănaḥnû nĕbārēk yāh mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām hallĕlûyāh — "but we — we will bless the LORD from this moment and forever. Praise the LORD!" — waʾănaḥnû (but we) is emphatic: in contrast to the dead who cannot praise, the living community takes up the obligation. The formula mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām echoes Ps 113:2; the Egyptian Hallel frames time itself as praise-time. The closing hallĕlûyāh returns to the beginning of Ps 113, closing the liturgical bracket of the first Hallel pair.

116.1: ʾāhavtî kî-yišmaʿ yhwh ʾet-qôlî taḥănûnāy — "I love, because the LORD hears my voice, my supplications" — uniquely, ʾāhavtî (I love) has no stated object in Hebrew; the object of love is implied from the whole psalm. The verb is Qal perfect, a settled state: "I am in a state of love." taḥănûnāy (my supplications, cries for mercy, from ḥānan, to be gracious) is plural, suggesting repeated urgent requests over time, not a single prayer.
116.2: kî-hiṭṭāh ʾoznô lî ûbĕyāmay ʾeqrāʾ — "because he has bent his ear to me; so for all my days I will call on him" — hiṭṭāh ʾoznô (inclined his ear, Hiphil of nāṭāh) — a vivid anthropomorphism: God tilts his ear downward to catch the prayer from below. The practical consequence is lifelong prayer: the experience of being heard generates a permanent posture of calling.
116.3: ʾăpāpûnî ḥeblê-māwet ûmĕṣārê šĕʾôl mĕṣāʾûnî — "the ropes/cords of death surrounded me; the constrictions of Sheol found me" — ḥeblê-māwet (ropes/cords of death) — ḥebel means "cord/rope" and (with different vowels) "sorrow/pain"; the LXX reads ōdines (birth pangs). Either way the image is entrapment. mĕṣārê šĕʾôl (constrictions/straits of Sheol, from ṣārar, to bind, constrict) — Sheol closes in like a vise. ṣārāh wĕyāgôn ʾemṣāʾ — "distress and anguish I found."
116.4: ûbĕšēm-yhwh ʾeqrāʾ ʾānnāh yhwh mallĕṭāh napšî — "then I called on the name of the LORD: 'O LORD, please — rescue my life!'" — ʾānnāh (please, I beseech you) is the interjection of urgent petition (also Ps 118:25; Jon 1:14). mallĕṭāh napšî (rescue/deliver my life, Piel imperative of mālaṭ) — Piel is intensive: "snatch me out, pull me free." The entire saving event hinges on this single cry.
116.5: ḥannûn yhwh wĕṣaddîq wĕʾĕlōhênû mĕraḥēm — "gracious is the LORD and righteous; our God is compassionate" — the same triad as Exod 34:6 and Ps 111:4 (ḥannûn, ṣaddîq, and raḥûm). In context the declaration is the psalmist's testimony after being heard: the rescue reveals what kind of God YHWH is. mĕraḥēm (showing compassion, Piel participle of rāḥam) derives from reḥem (womb) — divine compassion is womb-deep.
116.6: šōmēr pĕtāʾyim yhwh dallōtî wĕlî yĕhôšîaʿ — "the LORD preserves the simple (pĕtāʾyim); I was brought low, and he saved me" — pĕtāʾyim (simple, naive, from pāṭāh, to be open/gullible) — the Proverbs vocabulary for those without experience. The LXX renders nēpia (infants, little ones). The "simple" need a guardian, not because they are foolish, but because they are inexperienced and open. dallōtî (I was brought low, Qal perfect of dālal, to hang low, be reduced) — he made no claim of merit; he simply fell, and God caught him.
116.7: šûbî napšî limnûḥāyĕkî kî-yhwh gāmal ʿālāyĕkî — "return, O my soul, to your rest, for the LORD has been generous to you" — the psalmist speaks to his own nepeš (soul) — an interior dialogue rare in the Psalter (cf. Ps 42:5, 11; 103:1). limnûḥāyĕkî (to your resting place, from nûaḥ, to rest) — the same root as Noah's name. gāmal (to deal fully, to recompense, to be generous) — often used of both reward and punishment; here the generosity is the rescue just completed.
116.8: kî ḥillaṣtā napšî mimmāwet ʾet-ʿênî min-dimʿāh ʾet-raglî middĕḥî — "for you have rescued my life from death, my eye from tears, my foot from stumbling" — ḥillaṣ (to draw out, rescue, Piel of ḥālaṣ) — originally to pull off a sandal, extended to extricating from any trap. The triple object (life from death; eye from tears; foot from stumbling) covers the whole person: existential, emotional, practical. The three rescue clauses mirror the three death-forms of v3.
116.9: ʾethallēk lipnê yhwh bĕʾarṣôt haḥayyîm — "I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living" — ʾethallēk (Hithpael of hālak, to walk about, walk back and forth) — the Hithpael suggests ongoing, sustained walking, not a single stride. lipnê yhwh (before the LORD, in his presence) — covenant language for the life lived in YHWH's sight (Gen 17:1: "walk before me and be blameless"). bĕʾarṣôt haḥayyîm (in the land of the living) — the world of the living as opposed to Sheol.
116.10: heʾĕmantî kî ʾădabbēr — "I believed/trusted, because I spoke" (or "I trusted, therefore I spoke") — the syntax is famously ambiguous: does kî introduce a result clause or a causal one? The LXX reads it as "I believed, therefore I spoke" (episteusan, dio elalēsa), which is the version Paul cites in 2 Cor 4:13 for his own apostolic speech: "Since we have the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, 'I believed, and so I spoke,' we also believe, and so we also speak." heʾĕmantî (Hiphil perfect of ʾāman) = the root of ʾāmēn.
116.11: ʾănî ʾāmartî bĕḥopsî kol-hāʾādām kōzēb — "I said in my panic/haste, 'All men are liars/faithless'" — bĕḥopsî (in my alarm, haste, panic, from ḥāpaz, to be startled, flee in panic) — the same root as the sea's "flight" described in Ps 114:5. The panic-declaration that all humans are unreliable (kōzēb, a liar) is an extreme expression from within crisis — not a settled philosophical conviction. Paul quotes this in Rom 3:4 ("let God be true and every man a liar"), demonstrating how the same verse can be deployed in different theological contexts.
116.12: māh-ʾāšîb layhwāh kol-tagmûlôhî ʿālay — "what shall I return/give back to the LORD for all his benefits toward me?" — māh-ʾāšîb (what shall I return?) — šûb (return, restore) frames the gift-and-return dynamic of covenant life: YHWH has given; the psalmist asks what return is appropriate. tagmûlôhî (his benefits, his full dealings, from gāmal) — the same root as v7's generosity; the psalmist is overwhelmed by the magnitude of the gift.
116.13: kôs-yĕshûʿôt ʾeśśāʾ ûbĕšēm yhwh ʾeqrāʾ — "I will lift up the cup of salvations and call on the name of the LORD" — kôs-yĕshûʿôt (cup of salvations) — the plural yĕshûʿôt (salvations, rescues) is significant: not a single rescue but multiple or great salvations. Ellicott identifies the kôs with the drink offering (nesek) accompanying festival sacrifices (Num 15:5-10). The psalmist's return to God is not payment but further worship. The NT Last Supper's "cup of blessing" (1 Cor 10:16) stands in this tradition.
116.14: nĕdāray layhwāh ʾăšallēm neged-nāʾ lĕkhol-ʿammô — "my vows to the LORD I will pay, now in the presence of all his people" — nĕdāray (my vows, from nādar, to vow) — the psalmist had made vows during the crisis (v4: "I called on the name of the LORD"); now he fulfills them publicly. The public fulfillment of vows is liturgically essential: the congregation witnesses and validates the testimony. This verse repeats verbatim as v18, forming a refrain around the central v15.
116.15: yāqār bĕʿênê yhwh hammāwĕtāh laḥăsîdāyw — "precious/costly in the eyes of the LORD is the death of his faithful ones (ḥăsîdîm)" — the most theologically debated verse in the psalm. yāqār (precious, costly, rare, heavy) — the ambiguity is productive: (1) the death of his faithful is valued by God (making martyrdom meritorious); (2) the death of his faithful is costly to God (he grieves it, takes it seriously). Most modern scholars and the context favor (2): God does not regard the death of his ḥăsîdîm lightly — he takes it seriously, which is why he rescues (vv3-8) and why the vow-fulfillment matters (vv13-18). The ḥăsîdîm are the covenant people at their most devoted.
116.16: ʾānnāh yhwh kî-ʾănî ʿabdĕkā ʾănî-ʿabdĕkā ben-ʾămātĕkā pittaḥtā lĕmôsērāy — "O LORD, I am your servant — your servant, son of your handmaid; you have loosed my bonds" — ben-ʾămātĕkā (son of your handmaid/female servant) — a son born in the owner's household, a hereditary servant from birth rather than one acquired. This heightens the covenantal intimacy. Ps 86:16 uses the identical phrase. pittaḥtā lĕmôsērāy — "you have opened/loosed my bonds/fetters" — pātaḥ (to open, loosen) applied to mōsērôt (bonds, fetters) picks up the ropes of death from v3.
116.17: lĕkā ʾezbaḥ zebaḥ tôdāh ûbĕšēm yhwh ʾeqrāʾ — "to you I will offer a sacrifice of thanksgiving (tôdāh) and call on the name of the LORD" — the zebaḥ tôdāh (thanksgiving sacrifice) was a specific cultic category in Lev 7:11-15: a peace offering made when someone had been delivered from danger (illness, sea crossing, prison, desert). Ps 116 is itself a tôdāh psalm — both the literary form (testimony of rescue followed by praise) and the cultic act it likely accompanied.
116.18: nĕdāray layhwāh ʾăšallēm neged-nāʾ lĕkhol-ʿammô — verbatim repetition of v14, forming the refrain that frames v15. The public fulfillment of vows (v14 → v15 → v18) moves from obligation to declaration of God's seriousness about his servants' deaths to completion. The structural repetition signals liturgical performance: the congregation hears the vow-fulfillment formula twice, around the theologically central v15.
116.19: bĕḥaṣrôt bêt-yhwh bĕtôkēkî yĕrûšālāim hallĕlûyāh — "in the courtyards of the house of the LORD, in your midst, O Jerusalem. Praise the LORD!" — the psalm closes with geographic specificity: the vow is fulfilled not just before "all his people" (vv14,18) but in the temple courts, in Jerusalem. This is the pilgrimage psalm's destination — the pilgrim arrives and fulfills his vow. hallĕlûyāh closes the psalm and opens directly into Ps 117, the briefest and most universal of the Hallel psalms.

117.1: hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh kol-gôyim šabbĕḥûhû kol-hāʾummîm — "praise the LORD, all nations! Extol/laud him, all peoples!" — Ps 117 is the shortest psalm and the shortest chapter in the Bible. Its two commands address not Israel but the nations: gôyim (nations, Gentiles) and ʾummîm (peoples, tribes, ethnic groups) — the entire sweep of humanity. The verb šābaḥ (to praise, extol, laud) appears relatively rarely in the Psalter and has the connotation of public acclamation. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 15:11 as his fourth OT proof-text that God always intended the Gentiles to praise him — alongside Deut 32:43, 2 Sam 22:50, and Isa 11:10. The universal call is not a post-Resurrection innovation; it is embedded in the Psalter's own vision.
117.2: kî gābar ʿālênû ḥasdô wĕʾĕmet-yhwh lĕʿôlām hallĕlûyāh — "for his steadfast love (ḥesed) has prevailed/been mighty over us, and the faithfulness (ʾĕmet) of the LORD is forever. Praise the LORD!" — gābar (to be strong, prevail, be mighty, overcome) is an unusual verb to pair with ḥesed. Normally ḥesed is described as "great" or "enduring"; here it "prevails" — it overcomes, triumphs, wins. The two pillars of divine character — ḥesed (covenant love) and ʾĕmet (faithfulness, reliability) — recur from Exod 34:6 throughout the Psalter. Here they are the grounds on which the nations are called to praise: not Israel's military victories but YHWH's relentless covenant love, which is mighty enough to claim even the Gentiles.

120.1: šîr hamaʿălôt — "a song of the ascents" — the superscription that opens Ps 120 and heads each of the 15 psalms through Ps 134. maʿălôt (ascents, goings up, steps) from ʿālāh (to go up, ascend) — traditionally understood as songs sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals (Deut 16:16), or as liturgical songs chanted on the fifteen steps of the temple. baṣṣārāh lî qārāʾtî ʾel-yhwh wayyaʿănēnî — "in my distress I cried to the LORD, and he answered me" — the psalm opens with a completed rescue, framing the distress as already heard. This retrospective structure is characteristic of thanksgiving psalms.
120.2: yhwh haṣṣîlāh napšî miśśĕpat-šāqer millāšôn rĕmiyyāh — "O LORD, rescue my life from lying lips, from a deceitful tongue" — two paired nouns: śĕpat-šāqer (lying/falsehood lip) and lāšôn rĕmiyyāh (tongue of deceit/treachery). rĕmiyyāh (deceit, slackness, treachery, from rāmāh, to deceive, throw) — the word carries active betrayal, not merely casual dishonesty. The pilgrim's first distress is not war or famine but the social violence of false speech in a hostile environment.
120.3: mah-yittēn lĕkā ûmah-yōsîp lāk lāšôn rĕmiyyāh — "what will be given to you, and what more will be done for you, O deceitful tongue?" — a taunt-address to the lying tongue itself. The rhetorical questions set up v4's answer. The verse form (what will be given? what will be added?) echoes the curse-formula of ancient Near Eastern treaty violations: the offending party is asked what they deserve before the punishment is declared.
120.4: ḥiṣṣê gibbôr šĕnûnîm ʿim gaḥălê rĕtāmîm — "sharp arrows of a mighty man, with burning coals of broom wood" — the answer to v3's question: the deceitful tongue deserves YHWH's sharpened arrows and the fierce burning coals of broom wood (rĕtāmîm). rōtem (broom shrub, Retama raetam) — a desert shrub; its charcoal burns with exceptional, sustained heat. The image evokes YHWH's warrior arsenal (Ps 7:13; 18:14). The lying tongue has committed a war against the psalmist; it will be met with divine military response.
120.5: ʾôy-lî kî-gartî mešek šākantî ʿim-ʾohălê qēdār — "woe is me that I sojourn in Meshech, that I dwell among the tents of Kedar!" — Meshech appears in the Table of Nations (Gen 10:2) as a son of Japheth; associated with Anatolia (modern Turkey). Kedar (Gen 25:13) was a son of Ishmael; the Kedarites were semi-nomadic tribes of the Arabian desert. Together Meshech + Kedar represent the antipodes of the known world — the farthest possible distance from Jerusalem. This may be figurative for hostile pagans rather than geographic precision: the pilgrim feels exiled among those whose values are irreconcilably alien to covenant life.
120.6: rabbat šākenāh-llāh napšî ʿim śōnēʾ šālôm — "too long has my soul dwelt with those who hate peace" — rabbat (much, many, too long) — the accumulation of time among peace-haters has become intolerable. śōnēʾ šālôm (those who hate peace) — the compound noun-hater is not merely indifferent but actively hostile to šālôm (wholeness, well-being, covenant flourishing). This sets up the starkly personal antithesis of v7.
120.7: ʾănî-šālôm ûkî ʾădabbēr hēmmāh lammilḥāmāh — "I am peace (= for peace); but when I speak, they are for war" — the most compressed verse in the psalm. The Hebrew is jarring: ʾănî šālôm (I am peace) — the psalmist does not merely desire peace but identifies himself with it. The word order emphasizes the contrast: I = peace / they = war. The moment of speech becomes the flashpoint: the very act of speaking peace provokes war. The phrase anticipates Christ's beatitude on the peacemakers (Matt 5:9) and Paul's description of the gospel as the "good news of peace" (Eph 6:15).

121.1: ʾeśśāʾ ʿênay ʾel-hehārîm mēʾayin yābōʾ ʿezrî — "I lift my eyes toward the hills — but where does my help come from?" — the question mark is crucial; the verse is a genuine question, not an affirmation. The hills are not the source of help: the pagan high places (bāmôt) were located on hills, and lifting eyes to the hills might evoke idolatrous worship. The pilgrim raises the question deliberately — and immediately answers it in v2: my help is not from the hills but from the one who made them. The Ascents setting (the pilgrim traveling toward Jerusalem) lends this a pilgrimage urgency.
121.2: ʿezrî mēʿim yhwh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "my help comes from the LORD, the maker of heaven and earth" — the answer to v1's question. ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) — the same divine title as Ps 115:15, 124:8. The hills are part of the created order (earth); the LORD made them. Therefore the one who made the hills is greater than the hills; looking to the hills and then to their Maker is the psalm's theological argument. The participle ʿōśēh (ongoing making/sustaining) suggests not just original creation but active maintenance.
121.3: ʾal-yittēn lammôṭ raglĕkā ʾal-yānûm šōmĕrekā — "he will not let your foot slip; your keeper will not slumber" — šōmēr (keeper, guardian, watchman) appears here for the first of six times in eight verses — a structural concentration unique in the Psalter. yānûm (to slumber, nod off, doze) — a light, momentary sleep. The distinction between nûm (doze) and yāšan (full sleep, v4) is deliberate: God neither dozes for a moment nor falls into full sleep. The foot not slipping is both literal (rocky Judean terrain on the ascent to Jerusalem) and metaphorical (moral stumbling in covenant life).
121.4: hinnēh lōʾ-yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān šômēr yiśrāʾēl — "See — he who guards Israel will neither slumber nor sleep" — the emphatic particle hinnēh (behold, look!) draws attention. lōʾ-yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān — the double negation with two different sleep-words is emphatic: neither momentary dozing nor full sleep. The transition from the individual ("your keeper," v3) to the corporate ("keeper of Israel") situates the personal assurance within the covenant people as a whole — this is the LORD who never took his eye off Israel through the wilderness, the judges period, and the exile.
121.5: yhwh šōmĕrekā yhwh ṣillĕkā ʿal-yad yĕmînĕkā — "the LORD is your keeper; the LORD is your shade at your right hand" — ṣēl (shade, shadow) — in the ancient Near Eastern sun, shade was not comfort but survival. The divine shade is a metaphor for protective covering (Ps 91:1; Num 14:9 uses lack of shade for an unprotected enemy). ʿal-yad yĕmînĕkā (at your right hand) — the right hand is the position of the defender in battle (Ps 16:8; 109:31). The LORD-as-shade stands at the right hand of the pilgrim, between them and the blazing threat.
121.6: yômām haššemeš lōʾ-yakkĕkāh wĕyārēaḥ ballaylāh — "the sun will not strike you by day, nor the moon by night" — nākāh (to strike, smite) — the verb used for the plagues of Egypt (Exod 3:20). In the ancient world both solar heat and moonlight were thought to cause illness (sun-stroke, moon-stroke/lunacy). The psalm does not endorse these beliefs but uses them to frame a comprehensive protection: whatever threatens from above — day and night, sun and moon — the LORD's shade covers.
121.7: yhwh yišmāreĕkā mikkol-rāʿ yišmōr ʾet-napšĕkā — "the LORD will protect you from all evil; he will guard your very life/soul" — kol-rāʿ (all evil/harm) — the comprehensive scope: not merely physical dangers but every category of harm. napšĕkā (your life, your soul, your whole self) — nepeš here is not merely the spiritual soul but the whole living person. The LORD's keeping reaches to the core of the person, not merely the exterior.
121.8: yhwh yišmōr-ṣēʾtĕkā ûbôʾekā mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "the LORD will watch over your coming and going, now and forever" — ṣēʾtĕkā ûbôʾekā (your going out and your coming in) — a merism for all of life's movements: leaving and returning, the whole circuit of daily existence. Deut 28:6 uses the same formula for the covenant blessing ("blessed shall you be when you come in, and blessed when you go out"). The temporal span mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from now and unto eternity) matches Ps 113:2 and 115:18 — closing the pilgrim psalm with the eschatological horizon of Hallel praise.

122.1: śāmaḥtî bĕʾōmĕrîm lî bêt yhwh nēlēk — "I rejoiced when they said to me, 'Let us go to the house of the LORD!'" — the verb śāmaḥtî (I rejoiced, Qal perfect) is inceptive: "I broke into joy." The invitation from the plural ("they said to me") is the first moment of the pilgrimage experience — the community calling the individual into the journey. bêt yhwh (house of the LORD) — the temple, YHWH's royal dwelling on Zion. The superscription names David as author, placing this joy in the context of David's longing to build the temple (2 Sam 7).
122.2: ʿōmĕdôt hāyû raglênû bišʿārayik yĕrûšālāim — "our feet are standing within your gates, O Jerusalem!" — the perfect tense hāyû (have been/are) with the present participle ʿōmĕdôt (standing) creates a vivid present: the feet that were journeying now stand inside the city. The direct address to Jerusalem ("your gates") personifies the city. The gates (šĕʿārîm) were the legal, commercial, and social hub of ancient cities — the pilgrim's feet standing in the gates signal full arrival.
122.3: yĕrûšālayim habbĕnûyāh kĕʿîr šeḥubbĕrāh-llāh yaḥdāw — "Jerusalem — built as a city that is closely joined/compact together" — šeḥubbĕrāh-llāh yaḥdāw (that is joined together as one) — the Pual participle of ḥābar (to join, couple, unite). The architectural compactness of Jerusalem — its dense stone buildings and close-packed topography — becomes a metaphor for the unity of the covenant people who gather there. The phrase may also reflect the joining of the two kingdoms (Judah and Israel) in pilgrimage to the shared sanctuary.
122.4: šeššām ʿālû šĕbāṭîm šibṭê-yāh ʿēdût lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕhôdôt lĕšēm yhwh — "to which the tribes go up — the tribes of the LORD — as a testimony for Israel, to give thanks to the name of the LORD" — šibṭê-yāh (tribes of the LORD, with the divine shortform Yah) — the tribes belong to YHWH, not merely to their geographical territories. ʿēdût (testimony, statute) — the command to appear before the LORD at the festivals (Exod 23:17; 34:23) is here called a testimony/statute. lĕhôdôt (to give thanks/praise, Hiphil infinitive of yādāh) — the purpose of the pilgrimage is thanksgiving.
122.5: kî-šāmmāh yāšĕbû kissĕʾôt lĕmišpāṭ kissĕʾôt lĕbêt dāwid — "for there the thrones of judgment stand — the thrones belonging to the house of David" — kissĕʾôt lĕmišpāṭ (thrones for judgment) — the judicial seat of the Davidic king was in Jerusalem; the pilgrimage center was also the royal court for legal appeals (cf. 2 Sam 15:1-6; 1 Kgs 7:7). The plural kissĕʾôt (thrones) may refer to multiple courtrooms or the dynasty's collective judicial presence. The Davidic kingship and the temple worship are here inseparably linked.
122.6: šaʾălû šĕlôm yĕrûšālāim yišlāyû ʾōhăbāyik — "pray for the peace of Jerusalem! May all who love this city flourish" — the triple wordplay: šaʾălû (ask/pray) + šĕlôm (peace/shalom) + yĕrûšālāim (Jerusalem) — the city's very name may be heard as yĕrû-šālēm (city of peace), making the command to pray for its peace a play on its identity. yišlāyû (may they be at ease, prosper, have security, from šālāh, to be at ease) — those who love Jerusalem will share in the shalom they pray for.
122.7: yĕhî-šālôm bĕḥêlēk šalwāh bĕʾarmĕnôtāyik — "may there be peace within your walls and safety within your strongholds/citadels" — ḥêlēk (your walls, ramparts, from ḥēl = outer wall or moat) — the first line of the city's defense. šalwāh (safety, ease, prosperity) — a near synonym of šālôm, continuing the sound-cluster. ʾarmĕnôtāyik (your palaces, citadels, towers) — the innermost defended structures. The movement from outer walls to inner palaces maps the prayer's scope from the perimeter to the heart of the city.
122.8: lĕmaʿan ʾaḥay wĕrēʿāy ʾădabbĕrāh-nnāʾ šālôm bāk — "for the sake of my brothers and friends I now say, 'Peace be with you!'" — the psalmist's motivation is relational: the prayer for peace is not nationalist sentiment but affection for the ʾaḥim (brothers) and rēʿîm (friends/companions) who share the pilgrimage. The address bāk (in/with you) is to Jerusalem, confirming the personification of the city throughout the psalm.
122.9: lĕmaʿan bêt-yhwh ʾĕlōhênû ʾăbaqqĕšāh ṭôb lāk — "for the sake of the house of the LORD our God I will work for your wellbeing" — the final motivation: the welfare of Jerusalem is sought because YHWH's house is there. ʾăbaqqĕšāh ṭôb (I will seek good) — active pursuit, not passive wishing. The psalm ends with the divine presence (the house of the LORD) as the ultimate ground for all the peace-praying that precedes it. Jerusalem matters because YHWH has chosen it as his dwelling.

123.1: ʾēlêkā nāśāʾtî ʾet-ʿênay hayyōšĕbî baššāmāyim — "to you I lift my eyes, O you who sit enthroned in the heavens!" — the opening gesture (lifting eyes) echoes Ps 121:1 but with a crucial difference: here the eyes are lifted directly to YHWH, not ambiguously toward the hills. hayyōšĕbî baššāmāyim (the one who sits/is enthroned in the heavens) — the divine title stresses sovereign rule from above; this is the same God who bent down to look in Ps 113:6. The pilgrim looks up to find the one who has already looked down.
123.2: hinnēh kĕʿênê ʿăbādîm ʾel-yad ʾădônêhem kĕʿênê šipḥāh ʾel-yad gĕbirtāh kēn ʿênênû ʾel-yhwh ʾĕlōhênû ʿad šeyĕḥānnēnû — the sustained analogy of master/servant and mistress/maidservant. The servant watches the master's hand (yad) — in the ancient household economy, a subtle gesture of the master's hand gave the servant instructions without words. The servant's whole attention is on the hand that may signal need or command. ʿad šeyĕḥānnēnû (until he has mercy on us) — the waiting is not passive but attentive, watchful. The plural ʿênênû (our eyes) shifts to the whole congregation.
123.3: ḥānnēnû yhwh ḥānnēnû kî-rab śābaʿnû bûz — "have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy on us — for we have endured far more than our fill of contempt" — the doubled ḥānnēnû (have mercy on us / be gracious to us) is urgent petition. rab śābaʿnû bûz (we are exceedingly filled with contempt) — bûz (contempt, scorn, despising) is a cutting social wound, worse than hostility: to be despised is to be treated as of no account. śābaʿnû (we are filled/sated) — as one becomes surfeited with food, the psalmist is surfeited with contempt; they have had more than enough.
123.4: rabbat śābeʿāh lāh napšēnû hallaʿag haššaʾănannîm habbûz ligĕʾônîm — "we have had more than our fill of the mockery of the comfortable and the contempt of the arrogant" — laʿag (mockery, derision, scorn) from lāʿag, to mock, stammer at. šaʾănannîm (those at ease, the comfortable, the prosperous) — the word ironically echoes šaʾănān (ease, security); those who are comfortable mock the pilgrim community. gĕʾônîm (the proud, arrogant, from gāʾah, to be high, exalted) — pride and ease together: the psalm identifies social contempt as coming from those whose comfort makes them forget their own contingency.

124.1: lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl — "if the LORD had not been on our side — let Israel say it now —" — the psalm opens with the liturgical invitation yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl (let Israel now say) — this is a communal confession, repeated by the assembly. The construction lûlê (if not, unless) introduces a contrary-to-fact conditional: what would have happened if YHWH had not intervened. The repeated lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû (vv1-2) creates emphasis through near-verbatim repetition.
124.2: lûlê yhwh šehāyāh lānû bĕqûm ʿālênû ʾādām — "if the LORD had not been on our side when humans rose up against us" — bĕqûm ʿālênû ʾādām (when humans/mankind rose up against us) — the enemy is called ʾādām (humanity, mortal people) — not a demonic force but human opposition. The use of ʾādām rather than a specific ethnic group generalizes the threat while simultaneously diminishing it: these are mere mortals who rose against the people of YHWH.
124.3: ʾăzay ḥayyîm bĕlaʿûnû bĕḥărôt ʾappām bānû — "then they would have swallowed us alive when their fury blazed against us" — bĕlaʿûnû ḥayyîm (swallowed us alive) — bālaʿ (to swallow, engulf, to be a void, to destroy; Jonah 1:17 uses the same word for the great fish). ḥayyîm (living/alive) — not merely destroyed but consumed while still alive, as Sheol is said to swallow the living in Prov 1:12. ḥărôt ʾappām (the burning/kindling of their nose-anger) — anger in Hebrew physiology is located in the nose (ʾap): the flaring of nostrils accompanies rage.
124.4: ʾăzay hammayim šĕṭāpûnû naḥlāh ʿābar ʿal-napšēnû — "then the rushing waters would have swept us away; the current would have poured over our soul/life" — the metaphor shifts from predatory animal (v3) to flood. mayim šĕṭāpûnû (the waters swept over us) — šāṭap (to overflow, sweep away, rinse, inundate). naḥlāh (the torrent, wadi-stream) — the sudden flash floods of Palestinian wadis that sweep away everything in their path. ʿal-napšēnû (over our life/soul) — the waters threatening the nepeš (the living self).
124.5: ʾăzay ʿābar ʿal-napšēnû hammayim hazzêdônîm — "then the raging/insolent waters would have overwhelmed us" — hammayim hazzêdônîm (the insolent/raging/proud waters) — zādôn (pride, presumptuousness, insolence, from zûd, to boil over, act presumptuously) — the same word used for human pride and arrogance (Jer 50:31-32). The waters are personified as proud and insolent — as if the flood itself had the character of the proud enemies of v4:4. This is not pathetic fallacy but theological characterization: chaos and pride share the same root.
124.6: bārûk yhwh šellōʾ nĕtānānû ṭeref lĕšinnêhem — "blessed be the LORD, who did not give us as prey to their teeth!" — the doxology breaks the counter-factual conditional: the disasters of vv3-5 did not happen. ṭeref lĕšinnêhem (prey to their teeth) — the metaphor returns to the predatory animal image of v3 (swallowing alive). The enemies who would have swallowed them are now imagined as having teeth — a lion or wolf tearing prey. bārûk yhwh (blessed be the LORD) — the standard benediction formula for divine rescue (Gen 14:20; 1 Sam 25:39).
124.7: napšēnû kĕṣippôr nimlĕṭāh mippah yôqĕšîm happah nišbar waʾănaḥnû nimlāṭnû — "our soul escaped like a bird out of the snare of the fowlers — the snare was broken, and we escaped" — ṣippôr (a sparrow, bird) — small and vulnerable; the standard image for the weak and endangered (Ps 11:1; Matt 10:29-31). pah yôqĕšîm (the snare of the fowlers/trappers) — yāqôš (fowler, bird-trapper) — those who set hidden traps for birds. happah nišbar (the snare was broken) — not the bird's skill but the snare's failure; the escape is YHWH's doing, not the bird's strength.
124.8: ʿezrênû bĕšēm yhwh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "our help comes from the name of the LORD, the maker of heaven and earth" — the closing verse echoes Ps 121:2 verbatim: ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth). Within the Songs of Ascents, this repetition creates an intentional bookend — the keeper of Israel (Ps 121) is the same one who breaks the snare (Ps 124). bĕšēm yhwh (in the name of the LORD) — the name as the mediating presence of YHWH, his revealed character acting on behalf of his people.

125.1: habbōṭĕḥîm bayhwāh kĕhar-ṣiyyôn lōʾ-yimmôṭ lĕʿôlām yēšēb — "those who trust in the LORD are as firm as Mount Zion — unmovable, abiding forever" — the simile places divine trust on par with the geological permanence of Zion. bāṭaḥ (to trust, be confident) is the covenant-confidence word throughout the Psalter. lōʾ-yimmôṭ (will not be shaken/moved, Niphal of māṭaṭ) — cf. Ps 112:6 (the righteous will not be moved). Mount Zion's permanence is YHWH's own choice (Ps 132:13-14) — those who trust YHWH share in the stability he has chosen for his dwelling place.
125.2: yĕrûšālāim hārîm sābîb lāh wayhwāh sābîb lĕʿammô mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "as the mountains encircle Jerusalem, so the LORD encircles his people, from now and forever" — the geographic simile is exact: Jerusalem sits in a basin surrounded by higher ridges (Mount of Olives, Mount Scopus, Mount Zion, Gihon valley). The surrounding mountains are the city's natural fortification. YHWH's encirclement of his people (sābîb, around, on all sides) is at least as complete as the mountains around Jerusalem — and permanent in a way mountains are not.
125.3: kî lōʾ-yānûaḥ šēbeṭ hārāšāʿ ʿal gôral haṣṣaddîqîm lĕmaʿan lōʾ-yišlĕḥû haṣṣaddîqîm bĕʿawlāh yĕdêhem — "for the scepter of the wicked will not rest over the allotted territory of the righteous, lest the righteous put out their hands to do wrong" — šēbeṭ hārāšāʿ (the rod/scepter of the wicked) — political oppression by wicked rulers. gôral haṣṣaddîqîm (the allotment/lot of the righteous) — the land inheritance (gôral, the lot cast for tribal territories, Josh 14-19). The theological argument: prolonged oppression risks moral corruption of the oppressed — YHWH removes the oppressor not merely for justice but to protect the righteous from the temptation to adopt wicked means.
125.4: hêṭîbāh yhwh laṭṭôbîm ûlĕyišārîm bĕlibbôtām — "do good, O LORD, to those who are good, and to those who are upright in their hearts" — the prayer hêṭîbāh (do good, Hiphil imperative of yāṭab) — an appeal for positive divine action following the negative protection of v3. laṭṭôbîm (to those who are good) and lĕyišārîm bĕlibbôtām (to those who are upright in their hearts) — the internal qualifier "in their hearts" distinguishes genuine uprightness from external conformity.
125.5: wĕhammaṭṭîm ʿaqalqallôtām yôlîkēm yhwh ʾet-pōʿălê hāʾāwen šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — "but as for those who swerve onto crooked paths, the LORD will banish them along with those who do evil. Peace be upon Israel!" — hammaṭṭîm ʿaqalqallôtām (those who turn to their crooked ways) — ʿaqalqal (twisted, crooked, devious) — the word is an intensive form suggesting deeply bent, tortuous paths. pōʿălê hāʾāwen (workers of wickedness/iniquity) — the same phrase as Ps 6:8 and many other psalms. The closing šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl (peace upon Israel) is the standard liturgical close of many Psalms of Ascents (cf. Ps 128:6; 131:3).

126.1: bĕšûb yhwh ʾet-šîbat ṣiyyôn hāyînû kĕḥōlĕmîm — "when the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like people in a dream" — šûb ʾet-šĕbût (the idiom "restore the fortunes/captivity") — the phrase appears ~27x in the OT; it likely means both "restore the fortunes" (general reversal of loss) and "return from captivity" (exile and return); both senses apply to the post-exilic community. hāyînû kĕḥōlĕmîm (we were like dreamers) — so sudden, so complete was the reversal that it seemed unreal. The simile does not mean they were asleep but that the restoration felt like the impossible come true.
126.2: ʾāz yimmālēʾ śĕḥôq pînû ûlĕšônênû rinnāh ʾāz yōʾmĕrû baggôyim higgîl yhwh laʿăśôt ʿim-ʾēlleh — "then our mouths were filled with laughter and our tongues with shouts of joy; then the nations were saying, 'The LORD has done great things for them!'" — śĕḥôq (laughter, joy, the sound of laughter) — not mirth at another's expense but the laughter of relief and wonder. rinnāh (shouts of joy, exultant singing) — the distinctive joy-shout of the sanctuary. Crucially, even the gôyim (nations) recognize what has happened: YHWH's action is visible enough that outsiders can see it and name its source.
126.3: higgîl yhwh laʿăśôt ʿimmānû hāyînû śĕmēḥîm — "the LORD has done great things for us — and we are overjoyed" — the psalm quotes the nations' speech (v2) and then adopts it as Israel's own confession. The verb higgîl (to do greatly, to act magnificently) in Hiphil — God's action is characterized by magnitude. śĕmēḥîm (joyful, glad) — the simple and direct emotional state that results from YHWH's great action. The brevity of v3 after the longer v2 is a poetic technique of emphasis: the main thing is stated cleanly.
126.4: šûbāh yhwh ʾet-šĕbîtênû kāʾăpîqîm bannegeb — "restore our fortunes, O LORD, like the streams that suddenly fill the desert wadis" — the prayer šûbāh ʾet-šĕbîtênû (restore our fortunes/captivity) mirrors v1's bĕšûb yhwh (when the LORD restored) — the past restoration becomes the grounds for praying it again. kāʾăpîqîm bannegeb (like the wadis in the Negev) — the ʾăpîqîm (channels, stream-beds, wadis) of the Negev are bone-dry for most of the year but can fill with thunderous torrents in minutes when rain falls in the hills. The petition is for that kind of sudden, dramatic reversal.
126.5: hazzōrĕʿîm bĕdimʿāh bĕrinnāh yiqṣōrû — "those who plant/sow in tears will harvest with shouts of joy" — the agricultural metaphor of sowing and reaping maps perfectly onto the exile/return pattern: the exile is the season of tears/planting; the return is the harvest of joy. zāraʿ (to sow, plant seed) — the farmer sowing in tears: perhaps the tears of depleted energy at day's end, or grief at the seeming impossibility of harvest, or the tears of repentance. bĕrinnāh yiqṣōrû (with shouts of joy they will reap) — future tense: this is still prophetic for some in the community.
126.6: hālôk yēlēk ûbākkōh nōśēʾ mešek-hazāraʿ bōʾ-yābōʾ bĕrinnāh nōśēʾ ʾălumōtāyw — "the one who sets out weeping, carrying the seed bag, will come home with shouts of joy, loaded with his sheaves" — the infinitive absolute constructions (hālôk yēlēk = goes and goes, i.e. really goes out; bōʾ-yābōʾ = comes and comes, i.e. really does return) emphasize the certainty of both the going and the return. mešek-hazāraʿ (the seed bag/precious seed, lit. "drawn-out seed" or "trail of seed") — the precious seed carried with tears. ʾălumōtāyw (his sheaves, bundles of grain) — the return is loaded, arms full. The theological promise: no sowing in tears is wasted; every act of faithful obedience in a season of loss will have its harvest.

127.1: ʾim-yhwh lōʾ-yibneh bayit šāwĕʾ ʿāmĕlû bônāyw bô ʾim-yhwh lōʾ-yišmor-ʿîr šāwĕʾ šāqad šômēr — "unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labor in vain; unless the LORD guards the city, the sentinel watches for nothing" — šāwĕʾ (vanity, emptiness, futility, falsehood) — the same word used in the third commandment ("take the name of the LORD in vain," Exod 20:7). The attribution to Solomon (lĕšĕlōmōh) gives the verse a Solomon-temple resonance: the very temple Solomon built was only effective because YHWH chose to dwell in it (1 Kgs 8:27). bayit (house) has double force: the physical temple AND the Davidic dynasty ("your house shall be made sure," 2 Sam 7:11,16).
127.2: šāwĕʾ lākem maškîmê qûm mĕʾaḥărê šebet ʾōkĕlê leḥem hāʾăṣābîm kēn yittēn lîdîdô šēnāʾ — "it is pointless for you to rise early, stay up late, eating the bread of anxious toil — for he gives sleep to those he loves" — maškîmê qûm (those who rise early, from šāḵam, to be early, start at dawn) — the pre-dawn laborer. leḥem hāʾăṣābîm (bread of sorrows/pain/anxious toil) — ʿăṣab (pain, toil, sorrow) — the curse vocabulary of Gen 3:16-17 (toil, pain, eating by sweat). kēn yittēn lîdîdô šēnāʾ — "for so he gives to his beloved sleep" — dîdô (his beloved, from yādîd, beloved, dear) — the beloved of YHWH. Sleep is not earned by exhaustion but given as a gift; the one who rests in YHWH's provision does not need to anxiously secure their own existence through sleepless toil.
127.3: hinnēh naḥălat yhwh bānîm śākār pĕrî habbāṭen — "Yes, children are an inheritance from the LORD; they are a reward he gives" — naḥălat yhwh (the inheritance/heritage of the LORD) — children are not merely the product of human biology but a divine gift, a share in what the LORD distributes. śākār (reward, wages) — normally used for labor payment; here applied to the fruit of the womb, suggesting that children are the "wages" YHWH gives for covenant faithfulness rather than for anxious toil (cf. v2's bread of anxious toil → v3's inherited children).
127.4: kĕḥiṣṣîm bĕyad-gibbôr kēn bĕnê hannĕʿûrîm — "children born in a man's prime are like arrows in the hand of a warrior" — ḥiṣṣîm (arrows) — the plural of ḥēṣ (arrow, from ḥāṣaṣ, to cut). bĕnê hannĕʿûrîm (sons of youth/the prime years, lit. "sons of the youthful ones") — children born when the father is young enough that they will be fully grown when he is old, providing protection in his vulnerability. The arrow metaphor: straight, well-prepared, directed with force. Arrows in the hand of a warrior are ready for deployment — these children are the father's defense in the vulnerable years.
127.5: ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer millēʾ ʾet-ašpātô mēhem lōʾ-yēbōšû kî-yĕdabbĕrû ʾet-ʾôyĕbîm baššāʿar — "blessed is the man who has his quiver full of them! He will not be put to shame when he confronts his enemies at the gate" — ʾašpāh (quiver, arrow-case) — the image completes the arrow metaphor. baššāʿar (at the gate) — the city gate was the judicial arena where legal disputes were settled; the man with grown sons standing beside him would have moral weight in legal proceedings and practical protection against false accusations.

128.1: ʾašrê kol-yĕrēʾ yhwh hahōlēk bidrākāyw — "blessed is every person who fears the LORD and walks in his ways" — Ps 128 is a companion beatitude to Ps 112 (both begin with ʾašrê and focus on the God-fearing man's flourishing). The universal kol (every, all) opens the blessing to any person, not just the head of household — though the psalm will develop the domestic picture. hahōlēk bidrākāyw (the one who walks in his ways) — the walking (hālak) metaphor for covenant life is Deuteronomic in origin (Deut 8:6; 10:12).
128.2: yĕgîaʿ kappêkā kî tōʾkēl ʾašrêkā wĕṭôb lāk — "you will eat the fruit of your labor; you will be blessed, and things will go well for you" — yĕgîaʿ kappêkā (the toil/labor of your palms/hands) — kap (palm, hand, hollow of the hand) — the very hands that work. Contrasting with Ps 127:2 (which warns against anxious toil), Ps 128 says that the labor of the God-fearer is not vain labor but genuinely productive. The covenant blessing is concrete and physical: the hands that work will eat what they have produced.
128.3: ʾeštĕkā kĕgepen pōriyyāh bĕyarkĕtê bêtĕkā bānêkā kĕšĕtîlê zêtîm sābîb lĕšulḥānĕkā — "your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your home; your children like young olive shoots around your table" — gepen pōriyyāh (a fruitful vine) — the vine bearing fruit in abundance; fertility imagery from the agricultural blessings of the land. bĕyarkĕtê bêtĕkā (within the innermost parts/sides of your house) — not a public display but flourishing in the domestic interior. šĕtîlê zêtîm (shoots/seedlings of olive trees) — young olives require years to mature but then produce abundantly for generations; the children are investments in the future, not merely present joys.
128.4: hinnēh kî-kēn yĕbōrak gāber yĕrēʾ yhwh — "behold, thus shall the man be blessed who fears the LORD" — hinnēh kî-kēn (behold, for so, thus indeed) — the emphatic pointing-back formula: what was described in vv2-3 is precisely the content of the blessing. The verse functions as a summary and validation of the domestic vision: this is not an aspirational fantasy but the structural pattern of covenant life when lived from the fear of the LORD.
128.5: yĕbārekkā yhwh miṣṣiyyôn ûrĕʾēh bĕṭûb yĕrûšālāim kōl yĕmê ḥayyêkā — "may the LORD bless you from Zion; may you see Jerusalem flourish all the days of your life" — the blessing source is miṣṣiyyôn (from Zion) — YHWH blesses from his chosen dwelling place. The connection between the domestic (wife and children, vv2-3) and the civic (Jerusalem) is deliberate: the individual household's flourishing is part of the city's flourishing and vice versa. ûrĕʾēh bĕṭûb yĕrûšālāim (and see the good of Jerusalem) — to live long enough to see the city well is itself a blessing.
128.6: ûrĕʾēh-bānîm lĕbānêkā šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — "may you live to see your grandchildren. Peace be upon Israel" — bānîm lĕbānêkā (sons of your sons, i.e., grandchildren) — longevity enough to see the third generation. In the ancient world, dying before your grandchildren were born was a sign of divine disfavor; seeing them was the fullness of covenant blessing (Gen 50:23; Job 42:16). The closing šālôm ʿal-yiśrāʾēl (peace be upon Israel) — the standard liturgical coda of the Songs of Ascents (Ps 125:5; 131:3), extending the individual household's blessing to the whole people.

129.1: rabbat ṣĕrārûnî minnĕʿûray yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl — "many times they have afflicted me from my youth — let Israel say this now" — rabbat (many times, much, greatly) — the same opening word as Ps 123:4 (exceedingly filled). The liturgical call yōmar-nāʾ yiśrāʾēl (let Israel now say) echoes Ps 124:1 — this is Israel's communal testimony, not merely one individual's experience. minnĕʿûray (from my youth) — Israel personified as a nation from its origins (Egypt); the affliction has been lifelong, going back to the bondage in Egypt.
129.2: rabbat ṣĕrārûnî minnĕʿûray gam lōʾ-yāklû lî — "many times they have afflicted me from my youth, and yet they have never overcome me" — the verbatim repetition of the first half-line followed by the crucial gam lōʾ-yāklû lî (yet they have not prevailed against me) — the adversative gam (and yet, even, also). The repeated oppression combined with the repeated non-defeat is the theological testimony: enemies are persistent, but YHWH is more persistent. yākol (to prevail, be able, overcome) — the enemies' inability is the sign of divine protection.
129.3: ʿal-gabbî ḥārĕšû ḥōrĕšîm hē ʾărîkû lĕmaʿănîtôtām — "they plowed across my back; they made long their furrows" — ʿal-gabbî (upon my back) — the metaphor of the plowed back is one of the Psalter's most vivid images of suffering; the back being plowed evokes flogging, where the stripes of the whip cut furrows in the flesh. hē ʾărîkû lĕmaʿănîtôtām (they made long their furrows) — extended, deep, deliberate furrows. The agricultural metaphor frames brutal violence in terms of cultivation — the oppressors turn Israel's back into their field, treating the people as the ground for their own purposes.
129.4: yhwh ṣaddîq qiṣṣēṣ ʿăbôt rĕšāʿîm — "the LORD is righteous; he has cut through the ropes of the wicked" — ṣaddîq (righteous) — YHWH's righteousness here is his active justice on behalf of the wronged. qiṣṣēṣ (he cut, severed, Piel of qāṣaṣ) — the cutting is decisive and complete. ʿăbôt rĕšāʿîm (the cords/ropes of the wicked) — the ropes by which the wicked bound Israel, binding them to the plow (cf. v3's furrow). The image now inverts: the plowed field becomes the bound ox, and YHWH cuts the yoke-cords.
129.5: yēbōšû wĕyissōgû ʾāḥôr kōl śōnĕʾê ṣiyyôn — "may every one who hates Zion be put to shame and driven back" — the psalm shifts from testimony (vv1-4) to imprecation (vv5-8). yēbōšû (may they be ashamed, Qal jussive of bôš) — shame is the social opposite of honor in the ancient Near Eastern world; to be shamed is to be publicly exposed as having no standing. yissōgû ʾāḥôr (may they be driven back, retreating backwards) — the military image of an army in rout, retreating without turning to fight.
129.6: yihyû kĕḥăṣîr gakkôt šeqĕdem šālap yābeš — "may they be like grass growing on a flat rooftop, which withers before it even has a chance to grow" — ḥăṣîr gakkôt (the grass of rooftops/flat roofs) — the flat mud-brick roofs of ancient Palestine had cracks into which seeds blew; grass would sprout briefly in the rainy season but wither almost immediately in the dry season — no deep roots, no shade, no harvest. šeqĕdem šālap yābeš (which before it is drawn out, it withers) — before it even reaches maturity, before anyone could think of harvesting it, it shrivels. The image answers the plowing of v3: their "harvest" from Israel's back will produce nothing.
129.7: šellōʾ millēʾ kappô qôṣēr wĕḥiṣnô mĕʿammēr — "no reaper fills his hand with it, and no one gathers it into sheaves" — the harvest imagery completes the agricultural metaphor: the rooftop-grass gives no handful to the reaper (qôṣēr) and no armful (ḥōṣen) to the one binding sheaves (mĕʿammēr). The wicked who plowed Israel's back hoping for a harvest will be like this futile rooftop grass — their effort produces absolutely nothing worth gathering.
129.8: wĕlōʾ ʾāmĕrû hāʾōbĕrîm birkăt-yhwh ʾălêkem bērăknû ʾetkem bĕšēm yhwh — "and no one passing by will say, 'The LORD's blessing be on you! We bless you in the name of the LORD'" — the standard blessing exchanged between reapers and those passing by during harvest (Ruth 2:4: Boaz greets the reapers: "The LORD be with you!" and they reply: "The LORD bless you!"). The absence of this exchange signals total failure: there is nothing to bless, no harvest to celebrate, no encounter of generosity between the field and the passerby. The wicked will not even receive the customary pleasantries of a good harvest — because there is none.

130.1: mimaʿămaqîm qĕrāʾtîkā yhwh — "from the depths I cry to you, O LORD" — maʿămaqîm (depths, plural of māʿāmaq, from ʿāmaq, to be deep) — the absolute bottom, the lowest imaginable point; used of deep waters (Ps 69:2,14; 88:7), and here of existential distress. The cry is upward from the bottom: YHWH is above; the psalmist is below. The Latin translation de profundis gave this psalm its traditional liturgical name. The singular voice of v1 broadens in vv7-8 to include all Israel — individual experience becomes communal.
130.2: ʾădōnāy šimʿāh bĕqôlî tihyênāh ʾoznêkā qaššûbôt lĕqôl taḥănûnāy — "Lord, hear my voice; let your ears be attentive to my pleading voice" — the address shifts from yhwh (the covenant name, v1) to ʾădōnāy (the sovereignty name) — the psalmist addresses YHWH's lordship now rather than covenant relationship. qaššûbôt (attentive, listening, Qal participle of qāšab, to pay attention, prick up the ears) — the ears tuned and oriented toward the prayer. taḥănûnāy (my supplications, pleas for grace/mercy, from ḥānan) — same word as Ps 116:1; 28:2.
130.3: ʾim-ʿăwōnôt tišmār-yāh ʾădōnāy mî yaʿămōd — "if you, O LORD, kept a record of sins, Lord, who could stand?" — šāmar ʿăwōnôt (to keep/guard/watch iniquities) — to maintain a legal ledger of sins. mî yaʿămōd (who would stand?) — to "stand" before God is to survive the court's verdict, to remain upright in judgment. The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. The statement is profoundly theological: if divine mercy were replaced by strict accounting, even the most righteous person would be unable to stand. This anticipates Paul's argument in Romans 3.
130.4: kî-ʿimĕkā hassĕlîḥāh lĕmaʿan tiwwārēʾ — "but forgiveness is found with you — and that is why you are held in awe" — sĕlîḥāh (forgiveness, pardon) — appears only three times in the OT: here, Neh 9:17, and Dan 9:9 — all in contexts of profound penitence before YHWH. The rarity marks it as a significant, non-routine word. The startling theological argument: YHWH is feared (yārēʾ) precisely because he forgives, not because he judges. If he maintained strict accounts (v3), he would terrify. But his forgiveness produces a deeper awe — the awe of the one who knows they have been spared.
130.5: qiwwîtî yhwh qiwĕtāh napšî ûlidĕbārô hôḥāltî — "I wait for the LORD — my whole being waits — and in his word I put my hope" — qāwāh (to wait, hope, expect, look for — from a root meaning to stretch like a cord toward something) — intense, expectant waiting. The doubled verb (qiwwîtî + qiwĕtāh napšî) with the soul (nepeš) as subject: not only the mind but the whole living person is stretched toward YHWH. lidĕbārô hôḥāltî (in his word I hope, Hiphil of yāḥal) — YHWH's spoken word is the ground of the hope; this is not wishful thinking but expectation anchored in promise.
130.6: napšî layhwāh miššōmĕrîm labboqer šōmĕrîm labboqer — "my soul waits for the LORD more than those who watch for the morning, more than those who watch for the morning" — šōmĕrîm labboqer (those who watch for the morning, dawn-sentinels) — night watchmen on the city walls who wait for the first light of dawn to signal the end of their vigilance. The comparison and its repetition: the psalmist's longing for YHWH is more intense than the watchman's longing for dawn. The repeated phrase ("more than those who watch for the morning... those who watch for the morning") is unusual — the MT double-occurrence is generally considered deliberate emphasis, not scribal error.
130.7: yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh kî-ʿim-yhwh haḥesed wĕharbēh ʿimmô pĕdût — "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, for with the LORD is steadfast love, and with him is abundant redemption" — the psalm broadens from individual (vv1-6) to communal address. haḥesed (the steadfast love, with the definite article — this specific, well-known ḥesed) + harbēh pĕdût (abundant/plentiful redemption) — pĕdût (redemption by payment, from pādāh) with the adjective harbēh (much, abundant) — not merely one act of redemption but overflowing redemption. The contrast with v3: YHWH could keep accounts (mī yaʿămod = no one), but instead he offers abundant redemption.
130.8: wĕhûʾ yipdeh ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mikkōl ʿăwōnōtāyw — "he will redeem Israel from all its sins" — the comprehensive scope: kōl ʿăwōnōtāyw (all its iniquities) — the same word as v3's ʿăwōnôt (iniquities kept on record). What YHWH chose not to keep as a legal record (v3) he will instead redeem (pādāh, ransom, buy out). The movement of the psalm: from the depths of sin (v1) → impossible standing under strict accounting (v3) → forgiveness (v4) → waiting (vv5-6) → communal hope (v7) → comprehensive redemption (v8). The psalm is a miniature Gospel: sin, judgment, mercy, hope, redemption.

131.1: yhwh lōʾ-gābah libbî wĕlōʾ-rāmû ʿênay wĕlōʾ-hālaktî bigdōlôt ûbĕniplāʾôt mimmennî — "LORD, my heart is not proud; my eyes are not lifted up in arrogance; I do not walk in matters too great or too marvelous for me" — the three negations form a triple self-renunciation. gābah lēb (proud heart, lit. "the heart is high") — the opposite of the humble heart that fears the LORD (Prov 16:5). rāmû ʿênay (my eyes are lifted, i.e., haughty eyes) — the high look of arrogance (Ps 18:27). niplāʾôt (wonders, things too wonderful/marvelous for me) — the same word used for YHWH's wondrous works (Ps 111:4; Job 42:3); the psalmist deliberately steps back from presuming to comprehend what belongs to divine mystery.
131.2: ʾim-lōʾ šiwwîtî wĕdômamtî napšî kĕgāmûl ʿălê ʾimmô kĕgāmûl ʿālay napšî — "surely I have settled and silenced my soul; like a weaned child resting with its mother — like a weaned child, my soul is at rest within me" — šiwwîtî (I have stilled, made even, leveled — Piel of šāwāh) + dômamtî (I have silenced, made quiet — Hiphil of dāmam). kĕgāmûl ʿălê ʾimmô (like a weaned child with/upon his mother) — the key word is gāmûl (weaned child, from gāmal, to wean, also "to deal fully, to recompense"). A weaned child is no longer crying for milk — it has moved beyond the urgency of infancy into a resting relationship. The child rests not because it has received what it wanted but because the relationship itself is enough. This is the psalm's whole theology: the soul quieted not by receiving everything it desired but by settling into YHWH's presence without demand.
131.3: yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, now and forever" — the closing call to Israel echoes Ps 130:7 verbatim (the same yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh), creating a doublet between the two psalms. The individual soul's quieting (v2) becomes the model for Israel's collective posture: the nation is called to the same weaned-child rest in the LORD. mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from now and unto eternity) — the standard eschatological formula (Ps 113:2; 115:18; 121:8) extends this quiet trust into permanent reality.

132.1: zĕkōr-yhwh lĕdāwid ʾēt kol-ʿunnôtô — "O LORD, remember David — remember all his hardships" — zĕkōr (remember, Qal imperative) — divine remembering always entails divine action (cf. Gen 8:1, Exod 2:24). The prayer is for YHWH to act based on David's afflictions. ʿunnôtô (his afflictions, hardships, from ʿānāh, to be humbled, afflicted, bowed down) — the same root as the verb for Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exod 1:11-12). David's devotion to the ark, and his "affliction" in pursuing YHWH's house, is presented as the grounds for YHWH's ongoing faithfulness to the Davidic dynasty.
132.2: ʾăšer nišbaʿ layhwāh nādar lāʾăbîr yaʿăqōb — "how he made a solemn oath to the LORD and vowed to the Mighty One of Jacob" — ʾăbîr yaʿăqōb (the Mighty One of Jacob) — a rare divine title appearing in Gen 49:24 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph), Isa 49:26, 60:16 — it emphasizes YHWH's power/might (ʾabbîr = bull-strength, power). The title connects the Davidic covenant to the Abrahamic-Jacobite patriarchal promise.
132.3: ʾim-ʾābōʾ bĕʾōhel bêtî ʾim-ʾeʿeleh ʿal-ʿereś yĕṣûʿāy — "I will not enter my house, nor climb into my bed" — the vow is structured as two negative conditionals: ʾim-ʾābōʾ (if I enter... [I am forsworn]) — the conditional oath formula. David vowed not to allow himself the comfort of his own dwelling until he had secured a dwelling for YHWH. The historical reference is to David's desire to build the temple (2 Sam 7:1-3), though the ark-finding narrative in vv6 suggests a different specific moment (2 Sam 6 or 1 Chr 13).
132.4: ʾim-ʾettēn šĕnat lĕʿênāy lĕʿapʿappay tĕnûmāh — "I will allow no sleep to my eyes, no slumber to my eyelids" — ʿapʿappayim (eyelids, from ʿûp, to fly, flutter — the fluttering movement of eyelids) — a vivid anatomical term for the eyelids. The sleep deprivation vow (no sleep to eyes, no slumber to eyelids) reflects the urgency of the task. Ironically, YHWH is the one who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Ps 121:3-4) — David's vow imitates divine vigilance for the sake of YHWH's dwelling.
132.5: ʿad-ʾemṣāʾ māqôm layhwāh mišĕkānôt lāʾăbîr yaʿăqōb — "until I find a fitting place for the LORD — a dwelling for the Mighty One of Jacob" — māqôm (place, site) + mišĕkānôt (dwelling places, tabernacles, from šākan, to dwell, settle) — the repeated word-family behind the tabernacle/Shekinah concept. David's goal is a permanent māqôm for YHWH to settle (šākan), replacing the mobile tent of the wilderness. The title ʾăbîr yaʿăqōb (Mighty One of Jacob) appears for the second time in two verses — the structural echo stresses the Abrahamic-Davidic continuity.
132.6: hinnēh-šĕmaʿănûhā bĕʾeprātāh mĕṣāʾnûhā biśdê-yāʿar — "we heard of the ark at Ephrathah; we discovered it in the fields of Jaar" — Ephrathah = Bethlehem/its region (Gen 35:19; Mic 5:2) — the ark was associated with Kiriath-jearim (= Jaar, city of forests), where it had rested for 20 years after being returned from the Philistines (1 Sam 7:1-2). The "hearing" and "finding" suggest a community knowledge of where the ark was before the grand procession of 2 Sam 6. biśdê-yāʿar (in the fields of Jaar) — the wooded region around Kiriath-jearim.
132.7: nābôʾāh lĕmišĕkĕnôtāyw niştaḥăweh lahadōm raglāyw — "let us go to his dwelling place; let us bow down at his footstool" — mišĕkĕnôtāyw (his dwelling places) — YHWH's dwelling, using the šākan root again. hadōm raglāyw (his footstool) — the ark was described as YHWH's footstool (1 Chr 28:2; Ps 99:5), the place where the divine feet "rested" on earth. Prostration before the footstool is prostration before the King on the throne above.
132.8: qûmāh yhwh limnûḥātĕkā ʾattāh wĕʾărôn ʿuzzĕkā — "arise, O LORD, to your resting place — you and the ark that holds your power" — qûmāh (arise, Qal imperative of qûm) — the command to YHWH to arise echoes the ancient procession formula of Num 10:35 ("arise, O LORD, and let your enemies be scattered"). limnûḥātĕkā (to your resting place, from nûaḥ, to rest) — the Solomonic temple is envisioned as YHWH's permanent rest after the wandering years of the ark. ʾărôn ʿuzzĕkā (the ark of your strength/power) — not the ark of the covenant formula here but the ark of YHWH's ʿōz (might, power).
132.9: kōhănêkā yilbĕšû-ṣedeq waḥăsîdêkā yĕrannēnû — "clothe your priests with righteousness, and let your devoted people shout for joy" — yilbĕšû-ṣedeq (may they be clothed with righteousness) — righteousness as a garment (cf. Isa 61:10; Job 29:14). The priests clothed with righteousness corresponds to the ark clothed with divine power (v8). ḥăsîdêkā (your devoted ones, your faithful ones, from ḥesed) — those who embody the covenant loyalty that YHWH has shown them. yĕrannēnû (let them shout for joy, from rānan, to cry aloud, shout with joy).
132.10: baʿăbûr dāwid ʿabdĕkā ʾal-tāšēb pĕnê mĕšîḥĕkā — "for your servant David's sake, do not reject your anointed king" — baʿăbûr (for the sake of, because of) — the Davidic covenant as the ground of the prayer. mĕšîḥĕkā (your anointed, your Messiah) — the term that will carry the full weight of Messianic expectation; here it refers to the reigning Davidic king but within the covenant structure that points forward. The prayer not to "turn away the face" (lōʾ tāšēb pĕnê) of the anointed is a royal intercession.
132.11: nišbaʿ yhwh lĕdāwid ʾĕmet lōʾ-yāšûb mimmennāh mippĕrî biṭnĕkā ʾāšît lĕkissēʾ-lāk — "the LORD made a true sworn covenant with David — he will never go back on it: 'I will place one of your own descendants upon your throne'" — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness) — the oath is characterized by ʾĕmet; this is not a conditional covenant but a sworn unilateral commitment. lōʾ-yāšûb mimmennāh (he will not turn back from it) — the divine oath is irrevocable. mippĕrî biṭnĕkā (from the fruit of your body) — the physical Davidic line as the channel of the covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12-13; 16).
132.12: ʾim-yišmĕrû bānêkā bĕrîtî wĕʿēdōtî zō ʾălammdēm gam-bĕnêhem ʿădê-ʿad yēšĕbû lĕkissēʾ-lāk — "if your sons keep my covenant and the decrees I teach them, their sons too shall sit upon your throne forever" — this conditional element (vv11b-12) appears to place conditions on the Davidic covenant, in tension with v11a's unconditional promise. The tension runs throughout 2 Sam 7 and Ps 89: YHWH will not abandon the Davidic line even when individual kings sin, but the dynasty's practical flourishing depends on covenant faithfulness. The NT resolution: the Messiah who perfectly keeps the covenant enables the permanent throne.
132.13: kî-bāḥar yhwh bĕṣiyyôn ʾiwwāh lĕmôšāb lô — "the LORD has chosen Zion; he has made it his desired home" — bāḥar (to choose, elect) — the divine election of Zion parallels the divine election of David; both are unconditional acts of divine choice. ʾiwwāh lĕmôšāb lô (he desired it as a dwelling for himself) — ʾāwāh (to desire, long for, crave) — the language of passionate divine desire: YHWH longed for Zion as a home. This mutual longing (David for YHWH's ark, vv3-5; YHWH for Zion, v13) is the relational core of the Davidic covenant.
132.14: zōʾt-mĕnûḥātî ʿădê-ʿad pōh-ʾēšēb kî ʾiwtîhāh — "'This is my resting place forever; here I will live, for I have longed for it'" — YHWH speaks directly for the first time in the psalm. mĕnûḥātî ʿădê-ʿad (my resting place unto eternity) — the permanent rest after the wandering of the wilderness and the period of the judges. pōh-ʾēšēb (here I will dwell/sit) — the divine declaration of permanent habitation. The echo of v8's limnûḥātĕkā (to your resting place) closes the circle: what the congregation prayed for (v8), YHWH now declares as his own settled intention (v14).
132.15: ṣêdāh bārēk ʾăbārēk ʾebyônêhā ʾaśbîaʿ lāḥem — "I will richly bless her food supply; I will fill the poor with bread" — the divine promises flow from YHWH's choice of Zion. ṣêdāh bārēk ʾăbārēk (her provision I will bless abundantly, infinitive absolute construction) — the doubling of the verb (bārēk ʾăbārēk) for intensification: I will truly/abundantly bless. ʾebyônêhā ʾaśbîaʿ lāḥem (her poor I will satisfy with bread) — the social justice dimension of Zion's blessing: YHWH's presence ensures that the poor are fed (Ps 146:7; Luke 1:53 in the Magnificat).
132.16: wĕkōhănêhā ʾalbiš yĕšaʿ waḥăsîdêhā rannēn yĕrannēnû — "I will clothe her priests with salvation, and her devoted people will shout with joy" — the divine speech answers v9's prayer: there the congregation prayed for priests clothed with righteousness and faithful people shouting; here YHWH promises priests clothed with yēšaʿ (salvation, deliverance). The exchange: human petition (v9) → divine promise (v16). yēšaʿ (salvation) in place of v9's ṣedeq (righteousness) — salvation as the fulfillment of righteousness.
132.17: šām ʾaṣmîaḥ qeren lĕdāwid ʿāraktî nēr limsîḥî — "there I will make a shoot of strength grow for David; I have set a lamp ready for my anointed" — ʾaṣmîaḥ qeren (I will cause a horn to sprout/grow, Hiphil of ṣāmaḥ) — the pairing of qeren (horn = strength, power, dynasty) with ṣāmaḥ (to sprout, shoot up — agricultural growth verb) produces the "sprouting horn," a unique image. ṣemaḥ (a branch, shoot) will become the technical term for the Messiah in Jer 23:5, 33:15; Zech 3:8, 6:12 — the Branch. nēr limsîḥî (a lamp for my anointed) — the lamp burning in the dynasty; cf. 1 Kgs 11:36 ("that my servant David may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem"). Luke 1:69 ("a horn of salvation in the house of his servant David") and 2:32 ("a light for revelation to the Gentiles") echo these images.
132.18: ʾôyĕbāyw ʾalbîš bōšet wĕʿālāyw yāṣîṣ nizrô — "I will clothe his enemies in shame, but his crown will gleam/blossom upon his head" — the contrast of garments: enemies clothed in bōšet (shame, disgrace) :: the anointed wearing a gleaming crown. yāṣîṣ nizrô (his crown/diadem will gleam/blossom, from ṣîṣ, to shine, glisten, blossom) — the nēzer (crown, diadem, consecration, from nāzar, to consecrate/separate) is the priestly-royal crown. The verb ṣîṣ captures both shining (like polished gold) and blossoming (like a flower) — the Messianic promise closes Ps 132 with an image of radiant, flourishing kingship that will not fade.

133.1: hinnēh mah-ṭôb ûmah-nāʿîm šebet ʾaḥîm gam-yaḥad — "how good and how pleasant it is when brothers dwell together as one!" — hinnēh (behold, look!) — the exclamation points toward a visible reality. mah-ṭôb and mah-nāʿîm (how good! how pleasant!) — two different qualities of goodness: ṭôb (the basic goodness of creation, Gen 1:31) and nāʿîm (pleasantness, delight, aesthetic loveliness — the same root as Naomi). šebet ʾaḥîm gam-yaḥad (the sitting/dwelling of brothers also together) — gam-yaḥad (even/also together) — the gam (even, also) adds emphasis: brothers who are also one, not merely associated. The setting is the pilgrimage festival — the tribes assembled at Zion are "brothers" in the literal sense (one people) and in the symbolic sense (co-worshippers).
133.2: kaššemen haṭṭôb ʿal-hārōʾš yōrēd ʿal-hazzāqān zĕqan-ʾahărōn šeyōrēd ʿal-pî middôtāyw — "it is like costly anointing oil poured on the head, running down over Aaron's beard — flowing down to the edge of his robes" — šemen haṭṭôb (the good/precious oil) — the consecration oil of Exod 30:22-25 (myrrh, cinnamon, cane, cassia in olive oil) used at Aaron's anointing (Lev 8:12). The oil flows from the head down — unity flows from the top of the community downward, anointing the whole. pî middôtāyw (lit. "the mouth/opening of his garments" = the collar/hem of his robes) — the oil reaches to the very edge of the priestly garment. The anointing that consecrates the High Priest covers him completely — so unity covers the whole community.
133.3: kĕṭal-ḥermôn šeyyōrēd ʿal-harrê ṣiyyôn kî šām ṣiwwāh yhwh ʾet-habbĕrākāh ḥayyîm ʿad-hāʿôlām — "it is like the dew of Hermon descending on the mountains of Zion — for there the LORD has commanded the blessing: life that lasts forever" — ṭal-ḥermôn (dew of Hermon) — Mount Hermon (~2,814m) in the far north collects enormous quantities of moisture; its dew was proverbial for abundance and refreshment. Geographically, Hermon's dew cannot literally fall on Zion (300km south) — the simile is about the quality and abundance of refreshment, not geographic precision. kî šām ṣiwwāh yhwh ʾet-habbĕrākāh — "for there (šām, emphatic) YHWH commanded the blessing" — the place of unity is where divine blessing is decreed. ḥayyîm ʿad-hāʿôlām (life unto the age/forever) — the blessing is life itself, permanent life. The Songs of Ascents culminate in this vision: the pilgrims gathered at Zion in unity receive the blessing of life that endures.

147.1: hallĕlû-yāh kî ṭôb zammĕrāh ʾĕlōhênû kî nāʿîm nāweh tĕhillāh — "Praise the LORD! For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and praise is fitting." Three motivation-words stack in v1: ṭôb (good, morally and aesthetically excellent), nāʿîm (pleasant, delightful — the root of the name Naomi, Ruth 1:20), and nāweh (comely, beautiful, fitting — used of a bride's beauty in Isa 52:7; here applied to the act of praise itself). The word-order in Hebrew places the praise-act first and then unpacks its qualities; the invitation is both aesthetic and theological: praise is fitting because it corresponds to who God is.
147.2: bônēh yĕrûšālayim yhwh niddĕḥê yiśrāʾēl yĕkannēs — "the LORD builds up Jerusalem; he gathers together the outcasts of Israel." The active participle bônēh (building, Qal participle from bānāh) presents the act as ongoing, not completed — YHWH is the perpetual builder of Zion. niddĕḥê (the outcasts/scattered ones, Niphal participle of nādaḥ, to drive away, banish) — the participle carries the social reality of exile: people forcibly pushed to the margins. yĕkannēs (gathers together, Piel imperfect of kānaś) — the Piel intensive marks the thoroughness of the gathering; what was dispersed is completely assembled. Post-exilic Jerusalem reads this as the return from Babylon (Isa 56:8; Ezek 34:13).
147.3: hārōpēʾ lišbûrê lēb ûmĕḥabbēš lĕʿaṣṣĕbôtām — "he heals the brokenhearted and bandages their wounds." Two paired verbs: rāpāʾ (heal, Qal participle — the root of Raphael and rĕpāʾîm; the medical metaphor of divine therapy) and ḥābāš (bind up/bandage wounds, Piel participle — the verb used for binding a wound in Isa 1:6 and for YHWH binding the broken of Isa 61:1). šibūrê lēb (brokenhearted, literally "broken of heart" — šābar in Niphal participle + lēb) — the same construction appears in Isa 61:1, the Servant's commission. The juxtaposition of v2 (gathering scattered exiles) with v3 (healing inner wounds) is deliberate: restoration is both political and personal.
147.4: môneh mispār lakkôkābîm lĕkullām šēmôt yiqrāʾ — "he counts the stars by number; he calls them all by name." mānāh (counts, Qal participle — used for census-taking and rationing; the root of mĕnāʾēl, God has numbered) + mispār (number, count) — the combination emphasizes enumeration. The tension with v4b is deliberate: the stars are innumerable (Gen 15:5, where YHWH challenged Abraham to count them), yet YHWH assigns each one a number. lĕkullām šēmôt yiqrāʾ (to all of them he calls names) — naming as an act of sovereignty and intimate knowledge; in ANE thought, to name something was to define its nature and own it (Gen 1:5, 8, 10; 2:19–20). The God who knows the name of every star also knows the wound of every broken heart (v3) — the psalm's central theological paradox: cosmic omniscience and pastoral intimacy are the same attribute.
147.5: gādôl ʾădōnênû wĕrabbāh kōaḥ lîtĕbûnātô ʾên mispār — "great is our Lord and abundant in power; his understanding has no number/limit." gādôl (great) + rabbāh kōaḥ (abundant/great power) are familiar divine epithets, but the verse's force is in the final phrase: lîtĕbûnātô ʾên mispār — "of his understanding there is no number." tĕbûnāh (understanding, discernment, from bîn) is the faculty of deep insight — the same noun as in Prov 3:19 ("by understanding he established the heavens"). The echo of mispār (number) from v4 is the key: YHWH counts the stars' number (mispār), yet his own understanding is without number (ʾên mispār). The counter-who-cannot-be-counted.
147.6: mĕʿôdēd ʿănāwîm yhwh mašpîl rĕšāʿîm ʿădê-āreṣ — "the LORD lifts up the humble; he brings the wicked down to the ground." ʿōdēd (lifts up, sustains — Piel participle from ʿûd, to support, restore, refresh; rare form) — the sustained support of those who are bent down. ʿănāwîm (the humble/afflicted/poor — from ʿānāh, to be bowed down, afflicted; the class of those who are socially powerless and have only YHWH as their resource — same word as Ps 37:11 "the meek will inherit the earth" and Num 12:3 "Moses was very meek"). mašpîl rĕšāʿîm ʿădê-āreṣ (casts the wicked to the earth/ground, Hiphil of šāpal) — the reversal pattern that runs through Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:7–8), the Magnificat (Luke 1:52), and the beatitudes.
147.7: ʿănû layhwh bĕtôdāh zammĕrû lēʾlōhênû bĕkinnôr — "sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make music to our God with the lyre." The shift from description (vv 1–6) to imperative command marks a new movement. ʿānāh (respond, answer in song — Qal imperative; the verb used in the antiphonal singing of Exod 15:21 and 1 Sam 18:7) with tôdāh (thanksgiving, the thank-offering — from yādāh, to confess/praise; tôdāh refers both to the spoken thanks and the sacrificial offering that accompanies it, Lev 7:12). zāmar (make music on a stringed instrument — Piel imperative, the root of mizmôr = psalm; consistently used for instrumental music as distinct from vocal praise). kinnôr (lyre/harp — the instrument of David and the Levitical choir).
147.8: hamĕkassēh šāmayim bĕʿābîm hammēkîn lāʾāreṣ māṭār hammāṣmîaḥ hārîm ḥāṣîr — "he covers the sky with clouds, prepares rain for the earth, makes grass grow on the hills." Three Hiphil participles in sequence, each presenting YHWH as the ongoing agent of natural provision: kāsāh (covers, Piel), kûn (prepares/establishes, Hiphil), ṣāmaḥ (causes to grow, Hiphil). The sequence — clouds → rain → grass — traces the supply chain of agricultural life. The theological force is anti-Baʿal polemic: the Canaanite storm-god Baʿal was credited with sending rain; the psalm insists it is YHWH who prepares (actively, purposefully) rain for the earth. ḥāṣîr (grass/hay/green vegetation) on the mountains points to vv 8–9's chain: vegetation feeds animals; YHWH is the fountainhead of the entire food system.
147.9: nôtēn libhēmāh laḥmāh libĕnê ʿōrēb ʾăšer yiqrāʾû — "he gives to the beast its food, and to the young ravens when they call." bĕhēmāh (beast — large quadruped, domestic or wild; the general animal class) — YHWH provides food for animals in general before the ravens are specified. bĕnê ʿōrēb (sons of the raven, young ravens) — ravens were classified as unclean birds under Levitical law (Lev 11:15; Deut 14:14) and yet YHWH feeds their young. ʾăšer yiqrāʾû (who call/cry out) — the ravens' cries are presented as a form of petition that YHWH hears and answers; Job 38:41 uses the same image ("who provides for the raven its prey, when its young ones cry to God?"). Jesus echoes this in Luke 12:24 ("consider the ravens; they do not sow or reap... and yet God feeds them").
147.10: lōʾ bigĕbûrat hassûs yĕḥpāṣ lōʾ bĕšôqê hāʾîš yirṣeh — "he does not delight in the strength of the horse, nor take pleasure in the legs of a man." The verse is structured as a double negative: lōʾ... yĕḥpāṣ (does not delight in) / lōʾ... yirṣeh (does not take pleasure in). gĕbûrat hassûs (strength/might of the horse) — in the ANE, the horse was the decisive weapon of war; Egyptian and Assyrian armies were feared for their cavalry. šôqê hāʾîš (legs/thighs of a man) — the legs of an infantryman, his physical power. Both are military metaphors: YHWH is not impressed by conventional military strength (Ps 20:7; Isa 31:1; Deut 17:16 — the king must not multiply horses). Human military power is irrelevant to divine favor.
147.11: rôṣeh yhwh ʾet-yĕrēʾāyw ʾet-hamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô — "the LORD takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love." The contrast with v10 is sharp: YHWH's rāṣāh (pleasure, favorable regard) is not given to military power but to a spiritual disposition. yĕrēʾāyw (those who fear him — Qal participle plural of yārēʾ; the fundamental posture of covenantal reverence that combines awe, obedience, and trust). hamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô (those who hope/wait in his steadfast love) — yāḥal (to wait with expectant hope, Piel participle) + ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love — the central Hebrew theological term for YHWH's faithful, committed love within the covenant relationship). The combination is the Psalter's definition of godly life: reverential fear of YHWH coupled with hopeful waiting on his covenant faithfulness.
147.12: šabbĕḥî yĕrûšālayim ʾet-yhwh hallĕlî ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn — "praise the LORD, O Jerusalem! Praise your God, O Zion!" The second half of the psalm (vv 12–20) begins with a direct address to Jerusalem/Zion — a shift from the general praise of vv 1–11. šābaḥ (praise, commend, laud — Piel imperative; slightly distinct from hālal; šābaḥ tends toward public proclamation and commendation of another's excellence). The personification of Jerusalem as a praising subject is characteristic of the Songs of Zion (Ps 48; 87; Isa 40:9; 52:1). The LXX counts this as the beginning of a new psalm (LXX Psalm 147), suggesting early Jewish recognition of its distinct character.
147.13: kî ḥizzaq bĕrîḥê šĕʿārayik bērēk bānayik bĕqirbēk — "for he has reinforced the bars of your gates and blessed your children within you." ḥizzaq (reinforced/strengthened — Piel perfect of ḥāzaq) the bĕrîḥîm (crossbars/locking bars) of the šĕʿārîm (gates) — the practical image of Nehemiah's rebuilding: the gate-bars that lock the city against siege or attack (Neh 3:3, 6, 13; 7:3). The bars of the gates represent the entire defensive infrastructure of restored Jerusalem. The verse pairs external security (bars of gates) with internal blessing (children within) — the city restored to both protection and fertility.
147.14: haśśām-gĕbûlēk šālôm ḥēleb ḥiṭṭîm yaśbîʿēk — "he makes peace your border, and satisfies you with the finest wheat." šālôm as the content of borders — the territory under YHWH's covenant protection is not defined merely by geography but by wholeness and well-being. ḥēleb ḥiṭṭîm (finest/fat of wheat) — ḥēleb (fat, the richest portion; same word as the fat reserved for sacrifice in Lev 3:16) applied to wheat = the choicest grain. The fat of wheat as temple provision (Deut 32:14 refers to Israel being fed "the finest of wheat"; Ps 81:16) — the restored community receives sacrificial-quality provision for ordinary sustenance.
147.15: haššōlēaḥ ʾimrātô āreṣ ʿad-mĕhērāh yārûṣ dĕbārô — "he sends his command/word to the earth; his word runs swiftly." ʾimrāh (word/command — from ʾāmar, to say; the specific form that emphasizes authoritative speech) + dābār (word, matter, thing — the comprehensive divine utterance) appear together to emphasize the complete authority of YHWH's speech. yārûṣ (runs, Qal imperfect of rûṣ) — the personification of the divine word as a swift messenger racing across the earth. Vv 15–18 then describe the divine word's work in nature: it commands snow, frost, hail, cold, and then the thaw. This sequence creates the theological context for v19: the same word that commands weather is the word that commands Israel — Torah and cosmos share one divine speech-act.
147.16: hannōtēn šeleg kaṣṣāmer kĕpôr kāʾēper yĕpazzēr — "he gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes." Two similes: šeleg (snow) compared to ṣemer (wool) — the white, fluffy covering of both; and kĕpôr (hoarfrost, frost crystals) compared to ʾēper (ash/ashes) — the grayish, powdery scatter of both. yĕpazzēr (scatters, Piel imperfect of pāzar — to scatter, spread abroad; the Piel suggests thoroughness of the scattering). The poetic genius is in the domestic images: wool (from the flock) and ash (from the hearth) make cosmic phenomena familiar; YHWH's winter provision is as natural as household goods.
