147.17: mašlîk qārāḥô kĕpittîm lipnê qārātô mî yaʿămōd — "he hurls down his ice like morsels/crumbs; who can stand before his cold?" qeraḥ (ice, hailstones — solid frozen water; can refer to crystal-clear ice from its appearance) + pittîm (morsels, pieces, crumbs of bread — from pat, a morsel) — hailstones scattered like bread crumbs; the image is both vivid (size and quantity) and carries a trace of divine playfulness. mî yaʿămōd (who can stand/withstand?) — a rhetorical question expecting the answer "no one"; the divine cold is an overwhelming force. The question echoes Nah 1:6 ("who can stand before his indignation?") and Mal 3:2 ("who can endure the day of his coming?") — the divine power in nature foreshadows eschatological power.
147.18: yišlaḥ dĕbārô wĕyamsēm yašsēb rûḥô yizzĕlû-māyim — "he sends his word and melts them; he makes his wind blow and the waters flow." The structural echo is precise: v15 sends (šālaḥ) his word to earth; here v18 sends (šālaḥ) his word to melt the ice. The same divine speech-act that produced the cold (vv 16–17) now dissolves it. wĕyamsēm (melts them, Hiphil — causative of māsas, to melt, dissolve; the subject is the divine word melting the snow and frost). yašsēb rûḥô (he makes his wind blow — causes to blow, Hiphil of nāšab) — the warm wind (the sirocco/east wind that dries and thaws in the Levant); the same rûaḥ (wind/spirit) that swept over the waters at creation (Gen 1:2). yizzĕlû-māyim (the waters flow — Qal imperfect, the resulting thaw and runoff).
147.19: maggid dĕbārô lĕyaʿăqōb ḥuqqāyw ûmišpāṭāyw lĕyiśrāʾēl — "he declares his word to Jacob, his statutes and ordinances to Israel." The transition from cosmic dābār (vv 15–18) to covenantal dābār is the theological climax. maggid (declares/makes known — Hiphil participle of nāgad, to tell, report, declare) emphasizes the communicative act: YHWH does not merely possess his word but speaks it to Jacob. ḥuqqāyw (his statutes — from ḥāqaq, to engrave, decree; specific covenant terms) + mišpāṭāyw (his judgments/ordinances — the case-law of covenant governance). yiśrāʾēl (Israel) + yaʿăqōb (Jacob) — the covenant double-name; the two names together indicate the whole people under both their ancestral name (Jacob) and their covenant-struggle name (Israel).
147.20: lōʾ-ʿāśāh kēn lĕkol-gôy ûmišpāṭîm bal-yĕdāʿûm hallĕlû-yāh — "he has not done this for any other nation; they do not know his ordinances. Praise the LORD!" The exclusivity of the covenant-gift is stated plainly and without apology: lōʾ-ʿāśāh kēn (he has not done so) lĕkol-gôy (for any nation — the word gôy, nation, is used for non-Israelite peoples; here it is all-inclusive: no nation has received what Israel received). ûmišpāṭîm bal-yĕdāʿûm (they do not know his judgments — bal is the emphatic negation; the nations have simply not been given the Torah). This is a statement of historical election, not an assertion of the nations' moral worthlessness; Paul takes up the same theme in Rom 9:4–5 and 3:1–2 ("what advantage has the Jew? Much in every way; first, they were entrusted with the oracles of God"). hallĕlû-yāh — the doxological close.

148.1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh min-haššāmayim hallĕlûhû bammĕrômîm — "Praise the LORD! Praise the LORD from the heavens; praise him in the heights above!" The opening triple use of hallĕlû (praise — Piel imperative plural of hālal; the root of Hallel and Hallelujah) immediately establishes the cosmic register. min-haššāmayim (from the heavens) — the praise is summoned from above; heaven is not merely where God dwells but from where praise is called down. bammĕrômîm (in the heights above — from rûm, to be high; the superlative elevation). The structure of the psalm (vv 1–6 heaven; vv 7–12 earth; vv 13–14 climax) creates a praise that moves from the highest point of creation downward to encompass everything.
148.2: hallĕlûhû kol-mallāʾkāyw hallĕlûhû kol-ṣĕbāʾōtāyw — "Praise him, all his angels! Praise him, all his hosts/heavenly armies!" mallāʾkāyw (his angels/messengers — from mālāʾk, messenger, envoy; the divine attendants who serve in the heavenly court). ṣĕbāʾōtāyw (his hosts/armies — ṣābāʾ, host or army; the term for the divine warrior's forces, as in the divine title YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt, "LORD of hosts"). The angelic host is called to praise before the natural order; the heavenly court leads the cosmic liturgy. Ps 103:20–21 uses the same pairing: "bless the LORD, all his angels... all his hosts, his ministers."
148.3: hallĕlûhû šemeš wĕyārēaḥ hallĕlûhû kol-kôkĕbê ʾôr — "Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, all you shining stars!" The luminaries of Genesis 1:14–18 — šemeš (sun, the great light), yārēaḥ (moon, the lesser light) — are called to praise. kôkĕbê ʾôr (stars of light/shining stars) — all the lesser luminaries. The sun and moon were among the most prominent objects of ANE worship (Shamash the sun-god; Sin the moon-god in Mesopotamia; Shemesh in Canaan). Ps 148's call to these astral bodies to praise YHWH is an implicit polemic: the objects other nations worship are themselves worshippers, summoned to praise the one who made them.
148.4: hallĕlûhû šĕmê haššāmayim wĕhammayim ʾăšer mēʿal haššāmayim — "Praise him, you highest heavens, and you waters above the heavens!" šĕmê haššāmayim (heavens of the heavens, the superlative — literally "skies of the skies") — the divine abode beyond the created heavens (Deut 10:14; 1 Kgs 8:27; Ps 115:16). hammayim ʾăšer mēʿal haššāmayim (the waters above the heavens) — the cosmological upper waters of Gen 1:7 that are above the rāqîaʿ (firmament). The psalm takes the full ANE cosmological model and calls every tier of it to praise. The waters that were separated at creation (Gen 1:6–7) now join in the praise-order rather than threatening the ordered world.
148.5: yĕhallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh kî hûʾ ṣiwwāh wĕnibrāʾû — "let them praise the name of the LORD, for he commanded and they were created." ṣiwwāh (commanded — Piel perfect of ṣāwāh; the decisive divine command) + nibrāʾû (and they were created — Niphal perfect of bārāʾ, the distinctive creation verb used in Gen 1:1). The creation formula: the word and the act are simultaneous. This is the deepest possible basis for praise: the praising creatures exist because of the divine command. The kî (for/because) clause makes this the warrant for all cosmic praise — every creature praises because it was commanded into existence.
148.6: wayyaʿămîdēm lāʿad lĕʿôlām ḥōq-nātan wĕlōʾ yaʿăbôr — "he has established them forever and ever; he has issued a decree that cannot be transgressed." wayyaʿămîdēm (he established/stationed them — Hiphil wayyiqtol of ʿāmad, to stand; the causative: he made them stand permanently). lāʿad lĕʿôlām (forever and ever — the doubled temporal expression for eternity). ḥōq (decree/statute — the fixed law governing the regular behavior of natural phenomena; the same word used in Jer 5:22 where YHWH gives the sea its boundary-decree). wĕlōʾ yaʿăbôr (and it will not pass away/be transgressed — ʿābar, to pass over, exceed, transgress). The regularity of the seasons, the fixed paths of the stars — all governed by YHWH's unchangeable decree.
148.7: hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh min-hāʾāreṣ tannînîm wĕkol-tĕhōmôt — "Praise the LORD from the earth, you sea monsters and all ocean depths!" The shift to the earth-section (vv 7–12) begins with the most dramatic reversal: tannînîm (sea dragons/monsters — plural of tannîn; the term used for the chaos-creatures in Ps 74:13–14 ["You divided the sea by your might; you broke the heads of the sea monsters"] and Job 7:12). In ANE mythology these were the primordial chaos beings YHWH conquered at creation (Leviathan, Tiamat). Here they are called to praise — the chaos-monsters are worshippers, not threats. tĕhōmôt (ocean deeps, from tĕhôm — the deep of Gen 1:2, cognate with Akkadian Tiamat) — the depths called to praise alongside the monsters they harbor.
148.8: ʾēš ûbārād šeleg wĕqîṭôr rûaḥ sĕʿārāh ʿōśāh dĕbārô — "fire and hail, snow and mist, stormy wind fulfilling his word." Four elements of violent weather: ʾēš (fire — lightning), bārād (hail — the same term in the plagues of Exod 9:18–26), šeleg (snow), qîṭôr (mist/vapor/smoke — used of the smoke of incense, Gen 19:28). The unifying phrase is ʿōśāh dĕbārô (fulfilling/doing his word — participle of ʿāśāh): the violent weather phenomena are not chaos but servants executing YHWH's commands. The same logic as the divine warrior's weapons: natural catastrophe as divine instrument (cf. Ps 147:15–18 where the divine word commands weather).
148.9: hehārîm wĕkol-gĕbāʿôt ʿêṣ pĕrî wĕkol-ʾărazîm — "mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars." The geographical and botanical inventory: hārîm (mountains, the great elevations) + gĕbāʿôt (hills, the lesser heights) — the entire relief of the land. ʿêṣ pĕrî (fruit tree — literally "tree of fruit," the cultivated orchard) + ʾărazîm (cedars — the ʾerez, the great wild tree of Lebanon, symbol of height and majesty, Ps 92:12; 104:16). The cultivated and wild botanical realms both praise.
148.10: haḥayyāh wĕkol-bĕhēmāh remeś wĕṣippôr kānāp — "wild beasts and all livestock, creeping things and flying birds." The standard zoological categories of the Hebrew world: ḥayyāh (wild beast, living thing — the untamed animals of the field) + bĕhēmāh (livestock/domestic animals) + remes (creeping thing — the crawling creatures of the earth, Gen 1:24) + ṣippôr kānāp (bird of wing — a compound intensifying the flight characteristic; every winged flier). The four animal categories match the creation taxonomy of Gen 1:20–25.
148.11: malkê-ʾereṣ wĕkol-lĕʾummîm śārîm wĕkol-šōpĕṭê āreṣ — "kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all judges of the earth." The political order is called to praise. malkê-ʾereṣ (kings of the earth) — the highest human authority; lĕʾummîm (peoples, nations — plural of lĕʾom, a people-group; distinct from gôy in that lĕʾom emphasizes the communal solidarity). śārîm (princes, officials — the secondary power-holders) + šōpĕṭîm (judges — the judicial authority). Every tier of human governance is subsumed under the praise of YHWH; the same kings who issue decrees are themselves called to declare the greatness of the one who issues cosmic decrees (v6).
148.12: bĕḥûrîm wĕgam-bĕtûlôt zĕqēnîm ʿim-nĕʿārîm — "young men and also young women together, old men with children." The age spectrum of humanity: bĕḥûrîm (young men — the vigorous, marriageable males) + bĕtûlôt (young women/virgins — from bĕtûlāh, a young woman of marriageable age) + zĕqēnîm (old men/elders — the socially senior) + nĕʿārîm (children/young ones — the socially junior). The gam (also, even) before the women marks an inclusiveness: not just men but also women. This age-and-gender inclusiveness completes the human catalog before the climactic verses.
148.13: yĕhallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh kî-niśgāb šĕmô lĕbaddô hôdô ʿal-ʾereṣ wĕšāmāyim — "let them praise the name of the LORD, for his name alone is exalted; his splendor is above earth and heaven." niśgāb šĕmô (his name is exalted/set on high — Niphal of śāgab, to be inaccessibly high, beyond reach) — the name that is beyond the reach of any competing name. lĕbaddô (alone, by himself, exclusively — from bādad, to be separate/isolated; the emphasis on YHWH's exclusive majesty). hôdô (his splendor/majesty — from hôd, the majestic presence; related to the visible glory) ʿal-ʾereṣ wĕšāmāyim (above earth and heaven) — a merism: the entire created order is encompassed and transcended by YHWH's glory.
148.14: wayyārem qeren lĕʿammô tĕhillāh lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw libĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʿam-qĕrōbô hallĕlû-yāh — "he has raised high the horn of his people, the praise of all his faithful ones, of the children of Israel, the people near to him. Praise the LORD!" wayyārem qeren (he raised/lifted the horn — Hiphil wayyiqtol of rûm, to raise; qeren, horn — the horn as the symbol of strength and honor; to have one's horn raised = to be vindicated, exalted, given honor; 1 Sam 2:10; Ps 89:17, 24; 132:17). The horn raised for ʿammô (his people) is the covenant-community's vindication, not just an individual's. ḥăsîdāyw (his devoted/faithful ones — from ḥesed; those who embody covenant loyalty, the same community as in Ps 149:1, 5, 9). ʿam-qĕrōbô (the people near to him — from qārab, to draw near; the priestly term for approaching YHWH in the sanctuary, here describing Israel's covenantal intimacy with YHWH). The closing hallĕlû-yāh seals the cosmic praise with the covenant community's own doxological word.

149.1: hallĕlû-yāh šîrû layhwh šîr ḥādāš tĕhillātô biqhal ḥăsîdîm — "Praise the LORD! Sing to the LORD a new song; his praise in the assembly of the faithful." šîr ḥādāš (new song — the phrase appears at Ps 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9; 14:3; always in response to a new or decisive act of divine salvation). The newness is not aesthetic novelty but theological freshness: each new divine act of redemption requires a new praise-response. biqhal ḥăsîdîm (in the assembly of the faithful/devoted — from qāhal, the assembled community of Israel, and ḥăsîdîm, those characterized by ḥesed, covenant loyalty). The ḥăsîdîm are this psalm's central subject — they appear in vv 1, 5, and 9, forming an inclusio.
149.2: yiśmaḥ yiśrāʾēl bĕʿōśāyw bĕnê-ṣiyyôn yāgîlû bĕmalkām — "let Israel rejoice in their Maker; let the people of Zion be joyful in their King." ʿōśāyw (their Maker — Qal active participle of ʿāśāh, to make/do; plural of majesty construction — not "their makers" but the one Maker described by a plural of excellence). yiśmaḥ (rejoice — Qal jussive of śāmaḥ) + yāgîlû (be joyful — Qal jussive of gîl, to exult, spin with joy; a more exuberant word than śāmaḥ). malkām (their King) — YHWH as the divine King of Zion; the title connects the rejoicing community to the royal enthronement psalms (Ps 93; 95–99).
149.3: yĕhallĕlû šĕmô bĕmaḥôl bĕtōp wĕkinnôr yĕzammĕrû-lô — "let them praise his name with dancing; let them make music to him with tambourine and lyre." māḥôl (dance — the round dance, celebratory movement; used of Miriam's dance in Exod 15:20 and David's before the ark in 2 Sam 6:14). tōp (tambourine/hand-drum — the instrument Miriam took up after the Reed Sea, Exod 15:20) + kinnôr (lyre/harp — David's instrument). The echo of the Exodus praise-instruments situates Ps 149's praise within the tradition of Israel's great victory celebrations. The worship is fully embodied: voice (praise), movement (dance), and multiple instruments.
149.4: kî-rōṣeh yhwh bĕʿammô yĕpāʾēr ʿănāwîm bîšûʿāh — "for the LORD takes pleasure in his people; he adorns the humble with salvation." rāṣāh (takes pleasure/delight in — Qal imperfect; the same verb as Ps 147:11 where YHWH takes pleasure in those who fear him and hope in his ḥesed; here the object is simply ʿammô, his people). yĕpāʾēr (adorns/beautifies/glorifies — Piel imperfect of pāʾar, to beautify, adorn, glorify; the Piel of intensive beautification) the ʿănāwîm (humble/poor/afflicted — the same class as Ps 147:6) with yĕšûʿāh (salvation, victory — the root of the name Jesus/Yĕšûaʿ). Salvation as an adornment: the humble are clothed in divine victory, which functions as their honor-garment in a society where honor was everything.
149.5: yĕʿallzû ḥăsîdîm bĕkābôd yĕrannĕnû ʿal-miškĕbôtām — "let the faithful exult in glory; let them sing aloud even on their beds." ʿālaz (exult — Qal imperfect of ʿālaz, to rejoice, be jubilant; a strong emotional word for exuberant joy). bĕkābôd (in glory — either YHWH's glory as the ground of their exultation, or the glory that accrues to the ḥăsîdîm through the divine vindication of v4). yĕrannĕnû (sing aloud with joy — Piel imperfect of rānan, to ring out in joy, to shout aloud; the root of rinnāh, the ringing cry of praise) ʿal-miškĕbôtām (on their beds) — even in sleep-settings, the praise continues; possibly the night-vigil of those who meditate on YHWH in the night watches (Ps 63:6; 119:55). The midnight praise is as genuine as the daylight assembly.
149.6: rômmôt ʾēl bigĕrônām wĕḥereb pîpiyyôt bĕyādām — "with the high praises of God in their throats and a double-edged sword in their hands." The psalm's most striking image: two paired instruments of the ḥăsîdîm. rômmôt ʾēl (high praises of God — plural of rômāh, high things, exaltations; from rûm, to be high; these are praises that exalt God to his proper height). bigĕrônām (in their throats/gullets — from gārôn, the throat as the channel of vocal sound; Ps 115:7 uses it for throats that do not speak). ḥereb pîpiyyôt (double-edged sword — literally "sword of mouths"; peh = mouth; a sword with two mouths/edges, both cutting). The same image appears in Rev 19:15 (the rider's sword from his mouth) and Heb 4:12 (the word of God as a two-edged sword). The ḥăsîdîm are simultaneously worshippers and warriors — their praise and their action both serve YHWH's purposes.
149.7: laʿăśôt nĕqāmāh baggôyim tôkĕḥôt bĕlĕʾummîm — "to execute vengeance on the nations and punishment on the peoples." nĕqāmāh (vengeance — from nāqam, to avenge; YHWH's vengeance is judicial rather than personal; the divine warrior's act of setting right what was wronged). The ḥăsîdîm act as agents of this divine judicial action — not as initiators of personal revenge but as instruments through whom YHWH executes judgment. tôkĕḥôt (punishments/corrections — from yākaḥ, to reprove, correct, decide; the judicial sense of warranted correction, not arbitrary violence). The scope — nations and peoples — suggests an eschatological or historical judgment rather than an individual grievance.
149.8: lĕʾsōr malkêhem bĕziqîm wĕnikabbĕdêhem bĕkablê barzel — "to bind their kings in chains and their nobles in iron fetters." ʾāsar (bind — Qal infinitive construct with lĕ; the binding of captives as the completion of military victory, Josh 10:24; Judg 1:7). ziqîm (chains — restraints for prisoners) + kablê barzel (iron fetters — from kebesel, fetter; the iron chains of serious imprisonment, Ps 107:10, 14). nikabbĕdêhem (their nobles/honored ones — Niphal participle of kābad, to be heavy/honored; those who have social weight and dignity). The binding of kings and nobles in iron chains is the imagery of complete military conquest; the enemies who exalted themselves are brought low.
149.9: laʿăśôt bāhem mišpāṭ kātûb hādār hûʾ lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw hallĕlû-yāh — "to execute on them the judgment that is written — this is the honor/glory of all his faithful ones. Praise the LORD!" mišpāṭ kātûb (the judgment that is written — the decree already recorded in Scripture; most naturally Deut 28–32, the covenant curses that would come upon the nations that oppose YHWH's people, or more broadly the Torah's prophetic vision of divine justice). hādār hûʾ lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw (this is the honor/splendor for all his faithful ones — hādār, the majestic honor/beauty, same word as YHWH's hôd wĕhādār in Ps 104:1; 111:3). The privilege of executing YHWH's written judgment is itself the hādār of the ḥăsîdîm — their dignity comes from participating in divine justice, not from inherent status. hallĕlû-yāh — the doxological close that mirrors v1's opening.

150.1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlû-ʾēl bĕqodšô hallĕlûhû birqîaʿ ʿuzzô — "Praise the LORD! Praise God in his sanctuary; praise him in the vault of his power!" The Grand Doxology of the Psalter opens with three instances of hallĕlû in one verse. bĕqodšô (in his sanctuary/holiness — either the earthly temple in Jerusalem or the heavenly sanctuary where YHWH dwells; Ps 11:4 places YHWH "in his holy temple" while also "in heaven"). birqîaʿ ʿuzzô (in the firmament/vault of his power — rāqîaʿ from rāqaʿ, to beat out, spread out; the expanse of Gen 1:6–8 stretched out as the created sky; here it is described as ʿuzzô, his power, making the cosmic vault YHWH's display of might). The two locations — sanctuary and firmament — locate praise in both the earthly liturgy and the cosmic order.
150.2: hallĕlûhû bigĕbûrōtāyw hallĕlûhû kĕrōb gūdlô — "praise him for his mighty deeds; praise him according to the fullness of his greatness." bigĕbûrōtāyw (for/in his mighty deeds — from gĕbûrāh, might, heroic act; plural emphasizes the multiplicity of YHWH's powerful interventions in history: creation, Exodus, conquest, restoration). kĕrōb gūdlô (according to the fullness/abundance of his greatness — rōb from rābab, to be great in number; the overwhelming vastness of YHWH's gōdel, greatness). The praise is to be proportionate to its object: immeasurable greatness warrants immeasurable praise.
150.3: hallĕlûhû bĕteqaʿ šôpār hallĕlûhû bĕnebel wĕkinnôr — "praise him with the blast of the trumpet; praise him with harp and lyre." šôpār (ram's horn — the oldest and most significant Israelite wind instrument; used to signal the Sinai theophany, Exod 19:13, 16, 19; to assemble the covenant community, Num 10:10; to announce the Jubilee year, Lev 25:9; and to mark warfare and divine arrival, Josh 6). The šôpār begins the instrumental catalog with the instrument most associated with covenant-assembly and divine presence. nebel (harp/lute — a large-bodied stringed instrument, plucked; associated with the prophets, 1 Sam 10:5) + kinnôr (lyre — David's smaller, portable instrument; the instrument of poetry and the Levitical choir).
150.4: hallĕlûhû bĕtōp ûmāḥôl hallĕlûhû bĕminnîm wĕʿûgāb — "praise him with tambourine and dancing; praise him with strings and flute." tōp (tambourine/hand-drum — the membrane percussion instrument) + māḥôl (dance — the praise-dance; uniquely here dance is listed alongside instruments as a mode of praise). minnîm (strings — from mîn, a kind or class; the plural refers to stringed instruments as a class, not a specific instrument). ʿûgāb (flute/pipe — a wind instrument, possibly a flute or Pan-pipe; the word appears in Gen 4:21 as an instrument of Jubal, "the father of all who play the flute"; rare term, suggesting a specific instrument distinct from the šôpār). The verse uniquely includes dance as a praise-medium alongside instruments, incorporating the full-body worship of Ps 149:3.
150.5: hallĕlûhû bĕṣilṣĕlê šāmaʿ hallĕlûhû bĕṣilṣĕlê tĕrûʿāh — "praise him with resounding cymbals; praise him with clashing cymbals." Two types of cymbals receive two verses: ṣilṣĕlê šāmaʿ (cymbals of sound/hearing — cymbals that make their sound heard; the šāmaʿ form may indicate a sustained ring) and ṣilṣĕlê tĕrûʿāh (cymbals of the shout/alarm — tĕrûʿāh is the great shout used in warfare, at covenant renewal, and at the sound of the ram's horn, Num 10:5–6; Ps 89:16; Ezra 3:11–13). ṣilṣal is an onomatopoeic word imitating the sound of clashing metal — the word itself rings out. The climactic position of cymbals in the catalog is fitting: they are the loudest percussion, bringing the instrumental praise to its sonic apex before the verbal conclusion of v6.
150.6: kōl hanĕšāmāh tĕhallēl yāh hallĕlû-yāh — "let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise the LORD!" The most expansive possible invitation: kōl hanĕšāmāh (everything that has breath — kōl, all/every; nĕšāmāh, breath, the divine breath of life). nĕšāmāh is the word used in Gen 2:7 for the breath YHWH breathed into Adam: "he breathed into his nostrils the nĕšāmat ḥayyîm (breath of life) and man became a living being." Every creature that carries this original divine gift of breath is included in the final praise. The Psalter that began with "blessed is the man" (Ps 1:1) — a single, righteous individual — ends with kōl hanĕšāmāh — every breathing being without exception. The closing double hallĕlû-yāh seals not just this psalm but the entire Psalter: the final word of the 150-psalm collection is praise.

140.1: ḥallĕṣēnî yhwh mēʾādām rāʿ mēʾîš ḥămāsîm tiṣṣĕrēnî — "rescue me, O LORD, from evil men; protect me from violent men" — ḥālaṣ (to draw out, rescue, extricate — Piel) — originally to pull off a sandal, then extended to extracting from danger. ʾîš ḥămāsîm (men of violence, from ḥāmās, violence, wrongdoing) — ḥāmās is the pervasive OT word for physical and social violence that overturns right order; it is specifically what caused the Flood (Gen 6:11,13). The superscription names David, suggesting the setting of Saul's court or the Absalom rebellion.
140.2: ʾăšer ḥāšĕbû rāʿôt bĕlēb kol-yôm yāgûrû milḥāmôt — "who scheme wickedness in their hearts and stir up conflicts every day" — ḥāšab (to plan, devise, calculate, reckon) — used for skilled craftsmanship (Exod 31:4) and for deliberate scheming; the same mental intensity applied to both art and malice. kol-yôm (every day) — the constancy of their scheming makes it structural, not occasional. yāgûrû milḥāmôt (they stir up / assemble wars) — gûr in this context = to attack, stir up strife.
140.3: šānnĕnû lĕšônām kĕnaḥāš ḥămat ʿakšûb taḥat śĕpātêhem selāh — "they sharpen their tongues like a serpent; viper venom is under their lips" — šānan (to whet, sharpen) — the tongue as a blade being honed. The serpent (nāḥāš) metaphor links to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:1-5). ḥămat ʿakšûb (the venom of a viper/asp) — ʿakšûb is a species of Palestinian viper. Paul strings this verse into his universal-sinfulness catena in Rom 3:13 ("the poison of asps is under their lips"), bracketed by Ps 5:9 and Ps 10:7 — the psalmist's description of particular enemies becomes, in Paul's reading, a diagnosis of universal human fallenness.
140.4: šomrēnî yhwh mîdê rāšāʿ miʾîš ḥămāsîm tiṣṣĕrēnî ʾăšer ḥāšĕbû lidĕḥôt pĕʿāmāy — "keep me, O LORD, from the clutches of the wicked; preserve me from violent men who plan to make me stumble" — yād rāšāʿ (the hand/power of the wicked) — yād as agency and power, not merely the anatomical hand. ḥāšĕbû lidĕḥôt pĕʿāmāy (they have planned to push aside/thrust my steps) — dāḥāh (to thrust, push away, throw down); pĕʿāmîm (steps, footfalls) — the wicked do not merely obstruct but actively plan to knock the psalmist off his feet.
140.5: ṭāmĕnû gēʾîm pah lî ûḥăbālîm pārĕśû rešet lĕyad-maʿgāl môqĕšîm šātû-lî selāh — "the arrogant have hidden a trap for me and stretched out cords; they have spread a net beside my path and set snares" — gēʾîm (the proud, arrogant ones) as the subject of concealed trapping — pride and cunning working together. pah (a spring-trap, snare), ḥăbālîm (cords, ropes), rešet (net), môqĕšîm (snares) — four different trap-instruments in a single verse, creating the picture of a path surrounded and booby-trapped at every point. The bird-and-hunter imagery connects to Ps 124:7 and 141:9.
140.6: ʾāmartî layhwāh ʾēlî ʾattāh hăqîšîbāh yhwh qôl taḥănûnāy — "I said to the LORD, 'You are my God' — hear me, O LORD, listen to my pleas for mercy" — ʾēlî ʾattāh (you are my God) — the covenant confession is the foundation of the appeal. Amidst all the traps and violence, the psalmist's anchor is this relational identity: YHWH is my God, not merely a God. taḥănûnāy (my pleas for mercy, from ḥānan) — the petition follows from the relationship: because you are my God, hear my mercy-cry.
140.7: yhwh ʾădōnāy ʿōz yĕšûʿātî sakkōtāh lĕrōʾšî bĕyôm nāšeq — "O LORD the Lord, the strength of my salvation — you have shielded my head in the day of battle" — yhwh ʾădōnāy (the covenant name + the sovereignty name together) — this double divine address appears in several psalms and in Hab 3:19, emphasizing both relational and sovereign dimensions. ʿōz yĕšûʿātî (the strength of my salvation) — YHWH as the source of saving power, not merely the One who saves. sakkōtāh lĕrōʾšî (you have covered/shielded my head) — sākak (to cover, screen, shield, as with a shield or canopy); the head as the most vulnerable target in battle.
140.8: ʾal-tittēn yhwh maʾăwiyyê rāšāʿ zimĕzāmô ʾal-tāpēq yārûmû selāh — "do not give the wicked what they want, O LORD; do not let their evil plans succeed, or they will grow proud" — maʾăwiyyê rāšāʿ (the desires of the wicked, from ʾāwāh, to desire, crave) — granting the wicked their desires would validate their methods. zimĕzāmô (their evil scheme/device, from zāmam, to plan, devise, usually with evil intent). yārûmû (let them be exalted, they would be lifted up) — the imprecation protects cosmic order: a wicked man whose schemes succeed becomes proud, threatening the justice structure.
140.9: rōʾš mĕsabbāy ʿămal śĕpātêmô yĕkhassĕmô — "let the trouble caused by the lips of those who hem me in fall back upon their own heads" — rōʾš mĕsabbāy (the head of those who surround me) — the leader or front person of the encirclement. ʿămal śĕpātêmô (the trouble/toil of their lips) — the verbal harm they have spoken. yĕkhassĕmô (let it cover them, engulf them, from kāsāh, to cover) — the words they have hurled at the psalmist should become a covering over themselves. The lex talionis logic: the trap set for another springs on the trapper.
140.10: yimmōṭû ʿălêhem gĕḥālîm bāʾēš yappilēm bĕmaḥămōrôt bal-yāqûmû — "let burning coals rain down on them; let them be thrown into the fire — into pits so deep they never rise" — gĕḥālîm (burning coals, glowing embers) — the same image as Ps 120:4 (coals of broom wood). The imagery draws on the divine warrior arsenal (Ps 18:12-13; Isa 30:30). maḥămōrôt (pits, depths, from ḥāmar, to be red/boiling?) — deep pits or slime pits (cf. Gen 14:10 where the kings fell into asphalt pits). bal-yāqûmû (may they not rise) — the defeat is final.
140.11: ʾîš lāšôn bal-yikkôn bāʾāreṣ ʾîš-ḥāmās rāʿ yĕṣûdennû lĕmaddĕḥēpôt — "let no slander take root in the land; may evil relentlessly pursue the violent man until he falls" — ʾîš lāšôn (a man of tongue/slander) — the lying tongue that opened the psalm (v1-3) here receives its verdict. bal-yikkôn bāʾāreṣ (let him not be established in the earth) — kûn (to be established, set firm) — the slander-man should have no permanent footing. yĕṣûdennû lĕmaddĕḥēpôt (evil will hunt him to overthrows/downfalls) — dāḥāh (to push, thrust over) — the same verb as v4's plan to push aside the psalmist's steps.
140.12: yādaʿtî kî-yaʿăśeh yhwh dîn ʿānî mišpaṭ ʾebyônîm — "I know that the LORD will uphold the cause of the afflicted and defend the rights of the poor" — the psalm's confident affirmation after the imprecations: not wishing but knowing (yādaʿtî, perfect tense — settled knowledge). dîn ʿānî (the cause/case of the afflicted) and mišpaṭ ʾebyônîm (the justice/right of the poor/needy) — both legal-court terms; YHWH is the judge who takes up the case of those without human advocates.
140.13: ʾak ṣaddîqîm yôdû lišmĕkā yēšĕbû yĕšārîm ʾet-pānêkā — "surely the righteous will give thanks to your name; the upright will dwell in your presence" — ʾak (surely, only, truly) — the affirmative particle closing the psalm with confidence. yōdû lišmĕkā (they will give thanks/praise to your name) — yādāh (Hiphil, to praise, acknowledge) — the same root as tôdāh (thanksgiving). yēšĕbû ʾet-pānêkā (they will dwell in/with your presence) — the ultimate covenant blessing: dwelling with the face of YHWH (Ps 17:15; 73:23-24).

141.1: yhwh qĕrāʾtîkā ḥûšāh lî hăqîšîbāh qôlî bĕqorʾî-lāk — "O LORD, I call on you — come quickly! Hear my voice when I call to you" — ḥûšāh lî (make haste to/for me, hurry!) — ḥûš (to hasten, make speed) as an urgent imperative — the cry of someone who cannot wait. hăqîšîbāh qôlî (give ear to my voice) — qāšab (to pay attention, listen carefully, prick up the ears) — the prayer is for God's ears to be attuned to this specific voice in this specific moment.
141.2: tikkôn tĕpillātî qĕṭōret lĕpānêkā maśśĕʾat kappay minḥat-ʿārěb — "may my prayer be set as incense before you, and the lifting of my hands as the evening offering" — qĕṭōret (incense, smoke of burning spices) — the incense altar in the tabernacle/temple burned morning and evening (Exod 30:7-8); the rising smoke visualized prayer ascending to YHWH. maśśĕʾat kappay (the lifting/raising of my palms) — the gesture of open-palmed prayer (1 Kgs 8:22, 38). minḥat-ʿārěb (the evening meal-offering) — the temple's evening grain offering; the psalmist maps his personal prayer to both the incense offering and the grain offering, making prayerless temple worship impossible (the sacrifice without the prayer is incomplete) and temple-less prayer legitimate (the prayer maps to the sacrifice).
141.3: šîtāh yhwh šomrāh lĕpî nĕṣōr ʿal-delet śĕpātāy — "post a guard at my mouth, O LORD; station a sentry at the door of my lips" — the military metaphors of guarding (šāmar) and the door-sentry (delet) applied to the lips. The gate/door image frames the lips as the entrance to a fortified space: what passes through this gate must be controlled. The prayer is remarkable — the psalmist does not trust his own self-control; he asks YHWH to be the external guard of his speech. nāṣar (to guard, watch over, preserve — used of guarding Torah, secrets, and cities) — a guard of the highest order.
141.4: ʾal-taṭ libbî lĕdābar rāʿ lĕhitʿôlēl ʿălilôt bĕrešaʿ ʾet-ʾanšê pōʿălê ʾāwen wĕbal-ʾelḥam bĕmanʿammêhem — "do not let my heart be drawn to anything evil — to take part in the wicked acts of evildoers, or to taste any of their pleasures" — ʾal-taṭ libbî (do not incline my heart) — nāṭāh (to extend, bend, incline — used for pitching a tent, stretching out a hand, inclining the ear). The heart can be "bent" toward evil; the psalmist prays for its trajectory to be controlled. ʾelḥam bĕmanʿammêhem (let me not eat their delicacies/pleasures) — manʿammîm (delicacies, pleasant things) — the temptation of complicity is framed as food: tasting the pleasures that come with wickedness.
141.5: yĕhalmēnî ṣaddîq ḥesed wĕyôkîḥēnî šemen rōʾš ʾal-yānî rōʾšî — "let a righteous man strike me — it is a kindness; let him rebuke me — it is like oil on my head; let my head not refuse it" — yĕhalmēnî (let him strike me, beat me, Qal jussive of hālam) — the same verb for hammer-strikes (Judg 5:26). But the striker is ṣaddîq (righteous) and the striking is ḥesed (covenant love) — blows from a righteous friend are acts of love. šemen rōʾš (oil of the head) — anointing oil as welcome enrichment; the righteous man's rebuke is as enriching as anointing. The counterintuitive theology: honest rebuke from the righteous is to be welcomed more than the flattery of the wicked. James 5:19-20 and Prov 27:6 stand in this tradition.
141.6: nišmĕṭû bîdê-selaʿ šōpĕṭêhem wĕšāmĕʿû ʾămārāy kî nāʿēmû — "when their leaders are hurled down from the cliffs, the people will find my words welcome" — nišmĕṭû bîdê-selaʿ (they are thrown from the rock/cliff, Niphal of šāmaṭ, to release, let fall, drop) — the violent end of the wicked judges. šōpĕṭêhem (their judges/leaders) — the ones who currently threaten the psalmist. wĕšāmĕʿû ʾămārāy kî nāʿēmû (then they will hear my words, for they are pleasant) — the people who ignored the psalmist's words under oppression will find them welcome when the oppressors fall.
141.7: kĕmô pōlēaḥ ûbōqēaʿ bāʾāreṣ niprĕdû ʿăṣāmênû lĕpî šĕʾôl — "like clods of earth that are broken up and scattered, our bones lie scattered at the mouth of Sheol" — the difficult verse — the image of plowing/splitting the ground is compared to the scattered bones of the psalmist's companions. pōlēaḥ ûbōqēaʿ (splitting and cleaving) describes the plow's action on hard soil. ʿăṣāmênû (our bones) — the group has suffered such devastation that their bones are already scattered at Sheol's mouth. This may be the extremity of David's exile community rather than literal death.
141.8: kî ʾēlêkā yhwh ʾădōnāy ʿênāy bĕkā ḥāsîtî ʾal-tĕʿar napšî — "but my eyes are fixed on you, O Lord GOD — in you I shelter; do not leave my soul exposed" — the pivot from the extremity of v7 to fixed gaze. ʿênāy (my eyes) as the directional commitment — the gaze determines the trust. bĕkā ḥāsîtî (in you I have taken refuge) — ḥāsāh (to take refuge, shelter, flee to for protection) — the classic Psalter verb for running to YHWH as one's safe place. ʾal-tĕʿar napšî (do not pour out/expose my soul) — ʿārāh (to lay bare, uncover, pour out) — the soul left exposed like something stripped of its protective covering.
141.9: šomrēnî mîdê pah yāqĕšû-lî ûmôqĕšôt pōʿălê ʾāwen — "keep me from the trap they have set for me, and from the snares of evildoers" — pah yāqĕšû-lî (the trap they have laid for me) — yāqaš (to lay a bird-snare) — the fowler-imagery connects back to Ps 124:7 and 140:5. The path of the righteous is consistently described in these psalms as surrounded by hidden traps — requiring divine navigation to walk safely.
141.10: yippĕlû bĕmakmōrāyw rĕšāʿîm yaḥad ʾānōkî ʿad-ʾeʿĕbōr — "let the wicked fall into their own nets, while I pass over safely" — makmōrāyw (his own nets, from kāmar, to ensnare) — the nets the wicked set for others become their own trap. yaḥad ʾānōkî ʿad-ʾeʿĕbōr (while I alone pass over safely) — ʿābar (to cross over, pass through, pass by) — the Passover resonance is unmistakable: the righteous "passes over" while judgment falls on others. The psalm ends with a vision of escape through the very traps the enemy set.

142.1: qôlî ʾel-yhwh ʾezʿāq qôlî ʾel-yhwh ʾetḥannān — "with full voice I cry out to the LORD; with full voice I plead with him for mercy" — the doubled qôlî (my voice, my voice) — emphatic: this is audible, full-throated prayer, not silent or formal. The superscription connects this to David in a cave (1 Sam 22:1, Adullam; or 1 Sam 24, En-gedi), giving it the setting of fugitive desperation. ʾezʿāq (I cry out, shout for help, from zāʿaq, the verb for the Israelites' cry in Egypt, Exod 2:23) + ʾetḥannān (I plead for grace/mercy, Hithpael of ḥānan) — the two modes of desperate prayer: the urgent shout and the pleading for mercy.
142.2: ʾešpōk lĕpānāyw śîḥî ṣārātî lĕpānāyw ʾaggîd — "I pour out my troubles before him; I lay my distress before him" — ʾešpōk śîḥî (I pour out my complaint/meditation, from šāpak, to pour out + śîaḥ, meditation, complaint, musing) — the liquid metaphor for prayer: what has been accumulating inside is now poured out before YHWH. ṣārātî (my distress, from ṣārar, to be in a narrow place, constrained) — the claustrophobia of being hemmed in. Both śîaḥ and ṣārāh are laid "before his face" (lĕpānāyw) — the face of YHWH as the place of honest presentation.
142.3: bĕhiṭʿaṭṭēp ʿālay rûḥî wĕʾattāh yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî bĕʾōraḥ-zû ʾăhallēk ṭāmĕnû pah lî — "when my spirit grows faint within me, you know my path. In the path I must travel, they have hidden a trap for me" — hiṭʿaṭṭēp (to faint, languish, cover oneself, from ʿāṭap, to be faint, to cover) — the spirit collapsing over itself like someone falling unconscious. yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî (you know my path) — the Psalter's consolation: YHWH's knowledge is not merely informational but relational and protective. Even the hidden trap is known to him. ṭāmĕnû pah lî (they have hidden a trap for me) — the trap is concealed from the psalmist but not from YHWH.
142.4: habbeṭ yamîn ûrĕʾēh wĕʾên-lî makkîr ʾābad mānôs mimmennî ʾên dôrēš lĕnapšî — "I look to my right and see — no one acknowledges me; all escape has perished from me; no one cares for my soul" — habbeṭ yamîn ûrĕʾēh (look to the right and see) — the right side was the position of the defense advocate in court (Ps 109:31; 110:5). The psalmist looks for a defender and finds none. makkîr (one who knows/acknowledges, from nākar) — no one recognizes him or admits knowing him. ʾābad mānôs (escape has perished/is gone) — the exit has disappeared. ʾên dôrēš lĕnapšî (no one is seeking [the welfare of] my soul) — in Hebrew, to "seek" someone's nepeš can mean either to inquire about their welfare or to seek their life; the phrase is deliberately ambiguous — no one seeks his good, and those who are seeking him are his enemies.
142.5: zāʿaqtî ʾēlêkā yhwh ʾāmartî ʾattāh maḥsî ḥelqî bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm — "I cry to you, O LORD; I say, 'You are my shelter and my portion in this living world'" — maḥsî (my shelter, refuge, from ḥāsāh) — YHWH as the refuge when human advocates are absent (v4). ḥelqî (my portion, my share, from ḥālaq, to divide, to allot) — land-allotment language transferred to YHWH himself: the psalmist's inheritance is not land but the God who gives land. This identification (YHWH as the Levitical portion — cf. Num 18:20; Ps 73:26; Lam 3:24) removes the psalmist's ultimate dependence from any earthly allotment. bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm (in the land of the living) — not in Sheol's silence but in the world where YHWH acts.
142.6: haqšîbāh ʾel-rinnātî kî-dalôtî mĕʾōd haṣṣîlēnî mĕrōdĕpay kî ʾāmĕṣû mimmennî — "hear my cry, for I have been brought very low; rescue me from those who pursue me, for they are too strong for me" — dalôtî mĕʾōd (I am very low/reduced, Qal perfect of dālal, to hang low, be reduced, to be thin, impoverished) — complete depletion. rōdĕpay (my pursuers, from rādap, to pursue, chase, persecute) — the same verb for Saul pursuing David. ʾāmĕṣû mimmennî (they are stronger than I am) — the honest admission of being outmatched; the prayer is precisely because the psalmist cannot save himself.
142.7: hôṣîʾāh mimmaskēr napšî lĕhôdôt ʾet-šimĕkā bî yakkĕtrû ṣaddîqîm kî tigmōl ʿālay — "lead my soul out of this prison so I can praise your name; then the righteous will gather around me, because you will have been generous to me" — mimmaskēr (from the prison/enclosure, from sākar, to shut, enclose) — the cave of v1's superscription becomes the prison. lĕhôdôt ʾet-šimĕkā (to praise/acknowledge your name) — the motivation for rescue is worship: the dead cannot praise (Ps 115:17), so rescue enables the very thing YHWH desires. bî yakkĕtrû ṣaddîqîm (the righteous will crown/encircle themselves around me) — kātar (to surround, encircle, crown) — community restored around the delivered one. kî tigmōl ʿālay (because you will have been generous/will have dealt fully with me) — gāmal (to deal fully, recompense, wean) — the generous divine dealing becomes the rallying point of the community.

143.1: yhwh šĕmaʿ tĕpillātî haʾăzînāh ʾel-taḥănûnāy baʾămûnātĕkā ʿănēnî bĕṣidqātĕkā — "O LORD, hear my prayer; listen to my pleas for mercy; in your faithfulness and righteousness, answer me" — the appeal grounds YHWH's response not in the psalmist's merit but in two divine attributes: ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability, from ʾāman — the root of ʾāmēn) and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness, right action). The psalmist appeals to YHWH's character rather than to his own deserving — a structurally important opening that anticipates v2's disclaimer of personal righteousness.
143.2: wĕʾal-tābôʾ bĕmišpāṭ ʾet-ʿabdĕkā kî lōʾ-yiṣdaq lĕpānêkā kol-ḥāy — "do not bring your servant into judgment, for no living person can stand as righteous before you" — ʾal-tābôʾ bĕmišpāṭ (do not enter into judgment with) — if YHWH were to mount a full judicial review of the servant's life, the verdict would be fatal. lōʾ-yiṣdaq lĕpānêkā kol-ḥāy (no living person can be declared righteous before you) — one of the OT's most unambiguous statements of universal human moral inadequacy before the divine standard. This verse stands behind the NT's doctrine of justification by grace: if no one is righteous before God (cf. Rom 3:20, Gal 2:16 — Paul cites Ps 143:2 directly), then the only hope is God's own provision of righteousness. The verse is cited without modification in the Greek of Gal 2:16.
143.3: kî rādap ʾôyēb napšî dikkāʾ lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî hôšîbanî bĕmahăšakkîm kĕmētê ʿôlām — "for the enemy has hunted me down and crushed my life to the ground; he has forced me to dwell in darkness like those who died long ago" — dikkāʾ lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî (he crushed my life to the ground) — dākaʾ (to crush, smite, contrite — related to dakkāʾ, the crushed/contrite ones of Ps 51:17; Isa 57:15). mahăšakkîm (dark places, from ḥāšak, to be dark) — the places of darkness equivalent to Sheol. kĕmētê ʿôlām (like those who died long ago, the long-dead ones) — the psalmist is still alive but already experiencing the condition of the permanently dead.
143.4: waṭṭitʿaṭṭēp ʿālay rûḥî bĕtôkî yiššōmēm libbî — "so my spirit grows faint within me; my heart within me is filled with dismay/is appalled" — hiṭʿaṭṭēp rûḥî (my spirit faints/collapses, same verb as Ps 142:3) — the two cave/darkness psalms (142-143) share this vocabulary of spiritual collapse. yiššōmēm libbî (my heart is desolate/appalled, Hithpael of šāmam, to be desolate, devastated, appalled) — the word used for desolated land after catastrophe (Lam 1:13,16); the heart is a desolated landscape.
143.5: zākartî yāmîm miqqedem hāgîtî bĕkhol-paʿălĕkā bĕmaʿăśēh yādêkā ʾăśôḥēaḥ — "I call to mind the days of old; I meditate on all you have done; I reflect on the work of your hands" — zākartî yāmîm miqqedem (I remember the days of old) — the classic Deuteronomic move: when the present is overwhelming, look back to what YHWH has done in history. hāgîtî (I meditate, murmur, from hāgāh — the same verb as Ps 1:2's Torah-meditation). ʾăśôḥēaḥ (I reflect, muse, from śîaḥ — complaint/meditation, the same word as Ps 142:2) — here the musing is turned from complaint to contemplation of YHWH's past works.
143.6: pēraśtî yādāy ʾēlêkā napšî kĕʾereṣ ʿăyēpāh lĕkā selāh — "I reach out my hands toward you; my whole being longs for you like a parched land" — pēraśtî yādāy ʾēlêkā (I spread out my hands toward you) — the gesture of open-palmed prayer extending toward YHWH. napšî kĕʾereṣ ʿăyēpāh lĕkā (my soul is like a parched/thirsty land to/for you) — one of the Psalter's most vivid anthropological images: the whole living person (nepeš) is a dried, cracked landscape yearning for rain. The simile maps onto the agricultural reality of the Judean wilderness — soil that has split open from thirst literally gapes open seeking water. The soul's orientation toward YHWH is structural, like the cracked earth's openness to rain.
143.7: mahēr ʿănēnî yhwh kālĕtāh rûḥî ʾal-tastēr pānêkā mimmennî wĕnimšaltî ʿim-yōrĕdê bôr — "answer me quickly, O LORD — my spirit is failing! Do not hide your face from me, or I will be like those who sink into the pit" — kālĕtāh rûḥî (my spirit is finished/consumed, from kālāh, to be complete, finished, consumed) — the spirit running out like a candle nearly burned through. ʾal-tastēr pānêkā (do not hide your face) — the hidden face of YHWH as abandonment (Ps 27:9; 88:14; Isa 54:8); the presence of YHWH's face is the prerequisite for life. yōrĕdê bôr (those who go down to the pit) — bôr (a pit, cistern, also Sheol) — the alternative to YHWH's face is being like the dead.
143.8: hašmîʿēnî babboqer ḥasdĕkā kî-bĕkā bāṭāḥtî hôdîʿēnî derekh-zû ʾēlēk kî-ʾēlêkā nāśāʾtî napšî — "let me hear of your steadfast love in the morning, for in you I put my trust; show me the path I should take, for I lift my soul to you" — hašmîʿēnî babboqer (cause me to hear in the morning) — morning as the traditional time of divine salvation announcement (Ps 46:5: "God will help her at the break of dawn"; 90:14; Lam 3:23). The morning announcement of ḥesed (covenant love) answers the night of darkness (v3). derekh-zû ʾēlēk (the path by which I should go) — practical directional guidance embedded within the deep prayer for covenant love.
143.9: haṣṣîlēnî mēʾōyĕbay yhwh ʾēlêkā kissîtî — "rescue me from my enemies, O LORD — I run to you for shelter" — kissîtî (I have covered/hidden myself, from kāsāh, to cover) — the psalmist uses YHWH as a covering, a hiding place (Ps 32:7; 91:1-4). The phrase may also be read "to you I have fled for cover" — YHWH as the protective hiding place from enemy pursuit.
143.10: lammĕdēnî laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhāy rûḥăkā ṭôbāh tanhênî bĕʾereṣ mîšôr — "teach me to do your will, for you are my God; may your good Spirit lead me on a level path" — rûḥăkā ṭôbāh (your good Spirit) — one of only three explicit references to the divine Spirit in the Psalter (Ps 51:11: "take not your Holy Spirit from me"; Ps 139:7: "where shall I flee from your Spirit?"; here). ṭôbāh (good, pleasant, beneficent) distinguishes this from a merely powerful or ambiguous spirit. tanhênî bĕʾereṣ mîšôr (lead me on a level/smooth path, from nāḥāh, to lead + mîšôr, level ground, plain, straightness) — the Spirit's leading counteracts the rough, trap-laden terrain of the enemy's path (vv3-4). The Spirit levels the road for the psalmist's walk.
143.11: lĕmaʿan-šimĕkā yhwh tĕḥayyēnî bĕṣidqātĕkā tôṣîʾ miṣṣārāh napšî — "for the sake of your name, O LORD, keep me alive; in your righteousness lead my soul out of its distress" — lĕmaʿan-šimĕkā (for the sake of your name) — the appeal to YHWH's own reputation rather than to the psalmist's merit (cf. Ps 25:11; 31:3; Ezek 36:22). YHWH's life-preserving action demonstrates who he is. bĕṣidqātĕkā (in your righteousness) — righteousness not as judgment (as it could be in v2's legal setting) but as salvific activity: the righteous God acts righteously by saving the one who cries to him.
143.12: ûbĕḥasdĕkā taṣmît ʾôyĕbāy wĕhʾăbadtā kol-ṣōrĕrê napšî kî ʾănî ʿabdĕkā — "and in your steadfast love silence my enemies and put an end to all who oppress me — for I am your servant" — ûbĕḥasdĕkā (in your ḥesed) — the psalm closes as it opened: both the appeal and the action are grounded in divine character attributes (ʾĕmûnāh and ṣĕdāqāh, v1; ḥesed, v12). kî ʾănî ʿabdĕkā (for I am your servant) — the final self-identification; the claim is not moral excellence but covenantal relationship: the servant has recourse to the master. Ps 143 moves from universal human inadequacy (v2) to the servant's particular claim on the master's ḥesed (v12).

144.1: bārûk yhwh ṣûrî hamĕlammēd yādāy laqrāb ʾeṣbĕʿôtāy lammilḥāmāh — "blessed is the LORD, my rock, who trains my hands for war and my fingers for battle" — ṣûrî (my rock) — the divine epithet ṣûr (rock, cliff-face) emphasizes permanence and refuge (Ps 18:2; 19:14; 28:1). The psalm borrows heavily from Ps 18 — vv1-2 closely parallel Ps 18:2,34,47. hamĕlammēd yādāy laqrāb (who trains my hands for war) — the divine instructor of military skill: God himself is the one who makes David effective in battle. ʾeṣbĕʿôtāy lammilḥāmāh (my fingers for battle) — the detail of fingers alongside hands suggests precision skill (archery, swordsmanship) rather than mere brute strength.
144.2: ḥasdî ûmĕṣûdātî miśgabbî ûmĕpallĕṭî lî māginnî ûbô ḥāsîtî hārôdēd ʿammî taḥtāy — "he is my steadfast love and my fortress, my high tower and my deliverer, my shield and the one in whom I trust, who subdues peoples under me" — six divine titles stacked in rapid sequence: ḥasdî (my steadfast love / the source of my ḥesed), mĕṣûdātî (my stronghold/fortress), miśgabbî (my high place/refuge, from śāgab, to be high), mĕpallĕṭî (my deliverer), māginnî (my shield), and the one in whom I take refuge. The accumulation emphasizes that YHWH covers every dimension of protection — offensive, defensive, personal, and political.
144.3: yhwh mah-ʾādām wattēdāʿēhû ben-ʾĕnôš wattaḥaššĕbēhû — "LORD, what is a human being that you take notice of him, or the son of man that you think of him?" — near-verbatim parallel to Ps 8:4 (where the same question appears: mah-ʾĕnôš kî-tizkĕrennû ûben-ʾādām kî tipqĕdennû). But the registers differ: Ps 8 marvels at human dignity (the human is crowned with glory despite being small); Ps 144 marvels at divine attention to such an insubstantial creature (v4 explains why the question is shocking — humans are mere breath and shadow). Heb 2:6-8 cites Ps 8:4 and applies it to Christ; Ps 144's darker register of human transience is the foil that makes divine attention all the more remarkable.
144.4: ʾādām lahebel dāmāh yāmāyw kĕṣel ʿôbēr — "a human being is like a breath/vapor; his days pass like a shadow" — hebel (breath, vapor, vanity, futility) — the signature word of Ecclesiastes (Eccl 1:2: hebel hăbālîm, "vanity of vanities"); here applied to human existence itself. kĕṣel ʿôbēr (like a shadow passing) — the ṣēl (shadow) is the same word as YHWH's shade in Ps 121:5; here the insubstantial human shadow passes quickly. The theological point: because humans are so insubstantial, YHWH's attention to them is extraordinary condescension — yet he notices and cares (v3).
144.5: yhwh haṭ-šāmêkā wĕrēd gaʿ behārîm wĕyeʿăšānû — "bow your heavens, LORD, and come down; touch the mountains so that they smoke" — haṭ-šāmêkā wĕrēd (bow your heavens and come down) — the Sinai theophany vocabulary: YHWH descending in cloud, fire, and earthquake (Exod 19:18; Ps 18:9). gaʿ behārîm wĕyeʿăšānû (touch the mountains and they will smoke) — volcanic-theophany imagery; YHWH's mere touch causes mountains to smoke (Ps 104:32). The psalmist petitions for a repetition of the Exodus theophany, YHWH coming down into the present crisis.
144.6: bĕrôq bārāq ûtĕpîṣēm šelaḥ ḥiṣṣêkā ûtĕhummēm — "send out lightning and scatter them; shoot your arrows and put them to flight" — bĕrôq bārāq (flash lightning and scatter) — divine lightning as arrows (Ps 18:14; 77:17-18). šelaḥ ḥiṣṣêkā ûtĕhummēm (shoot your arrows and throw them into confusion) — hāmam (to confuse, rout, throw into panic) — the verb used for YHWH confusing the Egyptian army at the Red Sea (Exod 14:24). The petition is for YHWH to act as he did at the Exodus.
144.7: šĕlaḥ yādêkā mimmārôm pĕṣēnî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî mimmayim rabbîm miyyad bĕnê nēkār — "reach down your hand from above; rescue me and deliver me from the overwhelming waters, from the hand of foreigners" — šelaḥ yādêkā mimmārôm (send your hand from on high) — YHWH's hand reaching down from the heavens. mayim rabbîm (the great/overwhelming waters) — the primordial chaos waters (Ps 18:16; 93:4) as the image of the enemies' power. bĕnê nēkār (sons of foreignness, foreign people) — those outside the covenant, characteristically faithless to YHWH (v8's characterization follows).
144.8: ʾăšer pîhem dibbĕr-šāwĕʾ wîmînām yĕmîn šāqer — "whose mouths speak emptiness and whose right hand is a hand of deception" — šāwĕʾ (emptiness, vanity, nothingness, falsehood) — the same word as the third commandment (Exod 20:7: do not take the name of the LORD in vain/šāwĕʾ). Their speech is structurally empty, not merely accidentally false. yĕmîn šāqer (a right hand of deception) — the right hand used for oaths; a deceptive right hand is a perjured oath-hand. The foreigners are characterized by false speech and false oath — the opposite of YHWH whose word is ʾĕmet (truth) and whose oath is sure.
144.9: ʾĕlōhîm šîr ḥādāš ʾāšîr lĕkā bĕnēbel ʿāśôr ʾăzammĕrāh-llāk — "I will sing a new song to you, O God; on a ten-stringed instrument I will sing your praise" — šîr ḥādāš (new song) — the same formula as Ps 33:3; 96:1; 98:1; 149:1; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9; 14:3. A new song for a new act of divine salvation; the expected rescue (vv5-7) calls forth praise before it fully arrives. nēbel ʿāśôr (a ten-stringed lute/harp) — a specific instrument (Ps 33:2; 92:3), possibly with ten strings arranged in a specific tuning.
144.10: hannôtēn tĕšûʿāh lammĕlākhîm hampoṣeh ʾet-dāwid ʿabdô mēḥereb rāʿāh — "who gives victory to kings and rescues his servant David from the deadly sword" — hannôtēn tĕšûʿāh lammĕlākhîm (the one who gives victory/salvation to kings) — YHWH as the source of royal military victory; the king's success is not his own but YHWH's gift. dāwid ʿabdô (his servant David) — naming David explicitly in the body of the psalm alongside "kings" in general places David within a broader theology of divine royal protection.
144.11: pĕṣēnî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî miyyad-bĕnê nēkār ʾăšer pîhem dibbĕr-šāwĕʾ wîmînām yĕmîn šāqer — nearly verbatim repetition of vv7b-8. The repetition creates a refrain-structure: rescue petition (v7) + enemy characterization (v8) → new song (v9-10) → rescue petition + enemy characterization (v11) → covenant blessing vision (vv12-15). The refrain about the foreigners' empty speech and false oath frames the covenant-blessing section as the content that is true, in contrast to the foreigners' falseness.
144.12: ʾăšer bānênû kĕnĕṭîʿîm mĕguddalîm binĕʿûrêhem bĕnôtênû kĕzāwiyyōt mĕḥuṭṭābôt tabbĕnît hêkāl — "may our sons be like vigorous plants in their youth; may our daughters be like carved corner pillars of a palace" — kĕnĕṭîʿîm mĕguddalîm (like plants nurtured/grown large) — the agricultural growth metaphor for human flourishing (Ps 128:3). kĕzāwiyyōt mĕḥuṭṭābôt tabbĕnît hêkāl (like corner pillars carved after the pattern of a palace/temple) — zāwiyyōt (corner pillars, angles) — the daughters are compared to architectural caryatids or atlantes, the carved human-figure columns that supported ancient temple porticoes. The comparison is one of strength, beauty, and load-bearing function — daughters who hold up the household.
144.13: mĕzāwênû mĕlēʾîm mĕpîqîm mizzan ʾel-zan ṣōʾnênû maalîpôt mĕrubbābôt bĕḥûṣôtênû — "may our storehouses be full with every kind of supply; may our flocks produce thousands and ten thousands in our open country" — the covenant blessing catalog: mĕzāwênû mĕlēʾîm (our storehouses/granaries full) — the grain that represents security. maalîpôt mĕrubbābôt (producing in the ten thousands, from ʾālap, thousand, and rābāh, to be many) — the exponential multiplication of flocks echoes the Deuteronomic blessings of Deut 28:4-5 ("the increase of your livestock").
144.14: ʾallûpênû mĕsubbālîm ʾên-pereṣ wĕʾên yôṣēʾt wĕʾên ṣĕwāḥāh birhōbōtênû — "may our cattle be well-laden/strong for labor; may there be no breach and no going out, no cry of distress in our streets" — mĕsubbālîm (laden, carrying loads, from sābal, to carry a heavy load) — work-cattle able to bear their loads. ʾên-pereṣ (no breach in the wall) — a broken-through city wall signals military defeat and exile. wĕʾên yôṣēʾt (no going out, no exile) — citizens not being taken captive. ʾên ṣĕwāḥāh birhōbōtênû (no cry of distress in our streets) — the broad street (rĕḥôb) is the public space where distress becomes visible and audible; its silence signals peace.
144.15: ʾašrê hāʿām šekkākāh llô ʾašrê hāʿām šeyhwāh ʾĕlōhāyw — "blessed are the people for whom it is so; blessed are the people whose God is the LORD" — the double beatitude closes the covenant-blessing section. šekkākāh llô (for whom it is so, lit. "that it is thus to him") — the material blessings of vv12-14. ʾašrê hāʿām šeyhwāh ʾĕlōhāyw (blessed are the people whose God is the LORD) — the second beatitude deepens the first: the real blessing is not the material catalog but the relationship with YHWH that generates it. A people whose God is the LORD have the source of blessing, not merely its expressions.

145.1: tĕhillāh lĕdāwid ʾărômimĕkā ʾĕlôhay hammĕlek ʾăbārĕkāh šimĕkā lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "a praise of David: I will exalt you, my God and King, and bless your name forever and ever" — the superscription tĕhillāh lĕdāwid (a praise of David) — uniquely among the Psalms, Ps 145 is the only one explicitly titled a tĕhillāh (praise, from hālal). In rabbinic Judaism this became the Ashrei psalm, recited three times daily. Ps 145 is a Hebrew acrostic — each verse begins with successive letters (aleph through taw, with the nun letter missing in the MT — probably due to Amos 5:2's use of the nāpĕlāh [fallen] for Israel). ʾĕlôhay hammĕlek (my God and King) — dual title: relational (ʾĕlôhay, my God) and royal (mĕlek, king). YHWH is both covenant partner and sovereign.
145.2: bĕkhol-yôm ʾăbārĕkekā waʾăhallĕlāh šimĕkā lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "every day I will bless you and praise your name forever and ever" — bĕkhol-yôm (every day) — the daily rhythm of blessing: not annual or occasional but quotidian. The temporal markers expand: bĕkhol-yôm (every day) + lĕʿôlām wāʿed (forever and ever). Praise as the fundamental orientation of each day within an eternal horizon. The three-times-daily recitation in Judaism embodies precisely this daily rhythm.
145.3: gādôl yhwh ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd wĕligdullātô ʾên ḥēqer — "the LORD is great and highly to be praised; his greatness no one can search out" — gādôl yhwh ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd (the LORD is great and greatly to be praised) — the pairing of greatness and praise-worthiness: the magnitude of YHWH is the very ground of praise. ligdullātô ʾên ḥēqer (his greatness has no searching out/fathoming) — ḥēqer (searching, investigation, from ḥāqar, to search out, examine) — YHWH's greatness is incomprehensible, beyond the reach of investigation. The Psalter's "great" psalms make this claim repeatedly (Ps 48:1; 95:3).
145.4: dôr lĕdôr yĕšabbaḥ maʿăśêkā ûgĕbûrōtêkā yaggîdû — "one generation will commend your works to the next and will tell of your mighty deeds" — dôr lĕdôr (generation to generation) — the intergenerational transmission of praise is both historical continuity and theological necessity: the works of YHWH must be passed on, or they are forgotten. yĕšabbaḥ maʿăśêkā (they will laud/praise your works, from šābaḥ, to praise, commend — the same verb as Ps 117:1) — the emphasis on works and deeds (maʿăśêkā, gĕbûrôt) grounds the praise in history, not merely in abstract attribute.
145.5: hădar kĕbôd hôdĕkā wĕdibrê nipĕlĕʾōtêkā ʾāśîḥāh — "I will speak of the splendor of your glorious majesty and of your wonderful deeds" — hădār kĕbôd hôd (splendor, glory, majesty) — three overlapping words for divine magnificence; the Psalter uses such pileups to gesture toward what language cannot fully capture. nipĕlĕʾōtêkā (your wonderful deeds, from pālaʾ, to be extraordinary, beyond what is possible) — the pĕlāʾîm (wonders) are by definition things that transcend human capacity and expectation.
145.6: wĕʿezûz nôrĕʾōtêkā yōʾmērû ûgĕdullātĕkā ʾăsapĕrennāh — "people will tell of the power of your awe-inspiring acts, and I will declare your greatness" — nôrĕʾōtêkā (your awesome/fearsome acts, Niphal participle of yārēʾ, to fear) — divine actions that inspire awe and fear. The structure alternates between communal proclamation ("they will say," "people will tell") and personal testimony ("I will speak," "I will declare") — the communal and individual voices of praise interwoven throughout Ps 145.
145.7: zēker rab-ṭûbĕkā yabbîʿû wĕṣidqātĕkā yĕrannēnû — "they will overflow with praise for your great goodness and sing aloud of your righteousness" — yabbîʿû (they will pour out, bubble up, gush, from nābaʿ, to spring up, bubble forth) — praise as a spring that wells up and overflows, not a managed flow. rab-ṭûbĕkā (the abundance of your goodness) — ṭûb (goodness, beauty, prosperity) combined with rab (abundance, greatness). The pairing of ṭûb (goodness) and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) is characteristic of the Psalter's theology: YHWH's goodness is not separate from his right-ordering of all things.
145.8: ḥannûn wĕraḥûm yhwh ʾerek ʾappayim ûgĕdal-ḥāsed — "the LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and great in steadfast love" — verbatim reproduction of Exod 34:6, the divine self-disclosure at Sinai after the golden calf. This verse is the most-quoted passage in the entire Psalter (Ps 86:15; 103:8; 111:4; 112:4; 116:5; Neh 9:17; Jon 4:2; Joel 2:13) — each time it appears it carries the full weight of the Sinai revelation. In context, Ps 145 is specifically citing this tradition: YHWH's rule as king (vv1-2) is characterized by the covenant attributes of grace, compassion, patience, and abundant love.
145.9: ṭôb-yhwh lakkōl wĕraḥămāyw ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw — "the LORD is good to all; his compassion extends over all he has made" — ṭôb-yhwh lakkōl (the LORD is good to all/everything) — the universal scope is striking: not just to Israel, not just to humans, but to all. wĕraḥămāyw ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw (his compassion/tender mercies over all his works) — raḥămîm (plural of reḥem, womb-compassion) extends over the entire created order. This verse is the theological ground of the later description (v15-16) of YHWH feeding all living things.
145.10: yôdûkā yhwh kol-maʿăśêkā waḥăsîdêkā yĕbārĕkûkāh — "all your works will praise you, LORD, and your devoted ones will bless you" — the created order as the sphere of universal praise (anticipated in v9's universal compassion). kol-maʿăśêkā (all your works) — all creation as praise-community. ḥăsîdêkā (your devoted/faithful ones, from ḥesed) — within the universal praise-community, Israel's ḥăsîdîm are the explicit speaking/blessing subgroup. The distinction: creation praises implicitly by its existence; the covenant people bless explicitly by their speech.
145.11: kĕbôd malkûtĕkā yōʾmērû ûgĕbûrātĕkā yĕdabbērû — "they will speak of the glory of your kingdom and tell of your power" — the kingdom (malkût) theme begins here and runs through v13. kĕbôd malkût (the glory of the kingdom) — both YHWH's intrinsic glory and the manifestation of that glory in his royal rule. gĕbûrāh (power, strength, might) — the power exercised in royal action.
145.12: lĕhôdîaʿ libnê hāʾādām gĕbûrōtāyw ûkĕbôd hădār malkûtô — "to make known to all humanity your mighty deeds and the majestic glory of your kingdom" — libnê hāʾādām (to the sons of Adam/humanity) — the witness extends beyond Israel to all human beings; the ḥăsîdîm's testimony serves a universal communicative purpose. The psalmist's praise is not merely self-edifying but outward-facing: making known (hôdîaʿ, Hiphil of yādaʿ) the nature of YHWH's kingdom to those who do not yet know it.
145.13: malkûtĕkā malkût kol-ʿōlāmîm ûmemšaltĕkā bĕkhol-dôr wādôr — "your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations" — malkût kol-ʿōlāmîm (kingdom of all ages, lit. "kingship of all eternities") — the plural ʿōlāmîm emphasizes the absolute temporal scope. ûmemšaltĕkā (your dominion, from māšal, to rule) — royal authority persisting through every generation. This verse is cited in the doxology of the Lord's Prayer ("thine is the kingdom" — though the doxology is a later addition) and directly echoed in Dan 4:3,34 (even the Gentile king Nebuchadnezzar confesses YHWH's eternal kingdom).
145.14: sômēk yhwh lĕkhol-hannōpĕlîm wĕzôqēp lĕkhol-hakkĕpûpîm — "the LORD upholds all who are falling and lifts up all who are bowed down" — sômēk (the one who supports, upholds, from sāmak, to lean on, support, prop — the same root as Ps 111:8, 112:8) — YHWH as the structural support for those who would otherwise fall. hannōpĕlîm (those who are falling, from nāpal, to fall) — catching them in the act of falling. hakkĕpûpîm (those who are bowed down, bent double, from kāpap, to bend, bow) — those doubled over under a load. The verse is both physical (the fallen, the stooped) and social/spiritual (the crushed, the humbled).
145.15: ʿênê-khōl ʾēlêkā yĕśabbērû wĕʾattāh nôtēn-lāhem ʾet-ʾoklām bĕʿittô — "the eyes of all look to you, and you give them their food in due season" — ʿênê-khōl ʾēlêkā yĕśabbērû (the eyes of all are hoping toward you) — śābar (to wait, hope, expect) — the same expectancy as Ps 130:5's qāwāh. All creation is in a posture of expectant looking. bĕʿittô (in its time, in due season) — YHWH's provision is timed: not random or capricious but ordered to the rhythm of need.
145.16: pôtēaḥ ʾet-yādĕkā ûmaśbîaʿ lĕkhol-ḥay rāṣôn — "you open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing" — pôtēaḥ ʾet-yādĕkā (you open your hand) — the open hand of YHWH as the gesture of giving; the image condenses the entire theology of divine provision. ûmaśbîaʿ lĕkhol-ḥay rāṣôn (and satisfy the desire of every living thing) — rāṣôn (desire, pleasure, goodwill, delight) — not merely the physical need but the full desire of the creature. Every living thing (kol-ḥay) — the scope is universal: not just humans, not just Israel, but all life.
145.17: ṣaddîq yhwh bĕkhol-dĕrākhāyw wĕḥāsîd bĕkhol-maʿăśāyw — "the LORD is righteous in all his ways and faithful/loving in all his works" — ṣaddîq (righteous) + ḥāsîd (faithful, devoted, characterized by ḥesed) — the two attributes cover YHWH's moral alignment (righteousness) and his relational fidelity (ḥesed). bĕkhol-dĕrākhāyw (in all his ways) + bĕkhol-maʿăśāyw (in all his works) — the paired "all" is emphatic: no exception to righteousness or fidelity in any of YHWH's paths or acts.
145.18: qārôb yhwh lĕkhol-qōrĕʾāyw lĕkhol ʾăšer yiqrāʾûhû beʾĕmet — "the LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in sincerity/truth" — qārôb (near, Qal adjective from qārab, to draw near) — YHWH's nearness is the counter to his transcendence. The cosmic king (vv11-13) who governs all ages is also qārôb (near) to the individual who calls. beʾĕmet (in truth, sincerity, faithfulness) — the qualifier that the calling must be genuine. ʾĕmet-shaped prayer is prayer aligned with reality, not manipulation.
145.19: rĕṣôn-yĕrēʾāyw yaʿăśeh wĕʾet-šawʿātām yišmaʿ wĕyôšîʿēm — "he fulfills the desire of those who fear him; he hears their cry and saves them" — rĕṣôn-yĕrēʾāyw yaʿăśeh (the desire of those who fear him he fulfills) — the same rāṣôn (desire) as v16 where all living things' desires are satisfied; now the specific sub-community of the God-fearers receives particular attention. Fear of the LORD is the channel through which general divine goodness (vv9,16) flows specifically to those in relationship with him.
145.20: šômēr yhwh ʾet-kol-ʾōhăbāyw wĕʾēt kol-hārĕšāʿîm yašmîd — "the LORD guards all who love him, but he will destroy all the wicked" — the final antithesis in the acrostic (missing only the nun verse). ʾōhăbāyw (those who love him) — love reciprocating love; the God who showed ḥesed is loved by those who experience it. yašmîd (will destroy, from šāmad, to annihilate, destroy completely) — the same fate as the enemies of Israel under the ban. The Psalter does not sentimentalize divine goodness: the open hand of v16 and the destroying word of v20 belong to the same God.
145.21: tĕhillat yhwh yĕdabbēr-pî wîbārēk kol-bāśār šēm qodšô lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "my mouth will speak the praise of the LORD, and let all living flesh bless his holy name forever and ever" — the psalm closes by circling back to v1's individual pledge ("I will bless your name forever and ever") but expanding it to kol-bāśār (all flesh, all living creatures — the broadest possible biological category). The move from "I will exalt you" (v1) to "let all flesh bless" (v21) traces the psalm's theological arc: individual praise becomes universal praise. The acrostic that covers the whole Hebrew alphabet mirrors the universal scope of the praise content.

146.1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlî napšî ʾet-yhwh — "Praise the LORD! Praise the LORD, O my soul!" — Ps 146 opens the Hallelujah Quintet (Ps 146-150), each psalm beginning and ending with hallĕlûyāh. The self-address hallĕlî napšî (praise the LORD, O my soul) — the soul (nepeš) called to praise matches Ps 103:1-2 (same self-exhortation). The psalmist commands his own interior life to align with worship — the will engaging the emotions and the whole living self in the act of praise.
146.2: ʾăhallĕlāh yhwh bĕḥayyāy ʾăzammĕrāh lĕʾĕlōhay bĕʿôdî — "I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praises to my God as long as I live" — bĕḥayyāy (while I live, in my life) + bĕʿôdî (as long as I exist, while I am yet) — the two temporal phrases expressing lifelong praise. This stands in deliberate contrast to vv3-4: human princes have no life beyond death (their plans perish), but the psalmist's praise extends to the full length of his life. The reliability of YHWH (v6-9) is the ground for a life of praise.
146.3: ʾal-tibtĕḥû binnĕdîbîm bĕben-ʾādām šeʾên lô tĕšûʿāh — "do not put your trust in princes, in any human being, for there is no salvation in them" — nĕdîbîm (princes, nobles, willing/generous ones) — the highest rank of human power. ben-ʾādām (son of Adam, a human being) — the mortality-marker; humans are sons of Adam, subject to Adam's curse. šeʾên lô tĕšûʿāh (in whom there is no salvation/rescue) — tĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance, from yāšaʿ) — the same root as yĕhôšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus). No human being, however powerful, has the capacity to save; tĕšûʿāh belongs only to YHWH (v7-9).
146.4: tēṣēʾ rûḥô yāšub lĕʾadmātô bayyôm hahûʾ ʾābĕdû ʿešĕtōnōtāyw — "when his breath/spirit departs, he returns to his earth/soil; on that very day his plans come to nothing" — tēṣēʾ rûḥô (his spirit/breath departs) — rûaḥ as the animating breath; when it departs, the body becomes a corpse. yāšub lĕʾadmātô (he returns to his earth/soil) — the reverse of Gen 2:7 (YHWH breathed into the ʾādām made from ʾadāmāh); death is that creation reversed. bayyôm hahûʾ ʾābĕdû ʿešĕtōnōtāyw (on that very day his plans/thoughts perish) — ʿĕšĕtōnôt (plans, schemes, thoughts, from ʿāšat) — the most powerful person's best-laid plans evaporate the moment the breath leaves. This makes trust in princes not merely imprudent but structurally futile.
146.5: ʾašrê šeʾēl yaʿăqōb bĕʿezrô śibrô ʿal-yhwh ʾĕlōhāyw — "blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD his God" — ʾašrê (blessed, happy) — the beatitude form. In deliberate contrast to v3 (trust in princes = futile), v5 offers the alternative: help from the God of Jacob. ʾēl yaʿăqōb (the God of Jacob) — the patriarchal title emphasizing covenant continuity; Jacob's God is the God of the powerless-made-powerful, the wrestler, the schemer redeemed. śibrô ʿal-yhwh (whose hope is placed on the LORD) — śābar (to hope, wait, expect — same as Ps 145:15) — the posture of expectant trust.
146.6: ʿōśeh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ ʾet-hayyām wĕʾet-kol-ʾăšer-bām haššōmēr ʾĕmet lĕʿôlām — "who made heaven and earth, the sea and everything in them, and who keeps faith forever" — the classic creator-title ʿōśeh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) — appearing also in Ps 115:15; 121:2; 124:8 — grounds the help of v5 in creation sovereignty. The creator who made all things can rescue within what he made. haššōmēr ʾĕmet lĕʿôlām (the one who keeps faith forever) — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness, reliability) + šāmar (to keep, guard) — YHWH guards his own faithfulness; it is not something that can be violated or erode. Unlike the prince whose plans perish in a day (v4), YHWH's faithfulness is permanent.
146.7: ʿōśeh mišpāṭ laʿăšûqîm nôtēn leḥem lārĕʿēbîm yhwh mattîr ʾăsûrîm — "who secures justice for the oppressed and gives food to the hungry; the LORD sets prisoners free" — three divine actions in one verse: ʿōśeh mišpāṭ laʿăšûqîm (doing justice for the oppressed) — ʿāšaq (to oppress, squeeze, defraud) — those who have been wronged in the legal and economic system. nôtēn leḥem lārĕʿēbîm (giving bread to the hungry) — the provision of basic sustenance as a divine act. yhwh mattîr ʾăsûrîm (the LORD sets free those who are bound) — āsar (to bind, imprison). The six divine actions for the vulnerable in vv7-9 form the basis for Jesus' Nazareth manifesto (Luke 4:18-19) and the beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12) — liberation of precisely these categories of people signals the arrival of the kingdom.
146.8: yhwh pōqēaḥ ʿiwwĕrîm yhwh zōqēp kĕpûpîm yhwh ʾōhēb ṣaddîqîm — "the LORD gives sight to the blind; the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down; the LORD loves the righteous" — pōqēaḥ ʿiwwĕrîm (opening [the eyes of] the blind, from pāqaḥ, to open eyes, ears) — the opening of eyes as divine work (Isa 35:5; 42:7). Jesus' healing of the blind (Matt 11:5; John 9) is presented as fulfillment of this prophetic-psalmic pattern. yhwh zōqēp kĕpûpîm (the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down) — same as Ps 145:14 (zôqēp kĕpûpîm), making the connection explicit. yhwh ʾōhēb ṣaddîqîm (the LORD loves the righteous) — the love of YHWH is not merely obligatory covenant action but genuine ʾahăbāh (love).
146.9: yhwh šōmēr ʾet-gērîm yātôm wĕʾalmānāh yĕʿôdēd wĕderek rĕšāʿîm yĕʿawwēt — "the LORD watches over the alien resident; he supports the fatherless and the widow; but the way of the wicked he overturns/makes crooked" — the three classic vulnerable categories: gēr (alien/stranger resident, one without full civic rights), yātôm (orphan, the fatherless), and ʾalmānāh (widow) — the legal-social triad of those without a male protector, repeatedly named in Torah (Exod 22:21-22; Deut 27:19). yĕʿôdēd (he supports, sustains, from ʿûd, to support, restore) — YHWH as the structural support replacing the missing human protector. wĕderek rĕšāʿîm yĕʿawwēt (the way of the wicked he overturns/makes crooked) — the ironic use of ʿāwah (to twist, bend, pervert — used of injustice): the God who straightens the crooked paths of the poor perverts the plans of the wicked.
146.10: yimlōk yhwh lĕʿôlām ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn lĕdōr wādōr hallĕlû-yāh — "the LORD will reign forever — your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD!" — yimlōk yhwh lĕʿôlām (the LORD will reign forever) — the eternal kingship declaration closes the psalm: unlike the prince of v3 whose plans die with his breath, YHWH's reign has no term. ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn (your God, O Zion) — the universal sovereign is specifically Zion's covenant God; the cosmic and the particular are held together. hallĕlû-yāh — closing the first of the Quintet and opening the path to Pss 147-150, the great doxological finale of the entire Psalter.

119.1: ALEPH — ashre tamime-darekh — ashre (blessed/happy) opens both this psalm and the Psalter (Ps 1:1), forming a grand inclusio. tamim-darekh (blameless of way) uses tamim (the Noah/Abraham wholeness word: Gen 6:9; 17:1) applied to the path of life. btorat YHWH (in the instruction/Torah of the LORD) — blamelessness is not an inner achievement but a path walked within the covenant structure of Torah. The psalm's thesis: blessed life is life shaped entirely by divine instruction.
119.2: ashre nozre edotayw — edot (decrees/testimonies, from ud, to bear witness) — covenant stipulations that testify to God's character. bkol-lev (with the whole heart) — repeated throughout Ps 119: the Law is not a system to be gamed but a relationship to be inhabited wholly. yidreshuhhu (they seek/pursue him) — darash (to seek, pursue diligently) — the decrees are kept as the path toward the person of YHWH himself.
119.3: lo-palu avlah bidrakhav halakhu — uniquely, this verse lacks an explicit Torah-synonym, using instead derachav (his ways). avlah (wrongdoing, moral perversity) — the word for injustice violating both covenant and neighbor. The blameless person is described negatively (they do nothing wrong) and positively (they walk in his ways): blamelessness is a pattern of walking, not a catalog of rules.
119.4: attah tzivvita piqqudecha lishmore meod — piqqudim (precepts/mandates, from paqad, to visit/appoint/oversee) — specific applications of covenant instruction to particular situations. meod (greatly, very much) modifies lishmor (to keep): careful keeping is diligent, thorough attention. The verse acknowledges divine initiative: God commanded (tzivvita) — the precepts are given, not invented.
119.5: achale yikonu derachai lishmore chuqqecha — achale (would that!/if only — the optative particle expressing earnest wish) — rare and emphatic; the psalm's first personal confession of imperfection. chuqqim (statutes, from chaqaq, to inscribe/engrave) — decrees carved permanently into covenant life. The optative mood is key: the psalmist does not claim to keep the statutes perfectly but longs to. The tension between aspiration and reality runs through all 176 verses.
119.6: az lo-evosh behabbiti el-kol-mitzvotecha — mitzvot (commandments, from tzavah, to command). az lo-evosh (then I would not be ashamed) — bosh (shame) is the social experience of being publicly exposed as having failed the community's standard. The motivational logic: Torah-keeping produces no shame because alignment with God's character requires no hiding.
119.7: odecha beyosher levav belomdi mishpetei tzidqechah — mishpetei tzidqechah (your righteous decrees/judgments) — mishpat (judgment/ordinance) qualified by tzedqah: these are not arbitrary rulings but righteous ones. yashar levav (upright/straight heart) — praise is the natural expression of a heart aligned with the decrees it is learning. Learning and praise are simultaneous, not sequential.
119.8: et-chuqqecha eshmor al-taazeveni ad-meod — the Aleph stanza closes with vulnerability: al-taazeveni (do not abandon me, negative jussive of azav) — the same word for abandonment used in Ps 22:1. ad-meod (to a great/extreme degree) intensifies the plea.
